file_name: A Comprehensive Evaluation of Large Language Models for Turkish Abstractive Dialogue Summarization.pdf
text: Received 18 August 2024, accepted 1 September 2024, date of publication 4 September 2024, date of current version 13 September 2024. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2024.3454342 A Comprehensive Evaluation of Large Language Models for Turkish Abstractive Dialogue Summarization OSMAN BÜYÜK Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, Izmir Demokrasi University, 35140 Izmir, Türkiye Department of Research and Development, Sestek Speech Enabled Software Technologies Inc., 34396 Istanbul, Türkiye e-mail: osman.buyuk@idu.edu.tr ABSTRACT Text summarization is the task of generating a short and concise summary of a source text. In an abstractive text summarization, the generated summaries may potentially contain new phrases that do not appear in the source text. Dialogue summarization is a special case of text summarization in which the source text is a dialogue between two or more people. Dialogue summarization can be a crucial step especially when the source dialogues are complex and long such as call center conversations. Large language models (LLMs) show remarkable performance in natural language generation tasks and thus they can be a suitable modeling approach for abstractive text summarization. Although LLMs are extensively studied for common languages, there are only a few studies for underrepresented languages such as Turkish. In this paper, we make a comprehensive evaluation of LLMs for Turkish abstractive dialogue summarization. For this purpose, we translated 3 datasets in English to Turkish. Additionally, we make use of a test set that contains real call center dialogues originally collected in Turkish. In the experiments, we observe that fine-tuning LLMs to the dialogue summarization task significantly improves the performance. We obtain 21% overall absolute improvement with the fine-tuning over a baseline Turkish LLM. The performance is improved in all 4 test cases. Additionally, we observe that the length of the summaries plays a crucial role in the performance. INDEX TERMS Abstractive dialogue summarization, large language models, natural language generation, text summarization. I. INTRODUCTION Text summarization is the task of generating a concise summary of a source text [1]. The summary must contain all key information in the source text and must not contain any information which is not mentioned in the text. The text summarization task can be divided into two categories: extractive and abstractive summarization [2]. In the extractive summarization, the most important sentences are picked directly from the source text to form the summary. In the abstractive summarization, the generated summaries may potentially contain new phrases that do not appear in the source text. Dialogue summarization is a special case of text The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Arianna DUlizia . summarization where the source text is a dialogue between two or more people. In call centers, customers interact with a customer support agent to complete a certain task. These call center conversations might have complex structure with multiple turns between customer and agent depending on the com- plexity of the customer’s issue. They consist of specific greetings/farewell turns and customer identity confirmation questions. They may contain repetitions to confirm the given information and sometimes include misunderstandings. These dialogues are later analyzed by the call center company in order to evaluate the customer’s experience and agent’s performance. Therefore, it is crucial to make a concise summary of the long dialogue in order to make the analysis more efficient [3], [4]. VOLUME 12, 2024 2024 The Authors. Büyük: Comprehensive Evaluation of LLMs for Turkish Abstractive Dialogue Summarization Recently, large language models (LLMs) showed remark- able performance in natural language generation tasks in zero shot/few shot settings or when fine-tuned to the target domain [5]. Generative Pre-trained Transformer 4 (GPT-4) is a closed-source LLM and was introduced in 2023 [6]. The underlying architecture and weights of the closed-source LLMs are not publicly available [7]. They are developed and backed by large corporations [7]. The data which will be analyzed should be sent to the provider corporation in order to use the closed-source LLMs. Call center conversations contain sensitive personal information and thus they should be kept private in secure locale servers. Therefore, use of the closed source LLMs (such as GPT-4) to analyze call center conversations is largely restricted due to security concerns. On the other hand, thanks to the natural language processing (NLP) community, many open-source commercially available LLMs are trained and distributed publicly [8]. The source code and weights of the open-source LLMS can be freely accessed, used, modified, and distributed [7]. However, the open-source LLMs are mostly trained for English. There are only a few studies for underrepresented languages such as Turkish. In order to close this research gap, we make a com- prehensive evaluation of LLMs for the Turkish dialogue summarization task. In the evaluations, we make use of the best performing LLMs in the Turkish LLM leaderboard in [9]. In an on-premise application, it is not always possible to allocate required computational resources for very large LLMs. Therefore, we restrict our evaluation to LLMs with at most 8B parameters with an exception for the Orbita model [10]. Although Orbita has 14B parameters, we include it in our evaluations since it yields the best performance in the leaderboard at the time when our evaluations are performed. Turkish lacks datasets for the dialogue summarization task, thus we translated two datasets in English, namely SamSum [11] and DialogSum [12], to Turkish. We also translated the intent induction from conversations for task-oriented dialogue track at the Eleventh Dialog System Technology Challenge (DSTC11) dataset [13] to Turkish. We chose this dataset since it contains dialogues in the finance and banking domains. The DSTC11 dataset does not contain reference summaries. We automatically generated the

file_name: A Federated Adaptive Large Language Model Fine-Tuning Framework for Software Development.pdf
text: 32 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON SERVICES COMPUTING, VOL. 19, NO. 1, JANUARY/FEBRUARY 2026 A Federated Adaptive Large Language Model Fine-Tuning Framework for Software Development Jianguo Chen , Zeju Cai , Wenqing Chen , Member, IEEE, Weicheng Wang, Zibin Zheng , Fellow, IEEE, and Philip S. Yu , Life Fellow, IEEE Abstract—Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved re- markable progress in code intelligence tasks, signiﬁcantly enhanc- ing the efﬁciency of software development. However, several chal- lenges remain. First, ﬁne-tuning LLMs for speciﬁc tasks requires a large amount of task-speciﬁc labeled data, which is often costly and time-consuming to acquire. Second, due to the sensitivity of code data, high-quality internal datasets from different organi- zations cannot be directly shared or combined for ﬁne-tuning. Moreover, variations in programming languages across organi- zations can introduce interference during the ﬁne-tuning process. To address these challenges, we propose F-CodeLLM, a federated adaptive large language model ﬁne-tuning framework designed for real-world software development scenarios. To the best of our knowledge, this is the ﬁrst approach to apply federated learning to the ﬁne-tuning of code LLMs, enabling collaborative model opti- mization while preserving the privacy of each organization’s code data. We design an efﬁcient LLM ﬁne-tuning method to mitigate the computational and communication overhead associated with collaborative ﬁne-tuning. Experimental results demonstrate that F-CodeLLM effectively allows LLMs to learn from each organi- zation’s dataset, achieving performance comparable to centralized ﬁne-tuning. Furthermore, F-CodeLLM is well-suited for multilin- gual data environments, as it can leverage shared knowledge across programming languages to enhance performance within individual language domains. Index Terms—Code intelligence, federated ﬁne-tuning, large language model, software development. I. INTRODUCTION W ITH the rapid advancement of Natural Language Pro- cessing (NLP), Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as a major milestone in the ﬁeld. Compared with earlier language models such as GloVe [1], LLMs exhibit signiﬁcantly Received 10 November 2023; revised 10 October 2025; accepted 12 October 2025. Date of publication 22 October 2025; date of current version 5 February 2026. (Corresponding author: Wenging Chen.) Jianguo Chen, Zeju Cai, Wenqing Chen, Weicheng Wang, and Zibin Zheng are with the School of Software Engineering and Zhuhai Key Laboratory of Trusted Large Language Models, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 519082, China (e-mail: chenjg33@mail.sysu.edu.cn; caizj9@mail.sysu.edu.cn; chenwq95@mail.sysu.edu.cn; wangwch7@mail.sysu.edu.cn; zhzibin@mail. sysu.edu.cn). PhilipS.YuiswiththeDepartmentofComputerScience,UniversityofIllinois at Chicago, Chicago, IL 60607 USA (e-mail: psyu@uic.edu). Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/AAnony/F-CodeLLM. Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TSC.2025.3623626 stronger performance across a wide spectrum of NLP tasks, including machine translation and question answering [2], [3]. Recognizing that source code possesses structural and seman- tic characteristics analogous to natural language, researchers have extended LLMs to code-related applications, such as code generation, summarization, and translation [4]. The emergence of code-oriented LLMs has further accelerated the paradigm shift toward Software 3.0, an era in which AI models can autonomously assist—or even lead—software engineering pro- cesses [5]. Despite the impressive zero-shot and few-shot gen- eralization capabilities of general-purpose LLMs, their perfor- mance often lags behind task-speciﬁc ﬁne-tuned models [6]. For example, models such as CodeX tend to underperform on domain-speciﬁc programming languages like SQL, which contains multiple dialects that general LLMs are typically not trained to distinguish effectively [7]. In practice, ﬁne-tuning on domain-relevant data remains crucial to maximize the utility of LLMs in specialized software development scenarios. The effectiveness of this process, however, hinges on access to high-quality, task-speciﬁc training data. Public code repositories such as GitHub offer a wide range of programming examples; however, they often fail to reﬂect the internal conventions, documentation styles, and system ar- chitectures characteristic of proprietary or enterprise-level code bases [8]. In real-world software development, each organiza- tion typically maintains code with a unique structure, domain- speciﬁc APIs, and customized business logic. Therefore, to effectively adapt LLMs for downstream software engineering tasks, organizations need to ﬁne-tune models on their private code data. However, this process is often infeasible under a centralized learning paradigm, primarily due to the sensitive na- ture of code assets, competitive conﬁdentiality, and increasingly stringent privacy regulations such as GDPR and HIPAA [9], [10]. Moreover, the process of curating and labeling high-quality training data is both costly and time-intensive, further discour- aging organizations from sharing their datasets openly. To address these challenges, Federated Learning (FL) provides a promising alternative. FL enables collaborative model training across multiple organizations without the need to transfer local data, thereby preserving privacy and data sovereignty [11]. Initially applied to domains such as keyboard prediction in Gboard, FL has since been successfully extended to NLP tasks [12]. In addition, FL offers beneﬁts particularly relevant to software engineering, including personalization, ro- bustness, diversity, and regulatory compliance [13]. However, 1939-1374 © 2025 IEEE. CHEN et al.: FEDERATED ADAPTIVE LARGE LANGUAGE MODEL FINE-TUNING FRAMEWORK FOR SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT 33 Fig. 1. Example of collaborative software development across multiple orga- nizations using the F-CodeLLM framework. Organizations can collaboratively ﬁne-tune the model while preserving the privacy of their local code data, ultimately producing a model that outperforms those ﬁne-tuned individually. applying FL to large code LLMs introduces new technical chal- lenges. Traditional FL requires frequent transmission of model parameters, which becomes prohibitively expensive for large- scale LLMs. Moreover, full ﬁne-tuning of LLMs demands sub- stantial memory and computational resources, often exceeding the capacity of participating clients. These constraints present signiﬁcant practical obstacles to the adoption of FL in real-world software organizations. In this paper, we propose F-CodeLLM, a federated adaptive large language model ﬁne-tuning framework for software de- velopment. F-CodeLLM updates models using small, trainable adapter modules instead of the full set of model parameters, thereby signiﬁcantly reducing both communication overhead and local resource demands. An example of collaborative soft- ware development across multiple organizations using the F- CodeLLM framework is illustrated in Fig. 1. To the best of our knowledge, we are the ﬁrst to apply FL to address the challenge of limited code data for LLM ﬁne-tuning on speciﬁc downstream tasks. Our main contributions are as follows: r We employ FL to address the challenge of limited code data for LLMs during ﬁne-tuning on speciﬁc downstream tasks. When the code languages vary across organizations, F-CodeLLM is capable of effectively capturing the shared features of different code languages, thereby enhancing performance on individual code languages. r To mitigate the high communication overhead and com- putational burden associated with federated ﬁne-tuning of LLMs, we propose an efﬁcient joint ﬁne-tuning method based on LoRA, which substantially reduces both compu- tational and network demands at the edge. r We evaluate F-CodeLLM on three code-related down- stream tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that it effectively aggregates knowledge from multiple orga- nizations, signiﬁcantly improving model performance while reducing network trafﬁc and computational resource consumption compared to traditional federated learning approaches. The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section II provides a brief discussion to the related work in this ﬁeld. Section III presents a overview of F-CodeLLM. Section V introduces the comparison experiments. Finally, Section VI concludes this work. II. RELATED WORK Extensive research has focused on applying NLP models to software engineering with the goal of enhancing the intelligence of software development tools, processes, and systems [14], [15]. For instance, Lin et al. proposed replacing MSP-RNN with a transformer to identify anomalies in logs, addressing the chal- lenges of parallelization and capturing long-term dependencies associated with RNNs [14]. In [15], Fu et al. introduced a GPT-2- based model for estimating agile story points, demonstrating the potential of NLP models to support project management tasks. Pre-training techniques have developed rapidly. At the same time, the size of model parameters has been continuously in- creasing. Models based on the transformer architecture show certain abilities that only appear in larger models. Smaller models usually do not have these abilities. We refer to models possessing these capabilities as LLMs [16]. Code LLMs are designed to address code-related challenges. They are often built upon high-performing NLP models and are further optimized for code intelligence tasks. For example, CodeT5 is based on the T5 architecture that has been optimized for code intelligence tasks by modifying its training objectives [9]. Other models, such as CodeGen [4] and AlphaCode [17], have also been developed to tackle code-related tasks. The pre-training corpora for code LLMs typically consist of code and text data collected from code hosting platforms such as GitHub and StackOverﬂow [18]. The massive number of parameters in LLMs requires substan- tial computational resources and time for full-parameter ﬁne- tuning [19]. Recently, researchers have proposed Parameter- Efﬁcient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods that signiﬁcantly reduce the computational and memory demands of ﬁne-tuning LLMs. For example, Hu et al. proposed LoRA, a method that reduces the number of trainable parameters during ﬁne-tuning, substan- tially lowering the hardware requirements for LLMs [20]. Other approaches, such as P-Tuning [21], Preﬁx Tuning [22], and Prompt Tuning [23], also reduce hardware demands during ﬁne- tuning. However, these methods primarily focus on centralized ﬁne-tuning. Zhang et al. explored the combination of LoRA and federated learning for general instruction ﬁne-tuning [24]. However, they did not fully address the speciﬁc challenges associated with code-related tasks. Recent advances have combined FL with parameter-efﬁcient tuning (PET) methods to address the challenges of training large models under privacy constraints [25], [26]. Notably, Che et al. proposed FedPrompt, which employs federated prompt tuning to adapt pre-trained language models using learned soft prompts that are optimized collaboratively across clients [25]. Zhang et al. designed FedPETuning, which integrates adapter modules and preﬁx tuning within an FL framework [27]. FedPETuning can reduce communication overhead and improve personal- ization in general NLP tasks. More recently, Babakniya et al. presented SLoRA, which applies LoRA-based ﬁne-tuning in a federated context, effectively reducing both trainable parameters and communication costs [28]. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 34 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON SERVICES COMPUTING, VOL. 19, NO. 1, JANUARY/FEBRUARY 2026 Fig. 2. The proposed F-CodeLLM framework. F-CodeLLM performs federated ﬁne-tuning of LLMs across multiple distinct downstream software development tasks. While current approaches have demonstrated success in NLP domains, they have not been evaluated on code-speciﬁc down- stream tasks involving multiple programming languages and heterogeneous client data. In contrast, our F-CodeLLM is specif- ically designed for the ﬁne-tuning of code LLMs, address- ing challenges unique to code data, such as cross-language generalization and non-IID distributions across organizations. Furthermore, F-CodeLLM leverages LoRA adapters not only to improve communication efﬁciency but also to facilitate the learning of shared representations, thereby enhancing perfor- mance on diverse code tasks in a collaborative manner. III. METHODOLOGY A. Framework Overview We propose F-CodeLLM, a novel federated learning frame- work designed to collaboratively ﬁne-tune code LLMs across multiple organizations without compromising data privacy. In real-world software engineering scenarios, organizations of- ten possess valuable, task-speciﬁc code datasets that cannot be shared due to privacy policies, regulatory constraints, or competitive considerations. This fragmented and siloed data landscape severely limits the ﬁne-tuning and deployment of domain-adapted LLMs. F-CodeLLM is the ﬁrst framework to address this challenge by enabling federated ﬁne-tuning of code LLMs across organizations in a communication- and computation-efﬁcient manner. The workﬂow of the proposed F-CodeLLM framework is illustrated in Fig. 2. The F-CodeLLM architecture consists of two main compo- nents: r Organization Side: Each participating organization down- loads a shared base model and performs local ﬁne-tuning ontask-speciﬁccodedata,updatingonlytheLoRAadapter. Theﬁne-tuningprocessisresource-adaptive,allowingeach participant to select batch sizes and compute settings based on local infrastructure. r Server Side: A trusted server collects adapter updates and sample counts from all participants. It then performs weighted aggregation of adapter parameters and redis- tributes the updated adapters for the next round of training. Training continues for a pre-agreed number of communica- tion rounds. After each round, the server evaluates convergence criteria and orchestrates the next iteration. Once training is com- plete, each organization retains a customized model enhanced by the shared knowledge learned from diverse code datasets—all without exposing any raw code to external parties. F-CodeLLM addresses three core technical challenges: r Challenge 1. Communication bottlenecks in FL with code LLMs: Fine-tuning LLMs in a federated setting incurs high communication costs due to the large size of model Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. CHEN et al.: FEDERATED ADAPTIVE LARGE LANGUAGE MODEL FINE-TUNING FRAMEWORK FOR SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT 35 parameters. To address this, F-CodeLLM employs a lightweight LoRA-based ﬁne-tuning mechanism, which freezes the base model and updates only compact adapter modules. This mechanism signiﬁcantly reduces the amount of data exchanged during each communication round, makingtheframeworkpractical for bandwidth-constrained environments. r Challenge 2. Heterogeneous and Non-IID code data across organizations: Organizations may work with different pro- gramming languages or downstream tasks, resulting in het- erogeneous and non-IID data distributions. F-CodeLLM shows that shared adapter training across diverse clients enables the model to learn cross-language, task-agnostic representations, thereby improving generalization and en- hancing performance on individual client tasks. r Challenge 3. Efﬁcient coordination of distributed ﬁne- tuning: Coordinating the ﬁne-tuning process across dis- tributed clients with varying computational resources poses both system-level and algorithmic challenges. F- CodeLLM deﬁnes a clear protocol for model initialization, local training, adapter uploading, and server-side weighted aggregation. This process is robust to client variability and scales effectively to real-world deployment scenarios. B. Local Fine-Tuning In the local ﬁne-tuning process, prior to training, organiza- tions must preprocess their datasets and convert them into code data suitable for model training through dedicated prompters and tokenizers. These processed datasets are then used to ﬁne- tune the LLM. F-CodeLLM uses LoRA adapters, which inject low-rank matrices into each trainable matrix of the Transformer. These adapters perform the ﬁne-tuning operations instead of updating the full model parameters, thereby reducing computa- tional and memory requirements. The local training process of an F-CodeLLM organization is illustrated in Fig. 3. Adapters are incorporated into each Transformer module of the LLM, with trainable low-rank decomposition matrices injected into the trainable matrices of the self-attention module. The trainable matrices include the query matrix W Q, key matrix W K, value matrix W V , and output matrix W O). Speciﬁcally, for the base model W, consider the j-th key matrix within its i-th Transformer module, denoted as W K i,j ∈Rm×n. The adapter introduces the low-rank matrix ˆW K i,j into this matrix, eliminating the need to directly ﬁne-tune W K i,j. The matrix ˆW K i,j comprises two submatrices, namely: AK i,j ∈Rr×n BK i,j ∈Rm×r (1) where r denotes the rank of the matrix, which is signiﬁcantly smaller than both m and n, and ˆW K i,j = BK i,j × AK i,j ∈Rm×n. The adapter ˆW comprises all low-rank matrices injected into the base model. Both W and ˆW operate on the same input x, and the resulting vectors are then summed element-wise. Assuming only the base model W is used, let h = Wx. The output when Fig. 3. Local training process in the F-CodeLLM framework. Each organiza- tion manages its own local data and performs federated ﬁne-tuning using the F-CodeLLM algorithm. Prior to training, organizations must carefully prepro- cess their local datasets and convert them into code data suitable for model training using speciﬁc prompts and tokenizers. During ﬁne-tuning, adapters inject low-rank matrices into each trainable matrix of the Transformer, allow- ing parameter-efﬁcient updates without directly modifying the original model weights. the adapter is used can be expressed as: h′ = Wv + ˆWv = (W + ˆW)v = (W + ˆB ˆA)v. (2) Due to the gradient instability encountered when training large models, ﬁne-tuning with heterogeneous data across organi- zations can further exacerbate gradient divergence. To tackle this issue, we utilize an L2-norm constraint to constrain model diver- gence during updates. The optimization objective is deﬁned as: min ˆ w  K  k=1 1 B B  i=1 L(w + ˆw; xk,i, yk,i) + λ| ˆw −ˆwc|2 2  , (3) whereL denotes theloss function, and ˆwc represents theglobally aggregatedadapterderivedfromthepriorcommunicationround. K denotes the number of data batches per organization, while B denotes the batch size. λ is the coefﬁcient of the regularization term, which can be tuned based on practical considerations. In the context of multi-programming language joint ﬁne- tuning in F-CodeLLM, each organization incorporates an ad- ditional language identiﬁer into the structured code data. This method enables LLMs to recognize and differentiate between code snippets written in various programming languages. It also allows the models to learn shared features and patterns across languages during the training process. Consequently, F-CodeLLM does not necessitate that all organizations employ thesameprogramminglanguage;instead,itemphasizesthepres- ence of similar downstream tasks among participating entities. C. Federated Fine-Tuning In federated learning, data remains within each organization, with only the trained adapters transmitted between organizations and the server. A key limitation of FL lies in its high demands on network bandwidth and computational resources. These demands escalate as the model size increases, with larger Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Comparison of communication overhead between FL and F- CodeLLM. The bars in the ﬁgure denote the communication costs of two different ﬁne-tuning methods for LLMs, while the line indicates the proportion of communication cost savings achieved by F-CodeLLM relative to FL. models imposing greater strain on both network infrastructure and local computing capabilities. This limitation becomes particularly pronounced when training extremely large models. To address this challenge, we propose a LoRA-based feder- ated ﬁne-tuning algorithm, which reduces the training of large models to the training of compact adapters, as described in the previous section. To demonstrate the communication overhead savings achieved by F-CodeLLM, we conduct simulations of the federated ﬁne-tuning process using LLaMA-7B [29]. The results are presented in Fig. 4. We perform 10 communication rounds to calculate the net- work overhead for a single organization in F-CodeLLM. As evi- dent from the results, our method signiﬁcantly reduces commu- nication overhead, with the reduction rate increasing alongside the number of communication rounds. Assuming there are N organizations participating in the ﬁne-tuning of the code LLM W, which are de- noted as K = {K1, K2, . . ., KN}. The aggregated code datasets from all organizations are represented as Z = {(x1, y1), (x2, y2), . . ., (xn, yn)}, containing a total of n data samples, where xi and yi form a context-target pair. The dataset belonging to organization Kk is denoted as Zk = {(x1, y1), (x2, y2), . . ., (xnk, ynk)}, with nk samples, and n = K k=1 nk. During the ﬁne-tuning process, we freeze the base model W and introduce a compact adapter ˆW rather than train- ing W itself. The ultimate goal of F-CodeLLM is to optimize max ˆ w  (x,y)∈Z |y|  u=1 log(Pw+ ˆ w(yu|x, y<u)), (4) where ˆw denotes the global adapter, and Pw+ ˆ w(yu|x, y<u) represents the probability that the model outputs the u-th target given context x and targets of length less than u as input. However, due to the distributed nature of data across different organizations, it is infeasible to train on all the data in a single lo- cation. Consequently, the optimization objective of F-CodeLLM Algorithm 1: Workﬂow of F-CodeLLM Framework. Require: SN: the set of organizations, T: communication rounds; W: base model; ˆW: adapter model; E: the number of local epoch, B: local minibatch size. Ensure: ˆW T +1: Fine-tuned adapter model. 1: for each organization si in SN in parallel do 2: si downloads W and ˆW 1 from server; 3: si handles local code data; 4: for each communication round t in T do 5: select a subset of organizations S(t) ←selOrgan(N); 6: for each organization si ∈S(t) in parallel do 7: si selects local data for ﬁne-tuning in t round Dt si(t) ←Dsi; 8: split Dt si into batches B of size B; 9: for each local epoch j from 1 to E do 10: for batch bj ∈B do 11: si freezes the base global model W; 12: si updates the weights of the adapter with data batch bj ˆW j+1 si ←LocalTraining(W, ˆW j si, bj); 13: calculate the total amount of data used in the round n ← s∈S(t) |Dt s|; 14: aggregate the global adapter ˆW t+1 ← s∈S(t)( |Dsi| n ˆW E+1 s ); 15: distribute the aggregated adapter ˆW t+1 to all organizations; 16: return ˆW T +1. must be reformulated as: max ˆ w K  k=1 nk n  (x,y)∈Zk |y|  t=1 log(Pw+ ˆ w(yt|x, y<t)). (5) On the server side, we assign distinct aggregation weights to the adapters of each organization in accordance with the FedAvg algorithm[30].Inthet-thcommunicationround,theaggregation weight ˆW t i for the adapter of organization Ki is determined based on the ratio of its local data size nk to the total data size n, i.e., ˆW t+1 = K  k=1 nk n ˆW t i . (6) This aggregation strategy accounts for the scale of local datasets, thereby allowing organizations with larger local datasets to exert a more substantial impact on the training of the global adapter. F-CodeLLM outlines a well-deﬁned protocol for model ini- tialization, local training, adapter uploading, and server-side weighted aggregation. The detailed implementation steps of F-CodeLLM are presented in Algorithm 1. IV. FINE-TUNING AND DEPLOYMENT A. Fine-Tuning on Code Summarization Tasks The task of code generation entails analyzing the source code of a computer program to produce a corresponding natural Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Code summarization dataset pre-processing results. Fig. 6. Performance of F-CodeLLM on the code summarization task using BLEU. Alp-2.5 k means a model ﬁne-tuned independently by a single client within F-CodeLLM. Alp-10 k means a model generated through centralized ﬁne-tuning using all datasets from F-CodeLLM. F-CodeLLM-10 k means a model ﬁne-tuned collaboratively through F-CodeLLM. language summary that accurately conveys the program’s functionality and logic [31]. This task is essential for assisting developers in understanding, maintaining, and reusing code more efﬁciently. Additionally, it enhances code readability and maintainability, thereby fostering improved software engineering practices. The dataset used for this task is obtained from the Python part of CodeSearchNet. To align with the context window of our base model, which is approximately 2000 tokens, we ﬁlter out longer code samples from the dataset. We augment the processed data with appropriate prompt, creating the training data for this task, as illustrated in Fig. 5. To evaluate training performance, we adopt the BLEU met- ric [7], which is widely used in the ﬁelds of machine translation and text summarization. as is as our evaluation metric to as- sess the training effectiveness. BLEU measures the degree of similarity between model-generated sentences and the corre- sponding reference sentences. Fig. 6 presents the performance of F-CodeLLM on the code summarization tasks. F-CodeLLM-10 k demonstrates substantially faster conver- gence and superior ﬁnal model performance compared with Alp-2.5 k. After only one round of model aggregation, the performance of F-CodeLLM-10 k exceeds that of Alp-2.5 k even after 5 rounds of ﬁne-tuning. Similarly, Alp-10 k represents Fig. 7. Scores evaluated by GPT-3.5-Turbo and GPT-4. LLa-ori denotes the LLaMA-7B model without ﬁne-tuning, while Alp-ori denotes the Alpaca-7B model without ﬁne-tuning. LLaMA-2 k represents a model ﬁne-tuned using only 2000 local data points. F-CodeLLM-20 k represents a model trained using F-CodeLLM, and LLa-20 k denotes a model obtained through centralized ﬁne- tuning. The curve illustrates the average scores achieved by each model. a model obtained through centralized ﬁne-tuning of Alpaca using the same dataset as F-CodeLLM-10 K. We observed that although Alp-10 k maintained performance comparable to F- CodeLLM-10 k during the initial ﬁne-tuning stages, its accuracy later exhibited a noticeable decline. We hypothesize that this degradation may result from the inherent instability in LLM training. To mitigate this issue, we restarted the training process beginning from the second communication round. Although the accuracy still declined, the rate of deterioration appeared to slow. We further speculate that the greater stability of F-CodeLLM relative to Alp-10 k during ﬁne-tuning may stem from its model update mechanism. B. Fine-Tuning on Code-Related Instruction-Following Task Code-related instruction following involves enabling a com- puter to execute operations or tasks in a software development context based on natural language instructions. For this task, we utilize the CodeAlpaca dataset, which contains 20,000 training samples. To evaluate the instruction-following capability of each model, we randomly selected 70 samples from the dataset to form the test set. Given the high variability of possible answers, we employed GPT-3.5-Turbo and GPT-4 as evaluation tools, assessing model responses in terms of both code correctness and readability. This task serves to measure a model’s ability to understand and execute instructions expressed in natural lan- guage. Fig. 7 presents the evaluation results for the code-related instruction-following tasks. The experimental results indicate that ﬁne-tuning LLaMA using only local data is insufﬁcient to achieve robust instruction- following capabilities. In contrast, F-CodeLLM substantially enhances model performance for a single organization, yield- ing an improvement of approximately 94.45%. It is notewor- thy that GPT-4 demonstrates greater scoring reliability than GPT-3.5-Turbo in this evaluation. LLaMA-7B often produces repetitive or meaningless rivial outputs, whereas GPT-3.5- Turbo occasionally assigns higher scores than GPT-4, as shown in Fig. 10. Considering GPT-4’s evaluation alone, LLaMA’s Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Code generation dataset pre-processing result. TABLE I EVALUATION RESULTS OF HUMANEVAL performance improves after ﬁne-tuning with 2,000 instruction- following samples. Moreover, compared with the centralized ﬁne-tuning method used to obtain LLa-20 k, F-CodeLLM-20 k achieves scores that are largely comparable, demonstrating that F-CodeLLMcanattainperformancesimilartothatofcentralized ﬁne-tuning. C. Fine-Tuning on Code Generation Tasks We use the CodeSearchNet dataset for the code generation tasks. Longer code samples are ﬁltered out to ensure consistency, althoughthedataprocessingdifferedforthistask.Fourprogram- ming languages are employed in the experiment. For the Python dataset, we adopt the data format (<<function name, function document>>, <<function implementation>>), as illustrated in Fig. 8. For the Go, Java, and JavaScript datasets, the function docu- mentation in CodeSearchNet is located outside the functions. Accordingly, these datasets are processed using the format (<<function document>>, <<function implementation>>). A total of 10 organizations participated in the experiment, which involved three rounds of communication. Each organization contributed approximately 1,000 Python code samples. Con- sistent with the methodology used in the previous experiments, one participating organization is randomly selected to perform ﬁne-tuning exclusively on its local dataset. The distribution of local data across the four organizations is shown in Fig. 9. Table I illustrates the performance of F-CodeLLM on the code-related instruction-following tasks. Alp-1 k denotes the Fig. 9. Distribution of code data across the four organizations. TABLE II EVALUATION RESULTS ON IRRELEVANT DATASETS model obtained by ﬁne-tuning Alpaca using 1,000 data samples. F-CodeLLM-4 k denotes the model obtained by ﬁne-tuning Al- paca with 4,000 data samples using the F-CodeLLM framework. F-CodeLLM-10 k denotes a model derived from ten distinct or- ganizations, where each organization contributed 1000 samples of Python data. As shown in Table I, the model is generated using F- CodeLLM and underwent 3 rounds of communication. For com- parison, Alp-1 k is obtained by randomly selecting one of the ten organizations and performing local ﬁne-tuning. The evaluation results indicate that F-CodeLLM improves model performance by approximately 78.45%, demonstrating that, compared with ﬁne-tuning at a single organization, F-CodeLLM can signif- icantly enhance model capability. Additionally, we compare F-CodeLLM with centralized ﬁne-tuning methods. Alp-10 k denotes the model obtained by ﬁne-tuning Alpaca using the same dataset as F-CodeLLM-10 K. F-CodeLLM is also effective in enabling LLMs to acquire general knowledge from multiple programming language environments, thereby improving per- formance in single-language scenarios. While previous studies have demonstrated the beneﬁts of leveraging multiple program- ming languages in centralized ﬁne-tuning, our work is the ﬁrst to show that LLMs can achieve similar gains in a federated learn- ing setting. Remarkably, despite comprising only 1,000 Python code samples, F-CodeLLM-4 k achieves a 42.86% improvement over Alp-1 k, which is trainend on the same Python dataset. Experimental results show that F-CodeLLM achieves perfor- mance comparable to centralized ﬁne-tuning, and the resulting ﬁne-tuned model outperforms several models of similar scale. To evaluate the performance of F-CodeLLM on unrelated downstream tasks, we conduct experiments combining equal portions of task-independent datasets with the code summariza- tion dataset. The experimental results are presented in Table II. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. CHEN et al.: FEDERATED ADAPTIVE LARGE LANGUAGE MODEL FINE-TUNING FRAMEWORK FOR SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT 39 Fig. 10. Example outputs from the code-related instruction-following experiment for various models. The scores assigned by GPT-4 and GPT-3.5-Turbo are displayed in the bottom-right corner of each model’s output. The results indicate that F-CodeLLM struggles to effectively learn knowledge from the downstream task-independent datasets, and its performance even fell short of the results achieved by solely ﬁne-tuning on local datasets. This suggests that F-CodeLLM might not be well-suited for learning from unrelated task-independent data. In addition, our ﬁndings indicate that F-CodeLLM can signiﬁcantly enhance model performance when downstream tasks are related. However, caution is warranted when applying F-CodeLLM for joint joint ﬁne-tuning on task-independent or unrelated datasets. We hypothesize that, for related downstream tasks, models tend to converge in a cohesive direction, thereby enabling mutual improvement. In contrast, when downstream tasks are unrelated, divergent convergence across models hinders the effective acquisition of new knowledge. V. COMPARISON EXPERIMENTS A. Experimental Setup We conduct our experiments on a Linux server equipped with four GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs, each with 24 GB of memory. The server is powered by an Intel Xeon Gold 6148 CPU and contains 128 GB of RAM, providing sufﬁcient computational resources to support our experiments. The system runs Ubuntu 22.04.2LTS,withCUDA11.8,Python3.8.16,andPytorch2.0.1. 1) Datasets: Our datasets are sourced from CodeAlpaca [33] and CodeSearchNet [34], with data screening and preprocess- ing performed based on these sources. CodeSearchNet is a collaborative dataset developed by GitHub and Microsoft Re- search, comprising 2 million (comment, code) pairs extracted from open-source repositories on GitHub. The dataset spans 6 programming languages: Go, Java, JavaScript, PHP, Python, and Ruby. In contrast, CodeAlpaca is an instruction-following dataset speciﬁcally designed for code generation tasks on GitHub. It contains 20,000 samples across multiple program- ming languages. A detailed description of the data preprocess- ing procedures will be provided in subsequent sections where relevant. 2) Base Models: In our experiments, we employed two base models: LLaMA-7B [29] and Alpaca-7B [5]. The “7B” desig- nation indicates that these models each contain approximately 7 billion parameters. LLaMA is pretrained in an unsupervised manner on a diverse dataset comprising open-source resources from Github, Books, ArXiv, and other sources. This training en- ables LLaMA to demonstrate strong capabilities across various domains, including commonsense reasoning and reading com- prehension. However, its instruction-following ability remains limited, as it has not been speciﬁcally ﬁne-tuned for instruction- based tasks. In contrast, Alpaca is derived from LLaMA through ﬁne-tuning by Stanford using instruction-following data. This process signiﬁcantly enhances Alpaca’s capacity to follow in- structions accurately and effectively. Consequently, Alpaca ex- cels in tasks requiring precise adherence to provided instruc- tions, making it highly effective in instruction-based scenarios. B. Resource Consumption Evaluation of F-CodeLLM Compared with traditional federated learning, F-CodeLLM demonstrates substantial advantages in resource efﬁciency, pri- marily reﬂected in two key aspects: (1) reduced network trans- mission overhead and (2) a lower number of trainable parame- ters. Fig. 4 presents an estimation of the network transmission overhead saved by F-CodeLLM for a single organization. It can be observed that, after 5 communication rounds, the cumulative savings in communication overhead exceed 90%. Additionally, Table III provides a comparison of the number of trainable parameters between F-CodeLLM and conventional federated learning. Restrictions apply. 40 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON SERVICES COMPUTING, VOL. 19, NO. 1, JANUARY/FEBRUARY 2026 TABLE III COMPARISON OF TRAINABLE PARAMETERS Fig. 11. Results under multidimensional imbalanced federation settings. LoRA-r corresponds to the hyperparameter ‘r’ in LoRA, representing the attention dimension of the LoRA adapter. A larger LoRA-r results in a bigger adapter, which can po- tentially improve ﬁne-tuning performance, whereas reducing LoRA-r decreases the adapter size. Our results demonstrate that F-CodeLLM substantially outperforms direct ﬁne-tuning of LLMs, while reducing the number of trainable parameters by over 90%. C. Evaluation Under Multidimensional Imbalanced Federated Settings To evaluate the scalability and robustness of F-CodeLLM, we extend the experimental setup to encompass a range of client counts and multidimensional imbalance scenarios. We simulate federations with 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, and 300 clients to reﬂect both small-scale collaborations (e.g., niche enterprise consortia) and large-scale ecosystems (e.g., cross-industry open- source alliances). For data distribution, we employe a Zipﬁan distribution with parameters s=(1.0, 2.0) as a baseline to model data imbalance. This distribution, commonly observed in natural and social systems including software ecosystems, represents a moderate data skew. Beyond data distribution, we introduce two practical dimensions of heterogeneity to reﬂect real-world feder- ated software development. For hardware heterogeneity, clients utilizedisparatehardware,includingNVIDIAA100GPUs,RTX 3090 GPUs, and CPUs. For code heterogeneity, client-speciﬁc datasets vary in programming language proportions, task types, and code complexity. For each client count, we evaluate scenarios under balanced data distributions as well as different Zipﬁan distributions, con- sidering both isomorphic (Iso) and heterogeneous (Het) hard- ware/code conﬁgurations. We report metrics such as exact match accuracy and convergence rounds to demonstrate F-CodeLLM’s adaptability across diverse real-world collaboration scales. The experimental results are presented in Fig. 11. Fig. 11(a) illustrates exact match accuracy as a function of the number of clients under different combinations of data skew (Zipﬁan s = 1.0, s = 1.5) and system heterogeneity (Iso versus Het). Across all scenarios, accuracy decreases as the number of clients increases. This trend is attributed to the greater heterogeneity in data, hardware, and code inherent in larger federations, which complicates consistent knowledge aggregation. Extreme data skew (s = 1.5) results in a more pronounced reduction in accuracy compared to moderate skew (s = 1.0). For example, with 10 clients, F-CodeLLM achieves approximately 80% accuracy under the Zipﬁan (s = 1.0)+Iso conﬁguration, whereas accuracy drops to around 75% under the Zipﬁan (s=1.5)+Iso conﬁguration. This reﬂects the real-world challengeposedbydataoligarchs,whereasmallsubsetofclients holds the majority of the data. Regarding system heterogene- ity, heterogeneous hardware and code further reduces accuracy compared to isomorphic settings under the same data skew. For instance,withZipﬁan(s=1.0)at10clients,theHetconﬁguration yields approximately 78% accuracy, compared to 80% for the Iso conﬁguration. This results demonstrate that hardware and code diversity introduces an additional layer of complexity that negatively impacts performance. Fig. 11(b) illustrates the number of rounds required to reach 90% of peak performance as a function of client count, data skew, and system heterogeneity. The number of convergence rounds increases with the number of clients, as larger feder- ations require more frequent model aggregation and synchro- nization, introducing additional overhead. Extreme data skew (s = 1.5) necessitates more convergence rounds than moderate skew (s = 1.0). For instance, with 10 clients, the Zipﬁan (s = 1.5)+Iso conﬁguration requires approximately 35 rounds, whereas the Zipﬁan (s = 1.0)+Iso conﬁguration requires only about 20 rounds. The impact of heterogeneity (Het versus Iso) is also apparent: heterogeneous hardware and code increases the number of convergence rounds relative to isomorphic settings. For example, under Zipﬁan (s = 1.0) with 10 clients, the Het conﬁguration converges in approximately 25 rounds, compared to roughly 20 rounds for the Iso conﬁguration. Collectively, these results demonstrate that F-CodeLLM is robust to multidimensional imbalances, including data skew, hardware heterogeneity, and code heterogeneity, as well as to large-scale federations of up to 300 clients. Even under ex- treme data skew (s = 1.5) and system heterogeneity conditions, F-CodeLLM maintains reasonable performance, with accuracy degrading gracefully and convergence rounds remaining man- ageable. This resilience highlights the framework’s suitability for real-world federated software development scenarios. D. Performance Comparison To evaluate the effectiveness of F-CodeLLM, we conduct performance comparison experiments against FedPrompt, Fed- PETuning, and SLoRA baselines. The three baselines are im- plemented following their original methodologies and publicly available code repositories. For a fair comparison, all methods are evaluated on the same three downstream tasks considered in our study, including code generation, code summarization, and Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. CHEN et al.: FEDERATED ADAPTIVE LARGE LANGUAGE MODEL FINE-TUNING FRAMEWORK FOR SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT 41 TABLE IV RESULTS OF PERFORMANCE COMPARISON code classiﬁcation. In each case, we use identical data splits and preprocessingpipelines.Thecomparisonresultsaresummarized in Table IV. The experimental results indicate that, while all baselines beneﬁt from parameter-efﬁcient tuning in federated settings, F-CodeLLM consistently outperforms them across all evalu- ated code-related tasks. The improvements are particularly pro- nounced in heterogeneous, multi-language federated environ- ments, where F-CodeLLM’s ability to learn shared, language- agnostic representations enhances generalization. As a frame- work purpose-built for code-centric tasks, F-CodeLLM’s per- formance gains translate directly into improved developer pro- ductivity. In code generation tasks, F-CodeLLM achieves a BLEU score of 38.9%, outperforming SLoRA by 3.1%, Fed- PETuning by 4.4%, and FedPrompt by 6.8%. This indicates that F-CodeLLM generates code that is more syntactically correct and semantically coherent, thereby reducing debugging time and accelerating development. According to industry benchmarks, a 3% improvement in BLEU for Python code generation can reduce the ”ﬁx time” for generated code by approximately 20%. In addition, F-CodeLLM incurs a communication cost of only 45 MB per round, which is substantially lower than the baselines (SLoRA: 60 MB, FedPETuning: 95 MB, FedPrompt: 120 MB). This efﬁciency is critical in real-world federated settings, where participants often operate under limited bandwidth constraints, such as small enterprises or remote teams. The reduced overhead makes F-CodeLLM practical in these environments, whereas baselines like FedPrompt, with 120 MB per round, would be pro- hibitive. Lower per-round data transfer also decreases training latency, enabling faster iteration in agile software development workﬂows. Unlike SLoRA, which applies general LoRA-based feder- ated tuning without code-speciﬁc optimizations, or FedPrompt and FedPETuning, which lack explicit cross-lingual support, F-CodeLLM learns language-agnostic code representations. For example, in a federation with mixed Python and Java codebases, F-CodeLLM can leverage shared programming concepts—such as control ﬂow and data structures—to improve performance across both languages, a capability that the baselines do not possess. F-CodeLLM is explicitly designed for multilingual, hetero- geneous federations involving diverse programming languages (e.g., Python, Java, C++) and hardware, representing a key advantage over the baselines. Unlike SLoRA, which applies general LoRA-based federation tuning without code-speciﬁc optimizations, or FedPrompt and FedPETuning, which lack TABLE V COMPARISON RESULTS OF LORA RANK ANALYSIS explicit cross-lingual support, F-CodeLLM learns language- agnostic code representations. For instance, in a federation with mixed Python and Java codebases, F-CodeLLM can leverage shared programming concepts, such as control ﬂow and data structures. This enables the model to improve performance across both languages. Such cross-lingual knowledge transfer is a capability that the baselines lack. E. LoRA Rank Analysis To better understand the impact of LoRA conﬁguration on F-CodeLLM’s performance and efﬁciency, we conduct a sys- tematic study by varying the LoRA rank r. This parameter di- rectly controls the number of trainable parameters and the com- munication cost per client in the federated setting. We evaluate F-CodeLLM with LoRA ranks r ∈{2, 4, 8, 16, 32} across three downstream tasks, including Code Generation (HumanEval-C), Code Summarization (CodeSearchNet), and Code Classiﬁcation (CodeNet). Each experiment employs the same pre-trained base model (e.g., CodeT5+) and federated setup, with 5 clients partic- ipating in 10 communication rounds. All other hyperparameters are held constant to isolate the effect of r. During each round, every client performs one local epoch using LoRA-injected adapters. The comparison results are summarized in Table V. As shown in Table V, increasing the LoRA rank leads to consistent performance improvements across all three tasks. However, the marginal gain diminish beyond r=16, indicat- ing that larger ranks provide reduced returns. Notably, even with only 0.16% of trainable parameters (r = 8), F-CodeLLM achieves over 95% of its peak performance, demonstrating its strong parameter efﬁciency. Based on this analysis, we adopt r = 8 as the default conﬁguration for F-CodeLLM in all sub- sequent experiments, striking a balance between model quality, communication cost, and computational overhead. F. Evaluation Under Non-IID Settings To evaluate the robustness and generalizability of F- CodeLLM in realistic scenarios, we conduct experiments under controlled non-IID data distributions across three downstream tasks. We compare F-CodeLLM against FedAvg, FedPETuning, and SLoRA baselines under the same non-IID splits. We sim- ulate heterogeneous client environments by partitioning each dataset in a label-skewed non-IID manner. In code classiﬁcation (CodeNet) tasks, clients are assigned disjoint subsets of pro- gramming languages (e.g., one client received only Java, another only C++). In code summarization (CodeSearchNet) tasks, each Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 42 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON SERVICES COMPUTING, VOL. 19, NO. 1, JANUARY/FEBRUARY 2026 TABLE VI EVALUATION RESULTS UNDER NON-IID SETTINGS client is allocated samples from a limited set of repositories or authors to simulate different coding styles and documentation norms. In code generation (HumanEval-C) tasks, prompts are split by task category (e.g., string manipulation versus math logic), ensuring that no client access to all types during local training. All models are trained for 10 communication rounds with 5 clients, using LoRA rank r = 8. The evaluation results under Non-IID settings are presented in Table VI. As shown in Table VI, F-CodeLLM consistently outperforms all baselines across tasks under non-IID conditions. This demon- strates its superior ability to generalize across heterogeneous client data, particularly in the code domain, where language se- mantics and programming styles vary signiﬁcantly. Like SLoRA and FedPETuning, F-CodeLLM beneﬁts from a low communi- cation footprint due to LoRA-based adapter exchange. However, its weighted aggregation strategy and code-aware ﬁne-tuning yield greater utility under heterogeneity conditions. In addi- tion, adapter-only training in F-CodeLLM effectively isolates domain-speciﬁc shifts while enabling the sharing of high-level code structures. This feature is particularly advantageous for tasks such as code summarization, where stylistic variations across clients are prominent. VI. CONCLUSION This paper presented F-CodeLLM, a federated adaptive large language model ﬁne-tuning framework designed for practical software development. Our F-CodeLLM framework encom- passes two key components. First, we introduced a joint ﬁne- tuning framework for LLMs based on federated learning. By leveraging the FedAvg algorithm, we enable distributed joint ﬁne-tuning of LLMs while safeguarding the privacy of each organization’s code. Second, to tackle the substantial commu- nication and computational overhead associated with applying federated learning to LLMs, we devised an efﬁcient federated ﬁne-tuning method based on LoRA, which signiﬁcantly re- duces both communication and computational requirements. To validate the effectiveness of F-CodeLLM, we conducted experiments on three code-related downstream tasks. The results demonstrate that F-CodeLLM is particularly well-suited for sce- narios where code data is sensitive or limited. LLMs ﬁne-tuned using F-CodeLLM achieve signiﬁcant performance gains com- pared to models ﬁne-tuned individually by each organization. In addition, F-CodeLLM accommodates the heterogeneity of code data across organizations, enabling effective ﬁne-tuning for multi-language tasks while also enhancing performance on single-language tasks.

file_name: A Hybrid Strategy for Chat Transcript Summarization.pdf
text: Received 2 August 2024, accepted 24 September 2024, date of publication 4 October 2024, date of current version 16 October 2024. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2024.3473968 A Hybrid Strategy for Chat Transcript Summarization PRATIK K. BISWAS Artificial Intelligence and Data Center, Verizon Communications, Basking Ridge, NJ 07920, USA e-mail: pratik.biswas@verizonwireless.com ABSTRACT Text summarization is the process of condensing a piece of text to fewer sentences, while still preserving its content. Chat transcript, in this context, is a textual copy of a digital or online conversation between a customer (caller) and agent(s). This paper presents an indigenously (locally) developed hybrid method that first combines extractive (unsupervised) and abstractive (supervised) summarization techniques in compressing ill-punctuated or unpunctuated chat transcripts to produce more readable punctuated sum- maries and then optimizes the overall quality of summarization through reinforcement learning. Extensive testing, evaluations, comparisons, and validation have demonstrated the efficacy of this approach for large-scale deployment of chat transcript summarization, in the absence of manually generated reference (annotated) summaries. INDEX TERMS Extractive summarization, abstractive summarization, topic modeling, transformers, transfer learning, reinforcement learning, contextual multi-armed bandits, bandit testing. I. INTRODUCTION Automatic document summarization aims to compress a tex- tual document to a shorter, more informative format while keeping key information of the original text. Hence, auto- matic text summarization has become a very desirable tool in today’s information age. Numerous approaches have been developed for automatic text summarization that can be broadly classified into two groups: extractive summarization and abstractive summarization. Extractive summarization extracts important sentences from the original text and repro- duces them verbatim in the summary, while abstractive summarization generates new sentences. Hybrid Summariza- tion attempts to combine these two approaches in some form. Chat transcript is a record of the digital or online conver- sation between a caller (customer) and receiver(s) (customer representatives/agents). In this paper, however, we shall be confining ourselves to online chats of a phone company. These are not exchanges of tweets (postings) or text messages of restricted lengths, but unrestricted textual dialogs about problems and resolutions. Automatic summarization of chat transcripts (as with call transcripts), in our domain, pose certain unique challenges, as follows: 1) they are not con- The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Olarik Surinta . tinuous texts but include conversations between customers and agents, 2) they are either very short or very long, and can include a large number of sentences that are irrelevant and even meaningless, 3) they include several ill-formed, grammatically incorrect sentences, 4) they are either unpunc- tuated or are improperly punctuated, 5) there is a dearth of a large collection of human-crafted reference (annotated) summaries, and 6) existing open-source summarization tools don’t perform too well with chat transcripts. Hence the need for a new domain-specific chat summarizer that can meet the needs of a phone company. How do we achieve large-scale (industrial grade) summa- rization of domain specific chat transcripts (dialogs) in the absence of annotated (reference) summaries? How do we find the most effective LM/LLM-based, fine-tuned abstractive summarizer for this purpose? In this paper, we address these two questions by introducing a novel, hybrid summarization technique that integrates channel separation (separation into customer and agent transcripts), topic modeling, sentence selection, and punctuation restoration based extractive sum- marization and transfer learning based supervised abstractive summarization with contextual multi-armed bandits based reinforcement learning to generate, validate and optimize properly punctuated, fixed-length and readable customer and agent summaries, from the original chat transcripts, that 146620 2024 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ VOLUME 12, 2024 P. K. Biswas: Hybrid Strategy for Chat Transcript Summarization can adequately summarize customer concerns and agent resolutions. II. RELATED WORK Related research can be broadly grouped into three cate- gories: 1) extractive summarization, 2) abstractive summa- rization and 3) hybrid summarization. Radev et al. [1] defined summary as ‘‘a text that is produced from one or more texts, that conveys important information in the original text(s), and that is no longer than half of the original text(s) and usually, significantly less than that.’’ Automatic text summarization gained attraction as early as the 1950s. Different methods and extensive surveys of auto- matic text summarization have been provided in [2], [3], [4], [5], [6], and [7]. Luhn [8] introduced a method to extract salient sentences from the text using features such as word and phrase fre- quency. He proposed to weight the sentences of a document as a function of high frequency words, ignoring very high frequency common words. Gong and Liu [9] and Wang et al. [10] described how multiple documents could be summa- rized using topic models. Xu and Durrett [11] constructed a neural model for single-document summarization based jointly on extraction and syntactic compression. Miller [12] used Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transform- ers (BERT) for summarization of lecture notes. Liu [13] described BERTSUM, a simple variant of BERT, for extrac- tive summarization. Liu and Lapata [14] showcased how BERT could be generally applied in text summarization and proposed a general framework for both extractive and abstractive models. Tuggener et al. [15] provided an extensive overview of existing dialog summarization data sets and map- pings from data sets to linguistic models. Feigenblat et al. [16] introduced TWEETSUM, a large-scale database of customer support dialogs with extractive and abstractive summaries along with an unsupervised extractive summarization method specific to these dialogs. Lin and Ng [17] surveyed the state-of-the-art while Khan and Salim [18] reviewed the various methods for abstractive summarization. Nallapati et al. [19], Paulus et al. [20], See et al. [21] and Liu et al. [22] employed recurrent neural net- works, deep reinforcement learning, pointer-generator, and generative adversarial networks for abstractive summariza- tion. Rafael et al. [23] showed the effectiveness of transfer learning in abstractive text summarization through a uni- fied framework named as Text-to-Text-Transfer-Transformer (T5). Lewis et al. [24] developed BART, a denoising autoen- coder for pre-training sequence-to-sequence models, that is particularly effective when fine-tuned for text generation, e.g., abstractive summarization, translation, etc. Beltazi et al., [25] presented Longformer, useful for long document summarization. Zhang et al. [26] proposed PEGASUS, where important sentences were removed from an input document during pre-training and the model was tasked at recovering them. Gliwa et al. [27] introduced the SAMSum Corpus, a new dataset with abstractive dialog summaries and showed that model-generated summaries of dialogs performed better than the model-generated summaries of news; while Khalman et al. [28] collected ForumSum, a diverse and high-quality conversation summarization dataset to show that models trained on ForumSum transferred better to new domains com- pared to SAMSum. Fabbri et al. [29] crowdsourced four new datasets from news comments, discussion forums, commu- nity question answering forums, email threads and bench- marked state-of-the-art abstractive summarization models on their datasets. Zhong et al. [30] unveiled DialogLM, a denoising, pre-trained, neural encoder-decoder model for long dialog understanding and abstractive summarization. Chen and Bansal [31] followed a hybrid ‘‘extract-then- rewrite’’ architecture, with policy-based reinforcement learn- ing (RL) to fuse the extractor agent and the abstractor network. Bae et al. [32] rewrote sentences from a document and then paraphrased the selected ones to generate a sum- mary. Su et al. [33] combined the two summarization methods and a text segmentation model to generate variable-length, fluent summaries. Extractive summarization techniques, referred to above, did not include punctuation restoration. Also, the term and sentence selections in [8] were not based on similarity analysis using pre-trained embedding models. Abstractive summarization techniques, cited above, did not address the questions on what to do when there aren’t enough ref- erence summaries for model fine-tuning and how to find the most effective abstractive summarizer for a given task. Likewise, the hybrid techniques mentioned in this section didn’t consider using multi-armed-bandits-based reinforce- ment learning, for summarization optimization. Our approach attempts to address these gaps. The proposed hybrid strategy is a significant extension of our earlier work on extractive summarization [43]. It consists of three phases. The first phase is an adaptation, from the published extractive strategy, for domain-specific chat tran- script summarization. The second phase is an extension and elaboration of our work associated with a short, yet unpub- lished conference submission [46], while the third phase is a completely new addition to this strategy. III. MAJOR CONTRIBUTIONS Our main contributions and advantages can be summarized as follows: • We combine topic modeling, embedding-based sentence selection and transformer-based punctuation restoration for unsupervised extractive summarization. • We fine-tune powerful, transformer-based language models, on indigenously (locally) extracted summaries, for abstractive summarization of chat transcripts through transfer learning and identify the fine-tuned abstractive summarizer that is most effective for our transcripts. • We apply contextual multi-armed-bandits-based rein- forcement learning to validate the results of our sum- marization and optimize the overall quality of the chat summaries. Our contributions make our proposed approach unique and different from others and help address the two questions (introduced in Section I) which warranted this strategy. IV. PRELIMINARIES—CONCEPTS AND TECHNOLOGIES In this section, we clarify key concepts, terminologies and explain certain technologies, which provide the foundations for our work. A. AUTOMATIC SUMMARIZATION The automatic summarization of text is a well-defined task in the field of Natural Language processing (NLP). Automatic text summarization attempts to convert a larger document into a shorter form preserving its information content and overall meaning. A good summary should reflect the diverse topics of the document while keeping redundancy to a minimum. Next, we look at the two different approaches to automatic text summarization, namely extractive and abstractive. Extractive summarization methods condense long docu- ments into shorter versions by directly extracting sentences from the relevant sections in the original text. In contrast, abstractive summarization methods attempt to convey the main ideas from the original text through new sentences, and not by copying verbatim the most important sentences from the same text. In other words, they interpret, examine, and analyze the original text using advanced natural language techniques to get a better understanding of the content and then describe it through shorter and more focused text, com- prising of new sentences. Purely extractive summaries often give better results than automatic abstractive summaries, because abstractive summarization methods deal with seman- tic representation, inference and natural language generation, which are relatively harder than sentence extraction. Many abstractive summarization techniques, particularly the ones using deep learning, often depend upon the extracted sum- maries as the labeled data for building the training samples on which to train to generate new text. B. TOPIC MODELING Topic modeling is an unsupervised machine learning method for finding a group of words (i.e., topic), from a collection of documents, that best represents the information in the collec- tion. Many techniques have been used to obtain probabilistic topic models. Latent dirichlet allocation (LDA) is a popular topic modeling technique that represents documents as a random mixture of latent topics, where each topic is a proba- bility distribution of words [34]. The hierarchical dirichlet process (HDP) is another topic modeling technique that extends LDA. It is a nonparametric Bayesian approach that uses a mixed-membership model for unsupervised analysis of grouped data. Unlike LDA (its finite counterpart), HDP infers the number of topics from the input data. Latent semantic analysis (LSA), also known as latent semantic indexing (LSI), is an unsupervised method for extracting a representation of text semantics from observed words [35]. It attempts to bring out latent relationships among a collection of documents on to a lower-dimensional space. LSA is based on the principle that words closer in meaning occur in similar pieces of text (the distributional hypothesis). It has been used for multi- document summarization. C. TRANSFORMERS Transformers in NLP provide general-purpose, novel, neural network architectures for Natural Language Understanding (NLU) and Natural Language Generation (NLG) with over 32+ pre-trained language models. They were first introduced in [36]. Transformers are Seq2Seq deep learning models that transform sequential inputs to sequential outputs, while handling long-range dependencies with ease. They are based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing entirely with recurrence and convolutions of the earlier deep learning architectures. Transformers do not require that the sequential data be processed in order, which allows for much more parallelization than Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and therefore reduced training times [36]. Since their introduc- tion, transformers have become the model of choice for tackling many problems in NLP, replacing older recurrent neural network models such as the Long Short-term Mem- ory (LSTM). Transformer models can train on much larger datasets than before, as they can support more paralleliza- tion during training. This has resulted in the development of pre-trained systems such as Bidirectional Encoder Represen- tations from Transformers (BERT). BERT is a bidirectional transformer, pre-trained using a combination of masked language modeling objective and next sentence prediction, on a large corpus comprising the Toronto Book Corpus and Wikipedia, by jointly conditioning on both left and right con- texts in all layers. Consequently, a pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to cre- ate state-of-the-art models for a wide range of NLP tasks [37]. GPT-2 [38] and XLNet [39] are two other pre-trained NLP models. GPT-2 was trained on a 40 GB dataset called Web- Text and has approximately 1 billion parameters. It was trained to predict the next word. GPT-2 showed promis- ing results in commonsense reasoning, question answering, reading comprehension, summarization, translation, creating arguments and data augmentation without being instructed anything beyond how to predict the next word in a sequence. XLNet is an improved version of BERT that implements per- mutation language modeling in its architecture and randomly predicts the next tokens. Transformers often employ a 12-layered encoder-decoder architecture consisting of one stack of 6 encoding and another stack of 6 decoding layers. The encoders are all identi- cal in structure, each composed of two sub-layers, namely self-attention and feed-forward network. Each word in the input sentence flows through its own path in the encoder. However, there are dependencies between these paths in the self-attention layer, which help the encoder focus on other words in the input sentence as it encodes a specific word. The 146622 VOLUME 12, 2024 P. K. Biswas: Hybrid Strategy for Chat Transcript Summarization objective is to preserve the contributions of important words and drown-out those from the irrelevant ones, while encod- ing any given word. The feed-forward layer doesn’t have these dependencies and so the various paths (for encoded vectors) can exist in parallel while flowing through this layer into the encoder’s output. The decoder additionally has an attention layer, in between the two, that helps it to focus on relevant parts of the input sentence (like what attention does in Seq2Seq models). When we pass a sentence into a trans- former, it is embedded and passed into a stack of encoders, which processes the input iteratively through its layers. The output from the encoder is then similarly processed through each decoder layer in the decoder stack, eventually generating the output. D. EMBEDDINGS Embeddings are mathematical functions that map ‘‘entities’’ to a latent space with complex and meaningful dimen- sions. Words or sentences or paragraphs can be mapped into a shared latent space such that the meaning of the word/sentence/paragraph can be represented geometrically. Machine learning approaches towards NLP require words to be expressed in vector form. Word embeddings is a feature engineering technique in which words are mapped into a vector of real numbers in a pre-defined vector space. It is a learned representation for text, where words with the same meaning have similar representations. The idea of using a densely distributed representation for each word is a key to this approach. Word2Vec, GloVe, etc. provide pre-trained word embedding models in a type of transfer learning. Embedding techniques initially focused on words but later shifted attention to other types of textual content, such as n-grams, sentences, and documents. The Universal Sentence Encoder (USE) encodes text into high dimensional vectors used for text classification, semantic similarity, clustering, and other natural language tasks [40]. The model is trained and optimized for sentences, phrases, or short paragraphs, from a variety of data sources, to accommodate a wide range of natural language understanding tasks. The model maps variable length input English text into an output of a 512- dimensional vector. E. CONTEXTUAL MULTI-ARMED BANDITS Multi-Arm bandit (MAB) [41] is a classic reinforcement learning problem, in which a player faces a slot machine or a bandit with k arms, each with an unknown payout. Pulling any one of the arms fetches the player either a reward or a penalty and he/she can pull only a limited number of times. The player’s objective is to pull the arms one-by-one in sequence to maximize the total reward collected over time. At the beginning of the experiment, when odds and payouts are unknown, the player must determine which arm to pull, in which order and how many times. The solution involves exploration/exploitation tradeoff: the player should balance between trying different arms to learn more about each arm’s expected payout and taking advantage of the best arm that he/she knows. The contextual multi-armed bandit (CMAB) algorithm [42] is an extension of the multi-armed bandit approach where we factor in the context, when choosing a bandit’s arm. The context affects how a reward is associated with each arm, so as contexts change, the model learns to adapt its bandit choice, that is, the selection of its arm. These algorithms can adapt and learn from the environment automatically, without human intervention. Bandit testing applying MAB/CMAB can be thought of as an alternative to A/B testing. Unlike A/B testing where traffic is evenly split between two variations, MAB/CMAB allows the user to dynamically allocate traffic to better performing variations while allocating less and less traffic to the under- performing ones. V. HYBRID SYMMARIZATION OF CHAT TRANSCRIPTS We propose a hybrid strategy that extracts, fine-tunes, abstracts, validates, and optimizes. The main objective of this strategy is to provide a hybrid summarization framework that accomplishes the following: i) extracts good quality sum- maries of chat transcripts to create a large enough training sample; ii) uses this sample to fine-tune pre-trained, trans- former model based abstractive summarizers to generate new summaries of unseen transcripts on the fly through transfer learning, where the resultant summaries are at least as good as the extractive summaries (on which the abstractive summa- rizers have been fine-tuned), with the tacit expectation that the pre-training encoded in the abstractive approach would make the summaries more fluent, coherent and even help reduce some grammatical errors found in the original tran- scripts; iii) supports reinforcement learning, allowing us to summarize with multiple fine-tuned abstractive summarizers and helps not only in finding the best performing summarizer for a large number of transcripts but also the right variant for a given transcript, improving the overall quality of sum- marization in the process. So, the strategy involves three sequential phases. Phase I uses an extractive summarizer, Phase II uses abstractive ones, and Phase III optimizes the overall quality of the summaries. The strategy is driven by the fact that it will be used in a production environment, which requires the summarization of a very large number of chat transcripts. but where there is a paucity of manually generated reference (annotated) summaries from which the abstractive summarizers can learn in Phase II and with which we can compare our results.
