file_name: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts A Comparative Study of GPT With Human Experts and Machine Learning.pdf
text: Received 25 July 2025, accepted 8 August 2025, date of publication 18 August 2025, date of current version 26 August 2025. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3600146 AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts: A Comparative Study of GPT With Human Experts and Machine Learning CHRISTOPHER J. LYNCH 1, ERIK JENSEN1,2, ROSS GORE 3, VIRGINIA ZAMPONI4, KEVIN O’BRIEN4, BRANDON FELDHAUS4, KATHERINE SMITH5, JOSEPH MARTÍNEZ 1, MADISON H. MUNRO6, (Graduate Student Member, IEEE), TIMUR E. OZKOSE7, TUGCE B. GUNDOGDU8, ANN MARIE REINHOLD 6,9, HAMDI KAVAK 7, AND BARRY EZELL 1 1Virginia Modeling, Analysis, and Simulation Center, Old Dominion University, Suffolk, VA 23435, USA 2Modeling and Simulation Engineering Department, Old Dominion University, Norfolk, VA 23508, USA 3Center for Secure and Intelligent Critical Systems, Old Dominion University, Suffolk, VA 23435, USA 4Office of Enterprise Research and Innovation, Old Dominion University, Suffolk, VA 23435, USA 5Virginia Digital Maritime Center, Old Dominion University, Suffolk, VA 23435, USA 6Software Engineering and Cybersecurity Laboratory, Gianforte School of Computing, Montana State University, Bozeman, MT 59717, USA 7Computational and Data Sciences Department, George Mason University, Fairfax, VA 22030, USA 8Computer Science Department, George Mason University, Fairfax, VA 22030, USA 9Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, Richland, WA 99354, USA Corresponding author: Christopher J. Lynch (cjlynch@odu.edu) This work was supported in part by the Office of Enterprise Research and Innovation at Old Dominion University under Grant 300916-010, in part by the Department of Education Modeling and Simulation Program awarded to Old Dominion University’s Virginia Modeling Analysis and Simulation Center under Grant P116S210003, in part by the Commonwealth Cyber Initiative (CCI) Fellowship under Grant H-2Q24-016, in part by the Office of Naval Research under Grant N00014-19-1-2624, and in part by the Air Force Office of Scientific Research through the Minerva Research Initiative under Grant 22RT0286. ABSTRACT Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate social media posts, emails, narratives, and other forms of communication, yet few studies systematically assess how well these outputs align with their original prompt intent. This study evaluates the effectiveness of a zero-shot structured narrative prompt for generating 24 000 life event messages (birth, death, hiring, and firing events) using OpenAI’s GPT-4. A manually tagged sample of 2880 messages shows that 87.43% align with the intended life event when framed as X (formerly Twitter) posts. To scale this evaluation, we train nine machine learning (ML) models, including BERT, Keras-based architectures, eXtreme Gradient Boosting, Random Forest, and Support Vector Machines, per life event type, resulting in 36 binary classifiers. We apply an ensemble approach based on simple majority agreement to classify the remaining 21 120 messages. Manual validation of a 1% sample (n = 212) confirms a 90.57% match rate with human reviewers, with binomial tests confirming statistical significance above a 75% threshold across all event types (P-values ranging from 0.00025 to 0.0115). While valid messages are reliably identified, classifying invalid cases remains more challenging. This work offers a reproducible framework for validating AI-generated messaging and provides practical guidance for prompt-based applications. Limitations include the narrow event scope, exclusive use of English text, and reliance on structured prompts, which may not generalize to open-ended tasks. INDEX TERMS AI-generated messages, ChatGPT, generative AI, generative pretrained transformers (GPT), large language models (LLMs), machine learning, natural language processing (NLP), prompt engineering, structured narrative prompt. The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Wei Wang . I. INTRODUCTION Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for generating text, crafting narratives, storytelling, 147002 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ VOLUME 13, 2025 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts and other forms of communication [1], [2]. These models have seen rapid advancement and widespread adoption across domains such as education, healthcare, industry, and human-computer interaction [3]. LLMs are capable of generating coherent and contextually relevant text across a variety of domains and scenarios; however, quality, tone, and contextual alignment of AI-generated narratives are highly dependent on the prompt provided to the language model. Prompt engineering plays a pivotal role in shaping LLM outputs, particularly for tasks involving narrative generation, by guiding the model to produce responses that align with the intended communicative goals. Despite advances in fluency and style, challenges persist regarding the accountability, safety, reliability, and ethical use of LLMs for narrative generation [4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10]. Prompt engineering, and particularly the use of struc- tured prompts, provides a promising strategy for mitigating these challenges by enforcing clarity, specificity, and role- awareness in prompt formulation [11], [12]. By explicitly bounding LLM outputs to defined narrative parameters, effective prompt engineering not only improves relevance and coherence but also establishes a transparent mechanism for validating whether generated messages meet with the intent embedded in the original prompts. As AI-generated messaging becomes increasingly inte- grated into digital communication–particularly for publicly sharing personal life events—an essential challenge emerges: can we reliably determine whether an AI-generated output accurately reflects the intended meaning and context encoded in the prompt? Life events are highly personal and emo- tionally meaningful experiences, spanning domains such as relationships, health, employment, and identity [13]. These events are frequently shared on social media, where their visibility and audience engagement vary [14]. As LLMs are increasingly tasked with generating such content, validating alignment between prompt intent and generated message becomes a critical prerequisite for trust and interpretability, particularly as such messages may influence attitudes or decisions on emotionally sensitive or socially consequential topics [15]. This process can enhance LLM utility by enabling self-evaluation and quality control within the generation pipeline. Before downstream analyses such as sentiment detection, narrative coherence evaluation, or contextual appropriateness assessment can be meaningfully applied, it is essential to first assess whether the AI-generated response is a valid reflection of the original intent. This validation step enables filtering of invalid outputs, reducing noise in subsequent analysis. Without this foundational check, downstream assessments risk mischaracterizing irrelevant or misaligned content. This study addresses this gap by employing structured narrative prompts (SNPs) to guide the generation of LLM- based life event messages and introduces a comparative evaluation pipeline combining human expert validation and machine learning (ML) classifiers. Structured prompts allow transparent control over input parameters, such as tone, narrative perspective, and character roles, while also providing a reproducible mechanism for assessing alignment between prompt and output [11], [16], [17]. In a broader sense, narrative in science and health com- munication is effective and appealing for audiences across fields, topics, and mediums and helps to create openness to information [18]. Characters matter in evoking positive and negative audience experiences [19], [20]. Narrative generation utilizing LLM ChatBots can effectively merge character roles and science communications to produce more engaging language and foster emotional and social connec- tions with the reader [21], [22]. Integrating computational methods into message construction can further enhance the efficacy and efficiency of these narratives, addressing critical gaps in domains such as risk and crisis communication research [23]. This highlights the dual role of prompts in narrative generation and facilitating explainability for interdisciplinary applications. In this study, we assess validity through a compara- tive evaluation involving both human expert classification and machine learning ensemble models, enabling scalable identification of invalid narratives. These validated outputs form a necessary foundation for subsequent semantic, emotional, and contextual analyses and contribute toward a more trustworthy and interpretable AI messaging pipeline. We highlight the importance of prompt engineering and zero- shot learning in the context of AI-generated messaging with LLMs by exploring the role of ML models for automatically classifying messages generated from an LLM ChatBot using a structured prompt and zero-shot learning. LLMs can be distracted by the inclusion of irrelevant context [24]. By carefully crafting prompts, we can steer LLMs towards producing engaging, coherent, and contextually relevant messaging while yielding consistent and well-structured outcomes [11], [25]. By leveraging auxiliary information provided in the prompts, zero-shot learning empowers LLMs to generate narratives for events or scenarios unseen during training. This includes tasks requiring nuanced cognition and empathetic alignment [26] and expands applicability to diverse storytelling tasks [27], [28]. In this manner, prompts serve to operationalize the translation of technical outcomes and stakeholder comprehension while serving as a bridge for transparency and traceability. As emphasized by [29], the use of LLMs to generate textual explanations of simulation out- puts enables communication of complex results in accessible formats, enhancing stakeholder understanding and promoting confidence in simulation-derived insights. Current LLM-oriented research includes (1) sentiment evaluation of ChatGPT 3.5-generated narrative messages compared to tweets which identified statistically indis- cernible differences in sentiment levels in 4 of 44 evaluated sentiment traits [11], (2) that story weaving produced by an LLM only resulted in fewer logical flaws and was easier to understand than stories produced by an LLM in VOLUME 13, 2025 147003 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts conjunction with humans [30], (3) LLM-generated messages for health awareness were statistically indistinguishable from human tweets with respect to sentiment, clarity, and semantic structure [31], and (4) personalized storytelling using ChatGPT to address perinatal mental health challenges, demonstrating its ability to craft empathetic and culturally sensitive narratives that align with user prompts and raise awareness about sensitive issues [32]. Additionally, ChatGPT has been utilized to develop theme-relevant narratives for character driven simulation worlds [33] and to sift story-worthy events from a collec- tion of facts [34]. While LLMs increasingly demonstrate competitive performance for narrative generation, annotation quality, and complex tasks requiring nuanced understanding and context, but human expertise still produces superior results [35], [36]. In addition to guiding narrative generation, structured prompts can enhance LLMs’ capacity for emotion cognition, enabling them to align outputs more closely with human values and emotional contexts [11], [26]. By incorporating principles from cognitive psychology and leveraging advances in emotion-rich response generation, structured narrative prompts (SNPs) can serve as tools for both improving narrative quality and fostering stakeholder engagement through emotionally resonant and contextually relevant outputs [11]. Additionally, SNPs provide explicit pathways for capturing subject-narrator relationships as well as defining the characteristics of the messages’ subjects and narrators. Advancing this research area, we conduct a study to assess the validity of life event messages generated by GPT- 4 created through the use of SNPs via the methodology displayed in Fig. 1. We generate 24,000 life event messages using an existing SNP [11] across birth, death, hiring, and firing events using a publicly available repository [37]. For our study, we assume that the messages can be prompted based on a subject-narrator relationship, which we define in the prompt on a message-by-message basis, with contextual relevance, structure, tone, and urgency driven by a media platform-relationship, which we define as tweet-oriented for all messages. We then sample 2880 of the messages and manually classify whether each meets the intention of its structured prompt. Each message is independently assessed by two human reviewers and then independently assessed by a third human reviewer whenever a tie-breaker is needed. This tagged dataset is utilized to train and test a series of machine learning (ML) models. For interpretability and transparency, we assess the validity of the ML models’ precision on the classified data [38], [39]. Next, we apply the trained ML models to predict the classifications of the remaining 21,120 data points and assess the agreement between the models’ predictions. This research advances the study of LLM capabilities, limitations, and validity for creating natural language narratives using prompts and zero-shot learning while offering practical insights for message and narrative generation. For baseline validity of the match between the generated live event messages and the intention of their respective structured prompts, Table 1 provides the results of the manual classification of the ChatGPT-generated messages. In total, the messages were found to sufficiently meets the specifications of the SNP in 87.43% of cases. There exists a gap in the ability of ChatGPT to meet the intention of the prompt based on the type of life event, with only 72.08% classified as Valid for birth events but 96.67% Valid for hired events. However, this conveys strong evidence in support of using prompts to generate messages from an LLM using zero- shot learning. TABLE 1. Human-tagged classification results of the sample of the 2880 AI-generated Birth, Death, Hired, and Fired life event messages. A. KEY TAKEAWAYS • By leveraging prompt engineering and auxiliary infor- mation provided in prompts, LLMs can effectively generate contextually relevant messaging across a diverse range of topics. – ChatGPT GPT-4 averaged 87.43% (n = 2880) valid messages based on the SNP instructions and manual tagging. – Ensemble model classifier averaged 90.57% (n = 212) valid messages based on comparisons to manual tagging. • Precision results of the trained ML models indicates usefulness of various ML techniques and highlights timing concerns with message prediction pertaining to scalability of data for some techniques. – The precision and majority agreement rates observed indicate a high potential for integrating ensemble model validation into real-time LLM evaluation pipelines. • Agreement between ML models trained on generated message data can be utilized to automatically classify future messages. – An ensemble of trained ML models can be utilized recursively alongside the LLMs to assess and refine future message generation. – Erroneous messages can be automatically filtered. – The trained ML models displayed strong majority agreement with the ensemble classification of untagged messages with an average of 7.62 out of 9 models (sd 1.90, n = 212) agreeing with the ensemble prediction. 147004 VOLUME 13, 2025 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 1. Research methodology. GPT-4 is prompted using an existing structured narrative prompt (SNP) to produce 24,000 life event messages across four life event types. These events undergo manual tagging to determine if each life event message meets the intention of its prompt based on human expert evaluation. The tagged messages are used to train and validate nine ML models. The validated ML models are then utilized to predict the classifications on the remaining 21,120 narratives using an ensemble agreement approach. Finally, manual tagging of a sample of ensemble classified life event messages is conducted to provide a validation check on the outcomes. II. RELATED WORK Zero-shot learning has gained significant attention in the field of ML, particularly for its applications in natural language processing and text generation tasks. Models are trained to generalize to classes that are unseen during training, instead, leveraging auxiliary information that is provided at inference time. Thus, enabling language models to adapt to novel scenarios or domains without requiring explicit exam- ples for every task. In the context of LLMs, zero-shot learning techniques have been employed to extend the capabilities of text generation models by allowing them to generate narratives for events or scenarios not encountered during training [11], [31], [40] and for generating sequences of actionable tasks [41]. Instruction tuning has also been shown to be successful at improving zero-shot performance [42] as well as using unlabeled data to co-train a prompted model has also been shown to provide performance improvements under the right conditions [43]. Additionally, zero-shot learning has been explored to expand into the use of chain-of-thought prompting [27]. Prompt engineering has emerged as a methodology for guiding LLMs in generating coherent and contextually relevant text using explicit prompt structures that provide elements such as instructions, context, input data, and output indicators [44]. Prompts serve as templates to influence the outputs from pre-trained language models using textual strings that allow the language models to solve numerous tasks [2], [45], [46]. This allows LLMs to generate text that aligns with specific themes, styles, and objectives. Prompt engineering techniques range from simple prompts providing high-level guidance to complex prompts that incorporate constraints. The Goal Prompt Evaluation Iteration (GPEI) method- ology incorporates data inclusion and principles from explainable AI to promote transparency and justifiability in LLM responses [47]. Increasing prompt specificity can lead to intensified neutrality in ChatGPT responses [48]. Recommendations for improving prompt usefulness have also included asking ChatGPT to enter the process and make recommendations for its own prompts [40]. Narrative generation tasks utilize prompt engineering as a pivotal component in shaping narrative’s content, structure, and coherence. Researchers have explored various approaches to prompt engineering, including the use of SNPs [11], conditional generation techniques [46], and prompt fine-tuning strategies [42]. These approaches enable LLMs to produce narratives that meet the intended criteria while exhibiting desirable properties, such as consistency, realism, and contextual relevance. Prompting Frameworks provide structured guidance, enhancing the ability of LLMs to create high-quality, meaningful messaging across diverse contexts. In the study of message and narrative generation using Large Language Models (LLMs), the construction and use of effective prompts are crucial for achieving coherent, contextually accurate, and relevant outputs. This research explores several structured prompting frameworks designed to guide LLMs, like GPT-4, in text generation that align with specific event types and goals. These frameworks, when applied to LLM message generation, enhance the model’s ability to produce high-quality, event-specific outputs [49], [50], [51]. RTF (Role, Task, Format) offers a simple but effective method of guiding LLM responses by clearly defining the role the model should assume, specifying the task it must perform, and setting the format for the expected output. For narrative generation, RTF allows for clearly delineating the role of the model as a narrative generator, specifying specific tasks like narrating life events (e.g., birth or hiring), and defining a format that ensures the output is consistent and aligned with the event type. For example, the model can be instructed to generate a first-person narrative that reflects a hiring event, ensuring narrative coherence while maintaining a structured format. TAG (Task, Action, Goal) focuses on clarity of instructions. It emphasizes specifying the task, detailing the actions required to complete the task, and outlining the overall goal. This framework is particularly useful when the model must understand not only what event to narrate but also the actions that should be included and the overall narrative outcome. By defining these elements, TAG helps improve the precision of narrative outputs, ensuring that the generated stories not only reflect the intended events but also achieve a coherent goal, such as evoking empathy or providing detailed context. BAB (Before, After, Bridge) is a storytelling-oriented framework that structures the narrative by describing a situation before an event, the situation after the event, and the bridge connecting these two states. In the context of narrative generation, BAB guides LLMs in generating narratives that clearly describe the impact of events, such as transitions from unemployment to a new job or from life to death. This approach helps yield narratives that reflect change and VOLUME 13, 2025 147005 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 2. Sample Structured Narrative Prompt (SNP) used to prompt the LLM. Fields 1–5 provide Context about the type of message, the subject, and the narrator, while Fields 6–8 specify Communication tense and voice. Fields 10–16 define Constraints, such as temporal boundaries, affective targets, and history. Field 17 provides Caveats allowing for LLM-specific instructions. progression, making them more engaging and relatable to human readers or reviewers. CARE (Context, Action, Result, Example) focuses on the integration of context, actions, results, and illustrative examples within the narrative. Providing a strong context helps the LLM frame an event accurately, while actions and results ensure the narrative is complete and impactful. For example, when narrating a firing event, the CARE framework ensures that the model captures the relevant context (e.g., company downsizing), describes the actions taken (e.g., termination meeting), and outlines the results (e.g., impact on the employee’s life), along with specific examples to enhance realism and engagement. RISE (Role, Input, Steps, Expectation) emphasizes clear role definition, relevant input, outlined steps for completing the task, and clear expectations for the final output. RISE ensures that the model understands the inputs (e.g., details of an event), follows a logical sequence of steps (e.g., narrating events in chronological order), and produces an output that meets specific expectations, such as emotional clarity or narrative coherence. This framework can lead to more consistent and accurate narratives across types of events. SNP (Structured Narrative Prompt) supports reproducible and context-aware narrative generation [11], this framework follows four key dimensions: Context, Communication, Constraints, and Caveats. This structure guides the LLM in understanding not only what to generate, but also how and why. • Context captures the type of message as well as attributes of the subject and the narrator to situate the message appropriately. • Communication represents the targeted audience, as well as the tense and voice used in the message. • Constraints shape both the form and content of the generated message, including temporal boundaries, affective targets, agent history, and output formatting directives. While these constraints narrow the generation space, they also introduce flexibility by allowing tailored messaging across diverse life event scenarios. • Caveats provide LLM-specific instructions or behav- ioral guidance to reinforce message alignment and consistency. This structured layering enables the generation of narratives that are contextually grounded, communicatively aligned, and task-constrained, while remaining flexible to LLM interpretation. While traditional prompting frameworks such as RTF, TAG, BAB, CARE, and RISE offer strong foundations for guiding LLMs in generating structured and coherent responses, the SNP framework builds on these frameworks 147006 VOLUME 13, 2025 C. SNP uniquely integrates multi-field, event-specific details, such as subject and narrator characteristics, with consistent formatting across a diverse set of life events. These enhancements enable the generation of narratives that are not only structurally consistent and semantically aligned with prompt intent, but also coherent, interpretable, and realistic. By preserving the original narrative context while offering improved control over tone and framing, SNP offers a powerful and adaptable approach for life event simulation and other socially mean- ingful messaging tasks. Its structured, event-aware design provides the best guidance for generating AI narratives that need to be sensitive, precise, and aligned with human intent, making it the preferred framework for this study. III. EXPERIMENTATION Experimentation is carried out in five sequential steps, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The process begins with the generation of message sets using a SNP across four life event types. A sample of these messages is then reviewed and tagged by human evaluators to establish a ground truth dataset. This human-tagged data is used to train and validate a suite of machine learning models, each of which is subsequently used to classify the remaining untagged messages. An ensemble tagging approach is applied using simple majority agreement among the individual ML model predictions. Finally, a random subset of ensemble-tagged messages is selected for validation through a second round of manual human evaluation. All data, source code, model outputs, and evaluation scripts used in this study are publicly available via a reproducibility repository on Mendeley Data [52]. All experiments were conducted on a Linux Manjaro system equipped with an AMD Ryzen 5 3600 6-core processor, 8 GB of DDR4 RAM, and an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 GPU. All BERT and Keras model training was executed using GPU acceleration. A. PREPARING THE DATA An existing SNP [11] was utilized to prompt OpenAI’s GPT- 4 model using OpenAI’s ChatGPT API [53]. The SNP was utilized to create 24,000 life event messages based on four life event types: birth, death, hiring, and firing. Messages were evenly distributed across the life event types, resulting in 6000 messages per class. These four life event types were selected to span a range of emotional valence (positive vs. negative) and social domains (personal vs. professional), VOLUME 13, 2025 147007 C. J. Life event messages tagged as Valid are evaluated as positive cases and represented in blue. Life event messages tagged as Invalid are evaluated as negative cases and are represented in red. FIGURE 5. Average training and classification times in seconds, per model. Training is conducted from the human-tagged data set (n = 2880) and classification time is measured for the process of the ML models automatically tagging the non-human-tagged data (n = 21,120). All event types are averaged together. allowing the analysis to capture variation in tone, intent, and contextual sensitivity across diverse messaging scenarios. The SNP was populated with individualized data associ- ated with these four event types from simulated agent profiles, reusing code from a publicly available repository [37]. A random sample of 12% of the generated messages was selected to form the classification set, resulting in 2880 messages for manual tagging. Column 4 of Table 1 displays the distribution of tagged samples by life event type. Fig. 2 provides an example of the SNP structure used to prompt the LLM message generation. B. MANUAL DATA TAGGING The 2880 life event messages selected for classification were randomly divided among two groups of four reviewers each. Therefore, each message was assigned two reviewers, one from each group. One remaining person was assigned as a tie-breaker for any tied decision, for a total of nine reviewers. For each message being reviewed, reviewers were provided the specific prompt utilized to create the message as well as the response produced by the LLM. Reviewers provided a binary Valid or Invalid tag for each message based on whether they decided that the message met the intention of the given prompt. A predefined list of exclusionary criteria was provided to guide tagging decisions. Reviewers marked a message 147008 VOLUME 13, 2025 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 6. Example life event messages provided to reviewers for tagging. One for each life event type, (a) Birth event, (b) Death event, (c) Fired event, and (d) Hired event. The top portion of each example provides relevant information from the SNP and the bottom provides the resulting AI-generated life event message. as Invalid if it exhibited one or more of the following: (1) wrong event; (2) subject (target person) of narrative is wrong; (3) wrong subject-narrator relationship; (4) incorrect narrator or subject characteristics; (5) temporal error; or (6) narrative is not age-appropriate given age of narrator. For each message tagged as Invalid, reviewers also recorded the relevant exclusionary criteria. However, detailed analyses of these exclusionary rationales are beyond the scope of this study. All reviews, including tie-breakers, were conducted over a two-week period. An automated form was provided to the reviewers to help expedite the review process. For each message provided to the reviewer, this form presented the instructions for the reviewers, the characteristics of the subject and narrator contained in the SNP, the event type contained in the SNP, and the resulting life event message created by ChatGPT. For consistency during the review process, the provided reviewer instructions are the same on every message being tagged. Appendix A provides sample representations of the structured message content provided during the human review process. All reviews were conducted independently and a review coordinator handled the automated aggregation of responses. During this process, reviewer names were made anonymous. Any messages receiving both a Valid and an Invalid vote were sent to a tie-breaker for a final decision. The tie-broken message set was then merged back into the result set. Every message receiving two Valid votes received a classification of Valid and every message receiving two Invalid votes received a final classification of Invalid. In total, only 295 messages (10.24%) required tie-breaking. Table 1 provides the results of the aggregated data tagging for all messages as well as per event category. C. ML MODEL CREATIONS USING TAGGED DATA Nine ML models, each selected for its unique capabilities and architectures, were trained and validated on the human- tagged data set. These include Random Forest [54], Support Vector Machine (SVM) [55], eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) [56], and various Keras-based architectures such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) [57], Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) [58], Rectified Linear Unit (RELU) [59]. Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) [60], [61], [62] was also employed in three configura- tions with input sequence lengths of 64, 128, and 256 tokens. Each ML model was trained independently for each life event type to classify messages as Valid or Invalid, resulting in a total of 36 binary classifiers (9 models × 4 life event types). This per-class binary approach adopted to better capture the linguistic and contextual nuances unique to each event type. All models underwent 10 k-fold cross-validation to evaluate robustness and reduce variance across splits. To facilitate transparency and reproducibility, Appendix G provides detailed input conditions, hyperparameters, and preprocessing pipelines for each model. For each model, a 2 × 2 confusion matrix was constructed using the average of its k-fold predictions. To test for VOLUME 13, 2025 147009 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 7. Agreement matrices indicating the level of consistency between models with respect to their binary classification of life event messages for (a) Birth event messages, (b) Death event messages, (c) Hired event messages, and (d) Fired event messages. *’s represent statistically significant results between the models. statistically significant differences between model predic- tions and ground truth classifications, Fisher’s exact test was applied to each confusion matrix. This test is well-suited for cases involving small cell counts in contingency tables [63], [64]. In addition, McNemar’s test was conducted to assess whether the distribution of disagreement between Valid and Invalid classifications differs significantly between the model and human-annotated labels [65]. For the Fisher’s exact test, the null hypothesis assumes no association between model classifications and human- labeled outcomes (i.e., the classification distributions are independent). The alternative hypothesis is that model and human classifications are associated. For McNemar’s test, the null hypothesis assumes marginal homogeneity in paired classifications, while the alternative indicates a statistically significant difference in model error rates across classification categories. D. ENSEMBLE MODEL CLASSIFICATIONS ON UNTAGGED DATA Each of the nine ML models constructed using the 2880 tagged data points is utilized to predict the classification of the 21,120 data points comprising the set of untagged life event messages. This results in nine sets of predicted classifi- cations for each message. McNemar tests are used to identify statistically significant differences in the distributions of the Valid / Invalid variables for the predicted classifications across the sets of ML-predicted classifications. To form a comparison point for assessing the results of the predictions, as true classification tags are unknown, we construct an ensemble hypothesis consisting of binary Valid / Invalid classifications for each of the 21,120 untagged messages. Each ensemble classification for each message is tagged using a simple majority approach. A classification of Valid is assigned if at least 5 of the models predicted a classification of Valid; otherwise, the message classifies as Invalid. Using this approach, equal weighting is assigned for each of the ML model classifications. The level of agreement between the ensemble classifica- tions and the classifications assigned by human coders is then assessed through a manual validation effort. Following the same process as the initial data tagging effort, a sample of 53 ensemble classified message was randomly pulled from each life event type for a total of 212 samples. Due to timing constraints and the availability of the reviewers, each message was randomly assigned three anonymous reviewers 147010 VOLUME 13, 2025 C. J. Reviewers then independently assessed whether the life event message met the intention of its corresponding SNP. The human-tagged messages were then aggregated to form a baseline data set for comparing the validity of the ensemble classifications. To evaluate the validity of the ensemble classifications, several analyses are applied to the 212-sample validation set. Confusion matrices are constructed for each life event type to characterize classification outcomes in terms of true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives. In line with best practices for binary classification evaluation, we compute performance metrics including precision, recall, and F1 score to quantify how closely ensemble outputs replicate human judgments [66], [67]. To assess statistical significance, one-sided binomial tests are used to determine whether observed match rates exceed a practical performance baseline of 75% for each life event type [68]. In addition, Wilson score intervals are calculated to provide confidence bounds around the observed match rates, supporting a robust interpretation of classification reliability across event types. RESULTS The results and primary findings related to assessing the validity of the SNP, the significance of the models, and the significance of the predicted classifications of the untagged data are presented in this section. This high level of accuracy indicates that the messages generated by GPT-4 in response to the SNPs effectively conveyed the intention of the prompt throughout a majority of the messages across the birth, death, hired, and fired life events. The SNP provided clear guidance and constraints, while enabling the model to produce messages that aligned closely with the specified themes and objectives. E. ENSEMBLE CLASSIFICATION VALIDITY The validity of the ensemble classification method was assessed across two dimensions: life event type and ML model performance. A total of 212 life event messages, randomly sampled from the full set of 21,120 ensemble- tagged predictions, were reviewed by human evaluators. This validation process examines five key aspects: • The level of agreement between ensemble classifica- tions and human-assigned tags (Table 2). • The level of agreement between the ensemble classifi- cations and the number of individual ML models (out of nine) comprising the ensemble that agree with the ensemble classification per event type (Table 3). • The level of agreement between the human-assigned tags and the number of individual ML models (out of nine) that agree with the human-assigned tags per event type (Table 4). • For each ML model, level of agreement between its classifications with (a) human-assigned tags, (b) ensemble classifications, and (c) both simultaneously (Table 5). • The statistical validity of the ensemble classifications within the 212-sample validation set, assessed using confusion matrices, F1 scores, and one-sided bino- mial tests to determine whether classification accuracy significantly exceeds a 75% baseline (Tables 6-7 and Appendix F ). This framework provides a comprehensive assessment of how well the ensemble method aligns with human evaluation and how consistently individual ML models contribute to classification reliability. The ensemble classification method demonstrates strong agreement with human reviewers, with an overall 90.6% match rate (Table 2). This high reliability suggests that ensemble-based classification is a viable and scalable alternative to manual review, reducing time and resource con- straints in LLM validation. Event-specific analysis reveals that firing messages (94.3%) had the highest agreement, indicating that the ensemble effectively captures clear and structured event descriptions. While hiring (88.7%) and death (88.7%) events showed slightly lower agreement, the overall stability across event types supports the robustness VOLUME 13, 2025 147019 C. J. LIMITATIONS, ETHICAL CONSIDERATIONS, AND FUTURE WORK While this study sheds light on message generation across selected life event types and enhances transparency in prompting life event messages from LLMs, several limita- tions warrant consideration. First, the generalizability of our results may be constrained by the specific life event types evaluated. The effectiveness of SNPs and ML models in generating narratives for other life events remains uncertain and warrants further investigation. Future studies could 147022 VOLUME 13, 2025 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 20. Confusion matrices for KERAS layers with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models indicating the number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives with respect to their binary classification of messages for (a) Birth life event messages, (b) Death life event messages, (c) Hired life event messages, and (d) Fired life event messages. expand testing using established life event taxonomies [13] to cover a broader range of scenarios. Second, reliance on manual tagging introduces the poten- tial for subjective biases and inconsistencies that may affect classification accuracy. To mitigate this, each life event message was evaluated by multiple independent reviewers, and assignments were randomized to avoid systematic bias. While a structured tagging protocol was followed to support annotation quality, future work could incorporate inter-annotator agreement metrics, such as Cohen’s kappa, by adopting a fully crossed design in which multiple raters assess overlapping message sets. This would provide a formal measure of consistency and further strengthen the reliability of human-labeled ground truth data. Third, since the study was conducted in English and all human evaluators were from Western countries, the perspectives and norms reflected in the validation process may not fully reflect the cultural diversity or social values of a global audience. Furthermore, model selection and parameterization may also influence generalizability. Future research should explore a wider range of life event types, examine variation in ML model error types, and adopt standardized eval- uation frameworks to improve cross-domain applicability. In addition, incorporating user perception studies can help assess not only the technical validity but also the subjective reception of AI-generated narratives, building on recent work in personalized narrative interventions [22]. While majority ML model-voting improved classification stability, it may also reinforce systematic misclassification when models share similar biases. For example, hiring- related messages exhibited lower agreement with human reviewers, suggesting that ambiguous cases may be con- sistently misclassified by the ensemble. Additionally, some models (e.g., BERT 064) contributed little to classification diversity, raising questions about their sensitivity to subtle distinctions between valid and invalid messages. Future refinements could explore weighted voting schemes based on historical model accuracy to mitigate these limitations. Training and validation were also impacted by class imbal- ance [69], [70], due to the relatively low number of manually tagged Invalid cases. Although this reflects GPT-4’s general success in following prompt instructions, it limited early- stage ML model performance, with several models leaning toward a biased ‘‘always valid’’ selection strategy. While expanding the dataset could have helped increase the number of Invalid tags, time and resource constraints prevented further tagging. As a result, we introduced additional Keras VOLUME 13, 2025 147023 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 21. Confusion matrices for KERAS layers with Rectified Linear Unit (RELU) models indicating the number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives with respect to their binary classification of messages for (a) Birth life event messages, (b) Death life event messages, (c) Hired life event messages, and (d) Fired life event messages. and BERT models to better explore the classification problem space. Timing tests also suggest that larger tagged datasets may necessitate additional optimization to ensure scalability and generalizability across diverse life event types. Ethical oversight becomes essential as LLMs increas- ingly generate messaging in contexts involving emotion- ally sensitive or socially impactful topics. In this study, we focus specifically on validating whether AI-generated outputs align with the intentions encoded in structured prompts. This validation step is not only important for ensuring narrative fidelity but also for minimizing the risk of miscommunication, misinformation, or unintended persuasion. Automating personal announcements, such as messaging involving loss of life, job termination, or other life-altering events, carries unique ethical and emotional risks. In such contexts, even slight inaccuracies or tonal missteps can cause distress, violate social norms, or erode trust. LLM-generated outputs may unintentionally depersonalize or misrepresent these sensitive situations, especially when trained on data lacking in cultural or emotional nuance or biased toward the norms of specific groups. As such, any system intended to generate or assist in the generation of these announcements must undergo rigorous review, including human-in-the- loop safeguards, to ensure contextual appropriateness and emotional sensitivity. In addition to risks associated with AI-generated outputs, ethical concerns also arise from inputting personal or sensitive information into generative systems. Training or querying AI systems with human data, especially in the absence of clear oversight, can embed structural biases and raise serious privacy and accountability concerns [9]. Such inputs may raise privacy issues related to data storage and retention, unauthorized reuse, or exposure through unintended model behavior, particularly if model providers retain access to prompt history or training data. If connected to research, such systems should go through appropriate Institutional Review Board evaluation to ensure safe practices and proper protections of human subjects data, where appli- cable. For industrial use, organizations deploying generative systems should implement strict data governance policies, including minimizing the collection of personally identifiable information (PII), disabling prompt logging where feasible, and ensuring compliance with data protection regulations such as GDPR or HIPAA. Additionally, transparency around the handling of user data during inference as well as for any downstream model improvement is essential to maintaining user trust and preventing misuse. 147024 VOLUME 13, 2025 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 22. Confusion matrices for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) with 64 token limit models. The matrices indicate the number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives with respect to their binary classification of messages for (a) Birth life event messages, (b) Death life event messages, (c) Hired life event messages, and (d) Fired life event messages. Recent work reveals that LLM-generated messages can influence human opinions on complex policy issues [15] and can be leveraged for persuasive political messaging at scale [71]. These findings underscore the potential for both unintentional and deliberate LLM misuse, reinforcing the importance of upstream safeguards such as prompt-intent validation. A recent survey of persuasive applications of LLMs further emphasizes the urgent need for normative as well as technical frameworks to guide their responsible deployment within socially consequential domains [72]. While GPT-based models offer substantial utility, they also present critical risks related to bias, misinformation, and contextual misalignment [3]. This study addresses such concerns by using structured prompts to constrain model outputs, and validating results through both human experts and machine learning classifiers to assess validity and alignment with intent. Although this study does not involve deployment to end users, it lays important groundwork for developing safe, responsible, and trustworthy AI communication pipelines. Future implementations should incorporate robust safeguards against manipulative, biased, or emotionally inappropriate outputs, particularly in domains such as healthcare, public safety, and civic engagement. Furthermore, our reliance on human tagging raises questions about annotator bias and intersubjectivity, which future work should address through more diverse rater pools and standardized agreement metrics. VI. CONCLUSION This study highlights the transformative potential of SNPs in guiding LLMs to generate messages with depth, clarity, and authenticity. Our findings confirm the effectiveness of SNPs in shaping AI-generated messaging while also under- scoring key challenges in automated tagging, validation, and evaluation. By integrating machine learning classifiers to assess validity, this research provides a scalable approach for improving the reliability of AI-generated content across structured messaging applications. The implications extend beyond narrative generation, offering insights for AI-driven decision support, automated content validation, and explain- able AI frameworks. This study underscores the importance of structured methodologies in ensuring trustworthy AI outputs and reinforces key challenges such as bias detection, classifier misclassification, and transparency. The statistically significant results from our analysis serve as compelling evidence affirming the effectiveness of VOLUME 13, 2025 147025 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 23. Confusion matrices for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) with 128 token limit models. The matrices indicate the number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives with respect to their binary classification of messages for (a) Birth life event messages, (b) Death life event messages, (c) Hired life event messages, and (d) Fired life event messages. the utilized SNP in guiding life event message generation by LLMs. With 29 out of 36 significance tests at the 0.05 level classifying as statistically significant, our findings underscore the robustness of structured prompts in eliciting messages that closely align with prompt intentions. These findings not only validate the reliability of our approach but also shed light on the remarkable capacity of LLMs to comprehend and adhere to structured guidelines when crafting messages. This structured pipeline of combining SNP-guided message generation, human classification, and ML validation, presents a promising path toward automated classification. Moreover, integrating an AI-driven feedback loop could enable automatic evaluation of generated mes- sages, assist in further refining prompt structure, and continue advancing explainability as well as ethically driven AI message generation. These results validate the effectiveness of SNP-driven AI- generated messages, reinforcing their potential for automa- tion in content validation and structured communication. The strong agreement between ensemble classification and manual human tagging (90.6%) confirms its viability as an effective automation tool for LLM-generated life event messages. While refinements such as confidence-weighted voting could further enhance classification accuracy, the current ensemble method demonstrates high reliability, making it a scalable and practical approach for structured narrative validation. By demonstrating the SNP’s ability to yield messages that resonate with human intent, our research opens doors to a myriad of applications, from enhancing storytelling in artificial intelligence to generating effective and empathetic narratives from simulation agents to fueling impassioned communication between policy makers and their constituents. While this study focuses on structured prompting for AI-generated life event messaging in the form of short, tweet-style posts, the insights extend far beyond short-form communication. This structured prompt approach offers a scalable foundation for guiding AI models in generating text that is not only coherent and contextually appropriate but also emotionally aligned with human expectations. These findings pave the way for expanding AI-generated content beyond messaging toward full-length narratives tailored for broader applications, including personal milestone documentation pertaining to self and others, as well as for domains such as crisis communication and emotionally resonant storytelling. 147026 VOLUME 13, 2025 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 24. Confusion matrices for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) with 256 token limit models. The matrices indicate the number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives with respect to their binary classification of messages for (a) Birth life event messages, (b) Death life event messages, (c) Hired life event messages, and (d) Fired life event messages. Future research should refine structured prompting tech- niques to improve AI adaptability across diverse life events, enhance error detection mechanisms, and ensure that AI- generated messages and narratives remain authentic and trustworthy across real-world communication platforms. Through these advancements, we will continue progressing towards AI systems that generate accurate, ethically sound, socially meaningful, and human-centered narratives. Multi- lingual validation, domain-specific customizations, and real- time moderation are also areas that could be meaningfully explored in future work. Furthermore, established life event taxonomies can be leveraged as structured frameworks to assess AI-generated messaging across a broader range of life events; facilitate examinations of how different life event types impact emotional resonance and human perception, as well as how they influence AI accuracy from LLM or ML perspectives. APPENDIX A EXAMPLE VISUAL REPRESENTATION PROVIDED TO REVIEWERS DURING THE TAGGING PROCESS Every reviewer involved with the manual tagging of the data received their information to tag in the same format for every life event message. All messages start untagged and have two reviewers assigned to independently provide binary Valid / Invalid tags. Each reviewer reviews and tags every message in their respective set one at a time. Tags are then anonymously aggregated and any tied decisions are assigned an additional reviewer whose decision is used to break the tie. The set of tied reviewer tags is randomly assigned across the tie-breaker reviewers. Ultimately, at most three reviewers assess each life event message. Fig. 6 provides an example of an untagged message for each of the (a) birth, (b) death, (c) fired, and (d) hired life event categories. These AI-generated messages are listed under the CHATGPT NARRATIVE label at the bottom of each sub-figure. APPENDIX B AGREEMENT MATRICES OF ML MODELS’ PREDICTED CLASSIFICATIONS The agreement matrices provided in Fig. 7 assess the consistency between the binary classification of the ML models. Each row and column reflects one of the ML models and each cell represents the level of agreement between the two corresponding models. A significance level of 0.05 is VOLUME 13, 2025 147027 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts FIGURE 25. Confusion matrices for the Ensemble model’s classifications indicating the number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives with respect to their binary classification of messages for (a) Birth life event messages, (b) Death life event messages, (c) Hired life event messages, and (d) Fired life event messages. utilized to reflect statistically significant outcomes within the tables. APPENDIX C MCNEMAR TEST RESULTS USING THE MANUALLY TAGGED DATA SET Figures 8-11 provide the results of the McNemar tests for significant differences in the distribution of binary Valid / Invalid classifications of the AI-generated life event messages for each of the nine models for each life event type. A significance level of 0.05 is assigned to assess significance. Within each figure, the red dotted line represents the significance level. Each plot within each figure displays the P-value resulting from a comparison of the model listed at the top of the plot against each of the other eight models (ergo, there are nine plots in each figure, each containing eight bars.) These comparisons are conducted using the confusion matrices developed from each of the developed ML models from the tagged data (n = 2880) using 10 k-fold cross validation. In these cases, the true values are known as a result of the manual tagging. The null hypothesis in these cases are that there is no statistically significant difference in distributions of the binary classifications made by each model. P-values less than 0.05 indicate that evidence exists to support rejecting the null hypothesis in favor the alternative hypothesis that a difference between the distribution of binary classifications does exist. APPENDIX D MCNEMAR TEST RESULTS OF PREDICTED CLASSIFICATIONS ON THE ORIGINALLY UNTAGGED DATA SET Figs. 12-15 provide the results of the McNemar tests for significant differences in the distribution of binary Valid / Invalid life event messages’ predicted classifications of the originally untagged data set. A significance level of 0.05 is assigned to assess significance. Within each figure, the red dotted line represents the significance level. Each plot within each figure displays the P-value resulting from a comparison of the model listed at the top of the plot against each of the other eight models. This results in nine plots within each figure that each contain eight bars. These comparisons are conducted using the originally untagged data (n = 21,120) using an ensemble hypothesis comparison set where baseline Valid classifications are the result of 5+ models predicting a Valid. The null hypothesis 147028 VOLUME 13, 2025 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts TABLE 8. Input Conditions and Training Settings for ML Model Variants Used in Ensemble Classification. tested in these cases states that there is no statistically signif- icant difference in distributions of the binary classifications made by each model. P-values less than 0.05 indicate that evidence exists to support rejecting the null hypothesis in favor the alternative hypothesis that a difference between the distribution of binary classifications does exist. APPENDIX E CONFUSION MATRICES FOR ML MODELS Figures 16-24 provide the confusion matrices for each of the developed ML models. Each figure is divided into four parts: (a) confusion matrix for models built using Birth life event messages, (b) confusion matrix for models built using Death life event messages, (c) confusion matrix for models built using Hired life event messages, and (d) confusion matrix for models built using Fired life event messages. These confusion matrices convey the number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives for each of the developed models based on how well the models matched the 2880 tagged data points. Each figure provides the sample size and normalized percentage of samples within each cell of the confusion matrix. APPENDIX F CONFUSION MATRICES FOR ENSEMBLE VALIDATION TESTING Figure 25 presents the confusion matrices for the ensemble model’s classifications, grouped by life event type. The figure VOLUME 13, 2025 147029 C. J. Lynch et al.: AI-Generated Messaging for Life Events Using Structured Prompts is divided into four subplots displaying confusion matrices for: (a) Birth, (b) Death, (c) Hired, and (d) Fired life event messages. Each matrix shows the classification outcomes based on agreement between the ensemble model and the manually tagged validation data. These matrices display the counts of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives, reflecting how well the ensemble model reproduced human annotations across a total of 212 validation samples with 53 samples per life event type. Each cell contains both the raw count and the corresponding normalized percentage. In these matrices, a classification of 1 corresponds to messages labeled as Valid, while a classification of 0 corresponds to messages labeled as Invalid. APPENDIX G MACHINE LEARNING MODEL INPUT CONDITIONS AND TRAINING SETTINGS Table 8 provides detailed input conditions, training param- eters, and preprocessing settings for each machine learning model variant used in the ensemble classification frame- work. This documentation supports full transparency and reproducibility of experimental design. Each row specifies the specific model architecture or feature extractor, imple- mented using established libraries such as scikit-learn [73], XGBoost [74], TensorFlow/Keras [75], and HuggingFace Transformers [76], as well as sequence dimensionality, tokenization strategies, and cross-validation settings. Readers should interpret this table as a reference for how each model was configured and trained, including the use of 10-fold cross-validation and random seed variation to ensure robustness. Differences in batch size, input length, or model structure were selected to explore performance trade-offs across model families. For ensemble components trained with random seeds, outputs were aggregated using majority voting. This appendix complements the main text by clarifying how model heterogeneity and standardized evaluation contribute to the ensemble’s overall reliability. Scripts, data, and tagged messaged data sets are available in a publicly accessible online data repository [52]. title: APMSR: an intelligent QA system
file_name: APMSR-an-intelligent-QA-system-for-synthetic-biology-empowered-by-adaptive-prompting-and-multisource-knowledge-retrieval_2026_Nature-Research.pdf
text: APMSR: an intelligent QA system for synthetic biology empowered by adaptive prompting and multi- source knowledge retrieval Jinlin Wang1,3, Zhizheng Cao1,4, Zhiyong Tian2,3, Bin Sun1,4 & Li Cheng1,4 Synthetic biology is a highly specialized discipline characterized by diverse and heterogeneous knowledge sources. Leveraging large language models (LLMs) to achieve accurate, context-aware domain-specific question answering remains a significant challenge, particularly due to persistent issues such as knowledge gaps and hallucinations. To address this, we propose APMSR (Adaptive Prompt and Multi-Source Retrieval), an optimization strategy that integrates adaptive prompt generation based on question features with dynamic multi-source knowledge retrieval, guided by the LinUCB algorithm. This approach balances exploration and exploitation to enhance the relevance and precision of domain-specific information retrieval. We implemented APMSR in a question-answering system tailored to synthetic biology and evaluated its performance on complex, professional-level queries. Experimental results demonstrate that the APMSR-optimized system achieves up to 93% accuracy on multiple-choice and true/false questions. These improvements in accuracy and robustness highlight the potential of combining LLMs with retrieval-augmented strategies for advanced domain- specific question answering. Keywords Synthetic biology, Large language model, Retrieval-augmented generation, Prompt engineering, Domain-specific question answering system Domain-specific question-answering systems have become important tools for advancing scientific research and the application of technological achievements. These systems can effectively assist researchers in quickly extracting the necessary knowledge from vast amounts of information. In response to global demands for sustainable development and a green economy, synthetic biology has emerged as a key driver of technological advancements across diverse fields, including biofuels, biomanufacturing, and biotherapeutics1. The research outcomes in these fields have been growing exponentially2. However, building a question-answering system for synthetic biology presents numerous challenges, including complex domain knowledge structures, a vast array of specialized terminology, heterogeneous data sources, and rapidly evolving research content3. Therefore, effectively integrating and precisely processing this complex information has become a critical technical challenge in the development of QA systems for synthetic biology4. At present, the development of QA systems in this field is still in its early exploratory stages. Notable limitations persist in specialized knowledge parsing, retrieval path optimization, and model generalization capabilities5. Nevertheless, this area holds considerable potential for future research and technological advancement. In recent years, large language models such as GLM-46, DeepSeek R17, and GPT-48 have demonstrated outstanding performance across a variety of language tasks. In order to improve their adaptability to specialized domains, models such as BioGPT9 and BioBERT10 have been fine-tuned using domain-specific corpora, thereby enhancing their capabilities in biomedical question-answering. However, knowledge-based question answering in synthetic biology still faces numerous challenges11. On the one hand, existing fine-tuning methods heavily rely on large amounts of annotated data, leading to high training costs and difficulty in promptly reflecting the latest research advances12. On the other hand, the field’s vast and rapidly evolving knowledge system makes general language models prone to knowledge gaps, resulting in hallucinations during text generation13. To address these 1School of Computer Science, Hubei University, No. 368 Youyi Road, Wuhan 430062, Hubei, China. 2School of Artificial Intelligence, Hubei University, No. 368 Youyi Road, Wuhan 430062, Hubei, China. 3Key Laboratory of Intelligent Sensing System and Security (Hubei University), Ministry of Education, No. 368 Youyi Road, Wuhan 430062, Hubei, China. 4Hubei Key Laboratory of Big Data Intelligent Analysis and Application, Hubei University, No. 368 Youyi Road, Wuhan 430062, Hubei, China. email: tianzhiyong@hubu.edu.cn; chengli@hubu.edu.cn OPEN Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:7331 1 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38006-8 www.nature.com/scientificreports issues, Lewis et al.14 proposed Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). By combining external retrieval with the generative reasoning capabilities of LLMs, this approach effectively reduces model training costs and minimizes the risk of hallucinations15. With the application of RAG in knowledge-intensive professional fields such as public deliberation16, chemistry17, and fact-checking18, related research has gradually optimized the retrieval process by incorporating knowledge graphs and other structured evidence sources to improve the precision of retrieved information19. Meanwhile, RAG has also been introduced into automated question generation tasks in educational assessment20, combined with methods such as ensemble learning and ontology mapping to reduce topic redundancy and mitigate factual hallucinations in multi-hop question generation21,22. These studies demonstrate that RAG has broad applicability in highly specialized application scenarios. Our research group developed an in-house prototype named ZymoQA, a RAG-based question answering system for the synthetic biology field. The system focuses on answering specialized questions related to Zymomonas mobilis, a Gram-negative ethanologenic bacterium. It naturally exhibits highly efficient ethanol fermentation via the Entner–Doudoroff pathway and has therefore become a widely studied model microorganism in synthetic biology23. The system enables users to pose questions in natural language and returns relevant literature and answers. ZymoQA uses high-quality PubMed literature as its local knowledge base, covering areas such as genomics, metabolic pathways, and industrial applications. The retrieval mechanism of the system combines vector-based retrieval24 with BM25 retrieval25. In the generation module, a locally deployed LLM works in conjunction with a context re-ranking module to support multi-document reading and context-semantic alignment, ensuring the accuracy and semantic consistency of the answers. However, some issues have arisen during practical use. The current system mainly relies on the local knowledge base for retrieval and lacks integration with external real-time information sources, leading to an insufficient response to emerging research developments. For example, when a user poses a question such as “How can Zymomonas mobilis improve cellulose fermentation efficiency?” the system, by querying only the local knowledge base and overlooking recent scientific advancements, produces answers that deviate from the current state of the field. This paper aims to further optimize the existing question-answering system to enhance its performance in answering questions related to Zymomonas mobilis. To evaluate the optimization strategy proposed in this paper, we formulate three research questions: • RQ1: How does the optimized system compare with existing QA systems on domain tasks? • RQ2: How do individual components affect performance? • RQ3: How does compatibility between backend LLMs and optimization strategies affect performance? We propose APMSR, a strategy for optimizing question-answering systems in synthetic biology. This strategy consists of two core mechanisms: (1) Adaptive prompt matching based on question features. The large language model is used to classify question types and to dynamically select the optimal prompt template. (2) Dynamic multi-source path selection with the LinUCB algorithm. The three retrieval paths–local retrieval, external retrieval, and hybrid retrieval–are regarded as ”arms” of a multi-armed bandit, and LinUCB26 is employed to choose the most suitable retrieval path. Experimental results show that the system optimized with the APMSR strategy improves average accuracy by 39% and 13% on multiple-choice and true/false question answering tasks, respectively, compared with LLM-only and standard RAG baselines. Notably, the combination of Deepseek R1 with APMSR achieved optimal performance, reaching an accuracy of 93%. In summary, the contributions of this paper are as follows: 1. Method: An optimization strategy, named APMSR, is proposed for question-answering systems in the do­ main of synthetic biology. It integrates two core mechanisms: adaptive prompt matching based on question features and dynamic multi-source path selection using the LinUCB algorithm. 2. Application: Our optimized question-answering system helps researchers retrieve information from the rapidly growing body of specialized literature and provides a reference for the future optimization of knowl­ edge-based question-answering systems in this field. 3. Evaluation: Experimental results show that the APMSR strategy improves the accuracy and reliability of question answering in professional fields. We found that two components contribute most substantially to this performance enhancement: adaptive prompt matching and multi-source retrieval path selection. Com­ parison results across multiple models demonstrate that the combination of APMSR and DeepSeek R1 de­ livers the most effective performance. This confirms the importance of combining model structure with an optimization strategy in this field. Related work Evolution of question answering Question-answering (QA) systems aim to interpret natural language queries and deliver precise answers directly, making them powerful tools for information access in knowledge-intensive domains such as biology27. Early QA systems were rule-based, such as BASEBALL, which focused on narrow-domain factual queries28. However, their semantic understanding and reasoning abilities were still limited. The introduction of knowledge-base QA systems further improved answer precision and interpretability by transforming natural language questions into structured logical queries. However, their scalability was restricted by the cost of knowledge curation and limited domain coverage. With the advent of neural language models, generative QA systems began to produce answers directly instead of extracting them from documents. While this design improved flexibility, it often led to factual hallucinations due to dependence on static training data. To overcome these weaknesses, hybrid QA frameworks were proposed to combine the factual reliability of retrieval with the fluency of generation. This hybrid paradigm became more prevalent in biomedical research, as reflected Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:7331 2 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38006-8 www.nature.com/scientificreports/ in the BioASQ Challenge29, which encouraged systems that integrated IR and reasoning. In the biomedical and biological domains, the development of QA systems followed a similar trajectory but advanced more slowly. Early systems mainly relied on PubMed-based retrieval or prebuilt biomedical ontologies to support structured queries and concept-level reasoning. By the 2010s, query languages such as SPARQL enabled natural language access to structured biological databases, including UniProt and KEGG30. Later, the rise of domain-specific pretrained models such as BioBERT10 and BioGPT9 substantially improved biomedical text comprehension. Despite these advances, traditional QA systems still suffer from key limitations: fragmented multi-source data, shallow semantic understanding, weak reasoning capacity, and poor contextual interaction. To address these issues, retrieval-augmented generation was introduced, combining external knowledge retrieval with generative reasoning14. By incorporating retrieved passages into the model’s context, RAG enhances factual accuracy and interpretability. Therefore, retrieval-augmented generation has become a core framework for modern knowledge-intensive question-answering systems and provides theoretical support for the domain-specific optimization methods proposed in this paper. Large language models and prompt engineering Through large-scale pretraining, large language models (LLMs) have brought a paradigm shift to question answering, enabling coherent and context-aware answer generation. Models such as GPT-48, GLM-46, and LLaMA-331 have demonstrated remarkable reasoning and understanding capabilities across various natural language tasks. Despite their strong generalization ability, these models still encounter challenges in professional domains, including factual hallucinations, outdated knowledge, and difficulty in adapting to specialized terminology. To alleviate these limitations, prompt engineering has emerged as an effective strategy to guide model behavior and enhance reasoning reliability32. The core idea is to craft structured textual instructions that condition the model’s generation process to align with user intent. Prompt engineering can construct reasoning steps through the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) method, thereby strengthening a model’s logical reasoning33. ReAct integrates ”reasoning-acting” sequences to achieve a closed loop of retrieval and generation34. In domain-specific question answering, particularly in areas such as biomedicine and synthetic biology, such prompting strategies limit the model’s ability to handle diverse types of questions. To address these limitations, recent studies have begun exploring more flexible prompting strategies that adapt to contextual and task-specific cues. In synthetic biology question answering, a strategy that rewrites and decomposes queries before retrieval has been shown to improve the accuracy and reliability of domain-specific answers35. This approach improves the accuracy and reliability of answers in specialized domains and provides the theoretical foundation for the adaptive prompting module in our proposed APMSR framework. Biological question answering systems In specialized domains, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) dynamically integrates authoritative knowledge into the generation process, effectively mitigating knowledge gaps and hallucinations in large language models36. In the biomedical domain, several systems have demonstrated the potential of RAG-based architectures for knowledge-intensive reasoning tasks. For instance, MedGraphRAG and VectorGraphRAG organize biomedical texts into graph-structured representations to enable more reliable retrieval37,38. BioRAGent integrates a RAG module into an interactive environment, enabling users to view retrieved passages and citation links to improve interpretability39. Similarly, BioRAG combines dense retrieval with biomedical literature to enhance factual grounding and reduce hallucination risk40. Self-BioRAG further extends this paradigm by introducing a post-generation self-reflection mechanism that produces rationales and confidence scores41. However, current question answering systems remain primarily focused on the biomedical domain42, with limited support for highly specialized domains such as synthetic biology. Although SynBioGPT has been adapted for the synthetic biology domain, it primarily captures macro-level conceptual knowledge and lacks the capacity to perform detailed reasoning about specific research entities such as Zymomonas mobilis5. Therefore, the development of retrieval-augmented question-answering systems specifically designed for highly specialized biological domains holds practical importance. Algorithmic strategies in QA systems Algorithms play a central role in question-answering (QA) systems, influencing every stage from retrieval to reasoning and decision optimization. Effective algorithmic design determines how accurately relevant information is retrieved, how logically answers are generated, and how adaptively the system can respond to diverse user queries. At the retrieval stage, ranking algorithms are essential for selecting the most relevant knowledge segments from large-scale corpora. Traditional sparse retrieval methods (such as BM25) identify relevant texts through keyword matching and statistical term weighting25. In recent years, dense retrieval models such as DPR have achieved higher recall rates in domain-specific question-answering tasks by mapping queries and documents into a continuous vector space for semantic-level matching24. At the reasoning stage, algorithmic optimization focuses on enhancing logical coherence, factual grounding, and answer quality during text generation. Reinforcement learning techniques, such as PPO43, align model outputs with human p
