GHAZAL 2, (Senior Member, IEEE), QASEM M. KHARMA 3, MUNIR AHMAD 4,5, (Senior Member, IEEE), SAGHEER ABBAS6, (Senior Member, IEEE), MUHAMMAD FURQAN7, AND KHAN MUHAMMAD ADNAN 8, (Senior Member, IEEE) 1Department of Computer Science, Capital University of Science and Technology, Islamabad 44000, Pakistan 2Department of Networks and Cybersecurity, Hourani Center for Applied Scientific Research, Al-Ahliyya Amman University, Amman 19111, Jordan 3Software Engineering Department, Hourani Center for Applied Scientific Research, Al-Ahliyya Amman University, Amman 19111, Jordan 4Department of Computer Science, National College of Business Administration and Economics, Lahore 54660, Pakistan 5University College, Korea University, Seoul 02841, Republic of Korea 6Computer Science Department, Prince Mohammad Bin Fahd University, Al Khobar 31952, Saudi Arabia 7Department of Software Engineering, Capital University of Science and Technology, Islamabad 44000, Pakistan 8Department of Software, Faculty of Artificial Intelligence and Software, Gachon University, Seongnam-si 13120, Republic of Korea Corresponding authors: Munir Ahmad (munirahmad@ieee.org) and Khan Muhammad Adnan (adnan@gachon.ac.kr) ABSTRACT In the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP), the task of text summarization plays a vital role in understanding textual content and producing concise summaries. Text summarization approaches can be categorized as either extractive or abstractive, with the latter largely unexplored in the Arabic script languages. Previous research on abstractive summarization for the Urdu language has made progress in creating logical and succinct summaries. Still, it has failed to preserve the underlying semantics, often resulting in inconsistent and ambiguous outputs. This study focuses on designing and implementing an abstractive text summarization model for the Urdu language, aiming to capture the semantic meaning of the original text. The proposed model improves readability and comprehension by generating concise and meaningful overviews of original content while introducing new words and expressions. The study implements a multi-layer transformer encoder as part of the mBART (Multilingual BART) model. The encoder consists of multiple stacked transformer layers. It utilizes self-attention mechanisms to process and understand the input sequences effectively, achieving a BERTScore of 90%, a BLEU score of 43%, and ROUGE scores of 80.3% for ROUGE-1, 74.3% for ROUGE-2, and 80.3% for ROUGE-L. These results demonstrate the efficacy of the model in producing coherent and high-quality abstractive summaries for the Urdu language. INDEX TERMS Abstractive text summarization (ATS), natural language processing (NLP), semantic meaning, text generation, text summarization (TS). I. INTRODUCTION Artificial intelligence (AI) is the process of replicating human intelligence in machines [1]. The applications of AI include robotics, digital image processing, expert systems, data visualization, and NLP [2]. NLP is a subfield of AI that The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Arianna DUlizia . deals with tasks like text translation, text synthesis, and Text Summarization (TS) of natural languages by using Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) algorithms [3], [4]. The applications of NLP include voice-activated virtual assis- tants, translation, and text categorization [5]. NLP also deals with many challenges, such as lexical & syntactic ambiguity, context understanding, data limitation, and unavailability of high-quality labeled data [6]. Fatima et al.: Abstractive Text Summarization in Arabic-Like Script In NLP, a generative model learns patterns from data to produce new data. The goal of generative models in NLP is to generate text that is similar to human languages in terms of coherence and context [7]. These models are frequently used in tasks that include creative writing, text production, machine translation, and summarization [8]. Moreover, text generation is the act of predicting the next word (or group of words) in a sentence to produce a coherent and contextually relevant text [9]. The applications of text-generative models include chatbots, content creation, machine dialogue, and TS [10]. The rapid growth of AI-driven applications enables users to extract precise information from data repositories. TS is a technique in NLP that is used to generate condensed versions of text to simplify reading and searching for information from multiple sources [11], [12]. In recent years, significant advances have been made in the Urdu language. With each passing day, numerous online sources generate a substantial volume of Urdu text data that needs to be summarized. TS approaches can be categorized as either extractive or abstractive. The extractive TS techniques create summaries without understanding the semantic meaning of the sentences. In contrast, abstractive summarization is more precise than extractive summaries. However, the extracted summary is produced more quickly since statistical methods are quicker than linguistic processes. The approaches to TS are shown in Figure 1. FIGURE 1. Text summarization approaches. Extractive summarization finds prominent words, sen- tences, and phrases in the text, mostly using statistical criteria rather than developing a semantic understanding of the text [13]. The framework of an extractive TS is shown in Figure 2 [14]. FIGURE 2. Framework of an extractive text summarization system. Extractive summarization simplifies complex information, assists in making decisions, condenses text, and provides concise, rich summaries [15]. It preserves the concept and context of the original content, but may exclude nuances [16]. Abstractive Text Summarization (ATS) is a technique that generates a clear and concise summary by rephrasing and synthesizing information from the source text [17]. To grasp the essence of the text, it uses NLP techniques on a large scale along with DL models [18]. However, ATS struggles to handle anything outside the text domain, coherence, and factual accuracy [19]. Figure 3 illustrates the general framework of ATS [20]. FIGURE 3. Framework of an abstractive text summarization system. The applications of ATS include news articles, emails, chatbot transcripts, posts on social networks, and meeting minutes [21]. ATS extracts important information from lengthy articles, making it easier to read and comprehend the major concepts without having to read the entire text [22], [23]. It is difficult to provide a coherent summary because ATS requires the creation of new material; knowing the underlying ideas and the author’s objectives is necessary for semantic comprehension [24]. Factual errors and misunderstandings must be avoided, particularly when reading technical or specialized content [25], [26]. ATS analyzes and paraphrases text concepts to create a coherent summary [27]. The intricate morphology and dialects of the Arabic and Urdu languages present difficulties in generating summaries [28], [29]. FIGURE 4. Architecture for arabic and urdu text summarization system. Figure 4 illustrates a pipeline for modeling, processing, and evaluating a data set for an NLP task. Raw data is imported and processed through preprocessing, tokenization, 104978 VOLUME 13, 2025 W. Comparison of summarizing texts in arabic and urdu. stemming, and lemmatization to ensure consistency and precision for analysis [29]. Data representation stages convert pre-processed data into suitable formats, ensuring consis- tency in model input by creating word embedding vectors, transforming sentences into integer sequences, and padding them for consistent length [29]. The training set, validation set, and testing set are subsets that separate represented data, allowing for model fitting, performance improvement, and realistic testing on unseen data [30]. The model undergoes a structured training process, using preprocessed data for consistent formatting, inference, and evaluation using metrics like F-measure, ROUGE, and BLEU [31]. II. LITERATURE REVIEW This section presents a comprehensive review of the latest research, including empirical, experimental, and theoretical studies. The focus is based on synthesizing existing work, identifying gaps, and highlighting emerging trends. Addition- ally, advancements in ATS, particularly for Arabic-like script languages, are examined in depth. Table 1 illustrates, ATS in Arabic and Urdu is challenging due to the morphological complexity of the languages, lack of resources, dialectal differences, orthographic complexities, and evaluation chal- lenges [30]. The authors in [34] introduce the UATS-23 dataset to address the challenges of developing models for the low-resource Urdu language. The study aims to enhance Urdu summarization tools without proposing a new model, but highlights the effectiveness of the GRU with Attention model in improving the quality of Urdu TS. Attention-based GRU models perform well for abstractive summarization in Urdu, with the GRU with Attention model achieving the highest ROUGE scores (ROUGE-1 = 46.7, ROUGE- 2 = 24.1, and ROUGE-L = 48.7). Conversely, transformer models like BART and GPT-3.5 faced issues with Urdu morphological compatibility and data scarcity. The paper addresses these challenges by providing a large Urdu dataset, establishing performance baselines, and offering insights into model selection and training for Urdu summarization. Future studies should focus on expanding dataset sizes, devel- oping pre-trained models specifically for Urdu, exploring domain-specific and hybrid summarization strategies, and improving evaluation methods using semantic metrics and human assessments. The study [35] summarizes the difficulties of abstractive Urdu texts, including the proper availability of datasets, linguistic problems, and complex syntax. Using a labeled dataset of 19,615 documents, the authors proposed a transformer-based method that uses an encoder-decoder for summary creation and RoBERTa for contextual embeddings. The model outperformed extractive approaches in accurate and fluent summaries, achieving ROUGE-1 scores of 25.18, 12.5, and 23.27. The shortcomings of Urdu abstractive summarization models, including their worse performance and dependence on translated datasets, are also covered in the research. To overcome the shortcomings of the available resources and techniques, it suggests improving Urdu TS through the creation of extensive datasets, enhanced embeddings, the use of diacritical markings, and the proposal of a summary-length dataset. The authors in [36] proposed an LSTM-based encoder- decoder model implemented in Python and TensorFlow for abstractive summarization of Urdu text. ROUGE-1, ROUGE- 2, and ROUGE-L all had average F1 scores of 0.43, 0.25, and 0.23, respectively. However, the study has drawbacks, including its dependence on a particular news collection, the subjectivity of summaries created by humans, and difficulties in handling the subtleties of Urdu grammar. Moreover, it is not scalable and overfits. Future research ought to expand datasets, improve preprocessing techniques, look into complex models, address language-specific problems, take user input into account, become multilingual, and employ knowledge graphs. The authors [37] presented a hybrid method for generating abstractive summaries of Urdu text, combining extractive summarization techniques with the BERT model. The study uses a small dataset of 50 articles from sources like BBC Urdu and Jang News, covering topics like health, news, sports, and technology. The study achieved varying compression rates, with extractive summaries at 38% to 41%, hybrid summaries at 33%, and abstractive summaries at 25%. Expert evaluation emphasized the importance of human assessment over auto- mated metrics, resulting in fluent and meaningful summaries. However, the study has several limitations, including a small dataset, variable summary quality, pre-training constraints, computational resource challenges, subjectivity, and neglect of dialect variations and scalability. Future research should focus on expanding datasets, developing multi-document summarization techniques, incorporating user feedback, and enhancing performance and accessibility by leveraging transfer learning and integrating linguistic features. The [38] proposed a model using bidirectional encoding and a semantic extractor to handle Urdu’s complex structure and rich context. The model was tested on a dataset of Urdu news articles and blogs, addressing grammar and diacritic issues. The bidirectional encoder-decoder model outperformed traditional methods in fluency, coherence, and semantic accuracy, achieving higher ROUGE scores and VOLUME 13, 2025 104979 W. Fatima et al.: Abstractive Text Summarization in Arabic-Like Script better recall, precision, and F1 scores. However, the study has limitations such as robust preprocessing, limited general- izability, a small dataset, a lack of multilingual/cross-lingual evaluation, computationally intensive bidirectional encoding, and insufficient human evaluation. Future improvements include expanding the dataset, multilingual summarization, enhancing preprocessing techniques, integrating reinforce- ment learning, and optimizing for scalability and real-time applications. The authors [39] proposed a novel method for ATS in low-resource languages like Urdu using self-attentive transformer-based models. The authors used a custom Urdu news article summary dataset of 76,500 article summary pairs to demonstrate comparable performance to high-resource languages. The urT5 model, developed using multilingual transformer models, outperforms high-resource language models like PEGASUS and BART on the dataset. Despite its small size, the model outperforms high-resource language models like PEGASUS and BART. However, the urT5 model has limitations, including a small dataset, potential biases, and limited applicability across low-resource languages. Future research should expand the Urdu dataset, improve model scalability, apply the urT5 model to low-resource languages, and address biases in pre-trained models for improved performance. The article [40] discusses the challenges in multimodal summarization for low-resource languages like Urdu. Fur- thermore, they recommend the development of diverse, high-quality datasets like UrduMASD. The UrduMASD dataset uses pre-trained multilingual models to improve performance in low-resource languages like Urdu, improving ROUGE scores by 2.6%. The paper explores multimodal abstractive summarization using the UrduMASD dataset, comparing text-only models, text and visual data models, and multimodal fusion models. The study demonstrates that incorporating visual data into multimodal summarization improves ROUGE scores by 2.6%, outperforming existing datasets in terms of abstractity, compression, and coherence. However, the study identifies several limitations, including limited diversity in the dataset, transcription errors, chal- lenges related to code-switching, and potential pretraining requirements. Future research should focus on expanding datasets, enhancing automatic speech recognition systems, optimizing models, and exploring real-world applications. Previous studies on Urdu ATS have made significant progress toward producing concise and coherent sum- maries [39]. However, there is a significant gap in capturing and maintaining the semantics of the original text [41]. The underlying semantics of Urdu text were not successfully extracted and reflected by previously suggested methodolo- gies. It produces inconsistent, ambiguous statements that lack sufficient accuracy as well as clarity. III. MATERIAL & METHODS The proposed model comprises several components, includ- ing text pre-processing using NLP techniques, extracting features, sorting sentences based on weight and token frequency, and producing an abstract summary by the encoder-decoder after receiving the final summary. Figure 5 presents the proposed methodology for abstractive Urdu TS. FIGURE 5. Proposed methodology for abstractive urdu text summarization. A. PRE-PROCESSING NLP-based models mostly start with text preprocessing [42]. For the English language, many NLP-based models are available [43]. When it comes to Arabic, Urdu, Persian, and other closely related languages like Pashto, it is still difficult [44] to find the relevant language model [45]. For instance, punctuation marks clarify sentence meanings, enhancing readability and understanding [46]. Proper punc- tuation improves readability for humans and machines [47]. Similarly, in Urdu text, the absence of punctuation can cause ambiguity, as shown in Figure 6. FIGURE 6. Punctuation of urdu sentence. Figure 7 illustrates the process of tokenization, that is, the breakdown of a text into discrete or unique words [48], whether the text is a sentence, phrase, paragraph, or page. Tokenization uses word order to make the text easier to read [49]. In tokenization, lengthy statements are broken into words and phrases [50], [51]. Tokenizer utilizes exclamation points, question marks, and full stops are examples of paragraph separators [52], [53]. These tokenized phrases form the foundation for fur- ther processing, including the elimination of punctuation. By eliminating punctuation, models may focus on word 104980 VOLUME 13, 2025 W. Fatima et al.: Abstractive Text Summarization in Arabic-Like Script FIGURE 7. Tokenization of urdu sentence. semantics and patterns since the focus is diverted to the text content [54]. Removing punctuation normalizes the con- tent [55]. Moreover, elimination of punctuation is necessary before moving to the next step in our proposed model, which is sentence scoring, because punctuation contributes to unwanted noise in the text. In section III-D, punctuation is added again after sentence scoring for clear visibility. B. SENTENCE SCORING In the proposed methodology, the next step after pre- processing is sentence scoring. EXTRACTIVE SUMMARIES When the pre-initialization process is complete, the charac- teristics of the text are obtained. The technique of selecting important terms and phrases from the source material according to a standard is known as extractive summarizing, and it produces a comprehensive summary that accurately captures the primary idea [30]. For extractive summaries, punctuation plays a significant role in post-processing for clarity, readability, and contextual understanding. The most common technique for adding or removing punctuation is regular regression. Regular regres- sion involves using statistical models that analyze patterns VOLUME 13, 2025 104981 W. Multi-layer transformer encoder for abstractive urdu text summarization. in the text and make decisions where punctuation should be added or removed to improve clarity and readability. In our proposed model, we used regular regression for removing punctuation during pre-processing and added it during extractive summaries. The final step of the extractive summary, after adding the punctuation, is to generate the final summary. In the final summary, the text is clearer and more understandable by adding punctuation in post-processing. The figure 10 shows the final extractive summary that is more read- able and provides fluency, coherence in the generated summary. D. ABSTRACTIVE SUMMARY An encoder-decoder architectural framework will be used in this investigation. The proposed method used a multi-layer encoder instead of a single-layer encoder. There are three hidden state levels in the multi-layer encoder. Words (or tokens) in the input sequence are converted by the embedding layer into dense vectors of a certain size. In Eq. 5 the w represents a word (or token), E(w) represents the appropriate embedding vector, where d refers to the embedding space size. This layer is included in both the encoder and the decoder [59]. E(w) ∈Rd (5) If the word embedding size is d = 256 and the input sentence has 10 words, the output from the embedding layer would be a matrix with a shape of 10 × 256. The encoder in the model uses numerous transformer layers, while the decoder uses a single transformer layer. Multiple stacked transformer layers (e.g., 6–12 layers) make up the encoder, which uses self-attention mechanisms to identify complicated patterns in the incoming data [60]. The vertical stacking in Figure 12 indicates that there are three hidden levels in the encoder. After processing its input, each layer transmits its output to the layer above it. For a given input x, a single multi-head attention layer computes the following: Q, K, and V are linear transformations of the input x, and d k is the dimensionality of the key vectors. Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax QK T √dk  V (6) FFN(x) = max(0, x W1 + b1)W2 + b2 (7) The integration of the multi-layer encoder with the mBART model is illustrated in Figure 12, which shows the overall architecture of the abstractive text summarization. Figure 13 shows the tokenization process for the word, where the input text is broken down into smaller units (tokens) that the model can process. These tokens are converted into dense vector representations through an embedding layer, which the multi-layer encoder processes across several transformer layers, applying self-attention mechanisms to capture complex patterns and relationships within the input data. After processing, the encoder outputs a rich, contextual representation that is essential for generating meaningful summaries. The decoder, also shown in Figure 12, consists of a single transformer layer that generates the output summary, utilizing a multi-head attention mechanism to focus on specific parts of the encoder’s output while producing each word. Attention scores are calculated using the formula in Eq. 8, allowing the decoder’s hidden state to interact with the encoder’s hidden states to determine focus areas. The final output is generated by applying a softmax function to predict the next word based on the decoder’s hidden state and the context vector from the encoder’s output. The generated summary is a concise and coherent representation of the original text, demonstrating the effectiveness of the mBART model in handling the complexities of the Urdu language. 104982 VOLUME 13, 2025 W. The attention of the hidden states from the encoder’s output selects a specific mechanism that determines which parts the decoder should concentrate on to generate each word. This process allows the model to dynamically focus on relevant information and enhance its ability to produce correct translations as an output, which is based on the input sequence [54]. The attention score, where the attention weights for time step (t) are represented by (alphat), is computed by taking the dot product of each encoder’s hidden state and the decoder’s current hidden state [55]. αt = softmax(hdecoder t · hencoder) (8) The encoder’s hidden states are added together and weighted to determine the context vector [56]. ct = X t αthencoder (9) A softmax function is used to predict the next word based on the decoder’s hidden state and context vector [61]. By applying the suggested results, this research guarantees that NLP analysts and experts will get important data when completing the various seq2seq tasks [62]. A multilingual variant of the BART (Bidirectional and Auto-Regressive Transformers) model is called the mBART model [63]. It combines the benefits of pre-trained sequence- to-sequence models with the ability to handle many lan- guages [64], making it helpful for tasks like translation, summarization, and paraphrasing in a multilingual setting. Figure 17 indicates the working of the mBART architec- ture [39], where tokens are smaller units made from the input text. Tokenizers, most frequently sub-word tokenizers like BPE or WordPiece, are used for this. Tokenization breaks up text into smaller, more digestible chunks so that the model can process and comprehend a wider range of languages and uncommon words, as shown in Figure 13. The mBART model addresses out-of-vocabulary (OOV) issues through subword tokenization, which breaks down rare or unseen words into familiar subunits, improv- ing generalization and reducing OOV rates. Its multilin- gual pretraining further enhances this by using a shared vocabulary and enabling cross-lingual transfer, benefiting low-resource languages like Urdu that share linguistic roots with others in the training corpus. Additionally, token embeddings map even unfamiliar subwords into a dense semantic space, allowing the model to infer meaning from context and generate coherent representations for novel inputs. Tokenization assigns a dense vector representation to each token ID, translating it into a numerical repre- sentation using a token embedding matrix (E) of size ((vocabsize, embeddingdim)) [65]. Token embeddings enable the model to comprehend context by encapsulating the semantic meanings and relationships among tokens. The token embedding for each token is shown in Figure 14. FIGURE 14. Token embedding. FIGURE 15. Positional embedding. FIGURE 16. Final embedding. Positional embeddings are added to the token embeddings to indicate each token’s position in the sequence. Positional embeddings provide the essential details on the sequence’s structure since transformers lack a core comprehension of token order. The model adds positional embeddings to maintain token order, as shown in Figure 15. Suppose, we have a positional embedding matrix (P) of shape ((max_seq_len, embedding_dim)), where the max- imum sequence length is represented by (maxseqlen). The token and positional embeddings are multiplied to provide the final input embeddings, as shown in Figure 16. The formula for the final embeddings for each token is [66]. Final Embedding = Token Embedding + Positional Embedding (10) The encoder processes embedded embeddings through transformer layers, utilizing the Self-Attention Process to VOLUME 13, 2025 104983 W. The decoder generates tokens sequentially using masked self-attention and cross- attention, focusing on encoder outputs. It starts with a sequence length of one and a start token of [68], ensuring it relies on earlier tokens and generates tokens sequentially. The decoder layers generate a probability distribution across the vocabulary using the encoder output and the preceding token as input at each step [69]. Figure 17 processes input FIGURE 17. Bart architecture. data through token embeddings and position embeddings. The model uses tokens to represent the structure of the input, position embeddings to encode the positional information of tokens, and summation to create a single representation. It uses multiple layers of transformer blocks to process these embeddings, combining both encoder and decoder architec- tures. The final output, (Y1, Y2, Y3), reflects a context-aware representation generated by the model, suitable for various natural language processing tasks. The mBART model is used for fine-tuning the summarization task of TS in Urdu. The model is trained using a dataset specifically designed for Urdu as part of this fine-tuning process, which enables it to modify its parameters to capture the language’s nuances and semantics. mBART is not used without modification; it is adjusted during the fine-tuning process to enhance its performance for the specific requirements of Urdu TS. Table 2 highlights the novelty and specificity of the study by clearly contrasting the proposed method with baseline CNNs across multiple dimensions. It shows that the TABLE 3. Data collections from various sources. proposed encoder-decoder architecture enables abstractive output with fluent, context-aware generation, unlike the layered, extractive, and locally focused CNNs. The method demonstrates higher adaptability to morphological complex- ity and achieves better performance with higher ROUGE scores. This highlights the model’s quality and creative contribution to the area by demonstrating not just a technical improvement in addressing long-range relationships but also enhanced readability and flexibility. IV. EXPERIMENT AND OBTAINED RESULTS This section evaluates the effectiveness of the proposed summarizing approach using a number of tests. In section A, the datasets are described. Section B explains the evaluation measures. The setup of the experiments is covered in Section C. In section D, the experiments, findings, and discussion are presented. Section E concludes with a com- parison with other comparable systems. A. DATA SETS This study utilizes a dataset of 1 million news articles, organized into four categories: Science & Technology, Enter- tainment, Sports, and Business & Economics. It includes lengthy news articles with brief descriptions. The model was trained using 80% of the data, with the remaining 20% being utilized for testing. It is an enormous Urdu dataset accessible for solving natural language processing-related challenges. The data collected from various resources is shown in Table 3. The source code for the project has been uploaded to GitHub, providing access to the implementation details and allowing others to replicate or build upon the work. The dataset is publicly available at Mendeley.1 B. EVALUATION MEASURES The ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Eval- uation) measure is used in the quantitative evaluation in addition to the metrics recommended in this study [70]. It comprises a set of measures for the automatic assessment of text summarization. The results show that dual and multiple-encoder models outperform single-encoder models in terms of performance. ROUGE1, ROUGE2, ROUGE- L, and BLEU score measures are used to assess the final summary [71]. These metrics are calculated using the 1https://data.mendeley.com/datasets/834vsxnb99/3 104984 VOLUME 13, 2025 W. Fatima et al.: Abstractive Text Summarization in Arabic-Like Script following formulas [72]. Precision (P) = Number of Overlapping Unigrams Total words in Automatic Summary (11) ROUGE-1: Recall (R) = Number of Overlapping Unigrams Total words in Reference Summary (12) F1 = 2(P × R) P + R (13) ROUGE-2: Precision (P) = Number of Overlapping Bigrams Total words in Automatic Summary (14) Recall (R) = Number of Overlapping Bigrams Total words in Reference Summary (15) F1 = 2(P × R) P + R (16) ROUGE-L: LCS = max{s1, s2, s3, . . . , sn} (17) Precision (P) = |LCS| Total words in Automatic Summary (18) Recall (R) = |LCS| Total words in Reference Summary (19) F1 = 2(P × R) P + R (20) Using the BLEU score (BiLingual Evaluation Understudy), the model’s performance is further assessed [73]. The ROUGE score is mirrored in the BLEU score. Its main appli- cation is in machine translation tasks, while summarization tasks have also been found to be evaluated with it [74]. To compute the BLEU score, one must calculate the number of n-grams in the candidate sentence that match the reference phrase. The following equation represents the shortness penalty BP, n-gram precisions pn, n-gram length N, and positive weights wn [75]. BLEU = BP · exp N X n=1 wn log pn ! (21) The BERTScore is used as an evaluation measure for text summarization, as it considers implicit semantics and does not calculate matching between candidate and reference sentences [76]. It is based on BERT contextual embeddings and determines token similarity, similar to human assessment. BERTScore is a good choice for our assignment, with FBERT for F-measure, RBERT for recall, and PBERT for accuracy. This score is similar to assessments made by humans [77]. FBERT = 2 · (PBERT × RBERT) PBERT + RBERT (22) C. EXPERIMENTS SETUP AND TOOLS To implement various tasks, including sentence representa- tion and feature extraction, we mostly utilized Java. Because the tools we chose are Java-based and are published as JAR files, we also utilized Java to integrate the tools. Table 5 provides an overview of the strategies, resources, and constraints used in the recommended approach and outlines the parameters used in our system. D. OBTAINED RESULTS For instance, the suggested extent ratio for summaries should be between 33 and 40 percent, while some summaries have a size ratio of up to 80 percent of the given content. Figure 21 illustrates how the roughly thirty articles are divided into various areas. The evaluation paper had 10 to 21 lines of Urdu text. Figure 18 illustrates how the summary was produced using a sentence weight technique that included 6 to 7 lines, or over 50% of the whole article. Using the TF-IDF approach and sentence weight, the most significant sentences were organized and classified. As a result, the created summary kept the core ideas of the original text, which had 5 to 6 lines, or around 51% of the source document depicted in Figure 19. The produced summary was observed to have a combination of random and meaningful statements. The longest and most wordy statements were chosen. FIGURE 18. Input source test document. FIGURE 19. After that, this summary is used to construct the abstractive summary, which makes up half of the extractive summary. VOLUME 13, 2025 104985 W. The encoder-decoder model must employ the pre-trained model mBART to generate the abstractive summary. The abstractive summary demonstrates how a variety of additional vocabulary could be used in the proposed system to translate phrases written in Urdu. The abstractive summary that is produced could be described as brief and compact. As seen in Figure 20, this abstractive summary comprises 20% of the original test document. Figure 21 shows a comparison between the number of lines in the model’s output summary and the number of lines in the raw input text. The above illustration shows how well the summarizing method works to reduce lengthy texts to manageable summaries. The model can capture important information while removing extraneous details if there is a noticeable decrease in the number of lines, which is an indication of good summarizing. Table 6 evaluates the model’s accuracy across metrics like BERTScore, ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L. The model achieves an F-measure of 78%, precision of 98%, and recall of 64%, indicating high accuracy in capturing relevant text. It also achieves 70% F-measure, 55% recall, and 97% precision for bigrams, indicating its ability to preserve brief phrases but potentially losing broader con- textual connections. The model maintains the original text’s organization and structure, achieving 78% F-measure, 64% recall, and 98% precision. The summaries are semantically rich, maintaining the text’s main ideas. The model’s high precision in all measures indicates its ability to produce concise summaries, making it suitable for situations requiring it, and its high BERTScore suggests its ability to produce high-quality, semantically correct text summaries. title: Gan et al. EPJ Data Science            (2026) 15:6
file_name: Addressing-investor-concerns-a-Chinese-financial-questionanswering-benchmark-with-LLMbased-evaluation_2026_Springer-Science-and-Business-Media-Deutschland-GmbH.pdf
text: Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 https://doi.org/10.1140/epjds/s13688-025-00601-6 R ES EA RCH Open Access Addressing investor concerns: a Chinese ﬁnancial question-answering benchmark with LLM-based evaluation Yujian Gan1,4†, Yiyi Tao3†, Jiawang Mo1, Xianzheng Huang1, Yiwen Li1, Kexin Wang1, Yi Cai1, Lu Liang1, Shuzhen Xiong1, Qi Ke2*, Hua Zheng2 and Xiaochun Hu5 Handling Editor: Giancarlo Ruﬀo *Correspondence: keqi@gxufe.edu.cn 2Guangxi Key Laboratory of Seaward Economic Intelligent System Analysis and Decision-making, Guangxi University of Finance and Economics, Nanning, 530003, China †These authors contributed equally to this work. The order of authorship was determined by coin toss Full list of author information is available at the end of the article Abstract In recent years, large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance across various natural language processing tasks and are increasingly adopted in high-stakes ﬁelds such as ﬁnancial analysis. However, their eﬀectiveness in Chinese ﬁnancial contexts is hindered by the scarcity of high-quality, domain-speciﬁc datasets. To bridge this gap, we present the Chinese Financial Question Answering (CFQA) dataset, a novel resource designed to advance research in ﬁnancial analysis. CFQA is constructed from publicly available annual reports of multiple Chinese listed companies, paired with corresponding questions and human-annotated answers. Evaluation results reveal that existing QA methods perform poorly on this dataset. CFQA introduces several unique challenges: (1) source documents are in PDF format with complex tabular structures, making information extraction diﬃcult; (2) the length and intricacy of ﬁnancial reports complicate answer retrieval; and (3) the questions are tightly focused on domain-speciﬁc ﬁnancial content. Keywords: Financial natural language processing; Large language models; Retrieval-augmented generation; Financial benchmark datasets 1 Introduction The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has opened new avenues for au- tomating complex tasks across various domains. One such domain that stands to beneﬁt signiﬁcantly is ﬁnancial analysis, a broad ﬁeld encompassing a wide range of activities such as investment evaluation, risk assessment, credit analysis, and corporate strategy develop- ment. Among these, ﬁnancial statement analysis—which focuses on the careful examina- tion of companies’ annual and quarterly reports—plays a central role. These reports con- tain detailed quantitative and qualitative information critical for evaluating a company’s ﬁnancial health, operational eﬃciency, and future prospects. Given the structured-yet- complex nature of ﬁnancial statements, this area presents a rich and challenging testbed for evaluating and enhancing the reasoning capabilities of LLMs in real-world, high-stakes decision-making contexts. © The Author(s) 2025. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/ 4.0/. Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 Page 2 of 22 Traditionally, ﬁnancial statement analysis has been a labor-intensive process requiring expert knowledge and signiﬁcant manual eﬀort. LLMs have the potential to augment and automate these labor-intensive parts of ﬁnancial analysis due to their remarkable capa- bilities in natural language understanding, reasoning and writing [1, 2]. However, there remains a signiﬁcant gap in the availability of specialized datasets that can rigorously eval- uate current LLMs’ performance in ﬁnancial analysis tasks. Most existing datasets are in English and often lack the complexity and speciﬁcity re- quired for training and testing LLMs in ﬁnancial analysis. One such dataset, FiQA [3], focuses on aspect-based sentiment analysis and opinionated question answering for ﬁnan- cial data. On the other hand, FinQA [4] emphasizes numerical reasoning and document- based analysis, becoming a widely accepted benchmark for ﬁnancial Natural Language Processing (NLP). Building on FinQA, TAT-QA [5] further expands the context by com- bining numerical reasoning with both tabular data and textual data from 182 ﬁnancial reports. With 16,552 question-answer pairs, TAT-QA is one of the largest datasets for reasoning over ﬁnancial documents. However, these datasets primarily focus on sentiment analysis or numerical reason- ing tasks, often overlooking other critical aspects of ﬁnancial reports, such as manage- ment discussions, market conditions, and emerging ﬁnancial trends, which involve more complex and nuanced information. In particular, within the Chinese ﬁnancial domain, there remains a signiﬁcant lack of high-quality, large-scale ﬁnancial question-answering datasets. Due to substantial diﬀerences in accounting systems, reporting standards, and the expression of ﬁnancial terminology across countries, existing work cannot simply rely on translating English datasets to meet the needs of Chinese ﬁnancial NLP. Existing Chinese ﬁnancial datasets primarily focus on sentiment analysis and event ex- traction. For instance, Gu [6] and Mao et al. [7] both constructed ﬁnancial sentiment anal- ysis datasets by crawling major ﬁnancial news websites. Their focus on sentiment analysis, though valuable, limits their applicability to broader ﬁnancial applications. Additionally, Li et al. [8] created a ﬁnancial event dataset, FEED, which includes 31,748 company an- nouncements and 46,960 ﬁnancial event mentions. However, the dataset deﬁnes events narrowly, focusing on discrete ﬁnancial actions like Equity Repurchase and Equity Freeze, each tied to ﬁxed roles. This overlooks many other company-relevant dynamics and limits its support for deeper, multi-event reasoning. While these eﬀorts provide valuable foun- dations, they fall short in supporting more interactive and comprehensive tasks such as question answering over Chinese ﬁnancial reports. To address this gap, we construct a cross-company, cross-year Chinese Question Answering dataset, CFQA, based on annual reports of listed companies, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Our CFQA dataset includes recent pub- licly available ﬁnancial reports, paired with questions, human-annotated answers, and the speciﬁc page numbers within the PDF where the answers can be found. This ﬁne-grained annotation not only enables evaluation of QA systems, but also serves as a strong bench- mark for document-level information retrieval in ﬁnancial PDFs. Our primary contributions are as follows: • Large-scale Chinese ﬁnancial QA dataset: We annotate the ﬁrst large-scale Chinese ﬁnancial QA dataset. Based on it, we deﬁne two tasks: (1) Financial Report QA, where the model must identify and read the relevant report to generate an answer; (2) Page Retrieval, where the model must locate the correct page containing the answer. Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 Page 3 of 22 Figure 1 An example of one record on our Chinese Financial Question Answering dataset. Each record in CFQA comprises a ﬁnancial question pertaining to a speciﬁc company, a reference answer, the page number from which the answer is sourced, and the corresponding annual report • LLM-based automated evaluation: We propose an automated evaluation pipeline using LLMs to compare human annotations with model-generated answers. Our consistency analysis shows up to 91% agreement with human evaluations, demonstrating the reliability of our automated evaluation. • Baseline evaluation and insights: We evaluate several existing methods on our CFQA dataset and ﬁnd that they perform poorly on Chinese ﬁnancial QA tasks, revealing ample room for future research in this domain. 2 Related works 2.1 Financial NLP Financial NLP has become an increasingly important research area due to its wide-ranging applications in areas such as fraud detection [9, 10], sentiment analysis [11, 12], and opinion-based question answering [13]. This ﬁeld has also seen advancements in task- speciﬁc datasets, domain-speciﬁc model architectures, and evaluation benchmarks tai- lored to the ﬁnancial domain. In recent years, LLMs speciﬁcally designed for ﬁnance have gained signiﬁcant attention. BloombergGPT [14] has set new standards in ﬁnancial NLP by achieving state-of-the- art results across multiple ﬁnancial benchmarks. Similarly, FinGPT [15], an open-source and data-centric ﬁnancial LLM, emphasizes adaptability by leveraging diverse ﬁnancial datasets to cater to real-world applications. Further, ConFIRM [16] represents a notable step forward in conversational ﬁnancial NLP, providing a robust framework for informa- tion retrieval and question answering within ﬁnancial contexts. Our CFQA dataset provides a standardized benchmark to evaluate how well LLMs un- derstand and reason over complex ﬁnancial texts in Chinese, addressing current gaps in multilingual ﬁnancial QA research. By incorporating diverse question types, it can push Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 Page 4 of 22 the development of models capable of deep semantic understanding, cross-sentence rea- soning, and contextual interpretation, thereby improving performance on real-world ﬁ- nancial NLP tasks. 2.2 Retrieval-augmented generation Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a transformative framework in long-form question answering and broader natural language processing (NLP) applica- tions by leveraging external knowledge bases to enhance contextual understanding and response generation. RAG combines retrieval mechanisms with generative models, allow- ing for the integration of rich, external data without the need for extensive task-speciﬁc retraining [17]. This enables language models to deliver factually grounded and contextu- ally relevant outputs, surpassing traditional end-to-end generative systems. The development of RAG frameworks can be broadly categorized into three phases: Ba- sic RAG, Advanced RAG, and Modular RAG. Basic RAG introduces the foundational ap- proach of augmenting generative models with retrieval capabilities, improving eﬃciency and accuracy but facing challenges in scalability and dynamic knowledge integration. Ad- vanced RAG addresses these issues by reﬁning retrieval pipelines through advanced in- dexing methods, adaptive embedding strategies, and improved reranking mechanisms, enabling domain-speciﬁc optimization and better context-aware generation [18]. Modu- lar RAG further expands on these advancements by introducing specialized components tailored to diverse use cases, such as memory-augmented retrieval, query reﬁnement, and fusion techniques. Innovations like dynamic retrieval-routing and knowledge distillation modules have demonstrated signiﬁcant improvements in both retrieval accuracy and gen- eration quality [19]. Our proposed CFQA dataset oﬀers a valuable benchmark to evaluate RAG’s eﬀective- ness in enhancing the ﬁnancial reasoning capabilities of LLMs. By enabling targeted re- trieval from corporate annual reports, RAG-equipped models can deliver more accurate, grounded responses to complex ﬁnancial queries involving company-speciﬁc and time- based analyses. Moreover, the dataset supports ﬁne-grained analysis of component-level RAG performance, such as retrieval precision and reranking eﬀectiveness. 2.3 Financial analysis datasets High-quality datasets play a critical role in training and evaluating LLMs for ﬁnancial anal- ysis, enabling the assessment of tasks such as question answering, reading comprehension, logical reasoning, and information retrieval [20–22]. A variety of datasets and benchmarks have been developed to address the growing complexity of ﬁnancial NLP tasks. FiQA [23], an open challenge dataset, focuses on aspect-based sentiment analysis and opinionated question answering for ﬁnancial data. Despite its contributions, FiQA is limited in scope, as sentiment analysis represents only a small portion of the questions ﬁnancial analysts pose about companies. Addressing more complex scenarios, FinQA [4] provides over 8000 high-quality question-answer pairs curated by ﬁnancial experts. It emphasizes numerical reasoning and document-based analysis, establishing itself as a standard benchmark in ﬁ- nancial NLP. Building on FinQA, ConvFinQA [24] introduces a conversational dimension with multi-turn interactions, where the understanding of each question often depends on the context of prior exchanges. This dataset includes 3892 conversations with 14,115 questions, simulating real-world ﬁnancial analysis tasks that require iterative reasoning. Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 Page 5 of 22 Similarly, TAT-QA [5] emphasizes numerical reasoning and extends the challenge by in- tegrating both tabular and textual data from 182 ﬁnancial reports, comprising a total of 16,552 question-answer pairs. However, most existing benchmarks are built on English-language sources and rely heavily on structured or semi-structured data. In contrast, our work introduces a large- scale CFQA dataset grounded in real-world annual reports of listed companies. Unlike prior datasets, our CFQA benchmark supports both ﬁne-grained document retrieval and open-domain question answering, with human-annotated answers and page-level refer- ences in full-length PDFs. This not only ﬁlls a critical gap in multilingual ﬁnancial QA research but also introduces new challenges involving language-speciﬁc reasoning, docu- ment structure understanding, and cross-document retrieval in the ﬁnancial domain. 3 Task deﬁnition We propose the CFQA dataset to support structured information extraction and com- prehension from ﬁnancial documents. It is designed around two core tasks—Financial Information Retrieval (FIR) and Financial Question Answering (FQA)—as illustrated in Fig. 2. FQA is designed to emulate the capability of a human analyst in interpreting ﬁnancial documents and generating precise, contextually grounded answers to user queries. This task requires models to process lengthy, domain-speciﬁc texts and produce free-form an- swers that are directly supported by the source content. Formally, let D = {d1,d2,...,dn} denote a multi-page company annual report, q ∈Q represent a natural language ﬁnancial question pertaining to the company and a ∈A be the corresponding natural language an- swer. The FQA task requires the model to produce the answer a that is both accurate and contextually supported by the source document D. FQA requires the model to answer user questions accurately based on ﬁnancial reports. Given a multi-page annual report D = {d1,d2,...,dn} and a ﬁnancial question q ∈Q, the model is expected to generate a free-form answer a ∈A that is both correct and contextu- ally grounded in the content of D. Although FQA focuses on answer generation, its eﬀec- tiveness often relies on the accuracy of the FIR step. If the model fails to retrieve relevant Figure 2 Illustration of Financial Information Retrieval and Financial Question Answering Tasks on our CFQA Dataset Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 Page 6 of 22 content, it may struggle to produce correct responses. In practice, some systems bypass retrieval altogether by feeding the full PDF into large language models, but this approach is typically more computationally expensive and less interpretable. 4 Dataset We provide the CFQA dataset designed to train and evaluate models’ capabilities in an- swering questions based on ﬁnancial reports. This CFQA dataset includes annual reports of Chinese listed companies from 2019 to 2022, along with related questions. This section details the construction of our CFQA dataset, including the data collection and annotation process, as well as the quality control measures undertaken to ensure its reliability. 4.1 Data collection and preprocessing Our CFQA dataset consists of annual reports from multiple Chinese listed companies, paired with investor questions and their corresponding answers, covering the years 2019 to 2022. The reports were sourced from publicly available ﬁnancial databases and com- pany websites. In addition to the annual reports, we also provide a structured JSON ﬁle containing the annotated data. Each entry in this ﬁle includes the following ﬁelds: 1) Stock Code – the stock code of the listed company; 2) Company – the name of the company; 3) Question – the ﬁnancial question posed regarding the annual report; 4) Gold Answer – the answer provided by human annotators; 5) Answer Source – the page numbers in the annual report where the answer can be found. An example data entry is illustrated in Fig. 1. We source investor questions from the China Investor Forum,1 a platform where in- vestors post real-world inquiries. Questions that could reasonably be answered based on the content of ﬁnancial reports were retained, while others—such as those requiring real- time stock analysis or subjective opinions—were excluded. The questions span diverse ﬁ- nancial topics, including company performance, strategic initiatives, and governance. To increase coverage, we reused questions with shared intents (e.g., proﬁtability) by replacing company-speciﬁc mentions, allowing the same question template to apply across multiple companies. In addition to collecting questions, we also gathered answers from ChatPDF.2 These responses were used to support the development of automatic evaluation systems and to cross-check human annotations. 4.2 Dataset annotation We construct question-answer pairs that reﬂect real-world investor concerns, covering both straightforward factual questions and more complex queries requiring numerical reasoning or logical inference. All answers were manually annotated by a team of 7 ac- counting students, who underwent two months of training in interpreting ﬁnancial re- ports and ﬁnancial terminology to ensure annotation consistency and quality. To ensure answer quality, we adopt a three-step validation process. First, a human anno- tator reads the ﬁnancial report and writes an answer based on the given question. Second, the answer is cross-checked by another annotator for consistency and correctness. Finally, the annotated answer is compared against the output from ChatPDF to identify potential 1https://www.investor.org.cn. 2Data collection was conducted in 2023, during which ChatPDF (https://www.chatpdf.com) operated as a GPT-3.5-based PDF QA platform. Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 Page 7 of 22 errors or omissions. Annotators also assess whether the ChatPDF response is correct and label it accordingly. In addition to manual validation, we applied automated quality checks to identify duplicate questions, verify company names and stock codes, and standardize the question-answer format. 4.3 Dataset splitting To evaluate model performance from diﬀerent generalization perspectives, we adopt two complementary dataset splitting strategies: year-based split and company-based split. Each split is designed to test the model’s robustness under diﬀerent distributional shifts— temporal and entity-level, respectively. Year-Based Split. This setting assesses the model’s ability to make forward-looking pre- dictions using past data. The training set includes samples from 2019 to 2021, while 2022 data is held out for testing. This simulates a realistic ﬁnancial forecasting scenario, where models trained on historical reports are evaluated on the most recent ﬁscal year. The dataset is split into 9183 training, 1327 development, and 3065 test samples, adhering to a 65%/10%/25% split ratio. Company-Based Split. This setting evaluates the model’s generalization across compa- nies. The dataset is partitioned such that companies in the test set do not appear in the training set, allowing us to examine whether the model can transfer knowledge learned from one set of companies to unseen ones. It includes 9113 training, 1211 development, and 3251 test samples, also following a similar ratio. Together, these two splits oﬀer a more comprehensive benchmark: the year-based split focuses on temporal generalization, while the company-based split highlights cross-entity robustness. 4.4 Dataset statistics Our CFQA Dataset includes a total of 13,643 real-world investor questions, each paired with a corresponding answer. The CFQA dataset includes a total of 516 annual reports, spanning from 2019 to 2022, and covers a diverse set of publicly listed Chinese companies. As shown in Table 1, the CFQA dataset exhibits signiﬁcant variability in both question and answer complexity. On average, questions contain 22.22 tokens, while answers span an average of 67.85 tokens. The maximum question length reaches 66 tokens, whereas the most detailed answers extend up to 10,701 tokens, highlighting the need for models capable of processing long and information-dense text. Each answer is typically grounded in approximately 1.06 pages of the annual report, with some spanning up to 36 pages. Furthermore, answers reference an average of 0.78 distinct sources, with a maximum of 8 sources (i.e., sets of pages), indicating that some answers can be derived from multiple locations within the report. Table 1 Detailed Statistics of the CFQA Dataset: Question-Answer Lengths, Report Characteristics, and Coverage Total Questions 13,643 Total Reports 516 Questions Without Reports 1000 Unanswerable Questions 2065 Avg. Question Length 22.22 Max Question Length 66 Avg. Answer Length 67.85 Max Answer Length 10,701 Avg. Pages per Answer 1.06 Max Pages per Answer 36 Avg. Sources per Answer 0.78 Max Sources per Answer 8 Avg. Report Page Count 232 Max Report Page Count 398 Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 Page 8 of 22 While most questions are grounded in the content of annual reports, there are cases where answers could not be located. Speciﬁcally, 2065 questions have no answer found in the report, and 1000 questions are linked to companies for which the relevant report was unavailable. These cases highlight the real-world challenges of incomplete information or inaccessible documents. The ﬁnancial documents themselves are extensive: the average annual report comprises 232 pages, with some reports reaching as many as 398 pages. This imposes signiﬁcant challenges on retrieval-augmented and long-context models. 4.5 Question taxonomy To better illustrate the diversity and complexity of CFQA, we conduct a manual analysis of a random sample of 300 questions and identify four major types that frequently occur in ﬁnancial QA tasks: • A. Direct lookup (fact-based). Answers can be directly located in the annual report with minimal reasoning eﬀort (e.g., ‘What was ZTE’s operating revenue in 2022?”). This type accounts for 71 out of 300 questions (23.7%). • B. Indirect computation (reasoning or multi-step numeric). Answers are not explicitly stated but can be derived by combining or calculating ﬁgures reported in the document (e.g., ‘What proportion of intangible assets did Huatai Securities hold in 2020?”). This type accounts for 66 out of 300 questions (22.0%). • C. Textual summarization (descriptive reasoning). Questions require synthesizing non-numeric, content-rich information from multiple sections of the report (e.g., ‘Which business lines were included in Trina Solar’s photovoltaic segment in 2021?”). This type accounts for 89 out of 300 questions (29.7%). • D. Extended inference or open-ended reasoning (beyond explicit mentions). Answers are rarely stated verbatim and may require contextual or temporal reasoning, or recognizing that the report does not provide explicit evidence (e.g., ‘Did Jiayuan Technology consider peer-industry acquisitions in 2019?”). This type accounts for 74 out of 300 questions (24.7%). This taxonomy highlights the varied reasoning demands in CFQA: A-type questions test factual retrieval, B emphasizes numerical reasoning and table understanding, C focuses on semantic comprehension and summarization, and D challenges models with cross-page inference and implicit knowledge. Such categorization helps reveal the dataset’s diversity and supports more ﬁne-grained evaluation of model reasoning capabilities. 5 Metrics To comprehensively assess the performance of Q&A models, we employ a set of evaluation metrics that measure both the eﬀectiveness of information retrieval and the precision of the content generated. The evaluation consists of two key components: the retrieval score and the content score. 5.1 Retrieval score To evaluate the model’s ability to retrieve the page numbers from the annual report that contain the information needed to answer the questions, we use the Normalized Dis- counted Cumulative Gain metric (NDCG) [25]. Prior to evaluation, the retrieval model generates a ranked list of the top n pages most relevant to the question. The NDCG met- ric is then applied to score the result based on the rank position of the target page, with Gan et al. EPJ Data Science (2026) 15:6 Page 9 of 22 higher positions yielding higher scores. For example, if the answer set is [[4,5],[10]], then it means that the answer can be fully veriﬁed by information on page 4 and page 5 or by a single page 10. In this case, the relevance score for pages 4 and 5 is 1/2, and the relevance score for page 10 is 1. If the answer set is empty, indicating that the information required to answer the question cannot be found in the annual report, then the corresponding record should be excluded from the NDCG calculation. Suppose P is a subset of pages in the answer set A, the relevance score(rel) for a page p is deﬁne as: rel(p) = ⎧ ⎨ ⎩ 1 |P| if p ∈P and P ∈A 0 otherwise (1) Suppose reli is the relevance score of the page at rank i, the Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG) [25] at rank k is calculated as: DCG@k = k ∑︂ i=1 reli log2(i + 1) (2) Suppose R is the list of relevant pages in the ideal order, the Ideal Discounted Cumulative Gain (IDCG) [25] at rank k is the DCG value for the ideal ordering of pages: IDCG@k = |R| ∑︂ i=1 reli log2(i + 1) (3) The NDCG at rank k is then calculated as: NDCG@k = DCG@k IDCG@k (4) In addition to NDCG scores, we also employ the Hit@K and Recall@K metrics [26] to gain deeper insight into how eﬀectively relevant pages are being retrieved. Hit@K as- sesses whether at least one relevant item appears in the top-K retrieved results, indicating a successful retrieval. Recall@K, on the other hand, measures the proportion of all rele- vant items retrieved within the top-K, reﬂecting the system’s ability to comprehensively capture relevant content. 5.2 Content score The content of the Q&A model’s answers is assessed by comparing them with the refer- ence answers provided by human annotators. The objective is to determine whether the model-generated responses convey the same semantic meaning and critical information as the human-provided answers, even if the wording diﬀers. To achieve this, we employ an LLM evaluation model to assess the semantic consistency between the model-generated answers and the human-labeled

file_name: Agentic AI Arrives How Gen Z Adopts Autonomous AI Agents.pdf
text: Received 31 January 2026, accepted 12 February 2026, date of publication 16 February 2026, date of current version 23 February 2026. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3665348 Agentic AI Arrives: How Gen Z Adopts Autonomous AI Agents MARC HASSELWANDER 1 AND OLIVER LAH1,2,3 1Urban Living Lab Center, Technische Universität Berlin, 10623 Berlin, Germany 2Urban Electric Mobility Initiative (UEMI), a UN-Habitat Action Platform, 10437 Berlin, Germany 3Urban Living Lab Center, Wuppertal Institute for Climate, Environment and Energy, 10115 Berlin, Germany Corresponding author: Marc Hasselwander (hasselwander@tu-berlin.de) This work was supported in part by the Gemini Project under European Union’s Horizon Innovation Actions Program under Grant 101103801. ABSTRACT In the era of agentic artificial intelligence (AI), autonomous systems capable of operating in the background and intelligently performing tasks on behalf of users are rapidly emerging. However, little is known about the factors influencing user adoption of agentic systems. This study analyzes adoption intentions toward AI agents among members of Generation Z (born in 1997 or later), who are often described as early adopters of new technologies. The analysis is based on data collected through an online survey in Germany with 243 participants. Despite being the generation most accustomed to digital technologies, only about half of the respondents (49.4%) have previously interacted with agentic AI. Nevertheless, nearly 39.6% expressed interest in engaging with such technology, while only 11.1% would not want to use agentic AI at all. The PLS-SEM analysis revealed that perceived usefulness was the strongest predictor of intention to use, while perceived risk had both a significant negative direct effect on intention and an indirect effect via trust. A key finding is that although respondents expressed concerns about data privacy and reliability, (the lack of) trust did not significantly influence their intention to use AI agents. The NCA results further suggest that perceived usefulness and perceived humanness are necessary conditions for user adoption. These insights contribute to a deeper understanding of Gen Z’s attitudes toward AI agents and can inform strategies to accelerate the uptake as this technology continues to mature. We also discuss managerial and policy recommendations to guide responsible, transparent, and trustworthy application of agentic AI in society. INDEX TERMS Agentic AI, AI super assistants (AISA), human–AI interaction, AI adoption, Gen Z. I. INTRODUCTION Artificial intelligence (AI) has become deeply embedded in everyday life, supporting tasks such as information retrieval, content curation, and decision-making across per- sonal, professional, and institutional contexts [1]. Yet recent technological advances mark a profound shift: from reactive, task-specific systems to agentic AI, defined as autonomous, goal-oriented agents capable of planning, acting, and learn- ing with minimal human input [2]. Compared to established conversational AI chatbots, agentic systems like ChatGPT (agent mode), Claude (Chrome extension), and Manus act autonomously to complete multi-step processes rather than The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Wei Wang . merely responding to prompts. As a result, they enable users to accomplish more complex tasks with less direct effort [3]. Despite its growing relevance and real-world impact, empir- ical research on whether and why consumers would adopt agentic AI systems remains scarce. To the best of our knowl- edge, only one study has so far examined acceptance of highly autonomous AI agents – termed AI super assistants (AISA) – in the Philippines, identifying both drivers and barriers of adoption [4]. The findings suggest that users do not perceive highly autonomous AI agents as overwhelming but rather feel capable of engaging with their new functionalities and comfortable with increased levels of agency. Moreover, acceptance appears to be driven by positive experiential and performance-related expectations. However, the study only assessed stated p
