SiReRAG achieved approximately 5% higher average F1 scores than RAPTOR. SiReRAG also showed an improvement of up to 8.3% in F1 scores on MuSiQue compared to HippoRAG. Apart from indexing, another important aspect of a RAG pipeline is the rerankers. The reranking framework proposed in [21] improves retrieval quality in RAG systems. It effectively integrates user intent prediction to optimize the RAG process. Evaluation on two benchmark datasets demonstrates enhanced personalization and relevance in dynamic user query scenarios. A lot of the technologies involved in the RAG system depend on the language. Researchers in [22] conducted a comparative analysis of rerankers with first-stage retrieval methods in Polish. Based on the results, most rerankers failed to outperform the first-stage retrieval methods, with only two models achieving higher average NDCG10 values. The best- performing model, mt5-13b-mmarco-100k, set a new state- of-the-art for reranking in Polish, outperforming existing models with up to 30 times more parameters. It was also observed in [22] that some datasets, such as PolEval and MAUPQA, were easier for rerankers, whereas others, such as Web Datasets and BEIR-PL, posed significant challenges due to their data diversity. [15] shows that reranking is crucial for RAG with Large Language Models, as G-RAG outperforms PaLM 2 significantly. Also, not just reranking, but other parameters such as chunking and indexing are also very important for RAG performance, as shown in [23]. Response fidelity with dynamic chunking and re-ranking improved RAG performance, also showing the significant impact of index choice on performance metrics. In RAG systems, there are always challenges in text retrieval pipelines, particularly trade-offs among model size, ranking accuracy, and system requirements such as indexing and serving latency/throughput. Based on the results from the [24], smaller embedding models like snowflake- arctic-embed-l and NV-EmbedQA-e5-v5 showed consider- able improvement in ranking accuracy when combined with cross-encoders. The NV-RerankQA-Mistral-4B-v3 reranker achieved a significant accuracy increase of approximately 14% compared to other rerankers in the benchmark. For the larger NV-EmbedQA-Mistral7B-v2 embedding model, only the NV-RerankQA-Mistral-4B-v3 reranker improved its accuracy. The NV-RerankQA-Mistral-4B-v3 provided the highest ranking accuracy across all datasets evaluated. The paper [25] aims to provide insights for researchers and practitioners on leveraging RAG effectively to improve text generation tasks. [25] reviews the foundational principles and architecture of retrieval augmented generation (RAG) in natural language processing. [25] highlights the two-step RAG process, which involves retrieving relevant information from the input query and generating text informed by both the query and the retrieved knowledge. It emphasizes integrating retrieved knowledge into the generative model to enhance text quality and contextual relevance. The studies discussed in this section show that although significant advancements have been made in improving retrieval, reranking, and indexing strategies for RAG systems, no single strategy consistently prevails across datasets, languages, and system limitations. Improvements in relevance or accuracy may entail trade-offs in latency, cost, or scalability, and performance gains are often dataset-dependent. Prior studies are more focused on benchmark-driven evaluations [26], [27], which emphasize absolute performance across datasets or architectures; this study adopts a system-level empirical perspective. By apply- ing factorial ANOVA, effect size analysis, and regression modeling, we provide statistically grounded insights into which RAG components dominate system behavior. This allows us to move beyond descriptive comparisons toward explanatory conclusions that inform practical system design. III. METHODOLOGY This study assesses how chunking, indexing, retrieval, and reranking strategies affect question-answering performance in a structured RAG pipeline. Using several approximate nearest neighbor techniques, documents are first chunked and converted into dense embeddings, which are then stored and indexed. Using various similarity metrics and retrieval techniques, queries are embedded and compared to stored representations. Before being sent to a lightweight generative model for answer synthesis, retrieved candidates undergo cross-encoder reranking. Ranking, quality, efficiency, and statistical significance analyses are used to assess system performance and ensure both efficacy and robustness across configurations. Complete methodological flow of the study is shown in Figure 1. FIGURE 1. Methodology flowchart diagram. VOLUME 14, 2026 26055 H. Elkiran, J. Rasheed: Empirical Evaluation of Retrieval, Reranking, and Similarity A. DATASETS The first phase in this study is data collection. This study utilized the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD) [28]. It is commonly used as a standard for question-answering tasks in machine learning. SQuAD was developed by human annotators from Wikipedia articles. Each dataset instance consists of a Wikipedia passage, a corresponding question, and one or more ground-truth answers. SquAD consists of 100,000 instances. The small, medium, and large datasets [29] are constructed by random sampling 10k, 30k, and 100k instances, respectively, while ensuring proportional coverage of topics and question types. This scaling strategy enables controlled analysis of dataset size effects without introducing distributional bias. B. CHUNKING RAG systems rely heavily on document chunking, the technique of splitting texts into coherent chunks. More formally, Chunking is a transformation T : D →P that maps a document D to a set of passages P = {p1, p2, . . .}. The transformation is designed such that the semantic information contained in D is preserved within the passage set P. The transformation may be reversible, meaning that the original document can potentially be reconstructed from P, although reversibility is not a strict requirement [30]. Recent studies suggest that chunking methods affect downstream task performance [31], and that LLMs are sensitive to passage style and organization [32]. This study used chunks with a fixed window size of 1,000 and a character overlap of 200. This selected ratio provides a balanced granularity with contextual completeness. This study’s overlap set preserves information around chunk borders. Once chunking is performed, it yields a representation that enables efficient embeddings for downstream tasks. C. TRANSFORM INTO EMBEDDINGS Numerical representations of text, pictures, or other data types that capture semantic value in a high-dimensional vector space are called embeddings. Embeddings are a very important part of an RAG system; they provide appropriate representations of chunks for storage, retrieval, and downstream tasks. In this study, each passage pi from the chunking phase is then processed by OpenAI’s text-embedding-large-31 model. The model first tokenizes the text into a sequence of subword tokens (t1, t2, . . . , tm) using a byte-pair encoding (BPE) tokenizer. A deep transformer encoder maps these tokens to contextual representations, creating a series of hidden states that capture the passage’s syntactic and semantic dependencies. A pooling operation over the final-layer token representations yields a fixed-dimensional embedding vector ei ∈R3072. To facilitate effective semantic retrieval and comparison across chunked document representations, the resulting embedding space is 1https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/text-embedding-3-large optimized so that semantically similar passages are closer to each other in the vector space. D. STORING EMBEDDINGS The dense numerical representations (vector embeddings) obtained in the previous step enable similarity-based retrieval by encoding the semantic meaning of unstructured data. The embeddings produced in this work are stored in Milvus [33], a vector database designed for extensive semantic search. Fast and precise similarity queries are made possible by Milvus’s scalable high-dimensional vector storage and effective Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) indexing. Real-time embedding insertion, retrieval, and filtering are enabled by its distributed, cloud-native architecture, making it ideal for the RAG system. E. INDEXING Efficient similarity search over high-dimensional embedding spaces requires specialized indexing strategies to balance search accuracy, latency, and memory consumption. In this system, three approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) [34] indexing approaches are considered: HNSW, IVF, and ScaNN. 1) HIERARCHICAL NAVIGABLE SMALL WORLD (HNSW) HNSW arranges embeddings into a multi-layer graph struc- ture, with edges connecting semantically close neighbors and each node representing a vector. Logarithmic-time complexity and high recall are made possible by the search’s traversal of the graph from higher to lower layers during query time. Despite having a higher memory overhead, HNSW is especially useful for low-latency applications. 2) INVERTED FILE INDEX (IVF) IVF uses coarse quantization to divide the embedding space into a predetermined number of clusters. The number of distance calculations is greatly reduced by assigning each vector to its closest cluster centroid and limiting similarity searches to a subset of relevant clusters. With a manageable trade-off between speed and accuracy, IVF is ideal for large-scale datasets where scalability and memory efficiency are crucial. 3) SCALABLE NEAREST NEIGHBORS (ScaNN) Space partitioning, vector quantization, and optimized distance scoring are all combined in ScaNN’s hybrid methodology. ScaNN is appropriate for high-throughput, large-scale semantic search scenarios because it balances retrieval accuracy and computational efficiency by selectively searching promising partitions and re-ranking compressed representations. F. DISTANCE / SIMILARITY METRICS Similarity and distance metrics are used in RAG to estimate how relevant a chunk of text is to a user’s query [35], [36] 26056 VOLUME 14, 2026 H. Elkiran, J. Rasheed: Empirical Evaluation of Retrieval, Reranking, and Similarity [37]. These ratings help the retrieval system determine which passages to feed into a language model for answer generation. The choice of metric is greatly influenced by the embedding model’s training method. Embedding models are tailored to arrange their output vectors in ways that are consistent with specified distance measures. It is essential to index and store vectors. In this study, we have used three metrics. 1) COSINE SIMILARITY The angular similarity between two embedding vectors x, y ∈Rd is measured using cosine similarity, regardless of their magnitudes. It is defined by the equation 1. simcos(x, y) = x · y ∥x∥2 ∥y∥2 , (1) In equation 1 x·y shows the dot product and ∥·∥2 also denotes the Euclidean norm. 2) EUCLIDEAN DISTANCE Equation 2 defines Euclidean distance, which measures the straight-line distance between two vectors in the embedding space. This metric is frequently used when the absolute distance in the embedding space is significant, as it is sensitive to vector magnitude. dEuc(x, y) = ∥x −y∥2 = v u u t d X i=1 (xi −yi)2. (2) 3) INNER PRODUCT Equation 3 calculates inner product similarity, which evalu- ates the alignment of two vectors. Unlike cosine similarity, the inner product does not normalize vector lengths. It thereby captures both directional similarity and magnitude, making it appropriate for applications such as maximum relevance score in retrieval systems. simIP(x, y) = x · y = d X i=1 xiyi. (3) G. RETRIEVAL Retrieval is the Information Retrieval task that identifies the most relevant documents or passages from the embedding vector space in response to a query. Given a query q, it is first transformed into a vector representation q using the same embedding model as the indexed data based on the indexing techniques used in this study. Similarity between the query embedding and stored embeddings is then computed using a predefined distance/similarity metric, based on this, the top-k most relevant texts are retrieved. Advanced retrieval strategies further enhance recall and precision of a RAG system. In this study, we have used four retrieval strategies: fusion, hierarchical, HyPe, and HyDe. 1) FUSION Fusion-based retrieval combines results from different retrieval strategies or representations to improve overall recall and robustness. Typically, scores from dense vector retrieval and sparse keyword-based retrieval are added together using weighted summation or rank-based fusion, as shown in equation 4. In equation 4, si(d) denotes the score of document d from the i-th retriever, and αi represents its corresponding weight. Fusion leverages the strengths of various retrieval methods to produce more reliable results [38]. s(d) = n X i=1 αisi(d), (4) 2) HIERARCHICAL Hierarchical retrieval operates on multi-level document rep- resentations, such as document- and chunk-level embeddings. An initial coarse retrieval step finds relevant documents, followed by a detailed search within their associated pas- sages, as shown in equation 5. In equation 5, q is the query embedding, eD denotes document-level embeddings, and ep represents passage-level embeddings. This approach boosts efficiency while keeping retrieval precision. D′ = arg max D sim(q, eD), P′ = arg max p∈D′ sim(q, ep), (5) 3) HyPE HyPE (Hypothetical Passage Embeddings) improves retrieval by creating a hypothetical passage that represents the ideal answer to a given query. This generated passage is embedded and used as a retrieval query, as shown in equation 6. In equation 6, ˆp is the generated hypothetical passage and E(·) denotes the embedding function. By aligning retrieval with the expected answer structure, HyPE boosts semantic matching and recall [16]. qHyPE = E(ˆp), (6) 4) HyDE HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) follows a similar approach, generating a hypothetical document rather than a passage. The embedding of this synthetic document is used to retrieve relevant real documents, as shown in equation 7. In equation 7, ˆD is a generated hypothetical document. HyDE is particularly effective in zero-shot or low- resource settings because it reduces reliance on exact query wording by capturing hidden intent [39]. qHyDE = E( ˆD), (7) H. RERANKING A reranker model, also known as a cross-encoder, generates a similarity score for a query-document pair. We use this score to arrange documents or outputs by their relevance to our query. Rerankers are best used as a second-stage ranking retrieval step. The first heavy lifting is done via rapid, efficient embedding-based vector search (retriever), which retrieves a set of documents from a large knowledge base but may lack the high accuracy required in some use cases. Rerankers then refine a list of candidates, offering just the VOLUME 14, 2026 26057 H. Elkiran, J. Rasheed: Empirical Evaluation of Retrieval, Reranking, and Similarity most relevant documents to increase retrieval accuracy. In this study, we have used two rerankers, BGE and miniLM. 1) BGE We employed BAAI/bge-reranker-base2 in this study, which supports both Chinese and English. Because BGE is a cross-encoder model, it is less efficient but more accurate. In contrast to the embedding approach, the BGE reranker takes questions and documents as input and outputs similarity scores directly rather than via embeddings. Cross- encoders are frequently used to re-rank top-k documents produced by other basic models to balance accuracy and time cost. For instance, the top 100 relevant documents may be retrieved using the embedding model during the retrieval phase, and the final top-3 results can be obtained by the reranker model using the BGE reranker. 2) miniLM In this study, for miniLM reranker, we used cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2.3 This model is trained on the MS MARCO dataset and optimized for passage-level relevance estimation, enabling it to capture contextual alignment and semantic nuances that are not accessible during the initial dense retrieval stage. This re-ranking step significantly improves precision by prioritizing the most relevant passages to the query intent. Following the initial retrieval stage, a re-ranking phase refines the relevance of the top-k retrieved candidates. Given a query q and a candidate passage pi, the model encodes the concatenated input sequence [q; pi] and produces a relevance score shown in equation 8 si = fCE(q, pi), (8) In equation 8 fCE(·) denotes the cross-encoder scoring func- tion. The retrieved candidates are then rearranged according to si, yielding a refined ranking shown in equation 9 R = sort({pi}k i=1, si). (9) I. TEXT GENERATION In this study, the gpt-4o-mini model is used to generate the final response. This model produces a logical, natural- language response to the user query using the top-ranked chunks from the reranking stage as contextual input. The model minimizes hallucinations while producing grounded and context-aware responses by conditioning generation on retrieved external knowledge. Low-latency generation is enabled by gpt-4o-mini’s lightweight, effective design, making it suitable for interactive applications that demand both accuracy and responsiveness. The gpt-4o-mini model was selected as the generation component because our objective is not to optimize generation quality, but to isolate and evaluate the relative contributions of retrieval, 2https://huggingface.co/BAAI/bge-reranker-base 3https://huggingface.co/cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2 similarity metrics, indexing, and reranking components. Using a lightweight and cost-efficient generator serves that purpose. If we employ larger or more smart generator (e.g., GPT-4, Claude, or LLaMA-based models), it can improve the absolute correctness and fluency scores, but it would not change the statistical significance or effect sizes observed for retrievers, similarity metrics, or indexing strategies, used in this study prior to generation. J. PERFORMANCE EVALUATION System performance is evaluated across the retrieval, rerank- ing, and generation stages. We use a mix of ranking-, coverage-, quality-, and efficiency-related metrics. These metrics assess retrieval accuracy, answer quality, robustness, and system efficiency. 1) RECALL@1 (R@1) Recall@1 measures whether the most relevant document or passage is ranked first. It reflects the system’s ability to produce the correct result immediately. This measure is crucial for reranking and generation performance. 2) MEAN RECIPROCAL RANK (MRR) MRR evaluates the average inverse rank of the first relevant result across all queries. It shows how early the correct answer appears in the ranked list and is sensitive to improvements beyond the top position. 3) NORMALIZED DISCOUNTED CUMULATIVE GAIN AT 1 (nDCG@1) nDCG@1 measures ranking quality by considering both relevance and position, normalized against an ideal ranking. By focusing on the top-ranked result, it highlights precision in important retrieval scenarios. 4) COVERAGE Coverage quantifies the proportion of queries where the system successfully retrieves at least one relevant document. This metric reflects retrieval robustness and the ability to avoid empty or irrelevant result sets. 5) CORRECTNESS Correctness evaluates whether the generated response includes accurate information based on the retrieved context. It captures the system’s reliability and is essential for practical use. 6) FAITHFULNESS Faithfulness measures how well generated responses stick to the retrieved evidence. High faithfulness means less hallucination and better alignment between the retrieval and generation stages. 7) RELEVANCE Relevance assesses how effectively the generated output answers the user’s query. Unlike correctness, which focuses 26058 VOLUME 14, 2026 H. Elkiran, J. Rasheed: Empirical Evaluation of Retrieval, Reranking, and Similarity on factual accuracy, relevance emphasizes how well the response meets user intent and its usefulness. 8) LATENCY Latency is the average time from start to finish, including retrieval, reranking, and generation. This metric is key for evaluating system usability in real-time applications. 9) COST Cost measures the average computational or API-related expense per query. It offers insight into the economic efficiency of different configurations and helps analyze trade-offs between performance and operational cost. K. STATISTICAL TESTING To assess whether observed performance differences are statistically significant, we apply factorial statistical analysis across four experimental factors: index type, retrieval strat- egy, reranker, and similarity metric. To achieve this, we have performed following performed following statistical test. 1) ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE (ANOVA) One-way and multi-factor ANOVA tests are used to evaluate whether performance variations across factor levels are sig- nificant. The ANOVA F-statistic is defined using equation 10. A low p-value (p < 0.05) indicates statistically significant differences between groups. F = Between-group variance Within-group variance (10) 2) EFFECT SIZE (ETA SQUARED) To quantify the magnitude of observed effects, eta squared (η2) is computed using equation 11, where SS denotes the sum of squares. Large η2 values for retrievers and similarity metrics indicate a strong practical impact on system performance. η2 = SSfactor SStotal (11) 3) REGRESSION ANALYSIS Multivariate linear regression is applied to estimate the directional influence of individual factor levels. Regression coefficients (β) quantify the contribution of each factor from equation 12. Positive β values indicate performance improvements, while negative values reflect degradation. y = β0 + X j βjxj + ϵ (12) IV. SYSTEM IMPLEMENTATION All experiments were implemented in Python using the LangChain framework with Milvus as the vector database backend. Experiments were conducted on a MacBook Pro with a 2.3 GHz 8-core Intel Core i9 CPU, 32 GB of DDR4 (2667 MHz) memory, and macOS 15.6.1 oper- ating system. Dense embeddings are generated using text-embedding-3-large, while answer generation relies on gpt-4o-mini with deterministic decoding. Latency and API costs are recorded separately for retrieval, reranking, and generation using OpenAI callbacks. We have designed the RAG pipeline to be fully modular and configurable via command-line interfaces, enabling controlled variation of different parameters such as indexing strategy (HNSW, IVF, SCANN), similarity metric (cosine, inner product, L2), retriever (HyDE, HyPE, Fusion, Hierarchical), reranker (LLM-based or cross-encoder), and dataset size. RESULTS A. INDEXING PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Table 2 presents the mean performance metrics for the three indexing strategies: HNSW, IVF, and ScaNN. Among the evaluated methods, HNSW achieves the highest retrieval effectiveness, with a Recall@1 of 0.578, Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) of 0.654, and nDCG@1 of 0.578. In compar- ison, IVF shows slightly lower scores for R@1 and MRR at 0.564 and 0.638, respectively, while ScaNN performs marginally better than IVF, with an MRR of 0.640. Coverage during retrieval follows a similar trend, with HNSW achiev- ing the highest coverage of 0.796, compared to 0.775 for IVF and 0.778 for ScaNN. After reranking, the relative ordering of the indexing methods remains consistent, with HNSW maintaining superior performance, achieving an MRR of 0.654, over IVF (0.639) and ScaNN (0.640), indicating stable VOLUME 14, 2026 26059 H. In terms of generation quality, HNSW again outperforms the alternatives, achieving a correctness score of 0.783, a faithfulness score of 0.930, and a relevance score of 0.866. IVF and ScaNN show slightly reduced correctness with scores of 0.771 and 0.772, respectively, for relevance; both models were at 0.856, while faithfulness remains high and comparable across all methods, i.e., above 0.927. HNSW exhibits the highest mean latency at 3.176 seconds, whereas IVF and ScaNN achieve lower response times of 2.838 and 2.815 seconds, respectively. This latency analysis reveals a trade-off between effectiveness and efficiency. The average cost per query remains constant across all indexing methods at 1.16 × 10−4, indicating that performance differences are primarily driven by retrieval behavior rather than computational cost. Among retrievers, HyPE demonstrates the largest positive gains across all metrics with β for R@1 retrieval of 0.050, whereas hierarchical retrieval shows significant negative effects. Indexing methods (HNSW, IVF, ScaNN) do not exhibit statistically significant influence, indicating that retrieval quality is primarily governed by semantic modeling choices rather than ANN structure. Table 9 highlights clear trade-offs between system effi- ciency and answer quality. Inner product similarity signifi- cantly improves correctness with β, score of 0.016, and p value of less than 10−5 while also reducing computational cost, whereas L2 distance both increases cost and sharply reduces correctness. HyDE substantially increases cost due to additional generation overhead with β value of 2.4, while HyPE provides strong correctness gains with lower cost. The MiniLM reranker consistently improves correctness over BGE, confirming its effectiveness as a lightweight yet accurate reranking model. VI. RECOMMENDATION Based on our empirical and statistical findings, we offer the following practical recommendations for dense retrieval- based RAG systems under controlled general-domain settings: 1) Retrieval strategy selection should be prioritized over index optimization, as retrievers explain the largest proportion of variance in both retrieval coverage and generation correctness ( η2 = 0.31). 26062 VOLUME 14, 2026 H. Elkiran, J. Rasheed: Empirical Evaluation of Retrieval, Reranking, and Similarity FIGURE 5. Comparison of retrieval strategies. TABLE 7. Effect size analysis (Eta squared, η2) quantifying the relative impact of Index type, Retrieval strategy, Reranker, and Similarity Metric on key system performance measures. 2) Inner product or cosine similarity should be preferred for dense retrieval, while L2 distance should be avoided due to consistently negative performance effects. 3) Reranking should be applied selectively when down- stream correctness and relevance are critical, with lightweight cross-encoders (e.g., MiniLM) offering an optimal accuracy–latency trade-off. 4) Indexing choices should be guided by latency, scalabil- ity, and memory constraints rather than expected gains in retrieval effectiveness. 5) Smaller chunks improve precision and correctness, while larger chunks reduce latency and cost, making chunking a context-dependent optimization. Table 10 shows the summarized recommendation in tabular form. VII. DISCUSSION In this study, results were obtained by keeping the system parameters constant for the controlled comparisons. The Chunk size (1,000 characters) and overlap (200 characters) were selected to balance semantic completeness with retrieval granularity. Our results reflect a mid-range configuration that avoids the extremes. Similarly, retrieval depth (top-k) and reranking thresholds were held constant to prevent interaction effects from dominating statistical analysis. Our findings reveal important interactions between RAG components that are not evident from isolated comparisons. While weaker retrievers limit the impact of reranking regardless of model choice, strong retrievers like HyPE increase their efficacy by providing higher-quality candidate sets. Similarly, similarity metrics affect retrieval strategies differently. For example, when paired with hypothetical embedding-based retrievers, inner-product similarity yields the largest gains, but under hierarchical retrieval, its advan- tage diminishes. Reranking and similarity metrics should be viewed as conditional amplifiers rather than as separate performance drivers, given these interactions. Extremely small p-values (e.g., 10−9–10−11) arise due to large sample sizes and low within-group variance; these values are consistent with large observed effect sizes. To improve interpretability, effect sizes (η2) and 95% confidence intervals are emphasized as primary indicators of practical significance. The results highlight a clear hierarchy among RAG system components. Across all experiments, retriever choice emerges as the most influential factor, driving large effect sizes for Coverage and Correctness. Similarity metrics represent the second most impactful design dimension. Inner product and cosine similarity consistently improve retrieval and generation quality, while L2 distance degrades performance across almost all evaluated metrics. Reranking contributes meaningfully but selectively. While rerankers have minimal influence on first-stage retrieval metrics, they significantly affect reranked performance and generation correctness. MiniLM demonstrates strong positive effects on reranked R@1 and Correctness, indicating that lightweight cross-encoder rerankers can substantially enhance final answer quality without incurring prohibitive costs. In con- trast, index choice (HNSW, IVF, SCANN) shows no statis- tically significant impact on retrieval quality, with negligible effect sizes across metrics. This suggests that, under sufficient recall conditions, approximate nearest neighbor structures primarily trade off latency rather than effectiveness. Changes in system parameter values may shift absolute metric values, but the relative dominance of retriever choice and similarity metric is expected to remain stable, as confirmed by large effect sizes (η2 > 0.3) observed across configurations. VIII. CONCLUSION RAG system is a combination of closely associated compo- nents working together to solve the problem of LLM halluci- nation in a large context-aware application. The components (Retrievar, Reranker, Similarity metrics, Data size, Indexer) that form a RAG system vary based on the application. So it is important to understand the impact and the different types of these components. To create a framework and provide tangible recommendations, experiments must be conducted across a variety of configurations. In this study, VOLUME 14, 2026 26063 H. Elkiran, J. Rasheed: Empirical Evaluation of Retrieval, Reranking, and Similarity FIGURE 6. Performance comparison between BGE and MiniLM rerankers. TABLE 8. Multivariate regression analysis showing the influence of indexing method, similarity metric, retrieval strategy, reranker, and dataset size on retrieval and coverage performance. we conducted controlled experiments with a variety of RAG components, evaluated performance, and assessed the significance of different RAG components using ANOVA, effect size analysis, and regression modeling. We disentangle the relative importance of pipeline components and quantify their impact on both retrieval and generation performance. Our results demonstrate that retriever and similarity metric choices dominate system behavior, while index structures play a largely infrastructural role. Reranking improves down- stream quality but should be applied judiciously. Based on these results, we provide actionable guidance for researchers and practitioners building scalable, high-quality generative systems. IX. LIMITATIONS There are several limitations to this study. First, results may not generalize well to multilingual or domain-specific corpora because evaluation is conducted on a general-domain English dataset (SQuAD). Second, to minimize experimental complexity, chunking parameters and retrieval depths are fixed; different configurations may yield different absolute performance values. Third, although controlled analysis is supported by the generation model’s (gpt-4o-mini) deliberate lightweight design, stronger generators may yield greater downstream gains. We are using a single embedding model for controlled experiments, which is one of the limitations to full generalization. 26064 VOLUME 14, 2026 H. Elkiran, J. Rasheed: Empirical Evaluation of Retrieval, Reranking, and Similarity TABLE 9. Regression coefficients analyzing the effect of system components on computational cost and answer correctness. TABLE 10. Recommendation for rag system based on this study. X. FUTURE WORK Future work will extend this analysis to multilingual settings, larger-scale corpora, and domain-specific data such as HotpotQA, Natural Questions, and MS MARCO. The work can also be extended by adding more parameters and demonstrating that its findings are transferable to other configurations and datasets. We also plan to study parameter sensitivity more explicitly by varying chunk sizes, overlaps, and retrieval depth. Future experiments will also evaluate whether stronger generation (GPT-4, GPT-5, Claude, etc.) models increase or decrease upstream retrieval effects. This will clarify the separation between retrieval-driven and generation-driven performance gains in RAG systems. ACKNOWLEDGMENT Computing resources used in this work were provided by the National Center for High Performance Computing of Türkiye (UHeM). This study is part of a Ph.D. thesis titled ‘‘Performance Analysis of Advanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation Applications using Vector Databases, Indexing Algorithms and Distance Metrics’’ under the supervision of Jawad Rasheed (Cevat Resit). file_name: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Biomedical Literature Summarization Abstractive Versus Extractive Paradigms.pdf
text: Received 8 July 2025, accepted 27 August 2025, date of publication 29 August 2025, date of current version 5 September 2025. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3604351 Benchmarking Large Language Models for Biomedical Literature Summarization: Abstractive Versus Extractive Paradigms TUGBA CELIKTEN 1,2, (Student Member, IEEE), AND AYTUG ONAN 1, (Member, IEEE) 1Department of Computer Engineering, İzmir Kâtip Çelebi University, 35620 İzmir, Türkiye 2Department of Software Engineering, Manisa Celal Bayar University, 45140 Manisa, Türkiye Corresponding author: Tugba Celikten (tugba.celikten@cbu.edu.tr) This work was supported by the Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit of İzmir Kâtip Çelebi University under Project 2025-TDR-FEBE-0001. ABSTRACT Summarizing biomedical literature has become an important tool for researchers and healthcare professionals by increasing the speed of access to information. In this context, abstractive and extractive summarization methods are the main techniques used to make texts concise without distorting their meaning. While abstractive summarization restructures the text to create more meaningful and contextually consistent summaries, extractive summarization summarizes by directly quoting from the source text. Analyses conducted with a dataset created using scientific articles written in different biomedical fields analyzed how accurately and consistently the models could summarize biomedical texts through the metrics used (SBERT, BLEU, ROUGE, etc.). In the evaluations, while abstractive summarization methods generally provide higher semantic consistency, extractive summarization offers higher word-matching accuracy. While abstractive summarization exhibits superior performance in metrics such as SBERT and preserves semantic integrity better, extractive summarization achieves higher results in metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE. In addition, it has been found that large models are generally more successful in extractive summarization, while medium-sized models show balanced performance in both methods. In the future, with the increasing use of LLMs in the biomedical field, it will be possible to make more accurate, faster, and more meaningful summaries. In addition, the integrated use of summarization methods can make biomedical information systems more efficient. Future studies can focus on making LLMs more effective with wider datasets and contextual suitability. INDEX TERMS Extractive summarization, abstractive summarization, medical text summarization, large language models. I. INTRODUCTION Large language models (LLMs), supported by large datasets and advanced architectures, play a significant role in natural language processing (NLP) tasks by combining human- like language understanding and generative capabilities. These massive models have demonstrated an incredible ability to understand and process human language [1]. LLMs have great potential in specialized fields such as biomedical literature and have been actively used in a The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Md. Abdur Razzaque . wide range of fields, including text summarization, which has recently become a broad research topic [2]. The task of text summarization is to reduce long texts into short, meaningful expressions and capture general information. Although this task is usually performed by biomedical field experts, it is a difficult process that requires a significant amount of time and attention given the increasing volume of information. Considering the increasing number of studies in biomedical literature, the need for automatic summarization tools in this field has increased. In the biomedical field, text summarization addresses the problem of presenting a simplified version of the information in reports while 152682 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization accurately capturing critical details [3]. Therefore, there is a need for systems that can quickly and effectively handle the task of summarizing text. The choice of summarization technique presents an additional challenge, as different methods have varying requirements and produce distinct types of summaries. There are two main summarization methods: abstractive and extractive summarization. Among these methods, abstractive summarization focuses on the context of the text and reflects the meaning of the text with different expressions and new sentences [4]. However, this method may produce grammatically incorrect or questionable content. This may lead to summaries that contradict facts, are misleading, or contain false information. Another method, extractive summarization, provides a summary by selecting meaningful and critical expressions from the reference text. However, the disadvantage of this method is that it produces semantically inconsistent results. Because important parts are extracted from the text and then these parts are put together. This may disrupt the semantic integrity [5]. In addition to these problems, many recent studies have found it difficult to produce precise summaries due to the length of the texts or the density of the context [6]. Despite the inherent difficulties of summarization tasks, their ability to handle the complexities of language across domains makes LLMs a powerful alternative to automate the summarization task [7] by leveraging deep learning techniques and vast amounts of training data. The ability of LLMs to process and generate human-like text enables them to summarize long and complex documents efficiently while preserving key information and coherence. However, the effectiveness of these models varies depending on factors such as training data, model architecture, and the summa- rization approach used. By the way, summaries produced by LLMs should not be of inferior quality to summaries pro- duced by experienced clinicians; this is particularly important when making sensitive clinical decisions where accuracy, clarity, and reliability are crucial for ensuring patient safety and effective medical practice [8]. The summarization per- formance of LLMs improves significantly when fine-tuned on domain-specific corpora because domain knowledge increases the coherence and relevance of the generated content. When trained on biomedical literature, LLMs can better understand medical terminology, clinical guidelines, and research trends, leading to more contextually appropriate and semantically accurate summaries. The models exhibit a strong capacity to balance fluidity and information density, making them particularly useful for summarizing dense academic literature. Nevertheless, challenges remain in ensuring that the generated summaries do not omit critical details or introduce misleading interpretations. However, it is debatable whether general-purpose LLMs always perform as well in capturing the fine details required for more complex tasks since they might lack the depth of domain-specific knowledge necessary for specialized fields like medicine and biomedical research [7]. In this study, we seek to answer the following research questions regarding the effectiveness of LLMs in scientific and biomedical text summarization: • RQ1: How do different LLMs perform in summarizing scientific biomedical texts? • RQ2: What are the performance differences between abstractive and extractive methods for LLMs, and which method is more effective in the task? • RQ3: To what extent can different LLMs preserve semantic and contextual integrity while ensuring essen- tial information is conveyed in the summarization process? • RQ4: How do the lengths of summaries produced by different LLMs differ when summarizing validated biomedical texts, and how does this variation impact the comprehensibility and utility of the summaries? To address these questions, we conduct a comprehensive and systematic evaluation of 10 different LLMs, assess- ing their performance in both abstractive and extractive summarization tasks. Our analysis covers a diverse range of transformer-based LLMs, including Qwen, Mistral, Phi, Zephyr, Cohere, GPT, Claude, Llama, Gemini, and Falcon to provide a holistic understanding of their summarization capabilities. Unlike previous studies that typically compare only a few models, our research provides a broader perspec- tive by analyzing multiple models with varying architectures and training techniques. Additionally, we introduce an original, high-quality dataset specifically designed for biomedical scientific texts, allowing for a more targeted and effective evaluation of summarization capabilities. Through this study, we aim to offer in-depth insights into optimal model selection and summarization strategies for scientific text processing. Our findings will contribute to the ongoing development of LLM-based summarization techniques, providing valuable guidance for researchers and practitioners working with complex scientific and medical texts. In the remainder of this paper, we first review the existing literature on text summarization in Section II, providing an overview of prior studies and methodologies. Section III defines the text summarization task and introduces LLMs. Section IV presents our experiments and results, detailing the dataset, models, and evaluation process. Finally, Section VII provides the conclusion, summarizing key findings and suggesting directions for future research. II. RELATED WORKS Recent advancements in LLMs have significantly enhanced the capabilities of text summarization, especially in the domain of biomedical text mining. Several studies have explored the application of LLMs to improve summarization performance, addressing challenges such as text length, coherence, and domain-specific nuances. Table 1 provides an overview of the related works in this area, where relevant VOLUME 13, 2025 152683 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization studies are summarized and their contributions are discussed in detail. López-Úbeda et al. [3] conducted a study using BART and T5 models on text summarization for radiology reports. In addition, encoder, decoder, and attention mechanisms were used in an LSTM-based model, and the Optuna framework was applied for hyperparameter optimization. Models were evaluated with BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE, and BART Score metrics. Their findings highlight the effectiveness of fine-tuned T5 models, outperforming other approaches, while zero-knowledge methods and the LSTM-based model achieved comparatively lower success. Building on the need for domain-specific summariza- tion models, Kumar et al. [9] addressed the challenge of summarizing legal documents, both extractively and abstractively. The study encompassed four main experimental categories: 1) Abstractive methods (BART, T5, Pegasus, Legal-PEGASUS, Legal-BERT), 2) Extractive methods (TextRank, LexRank, LSA, KL-Summ), 3) Hybrid models (Longformer, Bert-Pegasus), and 4) LLMs (GPT-4 and LLAMA-2). Using 7,130 documents for abstractive summa- rization and 50 documents for extractive summarization, they demonstrated that Legal-PEGASUS excelled in abstractive summarization, while TextRank proved to be the most effective extractive summarization approach. Furthermore, hybrid models showed notable performance in some metrics, and prompt designs influenced the results of LLMs. Extending the use of LLMs to programming contexts, Zhu et al. [10] explored the potential of these models in producing statement-level code summarization. They utilized a large dataset comprising 342,448 and 152,975 comment data entries for Java and Python, respectively. Experiments with GPT-4, GPT-3.5, CodeLlama, and StarChat were assessed using SentenceBERT and BLEU metrics. The results revealed that LLMs significantly outperformed pre-trained models like CodeT5, and Few-shot learning techniques further enhanced their summarization capabilities. In the biomedical domain, Du et al. [2] identified limi- tations in existing methods, particularly in utilizing domain knowledge and learning hierarchical features effectively. To address these challenges, they employed BioBERT, pre- trained on large-scale biomedical texts, and incorporated a sentence position placement mechanism to capture structural information. Additionally, they fine-tuned the model on inferential summarization tasks using a Transformer-based architecture. Their results on the PubMed dataset under- score the strength of BioBERTSum in leveraging domain knowledge, excelling in both biomedical and open-domain summarization tasks. Tan et al. [11] propose the Uni-SciSum model, which combines multiple data modalities such as text, image, video, and audio to generate high-quality summaries. Using large language models and the BridgeNet trans- former, it aligns modality features with summaries. Uni- SciSum outperforms other models, underscoring the growing importance of multimodal summarization in scientific communication. In a similar domain-specific approach, Murugaraj et al. [12] present HistBERT for automatic summarization of historical texts. HistBERT uses a pre-trained encoder and a transformer decoder to effectively summarize historical docu- ments. The study shows that HistBERTSum-Abs outperforms general LLMs, emphasizing the value of domain-specific models. Jiang et al. [6] propose TriSum, a framework designed to transfer the summarization power of large language models (LLMs) to smaller models, making them suitable for resource-limited environments. TriSum improves sum- marization capabilities, especially with curriculum learning, highlighting the need for efficient models in low-resource settings. Parmar et al. [5] focus on increasing the consistency of extractive summarization by incorporating user feedback. Their three-stage training process significantly improves per- formance, particularly in Encoder-Decoder models. Human evaluations confirmed that Llama-2 provides more consis- tent, higher-quality summaries than other models. To fill an important gap in the scientific article evaluation process, Majadly et al. [4] conducted a study. This study aims to extract important sentences such as contribution, motiva- tion, claims, and claims support from scientific articles with different language models. Then, extractive summarization is performed using the extracted information. It is stated that the proposed summarization model outperforms the original summary and other models in ROUGE metrics. It is also emphasized that more effective results are obtained compared to the question-answer-based summarization model. An automatic AI solution for text summarization from news articles was developed by Compaore et al. [13]. For this purpose, GPT-3.5 Turbo and Pegasus Summarizer models were used. Experimental results showed that the summa- rization capacity of the GPT-3.5 Turbo model was better than other models. In particular, it produced results closer to human-written reference summaries. Pegasus Summarizer was reported to be successful in producing shorter but meaningful summaries. Cao et al. [14] explored the extraction of critical infor- mation, specifically, the 5W1H (What, When, Where, Why, Who, How) elements, from news articles. Their experiments on models such as LLaMA, Vicuna, and Guanaco, using a dataset of 3,500 classified articles, demonstrated that fine- tuned models like LLaMA outperformed zero/low-sample methods for 5W1H extraction. Notably, ChatGPT struggled with information loss and context insensitivity in longer texts, while GPT-4 showed performance similar to fine-tuned models, especially in low-sample scenarios. In a similar vein, Tariq et al. [15] tackled the challenge of summarizing radiology reports tailored to patients’ varying levels of medical knowledge. By dividing reports into sections like ‘‘findings’’ and ‘‘impression,’’ they trained 152684 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization TABLE 1. Overview of text summarization studies. VOLUME 13, 2025 152685 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization LLaMA to generate patient-friendly summaries from the ‘‘findings’’ section. The summaries were then further refined by training the T5 model, which was found to produce fewer errors and a lower rate of missing information compared to LLaMA, ultimately enhancing patient understanding through accurate and accessible language. Turning to clinical dialogue, Navarro et al. [16] evalu- ated multiple models for summarizing dialogues between patients and physicians, using both traditional and newer metrics. Their findings showed that ChatGPT achieved the best performance in human evaluations, underscoring the growing effectiveness of LLMs in clinical dialogue summarization. Further enhancing methods for text summarization, Chhikara et al. [17] proposed LaMSUM, a framework for extractive summarization that breaks down texts into chunks, creates summaries for each chunk, and re-summarizes at a higher level. By employing voting algorithms, such as Plurality Voting and Borda Count, LaMSUM outperformed existing methods, providing more accurate and comprehen- sive summaries when combining suggestions from different LLMs. Meanwhile, Ravaut et al. [18] investigated how context and position biases impact LLM summarization, focusing on the ‘‘U-shaped performance’’ model, where models perform well at the start and end of texts but poorly in the middle. Their experiments with models like Flan-UL2, LLaMA-2, and Mistral-7B showed that hierarchical and incremental summarization methods were not fully effective in addressing this issue. Together, these studies demonstrate the variety of approaches that have been adopted to improve text sum- marization, from integrating multimodal data to optimizing domain-specific models to transferring knowledge to smaller models and using user feedback for consistency improve- ments. As shown in Table 1, text summarization studies in the biomedical field are relatively limited. The vast majority of existing studies only address one of the abstractive or extractive summarization methods, and comprehensive studies evaluating both approaches together are quite rare. Our study makes a significant contribution to the literature by being specific to the biomedical field, while at the same time increasing the methodological diversity in this field by con- sidering abstractive and extractive summarization methods together. In addition, our study comprehensively evaluates the summarization performances of various model architectures by comparing 10 different LLMs. This multifaceted analysis aims to establish a comprehensive reference point in the field of biomedical text summarization. III. METHODOLOGY This section describes the methodologies used to evaluate the abstractive and extractive text summarization performances in the study. A. TEXT SUMMARIZATION TASK In recent years, with the rapid increase in textual data such as social media content or scientific articles on digital platforms, the need for fast and reliable processing of this data has increased rapidly. Thanks to NLP, it has become possible to process these texts written in natural language and extract meaningful information for different tasks. In addition, NLP is rapidly developing by contributing to the development of systems that can produce text summarization and human-like texts to meet the need for automatic text summarization in today’s data-driven environment [19]. Text summarization can be explained as a subtask of NLP that aims to identify semantic patterns from a reference text, extract important information, and reflect the essence of the source text [20]. In other words, summarization involves extracting, condensing, or enhancing the key information from the text to capture the main content or overall meaning of the original text [21]. There are two main types of summarization techniques: ‘‘extractive summarization’’ and ‘‘abstractive summariza- tion’’ which are followed in this paper, depending on the specific requirements of the task, including the level of detail needed in text summarization, the desired output format, and the complexity of the text. 1) ABSTRACTIVE SUMMARIZATION Abstractive summarization is an NLP technique that inter- prets and rephrases text. Abstractive summarization generates a summary by constructing new sentences that capture the core meaning of the original text. Rather than directly extracting segments from the original text, the model produces concise and coherent sentences that preserve the key information while staying closely relevant to the main subject [23]. In short, abstractive summarization involves converting the key information from a document into a concise summary that may contain newly generated text not explicitly found in the original content [24]. Abstractive summarization is usually performed with a deep learning-based encoder-decoder architecture (Trans- former, LSTM, Seq2Seq) [20]. It is difficult to train the model correctly, usually requiring large data sets and powerful computational resources. For this reason, new text generation may take more time. On the other hand, more fluent and meaningful summaries are produced that are more compatible with human writing. In addition, unnecessary words and details are removed with this method, and the text becomes more concise. On the other hand, the model can sometimes add information that is not in the source text, and this may cause hallucinations [24]. The abstractive summarization method can be used to create short versions of news in newspapers and media organizations, to produce short summaries of research articles, to create summaries of patient reports and scientific publications, and to provide short and meaningful informa- tion to users. 152686 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization TABLE 2. Comparison of abstractive and extractive summarization. 2) EXTRACTIVE SUMMARIZATION Extractive summarization models usually generate a sum- mary by selecting sentences from the original text. In doing so, they determine the importance of each sentence and create a new version of the text by ranking it according to its importance [22]. In this process, important sentences or words of the text are selected and combined, and a summary is created without losing the meaning of the original text. The sentences in the text are analyzed according to various criteria such as word frequency, semantic density, and the sentence’s contribution to the text. The selected sentences are then combined. However, it is important that the sentences form a logical whole and that the meaning is not lost during this combination [25]. In some cases, especially in very large texts, it isn’t easy to select important information manually. In contrast, extractive summarization is usually simpler than other types of summarization. Since there is no need to produce new text, only sentences are selected from the existing text. Since the original text is used without cutting during the summarization process, the meaning of the summary is consistent with the original text. Still, sometimes the language flow may be weak because the sentences are taken directly from the text. In summary, extractive summarization creates a summary by selecting sentences or words directly from the text, while abstractive summarization creates new sentences while preserving the meaning of the text. The extractive method is simpler and faster but may have weaknesses in the language flow. Abstractive summarization produces more creative and natural summaries, but the correct language reconstruction is more complex. A detailed comparison of the two methods is given in Table 2. B. LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS A language model is an artificial intelligence (AI) model used to understand human language and produce new text. VOLUME 13, 2025 152687 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization FIGURE 1. LLM Pipeline: From Data Collection to Deployment. A language model aims to calculate the probability of word sequences or sentences or produce meaningful text based on a given input [26]. These models such as GPT-4 [29], LLaMA [31], and Claude [32] are widely used in various NLP tasks such as text generation, translation, summarization, question-answering (Q&A), etc. LLMs are usually transformer-based, and as shown in Fig 1, the creation process involves collecting large amounts of data, cleaning it, designing and training a model, and then fine-tuning, testing, and putting it into production. These models use neural networks with billions of parameters and have a self-supervised learning approach [27]. The basis of LLMs is neural networks, the basic building blocks of AI. Neural networks work on data through a multilayered learning process by establishing connections between neurons that work similarly to neurons in the human brain. As a result of this process, the parameters in the model are learned and optimized with training data [28]. In this way, these networks in LLMs perform the necessary learning processes for language modeling and are used to model and understand text. In addition to neural networks, (Recurrent Neural Networks) RNNs, which are successful in modeling serial dependencies, are also used in the LLM architecture, but they have limitations in learning very long-distance dependencies. For this purpose, the transformer architecture, which has an attention mechanism in its structure, has been used to construct LLMs. Transformer Architecture Transformer is an artificial neural network model that can process serial data more effectively, providing superiority in terms of accuracy and performance compared to RNNs. These models revolutionize language modeling by processing large data sets and producing more detailed outputs [33]. Transformer architecture has strong scalability, so it can be used to build extremely large models with billions of parameters. It supports the structure of LLM models, which are leading in the field of NLP. In addition, the transformer provides powerful results not only in language processing but also in different areas such as processing visual data [34]. The transformer architecture, as shown in Fig 2, mainly consists of input embedding, positional encoding, and encoder and decoder modules, which include the attention mechanism. a. Input Embedding Input embedding is a critical step in transformer architecture because the model needs to convert words into a numerical form to process the text. This way, it converts words into vectors, allowing the model to perform mathematical operations on them. With input embedding, similar words have similar vectors [35]. Therefore, a representation is created that includes the meaning and context of the word. Words can be represented using a pre-trained embedding matrix (e.g., Word2Vec, GloVe) or by being learned from scratch by the transformer model. During training, the transformer learns to better understand the relationships between words by optimizing these embedding vectors. b. Positional Encoding A transformer is a mechanism used to teach the order of words to the model in the architecture. Transformers do not perform sequential processing like RNN and LSTM. Instead, they process all input words at the same time (perform parallel computation). However, the order of words is important in language models. For a transformer to learn the connections between words, it needs to know the order of words. For this, the positional encoding stage adds positional information to the vectors obtained in the previous input embedding step. In this way, the model can also take their sequential order into account when processing tokens. In other words, thanks to positional encoding, the order of words is preserved and the model learns the meaning of the sentence correctly [36]. In the positional encoding process, a unique position embedding vector is created for each token using the sine and cosine functions as expressed in Eq 1, and the model can 152688 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Encoding Layer In transformer architecture, an encoder is a structure that takes input data and transforms it into a higher-level representation by processing it in a meaningful way. The encoder takes each input token (word or sub-word units) and produces ‘‘contextual’’ vectors corresponding to each token, taking into account the context of these tokens [37]. The encoder module consists of multiple identical layers, each containing a multi-head attention mechanism and a feedforward neural network. Multi-Head Attention: The self-attention mechanism enables a model to understand the relationships of each input element (e.g. a word or a token) with other input elements. In the self-attention mechanism, each word’s vector is processed by three different matrices: Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V). These three matrices showed in Eq 2 are learnable parameters, and to create each one, X (the input vector) is multiplied by different weights [36]: Q = XWQK = XWKV = XWV (2) In the equation, WQ, WK, and WV are the learned matrices, which are learned by the model during training. Self-attention takes the dot product of the Query (Q) and Key (K) matrices. This process measures the relationship of each word (Q) to other words (K). The dot product result is normalized to make it more suitable using the formula in Eq 3. Here, division by √dK is usually done to balance large numbers. The Softmax function then converts the normalized dot product results into attention scores (Eq 4). Raw Attention Scores = QK T √dK (3) Softmax measures the amount of ‘‘attention’’ given to each word (Q) and converts all values into a probability distribution. These scores determine which words should be given more attention. Higher scores indicate that the word should receive more attention [37], [38]. Self-Attention Score = Softmax QK T √dK  V (4) In summary, the similarities between each word (Q) and all other words (K) are calculated with the self-attention mechanism. The similarities obtained are normalized with the Softmax function and the ‘‘attention’’ score that each word gives to other words is calculated. Multi-head attention works similarly to self-attention, but it allows self-attention to run in parallel using multiple heads. Here, heads are parallel processes that perform different attention computations on the same input data. That is, each head learns attention computations differently - each head has its own Q, K, and V matrices. These heads perform attention computations on VOLUME 13, 2025 152689 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization the same input using different parameters, and then these computations are combined as shown in Eq 5. Qi = XW i Q, Ki = XW i K, Vi = XW i V (5) For each head, separate weight matrices WQi, WKi, WVi are used to create (Q), (K) and (V) matrices. As a second step, with the formula in Eq 6, self-attention is applied to each heading. hi = Softmax QiK T i √dK ! Vi (6) Finally, information from different topics is combined to obtain the final attention output as shown in Eq 7 [38]. Multi-Head(Q, K, V) = Concat(h1, h2, . . . , hN)Wo (7) Wo is a parameter learned during the training of the model and is the weight matrix used to obtain the final version of the concatenated heads. The multi-head attention mechanism provides richer infor- mation by focusing on different parts of the input sequence to different degrees. Thus, the model gathers information in different areas (such as long or short dependencies) to create a more comprehensive understanding [39]. Layer Normalization (Add & Norm): This step helps balance the learning process by normal- izing the outputs across features for each data sample [39]. This process is also known as residual connection. Layer normalization is a critical component to make the training of the model more stable, especially when using deep networks. This can speed up the learning process and prevent problems such as vanishing gradients. Residual connections play an important role when using deeper structures in neural networks. These connections connect the input and output of each layer, thus making deeper models easier to optimize. The original transformer architecture [40] uses the post- norm structure. Post-norm is the structure where the layer normalization process is applied after the residual connection between the input and output is added. In the Eq 8, first, a function F(Hin) is applied on the output Hin from the previous layer or the initial data. Then, the input state Hin (its own input) is added to the result. Hout = LNorm(F(Hin) + Hin) (8) This is known as a residual connection. The purpose here is to prevent information loss and to make the model work more effectively in deeper layers. Finally, this total value is passed through the Layer Normalization (LNorm) function to create the output data that will go to the next layer [41]. Feed-Forward Network: Feed-forward network (FFN) is one of the basic components of the transformer architecture and is located after each self- attention layer. FFNs are deep neural networks that process the input by passing it through certain processing layers [7]. While the self-attention mechanism learns the relationships between words, FFN processes each word representation independently and extracts its features. FFN consists of two fully connected layers and its oper- ations are applied to all tokens separately. The input vector of a token from the self-attention block x is transformed by a weight matrix W1 and bias b1 as defined xW1 + b1. This is called a linear transformation. Then the ReLU activation function (max(0, x W1 + b1)) is applied. Finally, the second linear transformation is performed as shown in Eq 9 [40]. FFN(x) = max(0, x W1 + b1)W2 + b2 (9) In summary, the FFN block takes the input vector, processes it through a series of operations, and ultimately produces the output vector. This process can work like a neural memory. In this memory, data is stored as key-value pairs. The model’s input data is compared with these keys and converted to the output in a weighted manner [42]. d. Decoding Layer Based on the output of the encoder block, the decoder generates a target sequence in order. The goal of the decoder is to predict each token (word) in the correct order, which is especially critical in tasks based on sequential data. Generally speaking, the encoder processes the input data and the decoder uses this processed information to produce the correct output, respectively [34]. A general decoder block consists of masked multi-head attention, normalization, encoder-decoder attention, and FFN layers. Masked Multi-Head Attention: In masked multi-head attention, a mask is applied over the self-attention process. The goal is to ensure that each token in the decoder only pays attention to previous tokens and does not look at future tokens. This is especially important in the transformer, which is an autoregressive model because the model must make predictions using only the current and previous tokens each time [27]. Masking is usually done by adding a large negative value on top of the overlap. This results in the masked positions ending up as zero in the softmax function. That is, the masked tokens are completely ignored. This process allows the model to learn to produce a correct sequential output based only on the current and previous tokens, without seeing future tokens [39]. Layer Normalization (Add & Norm): Likewise encoder layer, layer normalization is applied to the output of each sublayer, especially in the decoder layer in the decoder layer. Layer normalization helps to increase the stability during the network learning process. In this layer, each lower layer creates residual connections by adding the input of that layer to its output. These connections prevent data passing through the layers from being lost while adding the output of each lower layer to its input accelerates the learning of the model [39], [43]. 152690 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization Encoder-Decoder Attention: The multi-head attention in the decoder, on the other hand, focuses only on the information coming from the encoder and produces a meaningful output in the target language. The main difference between the attention layer in the encoder layer and the attention layer is that while the attention in the encoder processes the entire input sequence, the attention in the decoder only takes into account the information received from the encoder and predicts the next token by using the outputs of the previous tokens [40], [41]. Feed-Forward Network: The feed-forward layer in the decoder in the transformer architecture is a key component that helps the model create a more complex and high-level representation for each token. At each step, the decoder builds a higher-level representation using the previous tokens and the information from the encoder. However, to increase the learning capacity of the model and explore more abstract relationships, it adds the feed-forward layer. It is usually done by two consecutive linear transformations and adding a ReLU or other activation function in between. This allows the model to learn non-linear features. As in the encoder, FFNs apply the same operation to each position (i.e., each token) independently. This allows the model to create richer representations by processing the features for each token separately [44]. e. Output Layer In the transformer architecture, output generation occurs in the final stages of the decoder layer. The decoder produces a hidden representation based on the inputs processed in the previous steps and the attention mechanism. With this hidden representation, a score vector is produced in the fully connected layer that determines the probability of each word. The Softmax activation function converts these scores into probability values. The word with the highest probability is selected and added to the output. The model continues to predict the next word by adding this generated word to the input. This process is repeated step by step as an autoregressive and a full text is created [45]. 1) GPT-4 AI has become an important tool in healthcare, especially in text-processing processes. EXPERIMENTS AND RESULTS In our study, we conducted extensive experiments to evaluate the performance of different LLMs in summarizing scientific texts. 14,492 scientific articles in the biomedical field obtained from PubMed were processed by 10 different LLMs and separate abstractive and extractive summaries were generated for each. In the study, our baseline model is illustrated in Fig 3. The experiments were designed to address the research questions defined in the Introduction section. Semantic similarity analysis was performed using SBERT, and n-gram-based metrics such as BLEU, GLEU, and ROUGE were used to evaluate the generated summaries. The details of the experiments are presented in Section IV-A Dataset and the experimental setup and inferences will be discussed in Section IV-B Experimental Settings, the evaluation of the summarization methods is presented in Section IV-C, and Section IV-D Results and Discussion presents and discusses our results. A. DATASET In this study, biomedical texts obtained from the PubMed database were used to evaluate the performance of LLMs in summarizing scientific literature. PubMed is one of the most comprehensive and reliable resources in the biomedical field, and the articles it contains reflect important develop- ments and innovations in medical research and healthcare worldwide. Therefore, the PubMed database was chosen as an ideal source to test the accuracy and general validity of summarizing biomedical texts. The dataset used in this study consists of 14,492 verified biomedical research meticulously selected from PubMed via the NCBI Entrez API. To construct our dataset, we first thoroughly reviewed the most commonly used medical terms and recent literature. Based on the objectives of our study, we identified relevant key terms. Using these terms, we retrieved approximately 5,000 articles for each term from PubMed via the NCBI Entrez API. The collected articles were then filtered based on criteria such as publication type (e.g., research articles), publication date, language (English only), and direct relevance to the terms (e.g., MeSH terms), as shown Table 3. Subsequently, the articles were manually reviewed for quality and relevance, and only those appropriate for our dataset were selected. Irrelevant or low- quality studies were excluded during this process. This approach enabled us to compile a reliable, relevant, and up- to-date dataset aligned with our research goals. Each article in the dataset was processed with 10 different LLMs that reflect scientific knowledge with the highest accuracy. For each model, abstractive and extractive summarization methods VOLUME 13, 2025 152693 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization FIGURE 3. System Architecture of the Study. were applied using special prompts for each summarization technique. The LLM-based text summarization section which is the summarization phase is explained step by step in Table 4. As seen in the table, each scientific text we obtained from PubMed was given as input to different LLMs with a prompt command, and a summary was requested to be created according to the prompts. The selection process aimed to ensure that the dataset was diverse and representative of the broad spectrum of topics within biomedical research. To achieve this, the articles were carefully chosen to cover various biomedical sub-disciplines, ranging from medical and genetic research to treatment approaches. The selection of articles from various biomedical fields as shown in Fig 4, was intentional and strategic. The dataset covers broad categories such as psychiatry, hematology, dermatology, allergy and immunology, and urology and incorporates subcategories within these major fields. This diversification is critical for capturing the nuances of the biomedical domain, as each field and subfield brings unique methodologies, research focuses, and clinical insights. The inclusion of such a wide range of topics was designed to ensure that the dataset reflects the multidimensional nature of biomedical research, spanning medical knowl- edge, genetic discoveries, treatment strategies, and emerging technologies. The LLMs include the most advanced language models — Qwen, Mistral, Phi, Zephyr, Cohere, GPT, Claude, Llama, Gemini, and Falcon. For abstractive summarization, the models were directed to generate new, coherent summaries by restating the original content to summarize the main points of each article. For extractive summarization, the models were directed to generate summaries that preserve the essential information in its original form by directly extracting the most important sentences or phrases from the text.
