EVALUATION CRITERIA FOR LLMS In the current study, we used several evaluation metrics frequently used in NLP to evaluate model performance. These are Sentence-BERT, BLEU, GLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE. 1) SENTENCE-BERT Sentence-BERT (SBERT) is a model developed to extract meaning at the sentence level. This model can be used to measure the semantic similarity of sentences more quickly and efficiently using Siamese BERT networks. The Siamese structure uses a common linear layer to process two sentences simultaneously and measure their similarity tasks [63]. SBERT is a model obtained by fine-tuning the pre-trained BERT language model. The pooling process is added to the output of the traditional BERT model. This process creates a fixed-length sentence vector by taking the average of all embeddings in the output (mean strategy). Model weights are updated with twin and ternary networks. Mean Squared Error (MSE) shown in Eq 10 is used to calculate similarity [64]. MSE(y, f (x)) = 1 n n X i=1 (yi −f (xi))2 (10) MSE is the average of the squared difference between the model’s predictions and the actual values. In this equation, yi actual values, f (xi) model’s prediction, and n represent the number of data. Since the difference is squared, high errors have more impact, and as MSE decreases, the accuracy of the model increases. SBERT performs strongly, especially in NLP applications such as semantic text similarity, information search, and Q&A systems. This method is also more suitable for sentence similarity measurements and has achieved high accuracy rates in general text-processing. 2) BLEU BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) calculates the similarity between the generated text and the reference text by comparing n-grams (word sequences). First, the formula in Eq 11 is used to check how often the n-grams in the generated text occur in the real text. Pn = P dk( ˆφ) P min(dk(φ), dk( ˆφ)) (11) dk(φ) and dk( ˆφ) represent the number of n-grams in the real text and the generated text, respectively. Since short sentences can have high precision values, BLEU metrics apply a penalty according to the sentence length. Here, the penalty score is calculated in Eq 12, where r represents the length of the real text and c represents the length of the generated text. Brevity Penalty = ( 1 if r < c e(1−r c ) if r ≥c (12) As a result, the final result of BLEU is calculated as in Eq 13: BLEUn = min  1 −r c, 0  + N X n=1 wn log(pn) ! (13) Here, wn is the weight for each n-gram and pn is the n-gram accuracy [65]. 3) GLEU In addition to the BLEU metric, the GLEU metric also evaluates the grammar and fluency of the compared text. The difference between GLEU and BLEU is that it considers not only the correctness of n-grams but also the semantic integrity and grammatical structure of the sentence. GLEU calculates recall using the ratio of the generated n- grams to the total n-grams in the reference text. Then, the ratio of the number of generated n-grams to the total number of n- grams produced by the model is used to calculate precision, and the final result of GLEU is calculated as the minimum of the recall and precision values, as expressed by Eq 14 [65]. GLEU = min(Recall, Precision) (14) 4) METEOR METEOR is a metric that helps measure similarities and meaning in language by considering features such as word matches, roots, and synonyms in texts. METEOR calculates a score by considering the number of words that match and the order of these matches. This score is based on the formula shown in Eq 15: P = m t (15) This formula states that the P (precision) value is the ratio between the number of matching unigrams (m) and the total number of model unigrams (t). Then, how well the words in the reference text match the text generated by the model is calculated as shown in Eq 16. Here, r is the total number of unigrams in the reference text. R = m r (16) 152698 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization TABLE 5. Comparison of AI Models Based on key attributes. Then, Fmean - harmonic mean is calculated using the α parameter which determines the balance between precision and recall as shown in Eq 17. Fmean = P · R α · P + (1 −α) · R (17) Finally, the METEOR score is calculated as follows (Eq 18): METEOR score = (1 −(γ · fragβ)) · Fmean (18) In 18, γ determines the maximum value of the penalty, and β defines the relationship between fragmentation and the penalty. Penalty is applied based on the order distortions of the matching words in the texts. The penalty is calculated depending on the fragmentation rate and the parameters γ and β. As a result, a match with high accuracy and low-order shifts will have a higher score [66]. 5) ROUGE ROUGE (Recall Subroutine for Gisting Evaluation) is used to evaluate the overlap between the generated and the reference texts. The aim is to determine how well the model preserves important information from the original document [7]. There are variants of this metric that measure the quality of summaries by counting overlapping units such as n-grams, word sequences, and word pairs. The ROUGE-N metric measures the n-gram matches between the reference summary (C) and the model-generated summary (S) and is VOLUME 13, 2025 152699 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization calculated by the formula shown in Eq 19 [22]: ROUGE-N = P gramn∈S Count(gramn) P gramn∈S Countmatch(gramn) (19) n is the n-gram length, gramn represents the n-grams in C and S, Countmatch(gramn) is the count of matching n-grams between C and S, and Count(gramn) is the total n-grams in S. ROUGE focuses on recall and measures how well the summary contains the information in the original text. D. RESULTS In this section, we present the evaluation results of different summarization methods and LLMs. The discussion is divided into three parts: in Section IV-D1 Metric-Based Evaluation compares the summarization performance based on various metrics; in Section IV-D2 Summary Method-Based Evalua- tion evaluates the effectiveness of abstractive and extractive approaches; and in Section IV-D3 Model-Based Evaluation analyzes the performance of different language models for their general characteristics. All the results are presented in Table 6 and Table 7. These analyses aim to provide a comprehensive understanding of the methods’ performance and their ability to preserve essential information. 1) METRIC-BASED EVALUATION According to the SBERT metric, Mistral (0.961) has the highest score and produces the summaries closest to the original texts. Falcon (0.881) has the lowest score and produces more different semantic structures compared to the other models. Qwen, Llama, Phi, Zephyr, GPT, and Claude have different degrees of closeness to the original texts with scores ranging from 0.90 to 0.95. These results reveal the differences in the language understanding of the models and the semantic similarity of the summaries they produce. In the evaluation with BLEU and GLEU metrics, Zephyr performed best by achieving the highest BLEU (0.226) and GLEU (0.431) scores. Mistral (BLEU: 0.202, GLEU: 0.386) came in second, while Cohere, Phi, and Qwen performed moderately. Falcon and GPT are among the models with the lowest scores. However, low BLEU and GLEU do not indicate that the models produce poor semantic summaries; they only indicate that the similarity at the word level is low, which is expected for abstractive summarization. Since METEOR takes into account synonyms and root forms in addition to word matches, these results show the semantic closeness of the models’ summaries to the original texts. In this context, Zephyr (0.521) showed the best performance by getting the highest score, while Falcon (0.234) and Gemini (0.215) got the lowest scores. Fig 5 presents a comparison of both summarization methods on metrics - SBERT, BLEU, GLEU, and METEOR metrics. When we look at the ROUGE values in Fig 6 and Fig 7, models such as Zephyr and Mistral have higher scores on ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-L metrics, indicating that they produce meaningful and coherent abstractive summaries. However, ROUGE-2 scores may be low in the context of abstractive summaries, since words may be expressed differently in such summaries. The low scores of models such as Falcon, Claude, Llama, and Gemini indicate that these models have difficulty in achieving coherence and are limited in producing more meaningful summaries. Overall, for abstractive summarization, Zephyr and Mistral performed well on all five metrics, indicating that they produce successful summaries in terms of both semantic accuracy and word and sentence level overlap. Models such as Falcon, Claude, Llama, and Gemini performed lower, indicating that word and sentence level overlaps are insufficient due to the difficulty of abstractive summarization. GPT (0.978) and Claude (0.968) are the models with the highest SBERT scores for the extractive summarization task. These models show high semantic similarity with the original text. Mistral (0.707) and Gemini (0.934) have lower SBERT scores compared to other models, reflecting their weaker semantic similarity with the original text. The extractive summarization results show a wide per- formance difference between the models. GPT performed best by getting the highest scores (BLEU: 0.482, GLEU: 0.607, METEOR: 0.663), summarizing the main messages and structure of the original text best. Claude came in second (BLEU: 0.440, GLEU: 0.554, METEOR: 0.578). Falcon produced poor summaries in terms of language usage, getting the lowest results (BLEU: 0.038, GLEU: 0.061, METEOR: 0.075). The other models performed at an average level, partially reflecting the main messages of the text. When the extractive summarization results are evaluated according to ROUGE metrics, GPT and Claude are the promi- nent models. GPT exhibited the most successful performance by achieving high results in all ROUGE metrics (ROUGE- 1: Precision: 0.911, Recall: 0.681, F1: 0.779; ROUGE-2: Precision: 0.721, Recall: 0.543, F1: 0.619; ROUGE-L: Precision: 0.85, Recall: 0.637, F1: 0.728). Claude (ROUGE- 1: Precision: 1.0, Recall: 0.564, F1: 0.720; ROUGE-2: Precision: 0.971, Recall: 0.545, F1: 0.697; ROUGE-L: Precision: 1.0, Recall: 0.564, F1: 0.720) came in second by achieving high scores, especially achieving excellent results in ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-L. However, Falcon received the lowest results (ROUGE-1: Precision: 0.130, Recall: 0.105, F1: 0.1; ROUGE-2: Precision: 0.038, Recall: 0.043, F1: 0.040; ROUGE-L: Precision: 0.103, Recall: 0.073, F1: 0.071), indicating that the summaries were inadequate in meaning and weak in language use. In response to the RQ1 research question, significant differences were observed between the abilities of different LLMs to effectively capture the main ideas and structure of the text. Consequently, the general result of metric-based evaluation is that in terms of abstractive summarization, models such as Zephyr and Mistral showed excellent performance, demon- strating strong capabilities in understanding the meaning and context of biomedical texts. In particular, the general success of both summarization methods is evaluated using SBERT, BLEU, GLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE F-measure metrics. Abstractive summarization is an approach that reconstructs the meaning and summarizes the text more flexibly, while extractive summarization offers a more word-based solution by taking direct sections from the source text. In light of this information, the performance differences between the two methods are analyzed on important criteria such as word similarities and semantic consistency. In Fig 8, the average results of all models are presented for SBERT, BLEU, GLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE-F1 score values. The purpose of taking the average of all models here is to evaluate the overall performance of LLMs for two summarization methods. In the ROUGE metric, we made an evaluation based on the F1 score because the F1 score is a balanced combination of precision and recall. We aimed to measure the balance between precision and recall of the model correctly by using the F-measure because evaluation based on a single metric can sometimes be misleading. In this way, it provides a measurement where both metrics are taken into account, and whether the model summarizes accu- rately (precision) and comprehensively (recall) is evaluated together. According to the overall performance results shown in the graph, in response to RQ2, some performance differ- ences were observed between the abstractive and extractive summarization methods. Abstractive summarization scored higher in semantic-based metrics such as SBERT an approach that performed better in preserving meaning and context. This shows that abstractive summarization produces more coherent and meaningful summaries by reconstructing the source text. Extractive summarization scored higher in word-matching-based metrics such as BLEU, GLEU, and ROUGE F-measure. This shows that extractive summariza- tion provides more accurate matches at the word level by directly quoting the source text, but sometimes it cannot preserve the semantic integrity sufficiently. As a result, in response to RQ2, abstractive summarization seems to be more effective for semantic and contextual integrity. For word-based accuracy and partially correct summarization, the extractive summarization approach performs more effectively in summarization tasks. For RQ3, abstractive summarization can preserve the meaning as a whole by reconstructing the text and providing contextual understanding. Since extractive summarization is a more word-oriented approach, it can be successful in conveying the essential information of the text correctly, but it may have difficulty in semantic and contextual integrity. This is shown by the high F-measure scores in ROUGE metrics, but its capacity to produce a more natural and contextual summary is lower than abstractive. As a result, abstractive summarization provides stronger semantic and contextual integrity while conveying essential information, while extractive summarization provides higher word accuracy and originality, but maintains integrity in a more limited way. While evaluating the performance of the models, the importance of the summary length was also taken into consideration in terms of both preserving the integrity of the meaning and conveying the essence of the text. The lengths of the summaries produced by the models of the abstractive and extractive summarization methods will be evaluated by considering the ratio between the original article summaries and the produced summaries. This approach aims to measure more accurately how accurately the produced summaries reflect the integrity and essence of the meaning of the original summary. Fig 9 presents the average number of words and the number of unique words in the abstract of the original text and the abstract of the generated summary. In general, abstractive summaries are longer and contain more unique words than extractive summaries in most models. The Zephyr model produces the longest and most diverse summaries, while the Gemini model produces the shortest and least diverse summaries. In terms of the number of unique words, the Zephyr and Owen models produce summaries with the richest vocabulary, while the Gemini and Falcon produce summaries with the most limited vocabulary. While there are significant differences between the abstractive and extractive methods in some models such as Mistral, Phi, and Owen, this difference remains minimal in models such as Claude and Gemini. As a result, models such as Zephyr are more suitable for users who want more detailed and diverse summaries, while models such as Gemini can be preferred for texts that contain more concise information. These explanations also answer for RQ4. Abstractive summaries are usually longer and contain more unique words than extractive summaries. As a result, longer summaries (e.g., Zephyr) can contain more context and detail, which can prevent information loss. However, very long summaries can sometimes make it difficult for the reader to grasp the information quickly. Short summaries (e.g., Gemini) can be more concise and clear but may cause important information to be missed. In academic research or clinical decision support systems, longer and more contextual summaries may 152702 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. STATISTICAL SIGNIFICANCE ANALYSIS To verify whether the observed differences in summarization performance between large language models are statistically significant, we performed pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests across four evaluation metrics: SBERT, ROUGE-L, BLEU, and GLEU. This non-parametric test is appropriate for evaluating paired model outputs that may not follow normal distributions. For each summarization paradigm (abstractive and extrac- tive), we conducted pairwise tests for selected model comparisons. These visualizations reinforce the conclusions derived from the Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, offering intuitive support for the claim that high-performing models not only outperform others in average score but also deliver more stable and reliable outputs across samples. VI. EXPERT EVALUATION AND METRIC COMPARISON To ensure the reliability of summarization in biomedical contexts, we conducted a detailed human evaluation involv- ing three board-certified radiologists (denoted R1, R2, and R3), each with over 7 years of experience. This evaluation complements automatic metrics by offering clinical judgment on output quality. Ten representative LLMs were assessed: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, GPT-3.5, Cohere, Phi-3, LLaMA, Mistral, BERT- Sum, and SciBERT. For each model, 10 summaries were 152706 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization FIGURE 9. Comparison of Average Word Count and Unique Word Count in Summaries Generated by Different LLMs. randomly selected and anonymized. Experts were blinded to model identity to avoid bias. The following four dimensions were rated on a 5-point Likert scale (1 = Very Poor, 5 = Excellent): • Factuality – Are all clinical facts accurate and grounded in the source? • Coverage – Is essential information completely and correctly captured? • Coherence – Is the summary logically organized and readable? • Conciseness – Is the information presented without redundancy? Each summary was scored by all three experts. Inter-rater agreement was calculated using Fleiss’ Kappa (κ = 0.72), indicating substantial agreement across annotators. Table 10 reports the mean and standard deviation for each model and dimension. GPT-4 consistently outperformed others, with high factuality (4.73 ± 0.29) and coherence (4.75 ± 0.24). Claude and Gemini also received favorable scores. Conversely, models like Phi-3 and SciBERT scored below 3.5 across multiple criteria, reflecting issues with factual integrity and information coverage. TABLE 10. Expert ratings (mean ± SD) for 10 LLMs across 4 criteria. Automated scores (e.g., SBERT, ROUGE-L, BLEU) showed partial alignment with human assessments. GPT-4 and Claude consistently ranked highest in both paradigms, VOLUME 13, 2025 152707 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization FIGURE 10. Comparison of LLMs by Size. affirming their superior summarization quality. However, several important divergences were identified: • GPT-3.5 showed strong semantic similarity via SBERT but missed key clinical concepts–reflected in lower human coverage ratings. • Phi-3 and Cohere produced summaries that appeared fluent and scored moderately on ROUGE but were criticized by experts for hallucinations and omissions. • SciBERT and BERTSum, while optimized for extrac- tive summaries, received relatively consistent but mediocre ratings across both automatic and expert assessments. Our findings confirm that while automatic metrics are valuable for large-scale benchmarking, they are insufficient proxies for clinical reliability. Expert assessment is critical in biomedical applications where factual accuracy and content completeness directly impact decision-making. We recommend a dual-evaluation strategy where human- in-the-loop validation is used to detect hallucination, under- specification, and logical inconsistency. This is especially essential in contexts such as diagnostic radiology, discharge summaries, and medical guideline distillation. Future directions include incorporating structured evalua- tion protocols, cross-specialty annotator panels, and hybrid metrics combining semantic similarity with factuality valida- tors based on biomedical knowledge graphs. VII. DISCUSSION In this study, we evaluated the performance of 10 different LLMs in summarization tasks, using both abstractive and extractive approaches. The results indicate that model archi- tecture significantly affects summarization quality, with some models excelling in semantic understanding while others perform better in surface-level alignment. These findings 152708 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization FIGURE 11. Comparison of LLMs by Specialization. align with those of Nallapati et al. [67], who highlighted that abstractive methods produce more meaningful and coherent summaries. An important insight from our analysis is that models with instruction-following capabilities perform better in both semantic similarity (measured by SBERT) and surface- level alignment metrics (such as BLEU and ROUGE) compared to models without such capabilities. This supports the findings of Wei et al.’s study [68]. They emphasize that instruction-following models tend to provide more accurate and informative summaries, especially by preserving contextual integrity. Additionally, it was observed that while extractive meth- ods generally provide higher surface-level alignment (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE), they struggle to capture the deeper meaning of the text. In contrast, abstractive methods, though less precise in surface-level metrics, produce more comprehensive and coherent summaries. This result is in line with the findings of Lopyrev et al. [69], who noted that extractive summarization methods often perform better in capturing surface-level similarities, but abstractive approaches are more suited for capturing deeper semantic understanding. This suggests that abstractive methods offer significant advantages in tasks requiring a more nuanced understanding of content, particularly when combined with models that maintain contextual relevance. The performance differences across model sizes also highlight the trade-off between computational resources and summarization quality. Smaller models, despite their limited capacity, demonstrated notable performance on certain tasks. This finding parallels Radford et al. [70], who showed that smaller models, with proper fine-tuning, can perform more successfully on specific tasks, even with fewer resources. Overall, our study contributes to the ongoing discussion regarding the effectiveness of extractive versus abstractive summarization approaches, reinforcing the need for a bal- anced approach that considers both surface-level alignment and deeper semantic understanding. ABLATION STUDY To provide a more comprehensive perspective on the performance of LLMs, we conducted an ablation study that includes several widely used baseline summarization methods, encompassing both unsupervised and transformer- based approaches. In particular, we evaluated TextRank [71] and LexRank [72] as representative extractive models, and BERTSum [73] and SciBERT [74] as transformer-based architectures. Unlike LLMs, these models do not rely on prompt-based generation and are traditionally developed for either extractive or abstractive summarization tasks. Nevertheless, their inclusion offers valuable insight into how LLMs compare with established summarization techniques in the literature. Table 11 presents the comparative results of these four models, highlighting differences in both semantic similarity and surface-level overlap with reference summaries. TABLE 11. SBERT, BLEU, GLEU and METEOR Scores for classical summarization models. The models shown in the table are structurally based on either extractive (TextRank, LexRank) or abstractive (SciBERT, BERTSum) summarization paradigms. Therefore, the natural working principles of these models are to apply specific summarization strategies rather than to produce flexible answers for different tasks by being sensitive to prompts as in LLMs. The models achieved the highest SBERT scores (0.926), indicating that they produce results semantically close to the reference summaries. However, in word and n-gram- based metrics such as BLEU and METEOR, they fall behind BERTSum. This suggests that extractive models, which directly select sentences from the source text, perform well in maintaining structural similarity but are limited in terms of lexical and expression diversity. BERTSum demonstrated the best performance in BLEU (0.419) and METEOR (0.599) scores, indicating its superior- ity in both lexical variety and accuracy. On the other hand, SciBERT generally showed lower performance compared to the other models, which may reflect suboptimal application or fine-tuning for the summarization task. A significant limitation of these traditional models, espe- ciallyared to prompt-based LLM approaches, is their lack of flexibility to adapt to prompts and task-specific requirements. Extractive models primarily select important sentences from the source, while abstractive models generate sum- maries based on pre-learned language patterns. Therefore, the insensitivity of these models to prompts limits their ability to accommodate flexible task definitions and diverse summarization styles. This constraint particularly highlights the performance boundaries of traditional models when contrasted with LLMs, which offer multitask learning capabilities and strong contextual understanding. Table 12 presents the ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE- L Precision, Recall, and F1 scores for four classical summarization models: TextRank, LexRank, SciBERT, and BertSum. Across all models, the ROUGE Precision scores are remarkably high (ranging approximately between 0.98 and 1.00), indicating that the generated summaries closely match the reference summaries in terms of content overlap. VOLUME 13, 2025 152711 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization TABLE 12. ROUGE metrics results of classical summarization models. However, the Recall values show greater variability, reflect- ing differences in how much of the reference content is successfully captured. Among the models, BertSum demonstrates the best overall performance with the highest Recall and F1 scores (e.g., ROUGE-1 F1 = 0.733), suggesting its superior ability to pro- duce both content-rich and concise summaries. In contrast, SciBERT yields lower Recall (0.31) and consequently lower F1 scores, indicating a more limited coverage of the reference text. These results can be explained not only by the metric outputs but also by the underlying nature of the sum- marization approaches. TextRank and LexRank are purely extractive methods, selecting sentences directly from the source text based on graph-based scoring mechanisms. SciB- ERT and BertSum, although based on neural architectures and contextual embeddings, still operate within a supervised extractive framework, classifying sentences for inclusion in the summary. Unlike prompt-based LLM summarizers, which typically follow an abstractive paradigm by generating summaries through natural language generation from scratch, the models evaluated here do not benefit from such open-ended generation capabilities. As such, their outputs tend to be more rigid, with limited paraphrasing and novelty. In this context, BertSum’s superior performance may stem from its deep learning architecture, which allows it to model contextual importance more effectively even within the constraints of extractive summarization. Nevertheless, the high Precision scores across all models reflect their strength in selecting relevant content, while the Recall and F1 scores illustrate the trade-offs between conciseness and comprehen- siveness inherent in classical, extractive approaches. The results reveal that classical summarization approaches- whether graph-based like TextRank and LexRank or neural extractive models such as SciBERT and BertSum-are effective at identifying salient information from scientific texts, as evidenced by consistently high ROUGE precision scores. However, their performance is inherently bounded by their extractive nature, limiting their ability to paraphrase, reorganize, or generate novel phrasings beyond the input. This limitation contrasts sharply with prompt-based LLMs, which typically operate in an abstractive mode, generating summaries that can synthesize and rephrase content more flexibly. Unlike traditional methods, LLMs do not rely on predefined sentence extraction or supervised binary classification but instead adapt their outputs based on task-specific prompts, allowing for greater diversity and fluency. As a result, while classical models may be efficient and structurally consistent, they often lack the semantic richness and generative capacity afforded by modern LLMs–a distinction that is increasingly relevant in summarization tasks requiring contextual abstraction and human-like coherence. IX. CONCLUSION In this study, we compared the performance of different LLMs in biomedical scientific texts in extractive and abstractive summarization methods and examined the effects of model features such as size, specialized skills, instruction- following capacity, etc. on summarization processes. The analyses revealed that abstractive summarization provided superior results in preserving the integrity of meaning and context, while extractive summarization showed higher success in word-based accuracy. While abstractive sum- marization produces longer and more contextually rich summaries, extractive summarization provides shorter and word-based accurate summaries. This distinction is partic- ularly important in the medical domain, where preserving critical information while maintaining readability is essential. Abstractive summarization may be more beneficial for generating clinical summaries, patient reports, or medical literature reviews, whereas extractive summarization can be advantageous for structured data extraction in electronic health records (EHRs) and clinical guidelines. When the size of the models is examined, large models generally exhibit strong performance in extractive sum- marization, while medium-sized models show balanced performance. Small-sized models have achieved exceptional results, especially in extractive summarization tasks. These findings show that large models perform better in processing more complex texts, but medium-sized and small models can provide very good results in certain situations. For instance, in resource-limited healthcare settings, smaller models could offer effective summarization solutions with lower com- putational costs while maintaining sufficient accuracy for 152712 VOLUME 13, 2025 T. Celikten, A. Onan: Benchmarking LLMs for Biomedical Literature Summarization practical applications. This study emphasizes that more comprehensive choices should be made, considering factors such as model size and specialized capabilities, to increase the efficiency of summarization models and produce more accurate summaries. It also concludes that models with instruction-following capabilities may be more effective in certain contexts, but this may limit performance and speed. Future studies may focus on further domain-specific fine- tuning of LLMs to enhance summarization performance in complex biomedical contexts such as radiology reports, clinical guidelines, and patient discharge notes. Integrat- ing structured medical knowledge–such as ontologies and knowledge graphs–into the summarization process could improve factual grounding, reduce hallucinations, and ensure terminological consistency. Although this study includes a structured expert evaluation of the generated summaries, future efforts can further enhance validation by incorporating multispecialist panels, larger evaluation sets, consensus-based rating schemes, and more extensive qualitative assessments. These expansions will provide deeper insights into semantic accuracy, rele- vance, and informativeness, ensuring the practical applica- bility of summarization models in real-world biomedical contexts. Another promising direction is the exploration of multilingual and cross-lingual summarization models, espe- cially for under-resourced clinical languages, which would support more equitable access to medical AI technologies. Finally, deploying summarization models in real-world decision-support systems–such as hospital EHR interfaces or biomedical literature review pipelines–and evaluating their impact on clinician workflow and decision accuracy remains a vital step for clinical integration and translational relevance. Finally, this study’s findings contribute to the advance- ment of AI-based summarization technologies in healthcare, paving the way for improved clinical decision support systems, automated medical documentation, and enhanced access to biomedical knowledge.
