ETHICS APPROVAL AND CONSENT TO PARTICIPATE As this study does not involve human participants or personal data, ethical approval was not required. The study was conducted in accordance with relevant guidelines and regulations. CONSENT FOR PUBLICATION As this study does not involve human participants or personal data, consent for publication was not required. DATA AVAILABILITY Not applicable. MATERIALS AVAILABILITY Not applicable. CODE AVAILABILITY Not applicable. AUTHOR CONTRIBUTION The authors have contributed equally to the conceptualiza- tion, methodology, and writing of this manuscript. They reviewed the results and approved the final version of the manuscript. REF

title: Received 19 December 2025, accepted 1 February 2026, date of publication 9 February 2026, date of current version 18 February 2026.
file_name: Beyond Surface Metrics Modeling Abstractivity in Summarization via Action-Based Spectrums.pdf
text: Received 19 December 2025, accepted 1 February 2026, date of publication 9 February 2026, date of current version 18 February 2026. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3662813 Beyond Surface Metrics: Modeling Abstractivity in Summarization via Action-Based Spectrums VICENT AHUIR 1, MARÍA-JOSÉ CASTRO-BLEDA 1,2, AND LLUÍS-F. HURTADO1,2 1Valencia Research Institute for Artificial Intelligence (VRAIN), Universitat Politécnica de Valéncia, 46022 Valéncia, Spain 2Valencian Graduate School and Research Network of Artificial Intelligence (ValgrAI), Universitat Politécnica de Valéncia, 46022 Valéncia, Spain Corresponding author: Vicent Ahuir (vahuir@dsic.upv.es) This work was supported in part by MICIU/AEI/10.13039/501100011033 and FEDER, EU, under Project PID2024-155948OB-C55; and in part by Spanish Ministerio de Universidades for University Teacher Training under Grant FPU21/05288. ABSTRACT Traditional views of abstractive summarization treat it as a free-form rephrasing of the source text, yet this notion is vague and difficult to measure; existing evaluations rely on surface-level metrics — such as novel n-gram counts, extractive fragment coverage, or extractive fragment density— that obscure the stylistic nuances that distinguish summaries along an abstractivity spectrum. We therefore propose a fine- grained, continuous characterization that does not claim to unify extractive and abstractive summarization into a single continuous dimension, but rather presents them as two endpoints of a spectrum defined by the frequency of text-transformation actions (sentence reduction, combination, paraphrasing, reordering, etc.) drawn from the foundational summarization literature. To operationalize this view, we introduce CLAsum, a human-annotated dataset comprising 600 unique samples; these arise from 200 document–summary pairs sourced from CNN/DailyMail and XSum datasets, each annotated by three independent annotators who assign one of five perceived abstractivity levels and record the prevalence of seven abstractivity-inducing actions per pair. Our analysis shows a strong Pearson’s correlation of 0.6 between the overall frequency of these actions and human perception of abstractivity, which validates the spectrum. Moreover, automatic prediction of the resulting Abstractivity-Inducing Features (AIFs) captures signals that existing metrics miss and yields significant performance gains of up to 9.1% in abstractivity-level measurement on the evaluated regression methods. This work delivers both conceptual clarity and practical tools for diagnosing and informing the design of controllable, style-aware summarization systems capable of producing summaries with precisely specified levels of abstractivity, thereby advancing the field toward truly user- tailored, task-adaptive summarization. INDEX TERMS Text summarization, abstractivity modeling, abstractive summarization, extractive summarization, controllable summarization, human-annotated dataset, linguistic actions. I. INTRODUCTION Automatic text summarization aims to condense the most relevant information from a source document into a shorter text. Traditionally, summarization systems are categorized as either extractive, which select and copy salient sentences verbatim from the source, or abstractive, which generate new text to convey the essential content, often described as being written ‘‘in the author’s own words’’ [1]. While The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Vlad Diaconita . early approaches were predominantly extractive, the advent of Transformer-based architectures [2] has made abstractive summarization the dominant paradigm. Despite this shift, the fundamental question of what makes a summary abstractive remains insufficiently characterized. In practice, abstractivity is typically quantified using surface- level heuristics, such as the proportion of novel n-grams or compression ratios [3], [4], [5]. Although useful, these metrics largely equate abstractivity with lexical novelty. However, a summary may be highly abstractive even when introducing few new words—for instance by extensively VOLUME 14, 2026 2026 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ 23983 V. Ahuir et al.: Beyond Surface Metrics: Modeling Abstractivity in Summarization reordering content, merging information from multiple sentences, or simplifying complex structures [6]. As a result, existing metrics fail to capture the full range of linguistic strategies humans employ when abstracting text. This limitation is particularly relevant in the context of controllable summarization. Recent work highlights abstrac- tivity as a key controllable attribute alongside length, content focus, and style [7], [8]. In many real-world applications— such as medical reporting, legal analysis, or journalism— the acceptable degree and type of rewriting varies across audiences. Experts may require summaries that closely preserve terminology and structure, whereas non-expert readers may benefit from summaries that aggressively paraphrase, simplify, and fuse information. Enabling such control requires abstractivity to be modeled in a way that is interpretable, measurable, and aligned with human perception. A linguistically grounded perspective on abstraction was introduced by Jing [9], who identified six core actions used by professional summarizers: sentence reduction, sentence combination, syntactic transformation, lexical paraphrase, generalization/specification, and content reordering. These actions describe how source content is transformed during abstraction, independently of surface lexical novelty. We hypothesize that abstractivity is not a binary property, but a continuous perception shaped by the frequency and combination of these actions. Under this view, extractive and abstractive summarization form the endpoints of a single spectrum, with intermediate levels corresponding to different abstraction strategies. In this work, we formalize this intuition by introduc- ing the notion of a level of abstractivity, a continuous dimension that provides diagnostic and analytic foundations for understanding extractive and abstractive summariza- tion. We operationalize this concept through Abstractivity- Inducing Features (AIFs), which quantify the presence of the abstraction actions identified by Jing [9]. To support empirical investigation, we introduce CLAsum, a human- annotated dataset comprising 600 unique samples from 200 document-summary pairs from CNN/DailyMail and XSum, annotated by three independent annotators. Each sample was labeled in one of the five levels of perceived abstractivity and fine-grained annotations of abstraction actions. Beyond dataset construction, we investigate whether these action-based features provide information beyond existing abstractivity metrics. Through analysis and prediction exper- iments, we show that standard metrics alone cannot reliably capture human perceptions of abstractivity, whereas AIFs provide complementary and necessary information. More- over, incorporating AIFs significantly improves abstractivity level prediction, demonstrating their practical utility. Even in the era of large language models, explicit abstrac- tivity modeling remains essential. While LLMs generate fluent summaries, they offer limited transparency and control over how source material is transformed. The proposed framework provides interpretable dimensions along which summarization behavior can be analyzed, evaluated, and ultimately controlled. Contributions. We summarize our main contributions as follows: (i) We propose a continuous, action-based model of abstractivity that provides diagnostic and analytic foun- dations for extractive and abstractive summarization along a single interpretable spectrum. (ii) We introduce and release CLAsum, a human-annotated dataset labeled with five abstractivity levels and seven abstractivity-inducing actions. It is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ELiRF/clasum (iii) We provide a detailed analysis of annotation quality and demonstrate strong correlations between abstraction actions and perceived abstractivity. (iv) We define two prediction tasks—AIF prediction and abstractivity level prediction—and show empirically that AIFs capture information not fully contained in existing metrics. (v) We demonstrate that incorporating AIFs significantly improves abstractivity prediction, supporting their rel- evance for future controllable summarization systems. II. RELATED WORK A. CHARACTERIZING ABSTRACTIVITY IN SUMMARIZATION The dominant approach to quantifying abstractivity in automatic summarization relies on measuring lexical novelty with respect to the source document. Commonly used metrics include the proportion of novel n-grams [4], [5], as well as related measures such as coverage and density [3]. These metrics have been widely adopted to analyze summarization datasets, compare extractive and abstractive systems, and assess the degree of rewriting performed by models. While effective for capturing surface-level differences, such metrics offer a limited view of abstractivity. They conflate lexical novelty with abstraction, failing to distinguish between summaries that introduce new wording through paraphrasing and those that achieve abstraction through structural operations such as sentence fusion, reordering, or reduction with minimal lexical change. Several studies have noted these limitations and emphasized that abstrac- tivity is a more nuanced phenomenon than simple word replacement [6], [9]. Other work has studied the interplay between abstractive- ness and other summary qualities. For example, researchers have analyzed the trade-off between abstractiveness and factuality in generated summaries, showing that increases in abstractiveness can correlate with changes in factual consistency [10]. Alternative perspectives on abstractivity have also been explored indirectly in works analyzing dataset properties or model behaviors [4], [5]. Yet these studies still largely rely on surface statistics or corpus-level patterns. Surveys of 23984 VOLUME 14, 2026 V. Ahuir et al.: Beyond Surface Metrics: Modeling Abstractivity in Summarization summarization evaluation metrics further highlight the need for richer, semantic, and structural dimensions of summary quality beyond lexical matches [11]. In contrast, our work builds on this literature by grounding abstractivity in explicit, interpretable linguistic operations, moving beyond lexical novelty toward a more human-aligned characterization. B. LINGUISTIC ACTIONS AND ABSTRACTION Early work by Jing [9] provides a foundational linguistic analysis of how professional summarizers perform abstrac- tion. As introduced in Section I, this work proposes a set of six core rewriting actions describing how source content is transformed during summarization. This taxonomy has been widely cited as a conceptual explanation of abstractive summarization, but it has rarely been operationalized or empirically validated at scale in modern datasets. Subsequent work has referenced these actions mainly as qualitative explanations or motivating examples, rather than as measurable features [3], [5]. To the best of our knowledge, no prior study has systematically annotated summaries with respect to these actions and examined their relationship to perceived abstractivity across contemporary summarization benchmarks. Our work directly addresses this gap by treating these abstraction actions as measurable dimensions —what we term Abstractivity-Inducing Features (AIFs)— and by empir- ically validating their correlation with human judgments of abstractivity. C. CONTROLLABLE SUMMARIZATION Controllable text summarization has emerged as an important research direction, aiming to allow users or systems to specify attributes of the generated summary, such as length, focus, style, or level of detail [8]. Several recent approaches explore multi-attribute control, often using special tokens, prompts, or learned representations to influence generation behavior. Notably, [7] introduced MACSum, a dataset and frame- work for multi-attribute controllable summarization, in which abstractivity is treated as one of several controllable dimen- sions. However, this work still relies on coarse attribute labels and does not provide explicit supervision for the underlying rewriting operations that produce different abstraction styles. Other work on controlled summarization includes neural models that condition on keywords or control signals for entity-centric or length constraints (e.g., CTRLsum) [12], [13], and models that apply constraints during generation via training frameworks like Constrained Markov Decision Processes to control attributes including abstractiveness [13]. Early neural controllable summarization work also explored the use of explicit user p

title: Received 27 November 2024, accepted 6 December 2024, date of publication 11 December 2024,
file_name: BioMDSum An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method Based on PageRank and Longformer Encoder-Decoder.pdf
text: Received 27 November 2024, accepted 6 December 2024, date of publication 11 December 2024, date of current version 19 December 2024. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2024.3514915 BioMDSum: An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method Based on PageRank and Longformer Encoder-Decoder AZZEDINE AFTISS 1, SALIMA LAMSIYAH 2, SAID OUATIK EL ALAOUI 1, AND CHRISTOPH SCHOMMER 2 1Engineering Sciences Laboratory, National School of Applied Sciences, Ibn Tofail University, Kenitra 14000, Morocco 2Department of Computer Science, Faculty of Science, Technology and Medicine, University of Luxembourg, 4364 Esch-sur-Alzette, Luxembourg Corresponding author: Azzedine Aftiss (azzedine.aftiss@uit.ac.ma) ABSTRACT Biomedical multi-document summarization (BioMDSum) involves automatically generating concise and informative summaries from collections of related biomedical documents. While extractive summarization methods have shown promise, they often produce incoherent summaries. On the other hand, fully abstractive methods yield coherent summaries but demand extensive training datasets and computational resources due to the typically lengthy nature of biomedical documents. To address these challenges, we propose a hybrid summarization method that combines the strengths of both approaches. The proposed method consists of two main phases: (i) an extractive summarization phase that uses k-means clustering to group similar sentences based on their cosine similarity between embeddings generated by the sentence-BERT model, followed by the PageRank algorithm for sentence scoring and selection; and (ii) an abstractive summarization phase that fine-tunes a Longform Encoder-Decoder (LED) transformer model to generate a concise and coherent summary from the sentences selected during the extractive phase. We conducted several experiments on the standard biomedical multi-document summarization datasets Cochrane and MSˆ2. The results demonstrate that the proposed method is competitive and outperforms recent state-of-the-art systems based on ROUGE evaluation measures. Specifically, our model achieved ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, BERTScore, and METEOR scores of 29.41%, 6.57%, 18.31%, 85.95%, and 22.15% on the Cochrane dataset, and 28.79%, 8.22%, 17.93%, 85.51%, and 25.17% on the MSˆ2 dataset, respectively. Furthermore, an ablation analysis shows that integrating extractive and abstractive phases in our hybrid summarization method enhances the overall performance of the proposed approach. INDEX TERMS Biomedical multi-document summarization, hybrid summarization, K-means clustering, sentence-BERT, PageRank algorithm, longformer encoder-decoder. I. INTRODUCTION With the rapid advance of digitization and Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks in the biomedical domain have garnered significant attention. The substantial amount of textual information available in electronic formats, such as biomedical literature, clinical notes, and electronic health records (EHRs) has driven this interest [1]. In particular, The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Shadi Alawneh . the biomedical field has experienced explosive growth in research articles, with around 27 million biomedical articles cited in PubMed [2]. This data revolution has paved the way for new tasks in the biomedical domain, including text summarization [3], named entity recogni- tion [4], and question answering [5], among others. These advancements assist researchers in progressing their work efficiently. Recently, there have been numerous advancements in text document summarization, a process that generates VOLUME 12, 2024 2024 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ 188013 A. Aftiss et al.: BioMDSum: An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method concise summaries from lengthy original texts, effectively capturing the essential information to represent the docu- ment’s subject [6]. However, the biomedical domain has not received as much focus, despite its unique challenges. These challenges include the complexity of lengthy doc- uments and the lack of annotated datasets specific to the field. Addressing these challenges is crucial for enabling researchers and practitioners to efficiently navigate and extract valuable insights from the vast amount of biomedical information available. Therefore, there is a critical need for a tool that assists clinicians and biomedical researchers in efficiently extracting insightful information from text documents, as manual processing is often time-consuming. Automatic text summarization provides a promising solution to the challenge of information overload by systematically highlighting essential content from original documents. It could also enhance comprehension, allowing clinicians and researchers to process large volumes of biomedical literature and reports more effectively, thus increasing productivity and accelerating research tasks. Several methods have been proposed in the literature for text summarization, which can be classified into two main approaches: extractive and abstractive. Extractive summarization methods identify and extract important sentences directly from the original text [7], [8]. While these methods are promising due to their efficiency, the generated summaries often lack coherence and fluency. In contrast, abstractive summarization methods generate summaries word by word, aiming for more coherent and human-like text [9]. However, abstractive summarization is complex, requiring extensive annotated data for training and involving tasks such as semantic representation, inference, and natural language generation. Additionally, biomedical documents are typically lengthy, further complicating the summarization process. To address these challenges, some researchers have adopted a hybrid approach, combining both extractive and abstractive techniques to generate coherent and informative summaries [10]. Furthermore, automatic text summarization can be differentiated by the number of input documents and the purpose of the summarization. Summarization can be categorized as either single-document or multi-document, depending on the number of input documents. While single-document summarization has seen significant development, multi-document summarization has emerged as a challenging branch of research, aiming to create coherent, concise, and fluent summaries from multiple documents [11]. As part of our research, we focus on biomedical multi-document summarization based on hybrid approach. In this paper, we propose a novel hybrid method for biomedical multi-document summarization that combines the strengths of both extractive and abstractive techniques. Specifically, in the extractive phase, we first prepro- cess the input text. Sentences are then encoded using the Sentence-BERT embedding model [12]. A clustering algorithm is applied to group sentences by similar topics, creating distinct clusters. Within each cluster, salient sentences are identified using the PageRank algorithm [13]. In the abstractive phase, we leverage the Longformer Encoder-Decoder (LED) model [14] for the final sum- mary generation. The choice of LED is motivated by the transformative impact of the Transformer architecture on natural language processing (NLP). Transformer models, such as T5 [15], BERT [16], and PEGASUS [17], have set new benchmarks across various NLP tasks. Building on this success, models like LED [14], BigBird [18], and LongT5 [19] have introduced advanced architectures that effectively handle long sequences by combining local and global attention patterns, thereby preserving context. These innovations have significantly improved performance in tasks such as text summarization [20], sentiment analysis [21], question-answering systems [22], information retrieval [23], and text categorization [24]. By utilizing LED, our method benefits from these advancements, enabling the generation of coherent and contextually rich summaries from lengthy biomedical documents. To summarize, the main contributions of this work are as follows: 1) We propose a hybrid biomedical multi-document summarization method, BioMDSum-Hybrid, based on the PageRank Algorithm and the Longformer Encoder- Decoder (LED). 2) We enhance the extractive summarization phase by implementing a clustering approach using the k-means algorithm and the SBERT embedding model. This improves the performance of the PageRank algorithm in identifying salient sentences. 3) We demonstrate that the proposed hybrid method, which combines extractive and abstractive tech- niques, improves the performance of biomedical multi- document summarization. 4) We compare our method with several state-of- the-art methods, including recent supervised deep learning-based methods on the Cochrane and MSˆ2 datasets. Experimental results show that our method achieves competitive performance with state-of-the- art methods using three different metrics (ROUGE, BERTScore, and METEOR). The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: In Section II, we review related work and previous research on biomedical summarization. Section III describes the proposed method. The experimental results are presented in Section IV. Finally, the conclusion and future work are discussed in Section VI. II. RELATED WORK In this work, we propose a hybrid text summarization method for biomedical multi-document summarization based on extractive and abstractive summarization phases. For this reason, we will present recent studies on biomedical text summarization covering extractive, abstractive, and hybrid methods. 188014 VOLUME 12, 2024 A. Aftiss et al.: BioMDSum: An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method A. BIOMEDICAL TEXT SUMMARIZATION Biomedical text summarization involves condensing large documents, whether single or multiple, into coherent sum- maries that highlight important information and sentences from the original text. In the literature, automatic text summarization methods are primarily divided into two main categories: extractive and abstractive. Additionally, some researchers have adopted a hybrid approach that combines both extractive and abstractive techniques to leverage the strengths of each method. Extractive text summarization approaches tend to select the salient sentences as they are from the original text, and the final summary is the concatenation of the extracted sentences. The proposed extractive methods are classified into unsupervised and supervised methods. The unsupervised methods transform the summarization problem into a ranking problem [25]. The segmented sentences are represented in numerical vectors using sentence embedding methods, and an unsupervised ranking algorithm is applied to score the top sentences. For instance, Moradi et al. [26] explored a graph-based approach to model input documents, treating sentences as vertices with weighted connections calculated by computing the cosine similarity between sentence embed- dings. These sentence embeddings were calculated using trained domain-specific word embeddings on a large biomed- ical corpus. To create the final summary, they employed graph ranking algorithms including PageRank, Hyperlinked Induced Topic Search (HITS), and Positional Power Function (PPF) to select the top N-scored sentences. In the same context, Davoodijam et al. [27] have proposed an extractive method called MultiGBS, where input documents were repre- sented as multi-layer graphs including Semantic Layer, Word Layer, and Co-reference Layer. Using this representation, a sentence selection algorithm was applied to create the final summary. A Bayesian extractive summarization method has been proposed by Moradi et al. [28], it utilized the Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) Metathesaurus and feature selection algorithms to extract representative sentences constituting the final summary. Moreover, in [29], the authors mapped the biomedical text into a set of concepts using the UMLS Metathesaurus. Based on the clustering of sentences, frequent itemset mining was employed to extract salient sentences based on the identified frequent itemsets. Supervised methods frame the extractive summarization problem as a binary classification task, where a deep learning model is trained to predict a label for each sentence in the documents [25], [30]. The sentences predicted as 1’s are included to form the extractive summary. For instance, Du et al. [31] have introduced extractive summarization in the biomedical domain named BioBERTSum, which is based on an encoder-decoder architecture. The encoder encodes the input, while the decoder generates scores for each sentence to determine its relevance. Additionally, Xie et al. [32] have utilized PICO (Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome) elements for identifying medical knowledge and injected this knowledge into pre-trained language models using lightweight knowledge adapters known as the Generative Adapter, Discriminative Adapter, and Adapter Infusion. These components help the proposed method in determining which sentences should be included in the final summary. Abstractive summarization aims to generate a summary word-by-word for given input text, Most abstractive sum- marization tasks are based on seq2seq architecture which consists of the two neural networks encoder and decoder [33], the encoder side produces vector representation of input text, where decoder generates the output summary. For instance, Alambo et al. [34] have proposed an abstractive summarization system consisting of two stages: the Entity- driven Knowledge Retriever and the Knowledge-guided Abstractive Summarizer. This method identifies named entities within biomedical documents to guide the abstrac- tive summarization process. Gigioli et al. [35] have used deep reinforcement learning techniques and domain-specific knowledge to train a sequence-to-sequence model to generate concise and informative summaries from biomedical docu- ments. Furthermore, Soleimani et al. [36] have investigated zero-shot aspect-based summarization using the BART model, a pre-trained transformer-based model, to generate summaries for specific aspects of a document. The generated summaries for each aspect are combined to form the final summary. Lalitha et al. [37] have studied the effectiveness of transformer models, specifically seq2seq, including T5 [15], BART [38], and PEGASUS [17], for addressing the challenge of medical document summarization.Tang et al. [39] have leveraged cited papers during the summary generation of the biomedical document. They introduce a novel attention mechanism aimed at integrating all necessary information with large language models. Wallace et al. [40] have explored new fine-tuning strategies for multi-document summarization of randomized controlled trials (RCTs), namely decorations and sorting, for the BART model. Decorations aim to inject PICO elements into the main findings of the input trials while sorting prioritizes high-weight information during summary generation. Guo et al. [41] have incorporated UMLS into the generative model for summarizing medical conversations. The authors utilized a transformer-based model combined with a pointer-generator mechanism. Otmakhova et al. [42] have adapted the PRIMERA model [43] for a biomed- ical multi-document summarization task. They integrated global attention mechanisms that specifically target impor- tant biomedical entities identified using the PICO tag- ger.Moreover, Tangsali et al. [44] and Yu et al. [45] have both investigated the pre-trained large language models, including BART [38], DistilBART [46], T5 [15], and LongT5 [19] for biomedical multi-document summarization task. Their approach involved fine-tuning these models for biomedical multi-document summarization by concatenating all documents into a single input string and feeding it to the language model, to generate high-quality summaries. VOLUME 12, 2024 188015 A. Aftiss et al.: BioMDSum: An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method Hybrid text summarization comprises two essential phases: extractive and abstractive. In the extraction phase, salient sentences are identified from the input text, while the abstraction phase generates an abstract summary from these selected sentences [47]. Generally, many researchers have adapted this framework across various domains due to its efficiency and effectiveness. For instance, Hsu et al. [48] have presented a hybrid method that combines an extractor and an abstracter. The extractor utilized a hierarchical bidirectional GRU architecture to select important sentences from the input text. Meanwhile, the abstractor generates the summary using a pointer-generator network [33]. Similarly, Muniraj et al. [49] have utilized the TextRank algorithm [50] for extractive summarization and they employed a sequence- to-sequence Bidirectional LSTM for generating abstract summaries in the abstractive phase. Moreover, other stud- ies have explored the integration of transformer models within the hybrid approach. For instance, Gidiotis et al. [51] and Bishop et al. [52] have utilized the extractive phase to segment documents into coherent sections. During the abstractive phase, a transformer model was employed to generate a summary for each section, with the final summary being formed by concatenating these gener- ated summaries. Ghadimi et al. [53], Zhang et al. [9], and Li et al. [54] employed a graph-based approach during the extractive phase to select salient sentences. In the abstractive phase, transformer sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) models were fine-tuned to generate coherent summaries from these extracted sentences. In the biomedical domain, there is a lack of research on hybrid summarization, the existing studies follow the same principle established in the generic domain. For instance, Du et al. [55] have proposed a hybrid approach called UGDAS, which consists of two main components: a sentence-level denoiser based on an unsupervised graph network, and an auto-regressive generator. The denoiser aims to clean and improve the quality of the input text by removing noise and redundant sentences, while the auto- regressive generator, specifically BART [38], focuses on generating coherent summaries. Moreover, Nunna et al. [56] have utilized BERTSUM [57] for extractive summarization and BERTSUMABS [57] for generating abstract summaries based on the extracted sentences for covid-19 studies. Givchi et al. [58] have implemented a hybrid summarization system. In the extractive phase, frequent itemset mining is employed on sentence concepts identified using UMLS methods to select salient sentences. In the abstractive phase, the T5 model is utilized to generate abstractive summaries of the extracted sentences. Additionally, Shinde et al. [59] have introduced a summarizer method using an extractive- abstractive approach. In the extractive stage, they utilized the BERT Extractive Summarizer [60] to select the top k sentences from each document. These selected sentences were then concatenated and used as input for the abstractive summarization model known as BigBird-PEGASUS [18]. This abstractive model was fine-tuned specifically for the summarization task. Giorgi et al. [61] have introduced a new approach to tackle the challenges of multi-document summarization. Their method is referred to as retrieve-then- summarize, in the first phase, the method extracts relevant documents based on a given query. These documents are then combined and fed into a transformer-based model, which generates a summary encapsulating the essential information from the input documents. B. LONGFORM ENCODER-DECODER FOR TEXT SUMMARIZATION The traditional sequence-2-sequence (se2seq) transformer- based models, including T5, BART, and PEGASUS, have quadratic complexity concerning sequence length, making them inefficient for handling long documents. As a solu- tion, Beltagy et al. [14] have proposed the LED model, a variant of Longformer designed for generative sequence- to-sequence tasks. It uses a combination of local and global attention mechanisms instead of the standard self-attention mechanism, which is incapable of handling long sequences efficiently. Local attention restricts the attention mechanism to a fixed window around each token, whereas global attention allows certain tokens to attend to all other tokens. This approach reduces the computational and memory overhead for long sequences. A study by Qin et al. [62] demonstrates the effectiveness of Longformer transformer models on NLP tasks, including question-answering and summarization tasks. Additionally, Rehman et al. [63] have employed the LED model by fine-tuning it to reduce hallu- cination in long documents. Furthermore, Khanna et al. [64] have investigated the potential of the LED model in the financial domain. Another Work by Lan et al. [65] has exploited the LED model for Chinese sentiment analysis. Kimera et al. [66] have evaluated the performance of the Longformer model in diagnosing depression. The LED model has been exploited in the law domain; Xiao et al. [67] have adapted LED for law use cases, including question answer- ing, reading comprehension, case retrieval, and judgment prediction. In this work, we employed the LED model in the abstractive phase, combined with an unsupervised extractive phase, to create the abstract summary of the extracted sentences. Despite the emerging progress in extractive biomedical text summarization, dealing with hybrid approaches for biomed- ical muti-document summarization is still a challenging task and few works have been proposed in this domain. With the emergence of transformer models, few studies have tried to adapt transformer-based models to enhance biomedical summarization. Moreover, the input texts that should be summarized are often lengthy, requiring careful consideration. In this paper, we propose a hybrid method for biomedical multi-document summarization based on transformer models. We aim to extract the most important information in the first stage, followed by an abstractive stage able to generate a coherent summary from the extracted texts. 188016 VOLUME 12, 2024 A. Aftiss et al.: BioMDSum: An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method FIGURE 1. Overall architecture of the proposed method. III. A HYBRID BIOMEDICAL MULTI-DOCUMENT SUMMARIZATION (BioMDSUM) METHOD In this work, we introduce a novel hybrid method for biomedical multi-document summarization that combines the strength of both extractive and abstractive approaches. Specifically, the proposed method consists of two main phases: 1) extractive summarization based on the PageRank Algorithm [13], and 2) abstractive summarization based on the Longform Encoder-Decoder Transformer (LED) [14]. The overall architecture of the proposed method is illustrated in Fig. 1 while its procedure is reported in Algorithm 1. A. EXTRACTIVE SUMMARIZATION Extractive summarization involves generating a summary by ranking sentences based on their importance and informa- tiveness. The proposed extractive process encompasses four main steps: (1) text preprocessing, (2) sentence embedding, (3) sentence clustering, and (4) graph construction for sentence selection. The following subsections provide a detailed description of the techniques used for each of these steps. 1) TEXT PREPROCESSING Biomedical documents often present challenges such as dense and complex sentence structures, technical jargon, domain-specific abbreviations, and inconsistent terminolo- gies. To address these complexities and ensure consistency in the input data, we employ a rigorous preprocessing pipeline that prepares the text for effective summarization. a: SENTENCE SEGMENTATION we leverage spaCy’s pre-trained English language model, en_core_web_sm,1 which efficiently detects sentence bound- aries and segments text into coherent sentences. Given a cluster of documents D consisting of n documents, denoted as D = {d1, d2, . . . , dn}, we start by splitting each document 1https://spacy.io/models/en di in D into a set of p sentences using the en_core_web_sm model proposed by spaCy library,2 This sentence-level segmentation is crucial for our extractive phase, as it enables more granular processing and allows the model to identify the most informative content with greater precision. b: DATA CLEANING AND NORMALIZATION Once segmented, each sentence undergoes several cleaning and normalization steps to further prepare the data for summarization. • Tokenization: Each sentence is tokenized into individual words using the NLTK library.3 • Lowercasing: All text is converted to lowercase using Python’s built-in string library to ensure uniform representation and to reduce redundancy, particularly useful for biomedical terms that may appear in mixed case formats across different documents. • Lemmatization: Biomedical text often contains variants of the same term (e.g., ‘‘diagnosis’’ vs. ‘‘diagnoses’’). We use spaCy’s lemmatizer to standardize these terms by reducing them to their root form, enhancing the model’s ability to recognize and group similar words. • Removal of Special Characters: We filter out non- essential characters such as XML/HTML tags, URLs, email addresses, and redundant whitespace using Python’s re library (regular expressions), as these elements do not contribute meaningfully to the summa- rization process and can introduce noise into the data. • Acronym Expansion: Biomedical text often contains biomedical abbreviations (e.g., ‘‘BP’’ is expanded to ‘‘blood pressure’’). We use the Medialpy library4 to expand all acronym and abbreviation terms. After these steps, the cluster D is represented by a set of N sentences, formally denoted as D = {S1, S2, . . . , SN}. 2https://spacy.io/ 3https://www.nltk.org/ 4https://github.com/AberystwythSystemsBiology/medialpy VOLUME 12, 2024 188017 A. Aftiss et al.: BioMDSum: An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method Algorithm 1 Pseudo Code of the Proposed BioMDSum Method Input: D: a cluster of documents, K: number of clusters Output: summary 1: sentEmbeddings, ES, G: N-dimensional arrays ▷Arrays for sentence embeddings, extracted sentences, and graphs 2: D ←preprocessing(D) ▷Preprocess the documents 3: for i = 1 to length(D) do 4: sentEmbeddings[i] ←SBERT(D[i]) ▷Convert each sentence to an embedding using SBERT 5: end for 6: C ←k-means(sentEmbeddings, K) ▷Cluster the sentence embeddings into K clusters 7: for i = 1 to K do 8: G[i] ←construct_graph(C[i], cosine_similarity) ▷Construct a graph for each cluster using cosine similarity 9: ES[i] ←PageRank(G[i]) ▷Apply PageRank to select important sentences from the graph 10: end for 11: intermediate_summary ←concatenate(ES) ▷Concatenate extracted sentences to form an intermediate summary 12: summary ←LED(intermediate_summary) ▷Generate the final abstractive summary using the LED model 2) SENTENCE EMBEDDING After text preprocessing, the next step is sentence embedding, which is considered a fundamental cornerstone task in extractive text summarization methods. While bag-of-words and word embedding representations have yielded promising results, they do not effectively capture the full meaning and semantics of a sentence in a single vector because they ignore both the ordering of words and the syntactic relationships between them. To address this, our method leverages pre-trained sentence embedding models to encode clusters’ sentences into fixed-length vectors. This approach ensures that sentences with similar meanings are represented by similar vectors, while distinct vectors represent sentences with different meanings. Specifically, we use the Sentence-BERT embedding model (SBERT) [12], a variant of the standard pre-trained BERT model [16], optimized for semantic search and fine-tuned using Siamese and triplet network structures [68]. SBERT employs two BERT encoders in a Siamese architecture to process two sentences in parallel, producing semantically meaningful sentence embeddings,5 which can be compared using the cosine similarity metric. We chose SBERT based on a study by Lamsiyah et al. [69], which demonstrated its effectiveness for unsupervised multi-document summariza- tion. More precisely, given the cluster D = {S1, S2, . . . , SN}, we use the SBERT model as a feature extractor without any further fine-tuning to map each sentence Si in D into an embedding vector −→ Si . D = {S1, S2, . . . , SN} SBERT −−−−→D = {−→ S1, −→ S2, . . . , −→ SN} (1) 3) SENTENCE CLUSTERING In this step, we aim to group sentences according to the topics they cover. Sentences that address the same topic are clustered together, ensuring clear distinctions between 5Semantically meaningful implies that similar sentences are close in the vector space. different topics. To this end, we use the K-means clustering algorithm [70]. Formally, given the embedding vectors of the cluster sentences D = {−→ S1, −→ S2, . . . , −→ SN}, the K-means algorithm clusters the N sentences into K clusters, denoted as C = {C1, C2, . . . , CK}. The objective is to minimize the within-cluster variance by grouping similar or closely related sentences into the same cluster while maximizing the separation or distance between different clusters. This is achieved by solving the optimization problem represented by the following equation: min C K X k=1 X −→ Si ∈Ck ∥−→ Si −µk∥2 (2) where: D = {−→ S1, −→ S2, . . . , −→ SN} represents the set of sentence embedding vectors. C = {C1, . . . , CK} denotes the set of K clusters. µk is the centroid of cluster Ck calculated by averaging the embedding vectors of all sentences in cluster Ck, as defined in Eq. 3. ∥−→ Si −µk∥2 is the squared Euclidean distance between the sentence embedding vector −→ Si and the centroid µk. µk = 1 |Ck| X −→ Si ∈Ck −→ Si (3) Consequently, the K-means clustering algorithm will produce K clusters, denoted as C = {C1, C2, . . . , CK}, where each sentence Si from the original cluster of documents D will be assigned to one of these clusters based on the similarity of its embedding vector −→ Si to the centroids µk. This results in a structured grouping of sentences into clusters, each representing distinct topics, which facilitates better extraction of the main ideas. 4) GRAPH CONSTRUCTION FOR SENTENCE SELECTION For each cluster Ci in C, we construct an undirected weighted graph Gi = (Ni, Ei), where nodes in Ni represent the sentences belonging to cluster Ci and edge weights 188018 VOLUME 12, 2024 A. Aftiss et al.: BioMDSum: An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method in Ei represent cosine similarity between the sentences’ embeddings. Consider a graph Gi for cluster Ci, which contains mi sentences, Ci = {Si1, Si2, . . . , Sim}. The edge weights are calculated using cosine similarity as E(Sip, Siq) = cosine(−→ Sip, −→ Siq), where −→ Sip is the embedding corresponding to the sentence Sip and −→ Siq is the embedding corresponding to the sentence Siq of cluster Ci with indices p, q. Ultimately, we obtain a set of graphs {G1, G2, . . . , GK}, where each graph Gi corresponds to cluster Ci, with K being the number of clusters. Due to the similarity in structure between web page networks and our context, we adopt the PageRank algorithm [13] for identifying important nodes determined by their links to other nodes. Let PR(Sp) represent the PageRank score of node Sp, and L(Sq) denote the number of nodes (sentences) linked to node Sq (neighbors of node Sq). The PageRank score PR(Sp) of the node Sp is calculated as follows: PR(Sp) = (1 −d) + d N X q=1 PR(Sq) L(Sq) (4) where d is the damping factor (typically set to around 0.85), N represents the set of neighbors of the node Sp, and PR(Sq) is the PageRank score of the node (sentence) Sq. To select relevant sentences for each cluster, we calculate the PageRank score for each node of the graph using Eq. 4. Scores of nodes are updated progressively with each iteration. These steps are repeated until convergence is achieved. Finally, sentences are sorted based on their scores. We select the top-k sentences according to their obtained scores to create a cluster summary. Convergence occurs when the algorithm runs for a fixed number of iterations or when the PageRank scores of nodes show no significant change between consecutive iterations. B. INTERMEDIATE EXTRACTIVE SUMMARY OUTPUT Once the extractive phase is completed, the top-ranked sentences identified by the PageRank algorithm from each cluster are concatenated to form an intermediate summary. This summary contains the most salient sentences from all clusters, providing a concise representation of the primary topics within the document cluster D. This intermediate summary bridges the gap between the extractive and abstrac- tive phases in our hybrid approach. The selected sentences from all graphs are concatenated to form the intermediate summary. Let ESi represent the extracted sentences from graph Gi, where ESi = PageRank(Gi) = {Si1, Si2, . . . , Sin} for i ∈ [1, K]. Here, Si1 to Sin represent sentences extracted by PageRank from graph Gi. The global extractive summary ES = {ES1, ES2, . . . , ESK}, where ESi denotes the extractive summary for cluster Ci in the set of K clusters. This extractive summary ES is then passed as input to the next phase to generate the final summary using an abstractive summarization process. The extractive summarization process is applied to all dataset examples. For each document cluster D, we compress and flatten it FIGURE 2. Illustration of the transition between extractive and abstractive phases in the hybrid summarization pipeline. into a new document that highlights salient information. Mathematically, this process can be expressed as follows: Dextractive = {(ES1, Sum1), (ES2, Sum2), . . . , (ESM, SumM)} (5) where (ESi, Sumi) is a pair consisting of the extractive summary and the ground truth summary, which together serve as the input to the LED model. Figure 2 illustrates the transition between the extractive and abstractive phases. C. ABSTRACTIVE SUMMARIZATION WITH THE LONGFORMER-ENCODER-DECODER (LED) Biomedical multi-document summarization often involves handling long sequences of information, which can pose sig- nificant memory and computational challenges. To address these issues, we propose a hybrid method that first selects relevant sentences from the input documents through an extractive summarization process. In the abstractive sum- marization phase, the Longformer Encoder-Decoder (LED) model [14] is employed. Given the constructed dataset Dextractive, we fine-tune the LED Model to generate abstrac- tive summaries. The fine-tuning process minimizes the cross-entropy loss function between the generated summaries and the ground-truth summaries, enabling the model to learn from the structure and content of accurate summaries. By leveraging the LED model, our goal is to generate summaries that are coherent, non-redundant, and closely aligned with reference summaries. IV. EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS In this section, we first present a brief description of the used datasets, evaluation metrics, and the experimental setup. VOLUME 12, 2024 188019 A. Aftiss et al.: BioMDSum: An Effective Hybrid Biomedical Multi-Document Summarization Method TABLE 1. Statistics of the MSˆ2 and Cochrane datasets. Aggregate Reviews represents the total number of main documents, Cumulative Input Studies/Trials denotes the total number of studies, and Mean Studies per Review indicates the average number of studies per review. Then, we provide a comparative analysis of the obtained results, intending to verify the following hypotheses: • Hypothesis 1: The proposed method is effective for biomedical multi-document summarization as compared with other recent state-of-the-art extractive, hybrid, and abstractive methods. • Hypothesis 2: Implementing a clustering approach based on the k-means algorithm and the SBERT embedding model improves the PageRank algorithm’s effectiveness in extracting highly salient sentences across diverse topics. • Hypothesis 3: The integration of extractive and abstrac- tive phases in our hybrid summarization method enhances the overall performance of the proposed approach. A. DATASETS In this work, we evaluate the proposed method using two biomedical summarization datasets: MSˆ2 [71] and Cochrane [40]. The MSˆ2 dataset is specifically designed for multi-document summarization in the biomedical domain. It is constructed from systematic literature reviews, which are types of biomedicine papers that compile results from various studies. This dataset includes 20,000 reviews and 470,000 studies, with each review summarizing an average of 23 stud- ies, ranging from 1 to 401 studies. The Cochrane dataset is another biomedical corpus used for multi-document summa- rization. It consists of 4,500 systematic reviews created by the Cochrane Collaboration. This dataset also includes the titles and abstracts of the trials summarized by these reviews. The abstracts of the systematic reviews contain an average of 75 words. Additionally, the reviews include about 10 trials, with each trial having approximately 245 words. Table 1 provides detailed statistics for both datasets. B. EVALUATION METRICS To evaluate the effectiveness of our method, we have used three different metrics: ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Under- study for Gisting Evaluation) [72], BERTScore [73], and METEOR [74]. Specifically, we have utilized ROUGE-N (ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-2) and ROUGE-L. ROUGE-N mea- sures the similarity between system-generated summaries and gold standard summaries based on n-gram overlap, while ROUGE-L measures the overlap of the longest common sub- sequence (LCS) between the reference summaries (ground truth) and the summaries generated by the model. BERTScore calculates the semantic similarity between the generated summary and the reference summary at the word level by leveraging contextual embeddings from the BERT model. One of the main advantages of BERTScore is its ability to handle issues related to word synonyms and antonyms, which ROUGE metrics may struggle with. METEOR, originally designed to evaluate machine translation performance, is also applied to text summarization tasks due to their similarity in text generation. METEOR assesses the quality of a generated summary by comparing it with reference summaries, con- sidering factors such as grammar, semantics, and synonym matching. C. EXPERIMENTAL SETUP Our current pipeline leverages a suite of Python tools, primarily utilizing the PyTorch library and the available implementation of the SBERT6 model. The preprocessing pipeline employs the SpaCy7 and NLTK8 libraries. We have excluded all documents lacking reference summaries from the used datasets. We tested two variants of the SBERT model: SBERTBASE and SBERTLARGE. The SBERTBASE model embeds sentences into 768-dimensional vectors, whereas SBERTLARGE provides 1024-dimensional sentence embeddings. Since the performance of both models was similar, we reported results using the SBERTBASE model. For clustering, we utilized the k-means algorithm. To determine the optimal value of the hyperparameter k (the number of clusters), we conducted experiments on randomly selected datasets of 200 samples. During the extractive phase, due to computational constraints and document length, we limited the summary of each cluster to a maximum of 50 words. We experimented with a range of cluster numbers from 2 to 30, evaluating the performance of each using ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L metrics. Figures 3 and 4 illustrate the trends of these metrics as the number of clusters increases for both datasets Cochrane and MSˆ2, respectively. For the Cochrane dataset, we observed that performance improvements stabilized at 23 clusters, so we selected 23 as the optimal value for k. For the MSˆ2 dataset, there were no further performance gains beyond 30 clusters. Therefore, we selected 30 as the optimal number of clusters. Furthermore, after determining the optimal number of clusters, we ran the extractive phase on the entire dataset. Table 2 shows the document length statistics before and after applying the extractive summarization phase. The table indicates a reduction in length, suggesting that unwanted text was removed, leaving only salient content. During the abstractive phase, we fine-tuned the LED model. For the MSˆ2 dataset, we used 13,694 samples for training, 497 samples for validation, and 2,021 samples for testing. Table 3 presents the data splitting details for both datasets. The model was fine-tuned using the Hugging Face library over 14 epochs. The maximum input length was set to 1,200 tokens, with a batch size of 3 and gradient accumulation set to 4. During fine-tuning, we employed a saving strategy at the epoch level, saving the optimal model based on ROUGE-2 scores. For optimization, we used the AdamW optimizer with β1 = 0.9 and β2 = 0.999. COMPARATIVE EVALUATION WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART METHODS To assess the effectiveness of the proposed method, we evalu- ate its performance against existing baselines and recent state- of-the-art (SOTA) methods for biomedical multi-document summarization using the Cochrane and MSˆ2 datasets. Tables 4 and 5 summarize the obtained ROUGE scores (R-1, R-2, and R-L), BERTScore, and METEOR for each dataset. 1) COMPARATIVE EVALUATION WITH EXTRACTIVE SOTA METHODS The first set of analyses compares the proposed method with the existing extractive SOTA methods. Specifically, FIGURE 8. R-1, R-2, and R-L scores on the validation set during fine-tuning for the MSˆ2 dataset. LSA [75] analyzes semantic relationships within a document cluster by applying Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to extract the most salient sentences. KLS method [76] employs the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence measure to identify the most important sentences. LUHN [77] scores and selects sentences based on word frequency and sentence position. LRS [78] is a graph-based approach that aims to find salient sentences in the document by applying centrality measures over the constructed graph between sentences. Extractive- BERT [60] is an unsupervised method that clusters sentences and then selects the centroids of each cluster as the salient sentences for the summary. ClinicaBERT [79] is a variant of the BERT model, pre-trained on clinical notes, discharge summaries, and other healthcare-related texts from electronic health records. It has been adapted to select salient sentences based on their similarity to all other sentences in the document. In the same context, PubMedBERT [80] is also a domain-specific model based on the BERT architecture, pre- trained on biomedical literature, including research articles and PubMed abstracts. The first block of Table 4 and Table 5 presents the ROUGE scores (R-1, R-2, and R-L), BERTScore, and METEOR for LUHN, LSA, KLS, LRS, ClinicaBERT, Extractive- BERT, and PubMedBERT on the Cochrane and MSˆ2 datasets, respectively. From these tables, we observe that on the Cochrane dataset, PubMedBERT achieves the best performance across all evaluation measures compared to the other extractive methods. In contrast, on the MSˆ2 dataset, the Extractive-BERT model outperforms the other extractive methods. These results are unsurprising given that both Pub- MedBERT and Extractive-BERT are trained on a large corpus of biomedical text. However, our method, BioMDSum- Hybrid, outperforms all other methods on both datasets across all evaluation measures, including PubMedBERT and Extractive-BERT. The improvements of BioMDSum-Hybrid over PubMedBERT and Extractive-BERT are notable. For instance, compared to PubMedBERT, our method shows an average improvement on the Cochrane dataset of 4.49% in R-1, 2.14% in R-2, 4.30% in R-L, and 1.97% in BERTScore, although there is a slight decrease of 0.50% in METEOR. Overall, these results clearly demonstrate that our proposed method, BioMDSum-Hybrid, achieves remarkable performance improvements over existing state- of-the-art extractive methods on both the Cochrane and MSˆ2 datasets. 2) COMPARATIVE EVALUATION WITH ABSTRACTIVE AND HYBRID SOTA METHODS The second set of analyses compares our method with recent abstractive SOTA methods. BART [38] is a sequence-to-sequence model that com- bines bidirectional understanding with autoregressive gener- ation. It uses both masked language model pre-training and denoising autoencoding objectives, achieving competitive performance in various NLP tasks, particularly in text generation and summarization. T5 (Text-To-Text Transfer Transformer) [15] is a transformer-based language model that frames all NLP tasks as text-to-text transformations, making it suitable for tasks such as translation, summariza- tion, and question-answering. LongT5-Pubmed [45] is an extension of T5 specifically designed for long document sum- marization and fine-tuned for biomedical multi-document summarization. PEGASUS [17] is a state-of-the-art model designed for abstractive summarization, utilizing a unique pretraining approach called Gap Sentence Generation, where important sentences are masked and predicted. It achieves top performance across various summarization tasks, making it highly effective for generating concise and coherent sum- maries. BigBird [18] is a transformer-based model that uses a sparse attention mechanism to reduce the computational and memory complexity of processing long sequences. [81]. PRIMERA [42] is a multi-document summarization model that integrates PICO elements (Populations, Interventions, Comparators, Outcomes) to enhance the quality of the generated sum- maries. text: IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 981 BioMedGPT: An Open Multimodal Large Language Model for BioMedicine Yizhen Luo , Jiahuan Zhang , Siqi Fan , Kai Yang , Massimo Hong , Yushuai Wu, Mu Qiao , and Zaiqing Nie Abstract—Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT have shed light on the development of knowledgeable and versatile AI research assistants in various scientiﬁc domains. However, they fall short in biomedical applications due to a lack of proprietary biomedical knowledge and deﬁciencies in handling bio- logical sequences for molecules and proteins. To address these issues, we present BioMedGPT, a multimodal large language model for assisting biomedical research. We ﬁrst incorporate domain expertise into LLMs by incremental pre-training on large-scale biomedical literature. Then, we harmonize 2D molecular graphs, protein sequences, and natural language within a uniﬁed, parameter-efﬁcient fusion architecture by ﬁne-tuning on multimodal question- answering datasets. Through comprehensive experiments, we show that BioMedGPT performs on par with human experts in comprehending biomedical documents and answering research questions. It also exhibits promising capability in analyzing intricate functions and properties of novel molecules and proteins, surpassing state-of-the-art LLMs by 17.1% and 49.8% absolute gains respectively in ROUGE-L on molecule and protein question-answering. Index Terms—Multimodal large language models, biomedical NLP, molecules, proteins. I. INTRODUCTION R ECENT advances of large language models (LLMs) like GPT-4 [1], LLaMA [2], and PaLM [3] have brought a paradigm shift in the natural language process (NLP) domain. Trained on web-scale corpora and human instructions, LLMs have showcased unprecedented power in solving NLP tasks [4] and generating human-preferred texts [5]. Beyond general- purpose NLP, LLMs have also demonstrated their potential in accelerating numerous scientiﬁc research such as chemistry [6], Received 10 February 2024; revised 22 July 2024; accepted 29 Au- gust 2024. Date of publication 25 November 2024; date of current ver- sion 4 February 2026. This work was supported in part by the National Key R&D Program of China under Grant 2022YFF1203002 and in part by PharMolix Inc. (Corresponding author: Zaiqing Nie.) Yizhen Luo, Jiahuan Zhang, Siqi Fan, Kai Yang, Massimo Hong, and Yushuai Wu are with the Institute for AI Industry Research (AIR), Tsinghua University, Beijing 100190, China. Mu Qiao and Zaiqing Nie are with the PharMolix Inc., Beijing 100190, China (e-mail: zaiqing@air.tsinghua.edu.cn). Our models, datasets, and codes are open-sourced at https://github. com/PharMolix/OpenBioMed. Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/JBHI.2024.3505955 materials [7], and mathematics [8]. Recently, harnessing the power of LLMs in biomedicine has attracted rising research interest, as the development of insightful and versatile research assistants could bring signiﬁcant beneﬁts to vast applications including drug discovery [9], lead optimization [10], biomarker identiﬁcation [11], and protein design [12]. While LLMs bear promise in accelerating biomedical re- search, there exist two major challenges in harnessing their power in this domain. As depicted in Fig. 1, existing LLMs are mostly designed for general purposes and trained on web- crawled corpora and instruction-tuning datasets for general purposes. However, biomedicine is a knowledge-intensive and multi-disciplinary ﬁeld that requires a deep understanding of scientiﬁc documents and domain expertise, and the biomedical knowledge encoded by existing LLMs could be deﬁcient due to a lack of proprietary training data [13]. Besides, LLMs struggle to comprehend biomolecules, including small chemical compounds and proteins. Comprised of atoms and bonds or amino acids, biomolecules encode diverse biomedical processes and mechanisms in a distinct modality. However, when encoun- tered with biomolecular tasks, LLMs tend to give hedging or hallucinating answers instead of faithfully extracting relevant information from the multimodal inputs [14]. The heterogene- ity of biomolecules and human natural language compromises the development of biomedical research assistants that ‘think’ and ‘read’ from diverse modalities like human experts before ‘talking’[15]. More recently, numerous research works have been proposed to address the aforementioned problems. To adapt general- purpose LLMs into the biomedical domain, BioMedLM [16] trains a GPT [17] model with 2.7 billion parameters on PubMed [18]. BiomedGPT [19] employs the BART [20] archi- tecture and incorporates biomedical texts and clinical images from 14 datasets as the pre-training data.1 PMC-LLaMA [21] ﬁne-tunes Llama-7B [2] with millions of biomedical publica- tions from the S2ORC dataset [22]. Besides, motivated by the success of multimodal large language models [23], [24], several approaches have been proposed to harmonize biomolecules and 1BiomedGPT and our model share a resemblance in nomenclature, with the differentiation resting on the nuanced use of lowercase and uppercase ‘m’ within the term ‘med’. Authors of the respective papers have agreed upon this subtle divergence to delineate the two distinct works. We emphasize that the primary focus of the two models diverges signiﬁcantly. Existing LLMs are trained on web-crawled corpora such as books, news, and casual dialogs, and applied for general purposes. Biomedical LLMs should be familiar with literature, patents, and databases within the speciﬁc domain, as well as the biomolecular structures of molecules and proteins, which compose data from a distinct modality. One line of approaches [25], [26], [27], [28], [29] leverage contrastive learning [30] to align the feature space of different modalities, but they are infeasible for LLMs due to heavy computational costs. Another line [31], [32], [33], [34], [35] attempts to incorporate biological sequences and texts simultaneously within a uniﬁed model. However, these models transform biomolecules into a series of soft tokens of varying lengths, which may disturb the original context of natural lan- guage and raise GPU memory consumption [36]. In this work, we present BioMedGPT, a multimodal large language model for biomedicine. As depicted in Fig. 1, we aim to develop a biomedical research assistant with a deep and comprehensive understanding of both biomedical corpora and biomolecules to facilitate real-world applications. The overall architecture of BioMedGPT is presented in Fig. 2. We ﬁrst train a biomedical language model, BioMedGPT-LM, by in- cremental pre-training with large-scale scientiﬁc literature on Llama2-7B-Chat [37]. Then, we employ off-the-shelf repre- sentation learning models [38], [39] to encode 2D graphs of molecules and amino acid sequences of proteins. Through extensive experimental evaluation, we show that BioMedGPT achieves promising performance in biomedical natural language processing and predicting a diverse range of biomolecular properties. BioMedGPT performs on par with human experts in comprehending biomedical literature and an- swering research questions. More importantly, it demonstrates outstanding capabilities in analyzing the properties and func- tions of novel molecules and proteins in free texts based on their structural information. BioMedGPT outperforms state- of-the-art models by 17.1% absolute gains in ROUGE-L on molecule-related question-answering, and 55.6% absolute gains in ROUGE-L on protein-related question-answering. Our contributions are summarized as three-fold: r We present BioMedGPT, an open-sourced multimodal large language model for assisting biomedical research. r We propose incremental pre-training and modality align- ment to aid LLMs’ deﬁciency in biomedical knowledge and comprehending biomolecules. r We show the outstanding performance of BioMedGPT in answering biomedical research questions and under- standingbiomoleculesthroughquantitativeandqualitative analysis. II. RELATED WORK A. Scientiﬁc Large Language Models Large language models [1], [2], [3], [37], [41], comprising billions of parameters, exhibit unprecedented capabilities in comprehending natural language and following human instructions. Recently, harnessing the power of LLMs in various scientiﬁc domains has emerged as a promising research topic. For example, BioInspiredLLM [42] ﬁne-tunes an LLM on structural biological and biomaterial publications and leverages a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) [43] strategy during inference. MechGPT [44] bootstraps LLMs with a question- answering dataset in multiscale materials failure by a knowledge distillation approach [45]. [46] harnesses LLMs to construct and reason over a knowledge graph in biomaterials to offer novel in- sights for this domain. X-LoRA [47] adopts a mixture of experts (MoE) [48] strategy with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) [49] mod- ules in multiple domains for solving complicated scientiﬁc tasks. PMC-LLaMA [21] develops a biomedical LLM by incremental pre-training and medical instruction tuning. Med-PaLM [50] adopts PaLM [3] as the base LLM and employs the same two- stage training approach for domain adaptation. By repurposing general-purpose LLMs in various domains, LLMs have shown promise in accelerating the scientiﬁc research progress. We obtain BioMedGPT-LM by incremental pre-training LLaMA2-7B-Chat on public biomedical literature. We perform modality alignment by ﬁne-tuning BioMedGPT-LM on molecule and protein question-answering datasets within a speciﬁcally designed prompt. B. Aligning Biomolecules With Natural Language Motivated by the success of large vision-language models [23], [24], [51], [52], numerous research works have explored strategies to jointly model biomolecules and natural language. One line of approaches including MoMu [25], MoleculeSTM [53] and ProtST [28] adopts contrastive learning [30] to align the feature space between biomolecules and texts to improve the biomolecular representations. Another line including KV-PLM [15], MolT5 [54], BioT5 [33], and MolTC [55] treats the sequences of biomolecules as a unique language and employs a uniﬁed language model to facilitate translations between the biomolecular language and natural language. Recent works have also begun to explore multimodal large language models with versatile capabilities in handling biomolecules. For example, GIT-Mol [56] proposes to incorporate multimodal information of molecules including 1D SMILES strings, 2D graphs, images, and texts. 3D-MoLM [57] develops an LLM by connecting 3D molecular conformations with natural language. For proteins, xTrimoPGLM [58] combines protein sequences and relevant texts in a preﬁx-LM architecture and employs different language modeling objectives for the two parts. Proteinchat [59] connects the 3D protein structure with texts by a fully-connected projector. Further, ProtChatGPT [34] proposes to combine the 1D and 3D representations of the protein within LLMs for a deeper un- derstanding of proteins. Mol-Instructions [32] proposes a com- prehensive dataset for both molecules and proteins to facilitate the instruction-tuning of LLMs. However, existing multimodal LLMs translate biomolecules into a series of soft tokens, whose length depends on the number of atoms within molecules or amino acids within proteins. This may interrupt the original context of natural language, compromise LLMs’ understanding of biomolecules, and introduce heavy GPU memory costs. BioMedGPT solves this issue with a cross-attention projector that transforms biomolecules into a ﬁxed number of tokens. III. METHODS A. Preliminaries BioMedGPT is a multimodal LLM that aims at comprehend- ing molecules, proteins, and biomedical texts. The molecule is formulated as a 2D graph G = {V, E} where V represents atoms and E represents bonds. The protein is formulated as an amino acid sequence P = [a1, a2, . . . , aL] of length L where ai Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Restrictions apply. 984 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 belongs to one of the 20 amino acids. The text is formulated as a series of tokens T = [t1, t2, . . . , tN] of length N. B. Model Architecture As illustrated in Fig. 2(a), BioMedGPT is composed of a molecule encoder with 1.8 million parameters, a protein encoder with 3 billion parameters, a large language model with 6.7 billion parameters, and two modality translation modules with 1.5 million and 21 million parameters respectively for molecules and proteins. Details of each model component are introduced as follows. The molecule encoder is a 5-layer GIN [60] initialized by the pre-trained checkpoint of GraphMVP [38]. It transforms the 2D molecular graph G into a dense representation matrix Hm ∈ R|V|×dm where dm is the hidden dimension, and each row of the matrix corresponds to the feature of an atom. The protein encoder is ESM2-3B [39], a protein language model with 36 Transformer layers. ESM2 takes the protein sequence P as the input and generate feature embeddings Hp ∈ RL×dp for each amino acid. The LLM, named BioMedGPT-LM, follows the same model architecture as LLaMA2-7B [37]. It is a decoder-based Trans- former model with 48 layers. The input sequence T is embedded into dense vectors ˜T and processed by each Transformer layer to obtain the hidden representations Ht ∈RN×d for each token. Given Hm and Hp from the representation space of biomolec- ular encoders, we aim to bridge the modality gap by translating them into the feature space of the LLM’s input embeddings. However, the number of atoms within the molecule |V| and the number of amino acids within the protein L vary from a few to thousands. Hence, treating each biomolecular representation as a unique token may disturb the original context of natural language and pose challenges for the LLM to extract pivotal information for biomolecules. Inspired by BLIP-2 [23], we design two translation modules that convert Hm and Hp into a ﬁxed number of k virtual prompt tokens ˜Hm, ˜Hp ∈Rk×d, where d is the feature dimension of BioMedGPT-LM. For molecules, we ﬁrst design a trainable query matrix Qm ∈Rk×dm. Then, we devise a multi-head attention module that takes Qm as queries and the molecule features Hm as keys and values. The attention outputs are calculated as follows: Q = WQQm, K = WKHm, Vm = WV Hm, ˜Hatt m = softmax QKT √dm  Vm (1) where WQ, WK, WV ∈Rdm×dm are trainable parameters. Fi- nally, we project the outputs into the same feature dimension of BioMedGPT-LM by a fully connected layer: ˜Hm = W proj m ˜Hatt m + bproj m , (2) where W proj m ∈Rdm×d and bproj m ∈Rdm×d. The protein trans- lation module follows the same architecture as the molecule translation module. Compared with prior works [32], [35] that directly feed 1D se- quences of biomolecules into LLMs, our approach exhibits two advantages. First, we adopt off-the-shelf biomolecular repre- sentation learning models pre-trained on billion-scale databases. Their biomolecular knowledge could improve the generalization capability of our model given limited biomolecule-text data. Besides, we introduce a lightweight translation module that transforms biomolecular representations of varying lengths into a ﬁxed number of tokens, which preserves the original context of LLM inputs and saves GPU memory. C. Training Objectives As illustrated in Fig. 2(b), the training process of BioMedGPT involves two steps, which are detailed as follows. 1) Bootstrapping LLM With Biomedical Corpus: State-of- the-art LLMs are typically trained on general corpora, poten- tially lacking a comprehensive understanding of biomedical documents that involve complex terminologies and domain ex- pertise.Toaddressthisissue,webootstrapexistingLLMs,exem- pliﬁed by LLaMA2-7B-Chat [37], with biomedical knowledge from scientiﬁc publications by incremental pre-training. We obtain 5.3 million biomedical publications from S2ORC [22], a public dataset with paper abstracts spanning various disciplines. We also collect 4.2 million papers with the main body from PubMed Central [61], the largest biomedical publication platform. We ﬁlter out author information, citation notes, reference lists, charts, and ﬁgures within the corpus. We split each document into numerous chunks with no more than 2,048 tokens. The processed corpus comprises a total of 26 billion tokens. We perform incremental pre-training with the same next token prediction objective [17] as most LLMs: L1 = ET ∈D1  N  i=1 LCE(f(T)i, ti)  , (3) whereE denotesexpectation, D1 isthepre-trainingdataset,LCE is the cross-entropy loss, and f is the LLM that predicts the probability distribution of the i−th token based on the previous i −1 tokens. The pre-training takes approximately 2,000 GPU hours on 24 Nividia A100 GPUs with a batch size of 1922 and a learning rate of 2 × 10−5. We adopt fully sharded data parallel (FSDP) [62], BFloat16 training, gradient checkpointing [63] and ﬂash atten- tion [64] to accelerate training. 2) Aligning LLM With Biomolecules: To endow BioMedGPT with a deep and holistic understanding of biomolecules, we per- form ﬁne-tuning on molecule and protein question-answering datasets, namely PubChemQA and UniProtQA. For Pub- ChemQA, we collect multiple columns from the PubChem database [65] including expert-written descriptions, IUPAC names, and calculated properties of molecules. We manually write text inquiries for each column. We remove molecules whose SMILES strings cannot be parsed by RDKit [66] into 2D molecular graphs, and those with more than 256 atoms. We collect a total of 569,516 proteins and 1,891,506 question-answering samples. We sample 3,000 samples from each dataset for testing and randomly partition the remaining data into training and validation sets at a ratio of 8 : 1. The characteristics of the two datasets are detailed in Table I. To alleviate the catastrophic forgetting problem in aligning LLMs with biomolecules, we design a prompt shown in Table II to organize the virtual token embeddings for biomolecules and the question in an instruction-following manner. We start with a system prompt to identify the role of LLM as a biomedical research assistant. We introduce special tokens <molecule>, </molecule>, <protein>, </protein> to help the LLM understand where the structural features of the molecule or protein start and end. The {moleculeHere} and {pro- teinHere} are populated by the soft tokens generated by the molecule and protein translator, and {question} and {an- swer} are populated by the question and answer respectively. The prompt design allows the LLM to answer questions by reasoning over biomolecular structures in an auto-regressive manner. Besides, it helps to maintain the original capability of the LLM given different instructions. The objective is calculated as follows: L2 = EQ,A∈D2  N  i=1 LCE(f([Sm; Hm; Q; A])l+i, Ai)  + EQ,A∈D3  M  i=1 LCE(f([Sp; Hp; Q; A])l+i, Ai)  , (4) where D2 and D3 are the molecule and protein question- answering datasets, Sm and Sp are the system prompts, and l is position where the answer starts. During training, we freeze the parameters of the molecule encoder and the protein encoder and apply low-rank adaptation (LoRA) [49] to BioMedGPT-LM to reduce training costs and mitigate catastrophic forgetting. The experiment is performed on 4 A100 GPUs with a maximum learning rate of 1 × 10−4 and a batch size of 32. We perform linear warmup steps for the ﬁrst 10,000 steps and a cosine annealing strategy to stabilize training. The training procedure takes a total of 800 GPU hours. IV. RESULTS AND ANALYSIS A.
