Performance Evaluation on Biomedical Question-Answering As illustrated in Fig. 3, BioMedGPT achieves outstanding performance in comprehending biomedical texts and answering professional questions. On PubMedQA, the prediction accuracy ofBioMedGPTisonparwithhumanexperts(77.1%v.s.78.0%). In comparison to LLMs with the same number of parameters (7 billion), BioMedGPT yields the best results, surpassing the ﬁne- tuned LLaMA2-Chat-7B model by a remarkable 1.6% absolute gain in accuracy. While BioMedGPT slightly underperforms Med-PaLM, it is noteworthy that Med-PaLM is close-sourced, trained on more biomedical documents, and comprises an order of magnitude more parameters (540 billion). In parallel with our work, more advanced LLMs such as Mistral [41] and LLaMA-3 [76] series have been publicly released. E. Transfer Capabilities on Novel Biomolecules When applied to real-world biomedical research scenarios, AI models are expected to make robust predictions on novel molecules instead of repeating the training data. To investigate this issue, we study the relationship between the structural simi- larity with the training set and the prediction accuracy. For each molecule in the test set, we ﬁrst extract top 10 similar molecules in the training set by MHFP [79], a fast and efﬁcient toolkit for comparing molecular structures. Then, we calculate the Tani- moto similarity between the Morgan ﬁngerprints of molecules to obtain the maximum structural similarity of molecules. For each protein in the test set, we adopt MMseqs2 [80] to extract homologous sequences in the training set and calculate sequence identity. As shown in Fig. 6, the performance of molecule question- answering shows little statistical connections with the novelty of molecules (R2 = −1.813), while the performance of protein question-answering is strongly correlated with the novelty of proteins (R2 = 0.730). We attribute this discrepancy to differ- ent distributions of molecules and proteins. Speciﬁcally, the chemical space of molecules is complicated and comprises a wide range of scaffolds. Slight alterations of the molecular structure may lead to signiﬁcant changes in its properties. In con- trast, proteins exhibit clustering trends, indicating that proteins within the same superfamily may exhibit similar structures and functions. Interestingly, BioMedGPT shows predictive power on out-of-distribution biomolecules whose maximum similarity with the training set is less than 0.3, demonstrating its robustness on unseen samples. V. DISCUSSION The advent of large language models has revolutionized the landscape of AI applications across a wide spectrum of do- mains, with BioMedGPT standing as a pioneering attempt for biomedical research. To address the knowledge deﬁciency of general-purpose LLMs in the biomedical domain, we develop BioMedGPT-LM by incremental pre-training with LLaMA2- 7B-Chat. More importantly, we shed light on bridging the modality gap between biomolecules and natural language. We develop a novel feature fusion architecture and perform modality alignment by ﬁne-tuning on large-scale molecule and protein question-answering datasets. All the models, data, and codes are commercially friendly and open-sourced at https://github. com/PharMolix/OpenBioMed. While BioMedGPT bears promise in accelerating biomedical research, there exist several research avenues for future explo- ration in improving the helpfulness and application scope of biomedical LLMs: Evaluation: In this work, we primarily focus on overlapping- based metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE scores for biomolec- ular question answering, which could be unsatisfactory for assessing the quality of model predictions. For example, the model could achieve promising ROUGE scores by mimicking the styles and frequently used expressions instead of providing accurate and insightful answers. Hence, we expect future works on (1) human evaluation assessing the quality of the generations from multiple axes, such as the agreement with biomedical consensus, the extent of potential harm, and the quality of task-accomplishing. and (2) more nuanced evaluation metrics, especially those for numeric values, to capture the accuracy and relevance of biomedical insights provided by LLMs. In-context learning: One of the unprecedented capabilities of LLMs lies in in-context learning, where the LLM solves a novel task given a prompt and multiple demonstrations. In principle, BioMedGPT could be applied to in-context learning scenarios since the translation module design mitigates the context length problem of incorporating multiple biomolecules. Unfortunately, we have not observed the transfer capability of our model across novel tasks, which we attribute to insufﬁcient modality alignment training and limited instruction tuning data. Besides, extensive evidence and rigorous reasoning are expected to support a claim in the scientiﬁc domain. Therefore, it is essential to improve the interpretability of BioMedGPT by retrieval-augmented generation [43] or chain-of-thought [81] strategies. Biomolecule design: The architecture of BioMedGPT is aimed at facilitating the prediction of biomolecular properties in natural language. However, the current framework has yet to support novel biomolecule design tasks such as text-based biomolecule generation [54], [82] and editing [27] that hold promise for various biomedical research applications. While nascent endeavors have attempted to generate the SMILES strings of molecules and amino acid sequences of proteins within LLMs, their performance is far from satisfactory in designing novel and human-preferred biomolecules. Hence, future works that jointly harness the power of LLMs and state-of-the-art biomolecular generation models are expected. Safety: BioMedGPT is built on the LLaMA2-7B-Chat model and inherits the safety problem of LLMs such as hallucina- tion [83] and the potential to generate bias, discrimination, or harmful contents [84]. Besides, the biomedical corpus may also exhibit discrimination in race, gender, and population [85]. As a result, there is a concern that BioMedGPT may be misused to generate harmful contents or dangerous molecules in ap- plication scenarios. Hence, we emphasize the responsible and Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:28 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 990 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 TABLE V PERFORMANCE COMPARISON ON THE TEST SET OF CHEBI-20 ethical use of BioMedGPT, that it should NOT be employed for any scenarios that endanger human life, and any further real- world applications should undergo cautious and professional supervision and comprehensive experiments. We also call on future works on improving the safety issues when harnessing LLMs for biomedicine. VI. CONCLUSION We present BioMedGPT, an open-source multimodal large language model for biomedicine. We incorporate biomedical knowledge from large-scale publications into LLMs by incre- mental pre-training and bridge biomolecules and natural lan- guage with modality alignment. BioMedGPT demonstrates out- standing performance in understanding biomedical documents, answering biomedical research questions, and analyzing the properties of novel molecules and proteins. These capabilities are indispensable in biomedical research applications such as drug discovery, lead optimization, and biomarker identiﬁcation. Under thorough analysis aimed at safety and responsibility, there’s potential for BioMedGPT to spark the next generation of biomedical research with human-AI interaction. APPENDIX I SUPPLEMENTARY RESULTS A. Detailed Results on Molecule Question-Answering We calculate MAE scores for 5 types of questions in the PubChemQA dataset involving numeric values. We implement two supervised models, namely MolCLR and GraphMVP, by applying a regression head composed of two fully connected layers and ReLU activation and training the models on each type of question. For LLMs, we extract the ﬁrst number within the answer as the predicted value and report the average MAE on samples where a valid number is provided. B. Performance Comparison on CheBI-20 We evaluate BioMedGPT’s performance by further ﬁne- tuning the model on CheBI-20 [54], a widely adopted bench- mark in molecular captioning. We report BLEU, ROUGE and METEOR scores of BioMedGPT and existing models presented in Table V. The statistics of the dataset is displayed in Table VIII. TABLE VI MEAN ABSOLUTE ERRORS (MAE) ON MOLECULE QUESTION-ANSWERING INVOLVING NUMERIC VALUES While BioMedGPT demonstrates predictive power on this task, it underperforms specialized models such as BioT5 [33] and retrieval-based models that adopt in-context learning on stronger LLMs such as MolReGPT [89]. We speculate that scaling the number of molecules for modality alignment and parameters of the backbone LLM will bring signiﬁcant improvements on this task, which we reserve for future exploration. C. Performance Comparison on PEER To investigate if BioMedGPT is applicable to more protein- related tasks, we evaluate our model on the PEER bench- mark [91]. Following BioT5 [33], we adopt two binary clas- siﬁcation tasks that aim to predict the solubility and localization (membrane-bound or soluble) of the protein. We also incorpo- rate two protein-protein interaction (PPI) benchmarks involving proteins within Homo Sapiens (Human) and Saccharomyces cerevisiae (Yeast). The statistics of these datasets are shown in Table VIII. We ﬁne-tune BioMedGPT on each task using a similar prompt template as in protein question-answering. We incorporate non-pretrained models including CNN, LSTM, and Transformer, and protein language models including Prot- Bert [90], ESM-1b [39] and ProtST [28], and multi-modal language models including BioT5 [33] and BioT5+ [88]. We report accuracy for comparison in Table VII . Compared with prior models that solely rely on protein se- quences, BioMedGPT attains competitive and even improved results in predicting protein properties and interactions. This corroborates prior studies [28], [33] that protein knowledge Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. While BioMedGPT slightly underperforms BioT5 and BioT5+, we argue that BioMedGPT comprises a signiﬁcantly smaller number of trainable parameters (60 M v.s. 252 M). Similarly, we expect future improvements by expanding the scale of protein-text data.
