file_name: Bridging the Question-Answer Gap in Retrieval-Augmented Generation Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings.pdf
text: Received 9 May 2025, accepted 27 June 2025, date of publication 15 July 2025, date of current version 28 July 2025. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3589499 Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings DOMEN VAKE 1,2, JERNEJ VIČIČ 1,3, AND ALEKSANDAR TOŠIĆ 1,2 1Faculty of Mathematics, Natural Sciences and Information Technologies, University of Primorska, 6000 Koper, Slovenia 2InnoRenew CoE, 6310 Izola, Slovenia 3Research Centre of the Slovenian Academy of Sciences and Arts, Fran Ramovš Institute of the Slovenian Language, 4274 Žirovnica, Slovenia Corresponding author: Domen Vake (domen.vake@famnit.upr.si) This work was supported by European Union’s Horizon 2020 under Grant 101135012. ABSTRACT Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems synergize retrieval mechanisms with generative language models to enhance the accuracy and relevance of responses. However, bridging the style gap between user queries and relevant information in document text remains a persistent challenge in retrieval-augmented systems, often addressed by runtime solutions (e.g., Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE)) that attempt to improve alignment but introduce extra computational overhead at query time. To address these challenges, we propose Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings (HyPE), a framework that shifts the generation of hypothetical content from query time to the indexing phase. By precomputing multiple hypothetical prompts for each data chunk and embedding the chunk in place of the prompt, HyPE transforms retrieval into a question-question matching task, bypassing the need for runtime synthetic answer generation. This approach does not introduce latency but also strengthens the alignment between queries and relevant context. Our experimental results on six common datasets show that HyPE can improve retrieval context precision by up to 42 percentage points and claim recall by up to 45 percentage points, compared to standard approaches, while remaining compatible with re-ranking, multi-vector retrieval, query decomposition, and other RAG advancements. INDEX TERMS Dense retrieval, embeddings, hypothetical prompt embeddings, large language models, retrieval-augmented generation. I. INTRODUCTION Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems have emerged as a powerful paradigm in natural language process- ing, combining the strengths of retrieval-based approaches with the generative capabilities of large language models (LLMs) [1]. By leveraging external knowledge sources, RAG systems enhance the factual accuracy and relevance of generated responses, addressing the limitations of standalone generative models, such as outdated knowledge or limited access to restricted information. Despite their success, existing RAG implementations often struggle with aligning retrieval and generation in a way that efficiently bridges the gap between user queries and relevant document content. The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Wei Wang . To optimize retrieval performance, researchers have explored a variety of strategies, ranging from efficient chunking (splitting texts into coherent subunits) [1], [2] to re-ranking methods that refine initial retrieval results via cross-encoders or boosted similarity scoring [3]. Advanced frameworks such as GraphRAG exploit graph structures to capture cross-document relationships for more nuanced multi-hop or contextual queries [4]. Meanwhile, domain adaptation techniques focus on tailoring retrieval to special- ized topics, ensuring that queries can be matched effectively, even in areas where language models might otherwise lack expertise. Despite these efforts, a persistent hurdle in RAG remains the mismatch between user queries, which typically adopt an interrogative style, and corpus content, which is usually expository or declarative in nature. This style difference 129952 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ VOLUME 13, 2025 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings hampers the alignment of query embeddings with document embeddings, occasionally allowing key information to go unretrieved. A notable solution to this problem is Hypothet- ical Document Embeddings (HyDE) [5], which prompts an LLM at query time to generate a synthetic answer, then uses that short text as the query for retrieval. In this paper, we introduce Hypothetical Prompt Embed- dings (HyPE), a new approach that tackles query-document style mismatch without adding overhead to every user request. Rather than generating synthetic answers at infer- ence, HyPE precomputes multiple hypothetical questions for each corpus chunk at indexing time. These question-like prompts are embedded and stored, so that query matching effectively becomes a question-question retrieval problem. By shifting hypothetical generation offline, HyPE avoids additional runtime LLM calls. To evaluate HyPE’s effectiveness, we compare it against a naive RAG implementation and HyDE across multiple datasets and evaluation metrics, including precision, recall, and faithfulness. Our results demonstrate that HyPE offers substantial improvements in retrieval efficiency, reducing the computational burden while achieving comparable or better retrieval accuracy and contextual relevance. The contributions of this paper are as follows: • We introduce the concept of precomputed hypothetical prompt embeddings to optimize retrieval efficiency in RAG systems. • We provide a comprehensive performance comparison between a Naive RAG implementation, HyDE, and HyPE. • We present experimental results showcasing the trade-offs in retrieval quality and the effect of retrieval approach on generation across various datasets. By shifting the hypothetical generation process from runtime to indexing, HyPE represents a scalable and efficient alternative for RAG systems, offering practical benefits for real-world applications requiring fast and reliable retrieval-augmented text generation. The rest of the paper is structured as follows: Section II presents the related works and surveys, Section III presents the used methodology. Follows the presentation of the experiment setting with the presentation of the datasets and the evaluation metrics. Section V presents the results with thorough analysis, Section VI presents the finals conclusions. II. RELATED WORKS Lewis et al. [1] introduced the original RAG framework, combining dense retrieval with generation to mitigate hal- lucination and provide grounding via external documents. While effective, early RAG pipelines [1], [6] often suffer from limitations in retrieval precision by simply embedding the user queries and retrieving text chunks via approximate nearest neighbour (ANN) search over a vector index. However, a persistent challenge remains: user queries are often phrased in question form, whereas documents or chunks are stored in an expository or statement-oriented style, creating a semantic or ‘‘lexical-conceptual’’ gap [7], [8]. This mismatch can degrade the retrieval’s accuracy and ultimately weaken the generative model’s faithfulness. Cross-encoder reranking and multi-vector representa- tions [9] enhance retrieval quality post hoc, but introduce additional inference-time latency. One direction of research attempts to alleviate this mismatch by expanding documents with likely queries. For instance, Doc2Query [10] uses a sequence-to-sequence model (often T5) to generate synthetic questions for each document, appending them to the text so that a bag-of- words ranker like BM25 [11] can match real user queries more easily. While Doc2Query often boosts first-stage recall, subsequent studies noted that the generation process can hallucinate irrelevant expansions, leading to index bloat. Among them, Doc2Query– (Minus-Minus) [12] addresses this by filtering out low-quality expansions, improving accuracy and reducing index size for BM25-based retrieval. However, these methods predominantly operate in a lexical retrieval space rather than dense embeddings, and they store expansions as text appended to each document and, as such, act more like an enrichment of the chunks. Another related direction focuses on generating synthetic training data to train or fine-tune dense retrievers in new domains. Ma et al. [13] propose using a question-generation model, trained on general-purpose Question-Answer(QA) pairs, to generate ‘‘pseudo-queries’’ for domain-specific passages. A dual-encoder retrieval model is then trained on these synthetic (question, passage) pairs—effectively learning domain adaptation in a zero-shot setting. While powerful for building domain-specialized retrievers, the method requires re-training or fine-tuning a dense model on large-scale synthetic data. By contrast, our approach bypasses model re-training at retrieval time and, instead, alters how we store the passages (i.e., their hypothetical question embeddings). More recently, HyDE [5] addresses query-document mismatch by generating a hypothetical answer or short passage at query time. Instead of embedding the user’s question directly, HyDE prompts an LLM to produce an approximate response, then embeds that synthetic text. This is used to retrieve relevant real documents from a vector index. While HyDE can improve retrieval accuracy for zero-shot question answering, it incurs an extra inference cost per user query. Additionally, the method may struggle, where the prompt queries for niche domain knowledge, where the model may not have sufficient knowledge to produce a representative sample. Building on this line of work, Eibich et al. [14] conducted an empirical study comparing RAG retrieval enhancements, including HyDE, reranking, multi-query expansion, and maximal marginal relevance (MMR). Their findings highlight HyDE’s strong performance in both recall and faithfulness metrics, while also noting its trade-offs in runtime efficiency. Several surveys have recently addressed retrieval- augmented systems. Gupta et al. [15] and Cheng et al. [16] VOLUME 13, 2025 129953 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings FIGURE 1. Illustration of the Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings (HyPE) framework, showcasing the process of precomputing hypothetical questions during indexing to optimize retrieval efficiency in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. offer extensive taxonomies of retrieval mechanisms, iden- tifying open challenges in domain adaptation, embedding alignment, and inference efficiency. Notably, both surveys identify the need for methods that preserve retrieval quality while reducing reliance on runtime LLM calls. In this context, our proposed Hypothetical Prompt Embed- dings (HyPE) introduces a novel retrieval strategy that pre-computes hypothetical question-style prompts at index- ing time. This shifts the burden of LLM generation to the offline phase. III. METHODOLOGY HyPE addresses the challenge of aligning user queries and relevant content by pre-computing hypothetical prompts at the indexing stage, contrasting with HyDE’s runtime generation of synthetic answers. This shift avoids additional inference overhead per query and improves retrieval precision by ensuring that both user queries and stored embeddings share a question-like form. The method begins by splitting the corpus D into coherent chunks C1, C2, . . . , Cn, where each chunk provides a self-contained unit of information. For each chunk Ci, an LLM G generates multiple hypothetical prompts Qi = qi1, qi2, . . . , qik, simulating possible user queries that the chunk might answer. This offline step does not introduce any additional computational cost at query time, as no new prompts need to be generated for each user request. Each hypothetical prompt qij is then mapped to an embedding vij = f (qij) ∈ Rd using a pre-trained dense retrieval model f . Rather than storing these prompt embeddings separately, we associate each vij with the original chunk Ci, thus building an index of vector-chunk pairs: E = {(v11, C1), (v12, C1), . . . , (vnk, Cn)} Each chunk is effectively represented multiple times, once for each hypothetical prompt. This extends the coverage of how queries may be phrased and matched. Algorithm 1 HyPE Indexing Phase (offline) Require: Corpus D = {d1, . . . , dM}; chunker C; generator LLM G; encoder f ; prompts-per-chunk k Ensure: Vector index E mapping embeddings to chunks 1: E ←∅ 2: for all document d ∈D do 3: C ←C(d) ▷split d into coherent chunks 4: for all chunk c ∈C do 5: Q ←G �GenerateQuestions(c), k  6: for all question q ∈Q do 7: v ←f (q) ▷embed question 8: E ←E ∪   (v, c) 9: end for 10: end for 11: end for 12: return E The retrieval process at runtime follows a standard approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search in the vector space. When a user query q arrives, it is embedded into q = f (q). The system then locates the nearest vij vectors within E, and retrieves the associated chunks for final answer generation by an LLM. Although the pipeline remains structurally similar to a Naive RAG, the key difference is that HyPE matches questions against questions, rather than questions against chunk text. At present HyPE treats every generated question in the set Qi = {qi1, . . . , qik} with equal importance: each prompt is embedded once and contributes a single vector to the index. We do not yet attempt to decide which 129954 VOLUME 13, 2025 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings hypothetical questions are ‘‘better’’ or discard those that are less representative. Determining prompt quality is an open issue—and likely to be domain-dependent. In settings where domain knowledge is available (e.g. biomedical literature or legal texts) conditioning the LLM on that knowledge could produce more accurate or stylistically appropriate questions, which in turn should strengthen retrieval. Investigating prompt scoring and domain-specific generation therefore remains future work. Algorithm 2 HyPE Retrieval Phase (online) Require: User query q; encoder f ; index E; top-k Ensure: Relevant chunk set R 1: vq ←f (q) 2: R ←ANN_Search(E, vq, k) ▷k nearest vectors return R This question-question alignment increases the probability of finding the correct chunks for two main reasons. First, many embedding models exhibit style-based clustering [17]. Texts of similar form (e.g., interrogative sentences) often lie closer in the vector space. As a result, a user’s real- world query naturally aligns more closely with the hypo- thetical prompts that share its interrogative style. Second, generating multiple hypothetical queries per chunk broadens the ‘‘semantic reach,’’ covering a wider range of possible question formulations. Even if a user query is phrased in a slightly different way, there is a higher chance that at least one of the chunk’s hypothetical questions closely corresponds to it. Another advantage of HyPE lies in how it addresses the inherent chunking tradeoff in retrieval systems. If chunks are too large, their embeddings become less precise because they encode a mix of multiple concepts, making vector-based similarity less reliable [18]. Conversely, reducing chunk size improves embedding specificity but risks losing crucial surrounding context. HyPE mitigates this issue by ensuring that each stored vector represents a specific piece of information within a chunk, while retrieval still returns the entire chunk with its broader context. This allows the system to retain the benefits of detailed, fine-grained embeddings without sacrificing the context for accurate retrieval. HyPE invokes the language model once per chunk, prompting it to return a set of m hypothetical questions in a single call. Consequently, a corpus consisting of n chunks requires n LLM calls during indexing, regardless of how many prompts are generated for each chunk. While this upfront cost can be substantial for very large datasets, it is paid only once and is strictly proportional to the dataset size. After the index is built, HyPE’s online path involves nothing more than standard vector search, incurring no additional LLM calls at query time and keeping serving latency and operating cost flat even as query volume grows. IV. EXPERIMENTS We evaluated three RAG pipelines to assess retrieval and generation performance as presented in Table 1. While a naïve retriever establishes how much can be achieved without any style-bridging, HyDE is the only published RAG component that explicitly targets the same ‘‘question-to-statement’’ gap as HyPE, albeit at inference time via synthetic-answer generation. Including HyDE therefore yields a good compar- ison and isolates the effect of moving hypothetical content creation from the query stage (HyDE) to the indexing stage (HyPE). TABLE 1. Key differences of compared pipelines. For evaluating the RAG pipelines, we used the RAGChecker framework [19], a comprehensive evaluation toolkit developed by Amazon Science for assessing both retrieval and generation performance in RAG systems. It provides structured metrics to analyse retrieval effective- ness through context precision, which measures how many retrieved passages are relevant, and claim recall, which assesses whether all necessary information is retrieved. For generation, RAGChecker evaluates faithfulness, ensur- ing the generated text remains grounded in the retrieved passages, along with the hallucination rate, which identifies unsupported claims, and context utilization, which measures how effectively retrieved passages contribute to responses. Additionally, it assesses robustness through noise sensitivity, testing the system’s response to query variations, and self-knowledge, which quantifies the model’s ability to recognize when it lacks sufficient information. Additionally, it computes precision, recall, and F1 scores, providing an overall measure of retrieval and response accuracy [19]. A. DATASETS We evaluated our approach on six datasets, chosen to test distinct aspects of RAG systems. MS MARCO [20], a large- scale question-answering benchmark, and Ragas-WikiQA1 evaluate general-purpose retrieval in real-world scenarios. RAG-dataset-120002 and MultiHopRAG [21] emphasize multi-hop reasoning, requiring systems to synthesize infor- mation across multiple documents. RAGBench [22] tests hybrid tasks demanding both precise retrieval and coherent generation. Finally, Single-Topic RAG dataset3 focuses on narrow domains, assessing precision in specialized contexts. A concise summary of their key statistics is given in Table 2. 1https://huggingface.co/datasets/explodinggradients/ragas-wikiqa 2https://huggingface.co/datasets/neural-bridge/rag-dataset-12000 3https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/samuelmatsuoharris/single-topic-rag- evaluation-dataset VOLUME 13, 2025 129955 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings FIGURE 2. The image depicts the workflows of three retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines tested in the experiments: Naive RAG, HyDE, and HyPE. Blue components show parts of the pipeline that are the same across all setups and the green components show additional steps in the pipelines. FIGURE 3. Prompt used to generate hypothetical prompts. TABLE 2. Descriptive statistics of the six datasets used in our study. For each dataset we list its thematic focus, the number of gold question-answer pairs, the total number of text chunks created by our preprocessing, and the average chunk length in tokens. All datasets already come pre-segmented into chunks, except for RAG-dataset-12000 that contains a single context block, and MultiHopRAG, which contains multiple larger documents. For these two cases, we manually split the source text into segments of maximum 500 tokens, overlapping each segment by 50 tokens to preserve cross-boundary coherence. This approach, while straightforward, may not be optimal as the choice of chunking strategy can significantly impact retrieval effectiveness and quality of generation. Accordingly, we apply the same chunking procedure across all three pipelines for consistency in our experiments, but we note that different pipelines might benefit from tailored chunking strategies. We leave an in-depth exploration of chunk size, overlap, and other segmentation heuristics as a direction for future research. B. EVALUATION METRICS For all pipelines, we tested retrieval depths k ∈{1, 3, 5, 10}. At k = 5, we additionally compared cosine similarity and Euclidean distance functions to assess their impact on chunk relevance ranking. The embedding model chosen for all pipelines was bge-m3 [23] for dense vector representations. The generator LLM used was Mistral-NeMo.4 We chose Mistral-NeMo because its openly released weights make the model easy for anyone to download and run, ensuring that every result in this paper can be reproduced without relying on a commercial API. During the time of testing, published benchmark scores place it in the top tier of 7-13 B open-source LLMs for instruction following and QA, so it provides competitive generation quality while remaining fully replicable. V. RESULTS WITH ANALYSIS In Table 3, we compare the three retrieval methods across six datasets and varying numbers of retrieved chunks (k). Each cell reports context precision (how many of the retrieved 4https://mistral.ai/news/mistral-nemo/ 129956 VOLUME 13, 2025 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings chunks directly match the query’s needs) and claim recall (how many relevant pieces of information are captured). Overall, HyPE improves recall by about 16 percentage points and precision by about 20 percentage points compared to Naive RAG, on average. The difference can be even larger on specific datasets, such as Single-Topic RAG at k = 1 where HyPE surpasses Naive RAG by more than 40 percentage points in precision and at k = 10 surpasses by 44.6 percentage points in recall. Although HyPE performs slightly below Naive RAG on MS MARCO at k = 1, it catches up or exceeds Naive RAG at deeper retrieval. For RAGBench and Ragas-WikiQA, HyPE also achieves strong gains, especially at lower k values, indicating its ability to retrieve the correct chunks accurately. FIGURE 4. Box plot comparison of Retriever Context Precision across different numbers of documents retrieved (k) for three methods: Naive, HyDE, and HyPE. The plot illustrates the distribution and variability of precision scores for each method and retrieval depth. Figure 4 compares Retriever Context Precision across different numbers of documents retrieved (k) for the Naive, HyDE, and HyPE methods revealing significant improve- ment in precision, using HyPE. As the number of retrieved documents increases from 1 to 10, HyPE consistently demon- strates higher precision. Its precision is notably superior to both Naive and HyDE methods. This suggests that HyPE’s approach of precomputing hypothetical questions during the indexing phase effectively aligns retrieved content with user queries, reducing the semantic mismatch often encountered in traditional methods. The narrower interquartile ranges for HyPE further indicate its consistency in retrieving relevant information across varying retrieval depths. Additionally, the figure 5 of claim recall complements these findings. Claim recall measures the proportion of rele- vant information successfully retrieved from the documents. The balanced performance in both metrics highlights HyPE’s effectiveness in bridging the gap between user queries and relevant document content. The comparison in Figure 6 shows that for all three methods the choice between Euclidean and Cosine distance metrics does not significantly impact their effectiveness. In Figure 7 we report generator metrics: context utilization, noise sensitivity, hallucination, self-knowledge, and faithful- ness. These scores are properties of the generator language model, not of the retriever itself. Because HyPE intervenes FIGURE 5. Box plot comparison of Retriever Claim Recall across different numbers of documents retrieved (k) for three methods: Naive, HyDE, and HyPE. The plot illustrates the distribution and variability of precision scores for each method and retrieval depth. only in the retrieval stage, we keep the generator LLM fixed and any other foundation model could be dropped in without changing the retrieval logic. Accordingly, shifts in these metrics across pipelines reflect how the quality of the retrieved context influences a given LLM’s behaviour, rather than inherent differences between language models. HyPE consistently achieves higher context utilization and faithfulness, indicating that its retrieval strategy provides more relevant and coherent supporting text for generation. However, the performance on noise sensitivity metrics presents a more nuanced picture. For ‘Noise sensitivity in relevant contexts’, HyPE registers a higher score compared to Naive RAG and HyDE. Within the evaluation frame- work [19], this higher score signifies worse performance, suggesting the generator makes more errors when relevant retrieved documents are affected by noise. A potential explanation lies in HyPE’s retrieval of multiple copies of the relevant chunks. Although beneficial for faithfulness through reinforcement, this very redundancy could increase the generator’s susceptibility to errors since noise is repeated along with relevant information. Conversely, HyPE shows marginally better performance on ‘Noise sensitivity in irrel- evant contexts’ (achieving a slightly lower score), indicating slightly improved handling of noise associated with irrelevant documents compared to the baseline methods. HyPE also exhibits lower hallucination rates compared to Naive RAG and HyDE, reinforcing the idea that better-aligned retrieval reduces the likelihood of introducing incorrect or unsupported claims. Although these results are based on a single LLM, the trend is likely to generalize across different models, as improvements in retrieval typically translate to improved generation performance. However, the exact degree of impact may vary depending on the LLM’s retrieval dependence and sensitivity to context quality. The combination of improved context grounding, reduced hallucinations, and stronger response alignment highlights HyPE’s potential for enhancing the reliability of RAG systems. Figure 8 compares the overall F1 scores for retrievers and generators of the pipelines through the datasets. With the VOLUME 13, 2025 129957 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings TABLE 3. Performance comparison of retrieval methods across datasets using context precision and claim recall metrics (bigger is better) with varying numbers of retrieved context chunks (k). Gradient intensity reflects metric strength (lighter to darker green indicates lower to higher values), with bold entries highlighting the best-performing method for each configuration. FIGURE 6. Comparison of Retriever Claim Recall and Context Precision across three retrieval methods using two distance metrics: Euclidean and Cosine. Performance measured at k = 5. exception of the MS MARCO dataset where all three methods show comparable performance, the F1 scores show, that HyPE consistently outperforms the other two methods, particularly in datasets like Single Topic RAG and RAG- dataset-12000, where the complexity and specificity of queries demand precise retrieval. The MS MARCO benchmark stands out as an outlier in our results, exhibiting comparable retrieval efficacy across the Naive RAG, HyDE, and HyPE pipelines. This convergence can be attributed to several factors inherent to the dataset that limit the differential impact of HyPE’s enhancements. Firstly, MS MARCO utilizes short, answer-centric passages (typically around 60 tokens), which facilitates strong baseline performance via direct query-passage matching, leaving less room for augmentation techniques to provide significant added value. Secondly, the high lexical overlap inherent 129958 VOLUME 13, 2025 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings TABLE 4. Aggregate performance across the six evaluation datasets (mean ± sd). For metrics marked ↑, higher is better; for those marked ↓, lower is better. Best value in each row is marked in bold. FIGURE 7. Comparative box plot analysis of various generator metrics when using one of the three retrieval methods, highlighting the performance differences among the methods in terms of their effectiveness and reliability in generating accurate and contextually relevant responses. between its web search-derived queries and corresponding passages substantially reduces the query-document style gap that HyPE aims to mitigate. Lastly, the high baseline retrieval scores observed in our experiments on MS MARCO, achieved even without augmentation using a powerful dense embedding model, suggest a performance saturation effect, where further architectural refinements struggle to yield significant marginal improvements. Consequently, while HyPE offers substantial gains on datasets characterized by longer documents and greater stylistic divergence between queries and context, its advantages are less pronounced given the specific properties of the MS MARCO passage retrieval task. Given the small paired sample size and the absence of any firm evidence for normality, we adopt the distribution-free Wilcoxon signed-rank test with Holm-Bonferroni adjustment instead of a paired t-test. Across all eleven metrics, the Wilcoxon results in Table 5 indicate a consistent advantage for HyPE over both baselines. Nine metrics achieve an adjusted p < 0.10 in both comparisons, and five of those also satisfy the stricter p < 0.065 step that corresponds to the minimum attainable exact level. Effect sizes are uniformly medium to large: Cliff’s |δ| ranges from 0.44 to 0.72, implying that a random sample from HyPE exceeds its baseline counterpart in roughly 70-85% of the paired datasets. The only metric without a discernible difference is noise-sensitivity in irrelevant context (padj = 1.0, |δ| < 0.11), indicating that all methods degrade equally when faced with distractor passages. Taken together with the statistics in Table 4, these findings show that HyPE’s VOLUME 13, 2025 129959 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings FIGURE 8. Bar chart comparison of F1 scores across six different datasets for three retrieval methods. Each subplot represents a dataset and shows the F1 scores for varying numbers of retrieved chunks (k = 1, 3, 5, 10). TABLE 5. Wilcoxon signed-rank tests (HyPE vs. each baseline). Holm-adjusted p-values below 0.065 and Cliff’s |δ|≥0.56 are bold. improvements are both statistically reliable and practically meaningful. Although HyPE is evaluated here as a stand-alone retriever, its design is orthogonal to other retrieval-side opti- mization approaches and therefore combinable with them. For example, the pre-computed question vectors can feed into query-decomposition modules (which break multi-hop questions into simpler sub-queries), query-expansion or rewriting steps (such as Doc2Query or RePlug), and even re-ranking or multi-vector fusion frameworks like BM25 or ColBERT. In these composite pipelines HyPE simply replaces the original passage vectors while leaving the higher-level orchestration intact, making it a drop-in upgrade rather than a competing subsystem. VI. CONCLUSION The paper presents Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings (HyPE), a framework that pre-computes hypothetical prompts at indexing time to reshape retrieval in RAG pipelines into a prompt-to-prompt matching process. Our experimental findings show that HyPE surpasses both Naive RAG and HyDE on multiple datasets and metrics, with notable gains in precision and recall. By eliminating the need for query-time synthetic answer generation and instead relying on strategically generated questions offline, HyPE improves efficiency and provides stronger alignment between user queries and relevant content. Although HyPE may not outperform every specialized RAG variant in all domains, it offers a flexible and modular upgrade to existing pipelines. Swapping in pre-computed question embeddings remains compatible with advances in chunking, re-ranking, multi-vector retrieval, and fine-tuning large language models. HyPE also integrates smoothly into agent-based systems, where prompt-level alignment can help specialized retrieval sub-agents handle distinct query types more effectively. Looking ahead, combining HyPE with GraphRAG, which maps information from documents or chunks as graph nodes, may further enhance multi-hop reasoning and retrieval accuracy in complex scenarios. Such a hybrid approach could be especially valuable for building robust RAG systems. Another direction for future research involves investigating the chunking tradeoff in the context of expanding LLM 129960 VOLUME 13, 2025 D. Vake et al.: Bridging the Question–Answer Gap in RAG: Hypothetical Prompt Embeddings context windows. As language models evolve to accommo- date larger input lengths, it becomes increasingly feasible to supply bigger chunks as prompts. However, larger chunks can dilute semantic specificity in their embeddings, resulting in less precise vector matching. This tension between maintaining detailed embeddings and preserving broader context may become more pronounced as context windows expand. Further testing of chunk size and indexing depth with HyPE would help clarify how embedding precision balances with retrieval breadth. Finally, we plan to validate HyPE on multilingual RAG benchmarks to confirm that the question-question alignment holds across languages and writing systems. Overall, HyPE demonstrates that shifting from question- to-document to question-to-question alignment leads to tangible gains in retrieval accuracy and cost-effectiveness. As RAG solutions continue to evolve, offline prompt generation strategies like HyPE can serve as a foundation for more efficient generation.

title: IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 28, NO. 12, DECEMBER 2024
file_name: CALLM Enhancing Clinical Interview Analysis Through Data Augmentation With Large Language Models.pdf
text: IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 28, NO. 12, DECEMBER 2024 7531 CALLM: Enhancing Clinical Interview Analysis Through Data Augmentation With Large Language Models Yuqi Wu , Student Member, IEEE, Kaining Mao , Yanbo Zhang , and Jie Chen , Fellow, IEEE Abstract—The global prevalence of mental health disor- ders is increasing, leading to a signiﬁcant economic bur- den estimated in trillions of dollars. In automated mental health diagnosis, the scarcity and imbalance of clinical data pose considerable challenges for researchers, limit- ing the effectiveness of machine learning algorithms. To cope with this issue, this paper aims to introduce a novel clinical transcript data augmentation framework by lever- aging large language models (CALLM). The framework fol- lows a “patient-doctor role-playing” intuition to generate realistic synthetic data. In addition, our study introduces a unique “Textbook-Assignment-Application” (T-A-A) parti- tioning approach to offer a systematic means of crafting synthetic clinical interview datasets. Concurrently, we have also developed a “Response-Reason” prompt engineering paradigm to generate highly authentic and diagnostically valuable transcripts. By leveraging a ﬁne-tuned DistilBERT model on the E-DAIC PTSD dataset, we achieved a bal- anced accuracy of 0.77, an F1-score of 0.70, and an AUC of 0.78 during test set evaluations, which showcase robust adaptability in both Zero-Shot Learning (ZSL) and Few- Shot Learning (FSL) scenarios. We further compare the CALLM framework with other data augmentation methods and PTSD diagnostic works and demonstrates consistent improvements. Compared to conventional data collection methods, our synthetic dataset not only demonstrates su- perior performance but also incurs less than 1% of the associated costs. Index Terms—Affective computing, post-traumatic stress disorders, transformer, large language models, data augmentation. Received 23 February 2024; revised 24 June 2024; accepted 24 July 2024. Date of publication 29 July 2024; date of current version 6 De- cember 2024. The work of Yuqi Wu and Kaining Mao was supported by China Scholarship Council under Grant 202308180002 and Grant 202000810031, respectively. (Corresponding authors: Yanbo Zhang; Jie Chen.) Yuqi Wu and Kaining Mao are with the Electrical & Computer Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, University of Al- berta, Edmonton, AB T6G 2R3, Canada (e-mail: yuqi14@ualberta.ca; mmao1@ualberta.ca). Yanbo Zhang is with the Department of Psychiatry, Faculty of Medicine and Dentistry, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB T6G 2R3, Canada (e-mail: yanbo9@ualberta.ca). Jie Chen is with the Electrical & Computer Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB T6G 2R3, Canada, and also with the Academy for Engineering & Technology, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, China (e-mail: jc65@ualberta.ca). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/JBHI.2024.3435085 NOMENCLATURE PTSD Post-Traumatic Stress Disorders. CALLM Clinical interview data Augmentation via Large Language Model. NLP Natural Language Processing. LLM(s) Large Language Model(s). ZSL/ZS Zero-Shot Learning. FSL/FS Few-Shot Learning. T-A-A Textbook-Assignment-Application Dataset Par- titioning Method. GPT-4 Generative Pre-trained Transformer 4. BERT Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. DistilBERT A BERT Variation via Distil Learning. SVM Support Vector Machine classiﬁer. NB Naïve Bayes classiﬁer. bACC Balanced Accuracy; Metric for evaluating imbal- anced datasets. F1 F1-Score; Metric for evaluating imbalanced datasets. AUC Area under Receiver Operating Characteristic; Metric for classiﬁcation. E-DAIC Extended Distress Analysis Interview Corpus; Contains PTSD Clinical Interviews. SM_aug Augmented dataset via SMOTE resampling. BT_aug Augmented dataset via Back Translation. TR_aug Augmented dataset via Text Replacement. ZSL_aug Augmented dataset via CALLM ZSL method. FSL_aug Augmented dataset via CALLM FSL method. I. INTRODUCTION T HE global burden of mental health issues, encompassing conditions such as depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), has steadily increased. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), an estimated one billion people worldwide are suffering from mental disorders, resulting in trillions of dollars in economic burden due to disability and decreasedproductivity[3].TheCOVID-19pandemichasfurther exacerbated mental health challenges, leading to overwhelming demand for mental health services [4], [5], [6]. However, mental health patients encounter substantial barriers when accessing care, signiﬁcantly impacting both individual well-being and 2168-2194 © 2024 IEEE. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 7532 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 28, NO. 12, DECEMBER 2024 public health [7], [8], [9], [10]. The scarcity of skilled mental health practitioners, particularly in rural and remote areas, leads to extensive waiting lists and delayed diagnosis and treatment. Inadequate insurance coverage for mental health services can render treatment unaffordable for many individuals, particularly in regions where such coverage is limited or nonexistent. Traditionally, diagnosing mental disorders has been the purview of experienced professionals who conduct clinical as- sessments. Nevertheless, due to limited resources in mental healthcare,securingtimelyaccesstomentalhealthprofessionals remains a global challenge. The emergence of artiﬁcial intelli- gence (AI) has sparked increased interest in harnessing machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) technologies to automate mental health assessments [11], [12]. Among DL technologies, naturallanguageprocessing(NLP)standsoutasapopularchoice for comprehending the nuances of human language, both spoken and written [12], [13]. NLP empowers machines to analyze the complex language utilized in clinical interviews and develop algorithms for disease prediction or diagnosis [13]. However, developing accurate disease prediction or diagnostic algorithms necessitates access to extensive clinical datasets, posing a sig- niﬁcant obstacle for NLP applications in the medical domain. Acquiring clinical interview data for research entails consid- erable time, costs, and ethical considerations. Consequently, available clinical interview databases for mental disorder studies are scarce. Existing datasets often exhibit small sample sizes and imbalanced data distribution [14], [15], resulting in biased and inaccurate ML outcomes. Techniques such as back translation [16] and text editing [17] have been employed to augment and rebalance data to address these challenges. Such methods often fall into the Few-shot Learning (FSL) category in ML, characterized by a limited amount of training data. When the ML model faces an absent of training data, it transitions into Zero-shot Learning (ZSL). The advent of large language models (LLMs), like ChatGPT [18], has prompted many researchers to explore novel applica- tions. Some employ these models to generate new text data to supplement datasets, particularly in hate speech detection and emotion analysis [19], [20], [21], [22]. Unlike earlier methods that altered sentence structures, these new techniques retain the core ideas while introducing detailed variations. This diversity enhances dataset quality, aiding ML models in acquiring more comprehensive and diverse information. To maximize the per- formance of LLMs, clear and well-structured prompts have be- come crucial to advance the evolution of “prompt engineering.” Researchers have developed various strategies for creating and utilizing prompts, consistently yielding improved results when used with LLMs [23]. In our previous research, we introduced an innovative method employing LLMs to augment clinical interview datasets [24]. In this study, we have further reﬁned our approach by intro- ducing a systematic “Textbook-Assignment-Application” strat- egy for dataset expansion and incorporating prompt engineer- ing techniques. Our “Response-Reason” prompting approach guides LLMs in generating highly authentic clinical interview transcripts for mental disorder diagnosis. This augmentation is tailored to enhance the training dataset, facilitating both FSL and, in certain cases, ZSL. We refer to this framework as CALLM: Clinical interview data Augmentation via Large Language Models. When applied to a widely used PTSD clinical dataset, our method demonstrated signiﬁcant improvements in PTSD diagnosis based on content analysis. This study represents a pioneering effort in utilizing LLMs for clinical interview data augmentation in AI-driven mental disorder diagnosis. Our novel contributions can be summarized as follows: leveraging LLMs to augment clinical interview datasets and validation of our approach using a widely used PTSD clinical interview dataset; amalgamation of various prompt engineering techniques and introduction of a “Response-Reason” prompting paradigm for generating professional content; introduction of a “Textbook- Assignment-Application” dataset partitioning strategy for con- structing artiﬁcial clinical interview datasets; and utilization of a distill learning classiﬁcation model to perform downstream PTSD classiﬁcation following the upstream emotional classiﬁ- cation task.1 II. RELATED WORK A. Data Imbalance in Medical Domain Within the domain of AI-assisted medical diagnosis, the issue of data imbalance is a pervasive challenge that impedes opti- mal model performance and generalization capacity. Typically, medical datasets consist primarily of “normal” samples with a smaller proportion of “abnormal” ones, thereby inducing the classiﬁer to overﬁt to the majority class. As a result, this issue poses signiﬁcant impediments to accurate and reliable disease classiﬁcation as misclassifying the minority class results in greater losses compared to misclassifying the majority class [25], [26]. The prevalence of data imbalance in the medical domain can be attributed to the challenges associated with acquiring samples and the distribution of normal and abnor- mal cases. Typically, the collection of clinical data involves a rigorous process, encompassing various stages such as Case Report Form (CRF) design, CRF annotation, database design, data entry, data validation, discrepancy management, medical coding, data extraction, and database locking. These stages are periodically evaluated for quality during clinical trials [27]. Each of these stages requires signiﬁcant time, labor, and ﬁnancial resources, and yet the collected data remains limited in quantity. Common methods that people use to resolve the medical data imbalance include dataset resampling techniques such as oversampling and undersampling, synthetic data generation methods such as Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE), ensemble models such as bagging and tree-based models, cost-sensitive learning, transfer learning, and data aug- mentation [13], [28], [29], [30], [31], [32], [33]. Data augmentation is a widely used technique to address the limited amount of data in machine learning. This approach generates new data by manipulating the existing dataset without signiﬁcantly increasing the number of samples [32]. In NLP, augmentation commonly involves introducing various types of noise to the existing corpus (lexical substitution), such as 1Data available at url https://github.com/YWU99u/CALLM Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. WU et al.: CALLM: ENHANCING CLINICAL INTERVIEW ANALYSIS THROUGH DATA AUGMENTATION WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS 7533 synonyms or grammatical variations, random insertion, random swap, and random deletion [17]. Another popular approach is the replacement of words with their synonyms, which can create a new and diverse set of examples for training machine learning models [34]. In addition, back-translation is rising as another popular method for textual data augmentation. In Sennrich’s work, they applied the back-translation method to translate the text to another language and then translate it back to the original language to enhance the original dataset [16]. In recent years, several studies have utilized LLMs including BERT and the GPT family, to generate high-quality synthetic textual data for augmenting NLP datasets. These methods have shown remark- able performance improvements, highlighting the effectiveness of LLMs in generating realistic textual data for NLP applications [24], [35], [36]. In this study, we systematically extend this method to align with psychiatric applications, to address data shortage obstacles in the mental health domain. B. Few-Shot Learning In the ﬁeld of medical AI, the performance of machine learning models is heavily impacted by the scarcity of labelled datasets. Obtaining a large dataset can be difﬁcult or impossible due to concerns regarding safety, ethics, or privacy. This type of problem is known as few-shot learning (FSL) in the machine learning community. Essentially, FSL refers to a machine-learning problem in which there are a limited number of examples available with supervised information for the tar- get. Overcoming FSL can advance machine learning towards human-like intelligence, allowing for the learning of rare cases when sufﬁcient data is not available, and reducing the effort and cost of data collection [37]. This type of paradigm was ﬁrst proposed by [38], [39] and various methods have been developed to cope with this issue. Common methods to resolve FSL include data augmentation approaches (training sample transformation, weakly labelled or unlabeled data transformation, similar data transformation), model enhancement approaches (multitask learning, embed- ding learning, external memory learning, generative modelling), and algorithm-based approaches (parameter reﬁnement, meta- learned parameter reﬁnement, learning the optimizer) [37], [40], [41], [42], [43], [44], [45], [46]. In this study, a data augmen- tation approach is developed to cope with zero-shot learning (a subset of FSL where the training set contains no data from the target [47]) by using LLMs. C. Large Language Model Large Language Models (LLMs) are advanced neural network-based models designed to understand and generate human-like text by leveraging massive amounts of data and parameters (usually more than billions of parameters). These models, such as GPT-3 [48] and GPT-4 [18] by OpenAI, Llama2 [49] by Meta, and Claude3 by Anthropic [50], have revolu- tionized NLP with their impressive capabilities in tasks like translation, summarization, and question answering due to their emergent abilities [51]. LLMs utilize transformer architectures [52], the most widely used learning architecture nowadays, to process input text in parallel, capturing complex patterns and dependencies within the data. Their applications span various domains, including conversational agents [53], commonsense reasoning [54], medical diagnosis support [55], and code gener- ation [56]. The continual improvement and scalability of LLMs have led to signiﬁcant advancements in their performance, mak- ing them indispensable tools in both academic research and commercial applications. In this study, the proposed CALLM framework utilizes the text generation capability to perform data augmentation, which enhances the NLP model performance and reduces data collection efforts in biomedical applications. D. Prompt Engineering To optimize the usage of conversational LLMs such as Chat- GPT, we need to carefully design the prompts that we feed into the LLMs. A prompt serves as a set of guidelines given to an LLM, aimed at tailoring, enhancing, or ﬁne-tuning the model’s functionalities [59]. In the traditional supervised learning frame- work for NLP, an input x, typically a text, is used to predict an output y based on a model P(y|x; θ). The output y could be a label, text, or various other types of output. To determine the parameters θ for this model, a dataset comprising parts of inputs and outputs is employed and the model is trained to estimate this conditional probability. This method often needs large numbers of annotated data. To cope with this issue, prompt-based learning approaches focus on learning a language model that estimates the probability P(y|x; θ) of the text x itself. This probability is then used to predict y, hence lessening or even eliminating the requirement for an extensive labelled dataset [23]. A pressing issue with existing LLMs is the occasional gen- eration of unreliable or ambiguous responses, which is com- monly referred as LLM hallucination [60]. This hallucination is particularly pronounced in complex subject matters such as mathematical queries, specialized knowledge, and engineering problems [61]. To address this, researchers have introduced various techniques to structure prompts more effectively, in- cluding Chain of Thoughts [62], Tree of Thoughts [63], and Self-Consistency [64]. These methodologies serve to enhance the LLMs’ comprehension of task requirements and establish stringent guidelines for the models to adhere to. In the present study, we have incorporated multiple prompt engineering techniques to generate more authentic and reliable content. III. DATASET AND METHOD Utilizing the CALLM framework, we adopted a PTSD clin- ical interview dataset for diagnostic classiﬁcation. Given the limitation of the original dataset, including its small size and signiﬁcant class imbalance, we utilized the power of LLMs to create synthetic yet lifelike transcripts through carefully de- signed prompt engineering. The data augmentation process is shown in Fig. 1. The CALLM workﬂow begins with the creation of a Mock Patient Generator by our team clinical psychiatrist. This tool generates comprehensive patient proﬁles, encompassing demo- graphic and clinical characteristics that closely resemble real- world scenarios. These proﬁles serve as inputs for two LLM Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 7534 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 28, NO. 12, DECEMBER 2024 Fig. 1. CALLM data augmentation workﬂow. transcript generators: the deterministic generator, constructed based on the “Response-Reason” approach in alignment with the DSM-V criteria [65], and the stochastic generator, designed for generating less structured responses. The resulting transcripts are categorized into the “Textbook” and “Assignment” datasets, respectively, with an additional “Application” dataset sourced from the original PTSD training set. These datasets are then mergedtocreateanaugmenteddataset. UtilizingtheDistilBERT pre-trained language model [66], we ﬁne-tuned a machine learn- ing classiﬁer tailored for emotional classiﬁcation tasks on this combined dataset. Subsequently, we evaluated the classiﬁer’s performance on the original development and test datasets. This comprehensive approach ensures CALLM’s aptitude for simulating and understanding clinical emotional contexts. In the ﬁnal stage, we trained the model using the augmented dataset and evaluated the performances on the original development and test datasets. A. E-DAIC Dataset In this study, we utilized the Extended Distress Analysis Inter- view Corpus (E-DAIC) dataset to validate our zero-shot classi- ﬁer [67]. The E-DAIC dataset is an expansion of the WOZ-DAIC dataset [68], comprising semi-structured clinical interviews de- signed to assist in identifying psychological distress conditions such as anxiety, depression, and post-traumatic stress disorder. The objective behind collecting these interviews was to develop a computational agent capable of conducting interviews with hu- man subjects and effectively detecting verbal and nonverbal cues indicative of mental illnesses. These interviews were facilitated by a computer-generated virtual interviewer named Ellie. These interviews were conducted in two settings, one be- ing the Wizard-of-Oz (WoZ) environment, where a human interviewer (wizard) remotely directed the actions of the virtual agent from a separate location. The second setting involved in- terviews conducted by an artiﬁcial intelligence-controlled agent, Fig. 2. E-DAIC sample distribution. whichutilizedvariousautomatedperceptionandbehaviorgener- ation modules to operate independently. The dataset included the clinical interview recordings of 219 participants, and associated transcripts and corresponding depression and PTSD severity levels. The dataset was partitioned into training (n = 163), developing (n = 56), and testing (n = 56) datasets with care- ful consideration of sample distribution. The E-DAIC dataset had a low PTSD Positive to PTSD Negative ratio; as shown in Fig. 2. Given the ethical considerations inherent in mental disorder studies, particularly those concerning PTSD, research in automated PTSD diagnosis often relies on datasets that are either self-collected or derived from social media texts. The former tends to be restricted in terms of public availability due to the sensitive nature of the data, while the latter, although more accessible, lacks the depth and speciﬁcity of clinical interview information. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. WU et al.: CALLM: ENHANCING CLINICAL INTERVIEW ANALYSIS THROUGH DATA AUGMENTATION WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS 7535 TABLE I DETERMINISTIC TRANSCRIPT GENERATOR EXAMPLE Due to these constraints, the E-DAIC emerged as the sole dataset suitable for our study’s requirements. Its selection was based on two key criteria: public availability and the foundation in standard clinical interview settings. The E-DAIC dataset’s adherence to these criteria makes it an invaluable resource for research in this domain, providing a rich base of data that is both ethically sourced and relevant to the clinical context of PTSD diagnosis. B. Prompt Design The prompt design constituted the initial step within the CALLM framework. Our prompts were composed of two parts: the system message and the human message. The system mes- sage deﬁned the role of the LLM agent and speciﬁed the pre- requisites for the text generation process. We integrated mock patient proﬁles and directed the agent to assume the persona of the simulated patient. The agent’s role was deﬁned using predeﬁned templates. The human message entailed selecting random, yet representative questions typically asked during PTSD clinical interviews, to which the agent was tasked with providing individual responses. Within our framework’s architecture, we incorporated two distinct types of transcript generators: the Deterministic Tran- script Generator and the Stochastic Prompt Generator. The ﬁrst was meticulously engineered to align with the DSM-V criteria for PTSD, demanding a rigorous approach to prompt engineering. Our goal was to compel the conversational agent, particularly GPT-4, to generate content that would effectively fa- cilitatetheclassiﬁerinaccuratelydistinguishingbetweenPTSD- positive and PTSD-negative transcripts. To achieve this objec- tive, we employed the Chain-of-Thoughts prompting method- ology [62]. This technique not only instructed the LLM agent to respond to inquiries or statements from human interlocutors but also encouraged it to elucidate the rationale behind the responses, mirroring the proﬁle and characteristics of a sim- ulated patient. A representative example of these “Response- Reason” prompts can be found in Table I. In contrast, real-world clinical interviews often diverge from standardized protocols, eliciting a wide range of unexpected patient responses. Moreover, clinicians frequently employ board questions to mitigate patient distress when recalling traumatic experiences. To address these challenges, we speciﬁcally engi- neered prompts to guide the agent in generating inventive yet clinically relevant responses without obscuring crucial PTSD indicators. This was achieved through our Stochastic Transcript Generator, which used less restrictive prompts to capture the inherent variability and latent information within the simulated patient’s transcript. An example of such a stochastic prompt is delineated in Table II. Within the GPT-4 API parameters, a “temperature” setting adjusts the randomness of the generated responses. A higher “temperature” value yields more random and creative responses. Accordingly, we opted to minimize the “temperature” setting for the Deterministic Transcript Generator to ensure greater precision and adherence to clinical guidelines. Conversely, the “temperature” was deliberately set higher for the Stochastic Prompt Generator to permit a broader exploration of potential patient responses. C. Augmented Dataset Partitioning We obtained three distinct datasets after the transcript gener- ation process, including the original E-DAIC training set. These datasets contained varying types of information and adhered to different standards of clinical relevance and ﬁdelity. The ﬁrst dataset, generated by the Deterministic Transcript Generator, exhibited a higher degree of standardization and closely aligned with the criteria stipulated in the DSM-V. Due to its prescrip- tive nature and alignment with conventional clinical guidelines, we designated this dataset as the “Textbook” dataset. In con- trast, the second dataset, emanated from the Stochastic Prompt Generator, was inherently designed to introduce elements of unpredictability and creativity. While this dataset deviated from the stringency observed in the “Textbook” dataset, it re- tained salient features in line with PTSD diagnostic criteria. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 7536 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 28, NO. 12, DECEMBER 2024 TABLE II STOCHASTIC TRANSCRIPT GENERATOR EXAMPLE Accordingly, we named this “Assignment” dataset to reﬂect its hybrid nature—less standardized yet clinically informative. The third dataset, identiﬁed as the “Application” dataset, was composed of samples from the original E-DAIC training dataset, ensuring data distribution and proﬁle consistent with the down- stream development and testing datasets while providing an authentic representation of real-world clinical scenarios. In sum- mary, the “Textbook” and “Assignment” datasets were synthetic datasets while the “Application” dataset was the real clinical dataset (E-DAIC). We concatenated these three datasets to create our aug- mented dataset, resulting in a comprehensive amalgamation. This dataset integration approach is known as the “Textbook- Assignment-Application” (T-A-A) partitioning strategy. The nomenclature parallels real-world learning paradigms: text- books offer foundational and standardized knowledge, assign- ments reinforce essential concepts while introducing variability, and applications present complex problems that demand solu- tions. The T-A-A framework guided the ML models’ learning process, particularly in PTSD transcript analysis. Initially, the model assimilated the most salient characteristics from the “Textbook” subset. It then reﬁned its capabilities by extracting latent features from the “Assignment” subset while disregarding irrelevant distractors. Finally, the model acquired the ability to comprehend the target distribution through the “Application” subset. Here, we introduce the T-A-A ratio as a tunable hyperpa- rameter in this experimental conﬁguration. This ratio speciﬁes the proportional distribution of samples across the “Textbook,” “Assignment,” and “Application” subsets. Researchers have the ﬂexibility to calibrate this ratio, allowing for the optimization of variousperformancemetrics,suchasmodelaccuracy,overﬁtting mitigation, and overall model efﬁcacy. Excluding the “Appli- cation” subset from the T-A-A deﬁnes the problem as a ZSL, as the training set does not contain any original data from the E-DAIC dataset. Conversely, including the “Application” subset characterizes the learning problem FSL. D. DistilBERT Model In recent years, transformer-based language models have dominated the ﬁeld of NLP [52]. The transformer architecture represented a departure from the recurrent or convolutional Fig. 3. DistilBERT architecture. neural network architectures common for sequence-based tasks at the time of its introduction. Instead, transformer architecture relies on self-attention mechanisms to process input sequences in parallel rather than sequentially. This allows more efﬁcient parallelization during training and handling of longer-range dependencies in the data. Devlin et al introduced one of the most widely used transformer models, Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) [57]. Our CALLM model adopts a popular variant of the BERT model known as DistilBERT for our downstream PTSD classiﬁcation task. DistilBERT was developed to offer a more lightweight alter- native that retains a substantial portion of the original model’s performance capabilities. The term “Distil” in DistilBERT refers to the concept of “knowledge distillation,” where a smaller model (the student) is trained to mimic the behavior of a larger, more complex model (the teacher). In the case of DistilBERT, the student model is trained to approximate the outputs of BERT with fewer parameters, resulting in lower computational requirements. The overall architecture of DistilBERT is shown in Fig. 3. In the DistilBERT model, we initiated the process by vec- torizing the text transcripts and converting them into text em- beddings. These embeddings are high-dimensional vectors that contain semantic information and word relationships in a nu- merical format, making them more amendable for ML models. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. WU et al.: CALLM: ENHANCING CLINICAL INTERVIEW ANALYSIS THROUGH DATA AUGMENTATION WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS 7537 Subsequently, we fed these embeddings into the teacher and student models, each comprising transformer layers. Notably, the teacher model boasted a greater number of transformer layers (n) compared to the student model (m). Each transformer layer block encompassed a multi-head self-attention layer and a feed- forward layer. The self-attention layer computed correlations andsimilaritiesamongwords,whilethefeed-forwardlayerfunc- tioned as a neural network layer that learned latent information. Regularization was achieved through the Add&Norm layer. For the teacher model, the ﬁnal outputs from the last trans- former layer were used to perform the “soft prediction” of the label with the SoftMax activation function. The generalized SoftMax activation function calculated predicted probabilities for each class, as determined by (1), where T represents the temperature, which controls the smoothness of the output dis- tribution. In the standard scenario with T set to 1, the predicted class had an output closer to 1, while other classes approached zero. Increasing the T value resulted in a smoother distribution from the SoftMax function, with the predicted class receiving relatively higher weights but not too distant from the other classes. This allowed us to extract latent information from the given text. Here, Zi represents the output logit, and yi(X|t) represents the output probability for the corresponding class. The teacher networks employed a higher temperature SoftMax function, generating soft labels. yi (X|T) = e zi(X) T  j e zj (X) T (1) Conversely, the student network utilized both one- temperature and high-temperature SoftMax functions to make hard and soft predictions. Therefore, the overall loss of function in DistilBERT consisted of a distillation loss (measuring the difference between the soft labels and soft predictions) and the student loss (evaluating the difference between hard pre- dictions and hard labels) to train the network. Notice that here soft labels and predictions were derived from high-temperature SoftMax while hard labels and predictions were derived from one-temperature SoftMax. DistilBERT aligns with our task re- quirements for several reasons. First, due to the model’s reduced size, it offers enhanced computational efﬁciency and mitigates the risk of overﬁtting. In clinical ML applications, datasets are often smaller compared to other NLP tasks, even with our aug- mented methods. DistilBERT helped prevent overﬁtting since it has fewer learning parameters and a simpler structure. Given the strong correlation between psychiatric issues and a patient’s emotional state, we incorporated two tasks in our ﬁne-tuning process. The upstream task involves emotional clas- siﬁcation, categorizing sentences as “positive” or “negative” in tones. Fine-tuning for the upstream task was performed using well-known sentiment analysis datasets sst-2 [69] and glue [70], [71]. This allowed the model to analyze emotional cues in each transcript. For the downstream task, we conducted a second round of ﬁnetuning using our augmented dataset. To evaluate our model performance, we employed metrics such as balanced accuracy (the arithmetic mean of sensitivity and speciﬁcity; we denote it as bACC in the following context.), F1-score (the harmonic means of precision and recall), and Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristics (the capability to distinguish different classes; also known as AUC-ROC or AUC). IV. EXPERIMENT AND RESULTS In this section, we analyzed the result of PTSD classiﬁcation on both the original E-DAIC dataset and the augmented datasets via various augmentation methods. Since the original E-DAIC dataset contains only English transcript, the CALLM framework also generated synthetic data in English. We then compared the overall PTSD diagnostic performance of CALLM frame- work to other PTSD diagnostic works on E-DAIC transcript datasets. Additionally, we compared the performances of the direct prompting method and our proposed “Response-Reason” prompting techniques. Finally, we examined the impacts of various T-A-A ratios, a tunable hyperparameter that can affect overall model performance. A. Experiment Setup All experiments were conducted using two NVIDIA RTX A6000 48 GB GPUs. The LLM employed in our transcript gen- eration was GPT-4. The DistilBERT model2 was ﬁnetuned using sst-2 and glue datasets for the upstream emotional detection task. To reduce overﬁtting, we adjusted the transformer dropout rate to 0.2 and the classiﬁcation dropout rate to 0.3. We employed a linear decay learning rate with an initial value of 8e-6, conducted training for 50 epochs with early stopping, and used a batch size of 32. To train the PTSD classiﬁer, we tokenized all the text tran- scripts, converting the text information into numerical represen- tations for embedding. Since DistilBERT can handle lengthy text, we directly used the entire transcript paragraph as the model input to preserve as much information as possible. Then, we fed the embeddings into the DistilBERT model to train the PTSD classiﬁer. The average ﬁnetuning duration for each model was around 10-15 minutes. The classiﬁcation in this study was binary, including PTSD-positive and PTSD-negative classes. To address the real-life problem, we exclusively uti- lized the E-DAIC development and test datasets for evaluation, maintaining consistency across all experiments. Notice that a cross-validation evaluation is not suitable in this case since by evaluating our model on the unchanged dev and test sets, we ensure that any performance improvements are attributable to the augmentation strategy and not to variations in data splitting. B. Original Dataset vs. Augmented Datasets As discussed in the previous sections, the original E-DAIC dataset faced challenges of small and imbalanced training data. The augmented datasets aimed to address these issues by expanding the training set with artiﬁcial transcripts. We experi- mented with two types of augmented datasets: the ZSL method (excluding the application subset) and the FSL method (T-A-A dataset). The sample size and distribution of the three datasets are reported in Table III. To maintain a balance between the 2https://huggingface.co/distilbert-base-uncased-ﬁnetuned-sst-2-english Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. The original dataset’s positive-to-negative ratio (PTR) was 0.43, indicating that the number of PTSD-positive samples was less than half of PTSD-negative cases. This sig- niﬁcant imbalance hindered the model’s learning ability given the small sample size. Therefore, we intentionally generated the same positive and negative classes in our augmented datasets to address the imbalance issue. To test if the CALLM augmentation method can signiﬁcantly boost the classiﬁcation performance, we compared it with var- ious commonly used argumentation methods, such as SMOTE upsampling(SM),BackTranslation(BT),andTextReplacement (TR). In summary, six different datasets were included in our experiment, including E-DAIC (the baseline), SM-augmented dataset,BT-augmenteddataset,TR-augmenteddataset,CALLM FSL-augmented dataset, and CALLM ZSL-augmented dataset. We then evaluated the classiﬁcation performance of three differ- ent models—DistilBERT, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Naïve Bayes (NB)—across three datasets. The results are de- tailedinTableIV, clearlydemonstratethatboththeZSLandFSL augmented datasets signiﬁcantly outperformed the original E- DAIC training dataset. Using the DistilBERT classiﬁer, the ZSL approach achieved the highest performance metrics: a bACC of 0.73, an F1-score of 0.67, and an AUC of 0.73 on the test set. Meanwhile, the FSL dataset, though slightly less effective, still surpassed the original dataset with a bACC of 0.65, an F1-score of 0.58, and an AUC of 0.65 on the development set using the same DistilBERT classiﬁer. With the same classiﬁcation model setting, SM, BT, and TR-augmented methods can indeed improve the overﬁtting effect (Higher F1-scores). However, the improvement is mild compared to both CALLM-augmented datasets. Confusion matrices of the baseline EDAIC and two CALLM augmented datasets are visualized in Fig. 4, reveal- ing insights into the model’s predictions. Notably, the original E-DAIC dataset suffered from the data imbalance issue, as the model primarily predicted the majority class (PTSD-negative). Instead, both augmented datasets balanced the distribution of minority and majority classes, allowing them to predict both classes effectively and addressing the data imbalance issue. These behaviors were also observed in the running performance (loss) curve in Fig. 5(a), where the evaluation loss of the E-DAIC dataset diverged while it converged with CALLM augmentation. This indicated that the model trained with E-DAIC dataset suf- fered from overﬁtting, yet CALLM succeeded in mitigating it. The ROC-AUC curve for test set is shown in Fig. 5(b).
