The larger sample size in the augmented dataset compared to the original E-DAIC training set aligned with our initial goal of expanding the limited real dataset. C. PTSD Diagnosis Performance In terms of diagnostic performance, the proposed DistilBERT classiﬁcation model consistently outperforms the baseline Naïve Bayes and Support Vector Machine model. From Table IV, it can be observed that the overﬁtting issue caused by the imbalanced data signiﬁcantly reduced, supported by superior F1 scores. To further verify the overall PTSD diagnosis performance of the proposed framework, we compared the CALLM + Distil- BERT combination to different current studies on automated PTSD diagnosis. We included two PTSD works on the E-DAIC dataset and summarized performance comparison in Table V. In Galatzer-Levy et al. work, they achieved a PTSD diagnostic bACCof0.74andF1of0.64byutilizingLLMsdirectlyconduct- ing diagnosis on transcripts [1]. By combining facial features to bag-of-words, Flores et al. achieved a multimodality diagnostic bACC of 0.70 and F1 of 0.69 on average [2]. In our proposed TABLE V PTSD DIAGNOSIS PERFORMANCE COMPARISON ON E-DAIC DATASET CALLM framework, we achieved an overall bACC of 0.77 and F1 of 0.70 by only using text modality. D. Prompt Engineering Effects Prompt design is pivotal in generating realistic and structural content in LLM-assisted text augmentation. Our approach in- volved designing two distinct transcript generators, the deter- ministic transcript generator and stochastic transcript generator, by employing different prompt engineering techniques to create the “Textbook” and “Assignment” subsets. Our experiments revealed the “Textbook” subset contained standard and evident indicators of PTSD, resulting in faster training loss convergence than the “Assignment” and “Application” subsets. However, the Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 7540 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 28, NO. 12, DECEMBER 2024 evaluation loss in the “Textbook” subset diverged and was the highest among the three datasets. The “Application” dataset, ow- ing to its non-standard nature, contained fewer PTSD indicators, resulting in slower training loss convergence. The “Assignment” subset, being semi-standard, displayed performance levels in between the other two subsets. In our previously published work [24], we employed a Direct Prompting Methodology (directly prompting instructions to the generative agent without prompt engineering) to synthetically generate a corpus of 3281 transcripts, resulting in a classiﬁcation bACC of 0.75, an F1-score of 0.71, and an AUC of 0.76 on the evaluation set. However, a notable constraint of this initial approach was the inability of the training loss to converge opti- mally, suggesting that the dataset lacked robust PTSD symptom indicators. Furthermore, the disproportionate size of the aug- mented dataset compared to the evaluation sets may have artiﬁ- cially inﬂated the training outcomes, potentially reducing model generalizability. To address these issues, we reﬁned our data augmentation strategy by implementing a “Response-Reason” framework to enhance the generated content’s structural in- tegrity. This iterative improvement raised classiﬁcation bACC to 0.82 and F1-score to 0.74 on the evaluation set, substantially reducing training loss. Remarkably, this enhanced performance was achieved with a dataset only half the original’s size, thereby underscoring our updated methodology’s efﬁcacy. E. T-A-A Ratio Test In our proposed T-A-A dataset partitioning strategy, we inves- tigated the impact of the T-A-A ratio as a tunable hyperparameter on overall classiﬁcation performance. To ensure a consistent sample size, we conducted experiments with the same number of samples but varied T-A-A ratios. We maintained a ﬁxed dataset size of 978, equivalent to six times the size of the original E-DAIC training set. We explored 11 T-A-A ratio combinations, including 0:5:1, 1:4:1, 2:3:1, 3:2:1, 4:1:1, 5:0:1, 5:1:0, 4:2:0, 3:3:0, 2:4:0, and 1:5:0, where each digit represented a multiple of 163 (the original E-DAIC trainset). The ROC-AUC plots for these 11 combinations are shown in Fig. 6. Notably, the T-A-A ratio signiﬁcantly impacted performance metrics, with AUC values ranging from 0.51 to 0.76. This variation underscores the importance of transcript distribution among different subsets in the augmented dataset, establishing the T-A-A ratio as a critical tunable hyperparameter. Further- more,ZSL_augdatasets(withanapplicationdigitof0)generally exhibited superior results compared to FSL_aug datasets (with an application digit of 1). The mean AUC value for ZSL_aug datasets was 0.69 with a standard deviation of 0.05, while FSL datasets had a mean AUC of 0.66 with a standard deviation of 0.08. This suggests that ZSL methods not only demonstrated enhanced efﬁcacy but also exhibited relative stability. Inter- estingly, the highest AUC value of 0.76 was achieved in an FSL dataset with a T-A-A ratio of 5:0:1, primarily composed of the “Textbook” and “Application” categories. Additionally, FSL datasets with T-A-A ratios of 4:0:1 and 3:0:1 outperformed ZSL_aug datasets, despite having identical sample sizes but different T-A-A conﬁgurations. However, it was observed that Fig. 6. ROC-AUC curves for 11 combinations T-A-A ratio augmented datasets with ﬁxed dataset size. these types of FSL datasets were susceptible to overﬁtting, as indicated by elevated evaluation loss metrics. F. Data Collection Cost Comparison During the artiﬁcial transcript generation process, we utilized the GPT-4 API provided by OpenAI, resulting in a total of 3000 transcripts. Excluding the experimental trials, we collected a total of 1900 transcripts. The cost of the GPT-4 API was $0.06/1k tokens (or $0.06/750words) for input prompts and $0.12/1k tokens (or $0.12/750words)3 for output transcripts. Excluding the experimental cost, the ﬁnal cost of both the ZSL and FSL augmented dataset was $86.79 USD. Regarding time consumption, it took us less than 2 months to design the experiment and 30 hours to generate the mock transcripts. In contrast, when considering a real clinical dataset project, the expenses and time consumption are notably higher than our augmentedmethod.Forexample,1900clinicalinterviewswould typically require a minimum of 1500 hours. With an average rate of $30/hour for a Ph.D.-level research assistant, the interview process could cost up to $45000 USD. Further, this calculation does not encompass additional expenses such as transcript costs, participant compensation, administrative and legal fees, travel and logistics, and expert annotation, which can further escalate the total costs. Our CALLM framework signiﬁcantly reduces the overall dataset collection expenses and the time required for data acqui- sition, making it a cost-effective and highly efﬁcient approach compared to traditional data collection methods. V. LIMITATIONS AND FUTURE IMPROVEMENT Several limitations are evident in the scope and methodology of this study. First, the quality of the mock samples generated by our framework is inherently linked to the capabilities of the LLMs responsible for transcript generation. Different LLMs may introduce variability, potentially impacting classiﬁcation 3https://openai.com/pricing#language-models Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. WU et al.: CALLM: ENHANCING CLINICAL INTERVIEW ANALYSIS THROUGH DATA AUGMENTATION WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS 7541 outcomes. This limitation can be expected to be resolved as the LLM develops. LLM providers benchmark their LLMs with others on a timely basis. According to their technical reports, new generations can signiﬁcantly improve the generative abil- ity. Second, despite advanced prompt engineering techniques, LLMs have inherent linguistic limitations inﬂuenced by the training data on which they are based. These limitations include repetitive patterns and restricted vocabulary in generated con- tent.Forexample,certaintermslike“onedge”or“anxious”were frequently used, potentially introducing bias into downstream classiﬁers. As a solution, further textual manipulations, such as synonym replacement or rephrase, can be adopted to minimize this effect. Third, LLMs have knowledge cutoffs corresponding to their training data, potentially missing out on terminologies or concepts introduced after their last training update. Therefore, a timely update of the dataset, as well as using the up-to-date LLMmodelisnecessarytocopewiththisissue.Lastly,thisstudy was only conducted on one dataset since the dataset which met our task requirement remained limited. The study can be further expanded once a new publicly available PTSD clinical dataset is released. In future work, it will be imperative to expand the validation scope of the CALLM framework by testing it on additional medical datasets. This study focused exclusively on PTSD clas- siﬁcation with prompts engineered to generate PTSD-speciﬁc responses. Future research should include other mental health conditions, such as depression and anxiety. Additionally, future studiesshouldincorporatefactorssuchascomorbidityintomock patient proﬁles, aimingtocreatemorecomprehensivetranscripts that address multiple correlated mental disorders. VI. CONCLUSION In this study, we introduced the CALLM framework, a novel approach for augmenting clinical interview datasets, and ap- plied it to PTSD classiﬁcation using machine learning (ML) algorithms. Our CALLM framework utilizes LLMs to address common challenges in the medical ﬁeld, such as data shortage and imbalance, and substantially reduces the costs and time re- quired to generate synthetic transcripts. The framework utilizes mock patient proﬁles and employs a “Response-Reason” prompt engineering technique to produce standard and semi-standard PTSD clinical interview transcripts. The resulting augmented dataset consists of three components: the standard “Textbook” component, the semi-standard “Assignment” component, and the “Application” component sourced from the widely used E-DAIC dataset. We term this triad of data subsets as the “Textbook-Assignment-Application” scheme and introduce the T-A-A ratio as an adjustable hyperparameter. DistilBERT, a transformer-based pre-trained model, is integrated for PTSD classiﬁcation. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed CALLM framework, we compared the PTSD diagnostic per- formance of CALLM-augmented datasets with 3 different tex- tual augmentation methods and the baseline E-DAIC dataset and demonstrated consistent and signiﬁcant improvement. In addition, the combination of CALLM-augmented dataset and DistilBERT classiﬁer achieved an overall diagnostic balanced accuracy of 0.77, F1 of 0.70 and AUC of 0.78, which outper- forms existing PTSD diagnostic works by utilizing only textual modality. ETHICAL STATEMENT The proposed data augmentation method created mock pa- tients without names, hence no personal information and privacy were exposed. However, when designing the synthetic data generator, the generated content should be monitored to avoid biases and stereotypes.

text: Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 https://doi.org/10.1186/s42400-025-00505-y RESEARCH CTI‑Thinker: an LLM‑driven system for CTI knowledge graph construction and attack reasoning Xiuzhang Yang1 , Ruijie Zhong2, Yuling Chen1,2*, Guojun Peng3, Di Yao1, Chaofan Chen1, Chenyang Wang3, Dongni Zhang3, Yilin Zhou3 and Zixuan Yang1 Abstract With the increasing frequency of APT attacks, cyber defense urgently demands high-quality threat intelligence support. Cyber threat intelligence (CTI) knowledge graphs have demonstrated significant potential in aiding threat detection and behavioral reasoning. However, existing CTI data often suffer from unstructured formats, fragmented knowledge, a reliance on manual annotation, and limited semantic mapping to attack techniques. These limitations hinder the robustness and accuracy of downstream reasoning tasks (e.g., attack attribution and intent inference). Moreover, traditional information extraction methods struggle to generalize in scenarios involving cross-paragraph dependencies, emerging threats, and low-resource samples, exhibiting weaknesses in context awareness and sensi- tivity to prompt variations. To this end, we propose CTI-Thinker, a novel system that integrates large language models with semantic alignment to the ATT&CK framework for CTI knowledge graph construction and threat reasoning. First, CTI-Thinker leverages in-context learning and LoRA-based fine-tuning to extract structured threat entities and relations. Then, it adopts vector-based alignment strategies to unify heterogeneous expressions, enabling entity normalization and knowledge fusion for constructing a high-quality CTI knowledge graph. Finally, a GraphRAG- based reasoning engine is built by incorporating the structured knowledge graph and external ATT&CK resources into a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework, enabling tactical-level inference and CTI-driven ques- tion answering. Experimental results demonstrate that CTI-Thinker accurately extracts threat entities and relations and constructs a reliable CTI knowledge graph. It also effectively infers attack intent and supports intelligent reason- ing. The system outperforms state-of-the-art methods in precision, robustness, and generalizability, offering a scalable and semantically enriched solution for cyber threat analysis and defense. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 Graphical abstract Page 3 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 Introduction Recently, advanced persistent threats (APTs) (Yang et al. 2025), zero-day vulnerability exploitation (Rahimi et al. 2025), DDoS attacks (Tang et al. 2025), and PowerShell fileless attacks (Yang et al. 2023) have posed critical risks to national security, industrial production, and social stability. It is urgent to detect adversarial behaviors and implement proactive defense by leveraging threat intelli- gence knowledge. Cyber threat intelligence (CTI) refers to evidence-based knowledge of threats that documents critical information about cyber incidents or malicious activities, with the objective of supporting defense strat- egy formulation and adversarial behavior modeling (Ain- slie et al. 2023; Hu et al. 2024). By extracting key entities (e.g., APT groups, attack tools, and vulnerability) along with their semantic relations from unstructured texts (e.g., security reports, alert logs, and threat bulletins), fragmented threat knowledge can be transformed into structured attack profiles and knowledge graphs, thereby enhancing the capacity of security systems to compre- hend and reason over complex attack chains (Gao et al. 2021; Jo et al. 2022). However, existing open-source CTI resources com- monly suffer from issues such as unstandardized struc- ture and fragmented knowledge. Also, they often rely excessively on expert annotations and lack deep semantic modeling of advanced adversarial tactics and techniques (Ainslie et al. 2023). These limitations significantly con- strain their effectiveness in tasks such as attack attribu- tion, intent inference, and intelligent question answering. Furthermore, many current approaches fail to fully exploit CTI resources, neglecting the automated extrac- tion of tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) and indicators of compromise (IOCs), which in turn under- mines the robustness and intelligence of threat detection and attribution systems (Zhao et al. 2024). Therefore, it is imperative to develop an intelligent system capable of automatic knowledge extraction and complex attack rea- soning to enable the efficient transformation of unstruc- tured CTI texts into structured security knowledge, thereby supporting advanced semantic inference. In the early stages, researchers primarily employed deep learning models (e.g., BiLSTM-CRF, BERT, and RoBERTa) and natural language processing (NLP) tech- niques to extract CTI entities and relations (Ranade et al. 2021; Yang et al. 2022). Several representative sys- tems further integrated ontology-based multilingual entity extraction and knowledge graph updating, includ- ing CyberEyes (Fang et al. 2021), CSKG4APT (Ren et al. 2022), and SecureBERT (Aghaei et al. 2022). While these methods have shown promise in extracting threat-related information, their heavy reliance on manually annotated data limits adaptability to large-scale and multimodal CTI corpora. Moreover, the separate modeling of enti- ties and relations often fails to capture global semantic dependencies, resulting in error propagation and frag- mented understanding. Also, the insufficient seman- tic alignment with TTPs restricts their effectiveness in supporting behavioral reasoning and high-level threat analysis. With the rapid advancement of large language mod- els, their superior performance in NLP tasks has sig- nificantly expanded the capabilities of security text processing. Equipped with strong capabilities in lan- guage understanding and contextual modeling, LLMs are able to perform complex semantic parsing even under zero-shot and few-shot scenarios, thereby signifi- cantly reducing reliance on annotated data and gradually facilitating the evolution of CTI from manual analysis to semantic understanding (Huang et al. 2025; Chang et al. 2024). Recent studies have begun integrating LLMs into CTI extraction tasks. For instance, CTINEXUS (Cheng et al. 2025) leverages in-context learning strategies to construct knowledge graphs under few-shot settings. LLM-TIKG (Hu et al. 2024) employs LoRA-based fine- tuning and triple generation to accomplish entity and TTP extraction. GPT-RE (Wan et al. 2023) reformulates relation extraction as a question-answering process and incorporates semantic reasoning to improve the struc- tured output. These approaches introduce novel para- digms for CTI extraction and threat analysis, thereby accelerating the development of structured threat intel- ligence systems. However, existing LLM-based threat intelligence extraction methods still face several challenges. First, some approaches focus on isolated entity or relation extraction tasks and lack unified modeling for both knowledge extraction and attack chain reasoning, which limits the construction of semantically complete CTI knowledge graphs. Second, real-world CTI corpora often contain a high degree of polysemy and ambiguity, and the absence of effective entity alignment and knowledge fusion techniques leads to semantic inconsistencies in the extracted knowledge. Third, some methods are highly sensitive to prompt formulation, prone to generating hal- lucinations, and exhibit limited capacity in attack chain reconstruction and deep reasoning due to the lack of graph-based structural assistance and insufficient han- dling of cross-paragraph dependencies and semantic inference. To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose CTI-Thinker, a threat knowledge graph construction and reasoning system enhanced by large language models and ATT&CK semantic mapping. Centered around an LLM architecture, the system integrates in-context learning (ICL), LoRA-based fine-tuning, and a GraphRAG-based Page 4 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 reasoning mechanism to systematically perform struc- tured extraction of threat entities and relations, semantic alignment of entities, knowledge graph fusion, and deep reasoning at the level of tactics, techniques, and proce- dures. This framework significantly enhances the capa- bility for automated threat knowledge extraction and improves the level of intelligent cybersecurity response. In summary, we make the following contributions: • Key insights. By integrating in-context learn- ing (ICL), a LoRA-based LLaMA3-8B model, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) techniques into CTI knowledge graph construction and attack reasoning, we develop CTI-Thinker, a unified system capable of structured knowledge extraction, threat entity alignment, and attack chain inference. We summarize three key insights that guide our research. These insights address critical challenges and intro- duce innovative solutions for extracting threat knowledge and mapping ATT&CK TTPs, including: (i) addressing automatic CTI annotation and entity– relation extraction; (ii) developing entity alignment and knowledge fusion approaches tailored for het- erogeneous threat documents from multiple sources; and (iii) designing a GraphRAG-based reasoning engine for tactical and technical question answering in APT attack scenarios. • Innovative knowledge extraction algorithms. We propose a threat entity and relation extraction algo- rithm that integrates in-context learning with LoRA- fine-tuned large language models. Leveraging struc- ture-aware prompting and cross-sentence contrastive learning, the method reduces redundancy and miti- gates semantic ambiguity in long CTI reports, yield- ing more robust and accurate extraction of threat knowledge. • Innovative knowledge Alignment algorithms. We further introduce a multi-granularity vector align- ment strategy that couples contextual clustering with semantic vector alignment to address the polysemy of CTI terms and cross-sentence disambiguation. The approach fuses threat mentions across paragraphs and paraphrased expressions, thereby constructing a TTP-level knowledge graph that enhances the visu- alization and interpretability of attack contexts. • Innovative attack reasoning engine. We propose a GraphRAG-based attack chain reasoning module that leverages ATT&CK tactic–technique mappings and graph-enhanced retrieval techniques to con- struct semantically coherent, structurally explicit, and tactically complete representations of attack chains. This module enables TTP-level inference and interactive question answering, effectively mitigat- ing hallucination risks and logical inconsistencies commonly observed in the native outputs of large language models. It also significantly enhances the system’s capability to provide interpretable under- standing of APTs. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 • Systematic evaluation and open-source data- set. We constructed a comprehensive CTI data- set encompassing diverse entity and relation types, which has been released as an open-source resource on GitHub.1 Through systematic experiments, we demonstrate that CTI-Thinker outperforms exist- ing state-of-the-art methods in CTI entity and rela- tion extraction. The proposed approach effectively constructs CTI knowledge graphs and generates detailed attack profiles. Moreover, empirical results validate that CTI-Thinker enables intelligent question answering for APT attack scenarios and supports the inference of adversarial tactics and techniques aligned with the ATT&CK framework, thereby exhibiting enhanced generalization and practical applicability. Our research offers substantial aca- demic and real-world contributions to advancing automated threat knowledge extraction and reason- ing. Background and related work This section provides a concise overview of recent works in extracting cyber threat intelligence (CTI). Threat entity recognition and relation extraction A knowledge graph (KG) is a multi-relational graph structure where entities are represented as nodes and semantic relationships as edges. It is designed to model real-world concepts and their interrelations, enabling the organization, storage, and reasoning over complex and heterogeneous knowledge (Hogan et al. 2021). As a key approach for the structured representation of complex information, KGs have been widely applied in domains including search engines (Xiong et al. 2017), healthcare (Piya and Beheshti 2025), industrial control system (Li et al. 2024a), and social network analysis (Abu-Salih et al. 2021). These advances provide a solid foundation for their integration into cybersecurity (Ainslie et al. 2023). Threat entity recognition aims to identify key information enti- ties (e.g., APT groups, malware, attack techniques, or vulnerabilities) from unstructured cybersecurity texts. Relation extraction captures semantic associations (e.g., use, attack) among these entities, constructing the rela- tional structures of CTI knowledge graphs (Ainslie et al. 2023). Figure 1 illustrates how structured triples can be extracted from free-text descriptions. For example, Patchwork using PowerShell and exploiting CVE-2017- 11882 can be represented as a knowledge graph to sup- port downstream tasks (e.g., semantic reasoning or attack detection). Traditional DL-based CTI Extraction. Previous research on CTI extraction methods has primarily relied on natural language processing and deep learning (DL) techniques, including conditional random field (CRF), convolutional neural networks (CNN), bidirectional long short-term memory network (BiLSTM), and Trans- former-based architectures (Zhao et al. 2024). These approaches focused on classifying cybersecurity entities and their semantic relations. CyberEyes (Fang et al. 2021) is a GCN-based cybersecurity entity recognition system that models non-local dependencies through graph rep- resentations of inter-entity relationships. CyberRel (Guo et al. 2021) reformulated entity recognition and relation extraction as a unified multi-sequence labeling task. CSK- G4APT (Ren et al. 2022) leveraged a multilingual BERT- based entity recognition model to support automatic graph updates, APT profiling, and attribution analysis. Yang et al. (2022) proposed an APT entity recognition model based on a BERT-BiLSTM-CRF architecture and constructed structured profiles to represent APT groups. In addition, Mouiche and Saad (2025) conducted a com- prehensive review of threat entity recognition and rela- tion modeling methods, offering a systematic reference framework for subsequent research. Bert-based Variants for CTI Extraction. Since the introduction of Bert, its bidirectional semantic modeling has significantly enhanced the extraction of complex lan- guage patterns, outperforming LSTM and CNN mod- els in handling long-range dependencies. Subsequently, various Bert-based pre-trained variants have been devel- oped to further improve the performance of CTI extrac- tion. Chen et al. (2021) proposed the Joint Bert model that integrated Bert embeddings with LSTM, ID-CNN, and CRF architectures to improve the recognition of software-related entities. CyBERT (Ranade et al. 2021), a domain-adapted model pre-trained on cybersecurity- specific corpora, enhanced contextual representation capabilities and enabled effective entity recognition in cybersecurity texts. Similarly, Aghaei et al. (2022) intro- duced SecureBERT that can recognize security entities and perform threat text classification tasks. TTP-based Threat Behavior Extraction. However, traditional approaches often overlook the logic of the attack chain in cyber intrusions, making it difficult to effectively capture the tactics, techniques, and proce- dures (TTPs) of adversaries. To address this limitation, several knowledge extraction methods have been pro- posed to model attack processes and behaviors. Chen et al. (2025a) introduced EMGE that enhanced cross- sentence relation extraction through semantic enrich- ment and connection modeling. TTPDrill (Husari et al. 2017) combined keyword-based retrieval and semantic matching to extract TTPs and map them to ATT&CK. 1 https://​github.​com/​eastm​ounty​xz/​CTI-​Think​er. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 rcATT (Legoy et al. 2020) reformulated relation extrac- tion as a multi-label classification task and leveraged the ATT&CK framework to enhance model discrimination. Satvat et al. (2021) proposed EXTRACTOR, a system that constructed attack path graphs using semantic role labeling to support traceability and correlation analysis of malicious activities. LADDER mapped extracted attack patterns to the ATT&CK framework based on seman- tic similarity, enabling large-scale threat extraction from CTI reports (Alam et al. 2023). In summary, current CTI extraction works utilize deep learning (e.g., Bert-BiLSTM-CRF), multilingual modeling, joint learning, and cross-sentence relation modeling to extract threat-related entities and relations. Subsequent research focuses on extracting ATT&CK labels from CTI reports and constructing attack path graphs using multi-label classification. However, these methods exhibit several limitations. First, they heavily depend on manually annotated data, which limits their adaptability to real-world CTI corpora. Also, the decou- pled modeling of entities and relations restricts global semantic integration, resulting in higher false-positive rates and limited robustness. Moreover, traditional approaches perform poorly in aligning adversarial TTPs and fail to infer attack behaviors. To address these chal- lenges, LLM-based approaches for threat knowledge extraction have gradually emerged. Large language models and retrieval‑augmented generation techniques Large Language Models. With the rapid advancement of deep learning, large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in language modeling, semantic understanding, and contextual reasoning. Rep- resentative models include the BERT family (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa (Liu et al. 2019)), the GPT series (e.g., GPT-3, GPT-4 (Achiam et al. 2023)), and open-source foundation models (e.g., LLaMA (Touvron et al. 2023) and DeepSeek (Guo et al. 2025a)). In the cybersecurity domain, LLMs have shown great promise in extracting and reasoning over cyber threat intelligence. They can effectively iden- tify entities, events, and relations from unstructured text, supporting the construction of extensible CTI knowledge graphs. Furthermore, their zero-shot and few-shot learn- ing capabilities reduce the dependence on annotated data, advancing automated and intelligent threat analysis (Shi et al. 2024). However, current LLMs face several challenges in practical deployment. First, lacking external knowledge retrieval, they are prone to hallucinations that deviate from factual information (Ji et al. 2023; Huang et al. 2025). Second, their generation often lacks controllability, interpretability, and stability, particularly in cybersecurity contexts with high contextual interference (Shi et al. 2023). Moreover, their understanding of threat semantics remains shallow, relying on pattern matching and struggling with polysemy, ambiguity, and subtle semantic variations, which limits deep reasoning and attack chain modeling (Srikanth et al. 2024). Retrieval-Augmented Generation. To address the above limitations, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising solution (Lewis et al. 2020). RAG introduces an external retrieval module that seman- tically fetches relevant information from knowledge bases and combines it with the input for generation. This enhances factual accuracy, contextual completeness, and output controllability. RAG has shown strong perfor- mance in knowledge-intensive tasks and is increasingly applied to cybersecurity text processing (Rajapaksha et al. 2024). Recently, Microsoft proposed GraphRAG (Edge et al. 2024), which integrates graph structures and community-based partitioning to model long-range cross-document dependencies. This advancement sup- ports APT chain reasoning, TTP extraction, and cross- source threat attribution. In summary, LLMs offer strong language understand- ing and generation capabilities, enabling structured threat intelligence representation and cybersecurity knowledge graph construction. The RAG framework fur- ther enhances factual consistency and contextual coher- ence. Accordingly, we propose a CTI entity and relation extraction approach that integrates LLM and RAG, along with a knowledge graph reasoning engine to support APT attack chain inference. LLM‑based threat intelligence extraction General-domain Knowledge Extraction with LLMs. LLMs were initially applied to general- domain knowledge extraction, demonstrating strong performance in automated KG construction on benchmark datasets. Representative works include SAC-KG (Chen et al. 2024a), which employed structure- aware prompts for triple generation; LKD-KGC (Sun et al. 2025), an unsupervised framework leveraging inter- document semantics; and iText2KG (Lairgi et al. 2024), which adopted multi-round extraction and incremental graph building. To address data scarcity, Liu et al. (2024) proposed a zero-shot self-prompting strategy for relation extraction, while ACKG-LLM (Wang et al. 2025) ensured scalability through generative capabilities without retraining. These methods have achieved notable advances in entity recognition and triple generation, supporting high-quality KG construction. However, their reliance on open-domain corpora and standardized datasets limits applicability to complex vertical domains such as cybersecurity, which features domain-specific Page 7 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 unstructured texts, terminological ambiguity, and implicit adversarial tactics. Consequently, researchers have begun leveraging LLMs for CTI extraction to advance structured representation and automated reasoning in security contexts. LLM-Based CTI Extraction. In the early stages, researchers primarily used unfine-tuned large language models for CTI extraction. Liu and Zhan (2023) constructed a CTI knowledge graph using ChatGPT and showed its advantage over traditional methods such as AttacKG (Li et al. 2022). Siracusano et al. (2023) introduced an open benchmark dataset and a structured extraction framework named aCTIon, which utilized GPT−3.5 to identify threat elements. GPT-RE (Wan et al. 2023) reformulated relation extraction as a QA task, incorporating a diversity control mechanism to address relation sparsity with gold label-induced reasoning. Fieblinger et al. (2024) explored the challenges of using vanilla LLMs (e.g., LLaMA) and KGs for extracting actionable intelligence. SEvenLLM (Ji et al. 2024) proposed a multilingual instruction-based framework for cybersecurity event analysis. Zhou et al. (2025) developed AEKG4APT, which extracted APT-related knowledge and built a targeted knowledge graph. While unfine- tuned LLMs support automated entity and relation extraction. However, they still suffer from significant limitations, such as sparse relation types, weak reasoning capabilities, and limited scalability. Fine-Tuned LLM-Based CTI Extraction. To over- come existing limitations, recent studies have explored fine-tuning strategies and prompt optimization to enhance the accuracy and robustness of CTI extraction (Chen et al. 2024b). AECR (Chen et al. 2025b) employed a fine-tuned LLM to extract attack techniques and inte- grated automatic verification mechanism to reduce hal- lucinations. LLM-TIKG (Hu et al. 2024) combined LoRA fine-tuning with large-scale models to build knowledge graphs from unstructured CTI and extract TTPs, effec- tively linking IoCs to high-level attack patterns. CTI- Bench (Alam et al. 2024) provided a benchmark for evaluating LLMs in CTI applications. LLMCloudHunter (Schwartz et al. 2025) generated candidate detection rules from textual and visual OSCTI data. In addition, LLM-based correlation techniques have been applied to attack detection (Du et al. 2024), autonomous cyber defense (Loevenich et al. 2025), incident QA (Sufi 2024), and phishing detection (Li et al. 2024b). However, these methods still struggle with deep semantic understand- ing and long-document extraction, often resulting in semantic drift, structural fragmentation, and redundant expressions. CTI Extraction Using In-Context Learning in LLMs. Existing CTI extraction methods often struggle to identify attack-level information (i.e., TTPs) due to limited contextual semantic awareness and poor handling of long-form text. The average CTI report contains about 408 words, frequently exceeding the context window of most LLMs (Xu et al. 2024). To overcome these challenges, enhanced approaches combining In-Context Learning (ICL) with semantic augmentation have been proposed. Representative methods include IntelEX, c-ICL, AttacKG+, and CTINEXUS. IntelEX (Xu et al. 2024) leveraged ICL and an LLM-based judgment module to extract TTPs from unstructured text. AttacKG+ (Zhang et al. 2025) used automated LLM- driven modeling to construct attack graphs, addressing semantic redundancy and structural fragmentation. The c-ICL framework (Mo et al. 2024) incorporated structure- aware prompts and contrastive learning to improve triple extraction quality. CTINEXUS (Cheng et al. 2025) optimized ICL for few-shot cybersecurity knowledge graph construction, supporting long-distance relation prediction in complex CTI reports. LLM-based methods have advanced TTP extraction and improved semantic structure. However, several critical challenges still persist. These include weak cross-paragraph reasoning, hallucinations, and high sensitivity to prompt design, which reduce robustness and reasoning accuracy. To address these issues, recent research has explored threat reasoning approaches based on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and graph-enhanced techniques. Enhanced Reasoning with RAG and Graph-based Techniques. The RAG paradigm has significantly improved retrieval accuracy and response generation in threat intelligence extraction. IntelEX (Xu et al. 2024) adopted GraphRAG to generate attack steps for red team simulation. APTSniffer (Xu et al. 2025), an encrypted APT traffic detection model, combined LLMs with RAG to transform raw traffic into interpretable reasoning traces. IntellBot (Arikkat et al. 2024), a security chatbot based on LLMs and RAG, supported threat intelligence and incident response. MalGTA (Guo et al. 2025b) com- bined dynamic sandbox with RAG to analyze malware tactics aligned with the ATT&CK framework, improving analytical accuracy and interpretability. Rajapaksha et al. (2024) applied a RAG-based QA model to assist in attack investigation and attribution. Jeon et al. (2024) com- pared Label Property Graph (LPG) and RDF graph mod- els for RAG-based threat tracing. DroidTTP (Arikkat et al. 2025) employed RAG and LLM fine-tuning to map Android malware behaviors with ATT&CK. Fayyazi et al. (2024) contrasted supervised fine-tuning of encoder-only LLMs with RAG-based approaches for decoder-only LLMs in threat analysis. Page 8 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 Summary and research motivation LLM-based threat intelligence extraction has made sig- nificant progress, particularly through the integration of ICL and RAG. However, most existing methods remain confined to isolated tasks (e.g., entity recognition or rela- tion extraction) and lack a unified framework for threat knowledge modeling and reasoning. Challenges such as fragmented entity alignment, limited semantic integra- tion, and the absence of modules for knowledge fusion, disambiguation, and attack chain reconstruction persist. Furthermore, their reliance on lengthy prompt interac- tions leads to high token consumption and poor scal- ability. To overcome these limitations, we propose CTI-Thinker, a system that integrates semantic enhance- ment with advanced inference capabilities. CTI-Thinker unifies entity recognition, entity alignment, relation extraction, and ATT&CK-based semantic reasoning. Leveraging in-context learning and fine-tuned LLMs, the system reduces prompt overhead and enhances extraction efficiency, demonstrating strengths in seman- tic accuracy, knowledge completeness, and reasoning interpretability. This section also presents a systematic comparison of representative CTI extraction approaches, as summa- rized in Table 1, across dimensions such as technique type, task focus, automation, reasoning ability, align- ment, contextual learning, and robustness. Our analysis indicates that traditional deep learning methods depend heavily on expert features, lack contextual modeling, and exhibit limited robustness, making them unsuitable for handling structurally fragmented and semantically ambiguous CTI texts. Although recent LLM-based meth- ods improve extraction performance, they still fall short in aligning with the ATT&CK framework and supporting attack chain reconstruction. Hallucinations and ambigu- ity remain unsolved. In contrast, CTI-Thinker offers a unified solution with strong contextual understanding, structural integration, and semantic reasoning, advanc- ing CTI extraction from shallow recognition to deeper comprehension and inference. Model design and implementation Section 3 introduces the overall framework of CTI- Thinker, followed by a detailed overview of the imple- mentation processes for each stage and its corresponding algorithms. Overview To enhance the extraction of cyber threat intelligence and the reasoning of attack behaviors, we innovatively design and implement CTI-Thinker. CTI-Thinker is an LLM-driven system for CTI extraction and reasoning that incorporates ATT&CK-based semantic mapping. It leverages in-context learning and fine-tuned LLMs to automate knowledge extraction, employs knowledge alignment to integrate threat profiles, and utilizes Gra- phRAG to enable question answering and attack scenario reasoning. As illustrated in Fig. 2, the overall architecture of CTI-Thinker comprises four key modules, which are described in detail below. Data Preprocessing. This stage aggregates multi- source CTI data, including unstructured CTI reports, APT analysis documents, and vulnerability alerts. Prompt templates are constructed using LLMs to guide the generation of structured outputs (e.g., entities, relational triples, or QA pairs). The generated results are manually reviewed and refined based on expert knowledge. This process yields high-quality, instruction-tuned annotated CTI corpora, thereby providing standardized inputs for downstream information extraction tasks. Entity and Relation Extraction. To enhance the recognition of key entities and semantic relations within CTI-related corpora, large language models (e.g., LLaMA3-8b and DeepSeek) are fine-tuned using Low- Rank Adaptation (LoRA) techniques. In addition, an in-context learning (ICL) strategy is adopted, where a K-nearest neighbor (KNN) retriever embeds current and candidate reports into a high-dimensional space and selects the top-k most similar samples to construct customized prompt templates. Then, the fine-tuned model outputs structured relational triples in the form of ⟨entity, relation, entity⟩ , covering key elements such as APT groups, attack methods, and targeted victims. These outputs serve as foundational semantic units for subsequent knowledge integration and reasoning. Knowledge Alignment and Aggregation. This stage takes entity–relation triples as input and applies lan- guage model and knowledge chunking for representa- tion learning. Using LoRA-tuned models and ICL, CTI entities and relations are coarsely grouped into seman- tically similar clusters. Vector similarity is used to align differing expressions of the same concepts. CTI-Thinker resolves ambiguity and redundancy by consolidat- ing various mentions that refer to the same cybersecu- rity entities or relationships, thereby enabling effective threat knowledge integration (as illustrated in stage (3) of Fig. 2). The system subsequently auto-generates a struc- tured CTI InfoBox encompassing key information (e.g., threat actors, geographic scope, attack events, tools, and vulnerabilities). Such output serves as the foundation for constructing a high-quality CTI knowledge graph, pro- viding semantic support for threat reasoning and query tasks. RAG-based Attack Inference. A reasoning frame- work based on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is constructed to support automated cyber threat infer- ence. Upon receiving a user query, the retriever module retrieves relevant external knowledge and graph-based information, which are subsequently used by the Gra- phRAG model to generate high-quality reasoning prompts. The fine-tuned LLM integrates structured knowledge graph content with contextual cues to pro- duce CTI inference results compliant with STIX 2.1 and ATT&CK standards. The resulting system functions as an intelligent question-answering platform for automated APT threat analysis, enabling accurate reasoning over tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs), and mapping them to corresponding ATT&CK phases. CTI-Thinker enhances situational awareness and supports strategic decision-making in cybersecurity defense operations. Page 11 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 LLM‑based threat data annotation To construct a structured and high-quality CTI knowl- edge graph, the CTI-Thinker system implements a comprehensive pipeline encompassing data collection, preprocessing, and LLM–based annotation, aiming to automate the extraction of key entities and relational information from threat intelligence corpora. The system fully accounts for the heterogeneity and diversity of cyber threat intelligence sources. Data is collected from a wide range of channels, including APT analysis reports published by major security vendors (e.g., FireEye, QiAnXin, 360, and Kaspersky), threat event and vulnerability alerts from open-source intelligence communities, as well as technical blogs and Prompt Template: You are an intelligent assistant specializing in APT threat intelligence analysis, with advanced expertise in cybersecurity, entity recognition, and relation extraction. Based on the following instructions, perform structured extraction for each input threat intelligence sentence, identifying only the specified entity and relation types, and output the results in standard JSON format. Entity Recognition Prompt: The task requires the identification of the following entity types: - (1) Attacker: The threat actor or APT group initiating the attack (e.g., Lazarus, APT28). - (2) Target: The victim organization or affected department (e.g., Sony, a government agency). - (3) Technique: Attack techniques or methods employed by the APT group (e.g., SQL injection, spear phishing, XSS). - (4) Equipment: Open-source or custom-built tools used in intrusion or exploitation (e.g., Metasploit, Gh0st). - (5) Vulnerability: The specific CVE identifier or vulnerability name exploited in the attack (e.g., CVE-2017-11882). - (6) File: Names of malicious files used in the attack (e.g., cmdl32.exe, Agent.btz). - (7) Industry: The targeted sector or industry (e.g., finance, education). - (8) Region: The geographical focus of the attack or the region/country associated with the attacker (e.g., North Korea, Russia). - (9) MalwareFamily: The malware family used in the attack (e.g., PlugX, Emotet). - (10) Indicator: Indicators of compromise (IOCs), including malicious URLs, IP addresses, file hashes, and C&C. - (11) Software: Software platforms exploited during the attack (e.g., Microsoft Word, Firefox, WinRAR, PDF Reader). - (12) ATT&CK: The technique ID from the MITRE ATT&CK framework (e.g., T1129, T1082, T1027). Relation Extraction Prompt: You are required to extract only the following relation types: - (1) Alias_of: Indicates different names for the same attacker or tool. e.g., <Darkhotel, Alias_of, APT-C-06> - (2) Related_to: Indicates a potential or uncertain relationship between two entities. e.g., <Attacker1, Related_to, Attacker2> - (3) Use: Represents a usage relationship between entities, an attacker using a tool. e.g., <Attacker1, Use, Tool/Equipment> - (4) Target_at: Denotes the target of an attack, which can be a region or industry. e.g., <Attacker1, Target_at, Financial> - (5) Exploit: Indicates that an attacker or tool exploits a specific vulnerability. e.g., <Attacker1, Exploit, CVE-2017-11882> - (6) Develop: Represents the development relationship between an attacker and a tool or malware. e.g., <Attacker1, Develop, Tool1> - (7) Located_at: Describes the geographic or organizational location of an attacker or entity. e.g., <DustSquad, Located_at, Russia> - (8) Belongs_to: Indicates that a tool, file, or malware is affiliated with a family or group. e.g., <Tool1, Belongs_to, Malware1> - (9) Indicate: Signifies that an Indicator points to a related attacker, malware, or IOC. e.g., <Indicator1, Indicate, APT28> - (10) Utilize: Indicates that an attacker uses a specific software application in cyber operations. e.g., <Attacker1, Utilize, Software1> - (11) Employ: Denotes the use of a specific ATT&CK technique by an attacker. e.g., <APT28, Employ, T1027 [Obfuscated Files or Information]> Instruction Requirements: You will receive multiple threat intelligence texts, each in the form of a sentence. For each sentence, upon successful identification of entities and their relations, output one standardized JSON object in the following format: - (1) If entities and at least one of the specified 11 relation types are identified, use: {"instruction": "Original sentence", "input": null, "output": "Entities: (Name, Type), (Name, Type)...\n Relations: (Entity1, Relation, Entity2), (Entity1, Relation, Entity2)..."} - (2) If only entities are identified but no relation exists among them, use: {"instruction": "Original sentence", "input": null, "output": "Entities: (Name, Type), (Name, Type)...\n Relations: No relevant relation"} - (3) If no relevant entities or relations are identified, use: {"instruction": "Original sentence", "input": null, "output": "No relevant entities or relations identified"} Important Notes: Only the specified 12 entity types and 11 relation types must be extracted. Each sentence must correspond to exactly one standard JSON object. The output must be structurally clear, semantically accurate, and ready for direct integration into security systems. A customized web crawler is employed to extract threat intelligence from these various sources, with extraneous elements such as advertisements removed to ensure the semantic integrity and usability of the collected content. To enhance LLM processing, raw documents are preprocessed through paragraph segmentation, content categorization, and data cleaning. Each paragraph is further split into sentence-level units suitable for LLM input. Irrelevant elements (e.g., formatting symbols and footnotes) are removed using text-cleaning techniques to ensure input consistency and readability. In the data annotation module, large language mod- els (i.e., LLaMA3-8b, DeepSeek, and ChatGPT) are employed to construct standardized prompt templates, guiding the labeling of specific entity and relation types under few-shot learning settings. As detailed in Fig. 3, twelve common entity types are defined based on the STIX 2.1 specification and APT domain characteris- tics, including Attacker, Target, Technique, Equipment, Vulnerability, Malicious File, Industry, Region, Malware Family, Indicator, Software, and ATT&CK. Additionally, eleven relation types are designed to capture seman- tic associations between entities, including Alias_of, Related_to, Use, Target_at, Exploit, Develop, Located_at, Belongs_to, Indicate, Utilize, and Employ (as detailed in Fig. 3). For each sentence, the model extracts only the entities and relations relevant to the target schema and outputs the result in a standardized JSON triple format ⟨instruction, input, output⟩ , ensuring reusability and ease of parsing. In cases where no valid entity or rela- tion is identified, a predefined annotation format is still produced to maintain output consistency across the model’s responses. Given the potential for large language models (LLMs) to produce overgeneralized or hallucinated outputs in certain scenarios, expert knowledge is incorporated to review and refine the initial annotations. This manual verification step helps enhance the overall quality of the annotated data. In addition, model fine-tuning is per- formed to progressively improve the LLM’s ability to recognize entity boundaries, contextual semantics, and domain-specific terminology within CTI corpora. These enhancements collectively provide a reliable data founda- tion for downstream knowledge graph construction and attack reasoning. In‑context learning and LLM fine‑tuning for entity and relation extraction To enhance the extraction of threat entities and their semantic relations, we design a novel knowledge extrac- tion framework that synergistically combines in-context learning with fine-tuned large language models. The overall architecture of the proposed method is depicted in Fig. 4. Split and Extract Threat-Related Sentences. Our system first automatically identifies and extracts threat- related sentences from raw CTI reports to construct a structured CTI corpus. These extracted sentences serve as the foundation for subsequent prompt template con- struction and example retrieval. In-Context Learning–Based Retrieval of CTI Semantic Samples. To enhance the generalization and data efficiency of LLMs in CTI extraction tasks, Fig. 4 The architecture for entity and relation extraction based on in-context learning and LLM Page 13 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 CTI-Thinker introduces an in-context learning (ICL) mechanism based on semantic similarity to support the structured extraction of threat entities and relations. Unlike conventional supervised learning that relies on parameter updates through backpropagation, ICL preserves model parameters and instead guides inference by constructing high-quality prompts. This approach enables the model to perform analogy-based reasoning and accurately predict structured outputs for unseen samples. CTI-Thinker embeds annotated CTI examples into a high-dimensional vector space and stores them in a demonstration vector repository. A k-nearest neighbors (KNN) retriever is then employed to identify semantically relevant examples by computing the cosine similarity between the semantic vector of the input sentence vx and each example vector in the repository, as defined in Eq. (1): CTI-Thinker selects the top-k semantically similar examples to construct a demonstration set, ordered by ascending similarity to prioritize relevant instances and mitigate confirmation bias. This strategy enables the ICL mechanism to capture input–output structural align- ment and infer latent extraction patterns through dem- onstration-based reasoning without parameter updates. Consequently, the model achieves efficient and accurate knowledge extraction with strong transferability across heterogeneous threat reports and ontologies. Construct ICL Prompt for Knowledge Extraction. We construct a standardized ICL prompt consisting of three components: (1) a task instruction that explicitly defines the target information types and output format; (2) k semantically relevant examples, each comprising a CTI input sentence paired with its annotated knowledge triples; and (3) the current input sentence to be pro- cessed. The final prompt is formulated as follows: The instruction component clearly defines the task objective, which is to extract specific types of entities and relations, as well as the required output format. Representative prompt templates are shown in Fig. 3. The example section presents standardized input– output pairs, and the query corresponds to the current threat sentence to be processed. This prompt design reformulates the complex triple extraction task into a question-answer format that aligns with the operational mode of large language models (i.e., Prompt-as-QA), (1) Sim(x, si) = cos(vx, vsi) = vx · vsi vx × vsi (2) Prompt = Instruction, Examples1:k, Query thereby significantly improving both inference efficiency and extraction quality. Fine-Tuned LLM for Entity and Relation Extraction. To enhance the model’s domain-specific generalization and semantic representation capabilities, CTI-Thinker performs instruction tuning on a pre-trained large lan- guage model (i.e., LLaMA-3-8B). Instead of relying on full-parameter fine-tuning, which demands substantial computational and memory resources, the system adopts Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). This approach freezes the original model parameters and updates only a lightweight set of trainable low-rank matrices, thereby significantly reducing training costs while improving adaptability to CTI-specific extraction tasks. Specifically, LoRA represents the update component W of the original weight matrix W0 ∈Rm×n as the product of two trainable low-rank matrices A ∈Rr×m and B ∈Rn×r , where r ≪min(m, n) . The final weight update can be formulated as follows: Our approach enables the model to retain pre-trained knowledge while effectively acquiring domain-specific representations related to CTI, including entity labels, contextual semantics, and structured triple patterns. During the fine-tuning phase, the model is trained on instruction–output pairs derived from annotated CTI corpora, learning to extract standardized knowledge tri- ples from semantically complex threat statements. Each training instance is constructed in the form of a task instruction, an input sentence, and the corresponding triple-level output. The annotated entities cover core CTI elements such as APT groups, malware families, exploi- tation tools, target industries, and CVE identifiers, along with common semantic relations including Use, Exploit, Target_at, Located_at, and Indicate. The fine-tuned LLM is capable of processing prompt templates generated by the in-context learning module, which incorporate the task instruction, semantically rel- evant examples, and the target query sentence. It then accurately produces structured knowledge units in the form of relational triples, as illustrated in Equation (4): These extracted triples serve as the fundamental seman- tic components for constructing cyber threat intelligence (CTI) knowledge graphs, supporting critical downstream applications such as threat attribution analysis, tactic– technique–procedure (TTP) reasoning, and alignment with the MITRE ATT&CK framework. This hierarchical strategy ensures semantic consistency and structural coherence across heterogeneous threat entities derived from diverse CTI sources. The overall architecture of this alignment process is illustrated in Fig. 5. LLM-Based Coarse-Grained Knowledge Grouping. CTI-Thinker extracts structured entities and relations from raw CTI documents, then applies prompt templates to instruct a fine-tuned LLM to infer semantic catego- ries. Vector-Based Fine-Grained Knowledge Alignment. On top of coarse grouping, CTI-Thinker introduces a semantic vector alignment mechanism to identify seman- tically equivalent entities with different surface forms (e.g., “APT32” and “OceanLotus”). The complete proce- dure is outlined in Algorithm 1. Each entity ei and ej is encoded into a high-dimensional semantic vector using a pre-trained and fine-tuned language model encoder. Cosine similarity is then employed to quantify the semantic closeness between the corresponding entity embeddings. Subsequently, entity alignment is performed by comparing the similarity score against a predefined threshold τ , where τ ∈[0, 1] denotes a validation-tuned similarity cutoff used to determine equivalence between entity pairs. This process enables CTI-Thinker to unify diverse entity mentions such as “Trojan” or “rundll32.exe file” into consistent nodes, facilitating semantic normalization and supporting downstream CTI knowledge graph construction, including threat attribution, TTP inference, and ATT&CK phase mapping. Algorithm 1 Knowledge alignment based on LLM and semantic vector analysis. CTI reasoning engine based on GraphRAG and ATT&CK semantic mapping We propose a CTI reasoning engine that integrates the GraphRAG architecture with semantic mapping to the MITRE ATT&CK framework, as shown in Fig. 6. Built upon the RAG paradigm, this reasoning module leverages GraphRAG to support structured knowledge retrieval and tactical behavior mapping across APT attack chains. Consequently, it facilitates automated threat reasoning and intelligent question answering within the context of CTI and APT analysis. Threat reasoning indexing stage. CTI-Thinker per- forms fine-grained semantic mapping on raw documents based on structured CTI corpora and external security knowledge sources, including the ATT&CK framework and STIX 2.1 standard. Specifically, each CTI document is segmented into semantically coherent chunks, from which the system extracts security-relevant elements (e.g., semantic entities and TTP behaviors) to construct a structured entity set. For each extracted entity, a cor- responding semantic description is generated, forming a set of entity–summary pairs. Subsequently, the system performs clustering and abstraction based on semantic embeddings and graph topological structures. Entities are grouped according to semantic similarity, where each cluster represents a coherent category of threat elements (e.g., attacker, Page 16 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 vulnerability, equipment). For each cluster, an aggregated semantic summary is generated to form a well-defined knowledge community. These communities are encoded into graph-representable node embeddings and stored in a graph index database to support downstream reasoning, enhancing the model’s understanding of domain-specific terms and contextual semantics through fact-grounded representations. Threat reasoning query stage. CTI-Thinker first encodes the user query into a dense vector representa- tion and projects it into a corresponding knowledge com- munity (e.g., APT attack stage or malware propagation network) via graph-based embedding. Then, GraphRAG identifies the most semantically relevant nodes and their adjacent entities within the graph, and constructs a con- text-aware subgraph by traversing the neighborhood structure. Unlike traditional RAG which concatenates plain text chunks, GraphRAG utilizes entity descriptions and intra-cluster summaries to generate graph-aware prompts, which are subsequently fed into the LLM for contextual reasoning and natural language generation. When a user query involves multiple communities (e.g., reasoning over a multi-stage ATT&CK chain), our system adopts a global path fusion and semantic aggre- gation mechanism to integrate reasoning paths across graph substructures. This ensures logical completeness while aligning the inference output with the TTP nodes defined in the ATT&CK framework, thereby enhancing both the interpretability and semantic adequacy of the results. As illustrated in Fig. 6, GraphRAG effectively identifies the target information of APT38, demonstrat- ing its capability to capture attacker-specific objectives. For example, in response to the query “Attack methods used by APT32 involving RemyRAT”, the system not only retrieves rel- evant vulnerabilities and tools but also outputs the ATT&CK tactical sequence such as “Remote Access Tro- jan Implantation → Persistence → Command and Con- trol.” The reasoning module supports both global and local queries, facilitating fine-grained analysis of specific behaviors or malware instances. We evaluate its performance to determine whether it outperforms state-of-the-art LLM approaches and traditional deep learning-based methods. Datasets and evaluation metrics To comprehensively evaluate the performance of CTI- Thinker in CTI entity recognition, relation extraction, and attack reasoning tasks, we construct and utilize a high-quality CTI dataset. The data sources include APT analysis reports released by leading cybersecurity ven- dors such as FireEye, McAfee, Kaspersky, Mitre, and Qihoo 360, as well as open-source CTI repositories. The final dataset comprises a representative collection of entities, including APT groups, attack tools, and vulnera- bility, along with their hierarchical relations. The detailed statistics are presented in Table 2. The dataset is split into training, validation, and test sets in an 8:1:1 ratio. Among them, the CTI-Thinker dataset is newly curated and open-sourced in this study, while LLM-TIKG (Hu et al. 2024) is a publicly available dataset characterized by diverse writing styles, document structures, and the com- plexity of real-world contextual scenarios. For evaluation metrics, this study adopts three widely used indicators in the fields of entity recognition and relation extraction: Precision, Recall, and F1-score. To mitigate randomness, all experimental results are aver- aged over ten independent runs. In terms of baseline configurations, two representative deep learning models (i.e., BiLSTM-CRF and BERT-BiLSTM-CRF) are selected as traditional baselines. Additionally, five representative fine-tuned large language models are included, namely Fig. 7 Comparison of F1-score across six representative entity types for different models Page 18 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 GPT-4o, DeepSeek-R1, LoRA-LLama3-8B, LoRA-QWen, and LoRA-Mistral-7B. To further assess performance in practical CTI scenarios, two state-of-the-art knowledge extraction systems, AttackG+ (Zhang et al. 2025) and LLM-TIKG (Hu et al. 2024), are incorporated for com- parison. All models are evaluated on the same dataset under a unified evaluation framework and server envi- ronment to ensure fairness and comparability. This com- prehensive setup enables a robust assessment of CTI Thinker’s effectiveness in CTI extraction tasks. Experimental setup In this experiment, we perform parameter-efficient fine- tuning of the LLaMA3-8B model using the LoRA tech- nique within the LLaMA-Factory framework for the cyber threat intelligence extraction task. Training is con- ducted on an NVIDIA A40 GPU with 48 GB of memory. We adopt a retrieval-augmented prompt construction strategy, where each test input is concatenated with the top-k semantically similar examples to enhance contex- tual understanding. LoRA modules are inserted into the key sub-layers of the attention mechanism and feedforward networks, enabling efficient adaptation without modifying the original model parameters. The input sequence length is set to a maximum of 1024 tokens, with a learning rate of 2e-5 and a cosine learning rate scheduling strategy. The model is trained for 10 epochs with a gradient accumulation step size of 8. Validation is performed every 50 steps, and checkpoints are saved every 100 steps. Mixed precision (FP16) training is employed to reduce memory consumption. Analysis of entity and relation extraction (RQ1 and RQ2) We conduct a comprehensive comparison of CTI entity recognition performance across representative methods, as summarized in Table 3. CTI-Thinker consistently demonstrates superior performance across all three key metrics, achieving a precision of 0.8314, recall of 0.8458, and an F1-score of 0.8385, thereby outperforming all baseline models. Compared with the traditional deep learning model Bert+BiLSTM-CRF, CTI-Thinker achieves a 9.97% improvement in F1-score. It also surpasses the knowledge-enhanced models ATTCKG+ and LLM-TIKG by 10.95% and 3.81%, respectively. Moreover, CTI-Thinker outperforms leading proprietary large models such as ChatGPT-4o and DeepSeek-R1 in terms of F1-score. When compared with state-of-the-art open-source LLMs, including lora-LLama3-8B, lora-QWen, and lora-Mistral-7B, our method maintains a consistent advantage. These results collectively demonstrate that CTI-Thinker offers a more robust and comprehensive entity recognition capability, underscoring its effectiveness in cyber threat intelligence (CTI) knowledge extraction. Furthermore, the integration of in-context learning with a LoRA-based fine-tuning strategy significantly enhances the model’s stability and generalization across diverse scenarios. ICL‑based knowledge extraction (RQ3) To further evaluate the effectiveness of in-context learning (ICL) and KNN-based example retrieval for CTI knowledge extraction, we conduct comparative experiments under three example selection strategies: (1) no-example input, (2) randomly selecting a fixed number of examples, and (3) retrieving the top-k most similar examples using a KNN-based retriever. The experimental results are summarized in Table 5. Among all configurations, the use of KNN-based retrieval with seven examples achieves the best over- all performance, yielding an F1-score of 0.7689 for triple extraction and 0.8385 for entity recognition. In com- parison to random selection, the KNN-based strategy consistently improves performance across all k values, demonstrating the effectiveness of leveraging embed- ding similarity to guide example selection. Notably, the zero-example input achieves better performance (0.6513) than randomly selecting one example (0.6310), suggest- ing that poorly chosen demonstrations may negatively impact model effectiveness. These findings confirm that both the inclusion of contextual examples and the quality of their selection are critical for improving few-shot CTI extraction performance. In summary, models equipped with KNN-based retrieval and in-context learning dem- onstrate enhanced accuracy in extracting threat entities and relations. However, increasing k is not monotoni- cally beneficial: performance peaks at k=7 and slightly declines at larger k, likely due to prompt-length con- straints and the inclusion of lower-similarity or conflict- ing demonstrations; this motivates an adaptive choice of k (e.g., thresholding on the k-th neighbor’s similarity) and diversity-aware re-ranking to balance relevance and coverage.
