Knowledge alignment with CTI‑Thinker (RQ4) This paper proposes a novel two-stage knowledge alignment strategy that combines a fine-tuned LLM with vector-based similarity computation to achieve high- quality normalization of CTI. This framework enables the effective unification of heterogeneous entities and relations from multiple sources, thereby facilitating the construction of consistent and semantically coherent threat knowledge graphs. To evaluate the performance of the proposed method, we conduct comparative experiments against three representative models: ECBERT, CTI-BERT, and ATTACK-BERT. As shown in Table 8, our method achieves an F1-score of 0.7349 for triple alignment and 0.8288 for entity alignment, outperforming all comparison models. Specifically, the proposed approach achieves an average improvement of approximately 5.81% in triple alignment and 5.50% in entity alignment over the three baseline methods. These results demonstrate its superior performance in integrating heterogeneous CTI knowledge. Overall, these findings confirm the advantages of our hierarchical alignment framework in promoting seman- tic consistency and cross-text integration. By leverag- ing the representational strength of SecureBERT and embedding-based similarity computation, the method significantly enhances the robustness and utility of CTI alignment, laying a solid foundation for constructing high-quality CTI knowledge graphs. Table 9 presents the performance of entity and relation alignment tasks at different similarity thresholds, ranging from 0.80 to 0.95. The results indicate that a threshold of 0.80 provides the best overall performance, achieving the highest F1-scores for both triple alignment (0.7718) and entity alignment (0.8383). At this threshold, the model captures a wider range of valid alignments, improving recall without sacrificing precision. We tested lower thresholds and found that 0.80 provides the best performance. Although thresholds below 0.80 showed a slight improvement in recall, further inspection revealed that entity alignment at these lower thresholds had little effect and was prone to overfitting the dataset. This overfitting leads to capturing unnecessary matches, which harms the model’s ability to generalize. Therefore, we concluded that further lowering the threshold does not provide meaningful improvements. Table 10 presents knowledge alignment examples across multiple entity and relation categories. The align- ment results demonstrate that CTI-Thinker effectively identifies synonymous expressions, linguistic variants, and abbreviation mappings. For instance, in the Attacker category, entities such as “Fancy Bear” and “APT28,” as well as “OceanLotus” and “APT32,” are correctly aligned, enhancing the normalization of heterogeneous named entities. In the Organization category, the method accu- rately aligns “MOIS” with its full form “Ministry of Intel- ligence and Security.” In the Technique category, it is also capable of handling phrasal variations, aligning terms like “SMS phishing” and “phishing.” These results confirm that the proposed two-stage alignment framework in CTI-Thinker exhibits strong capabilities in standardizing entities and relations from multi-source heterogeneous CTI texts, providing a solid foundation for constructing semantically consistent threat knowledge graphs. Fig- ure 9 further visualizes representative APT entities and their associated relations within the constructed knowl- edge graph. Threat reasoning analysis in CTI Contexts (RQ5) In this study, CTI-Thinker leverages a GraphRAG-based framework and semantic alignment with the ATT&CK knowledge base to enable intelligent question answering and TTP-level reasoning for cyber threat intelligence. We construct the experiment with the three models listed in Table 11 (CTI-Thinker, LLM-RAG, and a native LLM), and pose 30 queries covering 20 APT organizations (tar- gets, regions, tools, and TTPs). Using exact-match accu- racy after ontology/alias normalization as the metric, the averaged accuracies are 0.635, 0.615, and 0.223, respec- tively, providing a concise quantitative comparison of effectiveness and generalization. Through comprehensive comparative experiments, we validate the system’s capa- bility in understanding complex semantics and its advan- tage in ATT&CK-aligned knowledge representation. The results demonstrate that CTI-Thinker provides enhanced accuracy and interpretability in tasks such as target attri- bution, malicious tool analysis, and tactic–technique inference for threat actor profiling. Figure 10 illustrates the performance of CTI-Thinker in the target attribution task for APT38. Leveraging the GraphRAG framework, the system accurately identifies the financial sector as APT38’s primary target by rea- soning along the “APT group – target” relational path, while also revealing additional high-value sectors (e.g., telecommunications, government, and defense). Com- pared to conventional RAG and standalone LLM models, GraphRAG significantly reduces analytical overgenerali- zation and contextual mismatches by performing struc- tured retrieval over the knowledge graph and enforcing Page 24 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 Fig. 11 A comparative study of APT38’s TTPs and Malware analysis and the corresponding question answering results Page 25 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 precise semantic binding of entities. This leads to more focused, credible, and verifiable threat intelligence analy- sis, while enabling the system to provide clear and trace- able supporting evidence. Figure 11 highlights the advantages of GraphRAG in analyzing the attack techniques (TTPs) and malware associated with APT38. The CTI-Thinker system not only successfully identifies APT38-related malware (e.g., Hermes, KEYLIME, and DEYPACK), but also accurately maps them to their corresponding MITRE ATT&CK technique identifiers (e.g., T1059 for Command and Scripting Interpreter, T1566 for Phishing, T1055 for Pro- cess Injection), thereby enabling a closed-loop reasoning process across tactics, techniques, and tools. In contrast, LLMs without RAG often suffer from hallucinations and fail to correctly interpret TTP-related queries. Traditional RAG-based methods, while more informative, frequently introduce TTPs confusion due to unstructured document concatenation, resulting in overly broad descriptions that lack evidential grounding and proper alignment with the ATT&CK framework. Overall, GraphRAG ensures fac- tual consistency through entity linking, evidence binding, and contextual constraints, offering high-confidence sup- port for tactical threat analysis. Discussion In this section, we discuss some limitations of the current work as well as the future work. Limitations Limited generalization in complex attack contexts Although CTI-Thinker demonstrates strong performance in unstructured threat knowledge extraction, it still faces limitations in generalizing to complex attack contexts characterized by redundant expressions, heterogeneous data sources, and semantic ambiguity in APT reports. The model is prone to semantic drift or the generation of redundant triples, particularly in the absence of a clearly defined attack chain. These issues may lead to incorrect entity aggregation or omission, thereby affecting the completeness of the knowledge graph structure and the accuracy of downstream reasoning. Challenges in multi-turn reasoning and interpretability While CTI-Thinker integrates a GraphRAG framework to support attack chain reasoning, its ability to maintain coherent context in multi-turn reasoning scenarios (e.g., causal tracing or tactical transitions) remains unstable. The interpretability and consistency of reasoning paths require further improvement for deployment. Moreover, enhanced integration with real-world threat artifacts (e.g., malware samples and event logs) is necessary to support deeper event-level reasoning and attack behavior attribution. Future work Cross-modal threat knowledge extraction. We will further evaluate the applicability and robustness of CTI- Thinker across large-scale, multi-source, and multilingual CTI corpora, assessing its effectiveness in knowledge extraction and reasoning across diverse languages and security incident categories. Additionally, we plan to explore prompt engineering strategies and knowledge modeling mechanisms that integrate multimodal inputs (e.g., malware and log data), aiming to extend CTI- Thinker’s capabilities to cross-modal threat intelligence extraction. Enhancing attack chain interpretability via provenance graph modeling. Subsequent efforts will incorporate provenance graphs and the temporal dependencies of attack chain events to strengthen the logical relationships and sequential constraints among adversarial behaviors. This integration is expected to enhance the semantic coherence and interpretability of reasoning paths, thereby supporting more in-depth causal analysis and attribution of attack activities. Conclusion In this paper, we present CTI-Thinker, a novel sys- tem that integrates large language models (LLMs) with semantic alignment to the ATT&CK framework for constructing CTI knowledge graphs and enabling threat reasoning. CTI-Thinker leverages in-context learning (ICL) and LoRA-fine-tuned LLMs to extract threat enti- ties and relations from unstructured CTI texts. It further employs a two-stage alignment mechanism combin- ing semantic grouping and vector-based similarity to integrate heterogeneous CTI knowledge into a coher- ent graph. A GraphRAG-based reasoning engine is also introduced, supporting tactic–technique inference and threat-oriented question answering. Experimental results show that CTI-Thinker outperforms existing methods in both knowledge extraction and alignment tasks, achiev- ing F1-scores of 0.8385 for entity recognition and 0.7689 for relation extraction, surpassing deep learning base- lines, SOTA systems (e.g., ATTCKG+, LLM-TIKG), open-source large models (e.g., ChatGPT-4o, DeepSeek- R1), and fine-tuned models (e.g., LoRA-LLama3-8B, LoRA-QWen, LoRA-Mistral-7B). In the alignment task, it achieves an average improvement of 5.50% in entity alignment and 5.81% in triplet alignment over baseline models, verifying its effectiveness in integrating het- erogeneous entities and multi-source CTI expressions. Notably, CTI-Thinker aligns aliases, abbreviations, and semantic variants of APT groups, significantly enhanc- ing the consistency and standardization of the result- ing knowledge graph. Finally, CTI-Thinker supports Page 26 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 Table 12 Example of quantitative evaluation for CTI reasoning models Model Score Reasoning Answer CTI-Thinker 0.95 The answer correctly identifies that the Gamaredon Group mainly targets “Ukrainian government and defense-related organizations,” which matches both the question and the attribution described in the MITRE ATT&CK knowledge base. The response is precise and well articulated. Although it does not cite specific malware families or exploitation tools, its content remains aligned with the documented threat behavior in ATT&CK, thus meriting a high score Gamaredon Group is a threat group that primarily targets individuals involved in the Ukrainian government and organizations related to Ukraine’s defense and military sectors LLM-RAG​ 0.50 While the answer mentions the technology industry, Gamaredon Group does not pri- marily target the technology sector. According to the correct answer, Gamaredon Group mainly targets Ukrainian government, defense, and military sectors, which makes the response inaccurate. Though the mentioned tools and methods are relevant, the answer is not aligned with the focus on government and military sectors. Therefore, a lower score is given According to Analyst 1, Gamaredon Group primarily targets the technology industry, specifically Microsoft Office and Outlook, as indicated by their use of various tools and techniques to inject malware, steal data, and deliver malicious code Native LLM 0.20 This answer discusses content unrelated to the actual activities of Gamaredon Group. It describes an investment firm’s focus areas, not a cyber espionage group’s attack targets. Therefore, it does not align with the question requirements, resulting in a low score Gamaredon Group is a private investment firm that targets various industries, but it appears to have a focus on the following sectors: 1.Technology: Gamaredon Group invests in technology companies, particularly those with a focus on software, cyber- security, data analytics, and artificial intelligence. 2.Energy: Gamaredon Group has invested in energy companies, including those focused on renewable energy, oil and gas, and energy services. 3.Industrial: The firm has invested in industrial compa- nies, including those in the manufacturing, logistics, and supply chain management sectors. Gamaredon Group’s investment strategy is focused on identifying companies with strong growth potential, innovative products or services, and a competitive edge in their respective markets. The firm’s investment team works closely with portfolio companies to drive growth, improve operations, and create long-term value Page 27 of 29 Yang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:106 intelligent threat-oriented question answering and accu- rately identifies techniques, tactics, and common attack procedures associated with specific APT groups. In conclusion, CTI-Thinker offers an accurate, robust, and comprehensive solution for constructing high-quality, queryable, and inferable CTI knowledge graphs. Appendix This appendix introduces the scoring system used in this study to evaluate cyber threat intelligence reasoning models. To comprehensively assess the models’ perfor- mance in attack reasoning tasks, we designed three scor- ing dimensions: accuracy, completeness, and relevance. These are explained as follows: • Accuracy. This metric evaluates whether the information provided in the answer is accurate and consistent with established facts, data, or background knowledge. • Completeness. This metric assesses whether the answer comprehensively covers all relevant aspects of the question, ensuring that no key information is omitted. • Relevance. This metric measures whether the answer directly addresses the question and remains focused on its core content. Additionally, we have defined detailed scoring criteria to clearly demonstrate how the answers are evaluated. • Irrelevant or Incorrect (0 points). The answer is unrelated to the question or contains serious errors, making it unable to answer the question or com- pletely incorrect. • Partially Correct with Confusion (0.5 points). The answer contains some correct information but also includes inaccuracies or confusion. • Completely Accurate and Detailed (1 point). The answer accurately and clearly addresses the question, lists relevant information in detail, and provides suf- ficient background explanation. Table 12 presents the answers from three models: CTI- Thinker, LLM-RAG, and Native LLM, to the question “What industries does Gamaredon Group primarily tar- get?” The focus is on evaluating each model’s ability to correctly identify the target industries of the Gamaredon Group. Among them, CTI-Thinker performs the best, as its response accurately reflects the known targets of the Gamaredon Group and aligns closely with trusted sources such as the MITRE ATT&CK framework. Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 https://doi.org/10.1007/s44163-026-00902-3 *Correspondence: Eljas Linna eljas.linna@tuni.fi 1Tampere University, Tampere, Finland 2University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland Challenges for generative AI in legal reasoning Eljas Linna1* and Tuula Linna2 1 Introduction Large Language Models (LLMs), machine learning systems that produce remarkably human-like responses, are rapidly being integrated into a wide array of professional domains [1]. When assessed externally, these anthropomorphic artificial intelligence (AI) tools can appear to discuss, explain, and reason like human experts. This capability is fascinating, useful, and entertaining, but also fraught with risk when used carelessly. Shortly after the launch of the original ChatGPT tool in November 2022, embarrass­ ing examples emerged of advocates who had used artificially manufactured legal cita­ tions [2], most famously in the case of Mata v. Avianca, Inc. [3]. Today, we understand Discover Artificial Intelligence Abstract Large Language Models (LLMs) are being integrated into professional domains, yet their limitations in such high-stakes fields as law remain poorly understood. In response, this paper introduces examples of critical challenges to the functioning of generative and other forms of artificial intelligence (AI) as reliable reasoning tools in judicial decision-making. The study deconstructs core requirements and challenges for AI, including the ability to select the correct legal framework across jurisdictions, generate sound arguments based on the doctrine of the sources of law, distinguish ratio decidendi and obiter dicta in case law, resolve ambiguity arising from general clauses like “reasonableness”, manage conflicting legal provisions, and apply the burden of proof correctly. The paper then maps various AI enhancement mechanisms, such as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), multi-agent systems and neuro-symbolic AI, to these challenges, assessing their potential to bridge the gap between the probabilistic nature of LLMs and the rigorous, choice-driven demands of legal interpretation. Furthermore, the paper sketches a path towards an evaluation framework, proposing that legal requirements be organized into normative, doctrinal, evidential, and technical categories, and subsequently operationalized into domain- specific, testable design obligations. The findings indicate that these techniques can address specific narrow challenges, but they fail to solve the more significant ones that remain, particularly in tasks requiring discretion and transparent, justifiable reasoning. Therefore, we advocate for a staged adoption, first capturing efficiency in simple cases with technology already available today and thereafter sustaining long-term investment in new methods that handle hierarchy, temporality, and other requirements of legally sound reasoning, thus enabling expansion to complex adjudication in the future. Page 2 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 well that LLMs have severe limitations in their ability to reason and produce truthful responses. What remains less well understood, however, is where the boundaries of these limitations lie across domains. This paper is intended primarily for researchers working at the intersection of AI and law, as it presents examples of challenging scenarios in judicial decision-making on a general, yet identifiable, level. Here, the standards for reasoning are not abstract ideals or judicial theories but concrete requirements for determining people’s rights and obliga­ tions. Concerning AI in the legal context, it is useful to distinguish, at least, the follow­ ing five aspects: (1) the regulation of AI, (2) AI-assisted research in legal theory, (3) AI’s impact on access to justice, (4) AI-supported legislative drafting, and (5) AI-assisted or automated legal decision-making. First, the regulation of AI encompasses many important legal questions, such as copy­ right and privacy. Legal and ethical considerations have recently been raised about the methods of acquiring the training data for AI models, and about the application of results generated by these systems [4]. For example, The New York Times has already sued OpenAI and Microsoft for copyright infringement [5], in response to which inter­ national bodies and national administrations are taking action: the European Union (EU) has passed Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 [6], the first binding AI regulation in the bloc, while in the United States, action plans and executive orders have been issued to steer AI development [7]. As such, this paper assumes such legal problems will be regulated in the coming years and thus omits them from the herein discussion. The second aspect, legal theory and deontic logic, represents an abstract normative sci­ ence that studies the structure of normative systems and notions, such as “permissible”, “obligatory”, “optional”, “must” and their counterparts. Symbolic AI has also shown some promise when used in the context of such theoretical patterns [8], and novel approaches have been presented to using LLMs in the translation of legal texts into formal deontic logic representations [9]. Third, AI has a significant impact on access to justice. LLMs themselves are unlikely to create a competitive advantage among law firms, as all companies have access to the same leading commercial LLMs and open-source models. However, large law firms may gain an edge with proprietary data, especially in arbitration proceedings, where the datasets are not public. A vast proprietary knowledge base allows practitioners to utilize such techniques as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to ground LLM responses using examples and reference cases, leading to higher-quality responses from LLMs and thus higher-quality outputs. Conversely, AI can dramatically diminish the cost of legal aid; it has been noted that in high-volume litigation, a complaint response system reduced associate time from 16 h to 3–4 min, and productivity gains of greater than 100- fold have been observed [10]. One emergent area of AI is in legislative drafting, the fourth aspect. Because AI can streamline and optimize law-drafting processes, a cyclical interaction is enabled between LLMs and human legislative drafters, deepening the practitioners’ understanding of the legal material and enhancing transparency in the law-drafting process. The EU has already started assessing the incorporation of AI into legislation editing software [11]. This paper focuses exclusively on the challenges of applying AI in relation to the fifth aspect, AI-assisted or automated legal decision-making, while contract interpretation is left aside, as there are special requirements compared to the interpretation of statutes Page 3 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 and case law [12]. Our aim is not to test how reliably different LLM-based systems per­ form a given judicial task but rather to discuss the challenges that an AI system must overcome to succeed in the nuanced work of legal argumentation. The core question is what pitfalls AI must avoid and what new capabilities must still be developed for AI to serve as a reliable reasoning machine in the legal domain. To explore this question, this paper focuses on the process of legal reasoning from the standpoint of an impartial judge adjudicating according to the law. We use the Issue– Rule–Application–Conclusion (IRAC) model [13, 14] as our structuring device to avoid a mere list of challenges and to organize the discussion in a more coherent way. Thus, the IRAC model is not used as a theoretical model of legal reasoning or to depict how judges behave in real-life adjudication. It is merely a presentation technique to group some of the main challenges judges face in adjudication under different headings and to discuss the core questions of this paper. The paper is structured as follows. Section 2 establishes a foundational framework for legal reasoning, outlining some of the core principles that typically govern judicial deci­ sion-making. Section 3 introduces some of the most challenging points in judicial argu­ mentation for AI. Section 4 then maps the various AI enhancement mechanisms such as RAG, multi-agent systems and neuro-symbolic AI to these challenges, assessing their potential to meet the identified challenges. Section 5 outlines the steps towards a new framework for judicial AI, proposing a methodology to classify requirements into nor­ mative, doctrinal, evidential, and technical categories and to operationalize them as test­ able design obligations. Finally, Sect. 6 discusses the current state of AI in law, suggesting its most effective role today and looking towards a future in which human expertise and AI might work in symbiosis to uphold the rule of law. 2 The landscape of legal reasoning: a foundational framework Before examining the specific challenges facing AI in the legal domain, it is essential to establish a foundational understanding of the requirements of the rule of law and a fair trial. We emphasize that the requirements presented below and the challenges men­ tioned in the next section are largely common, regardless of how legal argumentation is theoretically or practically constructed (e.g., as a logical, inductive or deductive syllo­ gism, or construed according to, for example, moral-based Dworkinian interpretivism). For example, unclear wording of the law is a universal legal challenge. At its heart, the judicial process is bound by a set of imperatives designed to ensure fairness and uphold the rule of law, and this set includes the principles of impartiality, requiring decisions be made without bias; legal certainty, ensuring outcomes are pre­ dictable and consistent; and transparency, demanding the reasoning behind a judgment be accessible and understandable. Crucially, every step in the legal decision-making process must be justifiable and controllable, allowing the parties to a dispute via appel­ late courts and the public to scrutinize and validate the outcome. These principles are not mere aspirations; they are functional requirements of a legitimate legal system. An important example of practical requirements is procedural economy, i.e., how to imple­ ment the rule of law in legal proceedings cost-effectively. Here, as noted above, AI has the potential to save time and to speed up processes, but it may also have the opposite effect due to manual double-checking or correction of errors. The IRAC model breaks down legal reasoning into four distinct phases: Page 4 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 • Issue (I) Determining the core legal questions raised by the facts of a case. • Rule (R) Identifying the relevant legal rules and case law that govern the issue. • Application (A) Analyzing how the identified rules apply to the specific facts of the case. This is the heart of legal interpretation, where arguments are weighed and conclusions are drawn. • Conclusion (C) Stating the outcome of the analysis and the resolution of the legal issue. As noted, this paper uses the IRAC model as a technical framework for the discussion. It is not a depiction of real-world decision-making or an ideal model for high-quality legal argumentation. Rather, real legal argumentation is far more complicated than the IRAC model depicts. For example, IRAC lacks a dedicated letter for identifying relevant evidence and the specific facts of the case. When the letter F (Facts) is missing, the role of the relevant facts is discussed in the section concerning the application of rules (A). While AI can be a powerful tool in the initial “Issue” discovery phase, its greatest chal­ lenges and potential lie in the subsequent stages. Judges have a legal obligation to deliver a judgment; a judge cannot say “non liquet” (it is not clear), even when facing such sig­ nificant challenges such as a gap in the law. Therefore, the two demanding phases of legal reasoning are the selection of the correct Rule (R) and the nuanced Application (A) of said rule to the facts. Here, in the interpretation and application of law, the highest stan­ dards of judicial reasoning are required. Finally, alongside the interpretation of legal norms, the equally critical task of evidence evaluation forms another pillar of the judicial process. Here, we refer to the missing let­ ter F in the IRAC model. A significant part of any trial involves assessing evidence— witness testimonies, documents, and forensic data—to establish a factual record. This process presents a distinct set of challenges for AI. Evidence evaluation is difficult to improve with techniques like RAG because legal evidence pertains to the truthfulness of everyday facts; fine-tuning an LLM with legal corpora does not help it answer whether a certain person was in a certain place at a certain time. As such, LLMs struggle to evalu­ ate the truthfulness of real-world facts [15], a task often reliant on human experience and credibility assessments. Furthermore, legal systems can employ complex and often unwritten burden of proof rules to resolve cases in which evidence is insufficient. Thus, an AI system that attempts to decide a case based on weak evidence without correctly applying these rules would inadvertently bypass a cornerstone of procedural justice. 3 Prominent examples of complex legal scenarios The selection of rules, their application to facts, and the evaluation of evidence are not simple procedural steps but a series of complex legal choices that require deep domain knowledge, nuanced interpretation, and the ability to navigate ambiguity. This section discusses five core judicial challenges that AI systems must overcome to function reli­ ably in legal decision-making. The purpose of this section is, on a general level, to map the challenges and pitfalls that are difficult to recognize when developing AI solutions in the legal domain. In different legal systems, these challenges manifest in more nuanced and concrete ways. Nonetheless, we believe it is important to map the challenges to ensure attention is paid to potential legal pitfalls to avoid a miscarriage of justice when relying on the help of AI in the legal domain. Page 5 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 3.1 Selecting the correct legal framework Before any legal question can be answered, the correct body of law must be identified. However, this initial selection process is fraught with complexities, particularly in cases with cross-border implications or intricate national legal systems. 3.1.1 Navigating international and cross-border jurisdictions In an increasingly globalized world, legal disputes often transcend national borders. Similarly, AI must first be able to distinguish between the jurisdiction (which court has the authority to hear a case) and the applicable law (which country’s laws govern the dispute). This distinction is a foundational concept, as the former (jurisdiction) is a part of procedural law, while the latter (applicable law) belongs to private international law. Traditionally, these two inquiries are treated as separate and sequential stages: a court must first determine whether it has the authority to adjudicate the matter before it pro­ ceeds to the second stage of deciding which substantive law to apply. This separation is particularly pronounced in legal systems that employ rigid, non-discretionary rules for each jurisdiction, such as those within the EU, where a court, once its jurisdiction is established, generally has no discretionary power to decline it on the grounds that a foreign court might be more appropriate (forum non conveniens) [16]. Correctly distin­ guishing the two sides requires the proper application of international instruments, such as the EU’s Regulation (EU) 1215/2012 (Brussels I Recast) on jurisdiction and Regulation (EC) 593/2008 (Rome I) on applicable law. In addition, when determining the jurisdiction of the court, AI must identify whether a case falls under a uniform procedure under EU law or some other supranational regula­ tion where the procedure is the same in all countries in question, as well as whether the procedure is optional. Notable examples are the European Order for Payment Procedure (EC) 1896/2006 and the European Small Claims Procedure (EU) 2015/2421. These regu­ lations are optional and have their own jurisdictional provisions, giving national laws the power to regulate which court is competent. For example, in Finland, both procedures fall under the exclusive jurisdiction of the Helsinki District Court. Thus, in an EU country, AI must first decide whether the claim is of a national or inter­ national nature. If it is international, the AI system must decide whether EU regulation is applicable or whether the competent court is situated in a third country. If EU law applies, AI must choose between the Brussels I Regulation and the above-mentioned special EU procedures and then choose the competent court, while also considering that, for example, some provisions of the Brussels I Regulation and the special EU proce­ dures allow national laws to regulate the competent court within that country. If there is a third country in question, AI must be able to apply the often open-textured and discre­ tionary rules concerning connecting factors. Furthermore, AI must recognize the fundamental differences between the two main legal traditions of common law and continental systems. This paper does not compare these systems but instead focuses on the challenges that solutions depending on AI must resolve before automation can be possible. Within the common law framework, in addi­ tion to written law, AI must respect the binding effect of precedent under the doctrine of stare decisis. Moreover, AI must recognize how strong and strict the stare decisis doc­ trine is in that country. In this regard, among others, the following questions must be considered: Page 6 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 • whether all Supreme Court rulings bind all lower courts • whether decisions of other appeal courts bind district courts • whether stare decisis binds across court lines (general/administrative/family, etc.) • whether the Supreme Court is itself bound by its own precedents • what is the relationship between the highest state and federal courts? It is also worth noting that common law systems recognize various doctrines of non- binding or persuasive precedents [17]. While there is no formal rule of binding prec­ edent in continental systems, many times, a consistent line of judicial decisions (jurisprudence constante) from superior courts carries highly persuasive weight and is a crucial source for interpretation [18]. Thus, AI must also find relevant precedents in continental systems. In some domains, like international arbitration, it may even need to reason from non- codified sources, such as established trade practices (lex mercatoria), or based on fair­ ness (ex aequo et bono). In these cases, the challenges facing AI are especially prominent. While knowledge retrieval systems can find the relevant regulations, AI systems may struggle to interpret the connecting factors that determine jurisdiction, such as a defen­ dant’s habitual residence or where the damage occurred, which normally allow for judi­ cial discretion and context-sensitive application [19]. A multi-agent system could break the problem into sub-tasks (agent 1 finds jurisdiction, agent 2 finds applicable law, agent 3 reviews, etc.), but this still relies on the agents’ ability to interpret the often ambiguous and complex web of logical dependencies correctly. 3.1.2 Respecting national legal hierarchies and rules Even within a single country’s legal system, choosing the right provision is a multi-lay­ ered task. An AI system must thus be able to navigate several critical dimensions: • Hierarchy It must respect the hierarchical system of legal norms, prioritizing international treaties and constitutional law over ordinary statutes and decrees. Legal systems are structured with a clear hierarchy of norms, where rules from a higher source prevail over those from a lower source. An AI system must identify, for example, the relationship between international treaties and constitutions, as well as the relationship between federal and state laws. It must also recognize subordinate legislation, such as decrees and regulations. In the EU context, this includes understanding the difference between the direct applicability of EU Regulations and, conversely, the interpretative effect of EU Directives on national legislation. • Time AI must correctly handle the time dimension. Because laws are frequently amended, the version of the law that was in force when the events in question occurred must be applied. This concept, known as the temporal scope of law, is fundamental to legal certainty and its general principle is that of non-retroactivity. This requires checking for transitional provisions that may suspend or modify the application of new laws and correctly identifying whether a law applies retroactively. Non-retroactivity is especially important in criminal cases (nullum crimen sine lege), meaning an act can only be an offense if it was defined as such by law at the time of the offense. Transitional provisions can be complex when they bring some provisions into force immediately after the law has been ratified and the rest with different lengths of transition periods. Page 7 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 • Specificity AI must correctly apply the general maxim of lex specialis derogat legi generali, whereby a special law (e.g., a consumer protection act) supersedes a more general one (e.g., a general trade act). This principle is not used to solve contradictions but is applied to ensure the rule tailored to the specific situation is the one that governs it. This is not a simple keyword match; it requires the AI system to first correctly identify the legal status of the parties involved (e.g., consumer, employee) to determine whether a special law is applicable. The choice is simple when there is a single decisive factor, such as whether the plaintiff or defendant is in a particular position (such as a “consumer”) and the laws in question are completely exclusive of each other. However, the relationship between general law and special law can be considerably more complex. Legislators tend to “conserve regulatory text” by constructing the relationships between laws in such a way that some of the provisions of the general law are applied either as such or with certain exceptions or modifications in special legislation. The same special law may refer to several general laws or to another special law. The landscape of the relationships between laws is therefore quite layered and difficult for AI to perceive. Moreover, reference provisions with the proviso “where/as applicable” (mutatis mutandis) introduce their own nuances. These kinds of reservations give the applicant more leeway than a direct reference without any reservations. It remains to be seen how AI can account for such nuances. • Procedural choice Within a single statute, an AI system must be able to choose the correct internal proceeding. For example, in the EU, some national insolvency acts contain preventive restructuring frameworks and discharge of debt (see EU Directive 2019/1023). Some insolvency laws also cover liquidation, whether as a going concern or via piecemeal sale. The U.S. Bankruptcy Code (Title 11 of the United States Code) is an example of this kind of multi-purpose insolvency act. Moreover, in the United Kingdom and in some other common law countries, there are company law-based procedures called "schemes of arrangement", where an AI system must be able to select the appropriate procedure based on the goals of the laws or different purposes of the chapters of the same law, such as rescuing a viable business or implementing an efficient sale of the estate. This type of choice is not only judicial; it also requires an analysis of the condition of the business in question. The above-mentioned examples present a systemic reasoning challenge. An AI system must simultaneously consider multiple constraints, a task that is difficult to implement with simple prompts. For instance, determining the applicable version of a law (time dimension) while also checking for a superseding special law (specificity) and its place in the legal hierarchy requires a complex dependency graph. A neuro-symbolic AI sys­ tem, which combines LLMs with rule-based systems, could perhaps model hierarchies of statutes, but defining all the rules and their internal relations is a monumental under­ taking [8]. 3.2 Generating sound and defensible legal arguments 3.2.1 Interpretative maxims Once the correct legal text is identified, the core task of interpretation begins. This is not a matter of deductive logic but of constructing a persuasive argument grounded in accepted sources of law, generally accepted legal principles and the rule of law. Thus, Page 8 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 traditional symbolic AI is unable to perform interpretation tasks. That said, there are certain traditional and quite universal unwritten interpretation maxims [20], one of which is exceptio est strictissimae applicationis (exceptions must be narrowly inter­ preted). However, if the purpose of the exception is to protect, for example, a weaker party, this maxim can be questioned. Another maxim is lex specialis derogat legi gen­ erali, the application of which, as we discuss a bit later, can be quite challenging. A third example of interpretative maxims is lex posterior derogat legi priori, the idea being that newer rules indicate the normative system in force, even when an older piece of law has not been repealed. Nevertheless, this is true only when the scopes of the application of the laws are identical. Furthermore, the maxim maior continet in se minus (that is, the greater includes the lesser) means that if someone has the right to do something, they also have the right to do a smaller part of it. Finally, expressio unius est exclusio alterius means that if the legislator has listed the items, circumstances or facts exhaustively, an e contrario conclusion must be drawn if the thing in question is not listed. Even so, some­ times it can be unclear whether the list is meant to be exhaustive. The aforementioned interpretative maxims can be helpful when integrated into LLM prompting and agent architecture, but these maxims alone are insufficient tools in legal adjudication. More­ over, one must remember that the mentioned maxims may have various nuances and strengths in different legal systems and traditions. 3.2.2 Adhering to the doctrine of the sources of law Legal argumentation follows a generally accepted, though sometimes flexible hierarchy of sources [21]. A reliable AI must thus be able to construct its reasoning by starting with the literal wording of the provision and interpreting it in light of the legislator’s purpose. Usually, the judicial task is to ascertain and act upon the “true intention of the legislature” [22]; however, this is a potential pitfall, as the AI system must correctly source this purpose from preparatory works (e.g., government bills) and, crucially, verify whether Parliament amended the final law. If so, the reasoning in a parliamentary com­ mittee report supersedes the original preamble. AI must then integrate relevant case law and non-binding sources, such as the legal lit­ erature, according to their authoritative weight as legal sources in that jurisdiction. This entire process must be conducted while respecting overarching legal doctrines, such as the principle of legality and the rule of lenity (in dubio mitius) in criminal law. This is a problem of synthesis and validation, as an AI system must not only retrieve preparatory works but also verify whether the final law was amended, a step requiring a comparison of multiple documents and an understanding of the implications of any changes. This task demands more than simple information retrieval; it requires a structured compari­ son and logical inferencing about the significance of textual differences between legisla­ tive drafts, a higher-order reasoning capability that current LLMs often lack, leading to the risk of overlooking critical changes or hallucinating their importance [23]. 3.2.3 Interpreting case law Using precedents requires more than simply finding cases with similar keywords. AI must be able to perform a sophisticated analysis of case law by distinguishing the ratio decidendi (the core, binding legal rule of a judgment) from obiter dicta (incidental remarks that are not binding), a particularly difficult task, as the original court does not Page 9 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 explicitly label them. As described above, AI must thus identify which precedents have a binding effect and which are non-binding case law relevant for adjudication. Furthermore, AI systems must be able to conduct a legally relevant comparison of the facts between a precedent and the current case, but a precedent is binding only if the cases are legally similar in terms of the key facts or are at least sufficiently analogous on legally significant points, a form of analogical reasoning influenced by societal val­ ues that goes far beyond matching semantic similarities via RAG. Legal analogy is not a mechanical process of counting factual similarities but an evaluative judgment about whether the facts of two cases are comparable in legally relevant ways. This determina­ tion of relevance requires the identification of an underlying normative principle that connects the two cases, a step that moves from the specific facts to an abstract rule. This is one of the most difficult tasks for AI because ratio decidendi is an abstract legal con­ cept, not an explicit piece of text. Current AI systems, which excel at identifying seman­ tic patterns in text, struggle to make the independent, evaluative judgments needed to formulate or apply these normative principles [24]. 3.3 Resolving ambiguity and incompleteness Law is not always clear or comprehensive. Therefore, a key judicial function is to resolve ambiguity and fill gaps in the law, tasks that demand reasoning beyond a formal applica­ tion of rules. 3.3.1 Adjudicating with general clauses Legislators often employ open-textured terms like “reasonable”, “fair”, or “unconscio­ nable” to delegate discretion to the courts, allowing flexible, case-by-case adjudication. These provisions, known as general clauses or open rules, are the legal rules deliberately formulated in an imprecise manner. They empower judges to decide cases based on broad principles, such as good faith, fairness, or public policy, when the strict applica­ tion of a specific rule would lead to an unjust result–or when a specific rule does not exist. To apply such general clauses, AI cannot be a mere legal formalist that rigidly follows rules while disregarding non-legal factors. An understanding of social norms, ethics, and common sense is required, knowledge that is not explicitly codified. These general clauses are especially challenging for LLMs and cannot be solved simply with better information retrieval, as there is no reference material for general clauses in every con­ text, nor by agentic systems, because the definition of the open-textured terminology cannot be broken down into well-defined factors. 3.3.2 Addressing conflicting provisions and gaps in the law In cases of a lacuna in law (a gap where no specific rule exists in written law or binding case law), AI must be able to reason via analogies from related provisions or to derive a solution from the general principles that underpin that area of law. Instead of analogy, in some cases, the court can dismiss the claim or petition if there is no provision avail­ able. This outcome may be justified especially when it comes to whether an obligation should be imposed on the defendant/respondent or whether the plaintiff has a certain right regarding the defendant/respondent. An obligation that is not grounded on a clear Page 10 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 rule may hamper justice, legal certainty, and the protection of legitimate expectations and trust. Adjudicating without the support of a rule requires a deep, systemic understanding of the entire legal framework. While an LLM can process vast amounts of text, it struggles to track the dependencies and interactions across many factors, such as laws and prin­ ciples simultaneously [25], a critical point of failure for AI. 3.4 Establishing the facts and performing reliable evidence evaluation Distinct from interpreting legal norms is the foundational task of establishing the facts of the case, which are crucial, even during the initial identification of the issue (I) that needs to be resolved. Without preliminary knowledge of the relevant facts of the case, it is not possible to determine the applicable law either. The parties may disagree about what happened and how the matter should be assessed legally. Sometimes, the issues of evidence and law may be intertwined, where the issue of law determines what evidence is relevant, or vice versa, so that before the issue of law can be determined, the disputed facts must be clarified. During the court proceedings, evidence presented can lead to a situation where the applicable rules change. Thus, there is a continuous interaction between the facts and rules that can be difficult for AI to follow. Reliability and accuracy are of utmost importance in evidence evaluation, as count­ less other procedures in law depend on the facts of the case. Even though LLMs have shown promising early results in evaluating evidence, considerable challenges remain unresolved regarding reliability and explainability [26]. 3.4.1 Assessing factual truthfulness The ability to reason about law is meaningless if one cannot ground this reasoning in a correct understanding of the facts. LLMs face a fundamental challenge in evaluating the truthfulness of information, such as evidence [27], as their knowledge is derived from training data and provided context, not from lived experience or an innate sense of real-world plausibility. For example, whether a specific witness is credible or whether a sequence of events described in a document is physically possible often cannot be inferred from the textual case documents alone. Assessing witness credibility involves evaluating non-verbal cues, demeanor, consistency, and potential biases, factors that extend far beyond the analysis of a written transcript. While AI tools are being devel­ oped to assist lawyers in witness preparation by analyzing speech patterns, emotional tone, and even micro-expressions, these are assistive technologies incapable of making definitive judgments about deception or truthfulness [28]. Evaluating real-world plau­ sibility therefore requires common-sense reasoning, an area in which LLMs continue to show significant limitations, as they lack the embodied experience and intuitive under­ standing of the physical and social world that humans possess [29]. Evidence evaluation involves piecing together fragments of information with no authoritative source of truth available for the LLM’s reference, increasing the risk of hallucinations [30]. 3.4.2 Applying the burden of proof When the evidence presented is insufficient or contradictory, a case is not left undecided (non liquet). Instead, it is adjudicated according to the–often unwritten–burden of proof rules, which prescribe which party bears the negative consequences of an unproven fact. Page 11 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 The burden of proof is a general concept widely shared across most legal systems for resolving uncertainty when the facts of the case cannot be definitively established. The details, however, vary significantly by jurisdiction and by the type of law (civil, criminal, administrative law, and specialized fields like tax law). In some systems, for example, the "burden of persuasion" and the "burden of production" are separated. A general starting point is that each party has the burden of proving their claims, although there are excep­ tions concerning, for example, weak parties, such as consumers. In addition, courts can consider the difficulty of proving a negative fact. In this context, an AI system must be able to navigate these critical rules in civil cases. Moreover, in criminal cases, it must understand the high standard of "beyond a reason­ able doubt", or a similar concept indicating a high threshold, and acquit if the prosecu­ tion fails to meet it. An AI system that attempts to render a decision based on insufficient evidence, rather than correctly identifying the issue as “unproven” and applying burden of proof rules, would fundamentally undermine procedural justice. The legal concept of the burden of proof is, at its core, a sophisticated mechanism for managing epistemic uncertainty. To apply it correctly, a decision-maker must first be able to assess its own level of certainty and recognize when the evidence is insufficient to meet the required threshold. This points to a critical technical limitation of current models, as LLMs are notoriously poor at this form of self-assessment. Their confidence scores are often mis­ aligned with their actual accuracy, a problem known as poor calibration, and models fre­ quently exhibit overconfidence, expressing high certainty in answers that are factually incorrect [31]. 3.5 Upholding procedural fairness Finally, the legitimacy of a judicial decision rests not only on the quality of its reasoning but also on the fairness of the process used to reach it. The concept of a fair trial is multi­ faceted and ambiguous; usually, it includes the right to be heard and the opportunity for the parties to otherwise present their case; judges’ impartiality and integrity, combined with the rules of judicial disqualification; and the rules regarding timely notification. In a case of procedural or material misjudgment, the right to challenge the judgment is an important judicial safeguard, although it is not an absolute right in all systems. A cornerstone of a fair trial is the principle that both parties must have an opportunity to be heard and to contest the evidence and arguments presented by the other party. It is not enough to calculate how much time each party has had the opportunity to pres­ ent claims, defense or evidence. Rather, what is important is that there is a fair balance between the parties’ opportunities to appear and provide input. Important safeguards are that the parties are represented by professional lawyers and that the judge’s role in directing and managing the progress of a case is fair and sufficient. The essential questions concerning procedural fairness are: • whether the use of AI to manage the process like a judge hampers the fair trial requirement • whether AI can enable the discovery of any errors or deficiencies concerning procedural fairness • how to prevent parties knowledgeable about AI from gaining an unfair advantage in AI-powered processes over those with less understanding. Page 12 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 A judge’s case management proceeds according to an evolving determination of rel­ evance and can be quite passive in cases subject to party disposition (i.e., dispositive matters where the parties may settle the case). There is no predetermined path to be followed throughout the civil or criminal procedure, but the judge must be able to react according to the events and developments during the proceedings. However, it is not excluded that AI can manage, for example, repetitive small claims proceedings using digital forms for submitting the claims and objections of the parties. In such proceed­ ings, the parties could be allowed to decide for themselves whether they have had a suf­ ficient opportunity to present their case. An important legal safeguard is the possibility to appeal to a human judge. Another issue is whether AI can monitor procedural fairness, for example, when a party wants to challenge the adjudication and appeal to a higher court. Discovering these kinds of matters requires that the procedure be transparent. Many of these challenges are exemplified by prompt engineering, a standard proce­ dure in the use of generative AI systems and a critical skill where subtle changes in word­ ing, context, or structure can lead to vastly different outcomes [32]. With the exact same prompt, different models produce different outcomes depending on their training data [33]. In an adversarial system, these choices would be subject to intense scrutiny. The principle of procedural fairness dictates that if an AI’s output is to be used as evidence or to support a judicial decision, the process that generated that output must be trans­ parent and open to challenge. The specific prompt used to generate a legal analysis is no longer just a query; it becomes a piece of discoverable evidence, and the choice of a par­ ticular AI model is a methodological decision comparable to an expert witness selecting a specific scientific instrument for an analysis. This necessitates a new level of transpar­ ency, extending beyond the final output to the entire generative process. A reliable and fair AI-assisted process must therefore be transparent enough to allow for such adver­ sarial probing, ensuring that its methodological choices can be justified and challenged. 4 AI mechanisms and their application to legal requirements Having deconstructed the complex requirements of the judicial process in the previous section, we now survey the established and emerging mechanisms that seek to bridge the gap between AI’s probabilistic output and the discussed demands of legal reason­ ing. This section serves as an overview of AI methods and maps them to the challenges identified earlier. While prompting techniques are a core mechanism in interacting with LLMs [34], we do not discuss them here, as they are already well understood and repre­ sented in the legal literature. No single technique is a panacea; instead, a combination of approaches is required to achieve reliability, transparency, and fairness in AI-assisted adjudication. We outline below how each class of techniques can address specific legal reasoning needs, drawing on recent research developments and highlighting their drawbacks. The final mapping of techniques and challenges is summarized in Table 1. 4.1 Domain adaptation and factual grounding Before an LLM can reason about the law, it must first have reliable access to legal knowl­ edge. This requires techniques that either adapt the model’s internal parameters to the Page 13 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 nuances of the legal domain or ground its responses in external, authoritative sources, thereby enhancing factual accuracy and reducing the risk of hallucination. • Fine-tuning This method continues the training of a pre-trained model to adapt it to the legal domain’s specific vocabulary, style, and nuances. Fine-tuning with legal domain data has been demonstrated to produce “remarkable improvements in various metrics, including the newly proposed Statute Relevance Rate and Legal Claim Truthfulness” [35]. It allows an LLM to obtain a deeper foundation in legal domain knowledge, and it is useful specifically for grasping the structure and typical language associated with Sect. 3.3.1. However, fine-tuning alone is not enough to resolve the requirements discussed due to the variable and open-textured nature of the legal context, which often lacks an authoritative single truth. It also requires a high-quality dataset and risks “catastrophic forgetting”, which degrades the model’s performance in other capabilities that were not the focus of the fine-tuning process [36]. • Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) RAG is the foundational technology for grounding an LLM’s responses in a factual context, and it is an active subject of research in the legal domain [37]. It uses various methods to locate relevant information in an external knowledge base (e.g., statutes, case law) and provides the retrieved content to the LLM as context, making it a powerful tool in scenarios that require pinpointing relevant documents, cases, or other information from a large corpus. RAG is essential for such tasks as Sects. 3.1 and 3.2.2 which require collecting many relevant documents before a decision on, e.g., the right jurisdiction can be made. It also significantly reduces (though does not eliminate) hallucinations Table 1 Mapping of judicial requirements and challenges (Sec. 3) to AI techniques (Sec. 4) with limitations and relevant evaluation metrics Challenge Technique Limitations Evalu­ ation metrics Selecting the correct legal framework Authority-aware, jurisdiction-tagged retrieval using legal hierarchies and connecting-factor classification Incomplete metadata; cross-system bias SR, JAA Conflict of laws Knowledge-graph RAG encoding EU instru­ ments (Rome I, Brussels I) and fora relationships Sparse multilingual cover­ age; contextual ambiguity SR, PC Respect hierarchy, time, and specificity of rules Temporal- and rule-priority-aware RAG enforc­ ing lex posterior and lex specialis Requires versioned corpora; exceptions hard to model TVA, LSSA Apply sources of law and interpretation maxims Neuro-symbolic modules formalize interpreta­ tive canons for deterministic reasoning checks Rule-encoding effort; limited scalability PC, RDIA Interpret case law and extract ratio decidendi Case-entailment and ratio-extraction pipelines align reasoning with binding precedent Ratios implicit; analogies fact-sensitive PC, RDIA Resolve ambiguity (general clauses) Tree-of-Thought and multi-agent debate to expose competing readings of open-textured terms Debate instability; possible convergence to error EAQ, PC Resolve conflicts and gaps in law Argumentation frameworks balance principles and fill lacunae under encoded norms Formalization complexity; pragmatic nuance missing LSSA, PC Evaluate truthfulness of evidence Evidence-grounded RAG linking factual claims to verifiable sources Residual hallucinations; incomplete evidence base EAQ, PC Apply burden or standard of proof Confidence-calibrated generation abstains when uncertainty is high Calibration drift under domain shift AULC, ECE Procedural fairness and explainability Comprehensive audit logging, disclosure bundles, and human oversight Transparency vs. confidenti­ ality trade-off PLC, EAQ Page 14 of 26 Linna and Linna Discover Artificial Intelligence (2026) 6:170 [38]. On the downside, its effectiveness is only as good as the quality of the retrieval system and the knowledge base content. In addition, the most common RAG methods rely on semantic similarity rather than legal similarity, which may cause them to overlook relevant documents. • Temporal & authority-aware retrieval Recent advances tailor RAG for the legal domain by incorporating time-sensitive and authority-aware retrieval strategies. One approach is to use temporal knowledge graphs that index laws and cases by effective dates, ensuring the LLM

title: IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 29, NO. 10, OCTOBER 2025
file_name: Comparing Text-Based Clinical Risk Prediction in Critical Care A Note-Specific Hierarchical Network and Large Language Models.pdf
text: IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 29, NO. 10, OCTOBER 2025 7657 Comparing Text-Based Clinical Risk Prediction in Critical Care: A Note-Speciﬁc Hierarchical Network and Large Language Models Jinghui Liu , Anthony Nguyen , Daniel Capurro , and Karin Verspoor Abstract—Clinical predictive analysis is a crucial task with numerous applications and has been extensively stud- ied using machine learning approaches. Clinical notes, a vital data source, have been employed to develop natu- ral language processing (NLP) models for risk prediction in healthcare with robust performance. However, clinical notes vary considerably in text composition–written by di- verse healthcare providers for different purposes–and the impact of these variations on NLP modeling is also un- derexplored. It also remains uncertain whether the recent Large Language Models (LLMs) with instruction-following capabilities can effectively handle the risk prediction task out-of-the-box, especially when using routinely collected clinical notes instead of polished text. We address these two important research questions in the context of in- hospital mortality prediction within the critical care set- ting. Speciﬁcally, we propose a supervised hierarchical net- work with note-speciﬁc modules to account for variations across different note categories, and provide a detailed comparison with strong supervised baselines and LLMs. We benchmark 34 instruction-following LLMs based on zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompting with diverse prompt templates. Our results demonstrate that the note-speciﬁc network delivers improved risk prediction per- formance compared to established supervised baselines from both measurement-based and text-based modeling. In contrast, LLMs consistently underperform on this crit- ical task, despite their remarkable performances in other domains. This highlights important limitations and raises caution regarding the use of LLMs for risk assessment in the critical setting. Additionally, we show that the proposed model can be leveraged to select informative clinical notes to enhance the training of other models. Index Terms—Electronic health records, health infor- mation, large language models, natural language process- ing, predictive analysis. Received 29 January 2025; revised 11 April 2025; accepted 23 May 2025. Date of publication 27 May 2025; date of current version 7 October 2025. (Corresponding author: Jinghui Liu.) Jinghui Liu and Anthony Nguyen are with Australian e-Health Research Centre, CSIRO, Brisbane, QLD 4029, Australia (e-mail: jinghui.liu@csiro.au; anthony.nguyen@csiro.au). Daniel Capurro is with the School of Computing and Information Systems and Centre for Digital Transformation of Health, University of Melbourne, Melbourne, VIC 3010, Australia (e-mail: dcapurro@ unimelb.edu.au). Karin Verspoor is with the School of Computing Technologies, RMIT University, Melbourne, VIC 3001, Australia (e-mail: karin.verspoor@ rmit.edu.au). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/JBHI.2025.3574254 I. INTRODUCTION C LINICAL prediction models are important tools in health- care to support clinical decision-making and have been studied for many decades [1], [2], [3], [4]. Risk stratiﬁcation, a signiﬁcant category of clinical predictive analysis, assesses the severity of patients based on available information, with wide implications for patient management [5], [6], [7], [8]. Traditionally, clinical risk prediction models are engineered by medical experts, who apply their medical knowledge and practical experience to create speciﬁc scoring systems [1]. In re- cent years, numerous investigations have focused on data-driven approaches to learn from real-world clinical data [9], [10], [11], demonstrating superior performance in clinical risk predictions. The interest in these machine learning methods has been further enhanced by the widespread implementation of electronic health record (EHR), increasing the availability of data for developing these models. Although most clinical prediction models are based on struc- tured data, such as clinical measurements, medical codes, or pre- scriptions, a signiﬁcant amount of information is captured within clinical text written by various caregivers [12], [13], [14], such as nursing notes, radiology reports, and discharge summaries. Compared to structured data elements, unstructured clinical text contains more detailed information about patients, such as their history of illness and clinician’s assessments, and can better reﬂect the complexity of patient conditions. Consequently, it has become increasingly common to apply Natural Language Processing (NLP) methods to train prediction models based on clinical text [14], [15], [16], leading to various applications in risk assessment [17], [18], early diagnosis [19], [20], and resource prediction [21], [22]. However, clinical text is written by different healthcare providers for various purposes, leading to signiﬁcant variations in language and content [23]. For instance, nursing notes tend to focus on the patient’s daily care and condition, while radiology reports are more diagnostic. These variations in clinical notes can signiﬁcantly impact the performance of NLP models, as the models need to consider the nuances and context of the text to make accurate predictions. Meanwhile, most existing NLP modelsdonotaccountforthesevariations,anditremainsunclear how they affect the models’ performance. Furthermore, a paradigm shift in NLP research has been the development of Large Language Models (LLMs) that can © 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ 7658 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 29, NO. 10, OCTOBER 2025 perform well on NLP tasks by simply being instructed or prompted [24], [25], [26]. Pretrained LLMs demonstrate im- pressive capabilities in performing tasks in zero-shot setting without being further ﬁne-tuned, which has drawn signiﬁcant attention from the healthcare community [27], [28], [29]. Recent studies have shown that LLMs can achieve superior performance on medical question answering [30], [31], most famously on USMLE exams [32], and can handle various diagnostic tasks in different clinical specialties [33], [34], [35]. However, many of these studies examined LLMs using curated datasets, such as multiple-choice question answering, rather than routinely collected data from real-world practice. The performance of LLMs for clinical predictive analysis in this setting, especially in comparison with classical supervised ML and NLP models, is less explored. In this study, we consider two main research questions: 1) Can considering the variations in clinical notes further en- hance the performance in text-based clinical risk prediction? 2) How do supervised text-based models compare with instruction- following LLMs for clinical risk prediction in the critical care setting using real-world clinical text? To address these ques- tions, we conduct a benchmarking study to compare the per- formance of prompt-based LLMs with supervised text-based models, and propose a novel note-speciﬁc hierarchical model that accounts for the variations in clinical notes. We evaluate the models on a canonical in-hospital mortality prediction task in Intensive Care Units (ICUs), which is widely studied in the literature using both routinely collected structured mea- surements [6], [36] and unstructured text [18], [37], [38]. Our contributions are as follows: r We conduct benchmarking of 34 popular LLMs on in-hospital mortality prediction, exploring model sizes ranging from 0.5B to 72B parameters and employing vari- ous prompting and decoding strategies. Our results reveal several LLMs that produce competitive risk assessment scores based on routinely collected clinical text, although the best LLM still lags behind supervised text-based base- lines. r To enhance predictive accuracy, we propose a super- vised note-speciﬁc hierarchical model that accounts for variations in clinical note categories. Our results show that the proposed model outperforms previous supervised text-based models–including ﬁne-tuned Transformer en- coders and decoders–and is even competitive with a strong measurement-based baseline. r We also demonstrate that the proposed model can identify important clinical notes using an model explanation method known as attention ﬂow [39]. By selecting the most informative notes through attention ﬂow, the model can signiﬁcantly beneﬁt systems that rely on shorter input contexts. II. RELATED WORKS Clinical predictive analysis, such as mortality prediction, is widely studied in the literature [10], [40], [41], [42]. Clinical notes have been shown as a valuable source of input in the development of these prediction models [16], [43], and also provide potential beneﬁts of surfacing human documentation to support informed actions in the modeling process [44], [45]. Meanwhile, modeling clinical notes often underperforms that based on structured clinical measurements, such as vital signs and laboratory results, in predicting patient risk [46], [47]. Top measurement-based models, especially those can manage missing values, still provide most accurate results in clinical prediction tasks, although in comparison to text-based models they could be less robust and suffer more from data distribution shifts [48], [49]. LLMs have been widely studied in healthcare since the in- troduction of ChatGPT, which has been shown effective in answering complex medical questions [30], [32], [50]. They have been analyzed across a range of clinical specialties, such as cardiology [51] and hepatology [52], but mostly focusing on question answering. Furthermore, these studies tend to use curated datasets with relative short input text. In contrast, text- based predictive modeling often requires processing an aggre- gation of diverse and long clinical notes, which can be more challenging for LLMs. A recent study proposed a long clini- cal document benchmark on mortality prediction [18], which examines various methods including prompt-based LLMs to perform 30-day mortality prediction based on hospital discharge summaries. They prompt 7 LLMs to produce a binary class in a single word (0: alive or 1: dead) and report GPT-4 achieving the highest, yet still modest, F1 score of 32.2%. Our work differs from this previous study in four key ways. First, we focus on in-hospital mortality prediction from the ﬁrst day of admission to the ICU, a more challenging task due to rapidly evolving patient conditions during the hospital stay. In addition, the input notes are more diverse and noisier compared to a single type of discharge summary. Second, we ask the LLMs to produce a risk assessment score (0-100) instead of a binary label (0 or 1), which is critical for stratiﬁcation and triage in practice. This also enables evaluation using more robust metrics including AUR-ROC and AUC-PRC. Third, we examine a wider range of LLMs, prompts, and decoding methods, exploring their impact on the task. Although proprietary models are excluded due to restrictions from the relevant data usage agreement associated with the MIMIC-III dataset we use [53], we include the latest open-source LLMs as of early 2025 in the experiments. Fourth, we compare the LLMs with both text-based and measurement- based models to provide a more comprehensive benchmarking study. Clinicaltextisknowntocontainsubdomainsandnoteswritten by different clinicians and can present distinct linguistic fea- tures and medical concepts [23], [54]. Meanwhile, the value of considering these variations in designing NLP models is un- derexplored. We are inspired by the modular learning approach in language models [55], [56] and propose a model that takes into account the variations in clinical notes by note-speciﬁc modules. Our model is similar to the Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture, which has been shown to be effective in scaling up LLMs [57], [58]. We simplify this idea by using metadata to route notes to the corresponding modules, unlike most MoE modelsthatcontainalearnedroutertoassignmodulesorexperts. LIU et al.: COMPARING TEXT-BASED CLINICAL RISK PREDICTION IN CRITICAL CARE 7659 TABLE I DEMOGRAPHICS OF THE EVALUATION COHORT FOR IN-HOSPITAL MORTALITY PREDICTION III. MATERIALS AND METHODS A. Dataset and Task We utilize the Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care (MIMIC) III version as the data source for our experiments [53], which is a public EHR database collected from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center. We use MIMIC-III rather than MIMIC-IV because the latter has not released progress notes written during the hospital stay as of the time of our study. We follow [59] to prepare a cohort of patients for in-hospital mortal- ity prediction by the ﬁrst day of ICU admission, which includes patients over age of 15 and has only one ICU encounter during the hospital episode. We use their test set of 3,386 admissions to report model performances on. The patient demographics and in-hospital mortality rate of this evaluation cohort are shown in Table I. LLMs are prompted to assess these 3,386 patients without any parameter updates, while the supervised models are trained on the training set of 26,754 patients and validated on 3,375 patients following the same study [59]. For text-based modeling, all clinical notes charted prior to the 24th hour of admission are extracted, sorted by their chart time, and used as model input. Patients on average have 9.3 notes with a standard deviation of 6.2, and the 90 percentile is 18 notes. Each patient would have numerous notes from different categories, such as nursing notes and radiology reports, which are aggregated into a time series. Fig. 1(a) and (b) show the numbers of notes and words per patient by note categories. Each patient has on average 2112 words in their notes, with 90 percentile being 5194 words. These numbers further increased after tokenization when using LLMs. For example, Llama-3 tokenizer would increase the mean and 90 percentile to 4104 and 10665tokens, respectively. As anillustration, thedistributions of tokens by two different LLM tokenizers are shown in Fig. 1(c). For measurement-based modeling, we follow the pipeline from [60], a high-quality benchmark on MIMIC-III, to extract and preprocess 104 curated clinical measurements, including vital signs and laboratory results. The pipeline also includes handling missing values and outliers by imputation and clipping. We refer readers to the original paper for a complete list of measurements. We calculate the Area Under the Receiver Operat- ing Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC), the Area Under the Fig. 1. Distributions of clinical note types and input lengths. Precision-Recall Curve (AUC-PRC), and F1 score to eval- uate the model performance. We compare the performance of the LLMs with the supervised text-based models and the measurement-based models, all evaluated on the same test set. B. Prompting Large Language Models for Risk Prediction We prompt 34 popular open-source, instruction-tuned LLMs for the in-hospital mortality prediction task based on an aggre- gation of the clinical notes. These LLMs cover a wide range of model sizes and versions, including the Llama series from Meta [61], [62], Mistral and Mixtral from MistralAI [58], [63], the Qwen series from Alibaba [64], [65], the Phi series from Microsoft [66], the Gemma series from Google [67], and ﬁve medically adapted models [68], [69], [70], with parameters ranging from 0.5B to 72B. We create the prompt template by asking ChatGPT (4o) to improve a seeding instruction, by which we create a system prompt and a user instruction to ask LLMs to provide a risk assessment score between 0 and 100. Fig. 2 shows an illustration of such a template. In practice, the system and user prompts are customized to the speciﬁc chat template of each LLM by the corresponding tokenizer. We explore ﬁve different user instruc- tions and explore how sensitive the LLM performance is to the prompt variations, and report mean and standard deviation of the risk scores from the ﬁve prompts. We apply this prompting strategy to all LLMs and record the risk scores for the 3,386 patients in the evaluation co- hort.
