This validates the suitability of RBM as a method for relevant feature extraction. D. VECTORIZATION In this stage, the sentences are transformed into vector representations. Each sentence is segmented into a collection of individual words. A TF-IDF score is given to each word, and the matching list of words reflects this value. Potential vector shapes for the sentences are derived from the sum of these TF-IDF scores. These vectors are then fed into the algorithm, which processes them and generates output. E. HYBRID (CSO-HHO) The proposed model focuses on extractive text summarization for individual documents. It comprises three main phases: text pre-processing, feature extraction, and summary generation using a hybrid method known as CSO-HHO. In the pre- processing phase [48], the text undergoes segmentation, lemmatization, stopword removal, and tokenization. Initially, lengthy sentences are segmented into shorter ones, which are subsequently tokenized into individual words. Stopword removal follows, eliminating unnecessary terms from the text, and finally, lemmatization converts words to their base forms. During feature extraction, nine relevant word features are identified using the RBM architecture, followed by vectorization techniques like TF-IDF. After, feature extraction, it is proceeded for generating the summary using hybrid algorithm. The CSO-HHO method then utilizes these features as the objective function to generate the summary. By obtaining more precise fitness function, hybrid algorithm is proposed. It also reduces global and local optimization problems and their convergence speed is high. Cat Swarm Optimization (CSO) is a swarm-based meta- heuristic algorithm inspired by the social behaviour of cats, where their seeking and tracing modes are used to balance exploration and exploitation. In the context of text summarization, CSO efficiently selects the most informative sentences by optimizing feature scores, ensuring the summary retains key content while minimizing redun- dancy. Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO), inspired by the cooperative hunting strategy of Harris hawks, is an advanced optimization technique that enhances global and local search capabilities. HHO dynamically adjusts search strategies using surprise pounce and escaping energy tactics, allowing it to refine the selection of sentences for summarization. The hybrid CSO-HHO method leverages the strengths of both algorithms: CSO’s efficient feature selection and HHO’s adaptive exploration and exploitation mechanism. This com- bination improves the quality of sentence selection, leading to more coherent and informative summaries. The experi- mental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves improved summarization performance and higher accuracy compared to traditional optimization-based approaches. 1) CAT SWARM OPTIMIZATION (CSO) The CSO algorithm is a collective intelligence approach. This form of swarm intelligence operates by utilizing multiple entities, referred to as agents, which interact and communicate with one another to identify the optimal solution. There are two phases to the movement of these particles: exploration and exploitation [43]. They search the search space in order to find prospective locations during the exploration phase, and they concentrate on honing their positions inside those areas during the exploitation phase. Observing the behaviour of cats in their natural environment reveals two primary modes: seeking and tracing [44]. In the seeking mode, cats tend to remain stationary while remaining acutely aware of their surroundings. They maintain a high level of awareness regarding their activities and actively seek opportunities to pursue prey. When in tracing mode, cats are generally more mobile and focused on capturing their target. Cats are known to be lazy, although they actually spend very little of their day hunting and much of their time sleeping, which is known as seeking mode. The CSO algorithm’s implementation usually takes the solution’s position in the search space into account. The global best position of particles, in relation to the location of the solution, and their individual best positions have a major role in the movement of particles in this space. Particles, by default, tend to follow the global best and try to match their individual best placements with the global best that was reached in earlier iterations [45]. If techniques other than the default spherical CSO method were used to evaluate the particle’s individual best and global best scores, the particle progress in the search space would be substantially different from that of the spherical standard implementation and algorithm simulation. a: SEEKING MODE In this mode, the agent typically does not engage in locomotion. Instead, it assimilates information from its sur- roundings and the nearby environment to determine the most efficient next move toward its destination. However, the hybrid algorithm (CSO-HHO) is highly applicable in the context of text summarization due to its ability to balance exploration and exploitation, which is crucial for identifying key information from large documents. In the CSO phase, the algorithm explores the search space by simulating the seeking and tracing behaviours of cats, enabling it to discover promising sentence clusters in the document. The HHO phase, inspired by the hunting strategy of hawks, refines this exploration by using local and global VOLUME 13, 2025 24523 J. Rautaray et al.: DL-Based Feature Extraction Technique for Single Document Summarization search mechanisms to focus on the most relevant sentences and optimize the summary. By combining the strengths of both algorithms, the CSO-HHO hybrid efficiently selects sentences that represent the core content while eliminating irrelevant information. This approach enhances the summa- rization process by improving the accuracy and quality of the summary, ensuring that the most significant details are captured while maintaining conciseness. Though, the proposed methodology demonstrates improved summarization performance and higher accuracy, it has certain limitations. Since it is designed for extractive summarization, it may not effectively capture deep semantic relationships or generate more coherent summaries compared to abstractive approaches. The reliance on predefined feature selection through RBM and TF-IDF may limit adaptability across different document domains and writing styles. Additionally, the model is tailored for individual document summarization, making it less effective for multi-document summarization tasks that require handling redundancy and information fusion. The computational complexity may also increase for longer documents due to extensive feature extraction and optimization processes involved in the CSO-HHO method. Future improvements could focus on enhancing contextual understanding, reducing computational overhead, and extending the approach to multi-document summarization. IV. Python 3.10 is the version of Python used in this analysis. The outcomes of these generated summaries, referred to as candidate summaries, are assessed and contrasted using the Rouge score methodology. Rouge 1 looks at the overlap of unigrams between the reference summary and the candidate summary, whereas Rouge 2 assesses the overlap of bigrams. Rouge L also discovers the longest common subsequence of n-grams. Text summarization performance is evaluated using the DUC 2002, DUC 2003, and DUC 2005 datasets, which are standard benchmarks for assessing automatic summarization systems. Each dataset contains five documents, with an average of approximately 21 to 23 sentences per document. The data is sourced from the TREC (Text Retrieval Confer- ence) dataset, which is renowned for its diverse collection of information retrieval tasks. The ‘‘Length of generated summary in words’’ refers to the total word count of the summaries produced by the automatic summarization model. Rautaray et al.: DL-Based Feature Extraction Technique for Single Document Summarization its strength in preserving contextual relationships between words. Similarly, for Rouge-L scores, which measure the longest common subsequence and reflect sentence-level structure and coherence, the proposed algorithm consistently leads, demonstrating its ability to maintain sentence fluency and structure. This highlights the effectiveness of integrating CSO and HHO in generating summaries that are more accurate, contextually rich, and linguistically coherent. V. CONCLUSION Text summarization remains a challenging task due to the need to balance accuracy, coherence, and contextual relevance while handling diverse datasets and varying text structures. The proposed model integrates pre-processing, feature extraction, vectorization, and a hybrid algorithm to achieve superior results for single-document extractive text summarization. By leveraging RBM for feature extraction and TF-IDF for vectorization, it generates summaries with an impressive accuracy of 99.56%, outperforming existing methods like CSO, QABC, PSO, GJO, FF, SVM, and RF. The proposed CSO-HHO hybrid algorithm combines the strengths of CSO and HHO, offering improved convergence and robustness for text summarization tasks. This approach addresses critical challenges in text summarization and finds applications in fields such as legal, medical, and financial document summarization, where precision and coherence are essential. The model is limited to single-document summarization and may struggle to capture deeper semantic relationships or handle large-scale applications due to its computational com- plexity. Future work can extend this model to multi-document summarization, incorporate transformer-based techniques for better feature selection, and optimize its algorithm with novel fitness functions for enhanced accuracy and efficiency.

file_name: EBPGA Extractive Text Summarization Using Binary Particle Swarm Optimization and Masked Genetic Algorithm.pdf
text: IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTATIONAL SOCIAL SYSTEMS, VOL. 12, NO. 6, DECEMBER 2025 4873 EBPGA: Extractive Text Summarization Using Binary Particle Swarm Optimization and Masked Genetic Algorithm Hemamalini Siranjeevi , Swaminathan Venkatraman , and S. P. Raja Abstract—Automatic text summarization (ATS) deals with compressing a long document into a shorter version, retaining the key ideas of the original document. It aims to tackle the problem of information overload resulting from the continuous creation of documents. ATS helps people make news feeds, meeting reports, summarized legal and ﬁnancial documents, study tools, article outlines, social media analysis, and so on. Extractive text summarization (ETS) is a type of ATS that creates sum- maries (extracts) by choosing sentences from the source without modifying the semantics and structure of the sentences. The main problem in ETS is to select the appropriate sentences that will constitute a coherent, concise, and nonredundant summary that covers the entire document. The literature suggests many optimization-based algorithms for the selection problem. Some authors have used binary particle swarm optimization (BPSO), genetic algorithm (GA), and artiﬁcial bee colony (ABC) algorithm to address the selection problem. However, these algorithms suffer from the problem of premature or slow convergence. This article proposes an ETS algorithm using BPSO and masked GA (EBPGA). The novelty of the proposed system is the usage of the masked GA (MGA), which leverages the properties of the document, namely, coherence, redundancy, relevance, and cover- age factors, along with domain-speciﬁc mutation and crossover to tackle the premature convergence of the BPSO. Thus, the EBPGA algorithm selects the best sentences covering all the topics of the original document to create the summary that is coherent, relevant, and nonredundant. The proposed algorithm was evaluated by producing summaries of size 20%, 30%, and 40%, of the original document, and compared with the existing algorithms. The proposed summarization algorithm obtained a Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation (ROUGE) score that is comparable with the contemporary algorithms. Index Terms—Automatic text summarization (ATS), extractive summarization, feature extraction, masked genetic algorithm (MGA), particle swarm optimization (PSO). Received 8 April 2025; revised 11 June 2025; accepted 20 June 2025. Date of publication 16 July 2025; date of current version 3 December 2025. (Corresponding author: Swaminathan Venkatraman.) Hemamalini Siranjeevi is with the Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Srinivasa Ramanujan Centre, SASTRA Deemed University, Kumbakonam 612001, India (e-mail: hemajeevi@src.sastra.edu). Swaminathan Venkatraman is with the Artiﬁcial Intelligence and Con- trol System Laboratory, School of Arts, Sciences, Humanities and Ed- ucation, SASTRA Deemed University, Thanjavur 613401, India (e-mail: mvs_new@yahoo.com). S. P. Raja is with the School of Computer Science and Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, India (e-mail: avemariaraja@ gmail.com). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TCSS.2025.3583893 I. INTRODUCTION I N today’s digital world, several online and ofﬂine sources produce data at a continuous rate. The data, which are in the form of long documents or large ﬁles, make it difﬁcult for the readers to understand and interpret the essential information. In particular, textual data is abundant, mandating research in text summarization. Automatic text summarization (ATS) focuses on condensing the information from the source document into a shorter form called the summary, thus helping the readers to understand the document quickly. Many ATS techniques have been proposed in the litera- ture. Based on the nature of the summary produced, ATS can be classiﬁed as extractive and abstractive text summa- rization. Extractive text summarization (ETS) selects the most signiﬁcant sentences from the source documents and combines them to generate a summary. It does not modify the sen- tences of the original document. Abstractive text summariza- tion reformulates the original sentences from the source docu- ments by capturing the semantics and paraphrasing them into a summary. ATS is divided into two categories based on the number of input documents used: single-document and multi-document summarization. Creating a summary from a single document is known as single-document summarization, while creating a summary from multiple documents related to a speciﬁc topic is known as multi-document summarization. In the latter case, the most signiﬁcant sentences from each document are chosen and combined to create a single summary. Though single-document and multi-document summariza- tion have been vastly explored in the literature [27], single- document summarization is critical in situations such as elec- tronic health record (EHR) summarization and legal document summarization. In addition, such domains require that the ﬁ- delity of the document be preserved during summarization. Hence, extractive summarization is preferred compared to ab- stractive techniques. Most of the approaches utilized by extractive algorithms include statistical, topic-based, graph-oriented, semantic-based, machine learning, deep learning, optimization, and fuzzy logic. Statistical algorithms utilize features such as sentence po- sition, similarity, and term frequency/inverse document fre- quency (TF_IDF) to score the sentences and create the summary [22]. Although simple, it suffers from problems like avoiding 2329-924X © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artiﬁcial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 07:47:37 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 4874 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTATIONAL SOCIAL SYSTEMS, VOL. 12, NO. 6, DECEMBER 2025 important sentence due to low scores and the fact that it can create redundant summaries [35]. Topic-based methods rely on converting the input into an intermediate representation and assigning a score. These methods require a careful selection of topics as they affect the quality of the generated summary [35]. Graph-based methods represent the sentences as the ver- tices of the graphs and use sentence similarity to construct the summary [23]. Therefore, the accuracy of the similarity computation affects the quality of the summary [35]. Semantic- based methods score the sentences based on the co-occurrence of the words. The quality of the semantic representation affects the performance of the summarizer. Machine learning methods such as support vector machine (SVM) and recurrent neural network (RNN) [24] require large datasets containing human reference summaries to train [37]. The signiﬁcant challenges in using such supervised algorithms are the scarcity of labeled data and the fact that they are prone to generalization errors. Subsequently, many unsuper- vised ATS algorithms [26] have been proposed in the litera- ture, as they do not rely on labeled datasets, and they gen- eralize well. Deep learning methods can perform automatic feature selection but require humans to create the training data. Optimization-based methods such as Cuckoo search and Bat algorithm [39] ﬁnd the best summary by ﬁnding the optimal weights. Some authors have used optimization al- gorithms such as the artiﬁcial bee colony (ABC) algorithm [33] and the cat swarm optimization algorithm [34], to ob- tain extractive summarization by approximation. Nevertheless, these optimization-based methods suffer from limitations such as need to ﬁx the number of iterations before the execu- tion of the algorithm and the parameter controlling problem [36]. Fuzzy logic algorithms may ﬁnd solutions in ambigu- ous and uncertain environments but still require redundancy reduction [35]. On the whole, the literature shows that ATS methods try to maximize multiple objectives, and hence they belong to the computational complexity class of nondeterministic polynomial time (NP-hard) [40]. In particular, the major research problems of extractive summarization are as follows: 1) What strategy should be adopted for sentence selection? 2) How can redundant information from the summary be eliminated? Many ATS systems that combine the advantages of multiple methods have been proposed in the literature [35], and such ensemble models are found to achieve precise results compared to single models [38]. Inspired by these approaches, the authors propose an unsupervised, extractive, single-document summa- rization to tackle the problems of increasing the coverage and reducing the redundancy of the summary with the following contributions: 1) Hybridized two optimization-based algorithms, namely, the BPSO and the MGA into EBPGA (Algorithm 1). 2) Proposed a novel masked genetic algorithm (MGA) (Al- gorithm 2) to mitigate the stagnation problem of PSO using four masks each for the following: a) coherence; b) redundancy; c) relevance; and d) coverage. 3) Performed a comparative analysis of the effect of max- imization, minimization, and maxmin on the objective function. The article is organized as follows. Section I presents the in- troduction. Section II brieﬂy the related works. Section III describes the methodology proposed, which includes data pre- processing, feature extraction, binary PSO, and masked GA. Section IV elaborates on the dataset used, the results obtained, evaluation metrics, and comparative analysis using graphs. Fi- nally, Section V concludes the article with insights for future research. II. RELATED WORK ATS research began in 1958 by Luhn [11], who utilized a statistical analysis of the document to determine that words that occur more frequently are more important than ones that occur less frequently. Following his work, the ATS research community introduced many abstractive and extractive meth- ods. Many researchers have used ranking strategies to address the problem of sentence selection. Kumar Meena and Gopalani [4] studied the use of evolutionary algorithms to solve the sen- tence scoring, ranking, and selection problems of ETS. Holland [15] proposed a search-based optimization, namely, the genetic algorithm (GA), which draws inspiration from the evolution and natural selection among the population search space. He initiated the fundamental ideas of GA, namely, ﬁtness function, population, selection, crossover, and mutation. Karamanis and Manurung [16] proposed an evolutionary algorithm similar to the GA with ﬁtness function using the continuity heuristic to reach the global optimum quickly. The ability of GA to reach the global optimum inspired the text-mining research community to utilize its potential in var- ious NLP applications. In particular, Atkinson-Abutridy et al. [10] introduced an approach that relied on the semantic con- straints and the original text corpus by combining GA and information extraction technology to guide the knowledge dis- covery process. Kiani introduced one of the pioneering works for text summarization using evolutionary algorithms, namely, a hybrid fuzzy GA–GP approach. In this work, Kiani et al. [12] used genetic programming (GP) to process the structural part of the rule set and GA to process the string part of the rule set. Rojas Simón et al. [17] adopted GA to rank sentences, using the best sentence combination of DUC01 and DUC02 datasets for extractive summarization. Mojrian and Mirroshan- del [3] proposed a multidocument text summarization sys- tem using quantum-inspired genetic algorithm (MTSQIGA), which utilizes quantum-inspired GA for text summarization. Yang et al. [14] proposed a novel GA by improving the two steps of GA, namely, crossover and mutation. Notwithstanding all these improvements, GA suffers from slow convergence and the necessity to tune the parameters [36]. In addition, GA cannot ﬁnd the optimal solution when the distance be- tween particles becomes shorter because it does not search in local space [41]. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 07:47:37 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. SIRANJEEVI et al.: ETS USING BPSO AND MGA 4875 Particle swarm optimization (PSO) is an evolutionary op- timization technique inspired by the social behavior of bird ﬂocks and ﬁsh schools, introduced by Kennedy and Eberhart [2]. Later, to overcome the binary optimization problem in the search space, they proposed a modiﬁed PSO algorithm that op- erates on discrete binary values [13]. In this binary PSO, rather than representing the movement of trajectories in a continuous dimension, the probability of a coordinate is represented by the binary values 0 or 1. To increase the performance of the PSO, a new parameter called inertia weight was introduced by Shi and Eberhart [9]. This parameter describes how particles adjust their ﬂying behavior. This algorithm balances between exploration and exploitation by adjusting this inertia weight. Xi et al. [5] introduced a new mechanism that involves weighted mean best positions for the ﬁtness value of the particle showing faster convergence than traditional algorithms. They improved overall performance of the algorithm by balancing global and local searches. Khanesar et al. [7] provided a new deﬁnition of the velocity of particles. They addressed the difﬁculty in choosing the correct value of weights with a novel BPSO algorithm. Though PSO searches locally and globally, it focuses on global space. However, it may experience premature convergence or stagnation. Some authors such as Aote et al. [1], Bui et al. [6], and Peyrard and Eckle-Kohler [18] tried to alleviate the shortcom- ings of evolutionary algorithms by combining them, hoping that they would complement each other. As a result of the inspiration from such research, we propose the EBPGA algorithm, a hybrid of the BPSO and MGA. EBPGA aims to mitigate the problem of early convergence of PSO by complementing it with a modiﬁed version of GA, namely the MGA. III. METHODOLOGY Fig. 1 portrays the architecture of the proposed approach EBPGA. The following are highlights of the proposed approach: 1) The BBC News articles with their reference summaries are collected from Kaggle. 2) The news articles are preprocessed, and eight preliminary scores are calculated for each sentence. 3) A feature matrix is constructed using the scores evaluated from the preprocessed text. 4) The personal best and global best positions are updated. 5) In case of stagnation, MGA is applied. 6) Otherwise, the BPSO algorithm is repeated for the max- imum number of iterations and, in turn, the maximum number of epochs. A. Preprocessing Text preprocessing plays an important role in transforming the raw text format into a suitable format for analysis. Fac- tors such as punctuation, symbols, abbreviations, case-sensitive text, and stopwords make text data unﬁt for processing, affect- ing overall performance. Punctuation marks commonly include symbols such as question marks, quotation marks, the dollar symbol, parentheses, exclamation marks, and so on. Removing punctuation makes it easy to process the text. All letters of the input document are converted to lower case, namely, “the” and “The” are treated as “the.” Lower casing reduces the variabil- ity and helps to improve uniformity among the text. Sentence tokenization breaks the entire text into sentences, and word tokenization breaks each sentence into words. At the end of tokenization, each word is treated as a token. This process helps to process the document at the sentence and word levels. In English, words such as “this,” “that,” “is,” “was,” and so on are called stopwords. Since these words provide minor semantics to the text, they are not important. Hence, these words are removed from the text. Lemmatization is the process of converting words to their root form. Words such as “thinking” and “thought” are converted to their dictionary or root form “think.” This step can reduce the problem of vocabulary mismatch. The words of the lemmatized sentences are tagged with the corresponding parts of speech. This step helps identify the nouns/entities involved in the sentences. The count of proper nouns in a sentence is an important feature. B. Feature Extraction Early ATS research relied solely on the TF_IDF computation for feature extraction. Since this feature is purely based on statistical measures and does not take semantics into account, many other factors with semantic properties have been iden- tiﬁed for feature extraction [1]. In this work, each sentence of the document is represented by a particle. Hence, the par- ticle consists of a total of eight features, namely Title Fea- ture (TitleFeature), Sentence Length (SL), Sentence Position (SP), Numerical Data (ND), Thematic Word (TW), Noun and Proper Noun Score (PN), Term Frequency—Inverse Sentence Frequency (TF_ISF), and Centroid Similarity (CS). TitleFeature mentioned in (1), is the relative count of words of a sentence with respect to that of the title. The sentence length feature mentioned as SL in (2) provides a comparison of each sentence with the longest sentence. The sentences at the beginning of the text are considered more important than those at the end. The sentence position feature mentioned as SP in (3) calculates the distance of the given sentence relative to the entire document. Usually, sentences containing numerical data are more important than other sentences. The feature ND in (4) is the ratio of the number of numerical data to the number of words in the sentence. The thematic words are the document’s top ten most fre- quently occurring words. The thematic score feature TW of each sentence is calculated using (5) assuming the total number of thematic words is ten. Nouns usually play an important role in characterizing the entire text of a sentence. This score is calculated as PN using (6). The TF_ISF is a statistical estimate of the signiﬁcance of a word in a sentence, compared to that of all words within the document [29]. It is calculated using (9). The TF_ISF scores are obtained as an array with each entry normalized within the range of 0–1. It is a variant of the TF_IDF feature often used for multi-document summarization. Term Frequency (TF) in (9) refers to the number of times a word occurs in a sentence. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. SIRANJEEVI et al.: ETS USING BPSO AND MGA 4877 It is the relative frequency of a word in the document, calculated as in (7). TF may also overestimate unimportant words as they depend on their frequency. To avoid this, the Inverse Sentence Frequency, ISF of a word, is calculated as in (8), referring to the log of the ratio of the number of sentences in the document over the number of sentences in which the word occurs. ISF gives importance to words that rarely occur. Then, a product of TF and ISF, as in (9), gives the meaningful score of the word concerning the entire document. Finally, the sentence-wise TF_ISF score, namely, tfSum is calculated as in (10). In general, the centroid value of a graph provides a centrality index weighted with the values of all other nodes in the graph. In ATS, the sentence that has the highest TF_ISF score is de- termined as the centroid sentence [8]. This work used the cosine similarity between each sentence and the centroid sentence to calculate the CS feature, as in (11) [30]. This feature helps identify sentences that are important or similar to an important sentence. Subsequently, the eight scores are concatenated as in (12) to produce the feature matrix F, thus containing n rows and eight columns. C. BPSO The values of the eight features are calculated and the feature matrix F of order n × f is constructed (n is the number of sentences or particles and f is the number of features, here f = 8). Every feature value is normalized between 0 and 1, as one of the features may be much higher than the other features, and the sum of all features in a sentence may greatly inﬂuenced by the feature whose value is greater. This feature matrix is then TitleFeature = Total number of common words that match the title Total number of words in the title (1) SL = Total number of words in the sentence Total number of words in the sentence with maximum length (2) SP = Total number of sentences −Position of current sentence Total number of sentences (3) ND = count(numbers) in the sentence count(words) in the sentence (4) TW = Number of thematic words that the sentence contains Total number of thematic words (5) PN = Number of nouns in the sentence Total number of nouns present in the preprocessed text (6) TF(word, sent) = count(word) : word ∈sent count(word) : word ∈document (7) ISF(word) = log  count(sent ∈document) count(sent) : sent ∈document, word ∈sent  (8) TF_ISF(word, sent) = TF(word, sent) × ISF(word) (9) tfSum(sent) =  word∈sent TF_ISF(word, sent) (10) CS = cosinesimilarity(sent, centroid) (11) Fsent = < TitleFeature(sent), SL(sent), SP(sent), ND(sent), TW(sent), PN(sent), tfSum(sent), CS(sent) >. (12) Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 07:47:37 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 4878 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTATIONAL SOCIAL SYSTEMS, VOL. 12, NO. 6, DECEMBER 2025 Fig. 2. Representation of a particle having eight features. passed to the BPSO. The BPSO algorithm is used to ﬁnd the optimal value of the feature. BPSO uses a score function as the ﬁtness function to generate these optimal feature values. First, the particle position matrix is randomly initialized with 0’s and 1’s. The ﬁtness of each sentence is calculated by adding the weights of the features whose corresponding bit position in the feature matrix is 1. Fig. 2 shows the representation of particles that have eight features, where 0 is the absence of the feature, and 1 is the presence of the feature. 1) Working of PSO: PSO is an evolutionary algorithm that simulates the behavior of a bird ﬂock by representing each bird(particle) with a vector. This vector in the search space is multidimensional, for example, if the search space vector is d-dimensional, the ith particle is represented as a position vec- tor Xi = (xi1, xi2, · · · , xid). PSO uses another vector called the velocity vector, denoted as Vi = (vi1, vi2, · · · , vid) that deter- mines the movement of the particle. The velocity and position of particles are randomly initialized in the search space. The lower and upper bounds of the search space are the minimum and maximum scored particles. These bounds deﬁne the range of the search space, and are updated throughout the execution of the algorithm. The PSO algorithm updates the velocity of the particle based on the current velocity, the personal best position (piBest) explored till now, and the global best position (pgBest) explored by the entire swarm [7]. The personal best- visited position of the particle (piBest) is encoded as piBest = (Pi1, Pi2, · · · , Pid) and the best global visited position of the swarm is encoded as pgBest = (Pg1, Pg2, · · · , Pgd). 2) Working of the BPSO: In the Binary PSO algorithm, the position of the particle is represented as “0” or “1,” and the value can be mutated to “1” or “0.” Hence, the position vector Xi = (xi1, xi2, · · · , xid) mentioned in Section III-C1 is a binary vector, with each entry being a “0” or a “1.” In the BPSO, the velocity of a particle is the probability of changing the position bit to “1.” In this research, the authors calculated the piBest scores using the ﬁtness function as in (15), which takes feature values and the position of particles as input. The initial particle position is taken as the piBest position. The ﬁtness value of each particle is its respective score, and it is considered the piBest for the particles. The minimum scored particle among the piBest is considered as the pgBest. The velocity of the particles is randomly initialized with 0’s and 1’s. The changes in velocity over each iteration are computed using as follows: TABLE I BPSO PARAMETERS S.No Parameter Value 1 Number of Number of particles sentences 2 Number of Number of dimensions features 3 Maximum number 200 of epochs 4 Maximum number of 300 iterations per epoch vij(t + 1) = wvij(t) + c1r1(pij(t) −xij(t)) + c2r2(pgj(t) −xij(t)). (13) vij(t + 1) is the velocity of the ith particle in the jth dimension at time t + 1, w is the inertia weight representing how much the previous velocity vector affects the new vector, vij(t) is the velocity of the ith particle in the jth dimension at time t, c1 is the cognitive acceleration coefﬁcient, r1 and r2 are random variables (with a uniform distribution between 0 and 1), c2 is the social acceleration coefﬁcient, pij(t) is the personal best position of the ith particle at jth dimension at time t, Xi is the position vector of the ith particle, xij(t) is the position of the ith particle in the jth dimension at time t, pgj(t) is the global best position of the ith particle in jth dimension at time t. Here, the parameters w, c1, and c2 were ﬁxed via empirical trials as 0.5, 0.9, and 0.9, respectively. The position of a particle, xij(t + 1) is updated using the velocity vij(t) of the particle as in the following [19]: xij(t + 1) =  1, if r < 1 1+exp−vij (t) 0, Otherwise (14) where r is a random number between 0 and 1. The pgBest score and position are determined from the piBest of the particles. If the velocity exceeds the upper bound, it is clipped to the upper bound and vice versa [1]. This velocity clipping within the range of the search space helps the algorithm avoid divergence from the solution. D. EBPGA Algorithm 1 shows the working of the BPSO for extractive summarization. The Table I shows the parameters of the BPSO. The position of the particle depends on the sigmoid function of the velocity. The new score is calculated using the ﬁtness function as follows: Score(Si) = 8  j=1 Fij × xij (15) where Score(Si) indicates the score of the ith sentence, Fij (from feature matrix F) is the weight of the jth dimension for the sentence Si and xij is the position of the bit at the jth dimension for the sentence Si. Return the 20%, 30% and 40% summaries TABLE II MGA PARAMETERS S. No Feature Value 1 CrossoverRate 0.8 2 MutationRate 0.1 3 CoherenceFactor 0.7 4 RedundancyThreshold 0.7 5 CoverageFactor 0.7 6 RelevanceFactor 0.7 If the new score is greater than the piBest score, then it is taken as the new piBest score and the corresponding position vector Xi is recorded as the personal best position pbestPos. If the new score is less than the pgBest score, then the pgBest is updated as the new score, and its position vector, Xi, is recorded as the global best position gbestPos. This process is repeated for 200 epochs. In each epoch, the process is repeated for a maximum of 300 iterations. The average of all the epochs is considered as the ﬁnal score of the particles. At the end of each epoch, the algorithm checks whether stagnation occurs or not. Stagnation or premature convergence is a stage where the position of piBest does not change over a set of iterations, and this point is treated as the stagnation point in BPSO. This work identiﬁes the number of iterations that reach the stagnation point using empirical trials as ten. If there is no change in the scores for ten iterations, then the algorithm is said to have stagnated. To address this stagnation, the authors introduced a modiﬁed GA, namely, the MGA. E. MGA The general steps involved in GA include selection, crossover, and mutation. However, the GA suffers from the problem of premature convergence. As a result, many variants of GA have been proposed in the literature. Some of the variants are the improved GA [14], the masking-oriented GA [31], and the messy GA [32]. Inspired by the use of masking- oriented GA toward language tasks, the authors propose the MGA as a solution to the stagnation problem. In contrast to the GA, MGA uses only two steps, namely, crossover and mutation. Crossover occurs between the adjacent feature bits of each sentence based on the crossover rate. Mutation is performed as follows: ﬁrst, divide the features into two halves; To increase the randomness, the mutation rate is set to 0.1 for the ﬁrst half of features and 0.2 for the second half. The mutation is then applied to the feature matrix according to the mutation rate. Empirical trials were used to ﬁx the parameters of the MGA, shown in Table II. Algorithm 2 explains the implementation of the MGA. The MGA in the Fig. 1 depicts the working of the MGA. The MGA uses four masks, coherence, redundancy, rele- vance, and coverage, to update the personal best position vector pbestPos. The coherence mask is calculated for every feature, as in (16), and used to determine whether a bit is coherent. The importance of a feature depends on how often it occurs in a text. However, features that are higher than the redundancy threshold are considered redundant. That is, the relevance mask gives more importance to less redundant features and vice versa. Coverage is calculated on the basis of how many sentences have a particular feature. Thus, the proposed MGA combines the concept of crossover and mutation with domain-speciﬁc considerations. (22) At the end of the EBPGA, the ﬁnal scores are sorted in descending order and three types of summaries, namely, 20%, 30%, and 40% are extracted. IV. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION The proposed EBPGA algorithm was implemented in Python 3.10 on Google Colab. A. Dataset Description The research community has extensively utilized the document understanding conference (DUC) and the CNN DailyMail corpus. However, these datasets are primarily appropriate for tasks involving multi-document and abstractive Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Therefore, some of the authors of recent studies, such as Dong et al. [25] and Vo et al. [42], utilized a more general dataset, the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) News Summary dataset [28]. The BBC News Summary dataset is among the benchmark datasets in text summarization tasks. The characteristics of the BBC News Summary dataset are listed in Table III. It consists of 2225 documents stories from 2004 to 2005 in ﬁve topical areas: business, entertainment, politics, sport, and tech. Both the news articles and their reference summary are provided. The average reference summary length for the entire dataset is 42.786%. B. Analysis of the Proposed Approach An analysis of our proposed EBPGA is demonstrated to exhibit its effectiveness. We randomly selected a document from the BBC News Summary dataset and summarized that document using the proposed EBPGA. A sample BBC docu- ment is shown in Fig. 3, and its reference summary is shown in Fig. 4. Figs. 5–7 show the 20%, 30%, and 40% summaries produced by EBPGA, respectively. These ﬁgures show that both the reference summary and EBPGA’s summary are pretty similar. It is visible that the proposed EBPGA can provide a concise summary of the document that matches most of the sen- tences in the reference summary. The order of the sentences may change, but it covers most of the sentences from the reference summary. C. Evaluation Metric The performance of the proposed EBPGA is compared with other contemporary algorithms using the Recall- Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation (ROUGE) metric. This evaluation was implemented using Kavita Ganesan’s ROUGE Java toolkit [20]. This toolkit can produce results in terms of ROUGE −1, 2, · · · , N; L; S1, S2, S3, · · · , SN; SU1, SU2, SU3, · · · , SUN. Here, 1, 2, · · · , N represent the n −gram used for comparison: 1) represents unigram or word-level comparison; and 2) represents bigram, and so on. ROUGE −L comparison is based on the longest common subsequence. ROUGE −S uses the skip-bigram co-occurrence as a comparison measure. Here, skip-bigram represents any pair of words in sentence order, allowing gaps of various lengths. Skip-bigram co-occurrence measures the overlap of such skip-bigrams between the system and candidate summaries [21]. ROUGE −SU is an extension of the ROUGE −S by including unigram to count. Although ROUGE −SU with various skip distances of 1, 4 and 9 have been reported in the literature, most authors have used ROUGE −SU4. The literature shows extensive use of the evaluation metrics ROUGE −1, ROUGE −2, and ROUGE −SU4. The authors have evaluated the results using these metrics based on the precision, recall, and F1-measure as in (23)–(25), respectively. Here, refsum is the reference summary, and syssum is the system summary produced by the proposed approach. The precision and recall metrics calculate the ratio of the number of sentences that overlap between the reference summary and the system summary, over the system summary and reference summary, respectively. Since they are inverse of each other, the harmonic mean of precision and recall is calculated as the F1 measure Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. This performance is on par with the SOTA algorithms taken for comparison as they all have a worst-case time complexity of the order of O(n2), except LSA, which has a time com- plexity of O(n3), and Lead, which has a time complexity of O(1). Though Lead has a constant time complexity, its ROUGE scores are less than the proposed algorithm, as seen in Ta- bles V–VII. The poor score of Lead can be attributed to the fact that it retrieves the ﬁrst few sentences as the summary and is trivial. E. Comparative Analysis The authors conducted a comparative study using different approaches for the objective function in the Binary PSO algo- rithm (steps 10–12): maximization, minimization, and maxmin. In the maximization approach, the Algorithm 1 updates both piBest, and pgBest if the new score is higher than both piBest and pgBest. The minimization updates the piBest and pgBest scores if the new score is less than both these scores. In the maxmin approach, piBest keeps track of the maximum personal score of the particle, and pgBest serves as the minimum/lower bound of the entire swarm (Section III-C2). Table IV shows the results of ROUGE scores obtained by the 40% summary for the experimented approaches. Of the three approaches, maxmin produces better results than maximization and minimization. The performance of the proposed EBPGA approach is com- pared with that of seven popular methods, namely SummaRuN- Ner, BERT, ChunkRank, KLsumm, Lead, LSA, and SumBa- sic. SummaRuNNer [43] is an extractive summarizer which uses RNN that trains on abstractive summaries to produce ex- tractive summaries. Bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERTs) is a pretrained model that can under- stand the document’s information and identify the important sentences. ChunkRank [44] is a graph-based approach for extractive summarization. Lead [43] is a summarizer that se- lects the leading sentences from the input document to gener- ate the extracts. KLSum algorithm uses key-phrase extraction techniques to generate the summary. Latent semantic analysis (LSA) selects the most important sentences based on their se- mantic importance to the overall content. SumBasic is a greedy search approximation algorithm that uses term frequency (TF) to score the sentences. The Sumy package provides functions like BERT, KLSumm, LSA, and SumBasic for comparing ATS systems and the other methods, namely SummaRuNNer, Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Restrictions apply. 4884 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTATIONAL SOCIAL SYSTEMS, VOL. 12, NO. 6, DECEMBER 2025 ChunkRank, and Lead were implemented by the authors refer- ring to their algorithms. For each of these popular algorithms, 20%, 30%, and 40% summaries were extracted and compared with the proposed EBPGA’s 20%, 30%, and 40% summaries, respectively. The Tables V–VII compare the precision, recall, and F1-measure of EBPGA with other contemporary algorithms, wherein the bold entries indicate EBPGA scores. The proposed EBPGA exhibits better ROUGE scores than these algorithms. Also, one can see that the precision and recall scores are opposites. The precision decreases as the size of the summary increases. On the other hand, the recall measure increases with the size of the summary. Hence, a harmonic mean of these two measures, namely, the F1-measure, is also recorded in Table V. Table V shows that the ROUGE-1 precision of EBPGA is high compared to the remaining seven algorithms taken for comparison. Similarly, the ROUGE-1 recall measures show that the 30% summaries produced by SummaRuNNer and ChunkRank are comparatively better than EBPGA. The 30% summary produced by SummaRuNNer and ChunkRank ex- celled the performance of EBPGA. However, the proposed method shows a comparatively reasonable F1 measure for the 40% summary, approximately the same size as the reference summary. Table VI shows a comparative analysis of the seven algo- rithms and EBPGA in terms of the ROUGE-2 metric. Here, the comparison between the system summary and the refer- ence summary is in terms of bigrams. Similarly, the ROUGE- SU4 metric, as shown in Table VII, shows that even though the precision of EBPGA is higher than the other algorithms, the recall and F1 measure obtained by SummaRuNNer and ChunkRank are higher than that of EBPGA for 20% and 30% summaries, whereas EBPGA performs better at 40 whole, these tables demonstrate that EBPGA shows gradual improvement in performance in terms of precision, recall, and F1-measure for the 20%, 30%, and 40%. The ideal summary size for this dataset is approximately 40%. It may vary for other datasets. In conclusion, EBPGA works on par with almost all the algo- rithms taken for comparison, except the fact that it falls behind SummaRuNNer and ChunkRank in terms of the recall and F1- measure of ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-SU4 for the 30% summary. The better performance of SummaRuNNer in this case may be attributed to the fact that it gives importance to novelty [43]. The better performance of the ChunkRank summarizer may be because of the better coverage of topics organized as chunks [45]. Notwithstanding all these, EBPGA excelled in the case of the 40% summary size. V. CONCLUSION In this article, an extractive single document text summa- rization algorithm EBPGA is proposed as a hybrid of swarm- based BPSO algorithm and evolutionary based MGA and tested on the BBC News Summary dataset. Eight features have been used for feature extraction. The BPSO algorithm is used to ﬁnd the optimal values of the features. As it may sometimes enter into stagnation, the MGA, which consists of crossover and mutation, is used. MGA uses coherence, redundancy, rel- evance, and coverage masks to identify the optimal position vector, and in turn the optimal scores of the particles. The algo- rithm has been tested in three different optimization approaches: the maximization, minimization, and maxmin approaches. The maxmin approach is better compared to the other two methods. EBPGA scores better in terms of ROUGE when compared to SummaRuNNer, BERT, ChunkRank, KLSumm, Lead, LSA, and SumBasic summarizers. As mentioned in the Section IV-E, out of the three different sizes of summary, namely 20%, 30% and 40%, the 40% summary is better. An open avenue of research in ETS is using a different combination of features such as readability, conciseness, gram- maticality, ﬁdelity, and so on. The authors would like to extend this proposed EBPGA to utilize the above features to create the extracts. One more open research problem in ETS is the ordering problem. The summary produced by EBPGA may not be in the same order as that of the original document. This ordering property proves to be crucial in multi-document summarization domains. Hence, the authors would like to ﬁnd solutions for the ordering problem and extend the work to multi- document summarization.

title: Efficient Vector-Multiplicative Privacy-Preserving
file_name: Efficient Vector-Multiplicative Privacy-Preserving Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models.pdf
text: 1 Efficient Vector-Multiplicative Privacy-Preserving Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models Jinhao Zhou, Graduate Student Member, IEEE, Jun Wu, Senior Member, IEEE Abstract—Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds large language models (LLMs) in external knowledge, yet it is faces a fundamental trade-off between knowledge confidentiality and retrieval efficiency. Existing approaches fail to reconcile this trade-off: i) Cryptography-based solutions (e.g., homomor- phic encryption and secure multi-party computation) provide strong privacy guarantees but incur prohibitive computation and communication overheads. ii) Lightweight perturbation-based methods (e.g., differential privacy) offer higher efficiency at the cost of degraded retrieval accuracy or weakened security guar- antees. In this paper, we propose CipheRAG, an efficient vector- multiplicative privacy-preserving RAG framework that achieves a principled balance between robust privacy and high-performance retrieval. Technically, we first propose an efficient searchable inner product functional encryption (IPFE) mechanism enhanced with asymmetric locality-sensitive hashing (ALSH), enabling the retrieval of sensitive knowledge while effectively preserving data confidentiality. Secondly, we propose a decryption-enabled attention mechanism that uses linear weights of the attention layer to decrypt knowledge. This mechanism seamlessly inte- grates decrypted knowledge into the LLM’s generation pro- cess, achieving efficiency and accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CipheRAG achieves up to 35x faster generation and 15x faster QKV computation compared to FHE- and OT- based baselines. By avoiding linear retrieval and full-parameter encryption, CipheRAG enables privacy-preserving RAG with bounded computational and communication overheads, making it well-suited for deployment in privacy-sensitive environments. Index Terms—Large language model, inner product encryp- tion, retrieval-augmented generation, locality-sensitive hashing I. INTRODUCTION T HE retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) [1] technique has emerged as a promising paradigm to enhance the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) services [2]– [5]. By incorporating externally sourced information, referred to as “knowledge”, RAG allows LLMs to fetch contextu- ally relevant data from various knowledge repositories. As illustrated in Fig. 1, a typical RAG framework maintains a central knowledge database compiled from multiple providers. When a user submits a query, the LLM first consults this database to retrieve the most pertinent information, which it then integrates into its reasoning process to produce a more informed and accurate response. This ability to dynamically tap into external knowledge underlies the strength of RAG, This work was supported in part by the JSPS KAKENHI under Grants 23K11072, in part by the China Scholarship Council Program. Jinhao Zhou and Jun Wu are with the Graduate School of Informa- tion, Production and Systems, Waseda University, Fukuoka, Japan. (email: {jinhao@suou, junwu@aoni}.waseda.jp). making it a promising approach for building LLM applications that demand up-to-date, context-aware information [6]. Despite their effectiveness, RAG systems face persistent privacy risks because the retrieved knowledge often contains sensitive information. Such sensitive information can include confidential documents, domain-specific corpora, or intellec- tual property [7], which is vulnerable to attacks including em- bedding inversion [8] and knowledge poisoning [9]. Moreover, any leakage during retrieval can propagate into the downstream LLM, compromising model security and enabling inference attacks that threaten both the integrity of the model and the privacy of its users [10]–[12]. The consequences of such breaches are severe. For instance, when an authorized medical professional queries an RAG system to review the patient’s medical records or specialist recommendations, the underlying dataset encompasses infor- mation that must remain confidential. Unauthorized access to such information can result in severe consequences, encom- passing substantial legal penalties, erosion of patient trust, and considerable reputational harm. Therefore, it is essential to design a retrieval primitive that inherently protects both vector representations and metadata, for example by operating entirely over encrypted or enclave-protected data, to eliminate the principal leakage channel without compromising the accu- racy of retrieved knowledge, which is crucial for the generation quality of LLM services. A direct way to protect privacy is by adding perturbations to the data [13]; however, this approach often incurs a significant accuracy drop. Fig. 1: The workflow of a RAG system. A potential attacker threatens the privacy of the knowledge database. Encryption-based methods, which offer robust data con- fidentiality by enabling computations directly on encrypted This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing. This is the author's version which has not been fully edited and content may change prior to final publication. Citation information: DOI 10.1109/TDSC.2026.3669543 © 2026 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:02:06 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 2 data, have been proposed to ensure privacy protection while maintaining accuracy. They provide a promising solution for securely performing retrieval and generation tasks within RAG systems without exposing additional information. Homomor- phic encryption (HE) [14] and secure multi-party computation (SMPC) [15] have been widely adopted to secure sensitive in- formation during computation. For instance, CipherGPT [16], EncryptedLLM [17], and EncChain [18] integrate secure ma- trix multiplication or fine-grained access control with FHE to handle data within RAG frameworks securely. More recently, distributed frameworks like FRAG [19] and BumbleBee [20] support secure approximate k-nearest neighbor searches over encrypted vector databases, while PIR-RAG [21] leverages private information retrieval to strictly hide access patterns. However, the substantial computational overhead of these encryption-based methods limits the performance and practi- cality of RAG systems. This challenge arises because LLMs rely heavily on computationally intensive operations, such as inner products and matrix multiplications, which become significantly more complex and time-consuming when per- formed on encrypted data [22], [23]. Consequently, a critical gap remains for methods capable of ensuring strong privacy protection without sacrificing retrieval efficiency or accuracy. Addressing this gap requires novel encryption mechanisms and computational strategies that can efficiently operate on encrypted data, preserving the quality and responsiveness essential for real-world LLM applications. To address these challenges, we present CipheRAG, an efficient vector-multiplicative privacy-preserving RAG frame- work for LLMs. The key aspect is the secure computation of encrypted data through efficient inner product operations, ensuring fast and integral retrieval and generation. First, to ensure privacy protection while enabling efficient retrieval, we present an asymmetric locality-sensitive hashing (ALSH) [24] enhanced inner product functional encryption (IPFE) [25] storage mechanism. By hashing and encrypting knowledge, we keep sensitive information confidential and maintain fast data retrieval. Second, to preserve the integrity of encrypted knowledge and maintain generation performance, we pro- pose a decryption-enabled attention layer that leverages linear “query”, “key”, and “value” weights [26] to transform and decrypt the ciphertext. This partial encryption strategy signifi- cantly reduces the computation overhead over encrypted data. Compared to existing approaches, CipheRAG is preferable because the use of ALSH and IPFE ensures that retrieval remains as efficient and accurate as traditional unencrypted systems, overcoming the major computational limitations of prior encryption-based techniques. This makes CipheRAG particularly well-suited for practical deployment in privacy- sensitive LLM services, where both strong confidentiality and responsiveness are required. Our objectives are twofold: first, to guarantee that all knowledge and its associated embeddings remain encrypted before storage, thereby preventing any unau- thorized access to plaintext data; second, to enable secure, end- to-end computation over ciphertext, so that LLMs can generate responses without ever exposing the underlying knowledge. The main contributions are summarized as follows: • We propose the CipheRAG framework, an efficient vector-multiplicative privacy-preserving RAG framework, which enables both efficient and integral retrieval and generation of encrypted knowledge. • We propose a novel partial encryption strategy for LLMs. In contrast to existing methods that rely on full encryption and hinder computational efficiency, our strategy encrypts input data by IPFE while incorporating a decryption- enabled attention mechanism within the early layers of the LLM. This innovative strategy ensures robust data privacy while maintaining high-generation efficiency. • We conduct a rigorous theoretical analysis to establish the security properties of CipheRAG. Additionally, we perform experiments to demonstrate its efficient retrieval capabilities and its effectiveness in enhancing the perfor- mance of LLMs. The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section II reviews related work. Preliminaries are provided in Section III. The threat model and design goals are introduced in Sec- tion IV, and the proposed framework is detailed in Section V. Theoretical analyses are presented in Section VI. Experimental results are reported in Section VII. Further discussion is presented in Section VIII. Finally, Section IX concludes the paper and discusses the limitations. II. RELATED WORK In this section, we introduce preliminaries about RAG, the privacy risks, and the privacy-preserving RAG system. A. Retrieval-Augmented Generation RAG has emerged as a pivotal technique for enhancing LLM performance by integrating external knowledge sources into the generation process [27]. The foundational work by Lewis et al. (2020) [1] introduced RAG as a method that combines retrieval mechanisms with generative models to improve responses on knowledge-intensive tasks. By lever- aging a retrieval component, RAG enables models to access and incorporate relevant information dynamically, extending their capabilities beyond the static knowledge captured during training. Subsequent research has built upon this framework to address various challenges and expand its applications. For example, Guu et al. (2020) [7] proposed a retrieval-based pre-training approach that enhances open-domain question- answering performance by allowing the model to access a large corpus of text during inference. Similarly, Izacard et al. (2021) [28] developed a retrieval-augmented reader that processes retrieved documents jointly, improving the model’s ability to generate informed and contextually appropriate answers. Despite these advancements, existing RAG systems face significant challenges in securely handling sensitive data. Inte- grating external data sources raises concerns about privacy and data confidentiality, particularly when dealing with personal or proprietary information [29]. Compared to existing works, our research offers a general solution by integrating efficient privacy-preserving retrieval and generation. Privacy Risks of RAG System Although LLMs demonstrate strong performance, they can unintentionally memorize and reveal fragments of training data, underscoring the need to protect sensitive information against membership inference attacks [30]–[32]. Incorporating RAG frameworks introduces additional attack surfaces, as detailed in recent surveys [33]. Malicious queries against proprietary knowledge bases can force the model to disclose restricted content or leverage indirect prompt injections to manipulate outputs [10], [23]. Crucially, recent studies highlight the specific vulnerability of vector embeddings. While often perceived as opaque, em- beddings can be subjected to inversion attacks to reconstruct the original raw text with high fidelity [34]. More alarmingly, Huang et al. (2024) [8] recently demonstrated that such embedding inversion attacks are transferable, posing severe privacy risks even without direct query access to the target model. Furthermore, without integrity checks, RAG systems are susceptible to data poisoning, where adversaries inject malicious documents to corrupt retrieval results [9]. Con- sequently, an ideal defense mechanism must simultaneously address three critical threats: unauthorized leakage, vector inversion, and tampering with the knowledge base. C. Privacy-Preserving RAG System Integrating RAG within LLMs necessitates robust security measures to safeguard sensitive data. Traditional RAG systems often expose vulnerabilities that can lead to unauthorized access or leakage of confidential information [29], [35], [36]. To address these concerns, encryption-based methods have emerged as a key direction. Zhu et al. (2024) [37] introduce a semantic search mechanism designed to run on encrypted data, enabling accurate and privacy-preserving retrieval. Ex- panding on encrypted interaction, Zyskind et al. (2023) [38] employ SMPC to distribute queries securely across a privately constructed database. Similarly, Zhao [19] and Lu et al. (2025) [20] advocate for encrypted approximate k-nearest neighbor searches across dispersed data without compromising confidentiality. Recent advancements have further pushed the boundaries of FHE for LLMs. Hou et al. (2023) [16] present CipherGPT for secure matrix multiplication. Building on this, Encrypt- edLLM [17] and Ruoyan et al. (2025) [39] leverage GPU acceleration to mitigate the heavy computational overhead of FHE-based inference. Furthermore, Bae et al. (2025) [40] propose a privacy-preserving interaction framework combining homomorphically encrypted vector databases with Socratic chain-of-thought reasoning. In addition, Fu et al. (2024) [18] propose EncChain to ensure data remains secure throughout model outputs, while PIR-RAG [21] strengthens security by leveraging private information retrieval to hide access pat- terns. Another type of approach relies on randomization-based methods or data sanitization. DP [13], [41] is widely used to anonymize sensitive information. Yu et al. (2024) [42] and Koga et al. (2025) [43] propose textual DP mechanisms for RAG systems, masking sensitive content while attempting to TABLE I: Qualitative Comparison of Privacy-Preserving RAG Schemes Scheme Year Comp. Complexity (Encrypted Ops) Comm. Cost Retrieval Complexity Privacy Guarantee CipherGPT [16] 2023 High High (Multi-round) Linear O(N) Strong BumbleBee [20] 2025 High High Linear O(N) Strong EncryptedLLM [17] 2025 High High Linear O(N) Strong PIR-RAG [21] 2025 Moderate (linear scan) High Linear O(N) Strong Textual DP [43] 2024 N/A Low Sub-linear (ANN) Moderate (Utility Loss) CipheRAG (Ours) 2025 Low (partial encryption, one-time) Low (One-pass) Sub-linear O(Nρ) Strong Note: N is the database size. maintain reasoning capabilities. Complementary to these algo- rithmic defenses, Zeng et al. (2025) [44] explore a data-centric approach, mitigating privacy issues by substituting sensitive retrieval corpora with high-utility synthetic data. However, as noted by Majmudar et al. (2022) [45], perturbation and sanitization methods can struggle to sustain the quality and coherence of generated text due to the introduced noise or information loss. Table I provides a qualitative comparison between Ci- pheRAG and recent state-of-the-art secure RAG frameworks. While prior works have made progress in protecting re- trieval and inference, their practical deployment is predom- inantly hindered by the high cost of encrypted inference, where full-parameter encryption incurs prohibitive matrix- matrix operations and multi-round interaction overhead. In contrast, CipheRAG fundamentally rethinks the inference-time encryption paradigm by adopting a partial encryption strategy that confines cryptographic computation to lightweight vector- level projections, while allowing the remaining attention and generation processes to be executed in plaintext. This design significantly reduces inference overhead and communication latency, and is complemented by an efficient sub-linear re- trieval mechanism based on Asymmetric LSH. Together, these components enable CipheRAG to achieve scalable and ef- ficient secure inference without sacrificing rigorous privacy guarantees. III. Since x may be a decimal number with up to lD decimal places, we convert it into an integer by multiplying it by 10lD, yielding x′ = 10lD × x. Similarly, if another decimal number y is involved in the computation, we convert it into an integer as y′ = 10lD × y. We then compute the product z = x′ × y′, which corresponds to: z = (10lDx) × (10lDy) = 102lD(xy). (10) To obtain an integer representation of xy with lD decimal places, we need to scale z down by 10lD. However, since division may not be feasible within the integer domain Zp, we perform truncation by decomposing z as: z = z1 · 10lD + z2, (11) where 0 ≤z2 < 10lD. Here, z1 represents the integer part of z divided by 10lD, effectively truncating the last lD digits of z. The final truncated result z1 approximates xy scaled by 10lD, maintaining the desired precision for subsequent computations. IV. THREAT MODEL AND DESIGN GOAL In this section, we present the threat model and design goal. A. Threat Model 1) Adversary Model: The primary threat to the CipheRAG system comes from malicious attackers seeking unauthorized access to the RAG database. These adversaries can potentially exploit vulnerabilities in the system to access all knowledge stored within it, thereby compromising the privacy of knowl- edge providers. Specifically, attackers may obtain sensitive confidential data contributed by knowledge providers, which poses significant risks to privacy and data integrity. 2) KDC Trust Assumptions: We assume that the Key Dis- tribution Center (KDC) operates as a fully trusted authority responsible for issuing subkeys exclusively to authenticated users. It runs within a tamper-resistant and attestable envi- ronment (e.g., HSM or TEE), protecting its internal state, including the master secret key msk, from any unauthorized access or modification. The KDC deterministically executes the subkey-generation algorithm upon receiving valid query identifiers, ensuring correctness and preventing key forgery or duplication. All communication with clients is secured via authenticated and encrypted channels (e.g., mutual TLS). Other system components, such as retrieval nodes, are denied access to the secure enclave and cannot obtain msk, forge subkeys, or decrypt unauthorized inner-product results. B. Design Goal To address the privacy issues in RAG systems, we aim to design a comprehensive privacy-preserving architecture with the following goals: • Encrypted knowledge storage: Ensure that knowledge remains encrypted, preventing unauthorized access to unencrypted copies and preserving data confidentiality. • Confidential knowledge computing: Support secure computation on encrypted data, including retrieval of knowledge and its use in generating outputs by LLMs, thereby maintaining privacy throughout the process. V. DESIGN OF CIPHERAG In this section, we provide the detailed design of CipheRAG. A. Overview of CipheRAG The overview of the CipheRAG is illustrated in Fig. 2. The system features three main phases: the storage phase, the retrieval phase, and the generation phase. Solid lines depict the end-to-end workflow from query to model generation, whereas dotted lines indicate on-chain and off-chain storage; the latter will be described in detail in Fig. 3. In the storage phase, knowledge is stored through a dual approach based on blockchain [48] encompassing both on- chain and off-chain mechanisms. On-chain storage implements the ALSH-based encryption, facilitating secure and efficient knowledge retrieval. Concurrently, an inner product encrypted version of the original data is maintained off-chain. This dual storage methodology ensures privacy protection and efficient access to knowledge. The retrieval phase begins with the processing of a query. A subkey generation mechanism is utilized, which, together with ALSH transformations, facilitates maximum inner prod- uct search within the knowledge-based blockchain. This pro- cess enables CipheRAG to identify and retrieve the most relevant knowledge from the stored data, ensuring that the LLM can access the most pertinent information for gener- ating responses. The retrieval procedure is made verifiable by recording the retrieval inputs/outputs on-chain. The actual similarity computation can be executed by authorized off- chain nodes (e.g., oracles) and the resulting Top-k identifiers are committed to the contract state for public auditability. Clients submit their query q to the contract, which emits a retrieve event. A set of trusted oracle nodes then retrieve over the encrypted index on-chain and write the Top-k list of ciphertext identifiers back into the contract state. This on-chain execution guarantees immutability and public auditability of the exact retrieval result used for subsequent generation. All on-chain data remains encrypted, ensuring that the underly- ing information is never directly exposed. According to the security definition of IPFE, only the inner product values are revealed during computation, while the encrypted knowledge and queries themselves remain confidential. In the generation phase, the retrieved knowledge is fed into a decryption-based attention layer that decrypts the knowledge to generate the final response. This phase integrates the This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing. Fig. 3: The on-chain and off-chain storage of CipheRAG. As illustrated in Fig. 3, the CipheRAG effectively integrates blockchain and the InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) [49] to manage both on-chain and off-chain knowledge storage. • Efficient retrieval enabled on-chain storage, encrypted hashes are generated from sentence embeddings using ALSH, resulting in a lightweight storage solution. These hashes enable efficient retrieval on the blockchain. Once the on-chain retrieval identifies the relevant content, it links to the corresponding off-chain data. • Integrity generation enabled off-chain storage, to- kenized data is processed into word embeddings, en- crypted, and stored in IPFS. This approach ensures data integrity and security while enabling the LLM to generate responses using the complete off-chain content. This dual system achieves lightweight and efficient on-chain retrieval, with secure LLM generation using off-chain data. B. Privacy-preserving Storage with Ada-IPFE To preserve privacy in CipheRAG, knowledge embeddings are encrypted using the Ada-IPFE scheme, a concrete instan- tiation of the general class of IPFE methods. IPFE schemes are particularly well-suited for retrieval-augmented generation systems because they support efficient inner product similarity search, the core retrieval operation, directly on encrypted data. By employing Ada-IPFE, CipheRAG achieves strong privacy protection with significantly lower computational overhead than fully homomorphic encryption and without the accuracy loss associated with DP. In Ada-IPFE, both ciphertexts and decryption keys are associated with vectors, and decryption yields only their inner product, exposing no other information. Specifically, a knowledge vector x ∈Zn N is encrypted to a ciphertext ct; subsequently, an authorized party can obtain ⟨x, y⟩for a query vector y, without learning anything beyond that inner product. During the setup phase, the authority generates an RSA modulus N = ˜p · ˜q, where ˜p and ˜q are safe primes, samples a random generator g ∈Z∗ N2, and selects a master secret vector s ∈Zn N. The public elements are computed as ηi = gsi mod N 2 for i ∈[n]. The master public and secret keys are mpk = (g, {ηi}n i=1, N) , msk = s. In the encryption phase, the knowledge vector x is en- crypted as follows.
