This design provides two important guarantees: (1) A functional decryption key sky is derived using the master secret vector and a chosen attribute vector y ∈Zn N, enabling computation of ⟨x, y⟩and nothing else about x. (2) By encrypting knowledge vectors and enabling their use through weight matrices within neural network models, both privacy and on-chain data availability are achieved. C. Efficient retrieval enabled Preprocessing In CipheRAG, knowledge is processed and stored both on- chain and off-chain within the blockchain infrastructure. On- chain knowledge is primarily used for quick retrieval, whereas off-chain knowledge is used for generation. Consequently, different processing methods are applied. As shown in Fig. 3, we implement distinct embedding strategies for knowledge representation in the off-chain and on-chain phases. For knowledge stored off-chain, we adopt the standard LLM-based embedding process, which includes tokenization followed by contextual word embedding using a pre-trained language model [50]. For the on-chain phase, we follow a structured pipeline to ensure efficient and privacy- preserving retrieval: • Sentence Embedding. Each knowledge document is encoded into a 384-dimensional vector using Sentence- BERT [51]. • ALSH Transformation. To enable inner product-based nearest neighbor search, we apply the ALSH scheme proposed in [24]. Specifically, each knowledge vector x ∈R384 is transformed into P(x). • Hashing and Quantization. After ALSH transformation, we apply random projection with K = 128 hyperplanes to binarize each vector into a 128-bit hash code. This value of K balances retrieval speed and collision proba- bility in our deployment. • Encryption. The binary hash codes are encrypted using the IPFE scheme, which allows exact inner product evaluation without exposing either the plaintext vector or the projection direction. • On-chain Storage. The IPFE-encrypted hash codes are uploaded to the blockchain. 1) ALSH: Efficient knowledge retrieval is crucial, as en- tities with subkeys frequently need to access the blockchain to search for knowledge through inner product computations. To enable efficient knowledge retrieval, we transform the sentence embeddings using L2-norm-based LSH, which maps data points into buckets in a hash table, facilitating fast and approximate nearest-neighbor searches [24]. Specifically, MIPS is utilized for a given knowledge v and query q to identify similarities in the inner product with other knowledge within a specified range. To maintain generality, assume a constant I < 1 exists such that every vector vi ∈S satisfies the condition ∥vi∥2 ≤I. Otherwise, we can rescale the vectors by applying the following transformation: S(v) = V F · x, where F = max vi∈S ∥vi∥2, (13) where F represents the largest L2-norm among all the set S vectors. The transformation S(x) adjusts the vector x to ensure it complies with the norm constraint defined by I. However, direct computation of all relevant inner products for this purpose is impractical. To address this, we imple- ment the ALSH [52] transform, effectively transforming the MIPS challenge into a nearest-neighbor search problem under Euclidean distance. We define the P transformation and Q transformation as follows: P(v) = [v; ∥v∥2 2; ∥v∥22 2 , ..., ∥v∥2m 2 ], (14) Q(q) = [q; 1 2; 1 2, ..., 1 2], (15) Then, the following equation can be obtained, ∥P(v) −Q(q)∥2 2 = (1 + m/4) −2qT v + ∥v∥2m+1 2 = (v −q)2 + (1 2 −∥v∥2 2)2 + (1 2 −∥v∥2m+1 2 )2, (16) where the first term is constant with respect to v (for a fixed q). For the third term, given that each element of v is less than 1, it converges to 0 exponentially as m increases. This leaves us predominantly with the second term, which encompasses the required inner product. arg max v qT v ≈arg min v ∥Q(q) −P(v)∥2. (17) By finding the maximum inner product, we seek the minimum distance, transforming the original problem into a Euclidean nearest-neighbor search between P(v) and Q(q). Once the ALSH transformation maps both the knowledge vector v and the query vector q into a common Euclidean space via P(v) and Q(q), we further discretize the transformed space by applying random hyperplane hashing: h(v) = sign(rT P(v)), r ∼N(0, I), (18) h(q) = sign(rT Q(q)). (19) Each random projection r yields one bit of the binary hash code, and the concatenation of K such bits forms the final signature: H(v) = [h1(v), h2(v), ..., hK(v)] ∈{−1, +1}K, (20) H(q) = [h1(q), h2(q), ..., hK(q)] ∈{−1, +1}K. (21) To preserve privacy, we encrypt each hashed signature H(v) using IPFE. Let H(v) be treated as a binary vector in RK where entries are in {−1, +1}, we compute: Enc(H(v)). These encrypted hash codes are stored on-chain as secure bucket representations, without revealing the original vector or its transformed form. At query time, the client computes the cleartext signature H(q) based on the public projection vectors {ri} and constructs a decryption key using the IPFE scheme: This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing. Since H(v), H(q) ∈{−1, +1}K, the inner product s represents the number of matching bits (up to an affine transformation), i.e., s = PK i=1 Hi(v)·Hi(q). After computing the similarity scores s for all candidate vectors, the top-k highest similarity vectors are selected for retrieval. 2) Integrity-Verifiable On-Chain Retrieval: To prevent a malicious node from omitting or substituting retrieval re- sults, we implement the entire Top-k selection pipeline as an on-chain smart-contract call. The client computes the query hash signature H(q) off-chain and locally generates the corresponding IPFE decryption key Keyq. It then invokes the contract’s function: retrieve(queryId, skq) which, in a single atomic transaction, loads each encrypted bucket signature Enc(H(vi)) from the on-chain index and computes the inner product score si via IPFE decryption. These scores are used to determine the Top-k most similar entries. The resulting list of Top-k ciphertext identifiers is written to the contract’s retrievals[queryId] mapping and emitted via a Retrieved event. To provide auditability, every hash signature decryption operation is immutably recorded on- chain. Any unauthorized modification, omission, or substi- tution is immediately detectable, and external auditors can independently verify the correctness and consistency of the retrieval results by invoking the following query: getRetrieval(queryId) →bytes32[] cList. This independent recomputation and verification capability ensures the verifiability, transparency, and accountability of the entire retrieval pipeline, thereby safeguarding against integrity attacks and malicious manipulations. D. Generation with Decryption-Based Self-Attention Module In this subsection, we introduce a decryption-based attention layer that integrates the decryption of off-chain encrypted knowledge embedding x directly into the attention mechanism of the LLMs. Instead of using a separate linear transformation layer for decryption, we leverage the parameters of the Query- Key-Value (QKV) linear operations [26] within the attention layers to generate the decryption keys. This approach ensures the secure integration of encrypted knowledge into the model’s computations, naturally embedding the decryption process into the LLM architecture. 1) Integration into the Attention Mechanism: The attention mechanism in LLMs projects the input embedding x ∈Rn×1 and positional encoding pe ∈Rn×1 into Query, Key, and Value vectors via learned weight matrices:   Q K V  = W(x + pe) = Wx + Wpe, (22) where W ∈R3d′×n is formed by vertically stacking the Q, K, and V weights. In our privacy-preserving design, the embedding x is stored in encrypted form using Ada-IPFE. Algorithm 1: Encrypted Hash-Based Knowledge Re- trieval. VI. PROPERTY ANALYSIS In this section, we provide a theoretical analysis of the security of CipheRAG. A. Adaptive Security of the Ada-IPFE Scheme 1) Security notation: We formalize the simulation-based adaptive security experiment for Ada-IPFE as follows. Definition 1 (Challenge-response Simulation-Based Secu- rity for Ada-IPFE). The Ada-IPFE scheme is adaptively simulation-based secure (in the sense of IND-FE) if for any PPT adversary A, the advantage in the following experiment is negligible: Setup: The challenger runs the setup algorithm and gives the public parameters to A. Key Queries: A may adaptively query secret keys sky for any legal y. Challenge: A submits (z[0], z[1]), and receives the encryp- tion of z[b] for random b. Restriction: All key queries must satisfy ⟨z[0] −z[1], y⟩= 0. Guess: A outputs a bit b′; its advantage is Pr[b′ = b] −1 2 . The scheme is secure if this advantage is negligible. 2) Game-based analysis: We now prove that the Ada- IPFE construction achieves the above challenge-response simulation-based security. The proof proceeds via a sequence of games. Theorem 1. The Ada-IPFE scheme is adaptively secure under the DCR assumption. Proof: Let A be a probabilistic polynomial-time adver- sary interacting with the scheme. We describe a sequence of games to analyze the advantage of A. Game 0. The challenger runs the setup algorithm to produce the public parameters pp = (g, {ηi}n i=1, N, Z), which are given to A, and a master secret key s ∈Zn N, which remains private. During the challenge phase, A is permitted to specify a pair of vectors z[0], z[1] ∈Zn N such that ∥z[0]∥, ∥z[1]∥≤Z, where Z is the allowed norm bound. The challenger selects a uniform random bit b ∈{0, 1} and responds with the encryption of z[b], denoted ct∗. At any point prior to or after the challenge, A may issue secret key requests for function vectors y ∈Zn N, subject to the restriction that all queries satisfy the orthogonality property: ⟨z[0] −z[1], y⟩= 0, (27) with the inner product computed over the integers. This condition precludes trivial separation of the challenge bit via function queries aligned with the difference of the target messages. Ultimately, A outputs a guess b′ ∈{0, 1} for the challenge bit. The experiment is said to be won if b′ = b. The adversary’s distinguishing advantage is defined as Pr[b′ = b] −1 2 . (28) Game 1. The procedure follows Game 0, except that the encryption of the challenge vector z[b] is randomized at its core group element. Specifically, the challenger samples a random κ0 ←Z∗ N, and computes κ = κN 0 mod N 2. Then, the key ciphertext components are formed as: ct∗ 1 = κ2 mod N 2, ct∗ 4 = (ct∗ 1)α mod N 2, ct∗ 5,i = (ct∗ 1)si(1 + Nσz[b] i ) mod N 2, ∀i ∈[n], (29) This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing. Restrictions apply. 10 where α and si are as in msk, and σ is fresh randomness. All other parts of the ciphertext (such as ct∗ 0, ct∗ 2, ct∗ 3) are computed as in the real scheme. Since ct∗ 1 is now uniform in the 2N-th residue subgroup, the resulting ciphertext has distribution statistically close to Game 0: | Pr[S1] −Pr[S0]| ≤1 2λ . (30) Game 2. In this game, we further alter the encryption: now the core element κ used for ct∗ 1 is not of the special form κN 0 , but is simply chosen uniformly at random from Z∗ N 2: ct∗ 1 = κ2 mod N 2, where κ ←Z∗ N2. The remaining ciphertext components are generated just as in Game 1, using this κ. Under the DCR assumption, the challenge ciphertext distri- bution in Game 2 is computationally indistinguishable from Game 1: | Pr[S2] −Pr[S1]| ≤AdvDCR A (λ). (31) Game 3. In this game, we show that the adversary’s view, including all previously queried keys and the simulated chal- lenge ciphertext, is statistically independent of the challenge bit b ∈{0, 1}. For every i ∈[n], the i-th ciphertext component is: ct(b) i = ηp i (1 + σN)(z(b) i + apsi) mod N 2, (32) where p is a fresh random exponent, σ and ap are uniformly random, and si is the i-th entry of the secret vector s. The challenge message is z(b) ∈Zn, indexed by the hidden bit b. To analyze the statistical hiding, we define: δ = 1 d �z(1) −z(0) , d = gcd(z(1) 1 −z(0) 1 , . . . , z(1) n −z(0) n ), Lz = {y ∈Zn | ⟨z, y⟩= 0} . (33) Any legal key query y by the adversary must satisfy ⟨z, y⟩= 0, so all possible queries are confined to the lattice Lx. We note that, under the restriction ⟨z(1) −z(0), y⟩= 0, all valid key queries y lie in the nullspace of the difference vector, which forms an (n −1)-dimensional subspace of Zn N. We denote a basis for this subspace by {u1, ..., un−1}, and stack them as the rows of a matrix U ∈Z(n−1)×n N . Given this structure, the information that the adversary can obtain about the secret s through any valid queries is determined solely by the vector U · sT ∈Zn−1 N . Note that for any ciphertext component δ(b) = z(b)+aps (with ap uniformly random), we have U · (δ(b))T mod N = U · (z(b))T + ap · U · sT mod N. (34) Since ap is uniformly random and invertible in ZN, for any fixed U · sT the adversary’s view of δ(b) is statistically independent of b. Consequently, the conditional distribution of δ(b) given all obtainable information is uniform for either value of b, and no information about the challenge bit can be learned. Finally, for any efficiently computable function f, the sta- tistical distance between the adversary’s view when b = 0 and b = 1 is at most 1 2λ−1 . Together with the DCR assumption, we have: Pr[S0] −1 2 ≤AdvDCR A (λ) + 1 2λ−1 . (35) This concludes that the adversary’s distinguishing advantage is negligible, even after making arbitrary adaptive key queries. Thus, under the DCR assumption, the Ada-IPFE scheme is adaptively secure. B. Correctness of the Ada-IPFE Scheme We demonstrate the correctness of the proposed Ada-IPFE scheme, namely that the decryption of a ciphertext under an honestly generated functional key always yields the correct inner product ⟨z, y⟩over ZN. Theorem 2. The Ada-IPFE scheme instantiated above is correct: for all message vectors z and key vectors y, the decryption procedure yields τ2 = ⟨z, y⟩. Proof: Suppose the encryption and decryption algorithms are executed as specified before. Let the ciphertext of z be ct = (ct0, ct1, ct2, ct3, ct4, {cti}n i=1) and the decryption key for y be sky = (β, sy). According to the scheme: τ1 = ((ct2 · gct3)β·a−1 mod N mod N 2) mod N, τ2 =  ct(β−sy) mod N 1 ct4 Qn i=1 ctyi i τ −1 1 −1  mod N 2 N . (36) Expanding the above terms, we note that cti = ηr i (1 + Nσzi) mod N 2 and ηi = gsi, so n Y i=1 ctyi i = gr⟨s,y⟩· n Y i=1 (1 + Nσzi)yi mod N 2. (37) By appropriately combining all relevant terms in Equ. (12) and exploiting the scheme’s homomorphic properties, the decryption process cancels all blinding terms and randomizers. Thus, we obtain: τ2 = (1 + N⟨z, y⟩−1) mod N 2 N = ⟨z, y⟩. (38) Furthermore, by the norm bound assumption ∥z∥ ≤ Z, ∥y∥≤Y, ZY < N, we guarantee that ⟨z, y⟩< N, so no modular overflow occurs. The Ada-IPFE scheme thus correctly outputs the intended inner product for all valid inputs and keys. C. Standard Consistency Constraint of Ada-IPFE The standard consistency constraint requires that a cipher- text encrypted under a public key pky can only be correctly decrypted by the matching secret key sky, and not by a secret key generated for any other vector ˆy ̸= y. Theorem 3. The Ada-IPFE scheme satisfies the standard consistency constraint. Proof: Suppose, for contradiction, that there exist two distinct decryption keys sky and skˆy (generated for vectors This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing. Recall the key generation process: for vector y, sky = (β, sy), sy = ⟨s, y⟩+ α + β, (39) with public key pky = (gα mod N 2, gβ mod N 2), and for ˆy, skˆy = (ˆβ, sˆy), sˆy = ⟨s, ˆy⟩+ ˆα + ˆβ, (40) with public key pkˆy = (g ˆα mod N 2, g ˆβ mod N 2), where (ˆα, ˆβ) ̸= (α, β). The ciphertext for z under pky is constructed as Equ. (12). If skˆy could decrypt ctz, the decryption equations yield: ˆτ1 =  (ct2 · gct3) ˆβ · ct−ˆβ 0 mod N 2−1 mod N, ˆτ2 =  ct( ˆβ−sˆy) mod N 1 ct4 Qn i=1 ctˆyi i · ˆω−1 1 −1  mod N 2 N . (41) Working out the algebra, it follows that for successful decryption, both g ˆα−α = 1 and gβ−ˆβ = 1 must hold modulo N 2, which (since g is a generator) implies ˆα = α and ˆβ = β. But by construction, (ˆα, ˆβ) ̸= (α, β) for distinct key queries. Therefore, the ciphertext ctz under pky can only be decrypted with sky, not with skˆy. Hence, the Ada-IPFE scheme satisfies the standard consistency constraint. D. Master Secret Key Hiding of Ada-IPFE A crucial security requirement for functional encryption schemes is that the master secret key must remain information- theoretically hidden, even under adaptive key queries. In our Ada-IPFE construction, the msk is the vector s = (s1, . . . , sn). Theorem 4. The Ada-IPFE scheme achieves master secret key hiding. Proof: Suppose an adversary A adaptively issues l func- tion key queries for vectors y(1), . . . , y(l) and receives the corresponding secret keys sky(j) = ⟨s, y(j)⟩+ α(j) + β(j), ∀j ∈[l], (42) where α(j), β(j) are randomly chosen in each query. To recover s, A would need to solve the system of equations:        ⟨s, y(1)⟩+ α(1) + β(1) = sk(1), ... ⟨s, y(l)⟩+ α(l) + β(l) = sk(l), (43) Given that each (α(j), β(j)) is independently and uniformly distributed, the probability of correctly recovering s is at most N −2l, which is negligible for large modulus N. Brute-force matching of ηi is similarly infeasible. Thus, the Ada-IPFE scheme provides master secret key hiding. E. Encrypted Vector Hiding of Ada-IPFE Besides hiding the secret key, our scheme ensures that the encrypted message vector z cannot be recovered, even if the adversary obtains inner products ⟨z, y(1)⟩, . . . , ⟨z, y(n)⟩for linearly independent y(j). Theorem 5. The Ada-IPFE scheme achieves encrypted vector hiding. Proof: By construction, each ciphertext of z can only be decrypted with respect to one specific function key. That is, for any y, the decryption algorithm yields only ⟨z, y⟩; it does not expose additional linear relations that would enable recovery of the entire z. The randomness in the encryption process ensures that no adversary, even given n linearly independent key queries, can solve for z except with negligible probability. Thus, Ada-IPFE achieves encrypted vector hiding. VII. EXPERIMENTS We evaluate the performance of CipheRAG in terms of the retrieval and generation phases, respectively. A. Experimental Setup We use PyTorch to implement CipheRAG and evaluate it on an Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-14900F running at 2.00GHz, 64GB of RAM, and a Geforce RTX 4090 GPU. In this experiment, we leverage Ganache and Truffle1 tools for deploying and testing smart contracts while utilizing the IPFS for decentralized file storage. We chose the number of hash functions as k = 512 and the number of hash tables as L = 15. We set m = 3 and U = 0.83, following the parameters referenced by [52]. Datasets. We conduct experiments on two widely-used QA benchmarks: (1) Amnesty QA2 is a fact-based dataset derived from Amnesty International’s reports, focusing on grounded answers to global human rights issues; (2) Natural Questions (NQ) [53] consists of real user queries from Google Search, paired with answers grounded in Wikipedia passages. Both datasets are designed to evaluate the factuality and grounding of open-domain QA systems. Metric. We used response relevancy, faithfulness, and cor- rectness as metrics to measure the ability to generate retrieval augmentation for LLM. • Response Relevancy: Evaluates the semantic alignment between the generated answer and the original question by computing the cosine similarity between their embed- dings. Higher scores indicate more relevant and focused responses. • Faithfulness: Measures factual consistency by assessing the proportion of generated claims that are supported by retrieved context. Scores range from 0 to 1, with higher values indicating stronger adherence to source evidence. • Correctness: Assesses factual accuracy by comparing generated responses against reference answers using nat- ural language inference. Precision, recall, and F1 scores are computed to reflect factual overlap. 1https://archive.trufflesuite.com/ganache/ 2https://www.amnesty.org/en/research/ This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing. KDE provides a smooth representation of data distribution, though the smoothing effect may extend beyond actual data boundaries. This boundary effect is expected and does not indicate the existence of data beyond these limits. Model. We evaluate three representative LLMs: Llama3 [54] is Meta’s state-of-the-art Transformer for efficient text and multimodal generation; Qwen2 [55] is Alibaba DAMO Academy’s high-precision multilingual LLM; GPT-2 [56] is OpenAI’s autoregressive model that set the benchmark for coherent text synthesis. Baseline. This experiment presents an evaluation involv- ing comparisons with advanced LLMs and state-of-the-art encryption-based LLM frameworks in the inference phase. Retrieval Methods: Our evaluation compares three canonical RAG pipelines—Vanilla RAG, BGE-RAG, and FiD—against our ALSH scheme. • Vanilla RAG [1]: employs BM25 sparse retrieval over a document corpus, followed by an autoregressive genera- tor to synthesize answers. • BGE-RAG [57]: uses BGE dense text embeddings with FAISS–based approximate nearest neighbor search to retrieve semantically relevant passages. • FiD [28]: retrieves top-k passages via BM25 and fuses them jointly in a Fusion-in-Decoder architecture for en- hanced answer generation. Encryption-based Methods: We compare our approach with leading encryption-based methods, the Bumblebee and Ci- pherGPT, to demonstrate superior data security. • BumbleBee [20] is a secure two-party inference frame- work designed for large transformer models. It utilizes Oblivious Transfer (OT) [58] to enable secure model inference while preserving privacy. BumbleBee is par- ticularly optimized for transformer-based architectures, ensuring that sensitive data remains encrypted throughout the computation process, but it incurs significant commu- nication and computational overhead due to the multiple interactive rounds required between parties. • CipherGPT [16] is a secure two-party inference frame- work tailored for GPT-like language models. It leverages FHE to enable privacy-preserving computations directly on encrypted data. CipherGPT excels in scenarios re- quiring high levels of data confidentiality but suffers from high computational complexity due to the extensive overhead associated with FHE-based operations. B. Experiments for the efficiency The experimental results in Table III provide a compara- tive analysis of the computational overheads associated with standard IPFE and Ada-IPFE. Overall, we observe that Ada- IPFE incurs a moderate increase in average runtime, typically between 1.2× and 1.5× the cost of IPFE, across both the generation and retrieval phases. This increase primarily results from the difference in constant factors inherent to the two cryp- tographic constructions; however, both schemes exhibit similar asymptotic complexity with respect to the vector dimension. In practice, the runtime gap does not grow significantly as the embedding dimension increases, since the dominant cost per operation remains linear in vector size for both schemes. The retrieval phase leverages L2-norm-based hashing to enable efficient similarity matching, while the generation phase prioritizes complete security and data fidelity, resulting in higher latency. These results confirm that, despite the additional computational cost of Ada-IPFE, its performance remains practical for latency-sensitive applications, such as secure healthcare dialogue, financial services, and interactive customer support. The modest overhead is a reasonable trade- off for the improved security guarantees and resistance to linearity attacks offered by Ada-IPFE. TABLE III: Average runtime in generation and retrieval Operation IPFE Ada-IPFE Retrieval Generation Retrieval Generation Master Key generation 59.7 ms 1.91 s 89.6 ms 2.75 s Encryption 62.3 ms 1.97 s 97.5 ms 3.06 s Subkey generation 0.143 ms 4.06 ms 0.246 ms 6.67 ms Discrete logarithms 17.2 ms 0.477 s 25.8 ms 0.802 s Table IV compares our proposed approach with ALSH-RAG (non-encrypted), BumbleBee [20] (OT-based) and CipherGPT [16] (FHE-based) in terms of total generation time, while Table V highlights the QKV computation overhead within these frameworks. Both BumbleBee and CipherGPT represent significant advancements in secure model inference, leveraging strong cryptographic guarantees. However, these guarantees come at the cost of substantial computation and communi- cation overhead, particularly during large-scale matrix multi- plications involved in QKV calculations, which dominate the overall token generation process. This level of performance gain, achieved without relying on FHE or expensive secure protocols, positions CipheRAG as a practical alternative for real-time privacy-preserving inference in domains where ef- ficiency and scalability are prioritized over theoretical cryp- tographic guarantees. For instance, edge-deployed intelligent agents or cloud-based AI APIs could integrate CipheRAG to deliver secure yet performant user experiences under moderate trust assumptions. In contrast, our method adopts a partial encryption strategy that selectively decrypts data during the QKV vector calcula- tion. This approach avoids performing all operations in the ci- phertext domain, instead limiting cryptographic computations to vector-vector multiplications. By reducing the burden of fully encrypted matrix multiplications, our approach achieves significant efficiency gains. For example, generating one token with GPT2 requires 162.3s in BumbleBee and 246.1s in CipherGPT, whereas our method completes the same task in just 6.92s, yielding approximately 23× and 35× speedups, respectively. Similarly, for GPT2, CipheRAG achieves a 23× and 36× speedup over BumbleBee and CipherGPT, respec- tively. For Qwen2, the acceleration is 10× and 12×, while for Llama3, the gains are 11× and 12× compared to BumbleBee and CipherGPT, respectively. The QKV computation presented in Table V demonstrates similar trends, with our method achieving up to 15× faster results compared to CipherGPT. Experiments for Retrieval Augmented Generation Fig. 5 illustrates the performance of CipheRAG, Llama3, and Qwen2 across faithfulness, relevancy, and correctness scores. The distribution curves reveal notable differences in how these models handle various aspects of response qual- ity. CipheRAG consistently outperforms the other models, as indicated by its sharp peaks around 1.0 for all three metrics, demonstrating faithful, relevant, and correct outputs. In contrast, Llama3 has broader distributions around lower values, reflecting significant variability and lower reliability in maintaining faithfulness, relevancy, and correctness. Qwen2 performs slightly better than Llama3 but still falls short of CipheRAG’s consistency, particularly in terms of faithfulness and correctness, with scores generally closer to zero. Over- all, CipheRAG provides the most reliable performance, with consistently high-quality outputs across all metrics. Fig. 6 presents a side-by-side comparison of four RAG pipelines with Llama3, Vanilla-RAG [1], BGE [57], FiD [28], and our proposed CipheRAG, evaluated on Hit@10 recall, response relevancy, faithfulness, and answer correctness. The ALSH variant attains a Hit@10 of 0.96, nearly matching the BGE and FiD baselines (0.99 each) while vastly out- performing the Vanilla system (0.55). In terms of response relevancy, ALSH reaches 0.90, only marginally below BGE (0.94) and substantially above FiD (0.83) and Vanilla (0.87). For faithfulness and answer correctness, ALSH records 0.92 and 0.88 respectively, once again closely trailing BGE (0.94 and 0.89) and exceeding FiD (0.75 and 0.78). These results demonstrate that our ALSH-enabled encrypted retrieval pre- serves generative quality on par with state-of-the-art RAG methods, thereby validating its effectiveness for secure, high- fidelity knowledge integration. The consistently high scores across faithfulness, relevancy, and correctness metrics suggest that CipheRAG is well-equipped for knowledge-intensive gen- eration scenarios, such as legal content drafting, clinical guide- line summarization, and enterprise-level technical support. To maximize performance, it is essential to maintain a well- structured and semantically coherent encrypted knowledge base. This highlights the role of careful curation in enabling robust and accurate knowledge retrieval within CipheRAG’s framework, without introducing significant vulnerability to content misalignment. D. Ablation study According to the recommendations of NIST [59], κ rep- resents the symmetric security parameter, and ζ denotes the public-key security parameter. These parameters are spec- ified within the ranges κ ∈ {80, 112, 128} and ζ ∈ {1024, 2048, 3072}, corresponding to legacy security (up to 2010), medium-term security (2011–2030), and long-term security (beyond 2030), respectively. For this study, we selected κ = 112 and ζ = 2048 to align with medium-term security requirements and to demonstrate why the Ada-IPFE method was chosen for the decryption- enabled attention layer rather than HE-based or OT-based approaches. The rationale for this choice is outlined as follows: Firstly, with the Ada-IPFE method, knowledge providers can securely upload encrypted knowledge to a public database without requiring any interaction with the knowledge users or the LLM for decryption. This independence significantly reduces the complexity of the deployment process and en- hances scalability. In contrast, both HE- and OT-based methods involve one or multiple rounds of communication between the knowledge provider and user to perform decryption or com- putation securely, introducing additional latency and potential bottlenecks. TABLE VI: Theoretical complexity Crypotography Method Complexity HE O(n2 log n) OT O(nb2 + n log n + m) Ada-IPFE O(nlog2 7) Secondly, as illustrated in Fig. 8, the execution time of the HE scheme increases significantly with the vector dimension, which matches the theoretical analysis in Table VI. This rise is due to the need for n homomorphic multiplications and n −1 homomorphic additions during the calculation. In contrast, the Ada-IPFE method demonstrates remarkably stable and low execution times, remaining unaffected by increasing vector dimensions, which highlights its high efficiency. While OT also maintains relatively fast execution times with only a slight upward trend, its efficiency is primarily limited by commu- nication overhead. As shown in Table VII, the communica- tion overhead for QKV computation across different models is significantly lower when using the Ada-IPFE approach. This efficiency stems from Ada-IPFE’s streamlined design, requiring only one-way encrypted uploads from the knowledge This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing. In contrast, the HE-based method requires the transmission of significantly larger ciphertexts due to the full HE operations, while OT-based methods involve multiple rounds of interactive communication between parties. These additional communi- cation requirements result in considerably higher overhead for both HE and OT when compared to Ada-IPFE. While Ada-IPFE offers significantly lower communication overhead compared to HE and OT schemes, its security model assumes trustworthy key distribution and protected query issuance. For most real-world applications, including healthcare, finance, and enterprise deployments, this assumption is practically reasonable and aligns with moderate trust environments. How- ever, in scenarios with highly adversarial threat models, such as military-grade or zero-trust infrastructures, stronger cryp- tographic guarantees (e.g., full semantic security) may be required. In such cases, FHE-based protocols remain viable alternatives, albeit with higher computational costs. In Fig. 7, we simultaneously stress-test three axes of noise, corpus contamination (the fraction of unrelated documents injected into the index: 0 %, 20 %, and 50 %), query drop (the proportion of true query tokens randomly omitted: 0 %, 20 %, and 40 %), and query add (the fraction of spurious tokens inserted into the query: 0 % and 20 %). Across all contamination levels, the curves for add = 0 % and add = 20 % nearly coincide, indicating that inserting one extra token for every five genuine ones has virtually no effect on hit@1. Even under the extreme condition of dropping 40 % of query tokens, an unusually severe perturbation, recall declines by only about 12 %, a degradation that remains well within acceptable bounds. Finally, raising the contamination ratio from 0 % to 50 % (i.e. adding one irrelevant document for every two relevant ones) causes only a marginal performance drop. Together, these results demonstrate that our CipheRAG is remarkably robust: it tolerates heavy token deletion, mod- erate token insertion, and substantial corpus “chaff” without catastrophic loss of retrieval accuracy. In conclusion, CipheRAG adopts Ada-IPFE for encryption due to its efficiency, low communication overhead, and scal- ability. Unlike HE and OT, which require complex interac- tions and incur significant costs, Ada-IPFE enables one-way encrypted uploads, simplifying deployment while maintaining robust privacy protection. Its low computational complexity and stable performance across vector dimensions make it ideal for real-time and large-scale applications. VIII. DISCUSSION Our proposed framework not only enhances the security of RAG systems but also demonstrates strong practical util- ity across privacy-sensitive domains such as healthcare and finance. CipheRAG enables efficient and privacy-preserving access to encrypted knowledge, supporting regulatory com- pliance in settings such as HIPAA-constrained medical envi- ronments. Experiments confirm that our design achieves up to 35× speedups in token generation and significantly lower com- munication overhead compared to FHE or OT-based schemes. Moreover, CipheRAG’s blockchain-based design promotes de- centralized and tamper-evident knowledge sharing, ensuring transparency and auditability in collaborative scenarios. This property is particularly valuable in multi-party workflows such as federated healthcare or cross-institutional research. By balancing privacy, efficiency, and usability, CipheRAG offers a viable direction for deploying secure AI systems in real- world settings. Its empirical performance and architectural flexibility suggest promising avenues for standardization and policy development in privacy-preserving generative AI. IX. CONCLUSION In this paper, we presented CipheRAG, an efficient vector- multiplicative privacy-preserving RAG framework for large language models. Our framework tackles key challenges in se- cure knowledge retrieval and generation by combining ALSH with Ada-IPFE, enabling both privacy and performance. The proposed decryption-enabled attention mechanism further al- lows encrypted knowledge to be seamlessly incorporated into the language model’s generation process without compro- mising efficiency. Comprehensive experiments confirm that CipheRAG achieves substantial gains in computational speed while preserving strong privacy guarantees, offering a practical balance suitable for real-world applications. Despite these contributions, we acknowledge certain lim- itations regarding the threat model. While CipheRAG mini- mizes unintended data exposure by ensuring that only nec- essary decryption results are revealed, potential risks remain in scenarios where the LLM provider is not fully trusted. Specifically, adversaries could potentially infer original input information from intermediate states such as QKV vectors or the decrypted outputs of linear layers. To address this, future work may integrate existing privacy-enhancing techniques, such as adversarial training or differential privacy [60], [61], to process these initial layers. These measures would ensure that intermediate outputs cannot be exploited to recover sensitive data, thereby further reinforcing the system against privacy leakage.

text: Received 18 October 2024, accepted 5 November 2024, date of publication 11 November 2024, date of current version 21 November 2024. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2024.3495694 Emotional Text-To-Speech in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset MUJAHID JAMAL A. KHALIFAH , MICHAL PTASZYNSKI , (Senior Member, IEEE), AND FUMITO MASUI Text Information Processing Laboratory, Kitami Institute of Technology, Kitami 090-0015, Japan Corresponding authors: Mujahid Jamal A. Khalifah (mjk321@hotmail.com), Michal Ptaszynski (michal@mail.kitami-it.ac.jp), and Fumito Masui (f-masui@mail.kitami-it.ac.jp) ABSTRACT This study explores the feasibility of using artificial emotional speech datasets generated by existing artificial voice-generating software as an alternative to human-generated datasets for emotional speech synthesis. Focusing on the Japanese language, we assess the viability of these artificial datasets in languages with limited emotional speech resources. Our approach combines qualitative and quantitative analyses to evaluate the effectiveness of synthetic emotional speech in replicating human-like emotional expression. The results demonstrate that while artificial datasets can approximate certain emotional states, there are significant limitations in replicating the full range of human emotions, particularly in subtle or mixed emotions. These findings underscore the potential and current constraints of using artificial datasets in emotional speech synthesis, suggesting avenues for future research to enhance the quality and emotional expressiveness of synthetic speech. INDEX TERMS Emotional text-to-speech (TTS), artificially augmented dataset, Japanese speech synthesis, emotional speech synthesis (ESS), Tacotron 2, Voiceroid 2, mel-cepstrum distortion (MCD), band aperiodicity distortion (BAPD), mean opinion score (MOS), naturalness and intelligibility, emotional dataset creation, signal-to-distortion ratio (SDR), F0 voiced error (FVE), speech synthesis quality evaluation, synthetic speech evaluation. I. INTRODUCTION The focus of speech synthesis research, or the research aiming at the artificial generation of human-like speech by computers, has long been on enhancing the naturalness and realism of the generated speech. A notable subset of this research is Emotional Speech Synthesis (ESS) (also known as Emotional Text-to-Speech, or Emotional TTS), which focuses on enhancing synthesized speech with emotions, contributing to more expressive and engaging human- computer interactions. Emotions in speech play a crucial role in conveying nuanced information beyond explicit linguistic content, making interactions more relatable and effective [1]. Natural human speech comprises two main components: the linguistic component, which conveys the explicit mes- sage, and the para-linguistic component, which add depth, The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Maria Chiara Caschera . context, and emotional texture to the explicit linguistic layer through intonation, prosody, and various vocal cues [2]. These vocal cues convey feelings, attitudes, and intentions that enrich the understanding of spoken language [3], [4]. Efforts to infuse synthesized speech with emotions aim to bridge the gap between machine-generated speech and gen- uine human expression, which has the potential to improve the acceptability of speech-based applications across various domains, including virtual assistants, automated customer service agents, entertainment, and assistive technologies [5], [6], [7]. Despite the remarkable progress made in the realm of Emotional Speech Synthesis, significant challenges still remain. One such challenge is the scarcity of comprehensive, robust large-scale emotional speech datasets, particularly in languages other than English, such as Japanese. The development of emotionally expressive speech synthesis systems requires substantial amounts of high-quality data 167724 2024 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ VOLUME 12, 2024 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset encompassing a wide spectrum of emotional states, linguistic contexts, and speakers. For some languages, the availability of suitable datasets remains limited, hindering research progress [8], [9]. In this study, we investigate the feasibility of utilizing an artificial emotional speech dataset generated by readily avail- able voice-generating software, specifically, Voiceroid 2,1 as an alternative to deriving datasets directly from human speakers. Our objective is to ascertain whether such datasets offer a viable solution for emotional speech synthesis in languages that encounter limitations in terms of available emotional speech data. This approach could solve, or at least partially mitigate the problem of limited datasets and make it easier for future researchers of emotional speech synthesis to gather their required datasets. To assess the quality of the synthesized speech, we con- ducted a comprehensive evaluation using both a wide suite of objective evaluation metrics as well as a popularly used subjective Mean Opinion Score (MOS). Objective evaluation metrics, including Mel-Cepstrum Distortion (MCD) [10], [11], [12], [13], Band Aperiodicity Distortion (BAPD) [12], [13], F0 Voiced Error (FVE) [12], Signal-to-Distortion Ratio (SDR) [14], and Spectral Distortion (SD) [14], provided quantitative measures of synthesis quality. Additionally, the MOS survey gathered subjective evaluations from partici- pants to gauge the naturalness, intelligibility, and perceived emotions in the synthesized speech. The MOS survey was distributed in batches to a diverse group of participants, predominantly Japanese, to prevent duplicate responses and ensure balanced representation. The survey results revealed detailed insights into the perceived quality of speech synthesis systems across various dimen- sions, including emotional content, synthesis source, and sentence uniqueness. The findings of our experiments, in which we trained Tacotron 2, a generic speech synthesis model, with the Voiceroid 2 dataset, demonstrate the potential for generating emotionally expressive speech. Although the speech gener- ated with Tacotron 2 fell short of matching the naturalness and intelligibility of Voiceroid 2’s original synthesized outputs, the results were comparable and promising. The performance gap suggests the need for further refinement in training techniques, dataset representation, and model architecture to enhance synthesis quality. Notably, sentences where the text and voice represented the same emotion (emotionally congruent sentences, will be categorized as ‘‘unique’’ sen- tences in the following sections) were rated higher in quality compared to sentences where the text and voice represented different emotions (emotionally incongruent sentences, will be categorized as ‘‘shared’’ sentences in the following sections). This finding highlights the importance of emotional alignment in speech synthesis. This study aims to contribute to the field of emotional speech synthesis by addressing the challenges of limited 1https://www.ah-soft.net/shopbrand/ct92/ datasets in non-English languages and providing insights into the effectiveness of artificially augmented datasets. Our results emphasize the necessity for further research to improve the emotional fidelity and overall quality of synthesized speech, ultimately advancing the development of more expressive and engaging human-computer interactions. The structure of this paper is as follows: Section II offers a review of the pertinent literature that forms the foundation of our study. Section III briefly explains the architecture of Tacotron 2, the main tool used in the study. In Section IV, we elaborate on the evaluation metrics employed to gauge the quality of the synthesized output. Section V provides an overview of the preliminary study, outlining the initial steps taken in preparation for the subsequent experimentation. Subsequently, in Section VI, we detail the methodology used for acquiring the artificial dataset. Section VII to section XV constitutes the core of this paper, detailing the principal experiment conducted, as well as presenting and analyzing the obtained results. Finally, our conclusions are presented in the closing section. II. RELATED WORK Over the years, speech synthesis has evolved significantly, beginning with rule-based systems [15], which allowed for controlled synthesis outputs. This was followed by advancements in statistical parametric models [16] and emotional database creation efforts like those of [17] and [18], which laid the groundwork for more expressive speech synthesis. Later studies, such as [19] and [20], contributed to the refinement of emotional speech synthesis models, focusing on improving emotional nuance and fidelity. For example, some studies have investigated blending databases for emotional speech synthesis [21], generating emotions in synthetic speech using rule-based approaches [22], and creating emotional speech databases specifically for training emotional speech synthesis models [23]. In recent years, the field of speech data augmentation has gained attention as a means to improve speech synthesis models, especially in cases where data scarcity is an issue. Data aug- mentation techniques, such as those explored in [24], involve creating synthetic data to supplement existing datasets and improve model performance. Data augmentation has proven effective in speech recognition tasks, with [21] demonstrating improvements in model accuracy through the use of synthetic data. Similarly, [24] employed synthetic data to enhance training in speech recognition systems, an approach that can also be adapted for emotional speech synthesis, where data scarcity is a challenge. The field of emotional text-to-speech TTS synthesis has witnessed significant progress, with research spanning various approaches and techniques. For example, Wang et al. introduced Tacotron [25], a key tool for end-to-end speech synthesis, laying the foundation for generating speech from text input. Subsequent advancements have extended this paradigm to encompass the expression of emotions in VOLUME 12, 2024 167725 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset language as well, as demonstrated by the work of several authors [16], [19] [17], [26]. Shifting towards expressive TTS, early studies in emo- tional TTS were rooted in hidden Markov models (HMMs), which allowed for the synthesis of speech with specific emotions. One approach involved model interpolation, enabling the creation of speech with desired emotions [20]. Another technique incorporated unsupervised expression clusters during training, which enhanced the synthesis of emotionally expressive speech [27]. These studies introduced style tokens [16] and semi-supervised training methods [23] that significantly advanced the capability of synthesizing speech with nuanced emotional expression [17]. Moreover, fine-grained emotion strength control has gained attention, leading to techniques that allow for precise manipulation of the strength of the emotions expressed in synthesized speech. Efforts to enhance emotional distin- guishability have led to reinforcement learning techniques, which contribute to the generation of more emotionally expressive speech capturing subtle variations in emotional states [20], [28]. Incorporating deep learning and neural networks, model architectures have been modified to generalize emotional expressiveness in TTS synthesis more accurately. Among the notable advancements in end-to-end TTS systems are FastSpeech 2 [29] and Fairseq S2 [30], which illustrate the rapid progress in achieving high-quality speech synthesis. FastSpeech 2 is a non-autoregressive model designed to generate speech quickly and efficiently, reducing inference time while maintaining high synthesis quality. It excels in standard TTS tasks, but its architecture also allows for extensions that can incorporate emotional expressiveness [29]. Fairseq S2 is a sequence-to-sequence model framework that has been widely adopted for various speech synthesis tasks, though it was not originally intended for emotional synthesis [30]. Despite this, its flexible architecture enables researchers to experiment with emotional modulation in speech. Transformer-based approaches, such as one introduced in [31], have emerged as powerful methods for achieving fine-grained control over style and emotion in TTS. These approaches utilize self-attention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies in speech, which is crucial for effectively modeling the nuanced emotional expressions in speech. Unlike previous methods, which relied heavily on autoregressive models or non-autoregressive models like FastSpeech 2, Transformer-based models offer a more robust framework for capturing complex emotional patterns across longer sequences. This allows for more precise modulation of emotional expression in synthesized speech, making them particularly well-suited for tasks requiring detailed emotional nuances [23], [31]. Despite the progress in emotional speech synthesis, challenges remain, including the scarcity of comprehensive emotional speech datasets, particularly in languages other than English, such as Japanese. This scarcity hinders the development of emotionally expressive speech synthesis. Addressing these limitations can facilitate future research by providing researchers with the necessary datasets, or methods to collect them [15], [21], [32], [33]. The development of Emotional Speech Synthesis has been significantly influenced by foundational research efforts. Montero et al. [34] made crucial contributions by discussing the transition from speech databases to TTS systems for emotional speech synthesis, laying the groundwork for modern approaches. Burkhardt and Campbell studied the concept of emotional speech synthesis, highlighting its implications for human-computer interactions [5]. Other works, such as the German Text-to-Speech Synthesis System MARY by Schroder and Trouvain, have also contributed significantly to the advancement of this field [7]. Our study builds upon these foundations and aims to contribute to the field of emotional speech synthesis, particularly in addressing the challenges of limited datasets in languages other than English. III. ARCHITECTURE OF TACOTRON 2 Tacotron 2 is an end-to-end neural network architecture designed for text-to-speech synthesis. It consists of two main components: a sequence-to-sequence model with attention, which maps sequences of phonemes to mel-spectrograms, and a vocoder, which converts the mel-spectrograms into audio waveforms. The sequence-to-sequence model, based on LSTM layers with location-sensitive attention, is respon- sible for generating the acoustic features that represent the speech. The generated mel-spectrogram is then passed to the vocoder [19], [25] FIGURE 1. A simplified flowchart showing Tacotron 2’s architecture. WaveGlow is the vocoder used in this architecture. It is a flow-based generative model that synthesizes high-quality speech waveforms from mel-spectrograms. WaveGlow com- bines insights from both Glow, a generative flow-based model, and WaveNet, a state-of-the-art neural vocoder. The result is a model that produces high-fidelity audio with relatively low computational overhead, making it suitable for real-time applications [35] 167726 VOLUME 12, 2024 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset Tacotron 2 and WaveGlow were selected for this research due to their accessibility and well-documented implementation, making them practical for beginner researchers. While newer models such as NaturalSpeech [36] and large language models (LLMs) [37] have emerged, Tacotron 2 and WaveGlow continue to perform competitively, particularly in languages like Japanese, where there are fewer pre-built alternatives. However, the results achieved with Tacotron 2 and WaveGlow provide a solid benchmark for future research using state-of-the-art models. In our research, we utilize Tacotron 2 to generate mel-spectrograms from Japanese text, which are then con- verted into audio waveforms by WaveGlow, as shown in Fig. 1. IV. OBJECTIVE EVALUATION METRICS There are various ways to objectively evaluate synthesized speech, such as: Mel-Cepstrum Distortion (MCD) [16], [23] [26], [38], Band Aperiodicity Distortion (BAPD) [26], [38], F0 Voiced Error (FVE) [26], Signal-to-Distortion Ratio (SDR) [39], or Spectral Distortion (SD) [39]. In this research, we used all of the above mentioned ones to evaluate the automatically generated speech, due to them being widely used in speech synthesis evaluation. Although the automatic evaluation measures that is the most popular in speech synthesis is MCD, we decided to use multiple evaluation measures to assure sufficient amount of information about the synthesized speech that would allow reaching a conclusion regarding the quality of the developed models. A. MEL-CEPSTRUM DISTORTION (MCD) MCD measures the differences between the mel-cepstral coefficients of the ground truth (original) speech and the synthesized speech. It is widely used because it provides a quantitative measure of the spectral envelope’s accuracy, which is crucial for the perceived speech quality. It is calculated according to the equation 1 below. MCD(x, ˆx) = 10 ln(10) · 1 N N X i=1 v u u u t2 d X j=1 (xij −ˆxij)2 (1) where, • x and ˆx: Mel-cepstral coefficients of the original and synthesized speeches, respectively. • N: Total number of frames of synthetic speeches. • d: Dimension of the mel-cepstral coefficients. Mel-cepstral coefficients are a representation of the short-term power spectrum of a sound, based on a linear cosine transform of a log power spectrum on a nonlinear mel scale of frequency. They capture the spectral properties of speech signals in a compact form, making them essential for speech synthesis and recognition tasks. The lower the MCD, the closer the synthesized speech is to the original, making it a vital metric for evaluating how natural and intelligible the synthesized speech sounds. B. BAND APERIODICITY DISTORTION (BAPD) BAPD measures the differences in band aperiodicity between the ground truth and the synthesized speech. Band aperiod- icity affects the perception of voiced and unvoiced sounds, contributing to the naturalness of the synthesized speech. It is calculated according to the equation 2 below. BAPD(y, ˆy) = 10 ln(10) · 1 N N X i=1 q 2(yij −ˆyij)2 (2) where, • y and ˆy: Band aperiodicities of the original and synthetic speeches, respectively. • N: Total number of frames of synthetic speeches. Band aperiodicity refers to the degree of aperiodic (irregular or non-repetitive) components within specific frequency bands of a speech signal. These aperiodic components contribute to the perceived roughness or breathiness in speech sounds. This metric is essential for ensuring that the synthesized speech retains the fine nuances of the original, especially in terms of voice texture and clarity. C. F0 VOICED ERROR (FVE) FVE measures the quality difference between the ground truth and synthesized speech using the fundamental fre- quency (F0). The fundamental frequency, or pitch, is a critical component in conveying intonation and emotion in speech. It is calculated according to the equation 3 below. FVE(f , ˆf ) = sPN i=1(fi −ˆfi)2 N (3) where, • f and ˆf : F0 values of the original and synthesized speech, respectively. • N: Total number of F0 values. Fundamental frequency (F0) is the lowest frequency of a periodic waveform and determines the pitch of the sound. Accurate F0 reproduction is crucial for the naturalness of synthesized speech, as it affects how listeners perceive the intended emotions and prosody. D. SIGNAL-TO-DISTORTION RATIO (SDR) SDR measures the ratio of the signal power to the distortion power, indicating the overall signal quality. It is calculated according to the equation 4 below. SDR[dB] = 10 log10 cos2(S(n, f ) · ˆS(n, f )) 1 −cos2(S(n, f ) · ˆS(n, f )) ! (4) where, • S(n, f ) and ˆS(n, f ): Spectrograms of the original and synthesized speech, respectively. • n and f : Time and frequency indices, respectively. Spectrograms are visual representations of the spectrum of frequencies in a signal as it varies with time. A higher VOLUME 12, 2024 167727 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset SDR indicates better preservation of the original signal characteristics, making it a key metric for assessing the fidelity of the synthesized speech. E. SPECTRAL DISTORTION (SD) SD measures the differences between the spectrograms of the ground truth and synthesized speech. It is calculated according to the equation 5 below. SD[dB] = 1 T T X t=1 v u u u t 1 F F X f =1  20log10 |S (n, f )| ˆS (n, f )   (5) where, • S(n, f ) and ˆS(n, f ): Spectrograms of the original and synthesized speech, respectively. • T: Total time duration. • F: Total number of frequency bins. Spectral distortion evaluates how well the spectral char- acteristics of the synthesized speech match those of the original speech. It impacts the perceived audio quality and intelligibility, providing a comprehensive view of the synthesis performance. all of the voice features (mel-cepstrum, F0, spectrogram) were extracted using WORLD vocoder [13] and librosa [12]. V. INITIAL EVALUATION OF TACOTRON 2 FOR JAPANESE SPEECH SYNTHESIS In this section we describe a preliminary study with an aim to evaluate the basic feasibility of using Tacotron 2 to reproduce Japanese speech. To do this, we tested the model with a limited dataset using default settings. A. DATASET AND PREPROCESSING The initial dataset used in the preliminary study was Mozilla’s Common Voice [40], especially the Japanese dataset, which is a multi-speaker public speech dataset. The dataset was annotated with the gender of the voices (male or female) to be able to separate the dataset into a male-only or female- only subset. In this research, we used the female-only dataset, which contained 12,673 sentences and a total duration of 16 hours, 49 minutes, and 49 seconds. The data was preprocessed by converting .mp3 files to .wav, downsampling to 22050 Hz, converting stereo to mono, and transcribing original Japanese text to alphabet. B. EXPERIMENT AND RESULTS Tacotron2 was trained using the default settings with the female-only Common Voice Japanese dataset up to 50,000 iterations. This iteration count was chosen because the training loss had stabilized and stopped decreasing at this point, indicating that the model had likely learned as much as it could from the data. After Tacotron2 was trained, Waveglow was trained afterwards up to 20,000 iterations. The Waveglow model is responsible for the vocoding process, which converts the intermediate representation from Tacotron2 into the final waveform of the synthesized speech. The synthesized files, some of the examples of which were listed in Table 1, were then compared to the original dataset audio files using various evaluation metrics. The metrics used were Mel-Cepstrum Distortion (MCD), Band Aperiodicity Distortion (BAPD), F0 Voiced Error (FVE), Signal-to- Distortion Ratio (SDR), and Spectral Distortion (SD). These metrics collectively provide a comprehensive assessment of the quality of the synthesized speech. We presented the evaluation metrics for individual example files to highlight the variation in synthesized speech quality. Rather than showing the average results across all files, this table demonstrates how the quality of the synthesized speech varies significantly depending on the file. One reason for this fluctuation is that the Common Voice dataset used for the preliminary study was a multi-speaker speech dataset. Since the Tacotron 2 model combines voices from multiple speakers, this leads to greater variability in metrics like FVE and BAPD, as it attempts to synthesize speech with varying vocal characteristics. This natural fluctuation across files suggests that the model struggles to maintain consistent pitch and prosody when synthesizing voices with different speaker characteristics, which is reflected in the variability of the evaluation metrics. The results, as seen in Table 1, indicate that the quality of the synthesized speech varies significantly across different files. The average values for MCD, BAPD, and FVE suggest that while some files exhibit relatively good quality (e.g., TT50k_WG20k - 24688775.wav with an MCD of 3.824800), others show higher levels of distortion and error. 1) MEL-CEPSTRUM DISTORTION (MCD) Lower MCD values are preferred, as they indicate that the spectral envelope of the synthesized speech closely matches the original. The average MCD of 6.795100 suggests that, overall, there is moderate distortion in the synthesized speech. The file TT50k_WG20k - 24688775.wav, with an MCD of 3.824800, indicates relatively good spectral accuracy. 2) BAND APERIODICITY DISTORTION (BAPD) Lower BAPD values indicate fewer irregularities in the synthesized speech. The average BAPD of 3.262500 shows that there are noticeable aperiodic components, which could affect the naturalness and clarity of the synthesized speech. 3) F0 VOICED ERROR (FVE) Lower FVE percentages are better, as they reflect more accurate pitch reproduction. The average FVE of 48.10% is quite high, indicating significant pitch inaccuracies. This suggests that the synthesized speech often fails to match the original pitch, which is crucial for naturalness and emotional expression. 167728 VOLUME 12, 2024 M. J. A. The above results provide the following suggestions regarding the performance of the synthesized speech. Firstly, the fact that the dataset comprised only female voices likely helped to reduce some variability, leading to more consistent results across these metrics. Single-gendered vice dataset has a more consistent pitch ranges and formant frequencies compared to a mixed-gender dataset, which helps the model learn and generalize better. This consistency can result in lower MCD and BAPD values, as there is less variability for the model to handle. To improve the results, the subsequent experiments in this paper presented in section VIII explore the impact of using a warm-start training approach. The warm-start approach, which involves initializing the model with pre-trained param- eters before fine-tuning on the target dataset, is expected to enhance the synthesis quality by leveraging previously learned representations. C. DISCUSSION OF PRELIMINARY FINDINGS A significant challenge in the field of emotional speech synthesis is the limited availability of high-quality, diverse emotional speech datasets. Most existing datasets are con- strained by size and emotional variety, which restricts models like Tacotron 2 from producing natural and expressive emotional speech. This scarcity of data limits the ability of these models to generalize effectively, making it difficult to capture the wide range of emotional expressions present in natural human speech. In this preliminary study, we evaluated Tacotron 2’s performance using a standard, non-emotional, single-gender dataset. The results provided valuable insights into the model’s baseline capabilities, revealing both strengths and areas needing improvement. Tacotron 2 demonstrated a degree of accuracy in spectral reproduction, as indicated by relatively moderate Mel-Cepstrum Distortion (MCD) values. However, challenges were noted in pitch consistency and accuracy, as evidenced by high F0 Voiced Error (FVE) percentages. To overcome the limitations posed by the lack of diverse emotional speech datasets, this research explores the creation of an artificial emotional speech dataset. The aim is to simulate emotional variations that closely replicate those found in human-generated speech. By leveraging the foundational capabilities observed in this preliminary study, the artificial dataset will be designed to train Tacotron 2 to produce speech with rich emotional content. This approach seeks to validate whether artificially generated emotional speech data can serve as a viable substitute for traditional human-generated datasets, ensuring the quality of synthesized emotional speech remains high. These preliminary findings highlight the potential of Tacotron 2 for emotional speech synthesis but also underscore the need for advanced training techniques and improved dataset representation. By addressing the existing data limitations with an innovative artificial dataset, this research aims to enhance the model’s ability to produce natural and emotionally expressive speech, contributing to more engaging human-computer interactions. VI. ARTIFICIALLY AUGMENTED DATASET A. VOICEROID 2 Voiceroid is a Text-to-Speech (TTS) program developed by AH-Software.2 It allows for the manipulation of various voice-related settings such as tempo, pitch, and intonation. It is only available in the Japanese language. Voiceroid 2 is the latest version of the Voiceroid series released in 2017. It offers additional features such as having three emotion styles with which the expressed speech can be modified. The three emotion styles are happiness, anger, and sadness. Figures 2 and 3 show screenshots of Voiceroid 2’s workspace and emotional style sliders respectively. At the bottom of the workspace, there are sliders to control various settings of the output voice. These are: volume, speed/prosody, pitch height, intonation, duration of short and long pauses, as well as the presets for the 3 emotional styles. Voiceroid 2 was selected as the baseline for evaluation in this study because it was an accessible commercial TTS program available in Japanese, a language with limited emotional speech resources. While Voiceroid 2 does not provide the most advanced TTS synthesis, it was used due to the absence of publicly available emotional speech datasets, 2in Japanese: https://www.ah-soft.com/voiceroid/yukari/, accessed on 2024/07/17 VOLUME 12, 2024 167729 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset such as in JSUT [41], which only offers neutral speech. The main goal of this research was to determine whether synthetic emotional speech generated using Voiceroid 2 could serve as a substitute for human recorded emotional speech, filling a gap in available resources for emotional speech synthesis in Japanese. FIGURE 2. Screenshot of Voiceroid 2’s workspace. FIGURE 3. Voiceroid 2’s Emotional Styles’ Sliders (in the image the sadness slider is set as the highest). B. DATASET CREATION Using the three emotion styles of Voiceroid 2, namely happiness, anger, and sadness, in addition to the default settings to create a neutral-sounding voice, we used all the sentences available in the Common Voice Japanese dataset, spoken by both male and female voices. After removing all duplicates, this process produced a total of 15,875 unique sentences. Subsequently, we generated four sets of emotional speech audio data, each containing 15,875 files per style, resulting in a total of 63,500 voice files. Each emotion was represented by 10,000 sentences (files) for training and 5,875 sentences for validation, resulting in a total training set of 40,000 files and a validation set of 23,500 files across all emotions. This split ensures that each emotional category has a sufficient number of examples for model training while providing a robust validation set for performance evaluation. The Tacotron 2 model was trained using these filelists to learn the emotional speech patterns in Japanese. C. LIMITATIONS As stated above, Voiceroid 2 offers a set of predefined emotional styles, such as happy, sad, and angry. These styles are based on patterns of intonation, pitch, and pacing in the generated speech. However, these styles are relatively simplistic and may not capture the full spectrum of human emotions, which can be incredibly nuanced and layered. In this regards, Voiceroid 2 does not possess true natural human emotional expressivity. Rather, it relies on algorithms and pre-programmed patterns to mimic the most stereotypical sound of the three emotions. Thus, it cannot modify the different types of joy or sadness, as in a natural human speech, nor can it adapt the expression of each emotion to the context of the expressed content. These limitations can cause potential bias since Voiceroid 2 does not accurately capture real human emotions. We study those limitations in deep in further sections, particularly, the section on the MOS evaluation of the pretrained model, where we purposefully mix in the survey sentences with different semantic and phonetic emotional context (e.g., happy content said with sad voice, or angry content expressed with happy voice, etc.). FOUR EMOTION-SPECIFIC MODEL VERSIONS Utilizing exclusively the datasets created with the Voiceroid2 software on the basis of the textual content of the original 167730 VOLUME 12, 2024 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset Common Voice dataset, Tacotron2 was pretrained from scratch with default parameters to generate four distinct models, each corresponding to a specific emotion category, namley, happy, angry, sad and neutral. Subsequently, an additional set of four distinct models was developed, not from scratch, but employing the already pretrained ‘Common Voice model’ (or ‘CV model’) derived from the preliminary study with a warm-start. ‘‘Warm- starting’’ refers to the practice of initializing the training of a machine learning model, particularly in the context of updating the model with new data in an ongoing production system. The concept of warm-starting is used to efficiently incorporate new data into the model without discarding all previous knowledge captured by the model’s parameters [42]. We conducted s comparative analysis of performance metrics derived from both model sets, aiming to specify potential enhancements facilitated by the integration of a model trained on a Japanese speech dataset. Moreover, at intervals of 10,000, 20,000, and 30,000 iterations, the models from Tacotron2 and Waveglow were saved, in order to specify potential influences on the training process resulting from only using artificial data. Following the training phase of both model sets, a selection of 1000 files from each emotional category was synthesized. Subsequently, an evaluation of these synthesized files was conducted, facilitating a comparative analysis against the corresponding set of models of the same emotional category. B. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION Tables 2 and 3 represent the average performance metrics of 1000 files for each set of models without and with the Common Voice (CV) model warm-start. Additionally, table 4 represents the p-values and mean differences for MCD scores, highlighting statistically significant differences between the two training approaches. Table 5 explores the progressive enhancement in the statistical significance of differences as the model undergoes further training iterations. 1) MEL-CEPSTRUM DISTORTION (MCD) Lower MCD values indicate better spectral accuracy of the synthesized speech. Table 2 shows an average MCD of 6.795100 for models trained from scratch, whereas Table 3 shows an average MCD of 7.231362 for models with the CV model warm-start. This indicates that the warm-start models generally achieve better spectral accuracy, even though the original CV model was originally trained on data sounding completely differently than the data used for training in this experiment (average Japanese female speaker vs. artificial voice of a Viceroid with characteristic mannerisms). For example, at 30,000 iterations, the warm-start model for sadness (cv_TT30k_WG30k_sad) has an MCD of 7.646137, which is lower than the model trained from scratch (TT30k_WG30k_sad) with an MCD of 7.830084, suggesting better performance in spectral accuracy with the warm-start approach. 2) BAND APERIODICITY DISTORTION (BAPD) Lower BAPD values indicate fewer irregularities in the synthesized speech. The average BAPD in Table 2 is 3.262500, whereas in Table 3, it is 3.727372. This suggests that models trained from scratch have fewer aperiodic components. However, the differences are relatively small, indicating similar performance in terms of aperiodicity distortion. 3) F0 VOICED ERROR (FVE) Lower FVE percentages reflect more accurate pitch repro- duction. The average FVE in Table 2 is 48.10%, while in Table 3, it is 43.443512%, indicating that models with the CV model warm-start generally achieves more accurate pitch reproduction. For instance, the warm-start model for happiness at 10,000 iterations (cv_TT10k_WG10k_hap) has an FVE of 41.111604%, compared to the model trained from scratch (TT10k_WG10k_hap) with an FVE of 40.982057%, showing improved pitch accuracy. 4) SIGNAL-TO-DISTORTION RATIO (SDR) Higher SDR values indicate better preservation of the original signal. The average SDR in Table 2 is 16.854300 dB, whereas in Table 3, it is 15.902745 dB. This suggests that models trained from scratch have a minimal advantage in overall signal quality. For example, the warm-start model for anger at 30,000 iterations (cv_TT30k_WG30k_ang) has an SDR of 13.761156 dB, compared to the model trained from scratch (TT30k_WG30k_ang) with an SDR of 13.795674 dB. 5) SPECTRAL DISTORTION (SD) Lower SD values indicate better spectral similarity to the original speech. The average SD in Table 2 is 9.423400 dB, whereas in Table 3, it is 9.075956 dB. This suggests that models with the CV model warm-start have a slight advantage in spectral similarity. For instance, the warm-start model for sadness at 30,000 iterations (cv_TT30k_WG30k_sad) has an SD of 7.646137 dB, compared to the model trained from scratch (TT30k_WG30k_sad) with an SD of 7.830084 dB. 6) STATISTICAL SIGNIFICANCE OF DIFFERENCES IN MCD SCORES (TABLE 4) The p-values in Table 4 indicate the statistical significance of the differences in MCD scores between models trained with and without the CV model warm-start. A p-value less than 0.05 is typically considered as statistically significant. For instance, the p-value for the 10k→20k_ang comparison is 0.204900, indicating no significant difference, whereas the p-value for 10k→30k_ang is less than 0.0001, indicating a highly significant difference. These significant p-values suggest that the warm-start approach notably improves spectral accuracy, especially in longer training durations. VOLUME 12, 2024 167731 M. J. A. The consistently low p-values (often less than 0.0001) as the training progresses highlight that significant improvements in MCD scores are achieved as training continues. This trend demonstrates that the warm-start approach is effective in enhancing the model’s spectral accuracy over extended training periods. The primary discovery from this evaluation is that models using the Common Voice (CV) warm-start approach generally achieve better spectral accuracy, as indicated by lower Mel-Cepstrum Distortion (MCD) values, compared to models trained from scratch. This improvement is partic- ularly noticeable as training iterations increase, suggesting the effectiveness of the warm-start approach in enhancing emotional speech synthesis over longer training periods. For instance, at 30,000 iterations, the warm-start model for sadness achieves a lower MCD than the corresponding model trained from scratch, indicating a clear advantage in spectral accuracy. These findings are significant because they demonstrate the potential of leveraging a warm-start approach with models pre-trained on a general speech dataset to enhance emotional speech synthesis. The results not only show improvements in spectral accuracy but also suggest that warm-starting with pre-trained models can lead to more natural-sounding and emotionally expressive text-to-speech (TTS) systems. This is particularly valuable when working with limited emotional speech data, as it offers a more efficient and scalable method to develop high-quality emotional voice outputs for applications ranging from virtual assistants to immersive media environments. 8) IMPACT OF ITERATIONS We explored the impact of increasing the number of iterations from 10k to 30k to assess whether additional training would lead to significant improvements in the model’s performance. While there was no significant improvement in key metrics between 10k and 30k iterations, either with or without CV, One possible explanation for this result is that the Tacotron 2 model may have reached a learning plateau after 10k iterations, where additional iterations provide diminishing returns. This could also be due to the size of the dataset, which might not have been large or diverse enough to take full advantage of the additional iterations. The quantitative improvements in metrics such as MCD, Band Aperiodicity Distortion (BAPD), F0 Voiced Error (FVE), and Signal-to-Distortion Ratio (SDR) provide pre- liminary evidence of enhanced emotional expressiveness in synthesized speech. However, to fully understand the impact of these enhancements on listener perception, these findings will be further validated through a Mean Opinion Score (MOS) survey, discussed in the subsequent section. The MOS survey will offer subjective insights into the perceived quality and emotional authenticity of the synthesized speech, thereby complementing these objective measures. While the warm-start models show considerable promise, the observed variability in performance across different emotions and metrics indicates that there is still room for improvement. Further optimization of training methods and model architecture may be necessary to achieve consistent performance across all emotional categories. TABLE 4.
