MOS SURVEY After doing an automatic objective evaluation in section VII, we decided to do a subjective comparative evaluation between Voiceroid 2 and Tacotron 2’s outputs to see if the artificially generated dataset can be used as a viable replacement for a normal emotional speech corpus. A. MOS SURVEY SENTENCE COLLECTION AND PREPROCESSING Our dataset originated from a collection of 9,947 sentences predominantly spoken by female individuals from the Com- mon Voice dataset. To ensure consistency and manageability, we initially filtered the dataset based on two criteria: word count and audio duration. By selecting sentences around the average word count (13.28 words) and audio duration 167732 VOLUME 12, 2024 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset (4.42 seconds), we obtained a subset of 5,172 sentences. This selection allowed us to focus on a representative and manageable sample that captured the essential characteristics of the dataset, such as diversity in sentence structure and emotional expression, while avoiding the resource-intensive process of analyzing the entire dataset. Additionally, working with a subset minimized the risk of participant fatigue during the MOS survey and ensured that the results were statistically robust and relevant, without compromising the generalizability of our findings. Then, emotion detection was performed using ML- Ask [43], [44], a tool for emotion analysis of text in Japanese. Of the selected sentences, 910 were identified as containing one or more emotions, while the remaining 4,262 were judged as not containing any emotion expressions. However, this does not mean that these sentences were not emotional, but rather that they did not contain explicit linguistic ques regarding the expressed emotions. Since in the MOS survey we wanted to test the differences between semantic and phonetic expressions of emotions, we used only those 910 sentences which also contained explicit linguistic expressions of emotions. Then we filtered the emotional sentences based on the presence of the three emotions types used by the Voiceroid technology applied in this study, namely, [Happiness], [Sadness] and [Anger], which was represented by categories [YOR], [AWA] and [IKA], respectively, which stands for YOR: Yorokobi (Happiness in Japanese), AWA: Aware (Sadness in Japanese) and IKA: Ikari (Anger in Japanese). Inspection of the resulting subset revealed 296 sentences representing these emotions, while 614 were excluded due to a lack of those target emotions. Among the included sentences, a follow-up analysis revealed that 186 were analysed inaccurately by ML-Ask, 66 were partially accurate, and 44 were accurately representative of the YOR-AWA- IKA emotions after additional manual filtering. This detailed validation process ensured the integrity of our dataset in capturing the desired emotional nuances to be further analysed in the MOS survey. While the created dataset initially contained a sufficient number of happy sentences, it lacked adequate representation of angry and sad expressions. To address this imbalance, we ran the remaining 5,172 sentences again through a model that is distilled from the zero-shot classification pipeline on the Multilingual Sentiment dataset3 to determine if the sentences were positive, negative, or neutral. Notably, 1,939 sentences were classified as negative, prompting further analysis to delineate their specific emo- tional connotations. Subsequent sentiment analysis utilizing the Luke Japanese Large Sentiment Analysis model4 enabled the identification of 455 sad and 31 angry sentences within 3https://huggingface.co/lxyuan/distilbert-base-multilingual-cased- sentiments-student 4https://huggingface.co/Mizuiro-sakura/luke-japanese-large-sentiment- analysis-wrime the negative subset. This allowed us to acquire the remaining needed sad and angry sentences. After acquiring a sufficient number of emotional sen- tences, they were then categorized based on the emotion conveyed, the source of synthesis (i.e., Tacotron 2 or Voiceroid 2), and whether the sentences were unique or shared between the two sources. We detail this process below. B. SENTENCE CATEGORIZATION CRITERIA The categorization of sentences was conducted based on three primary criteria: • Emotion Category: Sentences were classified into one of four emotion categories: Angry, Happy, Sad, or Neutral, depending on the predominant emotional tone conveyed by the synthesis system. • Source of Synthesis: Each sentence was attributed to one of the two speech synthesis systems used in this study, namely Tacotron 2 or Voiceroid 2, based on the system responsible for its generation. • Uniqueness: Sentences were further categorized as either unique or shared. Unique sentences were those exclusively generated by one synthesis system, whereas shared sentences were those produced by both Tacotron 2 and Voiceroid 2. This last criteria allowed us to perform a twofold analysis. Primarily, the unique sentences allowed for a randomized diversified study. Thus, differences between Voiceroid 2 and Tacotron 2 for those sentences would assure a fully natural, random, unbiased differences, which would assure that the differences are not only statistically, but also causally different, as this methodology follows the use of randomized clinical trials in causal inference [45]. Secondly, the use of shared emotional sentences allowed for another twofold comparison. Namely, the same emo- tional sentences could be compared for Tacotron 2 and Voiceroid 2, which follows the randomized controlled trials (RCTs) methodology from causal inference [46]. Additionally, mixing of various emotional tones and semantic connotations would allow for a deeper analysis of relations between emotions expressed phonologically and semantically. For example, sentences that both express anger phonologically and semantically should be perceived as angry more clearly. On the contrary, sentences with mixed phonological and semantic emo- tional expressiveness would allow for verifying which of the two expressiveness layers has more priming, and for which emotions. It could also allow for additional discoveries of new perceptions, such as irony, which could result from a semantically happy sentence expressed with an angry or sad phonological features. C. DATASET COMPOSITION A total of 112 emotionally expressive sentences were collected for analysis, ensuring a balanced representation across different emotions. These sentences were distributed among the defined categories as follows: VOLUME 12, 2024 167733 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset Unique Sentences: – 10 sentences produced by Tacotron 2 in each emotional category (Angry, Happy, Neutral, Sad), totaling 40 sentences. These sentences are designed such that the emotional tone of the synthesized voice matches the emotion conveyed in the sen- tence’s semantic content, allowing for an evaluation of how effectively each emotion is conveyed by this system. – 10 sentences produced by Voiceroid 2 in each emotional category (Angry, Happy, Neutral, Sad), totaling 40 sentences. This setup provides a basis for comparative analysis with Tacotron 2, assessing which system better captures the nuances of each emotion. Shared Sentences: – 4 sentences, irrespective of their intended emotion, produced by Tacotron 2, totaling 16 sentences. These shared sentences serve as a constant bench- mark to compare outputs under varying conditions of emotional congruence. – 4 sentences, irrespective of their intended emotion, produced by Voiceroid 2, totaling 16 sentences. This consistency helps in directly comparing the performance and emotional fidelity of the two systems under both matching and non-matching conditions between voice tone and semantic content. D. DATASET SEPARATION Following the categorization process, the dataset was sep- arated into eight batches to facilitate subsequent analyses. Each batch was structured as follows: • One set of unique emotional sentences from one of the sources (Tacotron 2 or Voiceroid 2). This specific separation helps in analyzing the emotional output from one TTS system in isolation. • All shared emotional sentences from all sources. Includ- ing these in every batch ensures that all evaluations have a common ground for comparison. Thus, each batch comprised a total of 42 sentences, including 10 unique sentences of a certain emotion and all shared emotional sentences. This division ensured a balanced representation of both unique and shared emotional expressions across the dataset. This comprehensive dataset and structured division allow for a systematic investigation of the emotional expressions generated by Tacotron 2 and Voiceroid 2, enabling insights into the comparative performance and emotional fidelity of the two speech synthesis systems. The distinction between unique and shared sentences allows for an examination of how emotional congruence between voice and content impacts perceived speech quality. E. SIMPLIFIED MOS SURVEY DESIGN Our methodology for assessing speech quality is based on the framework proposed by Viswanathan and Viswanathan [47], which identifies intelligibility and naturalness as two fundamental factors influencing speech perception. Intelli- gibility encompasses aspects such as listening effort, pro- nunciation, comprehension, articulation, and speaking rate, while naturalness includes attributes like ease of listening, pleasantness, and audio flow. To streamline the assessment process, we simplified the MOS survey by focusing on the broader categories of intelligibility and naturalness. Instead of utilizing all nine components identified by Viswanathan et al., we distilled the survey to include three questions per audio file: 1) ‘‘How natural sounding was the audio?’’ (naturalness) • Respondents rated the naturalness of the speech on a scale from 1 to 5, ranging from ‘‘very unnatural’’ to ‘‘very natural,’’ respectively. 2) ‘‘Were the contents of the audio file easy to under- stand?’’ (intelligibility) • Respondents rated the ease of understanding the contents of the audio file on a scale from 1 to 5, ranging from ‘‘very hard to understand’’ to ‘‘very easy to understand,’’ respectively. 3) ‘‘If you thought that the voice expressed an emotion, which emotion was the closest?’’ • A multiple-choice question allows respondents to select the closest emotion conveyed, with options including happiness, anger, sadness, neutral/no emotion, and an open-ended ‘‘other’’ category. F. DATA COLLECTION AND SAMPLING The MOS survey was distributed across eight batches to local university students and participants recruited through Crowd- Works,5 a crowdsourcing platform in Japan. To prevent duplicate responses, measures were implemented to ensure that each respondent only answered one batch. The sample consisted of 56 participants, predominantly Japanese, with a minority of non-Japanese individuals. Gender distribution was balanced, with 24 males and 32 females, and age ranged from the 20s to the 50s. Respondents for the MOS survey were assigned to batches on a first-come, first-serve basis, without specific demographic criteria guiding their assignment. The survey was distributed across eight batches, and steps were taken to ensure that each participant only completed one batch to avoid duplicate responses. IX. MOS survey results by uniqueness of sentences. Unique sentences are sets of 10 distinct sentences where the emotional tone of the synthesized voice (phonetics) matches the emotion conveyed in the sentence semantic content (semantics). On the other hand, shared sentences consist of 16 sentences where the emotional tone of the synthesized voice sometimes matches, and sometimes purposefully does not match the semantic emotional content of the sentences. The distinction between unique and shared sentences allows for an examination of how emotional congruence between voice and content impacts perceived speech quality. The initial conclusion at first glance suggests that users on average perceive as more natural and more intelligible sentences where phonetic emotional tone matches the semantic emotional content. Although this result is fully understandable for Naturalness, the fact that the difference is similar also for Intelligibility suggests two things, which can be summarized as one phenomenon. Primarily, the respondents often conflate the two labels. In other words, what sounds unnatural, is also perceived as more difficult to understand. This connects to the second reason, namely, that humans understand language holistically, i.e., in normal everyday communication we do not perceive the phonetic layer and semantic layer separately, but as a whole. This means, that when the semantic layer is in a state of incongruence with the phonetic layer, the whole utterance feels difficult to perceive, i.e., it is difficult for the listener to understand what the speaker intended to say. These results are in line with previous finding. For example, this is evident in the perception of isolated Cantonese syllables, where experts demonstrate a stronger influence of holistic processing [2]. The importance of fine phonetic detail in speech understanding further supports this holistic approach [3]. The relationship between phonology, VOLUME 12, 2024 167735 M. J. A. A. RESULTS - DIVISION BY EMOTION (ANGER) To observe differences in synthesis quality between Voiceroid 2 and Tacotron 2’s outputs, we have decided to divide the results based on emotions first, in this case, anger. TABLE 10. MOS results by emotion (Anger). Table 10 explores how the speech synthesis systems’ angry utterances of sentences influence perceived speech quality per file. It shows that Tacotron 2 produces lower scores across naturalness and intelligibility compared to Ground Truth (Voiceroid 2), and overall, the unique sentences generally have lower scores compared to shared sentences. There is a discrepancy between the performance of Tacotron 2 and Ground Truth, indicating that the generated audio by Tacotron 2 may not be as natural or intelligible as Voiceroid 2. The performance gap between Tacotron 2 and the ground truth suggests that while Tacotron 2 was trained on the ground truth data, there are still limitations in its ability to accurately reproduce the emotional nuances and clarity of the original recordings. This performance gap could stem from various factors such as the complexity of modeling emotional speech, limitations in the training data, imperfections in the synthesis architecture, or just simply not enough training time. Discrepancies in response distribution across different files could suggest variations in respondent demographics, survey administration, or file characteristics, which may influence perceived speech quality and emotional expression. While Tacotron 2 was trained on the Voiceroid 2’s dataset, the quality and representativeness of the Voiceroid 2’s data itself could impact Tacotron 2’s synthesis performance. Inaccuracies or limitations in the Voiceroid 2’s data could propagate to Tacotron 2’s speech output, affecting MOS scores. In conclusion, while Tacotron 2, trained on a ground truth dataset, lays a solid foundation, its performance constraints underscore the necessity for additional research and refinement in training techniques, dataset representa- tion, and model architecture. These enhancements aim to bolster its synthesis capabilities, particularly in capturing emotional nuances and clarity. Moreover, considering part count alongside MOS scores enhances the depth and reliability of the analysis, offering insights into statistical confidence, response consistency, respondent engagement, and the robustness of insights drawn from survey data. This integrated approach facilitates more informed decisions con- cerning synthesis quality improvement and user experience optimization. Continuing the results analysis by dividing it by source we get tables 11 and 12. TABLE 11. MOS results by emotion (Anger) and Source (Tacotron 2). TABLE 12. MOS results by emotion (Anger) and Source (Voiceroid 2). Tables 11 and 12 show more clearly that Voiceroid 2 achieves a higher average naturalness score (3.265300) compared to Tacotron (2.452400). This indicates that speech synthesized from the ground truth dataset is perceived as more natural by respondents compared to Tacotron-generated speech. It also scores higher in intelligibility (3.989800) compared to Tacotron (3.496600). This suggests that speech synthesized from the ground truth dataset is perceived as more understandable or clearer compared to Tacotron- generated speech. Overall, this implies that, on average, respondents rate speech synthesized from the ground truth dataset more favorably in terms of overall quality compared to Tacotron-generated speech. Tacotron-generated speech may still exhibit and amplify subtle artifacts, synthetic timbre, or prosodic irregularities that it acquired from its training from Voiceroid 2’s data 167736 VOLUME 12, 2024 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset that detract from its perceived naturalness and intelligibility. Human listeners may detect these differences and prefer the authenticity and clarity of human-recorded speech over synthetic alternatives. Continuing the analysis of the results by dividing by uniqueness instead, we get tables 13 and 14. TABLE 13. MOS results by emotion (Anger) and Uniqueness (Unique). TABLE 14. MOS results by emotion (Anger) and Uniqueness (Shared). Tables 13 and 14. indicate that speech synthesized from unique sentences achieves higher scores in naturalness (2.992900), intelligibility (4.021400), and single file MOS (3.507100) compared to shared sentences. This suggests that unique sentences are perceived as slightly more natural, understandable, and of higher overall quality by respondents than shared sentences. The higher perceived quality of unique sentences com- pared to shared ones likely arises from the greater emotional congruence between utterance and meaning in the former. In contrast, shared sentences frequently exhibit emotional incongruence with the intended meaning. This lack of alignment likely contributes to the perception of shared sentences as lower in quality compared to emotionally congruent unique sentences. B. RESULTS - DIVISION BY EMOTION (HAPPY) Table 15 observations reveal that Voiceroid 2 generally outperforms Tacotron 2 in naturalness, intelligibility, and MOS for both unique and shared sentences. Unique sen- tences consistently score higher than shared ones, indicating the impact of sentence uniqueness on perceived quality. Tacotron 2 tends to fare worse on shared sentences compared to unique ones, while Voiceroid 2 maintains more consistent performance across both sentence types. Given that Tacotron 2 was trained on Voiceroid 2, it would be reasonable to expect Tacotron 2’s performance to TABLE 15. MOS results by emotion (Happy). closely resemble that of Voiceroid 2, particularly concerning naturalness and intelligibility. However, the results indicate that Voiceroid 2 consistently outperforms Tacotron 2 across all evaluated metrics. Several factors could contribute to this discrepancy. Firstly, limitations within the Tacotron 2 model architecture or training process may have prevented it from fully capturing the nuances present in the Voiceroid 2 dataset. Despite being trained on similar data, Tacotron 2 may struggle to faithfully reproduce the quality of Voiceroid 2 due to these inherent constraints. Secondly, while Tacotron 2 was trained on the Voiceroid2 dataset, the quality and diversity of the training data may have been insufficient to fully represent the variability present in natural speech. Consequently, Tacotron 2’s ability to generate high-quality outputs may be compromised by a lack of certain characteristics or nuances present in real-world speech. Lastly, overfitting may also be a contributing factor. Tacotron 2 could have overfitted to specific aspects of the training data, resulting in discrepancies between its synthesized outputs and Voiceroid 2. Overfitting occurs when a model memorizes the training data instead of generalizing it to unseen data, potentially leading to sub-optimal perfor- mance on new inputs. These reasons could also apply to the previously mentioned angry file MOS results. Dividing the results by the source to get a more nuanced analysis we get tables 16 and 17. TABLE 16. MOS results by emotion (Happy) and source (Tacotron 2). VOLUME 12, 2024 167737 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset TABLE 17. MOS results by emotion (Happy) and source (Voiceroid 2). Again, Voiceroid 2 consistently surpasses Tacotron 2 in naturalness, intelligibility, and single file MOS for both unique and shared sentences, indicating the inferior synthesis quality of Tacotron 2 compared to Voiceroid 2, irrespective of sentence type or emotion. This pattern persists even for the happy emotion, underscoring the consistency of Tacotron 2’s limitations in synthesis quality across various emotional contexts rather than being confined to specific emotional states. Continuing the analysis of the results by dividing by uniqueness instead, we get tables 18 and 19. TABLE 18. MOS results by emotion (Happy) and uniqueness (Unique). TABLE 19. MOS results by emotion (Happy) and uniqueness (Shared). Unique sentences consistently yield higher scores for nat- uralness, intelligibility, and single file MOS compared to shared sentences across both Tacotron 2 and Voiceroid 2. This suggests that the uniqueness/emotional congruence of sentences positively influences perceived synthesis quality, regardless of the synthesis model used. C. RESULTS - DIVISION BY EMOTION (NEUTRAL) Table 20 illustrates a recurring trend: the averages for natu- ralness and intelligibility tend to be lower for Tacotron 2 in TABLE 20. MOS results by emotion (neutral). comparison to Voiceroid 2, across both unique and shared files. Furthermore, the single file MOS also tends to lower ratings for Tacotron 2 when compared to Voiceroid 2. TABLE 21. MOS results by emotion (neutral) and source (tacotron 2). Tables 21 and 22 show that both Tacotron 2 and Voiceroid 2 exhibit higher scores for intelligibility compared to naturalness. This suggests that while the speech generated by both sources is generally understandable, there may be differences in the naturalness or fluency of the speech. Tables 23 and 24 show that both Tacotron 2 and Voiceroid 2 show a performance drop in shared files compared to unique files, particularly in naturalness. This indicates that shared files (emotional incongruence) may pose additional challenges for both Tacotron 2 and Voiceroid 2, potentially impacting the perceived quality of the synthesized speech. D. RESULTS - DIVISION BY EMOTION (SAD) Tables 26 and 27 again show the repeating pattern of, despite being trained on Voiceroid 2’s dataset, Tacotron 2’s overall performance in emotional speech synthesis still falls below that of Voiceroid 2. Tables 28 and 29 show that both unique and shared Voiceroid 2 files generally outperform their Tacotron coun- terparts in terms of naturalness and intelligibility. However, further investigation into the specific factors contributing 167738 VOLUME 12, 2024 M. J. A. Khalifah et al.: Emotional TTS in Japanese Using Artificially Augmented Dataset to these differences would provide valuable insights for improving emotional speech synthesis models. X. This diversified range of participants enables the acquisition of insights based on demographic factors, enriching the analysis beyond the scope of MOS results alone. Tables 33, 34, and 35 present the MOS results categorized by demographic factors. Analysis by gender reveals that males tend to rate perceived quality higher than females, sug- gesting potential gender-related p

title: IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026
file_name: Enhancing Psychological Assessments With Open-Ended Questionnaires and Large Language Models An ASD Case Study.pdf
text: IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 1707 Enhancing Psychological Assessments With Open-Ended Questionnaires and Large Language Models: An ASD Case Study Alberto Altozano , Maria Eleonora Minissi , Lucía Gómez-Zaragozá , Luna Maddalon , Mariano Alcañiz , and Javier Marín-Morales Abstract—Open-ended questionnaires allow respon- dents to express freely, capturing richer information than close-ended formats, but they are harder to analyze. Re- cent natural language processing advancements enable automatic assessment of open-ended responses, yet its use in psychological classiﬁcation is underexplored. This study proposes a methodology using pre-trained large lan- guage models (LLMs) for automatic classiﬁcation of open- ended questionnaires, applied to autism spectrum disorder (ASD) classiﬁcation via parental reports. We compare mul- tiple training strategies using transcribed responses from 51 parents (26 with typically developing children, 25 with ASD), exploring variations in model ﬁne-tuning, input rep- resentation, and speciﬁcity. Subject-level predictions are derived by aggregating 12 individual question responses. Our best approach achieved 84% subject-wise accuracy and 1.0 ROC-AUC using an OpenAI embedding model, per- question training, including questions in the input, and combining the predictions with a voting system. In addition, a zero-shot evaluation using GPT-4o was conducted, yield- ing comparable results, underscoring the potential of both compact, local models and large out-of-the-box LLMs. To enhance transparency, we explored interpretability meth- ods. Proprietary LLMs like GPT-4o offered no direct ex- planation, and OpenAI embedding models showed limited interpretability. However, locally deployable LLMs provided the highest interpretability. This highlights a trade-off be- tween proprietary models’ performance and local models’ explainability. Our ﬁndings validate LLMs for automatically classifying open-ended questionnaires, offering a scalable, cost-effective complement for ASD assessment. These re- sults suggest broader applicability for psychological analy- sis of other conditions, advancing LLM use in mental health research. Received 21 February 2025; revised 25 June 2025; accepted 6 Au- gust 2025. Date of publication 15 August 2025; date of current ver- sion 4 February 2026. The work of Maria Eleonora Minissi was sup- ported by Generalitat Valenciana under Grant GRISOLIAP/2019/137. This work was supported in part by The CDTI of the Spanish MCIU through R&D&I Project under Reference IDI-20201146, in part by MI- CIU/AEI/10.13039/501100011033 through R&D&I Project under Refer- ence PID2020-116422RB-C21, and in part by Generalitat Valenciana under Grant CIGE/2023/160. (Corresponding author: Alberto Altozano.) This work involved human subjects or animals in its research. Approval of all ethical and experimental procedures and protocols was granted by the UPV Ethics Committee under Application No. P_06_04_06_20, and performed in line with the Declaration of Helsinki. The authors are with the University Research Institute of Human- Centered Technologies (HUMAN-tech), Universitat Politècnica de Valèn- cia (UPV), 46021 Valencia, Spain (e-mail: aaltfer@htech.upv.es). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/JBHI.2025.3599643 Index Terms—Text classiﬁcation, questionnaire, open- ended, large language model (LLM), statistical learning, deep learning, computational psychiatry, autism, parental reports. I. INTRODUCTION C LOSE-ENDED questionnaires have gained increasing prominence in clinical assessments, particularly as screen- ing tools [1], [2]. These questionnaires typically feature prede- termined response options that focus on speciﬁc symptoms or manifestations of a condition. Methods such as Likert scales and checklists are commonly used for this purpose [3], making these tools both easy to administer and cost-effective. Their structured nature facilitates faster recognition, management, and outcome evaluation of clinical and neurodevelopmental disorders, which is essential in large-scale screenings [4]. Similarly, open-ended questionnaires are commonly used in psychological screening, even though offering distinct ad- vantages. The primary difference between these two types of inquiries lies in the degree of respondent autonomy [5]. While close-ended questions limit individuals to predeﬁned an- swers, open-ended questions allow respondents to express their thoughts without bias from suggested responses [6]. This makes open-ended questionnaires valuable for capturing spontaneous, nuanced, and potentially more diverse responses. Moreover, these responses can provide rich data that can be further explored qualitatively or quantitatively [7], [8]. However, open-ended questionnaires present their own set of challenges. Scoring or coding free-text responses requires signiﬁcant technical expertise and effort, often involving manual or computational methods to process the data into qualitative results [9]. This complexity increases the cost and time required, making them less feasible for routine screening purposes. Given the relevance of open-ended questionnaires in mental health assessment, efforts should be addressed to automate their costly processing, particularly in the ﬁeld of computational psy- chiatry, a relatively new research domain that aims to improve research in clinical assessment procedures through the use of computational models. It seeks to address the current limitations in clinical assessment procedures, which often rely on subjec- tive and semi-structured methods, by introducing objective and computational analyses of quantitative data [10], [11]. Compu- tational models have been increasingly applied to detect various © 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ 1708 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 clinical and neurodevelopmental disorders. Among these, the detection of Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) through com- putational models has been extensively investigated, with a primary focus on children’s behavior. This focus arises from the limitations of traditional procedures for evaluating ASD in children, such as the Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule (ADOS) [12], [13], [14]. However, besides children’s observa- tion, the traditional early detection of ASD typically relies on closed-ended questionnaires administered to caregivers, like the Modiﬁed Checklist for Autism in Toddlers (M-CHAT; [15]), and gold-standard open-ended semi-structured interviews like the Autism Diagnostic Interview Revised (ADI-R; [16]). So far, computational models aimed at detecting ASD have over- looked the role of caregivers. Parent’s reports are critical for identifying early signs of ASD in children, and understanding the contextual factors in which symptoms manifest. The lack of focus on caregiver’s perspectives in early ASD detection limits the effectiveness of novel early ASD diagnostic tools, and hinders the development of more personalized approaches for ASD detection. Simultaneously, recent advancements in natural language processing (NLP) have shown promise for automating certain open-ended linguistic tasks relevant to psychological assess- ments. For instance, tasks like the Cookie Theft Task [17] or analyses of free-text data, such as social media posts [18], [19], have beneﬁted from NLP models, particularly large language models (LLMs). Despite these advancements, few efforts have yet been made to apply novel NLP techniques to the assessment of open-ended psychological questionnaires. The potential to process and analyze open-ended questionnaire data with NLP could have far-reaching implications, not just for psycholog- ical screenings but for any type of survey requiring subject analysis. Given this gap, we aim to explore how modern NLP tech- niques, particularly ﬁne-tuning pre-trained LLMs for classi- ﬁcation, can be applied to assess open-ended questionnaires related to psychological assessment. While NLP classiﬁcation approaches have been extensively studied [20], [21], these methods generally operate at the level of individual samples. In contrast, analyzing open-ended questionnaires for subject- level assessments introduces new challenges. Key issues in- clude how to handle multiple responses from each individ- ual, how to manage questions spanning adjacent but dis- tinct domains, and how to process free-text data for effective classiﬁcation. To address these challenges, we conducted a case study where we developed a brief open-ended questionnaire tailored to parents of children with ASD. This questionnaire, adapted from the ADI-R, was administered to parents of both typically developing (TD) and ASD children. Using their responses, we explored the ability to ﬁne-tune pre-trained LLMs to predict whether the children have ASD. Particularly, we investigated LLM ﬁne-tuning strategies to analyze a questionnaire with 12 questions. In addition, we explored explainability methods to initially validate model reliability, which has not been previously explored in literature for questionnaire assessment. In summary, our contributions include: r We propose a framework to ﬁne-tune pre-trained LLMs on open-ended questionnaires responses as a tool for compu- tational psychiatry. r We provide a case study showcasing the potential of pre-trained LLMs for ASD screening through parental responses, achieving an accuracy of 84% and a ROC-AUC of 1, along with an exploration of interpretability. II. RELATED WORK Parental reports have proven valuable in ASD detection, as parents of autistic children tend to use more terms related to so- cial disinhibition, inadequate verbal interactions, and improper expression [22], suggesting that quantitative analysis of parental discourse could yield predictive insights. Studies using ADI-R have shown that parental reports can achieve higher classiﬁ- cation performance than the gold-standard ADOS [23], partic- ularly leveraging question domains tailored to speciﬁc symp- toms [24]. These ﬁndings support the potential of large-scale analyses of ADI-R responses for improving ASD assessments, yet no study has extensively examined open-ended parental reports in this context. The classiﬁcation of open-ended questionnaire responses falls under text classiﬁcation, a well-established NLP task. Early approaches relied on frequency-based representations such as TF-IDF, LIWC, sentiment analysis, and extracting lexical and syntactic features to train classiﬁcation models. In early approaches of open-ended questionnaire classiﬁcation, Spasic et al. [25] applied named entity recognition and sentiment analysis on responses to a ten-question patient questionnaire, generating feature vectors and training a separate model per question. This allowed each model to specialize in a particular domain but failed to leverage cross-question dependencies. Sim- ilarly, Badal et al. [26] used part-of-speech frequencies, pronoun usage, and sentiment scores to detect social isolation. However, they concatenated extracted features from all questionnaire responses into a single classiﬁcation model, thus incorporating subject-level information. Nevertheless, concatenating features may lead to a high number of dimensions as the number of questions in the questionnaire increases, limiting performance. Jayaratne et al. [27] focused on predicting personality traits using TF-IDF, LDA topics, Doc2Vec, and LIWC, but, like Spasic et al. [25], treated each response independently, limiting the potential beneﬁts of aggregating responses at the subject level. However, these traditional methods fail to capture the full semantic structure of open-ended responses, as they rely on pre- deﬁned term frequencies and sentiment scores without account- ing for broader contextual dependencies. Novel transformer- based models, such as BERT and GPT, have introduced a paradigm shift by leveraging contextualized language represen- tations through self-attention. These models are trained on next token prediction on large amounts of data, enabling complex lan- guage modeling and superior performance in NLP tasks, such as text classiﬁcation [28]. Yang et al. [29] demonstrated that large- scale pretrained LLMs can infer personality traits using zero- shotnexttokenpredictionfromfree-textresponsesbyprompting ALTOZANO et al.: ENHANCING PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENTS WITH OPEN-ENDED QUESTIONNAIRES AND LLMS 1709 models to simulate a multi-turn questionnaire, suggesting their potential for automating psychological assessments. Similarly, Ohse et al. [30] explored depression detection using direct LLM prompting, ﬁnding that dividing the questionnaire into symptom-based domains improved classiﬁcation accuracy. This approach was further validated by Feng et al. [31], who used a similar multi-domain framework to assess life satisfaction, prompting an LLM to score ﬁve quality-of-life dimensions before applying a machine learning classiﬁer. These ﬁndings highlight the advantages of domain clustering and large-scale LLM out-of-the-box language understanding for psychological questionnaire classiﬁcation. Another approach to leveraging transformer models involves embedding-based representations. These models are pre-trained or ﬁne-tunned on a contrastive objective aiming to capture semantic similarities between sentence pairs rather than per- forming generative tasks [32]. Strojny et al. [33] applied BERT- based embeddings to open-ended responses in a gaming disor- der assessment, using ridge regression to predict disorder risk. Harnisch et al. [34] explored a similar strategy for brief tran- scribed answer classiﬁcation in German medical close-ended questionnaires, evaluating whether including the question text improved prediction accuracy. However, both studies focused on question-level accuracy, potentially introducing biases where models learn user-speciﬁc patterns rather than the underlying psychological construct. Beyond embeddings, another strategy is ﬁne-tuning trans- former models on labeled datasets to improve classiﬁcation performance. Rashidiani et al. [35] demonstrated the effec- tiveness of ﬁne-tuning BERT on short close-ended question- naires, aggregating all questions and answers into a single text block. While this approach is useful for structured surveys, it is only applicable to short questionnaires due to the limited context window of LLMs. Ohse et al. [30] further validated ﬁne-tuning in clinical interviews, showing that training mod- els on structured interview sections improved classiﬁcation performance. While these previous studies have demonstrated the usability of NLP in open-ended questionnaire classiﬁcation, the diversity of the proposed strategies reveals a gap regarding a lack of agreement among these methodologies. Speciﬁcally, the litera- ture employs distinct approaches in three main areas: (1) Ques- tion representation, with some studies including the question in the sample, while others do not; (2) Classiﬁcation strategy, with some focusing on classifying single questions to leverage question-speciﬁc differences, others classifying all questions simultaneously, and yet others targeting subsets of questions tai- lored to speciﬁc knowledge domains; and (3) Transformer-based model, with recent work either using large-scale out-of-the-box generative LLMs for direct label prompting, small-scale gener- ative LLMs ﬁne-tuned for classiﬁcation, or LLMs pre-trained with contrastive loss used as embeddings and combined with a classiﬁcation head. Building on these ﬁndings, we compare these methodologies by evaluating their impact on our ASD case study. Furthermore, we provide an initial exploration and discussion of the interpretability methods employed within each modeling strategy, an aspect not yet thoroughly examined in the existing literature. These comparisons aim to reﬁne methodolog- ical best practices for applying LLMs to open-ended psycholog- ical questionnaire analysis. III. MATERIALS A. Participants This study engaged a cohort of 51 parents, comprising 26 parents with TD children and 25 parents with children diag- nosed with ASD. The entire sample consisted of 36 females and 15 males Spanish speakers with an age range from 30 to 45 years. The children’s ages ranged from 2 to 8 years. For inclusion in the ASD group, caregivers’ children were required to have a conﬁrmed ASD diagnosis and no comorbidity with cognitive, language, neurodevelopmental, anxiety or personality disorders. All children of the caregivers belonging to the ASD group were receiving a therapeutic treatment from a primary care center specialized in neurodevelopmental disorders. ASD children started intervention after the ASD diagnosis, which was received between the 2 and 5 years of life. Regarding the TD group, caregivers of the TD children had to report no previous diagnosis or risk of cognitive, neurodevelopmental, language, anxiety and personality disorders in their children. Prior to participation, caregivers provided informed consent, adhering to the ethical guidelines approved by the UPV Ethics Committee (Application No. P_06_04_06_20, approved on July 4, 2020), in compliance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Participation into the study was voluntary, and participants’ recruitment was made through the social media and the Development Neurocognitive Centre Red Cenit (Valencia, Spain). B. Questionnaire and Data Collection The questionnaire developed for this study comprises 12 open-ended questions, crafted to mirror the structure and psy- chological dimensions of the ADI-R [16]. These questions are distributed across three key dimensions [36]: 6 questions target communicationandsocial interaction; 3questions explorerepet- itive behaviors and motor skills; and the remaining 3 questions focus on language abilities. Participants answered the questionnaire verbally to enhance spontaneity. Questions were presented on a laptop screen, and participants responded using a microphone. Data collection took place in a quiet room to reduce external noise and maintain controlled recording conditions. First, participants completed a questionnairesaskingforsocio-demographicinformation.Then, the 12 open-ended questions were proposed and the audio of participants’ responses was captured using a Blue Yeti micro- phone (Logitech) at a 25 cm distance from the participant. These settings ensured high-quality and reliable audio for fur- ther analysis. Following the collection of audio responses, a transcription process was undertaken to convert these verbal responses into text. The transcription process involved capturing not only the semantic content but also integrating non-semantic characteristics. For instance, verbal ﬁllers such as “eh”, “mm”, “ah”, and instances of laughter were encoded using a uniform 1710 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 TABLE I LIST OF QUESTIONS USED AND THEIR CORRESPONDING PSYCHOLOGICAL DIMENSIONS codiﬁcation scheme. Additionally, instances of incomplete or repeated words and sentences were also encoded. Table I presents the original Spanish questions translated into English for the purposes of this paper. IV. METHODS A. Dataset Construction The dataset for this study consisted of transcriptions from 51 participants. However, one participant was excluded for failing to complete more than half of the open-ended questions. Additionally, some participants did not answer all questions, resulting in a total of 593 individual responses. To ensure data quality, a review was performed for any explicit mentions of ASD diagnosis or participation in ASD-related in- terventions on all 593 responses. While no direct mentions were found, it is noted that “therapy” was mentioned in 6 responses, though the speciﬁc reason for therapy was not stated. Addition- ally, “pictograms” were mentioned in 5 responses; while not conclusive, these are commonly associated with communication strategies used in ASD interventions. In this study, each response was treated as a separate data sample. This approach was chosen for several key reasons. First, modern NLP models have limitations on input length, and concatenating all responses into a single sample could exceed these limits. Second, treating each response as a distinct sample enhances resilience when questionnaires have missing answers, whereas concatenating responses could degrade performance if one or more responses are absent. B. Modeling Strategies In our experimental design, we applied various training strate- gies for questionnaire classiﬁcation. Fig. 1 presents our three proposed core strategies, motivated by insights from previous literature. 1) Frozen vs Fully Fine-Tuned Models: A key decision when ﬁne-tuning pre-trained LLM is whether to freeze the model’s layers or allow full ﬁne-tuning. Freezing layers can help mitigate castastrophic forgetting [37], especially in small datasets, by preserving pre-trained features. However, ﬁne-tuning all layers can enable the model to learn more task-speciﬁc features, Fig. 1. Methodology overview. which might improve performance on specialized tasks like classiﬁcation [38]. In this work, we tested three strategies, namely: a) Fully Fine-tuned LLM (Unfrozen): This strategy employs a multilingual RoBERTa [39] base model with a clas- siﬁcation head, where all layers of the LLM are updated during training. This allows the model to adapt to the speciﬁc dataset re- ﬁning its features to better suit the task. However, this method is computationally expensive and increases the risk of catastrophic forgetting, especially given the limited dataset size. b) Frozen LLM: In this approach, the RoBERTa model’s layers are frozen, and only the classiﬁcation head is ﬁne-tuned. This reduces complexity by keeping the pre-trained features unchanged. This method can stabilize training and prevent over- ﬁtting, particularly with smaller datasets [40], which might lead to enhanced results [41], [42]. However, since pre-trained LLMs are generally not designed for downstream classiﬁcation tasks, freezing the model could limit its ability to generate optimal features for classiﬁcation [43]. c) Embedding Model: To further explore the utility of ﬁxed representations, we also included a frozen embed- ding model — OpenAI’s text-ada-embedding-large-v3 [32] — with a classiﬁcation head. Unlike generative pre-trained LLMs, embedding models are explicitly designed for tasks such as ALTOZANO et al.: ENHANCING PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENTS WITH OPEN-ENDED QUESTIONNAIRES AND LLMS 1711 semantic similarity and classiﬁcation. As such, their pre-trained features are more suited to downstream tasks like ours, offering a compelling comparison to task-speciﬁc features learned during full ﬁne-tuning. In all three approaches, the classiﬁcation heads are comprised of a single layer that receives the output size of the pre-trained model as input and generates a single output with sigmoid activation, predicting class probabilities for ASD vs. TD classiﬁ- cation. By comparing these strategies, we aim to assess whether ﬁne-tuning the pre-trained LLM yields better performance by learning task-speciﬁc features, or whether frozen, pre-trained features are more effective for our classiﬁcation task. 2) Implicit or Explicit Questions: As highlighted by Harnisch et al. [34], another signiﬁcant consideration when dealing with questionnaire data is whether to explicitly include the ques- tion text along with each response. This involves deciding if each sample should consist of only the answer (implicit representation) or both the question and the answer (explicit representation). This distinction relates to recent work in prompt engineering, which involves designing prompts that inﬂuence how the pre- trained models represent features in their feature space. The goal is to guide the model into a speciﬁc representation that improves performance in downstream tasks [44], [45], [46]. In our case, adding the question to each response can be seen as a form of prompt engineering, as it potentially limits the model’s feature space and allows it to better differentiate between responses based on the context of the question. For example, the response “No, I don’t think he ever does that” could mean different things depending on whether the question is “Does your child use phrases or make sounds repetitively?” or “Does your child have interests in speciﬁc objects?”. By includ- ing the question, the model is expected to project the response into a more structured space that aligns with speciﬁc question contexts, effectively improving classiﬁcation. Alternatively, us- ing the implicit approach, where only the response is provided, could allow the model to learn more global patterns. It might focus on certain expressions or phrases that are highly indicative of ASD regardless of the question context. This more general approach could enable the model to recognize commonalities across responses, independent of the speciﬁc question. 3) Model Speciﬁcity vs Generalizability: Another important research question involves determining the level of speciﬁcity required when ﬁne-tuning models for questionnaire-based data. Typically, ﬁne-tuning a pre-trained model involves training it for a single, well-deﬁned downstream task [40]. However, our questionnaire spans multiple domains related to ASD, raising the question of whether training specialized models improves classiﬁcation performance compared to a single general model. Speciﬁcally, typically ASD assessment tools like the ADOS [47], or ADI-R [16], are organized into speciﬁc domains, each designed to assess different aspects of mental health or behavior. In our case, the questions in the questionnaire are organized into three distinct domains related to ASD symptoms. While we are trying to classify subjects based on their answers, each domain may require its own expert model to capture the unique characteristics present in that domain. To address this, we implemented three strategies with varying levels of speciﬁcity: a) Fine-Tuned by Question: One model is ﬁne-tuned per question (∼1/12 of the dataset per model). This approach maximizes question-speciﬁc specialization, ensuring that each classiﬁer captures the nuances of individual questions, similar to the approach taken by Spasic et al. [25]. However, training one model per question signiﬁcantly reduces the available training data per model, which could negatively affect performance. b) Fine-Tuned by Expert Domain: One model is ﬁne- tuned per domain (∼1/3 of the dataset per model). This follows the domain-clustering strategy, which has been shown to en- hance classiﬁcation performance by limiting the model’s focus to a speciﬁc area of expertise [30]. Clustering related questions into symptom-based domains has previously yielded improve- ments in generalization, as models beneﬁt from increased train- ing data while still maintaining domain-speciﬁc granularity. c) Fine-Tuned with All: A single model is ﬁne-tuned across all questions (the entire dataset). Some studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of training models on full ques- tionnaire responses, as this allows for cross-domain learning and helps capture global response patterns [30], [35]. However, this approach may come at the cost of losing domain-speciﬁc knowl- edge, as a general-purpose model might struggle to distinguish ﬁne-grained differences across expert domains. C. Finetuning, Validation and Testing To rigorously evaluate the proposed strategies, our training and testing procedure followed a nested subject-wise stratiﬁed cross-validation approach to prevent data leakage and maintain class balance. This methodology involves two nested cross- validation loops: an outer loop for model validation and an inner loop for hyperparameter selection. We ﬁrst performed a 5-fold outer subject-wise split, ensuring that each participant’s data was assigned exclusively to either the training or test set. Within each outer training set, hyperparameter tuning was conducted through a 4-fold inner cross-validation loop. For each model architecture (Embedding, Frozen RoBERTa, Unfrozen RoBERTa) and training strategy (by question, by expert domain, or all questions), a grid search was performed over three hyper- parameters: learning rate (ϵ ∈[1e-4, 1e-5, 1e-6]), batch size (ϵ ∈[8,16,24]), and the decay factor for a linear learning rate scheduler with warm-up (ϵ ∈[1e-1, 1e-2, 1e-3]). This resulted in 27 unique hyperparameter combinations. For each of these 27 combinations, models were trained across the 4 inner folds, leading to a total of 108 model trainings per outer fold for each proposed strategy. During each inner fold training, the inner test set was used to monitor performance metrics and for early stopping, with a maximum of 30 epochs. Once all 4 inner folds for a given hyperparameter combination were completed, the best performing hyperparameters were selected based on the lowest mean validation loss across folds. Following hyperparameter selection, the model was trained on the full outer training set. A separate validation set was created from 20% of this outer training data, ensuring it was subject-wise to prevent any data leakage. This validation set was 1712 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 exclusively used for early stopping to prevent overﬁtting, based on the validation loss. Training was performed for a maximum of 30 epochs, employing a linear learning rate scheduler applied over 29 epochs, with a warm-up period during the ﬁrst epoch. After training, the model from the best epoch was retrieved and evaluated on all samples in the unseen outer test set. Finally, the test predictions were evaluated using both a question-wise and a subject-wise evaluation (refer to Subsection IV-D). Speciﬁcally, the metrics used on both evaluations in- clude accuracy (percentage of subjects correctly classiﬁed), true positive rate (correct identiﬁcation of ASD parents; TPR), true negativerate(correctidentiﬁcationofcontrolparents;TNR),and ROC-AUC (the model’s ability to distinguish between positive and negative cases). D. Questions Predictions and Ensemble of Predictions In our study, we evaluated performance at both the question level and the subject level, as they provide different insights into model effectiveness: a) Question-wise performance: Each of the 593 responses was treated as an individual data point, labeled according to the participant who provided it. For each question, we aggregated predictions by calculating performance metrics speciﬁc to that question. This means we analyzed the responses separately to derive insights relevant to each individual question, allowing us to assess how well the model performed across different aspects of the questionnaire. b) Subject-wise performance: Since our primary goal was to classify participants using the entire questionnaire rather than individual responses, we derived subject-level predictions using an ensemble method. Speciﬁcally, for each participant, the ﬁnal prediction was generated by averaging the classiﬁcation probabilities across all 12 responses. This technique, known as unweighted model averaging, aggregated the individual predic- tions into a single, subject-level classiﬁcation [48]. Nevertheless, even though question-wise performance might be relevant to exploring which questions were the most effective towards classiﬁcation, we will focus on subject-wise perfor- mance as our main performance evaluation metric. E. Zero-Shot LLM Prompting In addition to our ﬁne-tuning approach for small models, we also aim to evaluate the effectiveness of zero-shot prompting a large-scale LLM for ASD questionnaire classiﬁcation. Specif- ically, we compare the performance of our proposed models with GPT-4o, a proprietary large-scale LLM, to assess the capa- bilities of out-of-the-box models for psychological assessment. Prior studies have demonstrated that LLMs possess strong prior knowledge in psychological evaluation tasks without requir- ing additional training [29], [30], [31]. Given this, we explore whether prompt-based classiﬁcation using GPT-4o can surpass ﬁne-tuned small models, despite the differences in computa- tional requirements, as deploying models of this scale requires substantially more VRAM and GPU power. For this experiment, we evaluate GPT-4o directly on the same outer test splits as the ﬁne-tuned models, ensuring a fair Fig. 2. Turn-based GPT-4o prompting structure. comparison. Instead of training the model, we prompt it to predictsubjectlabelsusingallquestionnaireresponsesinastruc- tured, turn-based approach. This prompt format is inspired by Yang et al. [29], who demonstrated that structuring interactions as multi-turn assessments improves LLMs’ ability to reason through complex classiﬁcation tasks. The full prompt structure used for GPT-4o is illustrated in Fig. 2. F. Model Interpretability Understanding the decision-making process of deep learning models is critical for their deployment, particularly in sensitive domains like psychology, where transparency enhances trust and reliability. To address this, two distinct interpretability method- ologies were employed: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). First, PCA was applied to each sample model’s embeddings. This method aimed to visualize the primary components within the embedding space. The application of PCA served two pur- poses: (i) to identify potential clusters of questions, reﬂecting whether their contextual differences were distinctly represented, and (ii) to identify if a comprehensible separation between ASD and TD classes emerged within these question-speciﬁc or global embedding clusters. Such visualizations provide a high-level understanding of how the model differentiates between question and classes. For a new test subject, visualizing their ques- tion embeddings within these PCA-derived clusters could offer insights into the model’s output by indicating their proximity to established ASD or TD patterns. Second, SHAP was utilized as a local, more granular inter- pretability method [49]. SHAP is a method based on game theory ALTOZANO et al.: ENHANCING PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENTS WITH OPEN-ENDED QUESTIONNAIRES AND LLMS 1713 TABLE II SUBJECT-WISE TEST RESULTS FOR VARIOUS TRAINING STRATEGIES, INCLUDING THEIR TRAINING TIMES PER OUTER FOLD that quantiﬁes the contribution of each input feature (token) to a model prediction. By calculating the marginal contribution of each feature value across all possible feature coalitions, SHAP provides a token-level importance score. A positive SHAP value indicates its inﬂuence in pushing the prediction towards the positive class (ASD), while a negative value signiﬁes its contri- bution towards the negative class (TD). This method effectively backpropagates the model’s decision to identify inﬂuential tex- tual elements. For this study, SHAP values were calculated on the original spanish responses, and then assigned to the closest english translation. The applicability of these techniques varied across models. While PCA was applicable to both OpenAI embeddings and RoBERTa by extracting their [CLS] token embeddings, SHAP requires full access to a model’s architecture. Consequently, SHAP values were only computable for the RoBERTa models. V. RESULTS A. Questionnaire Classiﬁcation Performance Table II(a)–(c) summarize the subject-wise test results (refer to Section IV-D). Each table presents the mean and standard deviation for test set subject-wise metrics, where every sample of a subject contributes a vote based on the model trained for that question. We also report the mean training time (in hours per outer fold) for each modeling strategy, conducted using an NVIDIA RTX 6000 GPU with 24 GB of VRAM. Firstly, among the trained models, the most consistent and highest-performingmodelacrossalltrainingstrategies,irrespec- tive of whether the questions were explicitly included in the dataset, was the embedding model with a classiﬁcation head. This model demonstrated less variability in ROC-AUC scores compared to other models, which exhibited greater ﬂuctuation in test results. Notably, in all experiments, the average ROC-AUC exceeded0.93,andinsomecases,itreached1.00acrossallfolds. These results indicate that the embedding model, particularly when paired with a question-speciﬁc classiﬁcation head, was able to achieve perfect class separation. However, this separa- tion was not centered around the 0.5 probability threshold, as reﬂected in the accuracy and TPR metrics. In terms of parameter freezing, signiﬁcant performance differ- ences emerged when comparing frozen versus fully ﬁne-tuned approaches on the same pre-trained language model. This effect was particularly evident in the case of training individual models per question, where mean accuracy differences reached as high as 30%. On the other hand, implicitly and explicitly adding the ques- tion to the samples had a lower impact compared to changes in model architecture or ﬁne-tuning. However, including the ques- tion in the input generally improved or maintained performance 1714 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 Fig. 3. Question-wise test results and voting system aggregation effect. across models. Although these differences were not signiﬁcant, theywereconsistentacrossalltrainingstrategies,suggestingthat explicit inclusion of question context provides a slight advantage in classiﬁcation performance. Beyond classiﬁcation performance, training overhead is an- other important consideration. Speciﬁcally, the classiﬁcation head trained with OpenAI embedding models, demonstrated the fastest training times, below an hour of training by fold, accounting for the inner cross-validation for hyperparameter selection. In contrast, RoBERTa-based models, incurred longer training durations, due to the increased overhead of locally running the LLM. The most computationally intensive approach was the fully ﬁne-tuned strategy, where the entire RoBERTa was optimized, elapsing over 13 hours by outer fold. In addition to the trained models, we evaluated the zero-shot GPT-4o model, whose results are summarized in Table II(d). Notably, GPT-4o achieved an accuracy of 86%, which matches the performance of the best ﬁne-tuned embedding model. Addi- tionally, GPT-4o displayed a high TNR (96%), indicating strong performance in correctly identifying control parents. These re- sults suggest that out-of-the-box large-scale LLMs can provide competitive classiﬁcation performance without requiring ﬁne- tuning, despite their signiﬁcantly higher computational require- ments. However, due to the lack of ROC-AUC values, a direct comparison with trained models remains limited, and further analysis is required to assess its robustness in distinguishing ASD and control cases. B. Impact of Aggregation on Predictions After analyzing the minimal impact of implicit versus explicit question representation, we further examined how different ﬁne-tuning strategies inﬂuenced performance, irrespective of how the questions were incorporated into training. Fig. 3(a) presents the question-wise ROC-AUC distributions, showing that models trained on all questions generally achieved the highest question-wise ROC-AUC. For instance, the embedding model trained with all questions achieved 0.80 ± 0.17, outper- forming the model trained by question (0.73 ± 0.20) and the one trained by expert domain (0.79 ± 0.17). Similar trends were observed across all architectures, where models trained with a larger dataset outperformed those trained by question. This suggests that larger datasets generally improve question-wise performance across all ﬁne-tuning strategies. While Fig. 3(a) highlights the question-wise advantage of training with larger datasets, the subject-wise results reveal a more nuanced picture. As shown in Table II(a)–(c), the em- bedding model trained by question-speciﬁc ﬁne-tuning achieved the highest subject-wise ROC-AUC after predictions were ag- gregated through the voting system. Similarly, for the un- frozen LLM, the model trained by question outperformed the one trained with all questions. Thus, this discrepancy between question-wise and subject-wise performance should have arisen as an effect of the voting system. To further analyze the impact of the voting system, we present Fig. 3(b), which illustrates how subject-wise ALTOZANO et al.: ENHANCING PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENTS WITH OPEN-ENDED QUESTIONNAIRES AND LLMS 1715 Fig. 4. Visualizations of the applied interpretability methods. performance evolved as more questions were progressively in- cluded in the voting system, starting with those achieving the highest question-wise ROC-AUC. This analysis revealed that models trained by question beneﬁted more from the voting system compared to those trained with all questions or by di- mension. For instance, the embedding model trained by question saw an average ROC-AUC increase of 0.015 per added question, compared to 0.010 for the model trained with all questions. Additionally, the standard deviation decreased more rapidly for models trained by question, indicating that the voting system helped stabilize performance for these models. In summary, while models trained with all questions achieved better question-wise ROC-AUC, models trained by question showed greater beneﬁts from the voting system, both in terms of improved subject-wise performance and reduced variability. C. Interpretability Fig. 4 presents a visualization from our interpretability analy- ses using PCA and SHAP values, offering insights into how the models process and differentiate between classes. Fig. 4(a) shows the 2D visualization of the embedding spaces across models. For the OpenAI text-ada-embedding-large-v3, we observed noticeable separation in the embedding space based on the question number. A similar, though slightly less pronounced, question-based separation was also observed in the PCA of the frozen RoBERTa embeddings. However, when ex- amining these representations in terms of the ASD vs. TD label, no clear clusters were discernible in either the OpenAI or frozen RoBERTa embedding spaces. This indicates that while these models are adept at capturing general semantic information, they don’t inherently separate the classes based on the psychological construct of interest. Contrarily, the ﬁne-tuned RoBERTa model, whose weights were updated for ASD classiﬁcation, generated an embedding space with a clearer separation between TD and ASD. However, while this clustering provides a high-level qualitative explana- tion for a prediction, it does not offer a granular understanding of which speciﬁc parts of the response the model focused on to make its decision, limiting interpretability. On the other hand, SHAP values (Fig. 4(b)) provide greater explainability. These values highlight the contribution of each 1716 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 30, NO. 2, FEBRUARY 2026 token to the model’s prediction, demonstrating how the model identiﬁed key linguistic features for classiﬁcation, enabling quantitative and transparent interpetability. For instance, the model consistently highlighted tokens as- sociated with common ASD traits. In an example detailing repetitive and speciﬁc interests, phrases like “obsessive”, “tow- ers”, (plays) “alone” and “placing (objects) one on top of each other”, were strongly inﬂuential towards the ASD classiﬁcation. Similarly, when describing repetitive movements or sounds, phrases such as “repetitions” and “imitates certain sound or a word and he repeats it several times” were also accurately detected as typical ASD behaviors. The model also identiﬁed atypical non-verbal communication patterns. For instance, when discussing non-verbal expressions, phrases like “doesn’t ﬁx his gaze”, “doesn’t maintain eye contact”, and “avoiding to look at your face” were correctly highlighted as contributing to the ASD label, indicating difﬁculties with direct eye contact. Furthermore, in another example that detailed how a child com- municates desires and needs for help, key phrases such as “opens his mouth and points inside with his ﬁnger”, “grabs my hand and takes me to the fridge”,and “opens it and points at the milk” were highly inﬂuential, showcasing the capacity to identify reliance on gestures over verbal requests. Conversely, the model also effectively highlighted behaviors associated with TD children. For example, the model identiﬁed negative SHAP values associated with “No, he does not have interests like that” in an instance where the parent was asked whether his child shows repetitive interests. The indication of the absence of these traits pushed the prediction towards the TD label. Similarly, for repetitive movements, phrases like “he does not exhibit” received values that aligned with a TD classiﬁcation. Additionally, in a TD-classiﬁed response describing sharing and social play, phrases such as “shares everything”, “used to sharing”, “do share”, and “likes us playing with them too” were correctly emphasized as indicators of typical social interaction. Furthermore, responses indicating that a child “makes himself perfectly understood” in moments of need or “express(es) emo- tions with his face” would similarly show strong contributions towards the TD label, showcasing the model’s ability to recog- nize typical communicative and expressive behaviors. However, our SHAP analyses also revealed limitations in the model’s interpretability, likely inﬂuenced by the small sample size. For instance, in models where explicit question prompts were included in the input, the model sometimes assigned high SHAP values to the question itself, which is not inherently related to the ﬁnal classiﬁcation label. Such instances suggest that while the model leverages contextual information, its in- terpretations are not always perfectly aligned with direct clini- cal relevance. Additionally, some misinterpretations occurred, such as the phrase “but in fact when we are in a place like a shopping center or shopping at a supermarket”, which was incorrectly highlighted as contributing to the ASD label, even though it appears to be semantically neutral. Another example of misinterpretation was the phrase “he’s come out German” being incorrectly emphasized as TD. These errors could be due to the model’s limited exposure to diverse linguistic nuances in a smaller dataset, or potentially reﬂect limitations of the SHAP explanation model itself in accurately reﬂecting the nuanced decision-making of the classiﬁcation model. VI. DISCUSSION A. Implicit vs Explicit Question Representation Our experimental results indicate that explicitly adding the question to the response had minimal to no impact on ﬁne-tuning the classiﬁcation task. However, in nearly all cases — except for the unfrozen LLM trained on the full dataset — explicitly including the question in the input consistently matched or out- performedtheimplicitapproach,evenwhenmodelsweretrained on individual questions. This ﬁnding may seem counterintuitive, as one might expect that, in a single-question classiﬁcation, the question text itself would not be necessary. The performance difference may arise because including the question enhances the model’s contextual understanding by re- ﬁning its representation space within both pre-trained language models and embedding models. Providing the question as part of theinputoffersadditionalcontext,whichisespeciallyhelpfulfor responses that are brief or vague, such as “No” or “Sometimes, I don’t know.” This is particularly true for the OpenAI embedding model, where the accuracy slightly increased with explicit ques- tions. This enhanced contextual understanding is also visually supported by the PCA visualization, which shows that explicitly adding the questions helps the model better interpret the speciﬁc context of each answer. Despite this general trend, the results for the unfrozen RoBERTa model trained on the full dataset showed a slight wors- ening of performance when the question was explicitly added. This might be explained by our SHAP analyses, which revealed that in some instances, the model assigned an incorrect SHAP value to the question itself, rather than keeping its contribution neutral to the classiﬁcation. Despite this, the results do not conclusively demonstrate that one approach is deﬁnitively superior. Therefore, we recommend opting for the approach that is easiest to implement, with a slight preference for explicitly including the question, specially when working with embeddings pre-trained for text similarity such as the OpenAI embedding model. B. Frozen vs Fully Fine-Tuned Models: Dependence on Pre-Training Strategy Across all experiments, the OpenAI embedding model out- performed both the frozen and unfrozen RoBERTa model, sug- gesting that pre-trained embeddings are highly effective for capturing general language patterns. These embeddings are op- timized for various natural language tasks, with a strong focus on semantic similarity. By using the embedding model represen- tations unchanged, we likely preserved a stable and high-quality feature space that the classiﬁcation head could leverage. Indeed, the PCA visualization for the OpenAI embeddings (Fig. 4(a)) revealed a noticeable separation in the embedding space based on question number, indicating its strong ability to capture contextual differences. This stability was evident in the perfor- mance consistency across different training approaches, whether ALTOZANO et al.: ENHANCING PSYCHOLOGICAL ASSESSMENTS WITH OPEN-ENDED QUESTIONNAIRES AND LLMS 1717 training on individual questions, expert domains, or the full dataset. In contrast, freezing the layers of the RoBERTa while ﬁne- tuning only the classiﬁcation head led to suboptimal perfor- mance. In this case, the model likely struggled to adapt its inter- nal representations to our speciﬁc classiﬁcation task, as the pre- training objectives of the LLM may have been too different from our task’s requirements. This can be qualitatively explained by the PCA visualization of the model embeddings, where despite there being a slight separation between questions, ASD and TD examples lie on similar clusters, with no clear separation. Since only the classiﬁcation head was updated, the model had limited ability to learn domain-speciﬁc features, leading to performance issues, particularly when training on individual questions. This is acknowledged in the results, where despite separating classes well (as indicated by the ROC-AUC), the model failed to classify within the deﬁned threshold, resulting in a TNR of 0% and a random accuracy of 50%. Finally, the fully trained version of RoBERTa performed better than its frozen counterpart, as it could ﬁne-tune both its feature representations and the classiﬁcation head, adapt- ing more effectively to the task. This aligns with ﬁndings from the literature, where ﬁne-tuning the entire model typi- cally yields better results than freezing layers [43]. The PCA visualization for the fully ﬁne-tuned RoBERTa model also showed more noticeable separation between the ASD and TD labels compared to its frozen counterpart, suggesting that the ﬁne-tuning process successfully adapted the model’s internal embedding space for better class distinction. However, even with this improvement, the fully ﬁne-tuned model still did not perform as well as the OpenAI embedding models, which consistently delivered higher ROC-AUC scores and more stable results. C. Speciﬁcity vs Generalizability: Dependence on Training Dataset Size In Fig. 3(b), we observe several trends regarding training strategies and dataset size. Notably, as the training dataset in- creases from one question per model to all questions in a single model, the question-wise ROC-AUC improves. However, there are little differences in subject-wise performance between mod- els trained on individual expert domains versus models trained on the full dataset. This is because, as seen in Fig. 3(b), while models trained on more focused domains with smaller datasets have an inferior question-wise performance, their contribution in the voting system is higher, whereas ﬁne-tuning less models on bigger data seems to scale worse in performance with the use of a voting system. We hypothesize that with larger questionnaire responses datasets, the lack of improvement seen in the more general modelswoulddiminish.Withmoredata,themodelcouldlearnto differentiate between classes across various question domains, developing a richer, more nuanced understanding of the class differences. This would allow the model to generalize better, capturing cross-domain patterns and improving overall perfor- mance. That said, given the limited size of subjects and questions of our dataset, the strategy of training one model per question providedthebestandmostconsistentresultsforboththeOpenAI embeddings and fully ﬁnetuned RoBERTa. This approach likely actedasaformofregularization,aseachquestion-speciﬁcmodel focused on a narrower scope, reducing the risk of overﬁtting. While a generalized model might have a higher sample-wise ROC-AUC, aggregating predictions from multiple questions does not provide additional information when relying on a single classiﬁer. In contrast, with question-speciﬁc models, each classiﬁer offers its own unique decision function, capturing dis- tinct linguistic or thematic patterns. These diverse, specialized predictions are combined during the voting process, leading to better overall performance. D. Fine-Tuned Small LLM vs Large-Scale Proprietary Models: Inﬂuence on Interpretability Our experiments encompassed a spectrum of model architec- tures, each presenting unique trade-offs between performance, deployability, and interpretability. This included ﬁne-tuned, lo- cally deployable small LLMs (RoBERTa), large-scale embed- ding models (OpenAI embeddings), and zero-shot evaluations with a large-scale LLM (GPT-4o). The zero-shot evaluation using GPT-4o achieved an accuracy of 86% (Table II(d)), comparable to our best-performing ﬁne- tuned models. This suggests that large, general-purpose LLMs can effectively analyze open-ended questionnaire responses without task-speciﬁc training, leveraging their broad linguistic modeling capabilities. Such models beneﬁt from extensive pre- training on diverse text domains, enabling high generalization even to specialized domains like ASD screening. However, de- ploying large LLMs like GPT-4o requires substantial computa- tional resources, often necessitating cloud-based services, which raises signiﬁcant privacy concerns as sensitive medical data must be transmitted to third-party providers. Another critical limitation for these proprietary, black-box models is their lack of interpretability, since direct access to their internal mechanisms or representations is unavailable, making intuitive analyses like PCA or detailed analyses like SHAP impossible. In contrast, our ﬁne-tuned RoBERTa models, representing small, locally deployable LLMs, achieved slightly lower per- formance while offering several key advantages. This approach preserves data privacy by processing information locally and reduces computational overhead, making it feasible for integra- tion into a wider range of clinical environments.
