D. Motivations As wireless communication systems advance toward 6G, they face unprecedented challenges in required intelligence levels, dynamic adaptability, and system efﬁciency [26]. Leveraging their massive parameter scales (typically reaching billions or even trillions of parameters), emergent capabilities, and powerful cognitive reasoning, LAMs are progressively transforming the application landscape of AI in commu- nications. These models demonstrate effective support for complex communication tasks, including semantic commu- nication, resource scheduling, and network self-optimization [20]. In 6G networks, LAMs can fulﬁll the following functional roles [27]: 1) Data Generator: As generative AI models with strong generalization and representation capabilities, LAMs can efﬁciently generate various types of communication data based on domain knowledge. By incorporating advanced generative architectures (e.g., autoregressive decoders, diffusion models), LAMs can synthesize high-quality Channel State Information (CSI) data to support critical tasks such as positioning estimation, bandwidth alloca- tion, and network architecture design. Such synthetic data exhibits realism while preserving user anonymity, providing cost-effective and reliable data support for 6G network modeling, optimization, and deployment without compromising privacy [20]. 2) Knowledge Organizer: With powerful cross-modal semantic reasoning and knowledge integration capabil- ities, LAMs can structurally process and deeply mine raw communication data to enable automated knowledge extraction and reorganization. In semantic communi- cation systems, LAMs can serve as knowledge bases to assist semantic encoding processes. Leveraging their extensive world knowledge and communication exper- tise, they effectively reduce ambiguity, improve semantic alignment quality, and enhance information transmission accuracy and contextual adaptability, thereby supporting the development of intelligent semantic representation and understanding frameworks [28]. 3) Resource Manager: Through real-time perception and modeling of network environmental states, user behavior patterns, and resource utilization efﬁciency, LAMs facilitate intelligent sensing and optimal allo- cation of communication resources. Integrated with Reinforcement Learning (RL) or long-chain reasoning frameworks, LAMs can dynamically formulate man- agement decisions such as spectrum allocation, power control, and access strategies in multi-user, multi-service scenarios, thereby enhancing overall system efﬁciency and fairness. Additionally, LAMs can predict future resource demand trends, enabling proactive network planning and QoS assurance. The key distinction between LAMs and Agentic AI lies in their working methods and intelligent decision-making capa- bilities. LAMs typically respond within ﬁxed input patterns and generate outputs through known knowledge, but lack autonomy and adaptability. In contrast, Agentic AI possesses the ability for proactive decision-making and self-optimization, enabling it to make independent decisions in complex and dynamic environments while actively learning and adjusting during task execution. This allows Agentic AI to handle more complex communication tasks, particularly in dynamic envi- ronments, where it can respond in real-time and continuously optimize its behavior. In 6G communications, Agentic AI can play the following roles: 1) Task Scheduler: Agentic AI has the ability to com- prehend complex instructions, assign subtasks, and coordinate multi-module execution, enabling it to serve as the core task scheduler in complex communication scenarios. It can dynamically deploy different algorith- mic modules and collaboratively generate solutions that meet speciﬁc task objectives. For instance, in multi-UAV cooperative communication scenarios, Agentic AI can autonomously plan service areas and ﬂight paths, avoid obstacles, and allocate various communication tasks to establish stable links while providing computation, storage, and communication services in emergency situ- ations, thereby signiﬁcantly enhancing system autonomy and response efﬁciency [29]. 2) System Designer: Leveraging its strong task comprehension and complex system logic modeling capabilities, Agentic AI can automatically design system architectures and conﬁgure modules based on the functional requirements of communication systems. In AI-integrated communication tasks, Agentic AI can combine knowledge from federated learning, resource scheduling, protocol stacks, and other domains to understand the design intent and operational Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Through prompt tuning and policy feedback, it iteratively optimizes communication system performance, demonstrating highly intelligent system design potential [30]. 3) Decision Executor: Agentic AI not only generates decisions but also directly executes them through interactions with algorithms, tools, and the network environment. In network fault detection and self-healing scenarios, Agentic AI continuously monitors network status, identiﬁes anomalies such as node failures or congestion, and autonomously performs recovery oper- ations in real time, such as trafﬁc rerouting or topology reconﬁguration. By integrating multidimensional inputs (e.g., latency, QoS degradation, and link reliability) and leveraging RL, causal reasoning, and meta-learning, it achieves optimal trade-offs among recovery speed, reliability, and energy efﬁciency. RL serves not only as the key mechanism for decision-making but also as a critical means of enhancing the agent itself, enabling continuous optimization through interaction and feedback. Moreover, Agentic AI can interact with network simulation platforms (e.g., NS-3, Mininet) to validate decision effectiveness prior to real-world deployment [31]. E. Contributions This tutorial, set against the backdrop of the intelligent evolution of communication systems toward the 6G era, sys- tematically reviews and thoroughly examines the pivotal roles of LAMs and Agentic AI in future intelligent communication systems. It offers a comprehensive overview from multiple perspectives, including model classiﬁcation, training method- ologies, Agentic AI system design, application scenarios, and research challenges. The main contributions of this work are summarized in the following ﬁve aspects. 1) Systematic Review of Core Components and Model Classiﬁcation in LAMs: A comprehensive synthesis of core components, including Transformer, Vision Trans- former (ViT), Variational AutoEncoder (VAE), Diffusion models, Diffusion Transformer (DiT), and Mixture of Experts (MoE), is provided, along with a classiﬁcation and comparative analysis of mainstream model types such as LLMs, LVMs, LMMs, LRMs, and lightweight LAMs. This synthesis clariﬁes the respective applica- bility and technical potential of each model category in communication systems. 2) Design of Datasets and Learning Mechanisms for Communication-speciﬁc LAMs: Addressing chal- lenges such as scarce domain knowledge, high task complexity, and diverse application requirements in communications, we propose a design framework for communication-speciﬁc LAMs. This framework features methodologies for constructing communication-relevant datasets, internal learning mechanisms encompassing pre-training, ﬁne-tuning, and alignment, as well as exter- nal learning mechanisms including RAG, KG and graph RAG. These components collectively ensure model efﬁ- cacy and usability in communication scenarios. 3) Construction of LAM-based Agentic AI Frame- work From Communication Perspective: A systematic integration of LAMs with Agentic AI technologies to establish a communication-oriented Agentic AI archi- tecture. First, we identify core system components including agents, planners, knowledge bases, tools, and memory modules. Then, we examine interaction patterns for multi-agent systems. Finally, we propose an inte- grated framework featuring multi-agent data retrieval, multi-agent collaborative planning, and multi-agent eval- uative reﬂection to support the intelligent processing of complex communication tasks. II. KEY CONCEPTS A. Components 1) Transformer: The Transformer is a novel neural net- work architecture proposed by Google in 2017 [32]. Its core innovation relies entirely on the self-attention mechanism to capture dependencies within the input sequence, and the cross- attention mechanism to connect the encoder and decoder. Self-attention is a key technique in the Transformer archi- tecture. It enables the model to consider all other words (or tokens) in the sequence when processing a particular word, computing weighted representations based on relevance. Additionally, Google introduced multi-head self-attention to compute attention in parallel, allowing the model to learn information from different representation subspaces [32]. The computation of self-attention is given by the following formula: Attention(Q, K, V ) = softmax QK⊤ √dk  V, (1) where Q, K, and V denote the Query, Key, and Value matrices, respectively; dk represents the dimensionality of the Key vectors; and softmax(·) is the normalization function. In addition, each layer within the encoder-decoder archi- tecture of the Transformer includes a Feed-Forward Network (FFN), Layer Normalization (LayerNorm), and residual con- nections. These design choices signiﬁcantly enhance the Transformer’s performance in modeling long-range dependen- cies, facilitating gradient propagation, and enabling efﬁcient parallel training, outperforming traditional neural network architectures in these aspects. The Transformer offers strong capabilities in modeling long-range dependencies and supports highly parallelized computation. However, a key limitation lies in the quadratic computational and memory complexity of the self-attention mechanism with respect to the sequence length n, i.e., O �n2 , which constrains its ability to handle long-sequence data efﬁciently. The Transformer is the cornerstone of LLMs. Its exceptional parallel computation capabilities and ability to capture long- range dependencies have enabled the scaling of models to tens or even hundreds of billions of parameters, as seen in models such as GPT and the LLaMA series. In communications, the Transformer has been widely applied to a range of tasks, including semantic communication [33], signal processing [34], multimodal perception [35], and resource management [36], signiﬁcantly advancing the level of intelligence in communication systems. 2) ViT: ViT was the ﬁrst to demonstrate that a pure Transformer architecture can be directly applied to image recognition in 2020, achieving performance on large-scale datasets that matches or even surpasses that of state-of-the- art Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) [37]. ViT ﬁrst divides an image into ﬁxed-size patches, linearly embeds these patches, adds positional encodings, and then feeds them into a standard Transformer encoder in the same way as word sequences are processed. Various visual tasks are then performed through different output layers, as illustrated below: y = Encoder (Concat(zcls, Flatten(Patch(I))) + Epos) , (2) where I denotes the input image, Patch(·) and Flatten(·) refer to the patching and ﬂattening operations, respectively, while Concat(·) represents the concatenation operation. zcls and Epos denote the CLS token and positional encoding, respectively, and Encoder(·) refers to the Transformer encoder. The advantages of ViT lie in its strong capability for global information modeling and scalability, which aligns well with the extensibility of the Transformer architecture. However, ViT lacks certain inherent visual inductive biases present in tradi- tional models, such as locality and translational invariance, which often necessitates pre-training on large-scale datasets to achieve competitive performance. Moreover, when handling high-resolution images, the increased sequence length leads to substantial computational overhead. ViT has become one of the foundational architectures for LVMs (e.g., SAM, DINO) and serves as a critical com- ponent in many LMMs. In communications, ViT has been widely applied to tasks such as semantic communication [38], line-of-sight blockage prediction [39], and automatic mod- ulation recognition [40]. Leveraging its powerful modeling capabilities, ViT enhances the system’s perception accuracy of spatially structured data and improves communication efﬁciency. 3) VAE: The VAE is a deep generative model based on variational Bayesian methods, integrating the architecture of Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. JIANG et al.: FROM LAMs TO AGENTIC AI: A TUTORIAL ON FUTURE INTELLIGENT COMMUNICATIONS 3513 autoencoders with the principles of probabilistic graphical models [41]. The VAE learns to encode input data into a low-dimensional latent space and enables sampling from this space to generate new data. Unlike standard autoencoders, VAEs learn a proba- bilistic distribution over the latent space, which facilitates the generation of diverse outputs. Speciﬁcally, the VAE encodes an input x into a latent distribution qφ (z|x), typically assumed to be a multivariate Gaussian distribution, samples z from this distribution, and then reconstructs an approximation of x through pθ (x|z). The training objective is to maximize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO): L = Eqφ(z|x) [log pθ(x | z)] −KL (qφ(z | x) ∥p(z)) , (3) where E denotes the expectation, the reconstruction term Eqφ(z|x) [log pθ(x | z)] ensures the quality of the generated out- puts, while the Kullback-Leibler term KL (qφ(z | x), |, p(z)) guides the latent space to align with the prior distribution p(z), typically assumed to be a standard normal distribution. φ and θ denote the learnable parameters of the encoder and decoder networks, respectively. The VAE offers a generative framework with a solid theoretical foundation. Its learned latent space is typically smooth, making it amenable to interpolation and interpretation. However, a common limitation is that the generated samples may appear blurry, and the ELBO represents only a lower bound of the true data likelihood. VAE is commonly used for discrete image encoding and data compression, forming the core conceptual foundation of LVMs such as DALL-E and Stable Diffusion. As a powerful generative model, VAE has been widely applied in communication tasks such as CSI feedback [42], seman- tic communication [43], and Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) detection [44]. By leveraging its latent variable modeling capability, VAE effectively enhances the operational efﬁciency of communication systems. 4) Diffusion: Diffusion models are probabilistic generative models based on Markov processes, proposed in 2015 [45]. Their core idea is to model data distributions through a “noise addition–denoising” process. Diffusion models operate through two key processes: the forward diffusion process, which gradually adds Gaussian noise to the data (e.g., images, videos) until it becomes pure noise; and the reverse denoising process, which starts from pure noise and progressively removes the noise using a neural network to generate clear data samples. Forward Diffusion Process: Noise is gradually added to the original sample x0 over multiple steps until it becomes nearly Gaussian white noise. The t-th step can be expressed as: q(xt | xt−1) = N p 1 −βt xt−1, βt I  , (4) where q(xt | xt−1) is a conditional probability distribution that deﬁnes the probability of the current noisy sample xt given the previous step’s noisy sample xt−1, βt is a noise variance parameter initialized to a small value, and I is the identity matrix, and xt and xt−1 denote the images at step t and step t −1, respectively. Reverse Denoising Process: A neural network ϵθ(xt, t) is trained to estimate the noise component, and the image at step t −1 is updated using a ﬁxed variance and the learned mean: xt−1 = 1 √αt  xt − βt √1 −¯αt ϵθ(xt, t)  + σtz, z ∼N(0, I), (5) where σtz represents noise with variance σt, which is used to maintain the diversity and stochasticity of the generated samples. θ denotes the learnable parameters of the denoising network, and the scaling factor βt √1−¯αt follows the standard parameterization proposed in [46], which is cited here as an early seminal work on diffusion probabilistic models. Diffusion models offer the advantages of generating high- quality and diverse samples, along with relatively stable training. However, their main drawbacks include slow gen- eration speed due to the need for multi-step iterative sampling and relatively complex theoretical derivation. Diffusion models have emerged as the dominant technology for high-quality image and video generation. LVMs such as Stable Diffusion [47], DALL-E 2/3 [48], and Imagen [49] are all based on the principles of diffusion models. In communi- cations, diffusion models have been widely applied to tasks such as channel estimation [50], semantic communication [51], channel enhancement [52], and signal enhancement [53], demonstrating high-ﬁdelity generation and strong robustness under complex channel conditions. 5) DiT: DiT, proposed in 2022, is a specialized design that applies the Transformer architecture to diffusion models [54]. It typically replaces the previously common U-Net structure with a Transformer during the reverse denoising process of the diffusion model to predict noise [54]. DiT maps the latent image representation zt, the timestep encoding t, and an optional condition y into a sequence of embeddings, which are then fed into a standard Transformer for global self-attention and feedforward processing. The ﬁnal output is either the predicted noise ϵθ(xt, t, y) or a direct prediction of the denoised image sample, as illustrated below: h = Encoder (Embed(zt, t, y)) , (6) ϵθ(xt, t, y) = Project(h), (7) where Embed(·) denotes the embedding matrix, Encoder(·) represents the Transformer encoder, and Project(·) is the projection matrix used to output the predicted noise. zt denotes the latent variable in the diffusion space, while xt represents the corresponding image in the pixel space; DiT performs the denoising process in the latent space, and xt is obtained by decoding zt through the image decoder. DiT offers excellent scalability, enabling signiﬁcant improvements in generation quality by increasing model size. However, it remains constrained by the inherently slow sampling speed of diffusion models, and the computational overhead of Transformers for high-dimensional inputs remains substantial. The introduction of DiT represents a signiﬁcant milestone in the development of diffusion models, demonstrating the powerful potential of the Transformer architecture in gener- ative tasks. It has inspired the design of numerous subsequent Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 3514 IEEE JOURNAL ON SELECTED AREAS IN COMMUNICATIONS, VOL. 44, 2026 large generative models, particularly world models such as OpenAI’s Sora [55], which also adopt the DiT architecture at their core to process spatiotemporal latent representation blocks. DiT has proven that the Transformer can serve as a universal and scalable backbone for a wide range of complex generative modeling tasks. 6) MoE: MoE is a model architecture paradigm designed to enhance model capacity through conditional computation while maintaining manageable computational costs [56]. It is not a standalone model but is typically integrated within speciﬁc LAMs. In a standard Transformer module, the original FFN sub- layer typically consists of two linear transformations and a non-linear activation function, applied independently to the representation of each token in the output of the self-attention layer. To replace it with an MoE layer, one must ﬁrst instantiate N independent “expert” networks, each of which is itself an FFN with the same architecture as the original but with its own set of parameters. Additionally, a gating network is introduced to compute a probability score for each expert. Based on these scores, a sparse routing strategy is usually employed to select the top-K scoring experts (with K typically being small, such as 1 or 2) to process the current token. Finally, the outputs of the selected K experts are combined through a weighted summation to produce the ﬁnal MoE output for that token, as illustrated below: g = softmax (Wgx + bg) , (8) yi = Experti(x), ∀i ∈IK, (9) output = X i∈IK gi · yi, (10) where Wg and bg denote the weight matrix and bias vector of the gating network, respectively. IK represents the index set of the top-K experts with the highest scores; Experti(·) denotes the i-th expert network; yi is its corresponding output; and gi is the score assigned to the i-th expert. The key advantage of MoE lies in its effective decoupling of parameter scale from computational cost, enabling the training and deployment of LAMs that signiﬁcantly exceed the size of dense models with comparable computational budgets. However, a major drawback is its substantial memory requirement: despite the sparsity in computation, all expert parameters must still be loaded into memory during inference. As a result, MoE models typically consume signiﬁcantly more memory than dense models with equivalent computational complexity. MoE is one of the key technologies enabling the develop- ment of state-of-the-art LLMs. Architectures such as Google’s GLaM [57], Mistral AI’s Mixtral 8x7B [58], and GPT-4 [11] have all adopted the MoE framework. By leverag- ing MoE, these models can scale to larger parameter sizes while maintaining acceptable training and inference costs. In communications, MoE has been widely applied to tasks such as communication security [59], satellite communications [60], and signal processing [61], where the expert activation and parallel processing mechanisms contribute to enhanced system intelligence, improved inference efﬁciency, and greater robustness. B. Classiﬁcation 1) LLM: LLMs represent a signiﬁcant branch in the ﬁeld of deep learning, referring speciﬁcally to neural network models that are pretrained on massive text corpora and contain an extremely large number of parameters, typically in the tens or even hundreds of billions. Their core capability lies in understanding and generating human-like natural language, enabling them to perform a wide range of language-related tasks with remarkable generalization and adaptability. By learning grammar, semantics, and commonsense knowledge from large-scale corpora, LLMs have acquired human-level cognitive and reasoning abilities, establishing themselves as a foundational technology driving advancements in NLP and the broader ﬁeld of AI. Structurally, most state-of-the-art LMs are built upon the decoder-only Transformer architecture, which leverages its powerful self-attention mechanism to effectively capture long- range dependencies and learn multi-level linguistic features and semantic representations. The GPT series developed by OpenAI (e.g., GPT-3 [7], ChatGPT [11]), the Gemma series by Google [12], the LLaMA series by Meta AI [62], and Claude by Anthropic are all promi- nent representatives of LLMs. In communications, LLMs have been widely applied to tasks such as semantic communication [63], network management [22], multi-agent systems [27], and communication security [64]. Leveraging their powerful language understanding and generation capabilities, LLMs sig- niﬁcantly enhance the intelligence, adaptability, and interaction efﬁciency of communication systems. 2) LVM: LVMs refer to deep neural network models trained on massive visual datasets consisting of billions of images and videos, and characterized by an extremely large number of parameters. These models are designed to learn general and powerful visual representations, enabling them to understand complex image and video content and generalize across a wide range of downstream vision tasks. LVMs signiﬁcantly expand the performance ceiling and application scope of computer vision systems. LVMs typically adopt CNNs or ViTs as their backbone architectures. These architectures extract visual features in a hierarchical manner by stacking multiple processing layers, progressively capturing representations ranging from low-level features (such as edges and textures) to high-level semantics (such as object parts and complete objects). In addition to pure Transformer-based designs, hybrid architectures that combine CNNs and Transformers are also common in LVM develop- ment, aiming to leverage the local feature extraction strength of CNNs and the long-range dependency modeling capabilities of Transformers. Representative LVMs include Masked Autoencoders (MAE) [65], DINO [66], and SAM [10]. Their applications in communications are primarily focused on semantic commu- nication [28], [67]. By incorporating LVMs such as SAM and MAE, lightweight knowledge bases and efﬁcient semantic encoders/decoders are constructed, enabling the compression and sharing of image semantic information. This signiﬁcantly improves communication efﬁciency and image reconstruction quality. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. JIANG et al.: FROM LAMs TO AGENTIC AI: A TUTORIAL ON FUTURE INTELLIGENT COMMUNICATIONS 3515 3) LMM: LMMs are designed to jointly process and understand data from multiple distinct modalities, such as text, images, audio, video, and potentially even code, point clouds, and sensor data [68]. The goal of LMMs is to enable comprehensive modality fusion and interaction, thereby more effectively simulating how humans perceive and understand the world through various inputs. These models are capable of performing complex cross-modal reasoning, content gener- ation, and seamless interaction. LMMs are widely regarded as a critical step toward achieving more general forms of AI. In terms of architectural design, LMMs are typically built upon powerful unimodal backbones and incorporate more sophisticated cross-modal fusion and alignment mecha- nisms. Their core architecture often includes: modality-speciﬁc encoders for different input types; one or more projection modules that map modality-speciﬁc information into a shared representation space and perform deep alignment, potentially using multi-layer cross-attention, modality gating mechanisms, or dedicated fusion networks; and a central processing unit, usually based on LLMs, responsible for semantic understand- ing, reasoning, and task execution. To support multimodal output generation, the LMM may also integrate corresponding decoders. OpenAI’s GPT-4 is a prominent representative of LMMs, natively supporting ﬂexible combinations of text and image modalities for both input and output. It signiﬁcantly reduces interaction latency and enhances performance on cross-modal tasks, demonstrating truly real-time multimodal dialogue capa- bilities. Similarly, Google’s Gemini series [12], [69] and the LLaVA series [70], [71], [72] are also powerful LMMs. By integrating a broader range of sensory inputs, these models enable unprecedented levels of complex cross-modal under- standing and generation, further blurring the boundary between digital intelligence and physical-world perception. LMMs have been widely applied in communication sce- narios such as semantic communication [63], [73], wireless network intent management [74], and multimodal task- oriented dialogue systems [75]. By integrating multimodal information such as images, text, and sensor data, these models signiﬁcantly enhance the communication system’s capabilities in understanding, adaptability, and intelligent interaction. 4) LRM: LRMs are AI models focused on enhancing systematic reasoning capabilities in complex tasks. Their pri- mary goal is to solve complex logical problems, such as those encountered in mathematics, programming, and scien- tiﬁc domains, through explicit multi-step logical reasoning. Compared to conventional LLMs, LRMs signiﬁcantly improve their performance in planning, problem decomposition, and dynamic knowledge integration by incorporating techniques such as RL [76], Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) [77], and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) [78]. Structurally, LRMs typically adopt a multi-stage training framework. Starting from a base pretrained model, they optimize the generation of reasoning chains through RL or hybrid training strategies (e.g., SFT + RL), and dynamically augment external knowledge using RAG mechanisms [79]. For instance, DeepSeek R1 [13] employs the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm [80], which balances answer accuracy and reasoning format consistency through a reward function. The reward function plays a crucial role in optimizing model performance within RL. It not only guides the model to generate accurate answers but also ensures consistency throughout the reasoning process. This objective is achieved through a multi-dimensional feedback mechanism, where the reward depends not only on the correctness of the output but also on the logical coherence and structural consis- tency maintained in the model’s reasoning steps. Additionally, it incorporates cold-start data, enabling the model to maintain high-quality reasoning while reducing redundant computation and optimizing resource consumption. Representative LRMs include models such as OpenAI-o1, DeepSeek R1, and Qwen-QwQ [81]. As an early bench- mark, OpenAI-o1 demonstrated complex reasoning capabil- ities through Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) [82] and Procedural Reward Modeling (PRM). However, its closed- source nature and high computational cost have limited its widespread adoption. In contrast, DeepSeek R1 [13] stands out for its open-source availability, offering reasoning performance comparable to o1 but at a signiﬁcantly lower cost, thereby greatly advancing the open-source community. Additionally, LRMs such as Qwen-QwQ [81] have exhibited reasoning abil- ities on par with o1-like models in domain-speciﬁc tasks such as code generation, further enriching the diversity of the LRM ecosystem. In communications, LRMs are primarily applied to enhance system intelligence, adaptability, and security [83] by leveraging their powerful multi-step reasoning and abstraction capabilities. 5) Lightweight LAM: Lightweight LAMs refer to those LAMs that are speciﬁcally designed and optimized to reduce model complexity, minimize storage size, lower computational resource consumption, and accelerate inference speed. The core value of such models lies in their ability to operate efﬁ- ciently in resource-constrained environments, such as mobile devices, embedded systems, IoT devices, and edge computing nodes. Additionally, they contribute to reducing the cost and latency of large-scale cloud deployments, thereby enabling LAMs to be more broadly and economically applied in various real-time or power-sensitive scenarios. Lightweight LAMs require carefully optimized architectural designs and trade-offs to maximize performance within con- strained resource “budgets.” Structural lightweight is typically reﬂected in several aspects: ﬁrst, by directly reducing model depth (number of layers) and width (hidden dimensions, number of attention heads); second, by adopting more efﬁcient component variants, such as Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) [84] or Multi-Query Attention (MQA) [85] in place of standard Multi-Head Attention (MHA), which signiﬁcantly reduces the required cache size during inference and improves efﬁciency. A series of representative lightweight LAMs have recently demonstrated their potential and value. For example, the TinyLLaMA project aims to replicate the architecture and training pipeline of LLaMA 2 with approximately 1.1 billion parameters, incorporating optimizations such as GQA to pro- vide a foundational model for extremely resource-constrained research and development [86]. LiteLLaMA typically refers to ofﬁcial or community-optimized versions of the LLaMA Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 3516 IEEE JOURNAL ON SELECTED AREAS IN COMMUNICATIONS, VOL. 44, 2026 TABLE III CLASSIFICATION OF LAMS AND THEIR APPLICATIONS IN COMMUNICATIONS series with fewer parameters, emphasizing a balance between performance and resource consumption [87]. MiniCPM is a multimodal model designed for edge deployment, with around 2 billion parameters, serving as a representative model tailored for mobile and edge devices [88]. In addition, Microsoft’s Phi series (e.g., Phi-2 [89], Phi-3-mini/small/medium [90]) is known for its “small size, high performance” characteristics, achieving results that surpass models of similar scale through training on high-quality datasets. The continual advancement of these lightweight LAMs is enabling powerful AI capa- bilities to be brought to a wider range of terminal devices and application scenarios. In communications, lightweight LAMs play a critical role in semantic communications [67], where their low computational overhead and strong inference capabilities allow for efﬁcient semantic information extraction, compression, and reconstruction directly on edge devices, signiﬁcantly enhancing both communication efﬁciency and robustness. The classiﬁcation of LAMs and their corresponding appli- cation scenarios in communications are presented in Table III. C. Summary and Lessons Learned 1) Summary: This section provides a systematic summary of the core components and model types involved in LAMs for communications. We detail the fundamental principles, architectural characteristics, and application scenarios in com- munications for typical modules such as Transformer, ViT, VAE, Diffusion, DiT, and MoE. Meanwhile, we review the deﬁnitions, structural features, and representative works of different categories of LAMs including LLM, LVM, LMM, LRM, and lightweight LAMs. This lays a comprehensive foundation for readers to understand the current technology system of LAMs. 2) Lessons Learned: Although signiﬁcant progress has been made in the model architectures, classiﬁcation mech- anisms, and communication applications of LAMs, multiple challenges remain. On one hand, models like Transformer, ViT, and diffusion require heavy computation when processing long sequences and high-dimensional data, diffusion models have slow generation speeds, and MoE structures consume high memory, all of which affect the real-time performance and deployment efﬁciency of LAMs in communication sce- narios. On the other hand, the stability and consistency of LAMs in multimodal fusion and complex reasoning tasks still need improvement. Future research should focus on optimizing model computation structures, enhancing sampling and reason- ing efﬁciency, constructing uniﬁed and scalable multimodal model architectures, and promoting efﬁcient deployment of lightweight LAMs in edge environments [91]. III. HOW TO DESIGN LARGE AI MODELS FOR COMMUNICATIONS In the context of learning communication knowledge, LAMs primarily adopt two approaches. The ﬁrst approach involves embedding communication knowledge directly into the model parameters through pre-training, ﬁne-tuning, and alignment. However, this method is time-consuming and less suitable for knowledge that requires frequent updates. The second approach combines RAG and KG, leveraging external vec- tor databases and graph databases to provide contextualized knowledge to LAMs without modifying their parameters. This approach is more adaptive to the demands of rapidly evolv- ing knowledge. In the following sections, we ﬁrst introduce the methodologies for constructing communication datasets, followed by a detailed discussion of both learning paradigms. A. Communication Datasets The construction of communication datasets serves as the foundation for training LAMs in communications. It encom- passes three key components: pre-training, ﬁne-tuning, and alignment datasets. The primary goal is to support the transition and enhancement of model capabilities from general-purpose intelligence to task-speciﬁc competence in communication tasks. 1) Communication Content Filtering: Currently, general- purpose datasets used for training LAMs contain substan- tial communication-related content. Representative datasets include Common Crawl,1 Pile,2 Dolma,3 and RedPajama- Data.4 Accordingly, domain-speciﬁc communication datasets can be constructed by extracting relevant content from these sources. This process involves identifying a set of commonly used communication-related keywords, ﬁltering the datasets based on these keywords to retain communication-relevant content, and applying deduplication techniques to remove 1http://commoncrawl.org/the-data/get-started/ 2https://github.com/togethercomputer/ 3https://huggingface.co/datasets/allenai/dolma 4https://github.com/togethercomputer/RedPajama-Data Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. JIANG et al.: FROM LAMs TO AGENTIC AI: A TUTORIAL ON FUTURE INTELLIGENT COMMUNICATIONS 3517 redundant or repetitive entries. These steps aim to enhance the training efﬁciency of LAMs while maintaining data diversity. During content ﬁltering, communication-speciﬁc keywords can be precisely selected based on the following criteria [92]: • Technical Relevance: Keywords should be closely asso- ciated with core communication theories. For example, “6G” represents the latest generation of mobile com- munication technologies, and “VoIP” refers to voice communication over IP networks, both indicating clear communication-speciﬁc contexts. • High Frequency: Keywords should be commonly used terms in professional communication literature. For instance, “Broadband” describes high-speed inter- net access technologies, and “LTE,” an abbreviation for long-term evolution, frequently appears in mobile communication scenarios. • Uniqueness: Keywords should possess distinctiveness and speciﬁcity in communications. Terms like “spectrum allocation,” a key concept in wireless communication, and “ﬁber-optic communication,” the foundation of mod- ern high-speed transmission, exemplify such uniqueness. • Authoritativeness: Keywords should originate from core communication standards and carry authoritative sig- niﬁcance. For example, “3GPP” deﬁnes global mobile communication standards, and “IEEE 802.11” speciﬁes wireless LAN standards, both accurately referencing formal communication technologies. • Timeliness: Keywords should reﬂect cutting-edge trends in communications. “Network slicing” allows ﬂexi- ble allocation of network resources, while “quantum encryption,” based on quantum mechanics, enables secure communication, both representing advanced and emerg- ing concepts. • Clarity: Priority should be given to keywords that precisely describe frontier communication technologies, avoiding vague or overly generic terms. Examples include “VoLTE” for high-deﬁnition voice services over LTE net- works, and “Non-Terrestrial Networks” refer to satellite communications and other non-ground-based networking technologies. 2) Pre-Training Datasets for Communications: The pre- training of LAMs requires vast and diverse communication- related data that spans multiple technical domains and data sources to comprehensively acquire domain-speciﬁc knowl- edge and enhance the model’s generalization and accuracy. The following are representative datasets that can be utilized for pre-training LAMs in communications: • TSpec-LLM [93] is an open-source dataset targeting 3GPP documents, covering over 30,000 documents from 1999 to 2023 with a total size of 13.5 GB. The dataset is formatted in markdown while preserving the origi- nal structural hierarchy, facilitating comprehension and processing by LLMs. • OpenTelecom Dataset [92] is a pre-training corpus designed for LAMs in telecommunications. It encom- passes a wide range of sources, including communication standards, research papers, books, and patents, with a particular emphasis on authoritative documents from 3GPP and IEEE. This dataset ensures that models acquire comprehensive and credible telecommunications knowl- edge, providing rich and high-quality textual resources for communication-related NLP tasks. • CommData-PT [94] is a high-quality pre-training corpus speciﬁcally constructed for LAMs in communications. It includes comprehensive content from 3GPP stan- dards, IEEE protocols, communication-related patents, academic papers, source code, and Wikipedia entries, covering knowledge across all layers of communication systems. The data are processed through LAM-assisted recognition, keyword-based ﬁltering, cleaning, and stan- dardization, resulting in a corpus with high domain speciﬁcity and structural consistency. This dataset pro- vides strong support for both the pre-training and downstream task performance of communication-oriented LAMs. 3) Fine-Tuning Datasets for Communications: In commu- nication, ﬁne-tuning enables LAMs to learn and understand domain-speciﬁc instructions and perform corresponding tasks. Instruction-tuning datasets play a crucial role in this process by providing paired instruction-response samples, allowing the model to learn how to execute tasks based on given instruc- tions. This signiﬁcantly enhances the model’s adaptability and accuracy in speciﬁc communication tasks. The following are several representative instruction-tuning datasets: • TelecomInstruct Dataset [92] is an instruction-tuning dataset tailored for telecommunication tasks. It covers a wide range of task types, including question answering, document classiﬁcation, code generation, and protocol interpretation. This dataset is designed to enhance the model’s ability to comprehend and execute telecom- speciﬁc instructions, thereby improving its effectiveness and generalization in complex communication scenarios. • CommData-FT [94] is a high-quality instruction-tuning dataset speciﬁcally designed for ﬁne-tuning LAMs in communications. Built upon the CommData-PT corpus, it contains well-structured instruction-response pairs cov- ering tasks such as protocol-related question answering, document classiﬁcation, and text summarization. The dataset adheres to standardized formatting and is man- ually curated to ensure data quality, providing strong support for model ﬁne-tuning and specialization in communication tasks. Task-tuning is another ﬁne-tuning approach that enables communication-oriented LAMs to directly perform speciﬁc communication tasks. By utilizing domain-speciﬁc com- munication datasets, the LAM learns task-related features, constraints, and decision logic within communication systems, thereby acquiring the capability to execute concrete com- munication tasks in real-world network environments. The following are several representative task-tuning datasets: • WSS24 [95] is a real 2.4 GHz ISM band dataset collected with an Ellisys BV1 device. It records RSSI values from 79 sub-bands every 100 µs, covering Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and Zigbee signals. The data is converted into 13 Wi-Fi Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 3518 IEEE JOURNAL ON SELECTED AREAS IN COMMUNICATIONS, VOL. 44, 2026 channels and used to train and test deep learning–based MAC protocols like DL-MAC. • MetaCC [96] is a meta-learning benchmark for channel coding, including ﬁve channel types—AWGN, Bursty, Memory, Multipath, and a real radio channel. Each task decodes a rate-1/2 convolutional code under different noise conditions. It provides realistic channel data for beam prediction and channel estimation, widely used in AI-based 5G/6G research. 4) Alignment Datasets for Communications: Alignment datasets provide high-quality feedback to LAMs to optimize their behaviors and decision-making processes, enabling the models to better align with human p

Cybersecurity FusionITD: enhanced cross‑modal insider threat perception framework via behavior‑semantic fusion Lu Yuan1, Dexian Chang1, Hao Hu1, Yingchang Jiang1, Heyu Chang1, Liguo Fang1* and Yuling Liu2 Abstract In recent years, insider threat incidents have occurred with increasing frequency, leading to severe data breaches and substantial economic losses. Most existing insider threat detection methods rely primarily on single-modal features, such as system logs and registry data, while failing to fully exploit the rich semantic information embedded in instant messaging and email content of insider users. To address the above issues, we propose FusionITD, a cross- modal insider threat perception enhancement framework based on the fusion of behavioral and semantic features. This framework combines users’ temporal behavioral characteristics such as file operations and login device patterns with the semantic information derived from web browsing and email content. By modeling user behavior baselines from multiple dimensions, FusionITD enables more accurate anomaly detection when deviations from the baseline occur. Firstly, based on the temporal distribution of user behaviors, the behavior data is segmented and aggregated according to the time window to form a user behavior graph. We propose WR-GNN based on graph representa- tion learning to capture temporal behavioral features, and introduce the Focal MSE loss function to address the data imbalance problem caused by sparse abnormal behavior data. Secondly, we propose a retrieval-augmented generation-based semantic analysis algorithm. We use cosine similarity to perform semantic matching and rank- ing between behavioral contents and historical behaviors. We extract features such as emotion, intention, and focus to achieve fine-grained anomaly detection for user behavior. Finally, we designed an adaptive weighting mechanism based on logistic regression to dynamically integrate the outputs of the previous two parts, enhancing the generaliza- tion ability for different threat scenarios. Experimental results conducted on the CERT datasets show that FusionITD outperforms other methods by achieving a 5% increase in AUC, a higher TPR, and a lower false positive rate. Keywords Insider threat detection, User behavior graph, Graph representation learning, Retrieval-augmented generation, Cross-modal Introduction In the digital age, insider threats have become one of the most destructive risks in enterprises and private networks, disrupting business operations and seriously compromising data security, resulting in massive eco- nomic losses. According to the Ponemon (2025) Insider Threats report, the average annual cost of insider inci- dents is as high as $17.4 million (Institute 2025). Unlike traditional external attacks, insider threats are often initiated by legitimate users who possess in-depth knowledge of the organization’s structure and secu- rity mechanisms, making their actions more covert and destructive (Alhamed and Rahman 2023). The existing detection methods mainly rely on log analysis, but they have obvious limitations in practical scenarios. As shown *Correspondence: Liguo Fang milan_flg@163.com 1 Information Engineering University, Zhengzhou 450001, China 2 Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100085, China Page 2 of 17 Yuan et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:119 in Fig. 1, for instance, employees may become depressed due to high work pressure and display a negative atti- tude in emails or instant messaging (emotion). Employ- ees may lack motivation at work, frequently browsing non-work-related websites during office hours, send- ing emails to contact other companies (focus). Access- ing illegal websites through internal hosts to download malicious programs, or uploading leaked internal confi- dential materials (intention), etc. It is difficult to compre- hensively depict the semantic intentions and behavioral patterns behind these behaviors based on a single log data, resulting in insufficient detection accuracy. There- fore, how to integrate structured behavioral features and unstructured semantic information to achieve more accurate modeling and identification of insider threats has become an urgent problem to be solved. Current insider threat detection methods can pri- marily be categorized into rule-based (Legg et al. 2015; Milajerdi et al. 2019), machine learning-based (Zhu et al. 2024; Min et al. 2017; Shen et al. 2018), and graph representation learning-based methods (Liu et al. 2019; Roy and Chen 2024; Xiao et al. 2024). Rule-based meth- ods detect potential threats by matching predefined behavioral patterns, which usually lead to a low false positive rate. However, such methods are incapable of handling unknown or emerging threats and suffer from poor generalization capability. Machine learning-based methods construct baseline models of benign behaviors by learning from large volumes of historical data, and deviations from the established baseline are regarded as potential threats (Gayathri et al. 2024), such as Random Forest or KNN. Although these methods do not require manually defined rules, traditional machine learning approaches typically demand substantial amounts of labeled training data. Graph-based methods analyze behavioral anomalies by exploring relationships among network entities (Roy and Chen 2024; Li et al. 2023). Nevertheless, these methods often ignore the semantic information underlying entity interactions. In recent years, the rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has provided new opportunities for insider threat detection. Due to their strong semantic understanding and context reasoning capabilities, LLMs can capture potential risks in unstructured text such as emails and chat records. However, methods based on prompt engineering, such as LogPrompt (Liu et al. 2024) and Audit-LLM (Song et al. 2024), have highly dependent performance on the quality of prompt words, and are difficult to dynamically adapt to changes in user behavior (Li et al. 2025). While fine-tuning methods can achieve higher accuracy on specific tasks, they rely on a large amount of supervised data, have high training costs, and are prone to ignoring the temporal features of user behavior during fine-tuning. Therefore, methods solely relying on LLMs are difficult to comprehensively depict the complexity of insider threats. To address the above challenges, we propose Fusion- ITD, a cross-modal insider threat perception enhance- ment framework that fuses behavioral and semantic features. Firstly, this framework constructs a user behav- ior graph and uses Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to capture the temporal characteristics of user behaviors and the correlation features among different behavior sequences. Secondly, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is employed to build a semantic feature knowl- edge base, parsing the implied emotional tendencies and potential intentions in the content. Finally, by integrating the insights from both behavioral and semantic modali- ties, the final detection result is obtained through an adaptive weighted fusion mechanism. The main contri- butions of this paper are as follows: Fig. 1 Insider abnormal behavior scenario examples Page 3 of 17 Yuan et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:119 (1) This paper proposes a cross-modal fusion frame- work for insider threat detection based on behav- ioral and semantic features. Driven by graph rep- resentation learning for behavioral modeling and RAG for semantic parsing, the framework achieves threat representation across structured behavioral data and unstructured textual semantics. (2) We have designed a multi-dimensional semantic retrieval-augmented method. By matching historical similar records in a knowledge base containing labels such as emotion, intention, and focus, it provides contextual evidence for reasoning, effectively solving the problem of insufficient utilization of text information in insider threat detection methods. (3) We designed an adaptive weighted fusion mechanism based on logistic regression. This mechanism dynamically combines the outputs of behavioral analysis and semantic parsing, enhancing the model’s accuracy in detecting abnormal behaviors. (4) We conduct extensive experiments on the CERT r4.2 and r5.2 datasets to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed model. Compared with other baseline methods, FusionITD achieves an AUC of up to 0.98 in identifying malicious behavior while maintaining a low FPR. Related work Rule‑based insider threat detection In the early stages, rule-based methods were widely applied in insider threat detection. These approaches rely on expert knowledge and use predefined rule sets to identify potential malicious behaviors. Legg et al. (2015) proposed a rule-based framework incorporating user and role behaviors to automatically detect insider anomalies, systematically integrating rule-driven logic with statistical analysis. Milajerdi et al. (2019) proposed a rule abstraction method based on the MITRE ATT&CK framework, which maps low-level logs to high-level attack stages. However, due to their heavy dependence on expert-defined rules, maintaining and updating these rule sets incurs high costs. Moreover, such methods struggle to adapt to emerging threats and often suffer from a high false positive rate. Machine learning‑based insider threat detection With the development and popularization of machine learning technology, machine learning-based methods have been widely applied in insider threat detection. Such methods can automatically learn patterns from data, thereby reducing the reliance on expert knowledge. Le et al. (2020) proposed a user-centric insider threat detection system based on machine learning, which performs data analysis across multiple granularity levels under realistic conditions. Soh et al. (2019) used the isolation forest algorithm for anomaly detection. d’Ambrosio et al. (2023) proposed a Bayesian attack graph-based risk management framework that can cover a wider range of threats. Ma et al. (2020)) proposed a new hybrid method, iMondrian forest, combining isolation forest and Mondrian forest for batch and online anomaly detection. Fei et al. (2025)) utilized the rich language information in log content and employed anomaly detection algorithms such as isolation forest to detect insider threats. However, machine learning methods face the problems of high complexity in feature engineering and insufficient handling of spatio-temporal features. Relying on manually designed features makes it difficult to capture complex semantics. Deep learning‑based insider threat detection In contrast, methods based on GNN have demon- strated significant advantages. As a deep learning tech- nique, GNNs can directly handle graph-structured data without the need for complex feature engineering steps (Goncalves and Zanchettin 2024). Firstly, GNNs can effectively capture the complex relationships between nodes in a network. Liu et al. (2019) proposed a modu- lar method based on heterogeneous graph embedding, log2vec, which converts log entries into a heterogeneous graph according to the different relationships between them. By combining temporal information, GNNs can better capture the long-term behavior patterns and their changing trends of users. Xiao, et al. (2024) proposed a spatio-temporal graph neural network, SENTINEL, with edge enhancement for insider threat detection. The behavior interactions of users within the internal net- work are formed into a graph, and ST-GNN is used to learn the spatio-temporal operation patterns of users. Li et al. (2024) proposed a meta-learning model based on graph modularization, which simulates the evolution of behavior in a few-shot scenario through episodic train- ing. Xiao, et al. (2022) proposed a multi-edge weighted relation graph neural network (MEWRGNN) for robust anomaly detection, converting the time series related to user behavior logs into a graph. Cai et al. (2024) proposed a real-time activity-level detection framework, LAN, which utilizes graph structure learning to capture the temporal dependencies and cross-sequence relationships of activity sequences, achieving real-time insider threat detection. Existing GNN-based methods have been able to effectively model the structural dependencies, hetero- geneous relationships, and spatio-temporal dynamics of Page 4 of 17 Yuan et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:119 user behavior. However, these methods ignore the rich semantic information contained in the original behavior. LLM‑based insider threat detection With the rapid development of artificial intelligence tech- nology, LLMs have demonstrated remarkable capabili- ties in semantic understanding and reasoning, providing new research ideas for cybersecurity anomaly detection (Peng et al. 2025; Jiang et al. 2025). Leveraging language understanding acquired through pretraining, LLMs can directly analyze behavior data in natural language form via prompt engineering or fine-tuning strategies, ena- bling zero-shot or few-shot detection. The comparison of existing insider threat detection methods based on LLM is shown in Table 1. It analyzes these methods from aspects such as modeling approach, cost, analysis gran- ularity, and interpretability. Liu et al. (2024) proposed LogPrompt, which uses specially designed prompting strategies to achieve interpretable zero-shot log anomaly detection without labeled data. Song et al. 2024 proposed Audit-LLM, introducing multi-agent task decomposition and an evidence-driven debate mechanism to improve detection accuracy and explanation reliability in log auditing. Prompt-based methods typically require no model training, offering low computational cost and a certain degree of interpretability. However, they struggle to precisely quantify and represent the multi-dimensional deep semantics implicit in logs, and they tend to overlook structural relationships in user interactions. For fine-tun- ing-based methods, (Song et al. (2025) fine-tuned LLMs and incorporated a contrastive learning task to effec- tively distinguish benign from malicious user behaviors. Zhang et al. (2025) proposed LLM-LADE, which com- bines data augmentation, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, and online knowledge base optimization to achieve log anomaly detection and explanation generation. However, fine-tuning-based methods face high training and adap- tation costs and usually lack sufficient interpretability due to their black-box nature. FusionITD combines the relational modeling capa- bility of WR-GNN with the retrieval-augmented reasoning mechanism of RAG, enabling explicit characterization of user behavior interaction structures and evidence-grounded inference. Compared with the above methods, FusionITD achieves greater utiliza- tion of structural information, lower cost, finer-grained analysis, and stronger interpretability. Cross‑modal behavioral‑semantic feature fusion method The framework of FusionITD This paper proposes a cross-modal fusion insider threat detection framework based on behavioral and semantic features. This framework combines the temporal behav- ior modeling based on graph neural networks with the retrieval-augmented semantic judgment mechanism, enabling deep detection of anomalous user behaviors. The architecture of this method is shown in Fig. 2 and consists of the following three core parts: Behavioral Pattern Analysis. Based on the relatively stable temporal characteristics of users during their working hours, discrete user operation behaviors are first aggregated into sequential representations to construct a user behavior graph. Subsequently, a WR-GNN model is designed to capture the temporal dependencies and periodic patterns within the behavioral sequences, enabling the identification of operations that deviate from the user’s normal behavioral baseline. Content Semantic Parsing. For the text content in user behavior, the RAG mechanism is introduced to retrieve similar samples from the semantic knowledge base that integrates historical behaviors and labels. Combined with a large language model, reasoning is conducted on user intentions, emotions, and focus to achieve seman- tic detection of potential insider threats. Adaptive Fusion. An ensemble learning strategy is adopted to fuse the outputs of the first two parts, and a dynamic weighting mechanism is designed to adap- tively adjust the confidence weights of behavioral pat- terns and semantic analysis, thereby achieving more accurate threat detection. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:119 Construction of user behavior graph In the internal network environment of enterprises, the distribution of user behaviors shows significant temporal regularity. Their behaviors are usually concentrated within standard working hours, and the behavioral patterns exhibit stable periodic characteristics. However, the behavioral patterns and high-frequency operation behavior sequences during non-working hours carry relatively high risks. Therefore, based on the temporal characteristics of user behavior patterns, this paper will conduct processing and analysis from two temporal dimensions and design a multi-granularity temporal modeling mechanism. User behavior data exhibit discrete and multi-source characteristics. To transform user data into structured graph data, discrete behaviors are aggregated within time windows to construct a user behavior graph. Define user behaviors as being sorted by time {t1, t2, . . . , tN} , where tk represents the timestamp of the kth behavior event. All behavior data (such as login behaviors, website visit behaviors, file operation behaviors, etc.) are uniformly sorted and integrated by timestamp to construct a global behavior time series. On this basis, coarse-grained aggregation is performed daily, T day = T day 1 , T day 2 , . . . , T day M . Considering that there are differences in behavior frequency, type distribution, and accessed resources between working hours and non-working hours for user behaviors, a more fine-grained division is required. The working hours window is T work k , and the rest hours are T rest k = T day k \ T work k . After dividing according to the selected time window, to reflect the interaction relationship between users and hosts in the network, a set of user behavior graphs G={G1, G2, . . . , Gt} is obtained. By further dividing the daily data based on users, the behavior set of all users on that day can be obtained, which is convenient for the learning and analysis of users’ daily benign behaviors. A single user behavior graph is defined as G = (V , E, A, w) , with specific details provided in Table 2. User nodes Vu = {u1, u2, . . . , ui} and host nodes Vp = p1, p2, . . . , pj . User ui is the source node, and host pj is the destination node. The operation behaviors executed by users on specific hosts are represented as the edges E connecting these two types of nodes. The edge attribute ea refers to the statistical frequency, time char- acteristics, etc. of various behaviors within the time slice. Quantitative statistics are conducted on the same type of behaviors (such as login behavior, URL access behavior, email sending behavior, etc.) within each time window to generate multi-dimensional feature vectors. The edge weight w refers to the sum of the statistical quantities of various behaviors in the edge attributes. According to the above description, each graph is a graph data object that includes nodes, edge indices, edge features, and edge weights. This method enhances the representational capability of the data by introducing node types and edge weights, while preserving the temporal characteristics of the original time-series data. Behavior pattern analysis In the insider threat detection task, user behavior typi- cally exhibits certain temporal regularities. To capture these regularity features and identify abnormal behaviors, this paper designs a behavior pattern analysis method WR-GNN (Work-Rest Graph Neural Network) based on graph representation learning, as shown in Fig. 3. By par- titioning user behaviors across different time periods, the method effectively models and distinguishes behavioral differences between working and non-working hours, thereby establishing a behavioral baseline for subsequent anomaly detection. When modeling user behavior, since user behavior is composed of a sequence of behavioral events, each event can be abstracted as an edge in the graph. Such a structure is naturally suitable for modeling with GNN. The GNN performs iterative updates of node representations through a message passing mechanism: where h(l) v represents the feature representation of node v at the l-th layer, N(v) represents the neighbor set of node v, cu,v is the normalization coefficient, and eau,v is the attribute feature vector of edge (u,v). This mechanism enables the model to capture both the dependencies among nodes and the semantic information embedded in the edge features. To further enhance the model’s performance, we have designed a graph encoder E that can incorporate edge features. Firstly, GCN is utilized to capture local structural information. However, when GCN aggregates the features of neighboring nodes, edges typically only represent the connection relationship between nodes and ignore the semantic attributes carried by edges. Therefore, a convolution mechanism that is aware of edge information is employed to jointly model edge features and node features, thereby improving the semantic understanding ability of graph-structured data. where Xt, At and Ft denote the node features, adjacency matrix, and edge features at time t. After passing through the graph encoder E , the embedding Zt at time t is output. Finally, by using GRU in the time series graph to capture the trend of nodes changing over time, it is possible to better understand the changes in user behavior patterns, thereby improving the accuracy of anomaly detection. The training process is shown in Algorithm 1. A self- supervised learning strategy is adopted to construct a benign behavior graph, which is then learned through an autoencoder. The GNN encoder E extracts graph structural features and generates node embeddings hi through neighborhood feature aggregation. The decoder D reconstructs the key attributes of the original graph by inputting the node embedding. Algorithm 1 WR-GNN training framework for behavioral pattern analysis. In insider threat detection, behavioral data often exhibit extreme class imbalance, with benign behavior data overwhelming the majority and anomalous behavior samples being extremely scarce. Under such an imbalanced data distribution, the traditional mean squared error (MSE) loss function is easily dominated by benign behavior samples, causing the model to overly focus on learning regular behaviors while neglecting the rare anomalous samples. Therefore, the standard MSE loss function is difficult to effectively capture abnormal behaviors in insider threat detection. To address this issue, we propose the improved Focal-MSE loss function, which is designed to increase the attention given to anomalous samples, effectively mitigating the scarcity of anomalous behavior samples. The definition of the Focal- MSE loss function is as follows: where y is the predicted value, y is the true value, and γ ≥0 is the focusing parameter. This loss function introduces a dynamic scaling factor γ based on the intensity of relative error, which controls the degree of attention paid to samples with abnormal behavior. When the model makes a prediction error and the sample reconstruction error is large, its loss will be adaptively magnified, making the model more focused on difficult- to-fit abnormal samples. During the early stages of (4) Lfocal−mse = 1 n n i=1 ( yi −yi)2 max(( y −y)2) + ǫ γ · ( yi −yi)2 training, the model fits benign behaviors well, with small reconstruction errors and corresponding lower loss weights. In contrast, rare abnormal behaviors deviate significantly from the normal pattern, causing higher errors, which in turn magnifies their loss terms, leading to larger gradient updates during backpropagation. As training progresses, the model continuously corrects its representation of such outlier behaviors, progressively enhancing its ability to detect subtle anomalies. Focal- MSE effectively alleviates the bias problem caused by data imbalance without the need for sample resampling or threshold adjustment, improving the sensitivity to rare threat behaviors and overall training stability. Content semantic parsing In cybersecurity threat detection scenarios, various user operations generate semantic content. These behav- iors not only reflect the user’s business operation hab- its but may also carry potential risk signals. As shown in Fig. 4, to achieve fine-grained user profiling analysis, a (user, window) tuple is adopted as the identifier, aggre- gating the same type of behaviors generated by the same user into a sequence according to the time window. Key elements are then extracted from these sequences to be processed by the subsequent semantic analysis module. To achieve fine-grained semantic understanding of user behavior, this paper introduces the RAG mechanism in the semantic analysis of user behavior, enabling mul- tidimensional analysis across three perspectives: emo- tion, intention, and focus. Emotional features reflect the variations in the user’s psychological state during inter- actions (Liu et al. 2018; Mohammad and Turney 2013). When users engage in malicious behaviors such as data theft or retaliatory sabotage, they often exhibit an accu- mulation of negative emotions. Behaviors such as fre- quent visits to social media or sending emotional emails may imply anxiety, anger, or complaints. Intentional features reveal the purpose and motivation behind user behaviors, effectively distinguishing between benign and malicious behaviors (Ruohonen and Mubashrah 2025; Greitzer, et al. 2010). For instance, if a user visits websites to download malicious programs or frequently uploads and downloads data before leaving a job, there may be a risk of data leakage. Focus features, on the other hand, reflect the stability and goal consistency of users in their work tasks. When users frequently switch tasks or visit external recruitment and competitive information, it may indicate a decline in their work focus and a potential tendency to leave or change jobs. Therefore, when users transition from a normal working state to a malicious or abnormal state, their behavioral semantics will undergo significant changes. Page 8 of 17 Yuan et al. (2) Intention Analysis: Based on the retrieved historical behavior text, the LLM identifies whether the current input behavior xt contains malicious semantic patterns such as sensitive data leakage or the use of attack tools. If xt is highly similar in semantics to known high-risk behaviors, it is determined that the intention is abnor- mal, lI(xt) = 1. (3) Focus Analysis: The LLM analyzes whether the input behavior deviates from the normal work scope (such as frequent visits to recruitment web- sites) by integrating the retrieved historical behav- ioral content, and outputs the focus label lF(xt) to identify potential insider risks. Ultimately, the module makes a comprehensive judgment based on the principle that a single abnormal label indicates overall abnormality, ensuring the timely capture of potential security threats in the case of imbalanced data and multi-dimensional risks. This paper conducts parallel analysis and fusion of WR-GNN and RAG-LLM under the default condition of sufficient resources. However, in the case of limited resources, a cascading process is adopted. WR-GNN first detects the full set of behaviors, and for the abnormal behaviors detected, it triggers RAG-LLM for semantic evidence reasoning to reduce false positives. Adaptive fusion This paper adopts an adaptive weight fusion method to combine the outputs of the behavior feature detection module based on GNN and the semantic analysis module based on RAG. Given the output SG of the behavior feature module and the output SR of the semantic analysis module, the two are fused through a weighted average method: where α and β represent the weights of the behavioral feature module and the semantic analysis module respectively, and α + β = 1. The optimal weights are learned through random sampling of data, and the output probability is calculated using a logistic regression model: where w0 is the bias term, and w1 and w2 are the weight coefficients corresponding to the behavior feature (9) Result(xt) = 1, iﬂE(xt) ∨lI(xt) ∨lF(xt) = 1 0, otherwise (10) Sfusion = α · SG + β · SR (11) z = w0 + w1 · SG + w2 · SR module SG and the semantic analysis module SR respec- tively. These coefficients are optimized through the logis- tic regression model, with the objective of minimizing the loss function, and the weighted coefficients of each module are obtained through the Sigmoid function. To calculate the weights α and β reasonably, we adopt a dynamic adjustment approach based on learning from the training data. During the training process, the learning of weights employs the cross-entropy loss function (Mao et al. 2023) to measure the discrepancy between the model’s predicted results and the true labels: In user behavior analysis, behavioral features (such as logins, file modifications, etc.) directly reflect the operation patterns of users and play a crucial role in distinguishing between benign and abnormal behaviors. Since behavioral features reflect actual operational activities, they often possess strong discriminative power. However, relying solely on behavioral features may overlook latent semantic anomalies, that is, when a user’s behavior appears normal on the surface, there may be hidden risks at the semantic level. Therefore, this model not only depends on the discriminative power of behavioral features but also enhances the ability to identify abnormal behaviors through semantic analysis, ensuring that potential insider threats can be detected when the behavior appears normal. Experiments Settings Dataset. To verify the effectiveness of the proposed method in insider threat detection, we conducted experimental evaluations on two public datasets, CERT r4.2 and CERT r5.2. These two datasets were devel- oped by the Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT) of the Software Engineering Institute (SEI) at Carnegie Mellon University for the purpose of simulat- ing data for insider threat detection (Lindauer 2020), as shown in Table 3. CERT r4.2 includes 32,770,227 behavior records of 1,000 employees, among which 70 employees exhibit abnormal behavior; CERT r5.2 contains 79,856,664 records of 2,000 employees, with 99 employees showing abnormal behavior. It can be seen that normal behavior dominates the datasets, and abnormal samples are sparse. The datasets include five types of behaviors: logon, device, file, http, and email, covering typical operations such as login, device usage, (12) α = 1 1 + exp(−z), β = 1 −α (13) L = − N i=1 yilog p yi = 1 + 1 −yi log p yi = 0  Page 10 of 17 Yuan et al. Among them, logon, device, and file mainly reflect behavioral characteristics at the operational level, while HTTP and email contain URL, web content, and email bodies accessed by users in the network, providing key textual information for semantic analysis. Since the behavior analysis module adopts unsupervised learn- ing, training is based solely on normal behavior data. The specific configuration of the experimental environment is shown in Table 4. Baseline. To evaluate the performance of FusionITD, we compared it with six baseline methods, namely RNN (Recurrent Neural Network), DeepLog (Min et al. 2017), Transformer, BST (Chen et al. 2019), LAN (Cai et al. 2024) and LogPrompt (Liu et al. 2024). Spe- cifically, RNN and Transformer are common sequence modeling architectures. DeepLog utilizes a deep neural network model with long short-term memory to model system logs as natural language sequences, automati- cally learning log patterns from normal executions and detecting abnormal logs that deviate from the normal model. BST adopts a Transformer-based model to cap- ture sequential information in user behavior sequences, and has been widely applied in click-through rate pre- diction within recommendation systems. LAN uses graph structure learning to autonomously learn user activity graphs without human intervention, integrating temporal dependencies and the relationships between user activities in different activity sequences. Log- Prompt uses the prompt engineering technology of large language models for zero-shot log anomaly detection. Metrics. This paper adopts true positive rate (TPR), false positive rate (FPR), and area under the ROC curve (AUC) as the main performance evaluation indicators for the insider threat detection model. • AUC. The ROC curve is a graph of the TPR against the FPR. In the detection of abnormal behavior, there is a serious class imbalance problem in the data (with an extremely high proportion of normal samples), and relying solely on Accuracy makes it difficult to reflect the actual performance of the model. The ROC curve, by jointly analyzing TPR and FPR, provides an intuitive way to display the balance between the model’s detection ability and false positives at different thresholds. AUC, as a numerical summary of the ROC curve, has the characteristic of being unaffected by the imbalance of class distribution and can stably evaluate the comprehensive performance of the model in extremely imbalanced data. Therefore, it is more suitable for insider threat detection tasks than Accuracy, Precision, or F1-score. Its range is from 0 to 1, and the higher the value, the better the detection performance. • TPR. TP represents the number of correctly identified malicious behavior samples, and FN represents the number of malicious samples misjudged as benign behavior. This metric directly reflects the model’s detection capability for insider threats and is one of the core indicators in threat detection scenarios. • FPR. FP represents the number of normal samples wrongly judged as malicious behaviors, and TN represents the number of normal samples correctly identified. This metric quantifies the extent of false positives generated by the model for benign behaviors.
