title: Hint recognition in Chinese and
file_name: Hint-recognition-in-Chinese-and-Russian-diplomatic-discourse-using-large-language-models_2026_Nature-Research.pdf
text: Hint recognition in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse using large language models Yingdong Guo1 & Xu Wang2 This study develops and evaluates a Large Language Model (LLM) system for hint recognition in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse by integrating a semantic–cognitive–pragmatic theoretical framework with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. Grounded in linguistic–pragmatic theory and employing discourse analysis, we systematically annotated real press-conference transcripts from the Chinese and Russian Ministries of Foreign Affairs, constructed a vectorized external knowledge base covering textual and logical hints, embedded CoT reasoning instructions into the prompts, and provided bilingual few-shot exemplars to guide LLM recognition. Experimental results demonstrate stable overall performance with consistently high recall across both corpora, with the Russian dataset achieving higher precision and F1 scores than the Chinese dataset. Error analysis reveals three major types of systematic bias in LLM hint recognition: semantic over–interpretation, hint-type misclassification, and literal-meaning misclassification. To address these problems, we propose several targeted improvements, including expanding negative samples with standardized “no-hint” diplomatic expressions, strengthening context anchoring to ensure pragmatic interpretations are grounded in discourse, introducing a repeated matching mechanism, calibrating sensitive trigger words, and introducing a pre-filtering and self-evaluation mechanism to better distinguish explicit statements from implicit meanings. This study provides a feasible pathway and practical guidance for improving the accuracy and stability of LLMs in multilingual, high-context implicit information recognition. Keywords LLM, Hint, Cross-lingual, Diplomatic discourse, RAG & CoT As an important medium of international communication, diplomatic discourse is highly representative and conventional. It not only conveys a nation’s official stance and attitude but also reflects the diplomatic wisdom of its envoys1. The extensive use of implicit communication in this context is not only because diplomatic discourse typically follows the face-saving principle2 and the politeness principle3, which help maintain harmony and mutual respect in international settings, but also because implicit communication itself, compared with direct expression, has a stronger persuasive effect on its audience4,5. Such linguistic practices reflect the ritualized nature of diplomacy6, in other words, diplomats employ implicit expressions to mitigate conflict and build consensus7. However, even among native speakers it is not uncommon to misinterpret the hints embedded in one another’s utterances8–10. In cross-lingual diplomatic discourse, the challenge of correctly grasping such implicit messages is even greater, and history offers many examples of diplomatic missteps arising from misreading what is left unsaid. For instance, in July 1956, as the Suez Crisis escalated, British Foreign Secretary Selwyn Lloyd assured U.S. envoy Robert Murphy that the use of force against Egypt would be only a “last resort,” seeking to calm Washington and forestall interference in Operation Musketeer. Yet Chancellor Harold Macmillan hinted that the expeditionary force was about to sail, sending contradictory signals that led U.S. officials to misread Britain’s intentions. Believing London to be on the verge of unilateral action, President Eisenhower and Secretary of State Dulles rushed envoys and personal messages to Prime Minister Eden to head off an attack. This episode deepened Anglo-American mistrust and left Britain and France isolated when they invaded Suez with Israel later that year, provoking a fierce international backlash and forcing a humiliating withdrawal11. Likewise, in August 2008 the Kremlin refuted a statement from the Élysée Palace claiming that Moscow and Paris had agreed to replace Russian troops in the security zone outside South Ossetia with an OSCE mission. In its clarification it stressed that President Dmitry Medvedev, during a telephone conversation with his French counterpart Nicolas 1Far Eastern Federal University, Vladivostok, Russia. 2Shandong Jianzhu University, Jinan, China. email: guoyd010401@gmail.com OPEN Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:5751 1 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-36338-z www.nature.com/scientificreports Sarkozy, had merely “confirmed his readiness to cooperate with the OSCE in this area in accordance with the fifth point of the conflict-resolution principles jointly developed by the Presidents of Russia and France”12. We argue that difficulties in recognizing hints stem not only from differences in culture, life experience, and social background, but also from individual factors such as attention, topic interest, and personality traits. China and Russia are both major actors on the contemporary international diplomatic stage. From a linguistic perspective, Chinese and Russian represent two typologically distinct language systems, namely analytic and fusional, which provide ideal comparative conditions for examining how structural, cultural, and pragmatic differences influence the realization and recognition of hints. From a practical standpoint, an in-depth exploration of the ways in which hints are used and interpreted in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse can contribute to the development of more effective linguistic strategies for supporting international communication between China and Russia. Therefore, while we acknowledge that every language has its own distinctive diplomatic style, the present study adopts a focused comparative approach in order to achieve deeper analytical insight while ensuring methodological feasibility. At present, the rapid advancement of large language models in the field of assisted recognition of implicit meaning has drawn widespread attention from the global academic community. Yue et al. conducted a study using LLM to recognize conversational implicature in multi-turn dialogues from the Chinese sitcom My Own Swordsman (武林外传). By comparing the AI-generated interpretations with human annotations, they found that ChatGPT 4.0 achieved a remarkable 94% accuracy in understanding Chinese conversational implicature, significantly outperforming other LLMs such as CausalLM (14B) at 78.5% and ChatGPT 3.5 at only 24%. These results suggest that the LLM has achieved, or is approaching, humaan-level performance. However, the LLM was not specially fine-tuned for the task, limiting the generalizability of their findings13. From a LLM architecture perspective, Iida et al. compared two approaches for implicature recognition: embedding LLM into cognitive architectures versus embedding cognitive LLMs into LLM. Their results indicate the superiority of the latter, particularly when cognitive mechanisms such as Belief–Desire–Intention (BDI) are integrated. These mechanisms significantly improved the LLM’s ability to capture hidden meanings in context. Nevertheless, the experimental design only covered three types of implicit meaning-sarcasm, tsundere (faux defiance), and caution།limiting the diversity and complexity of the semantic phenomena considered14. In another study, Naganna et al. applied LLM to the recognition of humblebragging in text. They designed four semantic tasks: “bragging content,” “bragging topic,” “humble mask,” and “expression type.” ChatGPT 4.0 achieved an accuracy of 0.88, outperforming human participants (0.85). Although the study proposed a novel paradigm for identifying hidden meanings, the lack of solid theoretical grounding weakened the objectivity and generalizability of the findings. Moreover, the scope of implicature types remained narrow and did not cover a broader range of pragmatic phenomena15. In summary, although recent years have witnessed significant progress in the application of artificial intelligence to implicit meaning recognition, existing research still faces three major challenges: first, most studies remain confined to single forms of pragmatic expression and lack generalizability; second, AI-based approaches have yet to be fully integrated with established cognitive–pragmatic theoretical frameworks; and third, there is a notable lack of methodological research on how LLMs recognize implicit information in cross- lingual tasks. Based on the above discussion, this study aims to systematically examine the capability of LLMs in identifying hints in cross-lingual diplomatic discourse, with a particular focus on Chinese and Russian diplomatic materials. Specifically, the study pursues the following objectives:1) To construct a theoretically grounded model for hint recognition under the framework of cognitive pragmatics, integrating artificial intelligence architectures— particularly RAG and CoT reasoning mechanisms. 2) To evaluate the performance of trained LLMs in recognizing hints in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse, and to further explore the influence of cross- linguistic structural features, cultural cognition, and pragmatic differences on recognition outcomes. Based on these research objectives and the conclusions drawn from previous studies, the following hypotheses are proposed:1) The integration of RAG and CoT reasoning mechanisms can significantly enhance the accuracy and reliability of LLMs in hint recognition. 2) After targeted training, LLMs can largely overcome the influence of linguistic structure, cultural cognition, and pragmatic differences in cross-lingual hint expression. Current study The emergence and definition of hints within the Linguistic–Pragmatic framework Hint can be viewed as a complex socio-pragmatic category, representing a type of implicit linguistic behaviour that operates in both interactive and social dimensions and is oriented toward the transmission of unspoken meaning16. Linguistic pragmatics, in turn, is a branch of linguistics that investigates how language functions in communication, with particular attention to the speaker’s intention, socio-communicative norms, and situational context in shaping utterance interpretation. Within this framework, the importance of studying implicature and hints from a linguistic–pragmatic perspective becomes evident. The study of hints in linguistic–pragmatic research dates back to the 1970s. In 1975, H. P. Grice first distinguished between the literal meaning of an utterance and the speaker’s communicative intention, defining implicature as “the act of implying Q by saying P”16, and thereby proposing his theory of implicature. Grice divided implicatures into two broad types: conventional implicatures, which do not require specific contextual conditions for their interpretation, and particularized (or private) implicatures, which are determined by a specific context17. By the end of the twentieth century, scholars further refined the notion of particularized implicature, distinguishing between generalized implicatures, expressed through semantic–logical inferences within a given background context, and particularized implicatures, which depend entirely on contextual reasoning18. On this basis, implicature is now commonly treated as comprising three basic types:1) Conventional implicature, Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:5751 2 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-36338-z www.nature.com/scientificreports/ based on context-independent lexical triggers; 2) Generalized implicature, conveyed through logical-semantic inferences; 3) Conversational implicature, activated through structural and contextual cues19. Despite its foundational status, Grice’s theory of implicature has several limitations. J. L. Austin argued that language is not merely a tool for expressing intentions, but also a form of social action capable of producing specific effects20. By contrast, Grice conceptualized implicature primarily as a hidden meaning generated by the speaker through linguistic means. His classification is largely “speaker-oriented,” presupposing that the hearer, guided by the Cooperative Principle, is able to recover the implicit content through rational inference21. G. M. Kostyushkina explicitly notes that the successful realization of implicit utterances presupposes a shared linguistic and cultural background between interlocutors—an assumption that is often difficult to meet in cross- cultural communication22. Similarly, Pu Zhihong emphasize that research conducted within the framework of implicit communicative acts is typically restricted to a single cultural context, thereby limiting the extent to which its findings can be generalized across different cultural settings23. In the same vein, J. Allwood argues that traditional approaches to implicit communication tend to underestimate the role of background knowledge in interpretation and, as a consequence, overlook individual differences in how addressees perceive and process hints24. Consequently, in cross-cultural communication, the realization and interpretation of hints are further complicated by structural differences between languages, by the functional use of language, and by culture- specific norms of verbal behaviour. The traditional tripartite distinction among conventional, generalized, and particularized implicatures in Gricean theory does not fully account for these socio-cultural and cross-lingual factors, thereby limiting its explanatory power in multilingual diplomatic settings. The classification of hints within the Cognitive–Pragmatic framework To overcome these limitations, the paradigm of cognitive pragmatics offers a more comprehensive perspective. It conceptualizes implicature as not merely the outcome of the speaker’s hidden intention, but also the product of the hearer’s active cognitive operations, such as information processing and contextual integration25. From this standpoint, context functions as a dynamically activated cognitive resource that guides the hearer from basic semantic decoding to higher-level cognitive and pragmatic inference, thereby forming a multilayered structure of meaning26. Consequently, the semantics–pragmatics boundary is understood as a gradually expanding continuum, extending from literal semantics to cognitive and pragmatic levels of interpretation27. Building on this perspective, we propose to conceptualize hint as a multi-layered semantic structure comprising three levels of realization: verbal–semantic, linguistic–cognitive, and motivational–pragmatic. 1) The verbal–semantic level corresponds to the surface linguistic layer—the. “fabric” of language—manifested in lexical choice and syntactic structure, and reflects structural phenomena across different languages. 2) The linguistic–cognitive level of hint realization captures the culturally and. historically shaped patterns of cognitive activity that underlie different ways of interpreting the world and the corresponding norms of discursive behaviour. 3) The motivational–pragmatic level concerns the communicative motives. encoded in hints and the pragmatic strategies through which these motives are realized in different socio- cultural contexts, emphasizing the purposive and strategic nature of hinting in actual interaction. This tripartite model provides a more dynamic and cross-culturally sensitive theoretical framework for analysing hints and lays the groundwork for subsequent empirical investigations of hint realization in cross- lingual diplomatic discourse. Research on the recognition of implicit information based on natural Language processing Among the existing approaches to recognizing implicit information in texts or discourse, Discourse Analysis (DA) remains one of the most traditional and mainstream methods. Grounded in the theoretical framework of pragmatic linguistics28, DA plays an essential role in examining language use, particularly in uncovering the mechanisms of implicit meaning transmission. By analyzing discourse, researchers can reveal how underlying ideologies are expressed through language. However, despite being theoretically grounded, DA lacks a consistent and universally accepted methodological standard. This lack of objectivity has led to increasing criticism regarding its scientific rigor29. To address the subjectivity of DA, corpus-based methods combine qualitative and quantitative approaches through manual annotation, thereby improving objectivity in the recognition of implicit content. Nonetheless, manual annotation is both costly and error-prone30, and the quality of annotation may vary among annotators and across studies with differing standards31. Recent advancements in artificial intelligence-especially LLM།have introduced a new research paradigm for implicit information recognition by providing standardized algorithmic frameworks. As a subfield of artificial intelligence, Natural Language Processing focuses on both data-driven and rule- based approaches to extract implicit information from texts. LLMs form the core of this extraction process by using prior linguistic knowledge and statistical inference to predict the structure and content of unseen texts32. The data-driven approach centers on constructing external knowledge bases that store prior knowledge and linguistic patterns. When processing new texts, LLMs retrieve relevant information from this repository to infer hidden structures and meanings. The main advantage of this approach lies in its strong generalization and adaptability, enabling it to handle complex, context-rich discourse with high accuracy and efficiency. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:5751 3 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-36338-z www.nature.com/scientificreports/ Conversely, the rule-based approach establishes explicit recognition rules derived from the LLM’s internal linguistic computations33. This approach emphasizes the LLM’s interpretability and provides a framework for systematically analyzing language. It is particularly effective in mitigating human bias in implicit information interpretation34. The application of RAG and CoT reasoning in hint recognition Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an artificial intelligence technique that integrates information retrieval mechanisms with large-scale language models. It enhances the model’s capacity for knowledge-intensive tasks by retrieving relevant information from external knowledge bases and providing it as prompts for language generation35. However, Yao and Fujita have pointed out the limitations of RAG, particularly its overreliance on external retrieval36. This limitation becomes especially evident in the recognition of the motivational–pragmatic level of hints, which by nature relies heavily on conversational context and exhibits inherent cancellability. Due to this strong context-dependence, motivational–pragmatic level cannot be consistently reduced to stable expressions, making them difficult to represent within a structured knowledge base suitable for RAG applications. To address this issue, we introduce the rule-based Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting strategy37. CoT is a technique designed to guide large language models through multi-step reasoning. By decomposing complex tasks into smaller, sequential steps, CoT improves model performance on inference-intensive tasks38. Moreover, by constraining the model’s reasoning trajectory, CoT reduces the likelihood of hallucinated or irrelevant retrievals39. We posit that the effectiveness of hint recognition fundamentally depends on how the model’s inference process is structured and constrained. Therefore, integrating CoT-guided reasoning into the RAG framework— supported by a structured external knowledge base—can substantially enhance the accuracy and reliability of large language models in identifying hints. Research background To verify the feasibility of large language models in such tasks, the necessity of fine-tuning, and the inherent complexity of multilingual and multi-type hint recognition, a pilot experiment was conducted. In this pre- test, ten samples were randomly selected from Chinese diplomatic discourse and ten from Russian diplomatic discourse, and zero-shot recognition was performed using the latest version of the large language model (ChatGPT 5.0). The unified prompt used in the experiment was:“Please first determine whether there are any hints in the response part of the text. If so, please identify and explain their meaning.” To ensure the reliability of the experimental results, the model was reset before each test, and all prompts used for recognizing Chinese and Russian hints were input in English, so as to minimize potential language interference on the model’s output. The results of the pilot experiment are shown in Fig. 1. The sample results indicate that, although ChatGPT 5.0 is able to identify to some extent the correct hint expressions and their implicit meanings, its ability to distinguish explicit from implicit semantics within specific texts remains markedly insufficient. In hint-recognition tests with the LLM (ChatGPT 5.0), conducted on ten Chinese segments (Appendix A) and ten Russian segments (Appendix B) of diplomatic discourse, ‘hallucination’ phenomena occurred in 100% of cases. We also carried out a role-assignment experiment by prompting the LLM to act as a “senior diplomat.” However, this adjustment yielded no improvement in its ability to identify hints, and the hallucination rate remained at 100% in both languages. These findings suggest that, in zero-shot recognition of hints in diplomatic discourse, the output of LLM still essentially amounts to sentence-by-sentence semantic analysis rather than a deliberate identification of implicit content. This observation both confirms the potential feasibility of using LLM for hint-recognition tasks and underscores the necessity of dedicated training and fine-tuning for this purpose. Based on the above observations and in order to ensure data consistency while controlling experimental variables, the research uses diplomatic settings as the unified experimental context, focusing specifically on press conferences of the Ministries of Foreign Affairs of China and Russia. The experimental procedure consists of: (1) bilingual corpus processing; (2) constructing a knowledge base of hint expressions using RAG; (3) targeting the distinct characteristics of the motivational–pragmatic linguistic level of hints in Chinese and Russian, and applying CoT for hint recognition through a few-shot example-driven inference process; and (4) evaluating the advantages and limitations of LLMs in cross-lingual hint recognition tasks. Methods Sources of experimental materials and their processing methods This study aims to identify hints in press conferences held by the Ministries of Foreign Affairs of China and Russia. We extracted the full transcripts of press briefings conducted between June and December 2024 from the official websites of the Chinese Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Russian Federation. Due to institutional differences between the two ministries, a total of 1,389 dialogue segments were collected-851 in Chinese and 538 in Russian. With most of these segments structured in a question– answer format, we divided the original transcripts into questions and answers and entered them into an Excel spreadsheet. For hint identification, this study adopts a linguistic–cognitive–pragmatic theoretical framework and, in combination with discourse analysis methods, conducts a systematic identification and classification of hint expressions in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:5751 4 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-36338-z www.nature.com/scientificreports/ The verbal–semantic level of hints in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse From a linguistic perspective, the differences in lexical expression between Chinese and Russian stem from the fundamental structural distinctions of the language families to which they belong. Chinese is an analytic language within the Sino-Tibetan family, characterized by the absence of morphological inflection, with semantic variation primarily realized through word order, lexical collocation, and contextual factors40. As a result, Chinese lexical units carry a relatively high semantic load. In contrast, Russian belongs to the Slavic language family and represents a fusional (inflectional) language type. Its most distinctive feature lies in its well-developed system of inflection, whereby semantic variation is mainly conveyed through changes in word endings and affixation. This results in a higher degree of regularity and structural dependency in semantic expression41. By way of comparison, the Chinese sentence “中方将一如既往在力所能及的范围内为马尔代夫经济社 会发展提供支持和帮助” (China will, as always, provide support and assistance for the economic and social development of the Maldives within its capacity) conveys the continuity and stability of action through the adverbial idiom yīrú jìwǎng (“as always”), which implies an unbroken temporal span encompassing past, present, and future commitment. In contrast, the Russian statement “Соответствующая координация по данной проблематике проводится нами с китайскими и северокорейскими партнёрами” (Relevant coordination on this issue is being carried out by us with our Chinese and North Korean partners) relies on the imperfective present-tense verb “проводится”, highlighting the ongoing and as-yet-uncompleted character of the process. As an inflectional language, Russian primarily expresses syntactic relations in simple sentences through morphological forms, while in complex sentences these relations are realized by conjunctions and their functional equivalents42. By contrast, syntactic relations in Chinese are not directly observable43. Chinese lacks conjunctions, and in complex sentences syntactic relations therefore rely more heavily on word order, semantic relations, and pragmatic factors. A comparative examination of the Russian formulation “Требуем неукоснительного соблюдения О. Азуле обязательств, вытекающих из ее статуса международного чиновника и руководителя Секретариата крупнейшей межправительственной гуманитарной организации” (We demand strict compliance by O. Azule with the obligations which arise from her status as an international civil servant and as the head of the Secretariat of the largest intergovernmental humanitarian organization) and the Chinese formulation “这是 中方的年度军事训练例行安排, 符合国际法和国际惯例, 不针对任何特定国家和目标” (This is China’s routine annual military training arrangement, which is in line with international law and international practice and does not target any specific country or objective) reveals marked differences in linguistic strategy. Although both statements contain attributive relations, their mechanisms of realization and pragmatic functions differ Fig. 1. Zero-Shot Recognition Results of Hints in Chinese and Russian Diplomatic Discourse by ChatGPT 5.0. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:5751 5 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-36338-z www.nature.com/scientificreports/ substantially. In the Russian example, the relative clause introduced by the active participle “вытекающих” formally specifies the source of the “obligations,” thereby constructing a normative framework that implicitly criticises O. Azule for failing to fulfil her institutional duties. In contrast, the Chinese example lacks explicit relative markers and relies on semantic linkage to emphasize legality and regularity, indirectly refuting external suspicions regarding hostile intent. The linguistic–cognitive level of hints in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse Alexander Semenov and Anatoly Tsvyk argue that China has long developed an integral thinking paradigm, according to which meaning construction in Chinese diplomatic discourse necessarily relies on internal and external contexts and can only be properly interpreted within specific cultural and historical frameworks; this paradigm tends to subsume the individual within a holistic cognitive structure. This mode of cognition stands in sharp contrast to the Western emphasis on individualism and linear logic5. Zhang Cun and other scholars likewise point out that in Chinese diplomatic discourse, hints can only be inferred through specific implicatures, particular source domains, situational contexts, and culturally embedded meanings. These schematic structures form an inseparable figure–ground configuration, fundamentally dependent on the national cultural thesaurus. By contrast, Western discourse strategies are more direct and structurally neutral, relying less on culturally loaded hints and more on explicit, and sometimes even openly impolite, expression44. Therefore, at the linguistic–cognitive level, hints in Russian and Chinese diplomatic discourse reflect culturally and historically conditioned cognitive patterns, giving rise to distinct modes of world interpretation. The differences in the cognitive structures activated in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse are primarily manifested in the role played by the cultural thesaurus. In Russian diplomatic discourse, a wide range of rhetorical devices such as metaphor, personification, allusion, and hyperbole are frequently employed; their semantic content tends to be relatively independent of the cultural thesaurus and allows implicit meanings to be recognized without extensive contextual activation. In contrast, Chinese diplomatic discourse more often relies on idioms, formulaic diplomatic expressions, and a limited number of metaphors. These expressions are semantically more neutral and require activation by the cultural thesaurus in order for their specific implicit meanings to be properly identified. According to the “figure (hint) – ground (cultural thesaurus)” model, in Russian diplomatic discourse, hints are realized through a clearly delineated structure of “foreground figure (hint) – neutral background (cultural thesaurus),” and their interpretation does not require a specific cultural or situational context. In Chinese diplomatic discourse, however, the roles of figure (hint) and ground (cultural thesaurus) are inseparable and integrated into a holistic unity; their production and interpretation depend on a specific diplomatic and cultural context. For example, in Russian diplomatic discourse, “Diagnosis”(диагноз) belongs to the metaphorical paradigm, employing disease-related vocabulary to hint at the irrationality of someone’s behavior or speech. The expression “a De Gaulle-style figure”(фигура де Голлевского типа) represents an allusive paradigm, drawing on Charles de Gaulle’s historical role in leading France against foreign invasion and safeguarding national independence to criticize contemporary France for its lack of strategic autonomy. The term “feeding” (подкармливать) falls within an anthropomorphic paradigm, using the act of feeding to satirize a regime’s dependence on another power and to imply its status as a subordinate or proxy. The expression “information bacchanalia”(информационная вакханалия) exemplifies a hyperbolic paradigm, exaggerating the chaos and aggressiveness of the opposing information environment in order to intensify critical evaluation. By contrast, in Chinese diplomatic discourse, “Exchange views”(交换意见) is a typical diplomatic formula. While it literally denotes the exchange of opinions during meetings or negotiations, as a set diplomatic expression it usually implies that no substantive results have been achieved, that the outcome remains inconclusive, or that significant differences persist45. The idiomatic expression “walking towards each other” (相向而行) literally refers to two parties moving in each other’s direction, but in diplomatic usage it often subtly suggests that a distance still exists between the sides and that mutual compromise and effort are required to narrow their differences. The Motivational–Pragmatic level of hints in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse G. Leech points out that every discourse is produced with a specific motivation3. P. Brown and S. Levinson likewise emphasize that all speech acts inevitably carry certain intentions or motives32. D. S. Drobot further argues that the realization of specific communicative goals (i.e., motivations) requires corresponding pragmatic strategies46. van Dijk maintains that pragmatic strategies are part of our shared knowledge, representing cognitive structures related to the process of understanding. These strategies constitute an open system that requires continuous investigation and development, as the emergence of new types of discourse and communication may give rise to new strategies47. Based on these perspectives, we argue that motivations and pragmatic strategies are not fixed but are dynamically adjusted in accordance with contextual variation. On this theoretical basis, and through systematic corpus analysis, we arrive at the following conclusions: the motivation-related pragmatic expressions of hints in Chinese and Russian diplomatic discourse mainly exhibit four shared motivations, namely criticism, refutation, warning, and stance expression. However, in terms of dominant tendencies, Russian diplomatic discourse is primarily driven by a critical motivation, whereas Chinese diplomatic discourse places greater emphasis on stance expression and additionally displays unique motivations of avoidance and self-praise. At the level of pragmatic strategies, both sides share strategies such as normative statements, factual statements, confrontation, and questioning. Nonetheless, Russian discourse further demonstrates contrastive and ironic strategies, while Chinese discourse tends to employ face-saving and euphemistic praise strategies. Overall, hints in Russian diplomatic discourse are more aggressive and emotionally charged, whereas those in Chinese diplomatic discourse place greater emphasis on coordination and restraint. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:5751 6 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-36338-z www.nature.com/scientificreports/ The motivations and pragmatic strategies identified in this study are defined as follows. The motivation of criticism refers to the speaker’s negative evaluation of the behavior, stance, or policy of a certain object, highlighting its impropriety, irrationality, or harmfulness. The motivation of refutation refers to the speaker’s denial of the legitimacy and validity of the other party’s statements, viewpoints, or accusations. The motivation of warning refers to the speaker’s act of signaling the need for the other party to adjust its actions or stance. The motivation of stance expression refers to the speaker’s articulation of their own position, attitude, or policy orientation. The motivation of avoidance refers to the speaker’s deliberate evasion of giving a direct response to sensitive issues. The motivation of self-praise refers to the speaker’s emphasis on the legitimacy or advancement of their own policies or actions. The strategy of normative statement refers to the speaker’s reinforcement of discourse authority by reiterating principles, international norms, or established positions through institutionalized language. The strategy of factual statement refers to the listing of objective facts, historical events, or data in order to present the discourse as neutral and objective. The strategy of confrontation refers to challenging and countering the opposing party’s viewpoints, behaviors, or policies through linguistic expression. The strategy of questioning refers to challenging the rationality of the opposing party through interrogative forms. The strategy of contrast refers to comparing different subjects, behaviors, or outcomes. The strategy of irony refers to conveying an opposite evaluation through apparently affirmative or neutral expressions, constructing implicit criticism through contextual incongruity. The strategy of face-saving refers to the speaker’s use of euphemistic or vague expressions to avoid directly threatening the addressee’s face. The strategy of euphemistic praise refers to the use of implicit formulations to express positive evaluations of one’s own or a partner’s behavior, while avoiding overt commendation. Motivation of criticism In Chinese diplomatic discourse, the motivation of criticism is commonly realized through the strategies of rule presentation, factual presentation, and face-saving. For example, 一个中国原则 是公认的国际关系准则和国际社会普遍共识, 也是中德关系的政治基础 (“The One-China principle is a universally recognized norm of international relations and a broad consensus of the international community, and it also constitutes the political foundation of China–Germany relations”). By emphasizing the One-China principle, China implicitly criticizes Germany’s behavior as damaging Sino-German relations. Another example is: 世界上已有183个国家在一个中国原则基础上同中国建立了外交关系 (“A total of 183 countries in the world have established diplomatic relations with China on the basis of the One-China principle”). Through the reference to the number of states, China implies that opposing countries are acting against the general trend of the international community. Expressions such as 开倒车 (“driving backward”), which uses metaphorical rhetoric to euphemistically criticize actions that run counter to the prevailing trend; 小院高墙 (“small yard, high walls”), which employs diplomatic formulaic language to indirectly criticize protectionist and isolationist behavior; and 以史为鉴 (“taking history as a mirror”), which uses an idiomatic expression to implicitly criticize the failure to draw lessons from history, all function as rhetorical means of delivering indirect and euphemistic criticism of specific behaviors. By contrast, in Russian diplomatic discourse, the motivation of criticism is commonly realized through the strategies of rule presentation, factual presentation, confrontation, mockery, contrast, and rhetorical questioning. Должна обсуждаться не только смена пола, но и что люди будут есть, пить, как они будут выживать (“Not only gender change should be discussed, but also what people will eat, drink, and how they will survive”) emphasizes a normative stance and criticizes the neglect of people’s real-life needs. По большому счету Вторая мировая война стала для Соединенных Штатов возможностью выйти из затяжной экономической, финансовой, политической депрессии (“By and large, the Second World War became an opportunity for the United States to emerge from a protracted economic, financial, and political depression”) presents a factual statement that criticizes the United States for deliberately provoking conflicts and sustaining national development through war. A. Бербок, которая дурно образована и мало разбирается не то что в международных делах, но и в собственной истории (“A. Baerbock, who is poorly educated and understands little not only about international affairs, but even about her own history”) directly criticizes her anti-Russian rhetoric and constitutes a linguistic counterattack. 200 копий сразу же отправились в штаб-квартиру ЦРУ в Вашингтоне. Там, видимо, сидят ценители литературы (“Two hundred copies were immediately sent to the CIA headquarters in Washington. Apparently, there are connoisseurs of literature sitting there”) uses the ironic label “connoisseurs of literature” to mock the CIA and thereby criticize its close surveillance of Russia. Австрийцы помнят и другие времена, когда получали фантастические бонусы от взаимодействия с нами (“Austrians also remember other times when they received fantastic benefits from cooperation with us”) contrasts current difficulties in cooperating with the West with the benefits previously gained from cooperation with Russia, thereby criticizing Austria’s abandonment of such cooperation. Как же это сделать, если они отказались от дешевой энергии (“How can this be done if they have refused cheap energy?”) employs a rhetorical question to critique misguided policy decisions. Motivation of refutation In Chinese diplomatic discourse, the motivation of refutation is commonly realized through the strategies of rule presentation, factual presentation, and questioning. For example, the statement “中 国海警船系依据国际法和国内法在中国管辖海域开展例行巡航” (“The China Coast Guard conducts rou­ tine patrols in waters under China’s jurisdiction in accordance with international law and domestic law”) reiter­ ates the legal basis to refute the claim that such lawful patrols infringe upon the sovereignty of other states. The statement “事件发生后, 不少当地市民献花悼念的自发行动表明了反对暴力、祈愿中日两国人民世代友 好的意愿” (“After the incident, the spontaneous actions of many local residents laying flowers in commemo­ ration demonstrated their opposition to violence and their wish for lasting friendship between the Chinese and Japanese peoples”) counters the allegation that anti-Japanese sentiment exists in Chinese society by referring to Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:5751 7 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-36338-z www.nature.com/scientificreports/ the actual behavior of Chinese citizens. The rhetorical question “美方似乎忘了‘沃尔夫条款’等国内法的存在, 不知道美国科学家和相关机构是否会被本国政府允许参与对华交流合作” (“The U.S. seems to have forgot­ ten the existence of domestic laws such as the ‘Wolf Amendment’ — whether American scientists and relevant institutions would even be permitted by their own government to participate in exchanges and cooperation with China”) refutes the claim that China refuses to cooperate with the United States. By contrast, in Russian diplomatic discourse, the motivation of refutation is typically realized through the strategies of rule presentation, factual presentation, questioning, contrast, mockery, and confrontation. For instance, “Они находятся в рамках уголовных или административных процессов.” (“They fall within the framework of criminal or administrative proceedings”) emphasizes procedural legality to refute accusations that Russia’s actions are unreasonable. “Большинство немцев хотели выстраивать с Россией прагматичные, взаимовыгодные, эффективные отношения, работающие на интересы своей страны.” (“The majority of Germans wanted to build pragmatic, mutually beneficial, and effective relations with Russia that serve their national interests”) invokes public opinion to counter the German government’s claim that confrontation with Russia is intended to protect its citizens. The rhetorical question “1941 г. случился, когда Советский Союз был?” (“Did 1941 happen when the Soviet Union existed?”) challenges the notion that the Soviet period was one of ‘relative peace’.The statement “Россия прекратила свое участие в Совете Европы с 16 марта 2022 г., но вопросы по поводу ЕСПЧ и его решений, которые он безудержно штампует в отношении нашей страны, продолжают поступать, как будто все это имеет какое-то значение.” (“Russia terminated its participation in the Council of Europe on March 16, 2022, yet questions regarding the European Court of Human Rights and its decisions, which it endlessly issues against our country, continue to arise, as if all this still had any significance”) employs contrast to deny the legitimacy of the Court’s rulings. The remark “Комментировать Ж. Борреля – это как зачитывать какой-то диагноз.” (“Commenting on Josep Borrell is like reading out some kind of diagnosis”) uses metaphorical mockery to discredit his statements. Finally, “посмотрите, что творится в подопечных ЕС и ‘коллективному Западу’ странах.” (“Look at what is happening in the countries under the patronage of the EU and the ‘collective West’”) counters Western accusations that Russia threatens global security and simultaneously launches a discursive counterattack against the governance and protection model of the EU and the collective West. Motivation of warning In Chinese diplomatic discourse, the warning motivation of hints is commonly realized through the strategies of rule presentation, confrontation, and face-saving. For example, the statement “贸易战 没有赢家, 也不利于世界” (“There are no winners in a trade war, and it is not beneficial to the world”) reiterates the general logic of trade conflicts and implicitly warns the other side to put an end to such actions. Expressions such as “引火烧身, 自食恶果” (“courting one’s own destruction and suffering the consequences”) refute the other party’s behavior and warn of its inevitable negative outcome, while “火中取栗” (“pulling chestnuts out of the fire”) employs an idiomatic formulation to euphemistically signal the risks and potential dangers inherent in the other party’s current actions, thereby cautioning it to reconsider or adjust its policy course. These expressions manage to convey explicit warning while maintaining a relatively restrained diplomatic tone. In comparison, in Russian diplomatic discourse, the warning motivation of hints is more often realized through the strategies of rule presentation, factual presentation, confrontation, questioning, and contrast. For instance, “Все понимают, что когда много обещают, то нужно смотреть, кто это обещает.” (“Everyone understands that when many promises are made, one must look at who is making them”) emphasizes the general rule that promises should be assessed in relation to their source, thereby warning that certain commitments may not be reliable. The statement “Напомню, 90% зерновых, которые идут в Армению, поступают из России.” (“Let me remind you that 90% of the grain supplied to Armenia comes from Russia”) highlights Armenia’s high dependence on Russian grain supply and implicitly warns it to refrain from participating in political activities considered unfriendly to Russia, such as video conferences held in the context of the ‘Ukrainian peace initiative and food security’.The sentence “Тогда это называлось план «Ост» Третьего рейха, а теперь это – план- программа НАТО.” (“Back then it was called the ‘Ost Plan’ of the Third Reich, and now it is the NATO plan- programme”) creates a discursive conflict between historical and contemporary

title: EDITORS: Rahul C. Basole, rahul.basole@accenture.com
file_name: How Visually Literate Are Large Language Models- Reflections on Recent Advances and Future Directions.pdf
text: EDITORS: Rahul C. Basole, rahul.basole@accenture.com Francesco Ferrise, francesco.ferrise@polimi.it DEPARTMENT: GRAPHICALLY SPEAKING How Visually Literate Are Large Language Models? Reﬂections on Recent Advances and Future Directions Alexander Bendeck and John Stasko , Georgia Institute of Technology, Atlanta, GA, 30332, USA Large language models (LLMs) are now being applied to the tasks of visualization generation and understanding, demonstrating these models’ ability to be “visually literate.” On the generation side, LLMs have shown promise in powering natural languages’ interfaces for visualization authoring while also suffering from usability and inconsistency issues. On the interpretation side, models (especially vision– language models) can answer basic questions about visualizations, synthesize visual and textual information, and detect misleading visual designs. However, models also tend to struggle with certain analytic tasks, and their takeaways from reading visualizations often differ from those of humans. We aim to both illuminate the state of the art in LLMs’ visualization literacy and speculate on where such work may, and perhaps ought to, take us next. I n the context of information visualization, visual literacy can be concisely described as the ability to create and understand visual representations of data. While visualizations are powerful aids for explor- ing data and communicating discovered insights with others, these beneﬁts can be inaccessible to many people who lack training in visual literacy. Many research projects over the years have sought to facili- tate either the generation or interpretation of charts by nonexperts, often with the stated goal of satisfying user queries or tasks speciﬁed only in natural language. Generative artiﬁcial intelligence (AI) models, and speciﬁcally large language models (LLMs), such as GPT-4, have demonstrated powerful capabilities on a variety of tasks. These models have greatly simpliﬁed the process of drafting emails and summarizing research papers—and now, creating and interpreting visualizations. Pretrained LLMs’ text generation capa- bilities can be easily employed to write code for visual- izations, while multimodal vision–language models can answer questions about chart images right out of the box. Critically, such models can help to greatly decrease the amount of time and effort required for users to generate visualizations and insights from data. While their performance is certainly imperfect, these visually literate models already represent a tech- nical advancement with signiﬁcant implications for visualization research. Features that would have previ- ously required advanced approaches in natural lan- guage processing or computer vision can now be implemented into visualization systems with ease using easily accessible LLMs and their APIs. Virtual assistants powered by generative AI models have the potential to greatly facilitate and accelerate visual data analysis.1 Given the rapid advancement of such models, it is worthwhile to take stock of the state of visually literate LLM-powered systems in visualization research and reﬂect on where we may be headed. Our discussion of visual literacy centers on the cre- ation and consumption aspects of the visualization workﬂow,1 assuming a ﬁxed dataset. As best we can, we also focus on the ability of models to complete 0272-1716  2025 IEEE. IEEE Computer Graphics and Applications Published by the IEEE Computer Society November/December 2025 120 uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply these visualization-related tasks separately from their ability to answer broader questions using their knowl- edge base. Thus, prompting an LLM to broadly “explain” a chart or generate takeaways from it falls within our scope, but asking a model to integrate real- world historical knowledge that contextualizes the chart does not. Throughout this article, we also offer reﬂections as to how LLMs could power virtual assis- tants capable of many visualization-related tasks. TECHNIQUES FOR MAXIMIZING LLM VISUAL LITERACY For this article, we assume that the reader has sufﬁ- cient knowledge of LLMs to understand a high-level discussion—for instance, at the level needed to use an LLM-based chatbot (e.g., ChatGPT) or a developer API. We will not spend time discussing model internals or ground-up training procedures. However, several best practices in prompting and tweaking LLMs to improve their performance are quite useful for visual literacy tasks and are often referenced in the visualiza- tion literature. We provide a very brief overview of the relevant terms and techniques here. Note that as practitioners use LLMs to design visual analysis assis- tants, prompting techniques should ideally remain “under the hood.” System end-users themselves may have limited familiarity with prompt engineering and should not be expected to construct nuanced prompts. Perhaps the simplest approach is to provide an off- the-shelf pretrained LLM (e.g., GPT-4) with a text prompt containing a dataset and an instruction—for example, to generate a code that will render a speciﬁc visualization. In practice, small tweaks are often made to improve generation performance. Few-shot prompt- ing is a strategy of including a sample input (i.e., a data table and visualization description) and output (i.e., code for generating a correct visualization) to the model as part of the prompt. This is in contrast to zero-shot prompting, wherein no explicit examples are included in the request. Researchers have found sig- niﬁcant performance beneﬁts to few-shot prompting over zero-shot; this holds true for as few as a single example (i.e., one-shot prompting) and in fact shows diminishing beneﬁts when providing more than a small handful of examples. Few-shot prompting is thus an efﬁcient and common way to improve model performance. More advanced approaches in LLM prompting and training have also been applied for visualization gener- ation and understanding. Increasingly, practitioners utilizing LLMs for complex, multistep tasks are employing chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. In this approach, the task is decomposed into constituent parts, which can then be given to the model as sequential prompts. Likewise, rather than using a pre- trained model directly out of the box, practitioners can conduct ﬁne-tuning—using task-speciﬁc datasets to tweak a model’s parameters, resulting in a more specialized model with improved performance. Both CoT prompting and model ﬁne-tuning have been effectively employed for visualization code generation and chart interpretation tasks. However, they are cer- tainly not universal approaches, especially in the visu- alization literature. VISUALIZATION GENERATION General “NL2VIS” Tasks Generating visualizations based on natural language queries or requests, also referred to as the “NL2VIS” task, has been a well-studied research direction in the visualization community for many years. Traditionally, work in this area utilized semantic and lexical parsing techniques along with rule-based generation heuris- tics to produce visualizations. Since the advent of pre- trained LLMs, a variety of new methods have been under active development and assessment. One recent evaluation is the VisEval benchmark,2 which compares multiple LLMs’ performance at using few-shot prompting to generate visualization speciﬁcations in Python using the Matplotlib and Seaborn libraries. The authors speciﬁcally developed this benchmark dataset for the NL2VIS task with LLMs in mind. They found that the GPT models (GPT- 3.5 and GPT-4) tended to perform better than Gem- ini-Pro and CodeLlama-7B, with the former pair achieving around a 60% “pass rate” of satisfactory chart generation. For all models, results differed greatly across chart type and human experts’ “hard- ness” assessment for each task. The most common errors tended to be producing invalid or nonexecut- able code, conducting improper or invalid data transformations, and generating charts with low readability due to issues with layout, scale, and axis ticks. VisEval’s scoring system aims to measure both result validity (i.e., the code runs) and readability (i.e., the generated visualization is well formatted), con- solidating checks for both of these measures into an overall “quality score.” More often, evaluations of LLM visualization gen- eration capabilities are conducted as part of system or toolkit development, rather than benchmarks in and of themselves. The developers of the Python-based NL4DV library for generating Vega-Lite November/December 2025 IEEE Computer Graphics and Applications 121 GRAPHICALLY SPEAKING uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply speciﬁcations recently released an update adding the option of a GPT-4-based engine, in addition to the pre- vious parsing-based implementation. The updated NL4DV-LLM approach3 meaningfully outperformed the traditional NL4DV on several benchmark datasets (87% accuracy versus 64%), while also successfully supporting ambiguously speciﬁed queries and conver- sational interactions (see Figure 1). Increased running time was a drawback of the LLM-based approach, although this should improve as models advance. Tian et al.4 built the ChartGPT system for gener- ating and reﬁning LLM-generated charts and evalu- ated it with potential users. The user study revealed that the system’s semantic understanding of users’ intended visualizations was quite powerful, and that participants preferred ChartGPT’s charts over those generated by prior work that employs parsing and deep learning approaches. This work reveals bene- ﬁts to their particular approach, which utilized both CoT prompting and ﬁne-tuning. Employing CoT enabled participants to iteratively reﬁne chart gen- eration at each step in the system interface, while the ﬁne-tuned data evidently led to fewer inexecut- able code snippets compared to out-of-the-box LLM methods. Specialized Tasks and Contexts An important consideration when evaluating NL2VIS systems—and one particularly relevant to the discus- sion of visual literacy—is how we decide whether a visualization is “correct” and/or “good.” It is one thing for a system to produce a technically correct visualiza- tion, but quite another for it to generate a visualization that is easily readable and aesthetically pleasing. To this end, LLMs can be utilized to self-correct their own visualizations; the Bavisitter system5 is a linter for LLM-powered visualization generation pipelines, designed to detect and ﬁx common issues (e.g., quality and legibility problems) in LLM-generated charts. While this approach seems quite promising, it mostly ignores the fact that the utility of the created charts depends on the context of use. For instance, will an AI-generated visualization be sufﬁciently trusted by a user that they would put it into a research paper or a report for their boss? Answering such questions requires us to go beyond existing evaluation approaches, even though readability and correctness are good ﬁrst principles. The question we pose above also reveals another tension in current evaluations of NL2VIS systems. We note that there often seems to be an implicit assump- tion that an NL2VIS system is meant to be used for data exploration or analysis. However, visualizations FIGURE 1. Recommended visualizations, rendered from Vega- Lite speciﬁcations, generated by the LLM-powered version of the NL4DV Python package. Adapted from Figure 2 in the work by Sah et al.3 122 IEEE Computer Graphics and Applications November/December 2025 GRAPHICALLY SPEAKING uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply are often utilized for communication in both personal and work-related settings. Take, for instance, the increasing popularity of narrative visualization techni- ques in data-driven stories, articles, and even videos. LLMs seem like a natural ﬁt for this type of work given their facility with generating narrative text. A few proj- ects, such as DataTales,6 have explored the utility of LLMs to assist in data story authoring. While current directions in this space mostly focus on creating a story around an existing user-provided visualization, the potential for integrating these capabilities with NL2VIS features seems like a logical next step. It is also worth noting that much of the LLM-based NL2VIS work focuses on generating static visualiza- tions and standard chart types. One investigation in an educational context7 found that LLMs, such as GPT-4, show some ability to write code for interactive visualizations using D3.js, albeit while demonstrating some atypical patterns of library use. However, careful assessments of exactly what types of interactive visu- alizations LLMs can generate, as well as their ability to appropriately compose more advanced and bespoke visualization dashboards, are currently lacking. Of course, interactive interface design requires more than just visual literacy skills. Regardless, considering a wider swath of visualization toolkits and libraries may be a valuable exercise to study the breadth of LLMs’ visualization generation knowledge beyond Python-based libraries and Vega-Lite speciﬁcations. Such broader evaluations are an important next step toward assessing LLMs’ abilities as capable assistants for visual analysis. If out-of-the-box LLMs fail to dem- onstrate sufﬁcient ﬂuency across a variety of tools and languages, practitioners can consider ﬁne-tuning their own models. VISUALIZATION UNDERSTANDING Chart Question Answering As with NL2VIS, the line of work in building systems that can understand visualizations goes back many years. In particular, research in chart question answer- ing (CQA) likely provides the closest analog to visuali- zation literacy for systems when it comes to reading and interpreting charts. In the recent past, CQA sys- tems have relied on computer vision and natural lan- guage processing techniques, along with task-speciﬁc heuristics and modules. Multimodal vision–language models, such as GPT-4 V and LLaVA, now offer similar capabilities out of the box. Users can provide an image and a question as part of a prompt to these models and receive answers with little additional work required. We recently conducted an evaluation8 of GPT- 4 V to assess the model on a variety of tasks related to visualization literacy, including CQA. The model showed remarkable versatility at chart interpreta- tion for high-level tasks, even with minimal prompt engineering. For instance, GPT-4 V can often cor- rectly answer questions involving comparisons between values or ﬁnding extreme values (see Figure 2, right). However, for more low-level tasks, we found a key limitation in GPT-4 V to be its inability to reliably extract data values from charts where the values are not labeled directly on the chart (see Figure 2, left). This leads to divergent performance on CQA tasks depending on whether a separate data extraction module is utilized to provide the underlying data as part of the prompt. If the data are not provided, the model struggles to compute derived values or even simply retrieve values from charts. On the other hand, when the data are provided, GPT-4 V can quite reliably perform value retrieval and computation. In other words, while the model shows some ability to ﬁnd high-level trends out of the box, it may currently need to be accompanied by another value retrieval module on CQA tasks. This is also reﬂected in our comparison of GPT-4 V’s performance to that of a non-LLM base- line system; while it matched or surpassed prior per- formance when it was provided the data, it failed to measure up when it was not. We also note that another main challenge for the model was correctly identifying colors and reading color legends, leading to some value-retrieval errors even when the model was provided the data. A subsequent study to ours by Hong et al.9 con- ducted evaluations of both GPT-4 V and Gemini, tak- ing a statistical approach to assessing the models’ abilities across different chart types and tasks. They found some support for their hypothesis that mod- els would be able to better answer questions visual- izations with fewer or simpler data marks (e.g., line charts and scatterplots). However, they also observed that the models had reasonable success at answering questions about more complex visual- izations, including bubble charts and stacked bar charts. The two models also had divergent perform- ances on certain chart types (e.g., pie charts and choropleth maps), and detailed statistical analysis of the results revealed an interaction effect between chart type and task type affecting model perfor- mance. This suggests that speciﬁc charts, analytic tasks, and even combinations of the two may be better suited for some models over others, a phe- nomenon that warrants further investigation. November/December 2025 IEEE Computer Graphics and Applications 123 GRAPHICALLY SPEAKING uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply Even more recently, Dong and Crisan10 con- ducted another VLAT-based evaluation, comparing the performance of GPT-4o, Gemini 2.0, and four open-source vision–language models, including LLaVA 1.5 and ChartGemma. They found that Chart- Gemma, which is ﬁne-tuned for the task of CQA, was able to perform nearly as well as the two propri- etary models. Their results demonstrate strong evi- dence of the beneﬁts of model ﬁne-tuning for LLM visualization literacy. They also characterized differ- ent types of common errors that models make on CQA tasks: data errors (e.g., extracting data values), encoding errors (e.g., misunderstanding the seman- tics of data marks), and reasoning errors (e.g., failing at multistep operations). This provides a more for- mal framework that builds on observations of model failures from earlier evaluations. While it may be tempting to eschew text-based approaches for CQA completely in favor of vision– language models, research suggests this may not always be preferable. An evaluation by Bursztyn et al.11 found that a ﬁne-tuned text-only LLM outperformed vision–language models on the CQA task using bar charts. In addition to answering questions about charts, the approach was also promising in generating explanations for answers—that is, pointing out rele- vant visual marks (e.g., “the largest orange bar”) corre- sponding to the generated answer. A comparative analysis as part of their evaluation also found, like our own evaluation, that models, such as GPT-4 V, can struggle to reliably understand colors and read color legends. The ability to explicitly convey visual encod- ing information, such as color, is thus a key beneﬁt of a text-only approach. However, future work should investigate providing both detailed textual descrip- tions of a chart and the chart image to a vision–lan- guage model to determine if the combination of the two in a single prompt improves CQA performance.
