Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 https://doi.org/10.1186/s13045-026-01781-y Journal of Hematology & Oncology *Correspondence: Ryuji Hamamoto rhamamot@ncc.go.jp Full list of author information is available at the end of the article Abstract The paramount challenge in precision oncology lies in further improving quality of life and response rates for individual patients. Efforts toward these goals are steadily expanding the scope of clinical implementation, despite ongoing challenges such as standardization, cost-effectiveness, and data harmonization. Building upon this maturing foundation, generative AI—which has evolved dramatically in recent years—is particularly valuable at this stage of advancing efficiency and adoption as an auxiliary technology linking literature, guidelines, trial protocols, and patient data. Specifically, through mutation interpretation, trial eligibility matching, and tumor board support, it is expected to contribute to advancing standardization, improving cost-effectiveness, accelerating data harmonization, and further accelerating human-centered decision-making. Accordingly, this review surveys the development history of generative AI and its current healthcare applications, organizing its implementation potential for precision oncology along three axes: (1) generative AI–based interpretation of genetic mutations and estimation of their pathological significance; (2) generative AI–driven verification of clinical trial eligibility; and (3) multimodal foundation models for imaging and pathology that compute “tumor phenotypes” using real-world data, contributing to report drafting and molecular surrogate estimation. In response, we propose a strategy centered on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and human-in-the-loop (HITL) workflows, encompassing data preparation based on OMOP, mCODE, and FHIR; multicenter prospective evaluation; auditable logs and governance aligned with Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) and the EU AI Act; and a synthetic data strategy including differential privacy. Ultimately, this approach validates value through real-world outcomes and charts a path toward “learning oncology,” accelerating patient-centered decision-making and clinical trial development. Keywords Precision oncology, Generative artificial intelligence, Large language model, Vision language model, Transformer, Retrieval-augmented generation Implementing generative artificial intelligence in precision oncology: safety, governance, and significance Ryuji Hamamoto1,2*, Takafumi Koyama3, Satoshi Takahashi1,2, Tomohiro Yasuda1, Kazuma Kobayashi1,2,3, Yu Akagi4, Nobuji Kouno1,2,5, Kazuki Sudo3,6, Makoto Hirata7, Kuniko Sunami8, Takashi Kubo8, Hiroshi Katayama9, Atsuo Takashima10, Tomonori Taniguchi1,2, Hiromi Matsumoto1,2, Ryota Shibaki1,2, Ken Asada1,2, Masaaki Komatsu1,2, Syuzo Kaneko1,2, Masayoshi Yamada11, Hidehito Horinouchi12, Katsuya Tanaka13, Yasushi Goto12, Ken Kato10, Yutaka Saito11, Kenichi Nakamura9 and Noboru Yamamoto3 Page 2 of 27 Hamamoto et al. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 Introduction Precision oncology is an interdisciplinary field that iden­ tifies optimal therapies and trial opportunities based on a tumor’s molecular profile and clinical context [1, 2]. Its origin dates back to the early 2000s, when the BCR- ABL inhibitor imatinib and the HER2-targeted antibody trastuzumab significantly improved survival, establishing the clinical efficacy of biomarker-guided therapy [3, 4]. Subsequently, the concept of tumor tissue independence was established. The efficacy of PD-1 inhibitors against mismatch repair deficiency (MSI-H/dMMR) and the high response rate of larotrectinib against TRK fusion tumors were reported, making tumor-agnostic molecular tar­ geted therapy a reality [5–7]. In support of clinical imple­ mentation, next-generation sequencing (NGS)-based gene panel tests are rapidly gaining widespread adop­ tion [8–10]. The prospective analyses of over 10,000 cases in MSK-IMPACT and more than 100,000 cases in the AACR Project GENIE real-world clinical–genomic repository support an iterative cycle of implementation and research [11, 12]. Conversely, while NCI-MATCH expanded treatment arms based on molecular abnor­ malities across diverse cancer types through platform testing, only 7 of the initial 27 substudies yielded positive signals, highlighting the challenge of translating molecu­ lar matching into clinical benefit [13]. Furthermore, liq­ uid biopsy based on circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) is expanding its applications to treatment selection, mini­ mal residual disease detection, and monitoring [14]. In terms of governance, ESMO revised and expanded its recommendations for tumor NGS in 2024, advancing the standardization of testing indications and operations [15]. Overall, precision medicine has evolved over the past two decades from “targeted drugs × single biomarkers” to “multi-layered omics × real-world data × adaptive trials.” While still facing challenges in standardization, cost-effectiveness, and data standardization, its scope for clinical implementation continues to expand [16]. Building upon this mature foundation, generative artifi­ cial intelligence (AI) technology is expected to acceler­ ate human-centered decision-making as an auxiliary tool that bridges literature, guidelines, trial protocols, and patient data, enabling mutation interpretation, trial eligi­ bility verification, and tumor board support. With the advent of deep learning, AI has made sig­ nificant progress and is advancing across diverse fields at a pace that is transforming the foundations of human society [17, 18]. The medical field is no exception, with AI-equipped program-based medical devices progress­ ing not only at the research level but also in clinical set­ tings, including achievements in diagnostic support [19–25]. In particular, generative AI—including foun­ dational models such as large language models (LLMs), vision language models (VLMs), and diffusion models— has made significant strides in recent years. Centered on self-supervised learning and large-scale pre-training, these models now substantially outperform conventional approaches in long-text comprehension and genera­ tion, multimodal reasoning, and knowledge grounding [26–30]. In the medical field, discussions of the general capabilities and limitations of LLMs are continuing, and design principles for clinical applications (safety, evalua­ tion, and operation) are becoming increasingly clear [31]. Specifically, clinical language models trained on free-text electronic health record (EHR) data have demonstrated effectiveness in multitask prediction and summariza­ tion [32], whereas VLMs have begun to achieve quality levels acceptable to specialist evaluation in practical gen­ eration tasks such as chest X-ray report generation [33]. Furthermore, in imaging domains such as pathology and ophthalmology, foundational models pre-trained on real clinical slides and retinal images have been reported to transfer robustly to numerous tasks [34]. However, grounding in the latest knowledge is essential for reliable generation in healthcare, and retrieval-aug­ mented generation (RAG) is positioned as a promising implementation framework from the perspectives of evi­ dence presentation, consistency, and fairness [35]. Clini­ cal use cases are steadily expanding to include medical record summaries, consultation support, drafts of imag­ ing and pathology reports, conference summaries, and HITL-based decision support grounded in guidelines and trial information [36–41]. This article provides a narrative review that compre­ hensively synthesizes recent developments in the medical application of generative AI, particularly its implemen­ tation in precision oncology, while proposing and dis­ cussing pathways toward reliable clinical deployment in light of the current lack of fully established frameworks for standardization, safety, and governance. The litera­ ture survey is primarily based on peer-reviewed articles indexed in PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus, and is complemented by an extensive review of data sheets for medical devices approved or cleared by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), as well as official docu­ ments released by companies and consortia. Broadly, our intended audience includes medical researchers and healthcare professionals; however, particular emphasis is placed on providing cancer researchers and oncologists with concrete guidance on how state-of-the-art genera­ tive AI technologies can be meaningfully connected to clinical challenges, with careful attention to feasibility and implementation considerations. We first outline the historical development of gen­ erative AI and its current applications in healthcare, and then organize key use cases that enable bidirectional translation between research and clinical practice. These Page 3 of 27 Hamamoto et al. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 include generative AI–based interpretation of genetic variants and estimation of their pathological significance, hypothesis generation for disease pathways and candi­ date therapeutics, and the summarization and structur­ ing of diagnostic findings and tumor board materials. We then discuss the potential of generative AI–driven clinical trial matching, in which eligibility criteria—par­ ticularly biomarker requirements—are extracted and structured from free-text trial descriptions and matched against patient electronic health records (EHRs). Further­ more, we describe recent advances and the current state of clinical implementation of “computational tumor phe­ notyping” using multimodal foundation models that inte­ grate imaging, pathology, genomics, and EHR data. At the same time, substantial challenges remain for the clini­ cal adoption of generative AI, including issues related to knowledge freshness and generalizability, safety and hal­ lucination, data standardization and interoperability, bias and fairness, privacy protection and the use of synthetic data, as well as regulatory oversight, accountability, and workflow integration. In response, this review proposes practical implementation strategies centered on retrieval- augmented generation (RAG) and human-in-the-loop (HITL) frameworks, supported by data harmonization based on standards such as OMOP and mCODE, mul­ ticenter prospective evaluation, continuous monitoring and model updating, synthetic data strategies incorporat­ ing differential privacy, and auditable governance aligned with Good Machine Learning Practice (GMLP) and the EU AI Act. Ultimately, we present a roadmap toward “learning oncology,” in which patient-centered value is demon­ strated through real-world outcomes, and a continuous learning cycle is established that iteratively evaluates clinical effectiveness using real-world data while updat­ ing treatment strategies under combined AI and human oversight. History and current trends in generative AI Generative AI is a field in which “deep generative mod­ els” have diversified and become increasingly sophis­ ticated alongside advances in modern deep learning. Restricted Boltzmann machines (RBMs) and deep belief networks (DBNs) are important early theoretical founda­ tions [42, 43] (Fig. 1). RBMs, energy-based models pro­ posed by Paul Smolensky in 1986 (initially introduced as harmony theory) [44], feature a bipartite graph com­ posed of visible and hidden units (with no intra-layer connections, only inter-layer connections) and probabi­ listically reconstruct and generate data. DBNs are char­ acterized by a training scheme that stacks RBMs and performs global fine-tuning after greedy, sequential pre­ training, as demonstrated by Geoffrey Hinton et al. in 2006 as a fast learning algorithm [43]. However, RBMs and DBNs have been criticized for learning instability and scalability limitations arising from Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, and consequently they did not become the mainstay of current large-scale gen­ erative AI [42, 45]. In 2013, variational autoencoders (VAEs) were proposed [46]. This framework simultane­ ously trains a probabilistic latent variable model and an inference model, enabling stable generation in a con­ tinuous latent space (Fig. 2) [46]. VAEs have been exten­ sively studied for tasks such as image reconstruction and anomaly detection in medical imaging, and applications continue to be reported today [47]. Generative Adver­ sarial Networks (GANs), introduced in 2014, establish a framework for producing high-quality samples through adversarial training between a generator and a discrimi­ nator, spawning numerous derivatives (Fig. 3A) [48–52]. Despite challenges such as training instability and mode collapse, systematic reviews have been published, and applications in medical image synthesis, completion, and domain adaptation are advancing [53, 54]. The Transformer, introduced in 2017, presented a new deep architecture centered on non-recursive, parallel­ izable self-attention, becoming the de facto standard for natural language, code, and multimodal generation (underpinning LLMs such as GPT and Gemini) (Fig. 3B) [55]. It has also been extended to the image domain as the Vision Transformer (ViT), substantially influencing med­ ical AI research [56, 57]. Moreover, recently, improve­ ments in computational and memory efficiency, such as those in the Efficient/Long-Context category, have been systematized [58, 59]. It is important to note that the Transformer continues to evolve rapidly, giving rise to diverse implementations. For example, the architecture now widely used in LLMs and VLMs is called a decoder- only Transformer, which omits the encoder component. By the 2020s, diffusion and score-based generation achieved high fidelity and diversity across images, audio, and multimodal data, becoming a mainstream approach [60–62]. Furthermore, in Transformer × Mixture-of- Experts (MoE), a design has been established and verified where Self-Attention remains dense while replacing the feed-forward network (FFN) layer with MoE to separate parameter scale and computational complexity through sparse computations. This approach has been reported to improve scaling efficiency and reduce inference costs (“Transformer + MoE-FFN” hybrid) [63–65]. Its adoption is increasingly expanding, including among commercial LLMs. In summary, while RBMs and DBNs are significant as the historical and theoretical foundations of deep gen­ erative models, the principal driving forces behind con­ temporary generative AI are VAE, GAN, diffusion-based/ score-based models, and large-scale Transformers (AR generation and multimodal). Particularly, diffusion-based Page 4 of 27 Hamamoto et al. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 models and large-scale Transformers (often combined with MoE) play a central role in both research and prac­ tical applications. Applications such as reconstruction, synthesis, anomaly detection, and data augmentation based on VAEs, GANs, and diffusion models are becom­ ing increasingly established in medical imaging. Table 1 presents an overview of major deep generative founda­ tion models. The information provided is as recent of December 2025; as this field evolves rapidly, it is strongly recommended to continually seek the most recent developments. Development status of generative AI specialized in medicine Ongoing efforts are directed toward applying generative AI in healthcare, aiming to reduce physicians’ documen­ tation workload through high-precision natural language generation, enable cross-referencing of clinical guidelines and research papers through long-context processing, and accelerate research productivity using synthetic data, while also advancing regulatory-cleared, workflow-inte­ grated deep-learning image reconstruction that improves diagnostic image quality under low-dose or fast-acquisi­ tion settings (Fig. 4; Table 2). Medical question-answering and decision support Medical question-answering (Q&A) and clinician- facing decision support have emerged as prominent use cases for foundation models. The current ecosys­ tem spans: general-purpose frontier models adapted to healthcare workflows, medical-specialized LLMs opti­ mized for clinical reasoning and safety, and open and institution-deployable models that enable local fine- tuning and privacy-preserving experimentation. Broad reviews emphasize that, while benchmark performance has advanced rapidly, real-world utility depends on Fig. 1 Structure of RBM and DBN. (modified from Figures in Ref. 216) [216]. RBM consists of two layers: a visible layer and a hidden layer. No connections exist between units within a layer. Units between layers—-that is, visible units ( v1, v2, · · · , vm) and hidden units ( h1, h2, · · · , hn)—are connected. When training data are provided to the visible layer, sampling is performed based on conditional probabilities in the order of Eqs. (1), (2), and then (1) again. Here, k ( ≥ 1) denotes the number of sampling iterations, and v(1) = v; wij is the weight connecting unit vjand hi, and bj and ci are the biases for the visible layer and hidden layer units ( vj and hi), respectively. After k sampling steps, the weights and bias are updated as shown in (3), (4), and (5). ϵ is the learning rate, W is a small random number, and b and c are initialized to 0. Regarding DBNs, one example network consists of three RBMs and a supervised learning engine. However, the actual number of RBMs constituting a DBN is variable. These RBMs are referred to as pre-training and function as unsupervised feature extractors. Conversely, supervised learning models, also known as fine-tuning, learn to output appropriate labels for unknown inputs by learning from pairs of input-target data (labeled training data). As shown in the figure, the hidden layer of the front RBM becomes the visible layer of the rear RBM. For simplicity, we considered each layer of the RBM (except the input layer) as a hidden layer of the DBN. Thus, the example in the figure represents a DBN with three hidden layers (note that the number of hidden layers equals the number of RBMs). Page 5 of 27 Hamamoto et al. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 integration design, calibration to local practice, and rig­ orous safety evaluation rather than model capability alone [31, 66]. Among medical-specialized models, Med- PaLM 2 demonstrates strong performance on USMLE- style benchmarks (MedQA) and, in structured human evaluation, physician raters preferred its long-form responses over physician-written answers across mul­ tiple clinical utility and safety-related axes; nevertheless, specialist-level consultation remains a challenging setting requiring careful deployment constraints (Table 2) [67]. In parallel with the Med-PaLM lineage, recent work has increasingly emphasized healthcare-adapted Gemini/ Gemma-based models (such as Med-Gemini and the open MedGemma family) highlighting the diversification of medical LLM development [68, 69]. Concomitantly, large clinical-language models trained on de-identified EHR narratives (e.g., GatorTron [70]) and biomedical generative models trained on literature (e.g., BioGPT [71]) illustrate a complementary direction: building domain-anchored foundation models that sup­ port medical Q&A as well as broader clinical natural lan­ guage processing tasks. Decision-support deployments increasingly extend beyond text-only Q&A to documen­ tation and summarization and, in some settings, multi­ modal reporting. For example, prospective evaluation in radiology has shown that generative AI can produce draft reports and improve documentation efficiency under clinical review, underscoring the importance of workflow-level validation rather than benchmark scores alone [72]. Concurrently, controlled studies indicate that simply providing an LLM tool to clinicians does not auto­ matically improve diagnostic reasoning compared with conventional resources, highlighting the need for user training, interface design, and clear delineation of appro­ priate reliance [41]. From a practical and governance perspective, the ulti­ mate responsibility for clinical decisions remains with human professionals, regardless of the underlying model family. Therefore, implementation should enforce data governance and auditability, perform in-house validation and bias/safety assessments aligned to the intended use, and ensure that outputs are treated as clinician-reviewed drafts or evidence-linked suggestions rather than autono­ mous recommendations [31, 66]. EHR notes: draft and structure The implementation of ambient AI (automatic note draft­ ing from conversations) was fully rolled out between 2024 and 2025 [73–75]. Microsoft × Nuance’s DAX Copi­ lot is natively integrated into Epic’s mobile app Haiku, enabling real-time audio recording during in-person or remote consultations, followed by the instant generation of draft notes (Table 2). In March 2025, Dragon Copilot was announced, integrating Nuance’s voice and ambient foundation with generative AI (​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​w​w​w​.​m​i​c​r​o​s​o​f​t​.​c​ o​m​/​e​n​-​u​s​/​h​e​a​l​t​h​-​s​o​l​u​t​i​o​n​s​/​c​l​i​n​i​c​a​l​-​w​o​r​k​f​l​o​w​/​d​r​a​g​o​n​-​c​o​ Fig. 2 VAE architecture (based on Ref. 46 and 217) [46, 217]. The purpose of training a VAE is to learn a generative model that mimics the data distribu­ tion of large-scale datasets D = { x(1), x(2), . . . , x(N) } . In VAE, instead of using the exact posterior distribution, we employed variational Bayesian inference utilizing an approximate distribution qφ (z|x) to make pθ (z|x) tractable. The fundamental hypothesis of a VAE is to model this prior distribu­ tion pθ (x|z) as a multidimensional normal distribution with K dimensions. The encoder ( qφ (z | x)) represents the probability distribution of the latent variable z from data x. In Bayesian graphical model terms, this is an “approximation of the posterior distribution.” Specifically, we estimate a combined vector consisting of the mean vector µ and the standard deviation vector σ for sampling z. Decoder ( pθ (x | z)) is a conditional decoder that generates (reconstructs) data x′ using the latent variable z as input (a conditional random variable). In Bayesian graphical model terms, this is the “likelihood of z.” VAE alternately trains the decoder parameters θ and the encoder variational parameters φ through learning by maximizing the marginal likelihood pθ (x). The figure shows the expression for decomposing the VAE objective function “marginal likelihood logpθ (x)” into two terms: the evidence lower bound (ELBO) and KL divergence. The VAE is trained using this lower bound on the log-likelihood (ELBO) as a surrogate objective function for SGD. N is the number of data, and ϵ is a probabilistic noise variable Page 6 of 27 Hamamoto et al. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 Fig. 3 (See legend on next page.) Page 7 of 27 Hamamoto et al. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 p​i​l​o​t). It has evolved into a platform supporting business operations such as multilingual documentation, letter and summary generation, and conversational ordering. Google Cloud previously offered MedLM (medlm- medium and medlm-large), a healthcare-specific model family on Vertex AI, for API-based medical question answering and summarization (e.g., drafting summaries from clinical notes such as SOAP-style notes and extract­ ing key data points from laboratory results). However, note that MedLM has been deprecated and is no longer available on or after September 29, 2025. By 2025, Ver­ tex AI Search for Healthcare has also been enhanced, advancing the integration of secure, patient-level search and generative responses for medical records, scanned documents, and similar materials. AWS has launched HealthScribe in general availability (​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​d​o​c​s​.​a​w​s​.​a​m​ a​z​o​n​.​c​o​m​/​t​r​a​n​s​c​r​i​b​e​/​l​a​t​e​s​t​/​d​g​/​h​e​a​l​t​h​-​s​c​r​i​b​e​.​h​t​m​l), ​o​f​f​e​ r​i​n​g speaker separation (diarization), medical terminol­ ogy extraction, and summary generation through a single API. Epic itself announced the full rollout of its AI scribe feature in 2025 [76] and is currently expanding note drafting and suggestion capabilities within EHR inter­ faces through collaborations with Nuance (Microsoft) and Abridge. Regarding clinical effectiveness, quality improvement and observational studies have reported reductions in “note-taking time per case” and “documentation bur­ den and burnout” following the introduction of ambient AI [77, 78]. Evidence is accumulating from quality met­ rics derived from EHR logs containing the “pajama time” indicator and PDSQI-9, suggesting improvements in note quality and work-related stress, although most studies are single-center, before-and-after comparisons, and ran­ domized trials remain in the early stages [79–82]. As a note of caution, in actual deployment, it is essen­ tial to establish governance measures such as audit log­ ging, verification of specialized terminology extraction, and patient consent and audio handling, assuming a workflow in which humans perform the final confirma­ tion. All major vendors emphasize safety measures and compliance frameworks specifically designed for health­ care contexts. Imaging report generation Recently, the generation of diagnostic imaging reports using generative AI has progressed rapidly from research to clinical evaluation and implementation phases [72, 83–86]. Google DeepMind’s Flamingo-CXR fine-tuned a vision-based model and underwent specialist evaluation using historical data from U.S. ICUs and Indian inpatient and outpatient settings. Results demonstrated that a high proportion of normal cases were assessed as equivalent to or superior to human-generated reports. In collabora­ tive settings (AI draft plus physician review), significantly more cases received favorable or equivalent evaluations compared with AI-only operation (e.g., Indian data: AI- only 51.2% → collaborative 71.2%) (Fig. 5; Table 2) [33]. However, clinically significant errors persist in both human and AI systems, suggesting that assistive use remains more realistic than full automation. For large-scale general-purpose vision-language mod­ els such as GPT-4 V/4o, findings have been mixed. Some studies have reported that attempts to directly generate “Impressions” in zero-shot settings yield suboptimal text and clinical metrics under rigorous cross-dataset evalu­ ation, whereas others demonstrate comparable accuracy through careful configuration and task decomposition [87–90]. Overall, challenges remain in localization and detailed lesion alignment, underscoring the advantages of domain-specific models and the necessity of struc­ tured input and stepwise inference [91, 92]. RadAlign has been reported to achieve a GREEN score (an index ranging from 0 to 1 that evaluates the quality of radiology reports based on clinically significant error) of 0.678 in report generation and to outperform existing approaches by linking concept-aligned vision-language and language models, while enhancing evidence-based reasoning through retrieval from prior cases (RAG) (Table 2) [93]. At the research level, the three-stage link­ age of concept alignment → language generation → case retrieval appears promising for improving both hal­ lucination suppression and readability. In evaluations closer to clinical settings, a multi-reader experiment using chest X-rays demonstrated that physician review of AI-generated drafts (domain-specific multimodal gen­ erative AI) reduced reading time, improved sensitivity for certain findings, and enhanced report quality [94]. However, operational challenges such as false positives, (See figure on previous page.) Fig. 3 A GAN architecture (based on Ref. 48) [48]. In adversarial learning, the value function V (D, G) is used as the objective function to perform alter­ nating optimization via a minmax game between G and D. Here, pz(z) is the random noise generating the latent variable z, and pdata(x) denotes the distribution for generating mini-batches randomly from the entire dataset. By alternately repeating the optimization of discriminator D as shown in (a) and generator G as shown in (b), once D and G have sufficiently learned, they eventually reach a state called the Nash equilibrium, which becomes the solution to the minmax game. The data generated by G(z) becomes sufficiently realistic, and D will only classify it as 1 (True). This marks the end of GAN training. B Transformer architecture (based on Ref. 54) [55]. The core of the Transformer is self-attention based on scaled dot-product attention. For each attention unit, we learned three weight matrices: the query weight matrix WQ, the key weight matrix WK, and the value weight matrix WV. For the i-th token, the query vector qi = xiWQ, the key vector ki = xiWK, and the value vector vi = xiWV are obtained by multiplying the input embedding xi by the respective weight matrices Page 8 of 27 Hamamoto et al. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 false negatives, and inter-physician variability were also explicitly noted. Furthermore, a prospective randomized controlled trial (ACER) is planned to evaluate the impact of AI triage combined with report support on clinical outcomes and cost-effectiveness (NCT06456203), estab­ lishing a framework for field evaluation toward clinical implementation. Synthetic EHR data Research on synthetic EHR generation based on Trans­ former architectures has accelerated in recent years, with efforts to standardize quality assessment across three major dimensions: (1) reproducibility of time series and code hierarchies, (2) privacy evaluation (MIA/AIA, re-identification AUROC, etc.), and (3) utility in down­ stream tasks (TSTR/TSTR-aug, distribution distance, etc.) [95, 96]. CEHR-GPT is a pipeline that extends tem­ porally embedded patient representations via CEHR- BERT using autoregressive generation, subsequently converting the generated sequences into OMOP CDM [97, 98]. Its implementation (GitHub) and technical documentation for OHDSI are fully developed, encom­ passing disease progression scenarios, exploratory counterfactual analyses, and cohort validity verification procedures. Hierarchy- and Semantics-Guided Transformer (HiSGT) is a framework integrating hierarchical relation­ ships (parent–child and sibling) within the ICD with the semantics of clinical language models as a hierarchical graph combined with embeddings, enabling code genera­ tion guided by both hierarchy and semantics [99]. Using MIMIC-III/IV datasets, it improves statistical fidelity (n-gram and dimensionality metrics) and utility in TSTR while including privacy evaluations such as MIA/attri­ bute inference. SynEHRgy introduces a dedicated tokenization strategy for mixed-type events (ICD codes, irregularly sampled time series, and covariates), enabling holistic sequential generation with a GPT-like decoder-only Transformer under a causal language modeling objective (Table 2) [100]. Using MIMIC-III, it benchmarks fidelity, util­ ity, and privacy against prior methods, reporting pri­ vacy indicators such as membership inference attack (MIA) AUROC values close to 0.5 (≒ random) under the evaluated distance metrics. The implementation code is publicly available, and MIMIC-III can be accessed via PhysioNet (credentialed access required). Synthetic4Health uses a mask-and-regenerate pipeline for de-identified clinical letters, systematically compar­ ing multiple pre-trained language models for masking and generation with a focus on Bio_ClinicalBERT (Table 2) [101]. Quality is evaluated primarily with BERTScore, and downstream utility is assessed via NER, showing that synthetic letters can serve as effective substitutes for real documents while promoting diversity (data/code avail­ able) [101]. From an operational standpoint, “complete anonymity” should not be assumed; privacy risk assess­ ment should combine complementary tests such as MIAs and identifier/PHI leakage detection (e.g., AUROC of an identifier detector) [102]. Stronger MIAs for synthetic data have been proposed (e.g., DOMIAS and subsequent variants), making comprehensive, reproducible evalua­ tion designs essential [103]. Governance measures such as re-identification audits, usage restrictions, and (where appropriate) differential privacy should be considered within the facility. Patient triage and health chatbots By 2025, the implementation and field evaluation of AI- powered patient triage systems and conversational chat­ bots are accelerating [104–112]. Infermedica has released Conversational Triage, which combines an LLM with a Bayesian knowledge graph and presents clinical valida­ tion results for safety and accuracy alongside its MDR Class IIb certification (​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​i​n​f​e​r​m​e​d​i​c​a​.​c​o​m​/​b​l​o​g​/​a​r​t​ i​c​l​e​s​/​l​a​u​n​c​h​i​n​g​-​c​o​n​v​e​r​s​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​-​t​r​i​a​g​e​?​u​t​m​_​s​o​u​r​c​e​=​c​h​a​t​g​ p​t​.​c​m) (Table 2). In the field of emergency medicine, prospective evalu­ ations of triage using general-purpose LLMs such as ChatGPT-4o as an adjunct have been reported. Find­ ings indicate a high level of agreement with specialist (gold-standard) assessments across multiple facilities and performance equivalent to or exceeding that of conven­ tional staff [113, 114]. However, variability among studies remains, and challenges for practical implementation— such as the risks of under- or over-triage and case selec­ tion bias—continue to be emphasized. Regarding primary to subacute care navigation and symptom self-assessment, the UK’s Tony Blair Insti­ tute estimates that full-scale implementation of AI tri­ age across NHS 111 and GP reception services could potentially replace 29 million appointments annually and reduce costs by £340 million [115]. However, the accumulation of randomized trials demonstrating actual improvements in medical outcomes and safety remains limited, warranting caution when estimating implemen­ tation effects [116]. Recent peer-reviewed studies on the diagnostic and triage accuracy of symptom checkers and chatbots indicate that performance varies depending on the tool and clinical setting, with some reports express­ ing concern about the risk of missing dangerous condi­ tions [117, 118]. Therefore, urgency assessments must always be conducted under clinician supervision, and guardrails (prompt design, escalation rules, audit logs) specifically designed to detect high-risk indicators are recommended. Advances in few- shot learning and instruction following have enhanced its versatility as a foundational model, enabling adaptation to diverse tasks without task-specific fine-tuning. It supports external system integration via tool (function) invocation, multimodal processing of text and images, and long-context workloads. GPT-5 is deployed as a unified system with a fast model, a deeper rea­ soning model, and a real-time router that selects between them based on conversation needs. In the API, GPT-5.2 supports a 400,000-token context window with up to 128,000 output tokens; because the context window is shared between input and output (and, for some models, rea­ soning tokens), the maximum input length depends on how much output you allow Gemini Gemini 3.0 Pro Google Gemini is a family of ‘born multimodal’ foundation models that can ingest text, images, audio, video, and documents, and generate high-quality outputs (typically text; with dedicated models available for media generation). With the introduction of Gemini 3 Pro (preview), Google positions the Pro tier as its most advanced reasoning model, capable of processing large and heterogeneous inputs—including text/code, images, audio, video, PDFs, and even large code repositories—within a 1 million–token input context window (with up to 64 K output tokens). On the developer side, the Gemini API/Vertex AI stack supports built-in tools (Search grounding, URL context, code execution, file search) and custom function calling (with current constraints on combining built-in tools and custom tool calls in a single request) Llama (Large Language Model Meta AI) Llama 4 Meta Llama models are decoder-only Transformer-based LMs, and the latest Llama 4 releases adopt a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) design with native multimodal capabilities and very long-context support. Llama 4 Scout and Llama 4 Maverick are distributed as open-weight models under the Llama 4 Community License (not OSI-approved open source): it includes attribution/redistribu­ tion requirements such as displaying ‘Built with Llama,’ requires additional permission for entities exceeding 700 million monthly active users, and mandates that any distributed AI model trained or fine-tuned using Llama 4 materials or outputs must begin its name with ‘Llama.’ Llama 4 Behe­ moth (~ 2 T total parameters) has been announced as a teacher model but remains in training. Practical ultra-long context usage may vary by deployment framework and hardware constraints Claude Claude Opus 4.5/ Claude Sonnet 4.5 Anthropic Claude is Anthropic’s family of LLMs; the current top-tier lineup includes Claude Sonnet 4.5 as a high-capability, general-purpose frontier model and Claude Opus 4.5 as the highest-capability option. Claude Sonnet 4 offers up to a 1 M-token input context window in public beta on the Anthropic API for eligible customers (and is also available via Amazon Bedrock and Google Cloud Vertex AI). For deeper reasoning, the Anthropic API provides Extended Thinking via a thinking configuration with an adjustable budget_tokens. Claude supports tool use and can analyze images and PDFs. In the Claude apps, Integrations can connect to services such as Google Work­ space and Slack; when using Claude in Slack, the included conversation context is capped (e.g., the most recent messages from a channel or thread), and some app features may be unavailable DeepSeek DeepSeek-V3.2 DeepSeek DeepSeek is a Chinese open-source LLM family. DeepSeek-V3 adopts a MoE architecture (671B parameters total/37B effective per token) and is pre-trained on approximately 14.8 trillion tokens. It optimizes inference and training efficiency through Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) and DeepSeekMoE, implementing load balancing without auxiliary losses and Multi-Token Prediction. DeepSeek also excels at multilingual tasks. DeepSeek Coder 33B reports top-tier open-source results on code benchmarks such as HumanEval Qwen Qwen3 Alibaba Alibaba’s Qwen is an open-weight LLM family. The latest Qwen3 spans 0.6B to 235B parameters (including MoE) and integrates “thinking” and “non-thinking” capabilities within a single model. Users can switch modes and control the thinking budget based on application needs. Qwen3 was pre-trained on ~ 36 T tokens and supports up to 119 languages and dialects, and the publicly released weights are distributed under the Apache-2.0 license. Previous generations (e.g., Qwen2 and Qwen2.5) cover 0.5B–72B, including dense and MoE variants, with context length depending on the specific model size (e.g., some 32 K, others up to 128 K). Tool/func­ tion calling is supported (with official templates and an agent framework via Qwen-Agent). For structured outputs, the Alibaba Cloud Model Studio stack provides JSON Object/JSON Schema– based response formatting for supported Qwen endpoints. Qwen-VL/Qwen2.5-VL extends the vision-language line with strong document and chart understanding, precise localization, and long-video comprehension Table 1 Overview of primary deep generative foundation models (Information available as of December 2025) Page 10 of 27 Hamamoto et al. Journal of Hematology & Oncology (2026) 19:14 In the industrial sector, Google Cloud previously offered MedLM on Vertex AI for healthcare-oriented question answering and drafting summaries from clini­ cal documentation, with human review required; it must not be used for any diagnostic or therapeutic purpose or in direct patient care, and direct patient use is pro­ hibited. Some deployments and pilots combined model outputs with healthcare data and EHR-connected work­ flows (e.g., generating draft clinical notes for clinician review), reflecting continued ecosystem development by major platforms. In terms of governance, the Chatbot Assessment Reporting Tool (CHART) was published in 2025 by The CHART Collaborative (correspondence to Bright Huo), establishing reporting standards for chatbot health advice studies to improve methodological rigor and transparency in both research and evaluation set­ tings [105]. Nevertheless, safety risks associated with premature implementation have been highlighted [119]. Furthermore, under the EU AI Act, medical chatbots may qualify as high-risk AI, necessitating enhanced risk man­ agement, transparency, and monitoring [120]. Implementation considerations include LLM-assisted human decision-making in emergency care, rule-based systems or knowledge graphs in primary care, and com­ prehensive safeguard designs integrating structured inputs such as vital signs, escalation thresholds, and audit logs [114]. file_name: Integrating Topic-Aware Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network With Transformer Model for Medical Scientific Document Abstractive Summarization.pdf
text: Received 9 July 2024, accepted 4 August 2024, date of publication 16 August 2024, date of current version 26 August 2024. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2024.3443730 Integrating Topic-Aware Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network With Transformer Model for Medical Scientific Document Abstractive Summarization AYESHA KHALIQ 1, ATIF KHAN 2, SALMAN AFSAR AWAN1, SALMAN JAN 3, MUHAMMAD UMAIR 4, AND MEGAT F. ZUHAIRI 5, (Senior Member, IEEE) 1Department of Computer Science, University of Agriculture Faisalabad, Faisalabad 03802, Pakistan 2Department of Computer Science, Islamia College Peshawar, Peshawar, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa 25120, Pakistan 3Department of Information Technology, Al Buraimi University College, Al Buraimi 512, Oman 4Department of Computer Science, City University of Science and Information Technology, Peshawar 25000, Pakistan 5Malaysian Institute of Information Technology, Universiti Kuala Lumpur, Kuala Lumpur 50250, Malaysia Corresponding authors: Megat F. Zuhairi (megatfarez@unikl.edu.my) and Salman Jan (salman@buc.edu.om) This work was supported by Malaysian Institute of Information Technology, Universiti Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. ABSTRACT The development of abstractive summarization methods is a crucial task in Natural Language Processing (NLP) that presents challenges, which require the creation of intelligent systems that are capable of extracting the main idea from text effectively and generate coherent summary. Numerous existing abstrac- tive approaches do not take into account the importance of the broader context or fail to capture the global semantics in identifying salient content for summary. Moreover, there is lack of studies that extensively evaluated abstractive summarization models for specific domains, such as medical scientific document summarization. With this motivation behind, this paper developed an integrated framework for abstractive summarization of medical scientific documents that integrates topic-aware Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network with a Transformer model. The suggested framework uses Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) for topic modeling to uncover latent topics and global information, thus preserving document-level attributes important for creation of effective summaries. In addition to topic modeling, the framework utilized a Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network (HGNN), capable of capturing the relationship between sentences through graph-based document representation, and allows for the concurrent updating of both local and global information. Finally, the framework is integrated with a Transformer decoder, which greatly enhances the ability of model to produce accurate and informative abstractive summaries. The performance of proposed framework is evaluated on publicly available PubMed dataset related to medical scientific papers. Experimental results illustrate that the suggested framework for abstractive summarization showed superior performance as compared to the state-of-the-art models, achieving high F1-Scores: 46.03 for Rouge-1, 21.42 for Rouge-2, and 39.71 for Rouge-L. Our research makes a significant contribution to the field of natural language processing, particularly in the area of medical scientific document summarization. It demonstrates superior performance and provides a deeper understanding of document structure, and has the potential to impact various applications by offering efficient access to information. INDEX TERMS BERT, LDA, GAT, TF-IDF, transformer, medical documents, abstractive summarization. The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Ganesh Naik . I. INTRODUCTION The primary goal of automatic text summarization is to enable a quick review of documents by generating a coher- ent and concise summary that retains the original essence VOLUME 12, 2024 2024 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ 113855 A. Khaliq et al.: Integrating Topic-Aware HGNN With Transformer Model of the documents [1]. Text summarization can be classified into two categories: extractive and abstractive. Extractive summarization involves the systematic selection of original sentences and content directly from the source document. Conversely, the abstractive summarization technique uti- lizes sophisticated procedures to significantly transform and rephrase sentences from the original input documents [2]. Most of the research in text summarization has focused news reports (brief documents), conversation records (collabora- tive documents), critiques (informal documents), and social media posts (tweets). Conversely, researchers have devoted less attention to summarization in medical domain, specif- ically for medical scientific documents concerning various diseases. Abstractive models align more closely with the human nature of summarization and can produce high-quality summaries [3]. Consequently, the research presented in this paper utilizes an abstractive technique for the summarization of medical scientific documents. Modeling the relationships between sentences is a cru- cial step for identifying the relevant content and main theme of the input text in the process of generating a sum- mary [4]. In recent years, several studies [5], [6], utilized Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) to grasp the relation- ships between sentences. However, recurrent models face challenges in accurately capturing long-range dependen- cies among sentences and efficiently managing computa- tional resources [7]. Modeling global information, especially topical information, constitutes another critical aspect of summarization. It significantly influences the selection of sentences and provides additional context for understanding documents [3], [8]. However, it has been observed that sev- eral previous studies [9], [10] have considered topic models as a standalone source of information, neglecting to investigate a cohesive approach that concurrently improves both the task of text summarization (capturing complex inter-sentence relationships within documents) and the effectiveness of topic models. In recent years, there have been notable achievements in applying Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) or Graph Atten- tion Networks (GAT) [11] to summarize documents [4], [12], [13], [14], [15], [16], [17]. This success arises from their ability to capture complex inter-sentence relation- ships within documents. Specifically, GNN models demon- strate proficiency in representing complex structural data by encapsulating semantic units (nodes) and their inter- connections (edges). Due to these capabilities, there is growing interest in leveraging the GAT framework along- side topic modeling for abstractive summarization tasks. The researchers in [12] proposed a heterogeneous neural graph structure using BERT to obtain contextual sen- tence representations. They simultaneously trained with latent topics using the Neural Topic Model (NTM) to model global information. The study of [18] proposed an expanded model that employed NTM for abstractive text summarization. The authors of [15] introduced a graph structure enriched with latent topical information. They employed K-Means and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) for topic extraction. The study of [3] proposed an innovative Graph-Based Topic-Aware abstractive text summarization model. Initially, documents were encoded with BERT to generate sentence representations. Concurrently, NTM identified potential top- ics, while GAT refined these sentences and topic nodes. Finally, the sentence embeddings, enriched with topic infor- mation, were fed into a Transformer-driven decoder to generate summaries. Another study by [19] introduced a heterogeneous graph that incorporated topical information for abstractive summa- rization of Chinese complaint reports. To achieve this, GAT was formulated using sentence context embeddings and latent topics. Both the document context embeddings and topic data were updated concurrently, thereby addressing the issue of semantic fragmentation [3]. Despite the effectiveness of existing methods, there remains a need for a comprehensive, topic-aware neural graph model that integrates global semantic information and is coupled with an advanced decoder designed specifically for abstractive summarization of medical scientific documents. This study introduces an innovative Topic-aware Het- erogenous Graph Neural Network (HGNN) model aimed at summarizing medical documents. Instead of focusing solely on creating a sentence-level graph, the model integrates additional semantic components such as words and latent topic nodes by incorporating them as auxiliary elements within the graph. The initial phase involves using a BERT- Encoder (Bidirectional Encoder Representations) [20] to encode the entire document, thereby generating sentence and word nodes. Following this, latent topic nodes are identified using a well-established generative statistical topic modeling technique known as Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) [21]. In the next phase, a heterogeneous document graph is con- structed, comprising sentences, words, and global topic nodes linked by edge features quantified as TF-IDF values. The representations of these nodes are then refined and updated using GAT. Ultimately, the sentence representations, enriched with topic information, are fed into a Transformer-based decoder to generate abstractive summaries. Our approach differs from previous works. For instance, the research by [3] relies on the Neural Topic Model (NTM) for topical informa- tion, which requires complex training setups [15]. Similarly, our method differs from work conducted by [19], which focused on constructing a heterogeneous graph solely for word and topic nodes, specifically for summarizing Chinese complaint reports. The key contributions of our model are outlined as follows: • This study introduces a novel Neural Graph struc- ture enriched with topical information for abstrac- tive summarization of medical scientific documents. This approach integrates the LDA Topic Extractor, BERT-Encoder, Graph Attention Network, and Trans- former model into a cohesive framework. 113856 VOLUME 12, 2024 A. Khaliq et al.: Integrating Topic-Aware HGNN With Transformer Model • The framework effectively captures global semantic information, guiding the summary generation process and addressing the challenge of limited semantic context in traditional summarization methods. • Leveraging data from the publicly available PubMed dataset, our research demonstrates consistent and favor- able results compared to state-of-the-art heterogeneous graph structures used for both extractive and abstractive summarization tasks. The paper is organized as follows: Section II critically reviews existing abstractive summarization techniques, Topic models, and Neural Graph-based models. Section III presents the proposed model’s methodology in detail. Section IV illustrates a comparative analysis of our model with state- of-the-art approaches. It also details the experimental setup, overall performance, and ablation studies of the proposed model. Finally, Section V concludes the paper and outlines potential avenues for future research. II. RELATED WORK Our research includes three key areas: 1. A review of fundamental methods used for abstractive summarization. 2. An examination of topic-aware models that combine topic modeling with other techniques to improve summarization. 3. An exploration of how neural graph-based networks are used for both extractive and abstractive summarization tasks. A. ABSTRACTIVE TEXT SUMMARIZATION Abstractive text summarization is dominated by the sequence-to-sequence framework [22]. Early versions relied on RNNs [23], specifically Long Short-Term Mem- ory (LSTM) units, to encode the input text into a vector representation and then decode it into a summary [3]. However, LSTMs struggle to capture long-range depen- dencies in lengthy sequences, leading to poor summariza- tion and inefficient use of computational resources [7]. Attention mechanisms [24] were introduced to address this issue. However, they can still suffer from issues like out- of-vocabulary (OOV) words and repetitive outputs. The Pointer-Generator Network with Coverage [25], tackles these problems by cleverly controlling the inclusion of words during generation, achieving impressive results. Conventional RNN architectures, although well-suited for NLP tasks due to their ability to handle variable-length sequences, suffer from several limitations. These limita- tions include challenges in parallelization during training, increased training complexity, and difficulties in captur- ing long-range dependencies and hierarchical relationships within the data. To address these shortcomings, Google intro- duced the Transformer model [7], a state-of-the-art archi- tecture that leverages self-attention mechanisms to enable parallel processing of the model. This approach represents a significant leap forward compared to traditional RNNs. BERT, a powerful pre-trained language model based on Transformers [20], has significantly improved performance in various summarization tasks [26], [27], [28], [29], [30], [31], [32]. Leading models like BART [33] (Bidirec- tional and Auto-Regressive Transformers), PEGASUS [34] (Pre-training with Extracted Gap-sentences for Abstractive Summarization), and ProphetNet [35], all employ Trans- former architecture for abstractive summarization task. The researchers in [36] introduced a groundbreaking approach for summarization and question answering using Large Language Models (LLMs). Their approach ensures LLMs are not overloaded with irrelevant data, saving time and resources. Additionally, scholars of [37] explored how knowl- edge graphs can enhance summaries [37]. They integrated knowledge graphs with BART and developed multi-source transformer modules that can process both textual and graph- based information, leading to more accurate and cohesive summaries. B. TOPIC-AWARE TEXT SUMMARIZATION Modeling topical information is another crucial aspect for selecting sentences in text summarization [8]. The authors in [12] used BERT to capture contextual information in sentences and employed the Neural Topic Model (NTM) to model global topics concurrently. The study in [18] proposed an expanded model using NTM for abstractive summa- rization. The authors of [15] introduced a graph structure enriched with topics extracted through clustering meth- ods like K-means and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM). An unsupervised extractive summarization approach is explored in [38]. This approach integrated clustering with topic modeling (using LDA) to reduce topic bias and utilizes K-Medoid clustering for generating summaries. Several studies explored integrating topic modeling with summarization techniques. The work in [39] proposed a novel approach that combined BERT for contextual embeddings, an NTM for topic discovery, and a transformer network to capture long-range dependencies. This facilitated an end-to- end process for topic inference and summarization. The study in [40] leveraged latent topic information derived from topic vectors and sequential networks to improve the quality and accuracy of summaries. The study of [41] effectively combined a graph contrastive topic model with a pre-trained language model to utilize both broad and detailed contextual information for extrac- tive summarization of long documents. Building upon the concept of topic awareness, the study in [3] proposed a novel Graph-Based Abstractive Summarization model. The document undergoes BERT encoding to generate sentence embeddings, capturing the semantic meaning of each sen- tence. Simultaneously, a Neural Topic Model (NTM) is used to identify potential topics within the document. A hetero- geneous document graph is then constructed. This graph incorporates nodes for both the sentence embeddings and the identified topics. To further refine the relationships within the graph, a modified version of Graph Attention Network (GAT) is employed. Finally, the sentence embeddings, enriched with VOLUME 12, 2024 113857 A. Khaliq et al.: Integrating Topic-Aware HGNN With Transformer Model the topic information from the refined graph, are given to a Transformer decoder to generate the summaries. C. NEURAL GRAPH-BASED TEXT SUMMARIZATION Modern text summarization research has shifted from homo- geneous graphs with static nodes to heterogeneous networks. These networks allow for the inclusion of diverse node types, which represent wide range of textual elements. In addition, they allow for dynamic updates during the summarization process. The approach of [4] constructed a graph neural network based on word co-occurrences within the document to capture word-level relationships. The study in [42] utilized syntactic graph convolutional networks (GCNs) to model the non-Euclidean structure of documents. This approach effectively captured long-range dependencies beyond simple word order. In addition, an attention mechanism is integrated to focus on relevant content for summarization. The authors of [43] employed a network with three distinct node types: sentences, Elementary Discourse Units (EDUs), and entities. They utilized RST discourse parsing to grasp the relationships between EDUs, which provides a deeper comprehension of the document’s structure. The research in [44] presented a heterogeneous graph network that incorporated information from both words and sentences. The model focused on redundancy dependencies between sentences, and iteratively refined sentence repre- sentations through a redundancy-aware graph. This iterative process aimed to enhance the model’s ability to capture the essential meaning of each sentence. Recent research examined novel network structures and attention mechanisms for extractive summarization of long documents [45]. One approach utilized a transformer-based architecture within a heterogeneous network and included distinct node types for tokens, entities, and sentences. The study discovered that a multi-granularity sparse attention mechanism assisted in focusing on important relationships between these diverse nodes during the summarization process. Another study proposed a novel Multiplex Graph Con- volutional Network (M-GCN) architecture [46], which were effective at capturing various kinds of relationships between words and sentences. The model took into account both rela- tionships within sentences (intra-sentential) and relationships between sentences (inter-sentential) to produce better sum- maries, particularly for long documents. Moreover, the work of [14] improved the current heteroge- neous graph approaches by adding passage nodes alongside word and sentence nodes. The inclusion of passage nodes enhanced the network’s ability to represent each sentence within the graph structure. This resulted in a more thorough comprehension of the document’s structure, which ultimately assisted the creation of more accurate summaries. Researchers of [47] employed the Text Graph Multi- Headed Attention Network (TGA) to effectively capture sentence representations across various types of text graphs at different levels. This method leveraged multi-headed attention to focus on relevant information within the graph structure. The study in [48] proposed a novel method based on hypergraph transformers. The method iteratively refined and developed strong representations of sentences by strategically incorporating various relationships between them. These relationships include underlying themes, key terms,

title: ItemRAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Item-Based Knowledge
file_name: ItemRAG Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Item-Based Knowledge Computing for E-Commerce Product Question Answering.pdf
text: ItemRAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Item-Based Knowledge Computing for E-Commerce Product Question Answering Changliang Xu, Yukun Kang, Quan Feng, Jinghua Hua, Piji Li, Feiran Wu, Hu Wei, Xiang Chen*, Sheng-Jun Huang, and Songcan Chen Abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into e-commerce platforms has significantly enhanced user experience through personalized recommendations and automated customer support. However, existing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) frameworks face challenges when applied to e- commerce product Question Answering (QA), such as handling extensive product catalogs, ensuring timely knowledge updates, and maintaining efficient retrieval performance. In this paper, we propose ItemRAG, a novel framework that combines RAG with item-based knowledge computing to address these challenges. ItemRAG decouples QA templates from specific products by leveraging a dynamic knowledge graph, enabling efficient updates and reducing the size of the knowledge base. The framework includes state analysis to capture user intent and context, grouped indexing for efficient retrieval, and knowledge computing to dynamically generate accurate answers. Experimental results demonstrate that decoupled-based ItemRAG significantly outperforms the Coupled-based RAG approaches (CoupledRAG) in retrieval accuracy and generation quality, achieving higher precision, recall, F1-score, and factual correctness. Our work highlights the efficacy of integrating the knowledge graph with RAG to enhance LLM-based e-commerce customer service systems. Key words: retrieval augmentation; knowledge computing; e-commerce customer service; Large Language Models (LLMs) 1 Introduction The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has revolutionized various domains by enabling advanced natural language understanding and generation capabilities. In the e-commerce sector, LLMs have been increasingly applied to enhance the online shopping experience. Applications range from personalized product recommendations to automated content creation and intelligent customer support. For Changliang Xu and Yukun Kang are with Hangzhou Institute for Advanced Study, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hangzhou 310024, China.
