file_name: MGPRAG Enhancing Medical Large Language Models via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation.pdf
text: Received 10 September 2025, accepted 20 October 2025, date of publication 23 October 2025, date of current version 29 October 2025. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3624887 MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical Large Language Models via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation YANWEN SHEN , DAHONG XU , XI LI , LEI FU, AND HONG LIU College of Information Science and Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China Corresponding author: Xi Li (lixi@hunnu.edu.cn) ABSTRACT Large language models(LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance in general tasks, but remain insufficiently trusted in complex clinical question answering (CQA). This is largely due to concerns about the accuracy of the generated content. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), which supplements LLMs with external knowledge, has emerged as a promising solution. However, existing RAG approaches often suffer from imprecise knowledge retrieval, leading to redundant information and reducing response quality. In this paper, we propose MGPRAG (Medical Graph Precision RAG), a precision retrieval-augmented generation framework based on medical knowledge graphs. Specifically, it incorporates a structured task parsing module that decomposes complex CQA tasks into clinical manifestations, core medical entities, and sub-questions. Additionally, it adopts a Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder to improve semantic alignment between the extracted core medical entities and corresponding entities in the medical knowledge graph. Finally, it implements a Medical Value-Driven retrieval algorithm, which leverages a medical encoder and a disease relevance filtering strategy to achieve precise knowledge retrieval. Extensive experiments conducted on CQA benchmarks show that MGPRAG significantly outperforms strong baselines in both retrieval precision and generation quality. Moreover, the proposed Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder exhibits superior performance on both public datasets and self-constructed Medical Entity Semantic Matching (MESM) dataset. INDEX TERMS Clinical question answering, large language models, medical knowledge graph, retrieval- augmented generation. I. INTRODUCTION In recent years, large language models (LLMs), such as GPT- 4o [1] and DeepSeek-V3 [2], have been widely applied to various downstream tasks [3], [4]. These include, but are not limited to, data cleaning [5], machine translation [6], and text summarization. This can be attributed to their advanced semantic understanding [7] and generation capabilities [8]. Nevertheless, when applied to complex clinical question answering (CQA) tasks that demand high accuracy in the generated content, these models exhibit increasingly The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Fu Lee Wang . severe hallucination issues [9]. This greatly undermines their trustworthiness [10], [11]. To improve the accuracy of content generated by LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) [12] has emerged as a pivotal technique. It integrates external knowledge sources with LLMs’ generation abilities and has demonstrated remarkable effectiveness [13], [14]. Current RAG methods can be broadly categorized into two paradigms: document- based RAG and knowledge graph-based RAG. As illustrated in Fig. 1(a), document-based approaches typically segment the original corpus into numerous chunks. Both the user question and these chunks are then encoded into vector representations, after which semantic similarity is computed between the question and each chunk. The top-k most similar VOLUME 13, 2025 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ 183225 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation FIGURE 1. Three retrieval methods in medical RAG systems.(a) Document-based retrieval: selects the top-k chunks from a corpus that are most semantically similar to the question as external knowledge.(b) TOG retrieval: Utilizes LLMs to select relations and entities in the knowledge graph that are relevant to the question, and then generates answers by leveraging both the retrieved knowledge and the internal knowledge of the LLM.(c) MGPRAG retrieval: Employs a Medical Value-Driven retrieval algorithm to select relations and entities in the knowledge graph that are relevant to the question. If no knowledge is retrieved in the current round, the system automatically proceeds to the next round of retrieval.E, E1, and E2 denote entities involved in the knowledge graph retrieval process. chunks are subsequently selected as external knowledge to augment the generation process [15], [16]. However, in the medical domain, such approaches encounter several issues: medical documents often contain complex sentence structures and interwoven information, which can lead to the retrieval of redundant knowledge [17]; moreover, some medical tasks require multi-step reasoning, and a single-step retrieval mechanism is insufficient to support this process [18]. In contrast, knowledge graphs represent information in a structured manner using triples (entity–relation–entity). These explicit semantic relations and precise entity associa- tions not only enhance the precision of knowledge retrieval [19], [20], but also inherently support multi-hop reason- ing through graph traversal mechanisms (e.g., A→B→C). Therefore, knowledge graphs serve as an ideal external knowledge source for improving LLM’s generation abilities [21], [22], [23]. Most existing knowledge graph-based RAG methods proceed by first identifying core entities from the input task, then matching these entities with relevant nodes in the knowledge graph, and finally retrieving related knowledge to guide LLM generation [24]. However, directly applying these methods to complex CQA tasks remains highly challenging. Even sophisticated approaches like TOG [25], which leverage LLMs to construct multi-hop reasoning paths over knowledge graphs (Fig. 1(b)), still face notable difficulties. One of the key challenges lies in the entity matching stage. Current approaches employ pretrained encoders to embed both task-related core entities and knowledge graph entities into vector representations, with the subsequent matching process relying on semantic similarity between these embeddings [26]. However, the inherent semantic complexity of medical entities, where a single concept may have multiple names or synonyms [27], makes accurate matching particularly difficult. Such mismatches in the entity matching stage can propagate through retrieval steps, ultimately reducing the relevance and usefulness of the retrieved knowledge. Another major challenge stems from the inherent uncontrollability of LLMs during the retrieval process [28]. They often struggle to consistently identify entities and relations in the knowledge graph that are truly relevant to the input task. This can lead to the retrieval of noisy or misleading information, further degrading the overall quality of the generated responses. To address the above challenges, we propose a precision graph-based RAG framework, called MGPRAG (Medical Graph Precise RAG). It consists of three innovative modules: 183226 VOLUME 13, 2025 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation FIGURE 2. Overview of the MGPRAG Framework. The framework includes: (a) Task Parsing Module, (b) Medical Entity Semantic Matching Module, and (c) Knowledge Retrieval and Generation Module. The figure illustrates the processing flow of a complex clinical question answering task. The DFCE (Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder) is applied to entity matching, while the MVD (Medical Value-Driven) is responsible for knowledge retrieval. task parsing, medical entity semantic matching, and knowl- edge retrieval and generation. These modules collectively enhance LLM performance on complex CQA tasks in the medical domain. Specifically, (1) The task parsing module, inspired by Chain-of-Thought (CoT) [29], decomposes each task into three structured components: core medical entities, sub-questions, and clinical manifestations. This approach not only enhances the LLM’s ability to identify key information within the task, but also ensures that the retrieval process cov- ers all essential information required for each sub-question. (2) The medical entity semantic matching module proposes the Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder (DFCE), which dynamically integrates pre-trained semantic representations with domain-specific medical features. This design enables precise semantic alignment between core entities in tasks and their counterparts in the medical knowledge graph. (3) The knowledge retrieval and generation module implements a Medical Value-Driven (MVD) retrieval algorithm (Fig. 1(c)), which combines a medical encoder with a disease relevance filtering strategy to select question-relevant knowledge for LLM generation. We evaluate MGPRAG through extensive experiments on multiple public medical question answering datasets. Results demonstrate that our framework outperforms mainstream medical document-based RAG and medical knowledge graph-based RAG methods in both the precision of knowl- edge retrieval and the accuracy of generated content. In addi- tion, to further assess the performance of the proposed DFCE, we carried out comparative experiments on public datasets and a self-constructed medical entity semantic matching dataset. The results show that DFCE achieves significant improvements in entity matching accuracy compared to existing encoder-based methods. II. RELATED WORK A. RETRIEVAL-AUGMENTED GENERATION Many powerful large language models(LLMs) specialized for medical tasks have emerged, such as ChatDoctor [30], BioGPT [31], and BioMedGPT [32]. Their enhanced performance largely stems from fine-tuning on medical question answering datasets and biomedical literature. How- ever, as model scales now reach hundreds of billions or even trillions of parameters, fine-tuning requires substantial computational resources and high-quality annotated data. This makes it difficult to sustain in real-world medical appli- cations. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) [12] has been increasingly adopted in the medical domain. It enables models to incorporate external knowledge sources to improve generation quality, without requiring further model training. Recent research has focused primarily on document-based RAG methods [15], [16], [33]. Yet, in complex clinical question answering scenarios, these methods often suffer from noisy or redundant retrieval results, which prevents RAG from achieving the desired augmentation effect. In this paper, we propose a novel graph-based RAG framework that integrates a Medical Value-Driven (MVD) retrieval algorithm to provide more relevant knowledge to support LLM generation. B. MEDICAL ENTITY SEMANTIC MATCHING Entity matching is a critical preprocessing step in knowledge graph-based RAG. It aligns core medical entities extracted VOLUME 13, 2025 183227 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation from input tasks with their corresponding nodes in a knowledge graph. Traditional approaches often rely on string- matching techniques, such as Levenshtein distance and Jac- card similarity [34], [35], or rule-based templates [36]. With the advancement of deep learning, semantic encoding-based methods have become dominant. For instance, Sentence- BERT [37] encodes entities into vectors [38], [39] and computes semantic similarity to improve matching perfor- mance. However, due to the semantic complexity of medical entities, the direct application of pretrained models to medical entity matching often results in suboptimal performance. Furthermore, the scarcity of publicly available datasets for medical entity matching tasks makes it challenging to effectively fine-tune pretrained models. Such attempts may lead to overfitting [40] or even catastrophic forgetting. In this paper, we propose a Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder (DFCE), which dynamically integrates general semantic representations with domain-specific medical knowledge. This design enhances the robustness and accuracy of medical entity semantic matching under limited supervision. III. METHODOLOGY Fig. 2 shows the overall architecture of MGPRAG, which consists of three core modules: the Task Parsing Module (Section III-A), the Medical Entity Semantic Matching Module (Section III-B), and the Knowledge Retrieval and Generation Module (Section III-C). A. TASK PARSING MODULE In the medical domain, complex clinical question answering (CQA) tasks often comprise multiple sub-questions. Without proper preprocessing, it becomes challenging to retrieve pre- cise knowledge for each sub-question, potentially leading to the omission of critical information. Additionally, redundant content within the task can hinder the comprehension and generation capabilities of large language models (LLMs). Therefore, MGPRAG introduces a Task Parsing Module (Fig. 2(a)) based on structured prompt engineering. Guided by predefined prompt templates (Appendix B), this module leverages the semantic understanding capabilities of LLMs to analyze the input task T and decompose it into three interrelated semantic units: • Core Medical Entity Set (E): Extracts essential medical entities (e.g., diseases, symptoms) mentioned in the task. These entities serve as the foundation for subsequent entity matching. • Sub-Questions (Q): Extracts sub-questions from the input task. For each sub-question, relevant knowledge is retrieved from the knowledge graph to ensure comprehensive coverage of medical information. • Clinical Manifestations (S): Captures the progression of the patient’s condition and filters out irrelevant information in the task to avoid interference with the final response. This provides clinical context that FIGURE 3. Architecture of the Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder. During training, the parameters of the Frozen Encoder remain fixed, while those of the Dynamic Encoder and the multi-head attention mechanism are updated. E1, E2, . . . , Em represent the entities used for training. assists LLMs in generating accurate and context-aware responses. B. MEDICAL ENTITY SEMANTIC MATCHING MODULE The core objective of the Medical Entity Semantic Matching Module (Fig. 2(b)) is to match the core medical entities extracted by the Task Parsing Module with corresponding entities in the medical knowledge graph. This is achieved using the Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder (DFCE) (Fig. 3), which generates a set of matched entities to guide knowledge retrieval. 1) THE SEMANTIC MATCHING PROCESS OF MEDICAL ENTITIES Let the set of core medical entities be denoted as: Ecore = {ecore 1 , ecore 2 , . . . , ecore n }, each entity eicore ∈Ecore is encoded using both a Frozen Encoder (FE) and a Dynamic Encoder (DE), yielding two vector representations: hcore,i FE and hcore,i DE . These vectors are then concatenated and fused using a Multi-Head Attention (MHA) mechanism, followed by Mean Pooling to generate the fused vector representation: hcore,i = Mean(MHA([hcore,i FE ; hcore,i DE ])) (1) Similarly, the entities in the medical knowledge graph are denoted as: Ekg = {e1kg, e2kg, . . . , evkg}, each entity ejkg ∈ Ekg is processed through the same encoding pipeline to obtain its corresponding fused vector representation: hkg,j = Mean(MHA([hkg,j FE ; hkg,j DE ])) (2) All encoded knowledge graph entities form the matrix: Hkg = [hkg,1; hkg,2, . . . ; hkg,v] (3) 183228 VOLUME 13, 2025 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation FIGURE 4. An Example Illustrating the Partial Knowledge Retrieval Process of MGPRAG. In this example, retrieval starts from the entity ‘‘osteoarthritis’’. After screening the set of adjacent relations using the medical encoder, two high-value relations are retained: ‘‘complication’’ and ‘‘symptom’’. The system then continues to perform the next-hop knowledge retrieval based on these relations. During the retrieval process, it is observed that the entity ‘‘joint pain’’ is linked to ‘‘UTI in the Elderly’’ through the ‘‘complication’’ relation. However, since the patient’s clinical manifestations are not associated with the symptoms of the disease ‘‘UTI in the Elderly’’, this entity is ultimately filtered out by the disease relevance strategy. Entity matching is performed by computing the cosine similarity between the fused representation hcore,i of each core medical entity and all entity embeddings in Hkg. Specifically, we compute: R = hcore,i · Hkg ∥hcore,i∥∥Hkg∥ (4) where R ∈RV is a similarity vector of length V, with each element R[j] representing the semantic similarity between ecore i and the j-th knowledge graph entity ekg j . To identify the most semantically aligned entity, we select the index with the highest similarity score: p∗= arg max p R[p], p ∈{1, 2, . . . , V} (5) Finally, the knowledge graph entity ekg p∗corresponding to the index p∗is selected as the best match for ecore i , completing the semantic matching process. 2) LOSS FUNCTION During the training of DFCE, the entity semantic matching process is formalized as a multi-class classification task, and the model is optimized using Cross-Entropy Loss. The training samples are drawn from the Medical Entity Semantic Matching (MESM) dataset (Appendix A). Let B denote the batch size and V the total number of entities in the medical knowledge graph. For each training instance, we denote the fused vector representation of a core medical entity eicore as hcore,i, and that of an entity ejkg from medical knowledge graph as hkg,j, The loss function is defined as: L = −1 B B X i=1 log exp �hcore,i · hkg,r  PV j=1 exp �hcore,i · hkg,j  ! (6) Here, r denotes the index of the knowledge graph entity that is semantically closest to the core medical entity ecore i . During training, the Frozen Encoder (FE) remains fixed to preserve general semantic representations obtained during pre-training. In contrast, the Dynamic Encoder (DE) is fine-tuned to capture domain-specific medical semantics. Additionally, the parameters of the Multi-Head Attention (MHA) mechanism are also updated, enabling it to dynamically combine these two types of representations. VOLUME 13, 2025 183229 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation TABLE 1. GPT-4o and DeepSeek-v3 rankings for all methods on the CMCQA dataset. C. KNOWLEDGE RETRIEVAL AND GENERATION MODULE The goal of the Knowledge Retrieval and Generation Module (Fig. 2(c)) is to retrieve question-relevant medical knowledge and provide it to the LLM for answer generation. To achieve this, MGPRAG incorporates a Medical Value-Driven (MVD) retrieval algorithm, which leverages a medical encoder and a disease relevance filtering strategy to retrieve relevant knowledge, as illustrated in Fig. 1(c). 1) THE KNOWLEDGE RETRIEVAL AND GENERATION PROCESS For each sub-question q, knowledge retrieval begins with the entity set produced by the Medical Entity Semantic Matching Module and proceeds through a depth-first search (DFS) over the medical knowledge graph. At each entity ekg encountered during traversal, all of its adjacent relations Rekg = {r1, r2, . . . , ro} are retrieved from the graph. Considering that not all adjacent relations are valuable for answering the given sub-question q, we design a relation pruning strategy based on a fine-tuned medical encoder. Specifically, the sub-question q and each adjacent relation ri ∈Rekg are encoded into vector representations vq and vri using the medical encoder. Their cosine similarity is then computed as: Sim(q, ri) = vq · vri ∥vq∥· ∥vri∥ (7) Relations with similarity scores below a predefined threshold θ are regarded as irrelevant or low-value and are subsequently filtered out. Only high-value relations are retained to guide the continuation of the DFS. It is worth emphasizing that in medical knowledge graphs, disease entities typically serve as the central nodes. This is because other medical entities, such as diagnostic techniques, medications, symptoms, and food, are largely organized through their associations with diseases. Moreover, it is common in medical knowledge graphs for a single medical entity to be associated with multiple diseases. For example, a symptom such as ‘‘headache’’ is associated with numerous conditions, including but not limited to the common cold, influenza, meningitis, and sinusitis. However, in clinical practice, a patient’s actual condition is usually closely related to only a subset of these diseases. Based on this observation, during the retrieval pro- cess we distinguish between candidate entities expanded through high-value relations: non-disease entities are directly retained, whereas disease entities undergo an additional filtering procedure, referred to as the disease relevance filtering strategy, in order to improve the clinical relevance TABLE 2. BERTScore Results on CMCQA with GPT-4o-mini as the backbone. of the retrieval results. The strategy is defined as follows: Let the set of disease entities expanded from the current node via retained relations be I = {S1, S2, . . . , Sk}, and let the set of core medical entities extracted by the Task Parsing Module be Ecore = {e1core, e2core, . . . , encore}, For each disease entity Sj ∈I, we retrieve its associated set of symptoms from the medical knowledge graph: USj = {u1, u2, . . . , um}. To identify disease entities that are clinically relevant, we define the following constraint: ∀ecore i ∈Ecore, ∃uc ∈USj, cos(ecore i , uc) ≥τ (8) where cos(·, ·) denotes semantic similarity, τ is a dynamically adjusted similarity threshold, and uc may depend on ecore i , meaning that different core entities can be matched to different symptoms within USj. A disease entity Sj is retained if it satisfies the constraint in Equation 8. This strategy ensures that each retained disease entity contains at least one symptom that is semantically similar to a core medical entity, thereby reflecting its clinical relevance. It should be noted that τ is initially set to a relatively high value, which may cause some potentially relevant diseases to be excluded. To address this, the system evaluates the retrieved knowledge after each round: if no suitable knowledge is found, it automatically proceeds to the next retrieval round and exponentially decays the threshold τ, thereby gradually broadening the coverage of potentially relevant entities. During DFS traversal, backtracking is triggered under either of the following conditions: (1) none of the current entity’s relations are relevant to the sub-question, indicating no further expansion is possible; or (2) all neighboring entities reachable via relevant relations have already been visited, implying that the current subgraph region has been fully explored. This design ensures thorough exploration of high-value knowledge subgraphs while avoiding unnecessary traversal. A detailed example of this retrieval process is illustrated in Fig. 4. 183230 VOLUME 13, 2025 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation TABLE 3. BERTScore Results on CMCQA with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the backbone. 2) LOSS FUNCTION Before training the medical encoder in the Medical Value- Driven(MVD) retrieval algorithm, we first classify the relations in the medical knowledge graph based on their value in answering each question in the training set. Specif- ically, relations that are valuable for answering the current question are treated as positive samples, while others are considered negative samples. The medical encoder separately encodes the question q, the positive relation r+, and the negative relation r−into vector representations vq, vr+, and vr−, respectively. It then computes the semantic similarity between the question and both the positive and negative relations. The model is optimized using the following loss function: L= X q  max(0, λ−cos(vq, vr+))+max(0, cos(vq, vr−)+λ)  (9) where λ is a margin threshold that controls the desired separation between the similarities of positive and nega- tive samples.Through this fine-tuning process, the medical encoder is able to effectively identify and prioritize the relations that are most valuable for answering the current question. IV. EXPERIMENTS A. DATASETS To evaluate the performance of the MGPRAG framework, we conduct experiments on two clinical question answering datasets: CMCQA [41], a benchmark dataset featuring complex clinical question answering, and GenMedGPT- 5k [30], a relatively simpler dataset that focuses on single-turn medical question answering. Additionally, to fur- ther assess the effectiveness of the Dynamic-Frozen Collab- orative Encoder (DFCE), we employ the publicly available datasets KUAKE-QIC [42] and CHIP-CTC [42], along with self-constructed Medical Entity Semantic Matching (MESM) dataset. Detailed dataset information is provided in Appendix A and Appendix C. B. EXPERIMENT SETTING We compare MGPRAG against six representative retrieval- augmented baselines: TOG [25], GraphRAG [43], SETR [44], BM25-based retriever [33], [45], Word2Vec-based Retriever [15], [46], and BERT-based Retriever [16], [47]. All retrieval-augmented strategies adopt GPT-4o-mini [1] and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct [48] as backbone models. In addition, we evaluate MGPRAG against the medical large language model HuatuoGPT-II (7B) [49] to assess the quality of generated content. For the entity matching task, DFCE is compared with both fine-tuned and pre-trained encoders. C. EVALUATION METRICS We adopt multiple evaluation metrics to comprehensively assess the performance of MGPRAG and other baselines. GPT-4o Ranking and DeepSeek-v3 Ranking are used to evaluate the practical value of the retrieved knowledge in answering questions. BERTScore [50] is used to measure the semantic similarity between generated responses and reference answers. In BERTScore, Precision indicates the accuracy of the generated content, Recall reflects how well the generated content covers the reference, and the F1 Score provides a balanced view of both aspects. Furthermore, the number of tokens is used to assess knowledge redundancy, especially when compared with knowledge graph-based methods such as TOG and GraphRAG. For the entity matching task, we use Match Accuracy [51] as the main metric, which quantifies how accurately the encoder matches entities. D. EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS 1) KNOWLEDGE RANKING AND BERTScore As shown in Table 1, On the CMCQA dataset, MGPRAG achieves a ranking score of 1.6264 with GPT-4o and 1.4456 with DeepSeek-v3, outperforming all baselines and demonstrating its effectiveness in precise knowledge retrieval. As shown in Table 2 and Table 3, when using GPT-4o-mini and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as backbone models, MGPRAG attains the highest BERTScore metrics. These results highlight the value of the retrieved knowledge in enhancing the response quality of large language models for complex clinical question-answering tasks. We also conduct experiments on the GenMedGPT-5k dataset. This dataset is selected to further evaluate the effectiveness of the MGPRAG retrieval algorithm in simpler clinical question answering (CQA) scenarios. As shown in Table 4, MGPRAG achieves an average GPT-4o ranking of 1.8392 and an average DeepSeek-v3 ranking of 1.9501, exceeding all baselines. Moreover, as presented in Table 5 and Table 6, MGPRAG maintains a leading position across most BERTScore metrics, indicating the robustness of MGPRAG in diverse CQA tasks. In comparison with HuatuoGPT-II (7B), our MGPRAG framework is built upon Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. We further report the performance of the vanilla Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct without retrieval augmentation, providing a more transparent comparison between retrieval-augmented generation and domain-specific model fine-tuning. VOLUME 13, 2025 183231 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation TABLE 4. GPT-4o and DeepSeek-v3 Rankings for all methods on the GenMedGPT-5k Dataset. TABLE 5. BERTScore Results on GenMedGPT-5k with GPT-4o-mini as the backbone. TABLE 6. BERTScore Results on GenMedGPT-5k with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the backbone. TABLE 7. BERTScore Comparison of MGPRAG, HuatuoGPT-II (7B), and Vanilla Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct on CMCQA and GenMedGPT-5k datasets. The results in Table 7 show that MGPRAG outperforms HuatuoGPT-II (7B) on both the CMCQA and GenMedGPT- 5k datasets. Moreover, in the absence of retrieval augmen- tation, the vanilla Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct produces responses of lower quality compared to HuatuoGPT-II (7B), indicating that fine-tuning on domain-specific medical data effectively enhances a model’s clinical understanding and generation capabilities. These findings highlight two key points: first, specialized training on medical corpora remains a powerful approach for improving LLM performance in healthcare; second, MGPRAG can substantially boost the performance of large language models (LLMs) in clinical question answering without any parameter updates. By leveraging external knowledge through retrieval from a medical corpus, MGPRAG achieves superior results while avoiding the need FIGURE 5. Average number of tokens retrieved per task by MGPRAG, TOG, and GraphRAG on the CMCQA and GenMedGPT-5k datasets. TABLE 8. Ablation Study of MGPRAG: Effects of Removing DFCE, Task Parsing, and Dynamic τ Update on the CMCQA Dataset. for costly data collection and model retraining, offering a more efficient and scalable solution for medical AI applications. 2) KNOWLEDGE REDUNDANCY ANALYSIS We calculated the average number of tokens retrieved by MGPRAG, TOG, and GraphRAG on the CMCQA and GenMedGPT-5k datasets. As shown in Fig. 5, on the CMCQA dataset, the number of tokens retrieved by MGPRAG amounts to only 48% of that retrieved by TOG and 96.4% of that retrieved by GraphRAG. On the GenMedGPT- 5k dataset, this ratio further decreases to just 19.1% of TOG and 34.4% of GraphRAG. When combined with the knowledge value rankings reported in Table1 and Table 4, these results demonstrate that MGPRAG retrieves more valuable and compact knowledge than TOG and GraphRAG, thereby effectively reducing content redundancy. 3) ENTITY MATCH ACCURACY As shown in Fig. 6, on the MESM dataset, the proposed Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder (DFCE) achieves the highest entity matching accuracy, surpassing the Fine-tuned 183232 VOLUME 13, 2025 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation FIGURE 6. Entity Matching Accuracy of DFCE: Comparison with Fine-tuned and Pre-trained Encoders on MESM, CHIP-CTC, and KUAKE-QIC Datasets. FIGURE 7. Ablation Study of DFCE: Effects of Removing FE and MHA Components. Encoder by 18.5% and the Pre-trained Encoder by 34%. Similarly, on both the CHIP-CTC and KUAKE-QIC datasets, DFCE consistently outperforms the other encoders. These results confirm that DFCE effectively integrates gen- eral semantic representations with domain-specific medical knowledge through the multi-head attention mechanism, thereby enhancing entity matching accuracy. 4) ABLATION STUDY To evaluate the contributions of individual components within the MGPRAG framework, we conduct an ablation study on the CMCQA dataset, a benchmark for complex clin- ical question answering. As shown in Table 8, we compare the full MGPRAG framework against three ablated variants: • w/o Task Parsing Module: only core medical entities are extracted, and knowledge retrieval is performed directly based on these entities without sub-question decomposition or clinical manifestation extraction; • w/o DFCE: the Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder is replaced with pre-trained encoders for entity match- ing, without fine-tuning; • w/o Dynamic τ Update: the similarity threshold τ is fixed throughout all retrieval rounds and not adaptively adjusted even when no relevant knowledge is retrieved. All ablation experiments are conducted using GPT-4o-mini as the backbone for all methods. The results demonstrate that each component plays an essential role in enhancing answer quality. Notably, the Task Parsing module has the largest impact in the ablation study, underscoring that proper pre- processing of the original task is a critical step for improving the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented generation. To further analyze the contribution of each component within DFCE, we compare the full model with two variants: one that removes the Frozen Encoder (FE), and another that excludes the Multi-Head Attention (MHA) mechanism. The results are shown in Fig. 7. From the analysis, we observe the following: (1) Compared to the variant without MHA, incorporating the attention mechanism significantly improves entity matching accu- racy. This is because MHA effectively fuses the general semantic features learned by the frozen encoder with the domain-specific medical knowledge captured by the dynamic encoder. (2) Compared to the variant without FE, the general semantic features from FE help enhance entity matching performance. However, on datasets such as CHIP-CTC and KUAKE-QIC, where entity distinctions are relatively minor, the contribution of FE is less pronounced. In these cases, the model can more easily learn semantic associations. VOLUME 13, 2025 183233 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation FIGURE 8. Representative Case. This case involves a breastfeeding woman who described her clinical symptoms. Through task parsing, medical entity semantic matching, and knowledge graph retrieval, MGPRAG successfully identified medical knowledge highly relevant to the symptoms and provided supporting information to the large language model. This process significantly improved the accuracy of the model’s diagnostic judgment, demonstrating the practicality and reliability of MGPRAG in clinical scenarios. V. DISCUSSION This section discusses the results and implications of MGPRAG in the context of clinical question answering. We begin by analyzing computational overhead and scalabil- ity to assess the model’s practicality for real-world clinical deployment. Next, we examine the key threshold parameters in MGPRAG and their impact on retrieval performance. Additional case studies on the CMCQA dataset are presented to further validate the framework’s effectiveness. We then identify challenging scenarios that reveal current limitations of the model. The reliability of MGPRAG in the medical domain is also discussed, followed by a critical analysis of its limitations and a set of promising directions for future work. A. COMPUTATIONAL OVERHEAD AND SCALABILITY Although MGPRAG demonstrates strong performance, the Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder (DFCE) and multi-hop retrieval process inevitably introduce substantial computational overhead, which could become a performance bottleneck in both large-scale deployments and real-time clinical applications where low latency and high throughput are critical. To assess its efficiency, we conducted an evaluation on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU with 24GB of VRAM, focusing only on the knowledge retrieval oper- ation. Using Neo4j for storage, querying, and management of the medical knowledge graph, we tested 942 patient questions, and the average retrieval time per query was approximately 13.7 seconds. Moreover, when employing the open-source Qwen-7B-Instruct as the generation model without fine-tuning, it ran smoothly on the same device. These results indicate that MGPRAG has acceptable compu- tational overhead in small-scale experimental environments. However, to meet the clinical demand for real-time response and to ensure scalability under large-scale deployment, further optimization is necessary. In future work, we plan to improve efficiency along two directions: (1) Model lightweighting: We will explore knowledge distillation methods to compress the dynamic encoder into a smaller and faster student model, significantly reducing inference cost while maintaining performance. (2) Vector caching mechanism: We will pre-compute and cache representations of frequent core medical entities and knowledge graph nodes. During subsequent retrieval rounds, cached vector representations are reused whenever available, and only entities not yet cached are encoded in real time. This strategy can effectively reduce redundant computation and improve inference efficiency. These improvements aim to enhance both the response speed and scalability of MGPRAG, paving the way for its practical deployment in real-world clinical scenarios. B. KEY THRESHOLD PARAMETERS IN MGPRAG 1) THRESHOLD FOR VALUE-RELATION SELECTION To identify relations in the knowledge graph that are valuable for answering a given question, we fine-tuned a medical encoder using the loss function defined in Equation (9). In our experiments, we set the hyperparameter λ = 1 in Equation (9). After fine-tuning, we observed that for positive 183234 VOLUME 13, 2025 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation relations that are useful in answering the question, the semantic similarity: cos(q, r+) = vq · vr+ ∥vq∥· ∥vr+∥ (10) is generally close to 1. In contrast, for negative relations that contribute little to the answer, the similarity: cos(q, r−) = vq · vr− ∥vq∥· ∥vr−∥ (11) tends to approach −1. Here, vq denotes the vector represen- tation of the question encoded by the medical encoder, vr+ represents the vector of a positive relation, and vr−represents the vector of a negative relation. These results demonstrate that the fine-tuned medical encoder can effectively discriminate between positive and negative relations, thereby providing a solid foundation for setting an appropriate threshold θ. Moreover,We evaluated θ at values of 0.8, 0.85, and 0.9, and found that the model consistently and accurately selected relations that were valuable for answering the question across this range. This indicates that θ can be flexibly set within a high-similarity range, thereby enhancing the robustness of the model in practical applications. 2) THRESHOLD FOR DISEASE-RELEVANCE FILTERING To ensure that the retrieved diseases are highly relevant to patient symptoms, we adopted a multi-round dynamic thresholding approach. At the beginning, the threshold τ is set high to guarantee precision. If no relevant knowledge is retrieved in the current round, the threshold is gradually relaxed in subsequent rounds to improve recall, with the relaxation rate decreasing progressively to avoid introducing excessive irrelevant information. The update rule is as follows: τe = max τ0 · e Y i=1  1 −1 −γ0 i  , τmin ! (12) Here, τ0 denotes the initial threshold, which is set relatively high to ensure strong precision during the early retrieval stages; τmin represents the lower bound of the threshold, preventing it from decreasing indefinitely and avoiding the introduction of low-quality knowledge. If τ falls below τmin, the retrieval process is terminated even when no knowledge is retrieved. γ0 is the initial decay factor, which controls the rate of decline in the first round, and e denotes the current retrieval round. This design balances precision and recall by following the principle of ‘‘maintaining a high threshold in the early stages for accuracy, while gradually relaxing it in later stages for broader coverage.’’ Sensitivity analysis of the threshold: If τ is initialized too high, some diseases that are clinically relevant to the patient may fail to be retrieved; if τ is set too low initially, it may lead to the inclusion of diseases with weak relevance. Therefore, properly setting the initial value of τ is crucial for retrieval performance. C. ADDITIONAL CASE ON MGPRAG To better reflect the broader challenges of Western, evidence- based clinical settings and enhance the representativeness and generalizability of our study, we incorporated additional test case, as illustrated in Fig. 8. This case is designed in the style of electronic health records (EHRs) and employ standardized terminologies from mainstream medical ontologies such as SNOMED CT and UMLS. The case involves a 29- year-old breastfeeding woman presenting with significant pain in the right breast and poor milk ejection, symptoms highly suggestive of acute mastitis. Through MGPRAG’s retrieval process, the system successfully identified relevant knowledge fragments linking ‘‘breast pain’’ and ‘‘reduced milk secretion’’ to ‘‘acute mastitis,’’ along with associated diagnostic examinations (e.g., blood test, breast exam, biopsy, drug sensitivity test) and treatment options (e.g., cephalexin capsules). Leveraging this retrieved knowledge, the LLM generated an answer that accurately reflected the clinical reasoning process: recognizing acute mastitis as the likely diagnosis and recommending both antibiotic treatment and further diagnostic evaluations. This case illustrates that MGPRAG can effectively handle realistic clinical scenarios expressed in EHR style, demonstrating its ability to support accurate clinical decision-making and adapt to internationally recognized healthcare information standards. D. CHALLENGING SCENARIOS FOR MGPRAG In extensive clinical question-answering tasks, we observed that although MGPRAG performs well in most common disease scenarios, it still demonstrates notable performance degradation in certain specific contexts. To further illustrate these weaknesses, we analyze representative scenarios where MGPRAG underperforms and provide concrete failure cases, as detailed below. Scenario 1. Vague or non-standardized patient expressions with insufficiently specific symptom descriptions: Some patient queries are vague, use inaccurate medical terminol- ogy, or include colloquial expressions. Although we have incorporated the DFCE encoder into the entity matching module to enhance semantic alignment between core medical entities in clinical questions and entities in the medical knowledge graph, the system may still retrieve weakly relevant knowledge fragments with limited clinical utility in cases of high semantic ambiguity or missing contextual information. Failure case: ‘‘I feel uncomfortable in my stomach every time after eating. What is the reason?’’ This query is highly ambiguous: the expression ‘‘stomach discomfort’’ may refer to a wide range of symptoms, such as bloating, dull pain, nausea, and acid reflux, and may involve multiple organ systems, including the stomach, intestines, gallbladder, and pancreas. During entity matching, ‘‘stomach discomfort’’ was mapped to the symptom entity ‘‘stomach pain’’ in the knowledge graph. However, this symptom is associated VOLUME 13, 2025 183235 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation with a broad spectrum of potential diseases in the medical knowledge graph, including chronic gastritis, peptic ulcer, gastric cancer, pancreatitis, irritable bowel syndrome, and cholecystitis. As a result, the retrieval module returned a large number of broadly relevant but poorly targeted knowledge fragments, leading to generated answers that were overly generic and lacked precision, thereby failing to provide specific or actionable clinical recommendations. Scenario 2. Rare diseases or atypical clinical cases: When patient questions involve atypical clinical manifestations or rare diseases, current medical knowledge graphs (e.g., CM3KG, EMKG) have limited coverage of relevant entities, symptom descriptions, and associations. As a result, the retrieval module may fail to recall critical supporting knowledge. Even when the query is semantically clear and entities are identifiable, the model often fails to generate effective answers due to ‘‘knowledge absence.’’ This underscores the fundamental impact of external knowledge coverage breadth on the performance of retrieval-augmented generation approaches. Failure case: ‘‘My child has corneal K-F rings, could this be a genetic metabolic disease?’’ The corneal K-F ring is a typical clinical sign of Wilson’s disease and has high diagnostic value. However, in the medical knowledge graphs used (CM3KG, EMKG), ‘‘corneal K-F ring’’ is not recorded as an independent entity, nor is its association with Wilson’s disease explicitly represented. Consequently, despite the clear clinical direction of the query, the system failed to retrieve the relevant knowledge. To mitigate these challenges, we first plan to expand the coverage of the medical knowledge graph to include more rare diseases and their characteristic clinical findings, and second to further enhance the DFCE encoder’s capac- ity to disambiguate ambiguous and multi-meaning patient expressions. These improvements are expected to increase MGPRAG’s practicality, accuracy, and reliability in complex, real-world medical scenarios. E. RELIABILITY IN THE MEDICAL DOMAIN With the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence, par- ticularly large language models (LLMs), their applications in healthcare have expanded considerably. However, the correctness of their generated outputs remains a subject of debate. To address this, researchers have proposed a variety of quantitative metrics, such as Precision, Recall, and F1 Score, which are also employed in this paper. Yet, Kim et al. [52] emphasized that, in order to gain user trust, medical AI systems must achieve sufficiently high levels of performance (e.g., accuracy), though the exact benchmarks remain unspec- ified. Similarly, Abbasian et al. [53] argued that existing evaluation metrics for general-purpose LLMs fail to capture essential medical concepts (e.g., symptoms, diagnostic tests, diagnoses, and treatments) and their interrelationships, and do not define the quantitative thresholds required to ensure the reliability of model outputs. Furthermore, Zhou et al. [54] highlighted that many automated metrics fall short in assessing diagnostic interpretability and clinical validity. They also noted that human evaluation by medical experts can provide more nuanced and comprehensive assessments, but this approach is costly, time-consuming, and subject to subjectivity, making it impractical for large-scale use. Given the life-critical nature of medical applications, we argue that establishing a unified, comprehensive, and widely accepted evaluation framework is essential. Such a framework should incorporate multidimensional metrics, including accuracy, factuality, interpretability, and safety, and be refined through extensive clinical validation. Only when model performance consistently meets these benchmark standards can the reliability of generated content be assured, thereby enabling the safe and effective deployment of artificial intelligence in the medical domain. F. LIMITATIONS Despite MGPRAG’s strong experimental performance, it still has several limitations. First, the Medical Entity Semantic Matching Dataset (MESM), which underpins the training of the Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder (DFCE), faces challenges in terms of generalizability. Its coverage of medical entities is limited: MESM was primarily constructed from the medical QA dataset CMCQA, which means that certain medical entities in other datasets, particularly those related to rare diseases, may not be adequately represented. This limitation could restrict its generalizability and consequently affect DFCE’s performance. In addition, the accuracy of annotations is constrained: since most annotations were carried out by our research team, the outcomes may be subject to biases or deviations due to limitations in the annotators’ medical knowledge. Second, the process of MESM dataset creation is resource-intensive and could introduce biases. The high resource consumption primarily stems from our reliance on the LLM-based pre-screening stage. Since entities from the medical knowledge graph are repeatedly input into the LLM for preliminary filtering, the token cost increases significantly when the number of entities is large. Bias, on the other hand, may be introduced both during the LLM pre-screening phase and in the manual review process. At the pre-screening phase, the LLM’s semantic understanding and knowledge coverage are inherently imbalanced: the model tends to capture common medical entities more effectively but struggles with rare diseases or emerging medical terms, which biases the candidate pool toward more frequent or familiar entities. At the manual review phase, the final labels depend heavily on the annotators’ medical background, expertise, and choice of reference materials. As a result, different annotators may arrive at different conclusions for the same entity, thereby introducing annotation bias. Third, the completeness of external knowledge sources also constrains MGPRAG’s performance. Since retrieval-augmented generation depends on external 183236 VOLUME 13, 2025 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation FIGURE 9. Construction Pipeline of the MESM Dataset. The figure illustrates the construction process of the MESM dataset, which consists of two main stages. In the first stage, a LLM performs preliminary screening of entity subsets from the medical knowledge graph to generate candidate entity sets. In the second stage, human annotators review these candidates with reference to authoritative medical resources and select the entity that best matches the core medical entity in semantic alignment as the final label. knowledge to enhance LLM outputs, MGPRAG relies on medical knowledge graphs such as CM3KG and EMKG. The quality and coverage of these graphs directly affect retrieval effectiveness. During our experiments, we clearly observed that when the core medical entities in patient queries are absent from the knowledge graph, such as rare or niche disease entities, the system fails to retrieve relevant knowledge, thereby impairing the model’s ability to generate accurate responses. Finally, our current evaluation approach poses its own limitations. The experiments primarily rely on automated metrics (e.g., BERTScore and ranking scores) to evaluate the precision of retrieved knowledge and the quality of generated responses. While these metrics provide objectivity and reproducibility in assessing semantic consistency, content completeness, and retrieval accuracy, they cannot fully cap- ture whether the outputs align with clinical decision-making processes or effectively support healthcare professionals. The absence of direct feedback from frontline medical practitioners limits our ability to assess MGPRAG’s true clinical utility, thereby reducing confidence in its practical reliability. G. FUTURE WORK The future development of MGPRAG will aim to address its current limitations through a series of targeted measures. First, to mitigate concerns about the generalizability of MESM, we plan to improve the dataset along three key directions. (1) Expanding coverage: future iterations will progressively enrich the number and types of medical entities in MESM, including but not limited to diseases, symptoms, treatment techniques, drugs, and food-related entities. (2) Incorporating multi-source data: we will integrate medical TABLE 9. Entity matching dataset. entities from additional medical QA datasets to enhance the comprehensiveness of knowledge coverage. (3) Community openness and validation: the dataset will be gradually released to the research community, enabling broader par- ticipation in validation, expansion, and refinement. This collaborative approach can help reduce potential biases and inconsistencies caused by reliance on a single annotation team, thereby improving the generalizability and reliability of MESM. Second, to alleviate the resource-intensive nature of dataset construction, we plan to explore more efficient and robust workflows. In particular, rather than uniformly dividing entities into batches, we will introduce a lightweight pre-screening module (e.g., BERT-based filtering) prior to the LLM step, thereby reducing the input size and lowering token consumption. To address biases arising from the LLM pre-screening phase, we intend to adopt a multi-model screening strategy that incorporates different medical LLMs trained on diverse corpora. This approach may balance out model-specific biases, although we acknowledge that it could introduce additional resource costs. For annotation-related biases, we will involve multiple annotators working inde- pendently, apply secondary reviews for high-disagreement cases, and cross-check results against authoritative medical knowledge bases (e.g., UMLS, SNOMED CT, ICD-10). VOLUME 13, 2025 183237 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation FIGURE 10. Prompt template used in the Task Parsing Module to guide large language models in decomposing complex clinical question answering tasks into three interrelated semantic units. In parallel, releasing MESM to the broader community will enable collective validation and refinement, further enhancing both its robustness and generalizability. Third, to overcome the limitations of incomplete knowl- edge graphs, we plan to develop a hybrid retrieval framework that adopts a ‘‘graph-first, text-supplementary’’ strategy. The system will prioritize structured knowledge graph retrieval; if no relevant knowledge is found, it will supplement with unstructured medical text resources (e.g., PubMed articles, electronic medical records). In this process, core medical entities in patient queries will be semantically TABLE 10. Statistics of the medical knowledge graph. matched with domain-specific terms in the medical text, and the top-k most semantically relevant entries will be selected. The corresponding paragraphs or text segments will then be extracted as external knowledge. Compared with knowledge graphs, medical texts offer broader coverage and timeliness, reflecting the latest research findings or rare case reports. By combining the precision and organization of structured knowledge graphs with the richness and dynamic nature of medical texts, MGPRAG can become more robust and adaptable in handling niche or rare medical scenarios. Finally, to achieve more comprehensive evaluation, we plan to incorporate expert human assessments alongside automated metrics. Clinicians from multiple specialties and medically trained researchers (e.g., graduate students with strong clinical knowledge) will be engaged to evaluate MGPRAG’s outputs on dimensions such as correctness, inter- pretability, and clinical usability. This human-in-the-loop evaluation will complement automated metrics, providing a more nuanced and reliable assessment of MGPRAG’s clinical utility. In addition, we aim to systematically expand evaluation across a wider set of public clinical QA datasets covering diverse medical knowledge graphs, thereby val- idating MGPRAG’s adaptability and generalization across varied scenarios. Beyond evaluation, we also plan to support the construction of larger-scale, high-quality open-source medical QA datasets paired with knowledge graphs, pro- viding foundational resources to advance research in this field. VI. CONCLUSION This paper presents MGPRAG, a novel framework for precise retrieval-augmented generation based on medical knowledge graphs. The framework consists of three core modules: the Task Parsing Module, the Medical Entity Semantic Matching Module, and the Knowledge Retrieval and Gen- eration Module. The Task Parsing Module extracts essential information from complex clinical questions, ensuring that retrieved knowledge is both comprehensive and minimally affected by irrelevant details. The Medical Entity Semantic Matching Module leverages a Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder to strengthen semantic alignment between core medical entities in the task and entities in the knowledge graph. The Knowledge Retrieval and Generation Module incorporates a Medical Value-Driven retrieval algorithm to enhance knowledge relevance and reduce redundancy. Evaluation on clinical question answering benchmarks shows that MGPRAG achieves substantial improvements over existing approaches in retrieval precision and answer 183238 VOLUME 13, 2025 Y. Shen et al.: MGPRAG: Enhancing Medical LLMs via Precision Retrieval-Augmented Generation generation quality. Its outstanding performance provides a more practical and scalable approach for integrating large language models into healthcare applications. APPENDIX A MEDICAL ENTITY SEMANTIC MATCHING DATASET (MESM) To support the training of the Dynamic-Frozen Collaborative Encoder, we constructed the MESM dataset. It comprises a large collection of core medical entities extracted from complex clinical question answering tasks, each paired with its corresponding entity in the medical knowledge graph. The construction process proceeds as follows: For each core medical entity to be labeled, we adopt a two-stage annotation pipeline consisting of large language model (LLM) pre- screening and human review. Let the total number of entities in the medical knowledge graph be V. To address the context length constraints in LLMs, the entity set is divided into K batched subsets. Within each batch, the LLM ranks entities by semantic relevance and selects the most related ones to the core medical entity, thereby generating multiple candidate matching sets. Subsequently, human annotators manually review these candidates by consulting authoritative medical resources and select the entity that best matches in semantic alignment as the final label. This annotation workflow is also more clearly visualized in Fig. 9. Table 9 summarizes the dataset partitions used for evaluating DFCE performance. APPENDIX B TASK PARSING PROMPT TEMPLATE See Figure 10. APPENDIX C MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE GRAPHS AND DOCUMENT CORPORA To enable retrieval-augmented generation, we utilized the following medical knowledge resources: (1) CM3KG — an open-source multimodal medical knowledge graph [41]; (2) EMKG — constructed using the disease database provided by ChatDoctor [30]. To compare with document-based retrieval methods, we constructed 8,808 medical knowledge documents from CM3KG. Similarly, we built 796 documents from EMKG. Each document provides comprehensive med- ical information about a specific disease. For the test QA data, we extracted 1,001 complex clinical question answering tasks from the CMCQA dataset and 942 clinical question answering tasks from the GenMedGPT-5k dataset. Table 10 presents a statistical overview of the knowledge sources used in the experiments.

title: TSINGHUA SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY
file_name: NICT-ATR Chinese-Japanese-English speech-to-speech translation system.pdf
text: TSINGHUA SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY ISSN1007-021418/22pp540-544 Volume 13, Number 4, August 2008 NICT/ATR Chinese-Japanese-English Speech-to-Speech Translation System Tohru Shimizu**, Yutaka Ashikari, Eiichiro Sumita, ZHANG Jinsong (჆࠱ഞ), Satoshi Nakamura Knowledge Creating Communication Research Center, National Institute of Information and Communications Technology; ATR Spoken Language Translation Research Laboratories, 2-2-2 Keihanna Science City, Kyoto 619-0288, Japan Abstract: This paper describes the latest version of the Chinese-Japanese-English handheld speech-to- speech translation system developed by NICT/ATR, which is now ready to be deployed for travelers. With the entire speech-to-speech translation function being implemented into one terminal, it realizes real-time, location-free speech-to-speech translation. A new noise-suppression technique notably improves the speech recognition performance. Corpus-based approaches of speech recognition, machine translation, and speech synthesis enable coverage of a wide variety of topics and portability to other languages. Test results show that the character accuracy of speech recognition is 82%-94% for Chinese speech, with a bilingual evaluation understudy score of machine translation is 0.55-0.74 for Chinese-Japanese and Chinese-English. Key words: speech-to-speech translation; speech recognition; speech synthesis; machine translation; large-scale corpus Introduction Language differences have been a serious barrier for globalization, international travel, and international business. Breaking the language barrier will bring lar- ger markets for international business and international tourism, with mobile speech translators contributing to improve communication ability. Our goal is to realize automatic speech-to-speech translation for many lan- guage pairs. Speech recognition, speech synthesis, and machine translation research started about 50 years ago. They have developed independently for a long time until speech-to-speech translation research was pro- posed in the 1980’s. The feasibility of speech-to- speech translation was the focus of research at the be- ginning because each component was difficult to build and integration seemed more difficult. Recentprogressincorpus-basedspeechandlanguage processing technologies has made it possible to realize multi-lingual speech-to-speech translation in real situa- tions. The advantages of the corpus-based approach are that it achieves wide coverage, robustness, and port- ability to new languages and domains[1]. This paper de- scribes recent progress of the NICT/ATR speech-to- speech translation system. 1 Speech-to-Speech Translation System 1.1 Automatic speech recognition (ASR) Robust speech recognition in noisy environments is an important issue for speech-to-speech translation in the real world. Both a minimum mean square error (MMSE) estimator for the log Mel-spectral energy co- efficients using a Gaussian mixture model (GMM)[2,3] and a particle filter[4] are introduced here to suppress interference and noise and to attenuate reverberation. To obtain a compact and accurate model from cor- pora with limited size, the minimum description length Received: 2007-09-10; revised: 2008-03-18 ** To whom correspondence should be addressed. E-mail: tohru.shimizu@nict.go.jp; Tel: 81-774-951301 Tohru Shimizu et alġNICT/ATR Chinese-Japanese-English Speech-to-Speech … 541 successive state splitting (MDL-SSS)[5] algorithm is used for acoustic modeling, with the composite multi- class N-gram models[6] used for acoustic and language modeling. MDL-SSS algorithm automatically deter- mines the appropriate number of parameters according to the size of the training data based on the maximum description length (MDL) criterion. Japanese, English, and Chinese acoustic models were trained using data from 4200 Japanese speakers, 532 English speakers, and 1600 Chinese speakers. The models were also adapted to several accents, e.g., US (United States), AUS (Australia), and BRT (Britain) for English, and Putonghua, BJ (Beijing), SH (Shanghai), CT (Canton), and TW (Taiwan) for Chinese. A statistical language model was trained using a large-scale corpus (852×103 Japanese sentences, 710×103 English sentences, and 500×103 Chinese sentences) drawn from travel related topics. The model quickly adapts to different tasks and situations by separating the lexicon of location-specific proper nouns (e.g. place names, hotels, restaurants) from the lexicon of common nouns. Even when the acoustic and language models are well-trained, environmental conditions such as the variability of speakers, mismatches between training and testing channels, and interference from environ- mental noise may cause recognition errors. A general- ized word posterior probability (GWPP) is used for the post processing of the speech recognition to automati- cally reject erroneous (low confidence) utterances[7-9]. 1.2 Machine translation (MT) Two translation modules, TATR (a phrase-based SMT module) and EM (a simple memory-based translation module) were automatically constructed from large- scale corpora. The EM matches a given source sen- tence against the source language parts of translation examples. If an EM is achieved, the corresponding tar- get language sentence will be output. Otherwise, TATR is called. TATR, which is built within the framework of feature-based exponential models, uses a phrase translation probability from source to target, an inverse phrase translation probability, a lexical weight- ing probability from source to target, an inverse lexical weighting probability, and a phrase penalty. A three-step subword-based approach is used for word segmentation of Chinese[10]. The first step is a dictionary-based step, similar to the word segmentation provided by the linguistic data consortium (LDC). The second step is a subword-based IOB (I: inside, O: out- side, B: beginning) tagging step implemented by a conditional random fields (CRF) tagging model. The subword-based IOB tagging achieves better segmenta- tion than character-based IOB tagging. The third step is confidence-dependent disambiguation that combines the previous two results. The subword-based segmen- tation was evaluated with two data sets from the Sighan Bakeoff and the NIST machine translation evaluation workshop. Our segmentation gave a higher F-score than the best published results for the second Sighan Bakeoff data sets[11]. This segmentation method was also evaluated in a translation scenario us- ing the NIST translation evaluation 2005 data[12], where its billingual evaluation understudy (BLEU) score was 1.1% higher than that using the LDC word segmentation. The language model plays an important role in the statistical machine translation (SMT). The effective- ness of the language model is significant if the test data happens to have the same characteristics as the training data for the language models. However, this coinci- dence is rare in practice. To avoid this performance re- duction, a topic adaptation technique is often used. For this purpose, a “topic” is defined as clusters of bilin- gual sentence pairs[13]. The topic-dependent language models were tested using IWSLT06 data. Our ap- proach improved the BLEU score between 1.1% and 1.4%[14]. 1.3 Speech synthesis (SS) A speech synthesis engine, XIMERA, was developed using four large corpora (a 110-h corpus of Japanese male, a 60-h corpus of Japanese female, a 16-h corpus of English male, and a 20-h corpus of Chinese female). This corpus-based approach makes it possible to pre- serve the naturalness and personality of the speech without introducing signal processing to the speech segment[15]. XIMERA’s hidden Markov model (HMM)-based statistical prosody model is automati- cally trained, so it can generate a highly natural F0 pat- tern[16]. In addition, the cost function for segment se- lection has been optimized based on perceptual ex- periments, thereby improving the naturalness of the se- lected segments[17]. Tsinghua Science and Technology, August 2008, 13(4): 540-544 542 1.4 Speech-to-speech translation terminal The speech-to-speech translation system is designed for use with mobile terminals such as shown in Fig. 1. Speech-to-speech translation can be performed for any combination of Japanese, English, and Chinese lan- guages. The device is 150 mm (width)×32 mm (thick- ness)×95 mm (height). A uni-directional microphone is used for speech recognition in noisy environments. Fig. 1 Handheld Chinese, Japanese, and English speech- to-speech translation system The display has essentially two windows. The first window is for the speech recognition results, while the other window is for the translation results. System messages are shown in each window. 1.5 Connections between internal and external speech-to-speech translation resources The device can be connected to internal and external speech-to-speech translation resources (e.g. speech recognition, machine translation, speech synthesis servers of other language/language pairs), through a machine translation markup language (STML) and to web services using STML as shown in Fig. 2. 2 Evaluation of the Speech-to-Speech Translation System 2.1 Speech and language corpora The system was evaluated against three kinds of speech and language corpora, the basic travel expres- sion corpus (BTEC), machine aided dialog (MAD), and field experiment data (FED)[18-21]. The test set Notes: J is for Japanese, E is for English, and C is for Chinese. Fig. 2 Connections between internal and external speech-to-speech translation resources using STML includes different speaking styles, read speech, semi- spontaneous speech, and fully natural spontaneous speech. BTEC, in particular, includes parallel sen- tences in two languages that are commonly used in in- ternational travel. MAD and FED are dialogues using speech-to-speech translation. MAD was collected in an office and FED was collected at the Kansai Interna- tional Airport, spoken by actual travelers in the airport. 2.2 Speech recognition module The speech recognition performances with the test sets are given in Tables 1 and 2 for real time factors of 1 and 5 with a 2.8-GHz CPU, where the real time factor is a time ratio of the analysis time to the length of the utterance. The vocabulary size was about 35h103 words in canonical form and 50h103 words with pronunciation variations. Although the speech recognition perform- ance for dialog speech is worse than for read speech, the utterance correctness excluding erroneous recogni- tion output using the GWPP[7,9] was greater than 83% in all cases. 2.3 Machine translation module An evaluation of the system accuracy for sixteen refer- ence translations is shown in Table 3. The accuracies of the translation outputs ranked A (perfect), B (good), C (fair), or D (nonsense) by pro- fessional translators are shown in Table 4. The results agree with the BLEU scores in Table 3. Table 3 Objective evaluation of machine translation module Language pair BLEU Japanese-to-English 0.6998 English-to-Japanese 0.7496 Japanese-to-Chinese 0.6584 Chinese-to-Japanese 0.7400 English-to-Chinese 0.5520 Chinese-to-English 0.6581 Table 4 Human evaluations of the translation accuracy Translation accuracy (%) Language pair A A+B A+B+C D Japanese-to-English 78.4 86.3 92.2 7.8 English-to-Japanese 74.3 85.7 93.9 6.1 Japanese-to-Chinese 68.0 78.0 88.8 11.2 Chinese-to-Japanese 68.6 80.4 89.0 11.0 English-to-Chinese 52.5 67.1 79.4 20.6 Chinese-to-English 68.0 77.3 86.3 13.7 3 Conclusions This paper describes recent progress on the NICT/ATR speech-to-speech translation system. Corpus-based ap- proaches for speech recognition, machine translation, and speech synthesis enable coverage of a wide variety of topics and portability to other languages. Since the entire speech-to-speech translation function is imple- mented into one terminal, it provides real-time and lo- cation-free speech-to-speech translation service for many language pairs. Test results show that speech recognition character accuracy is 82%-94% for   Chinese, with BLEU score for machine translation of 0.55-0.74 for Chinese-Japanese and Chinese-English.
