file_name: Nalvakku-factbased-counter-narratives-for-Tamil-hate-speech_2026_Springer-Science-and-Business-Media-Deutschland-GmbH.pdf
text: International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 https://doi.org/10.1007/s41060-026-01023-x REGULAR PAPER Nalvakku: fact-based counter narratives for Tamil hate speech Narendran Ravikumar1 · Prasanna Kumar Kumaresan1 · Saranya Rajiakodi2 · Subalalitha CN3 · Bharathi Raja Chakravarthi1 Received: 7 October 2025 / Accepted: 5 January 2026 © The Author(s) 2026 Abstract Warning: This paper contains insulting statements that may cause discomfort for readers. The rapid proliferation of digital platforms has intensiﬁed online hate speech, especially in low-resource languages such as Tamil, where automated moderation techniques remain underdeveloped. This paper presents a three-stage methodology for generating counter narratives in Tamil. First, a seed dataset of 220 hate speech counter narrative (HS-CN) pairs is expanded to 5,000 through a human-in-the-loop Author Reviewer framework with expert validation. Second, a fact-based retrieval augmented generation (RAG) system is employed to incorporate external knowledge to enhance factual accuracy and persuasiveness. Finally, the human post- edited dataset is integrated to the RAG system as a curated knowledge base yielding a Fact-RAG system with stronger factual grounding and cultural appropriateness. Assessment through intrinsic indicators and LLM-based evaluations indicates that our methodology generates counter-narratives that are varied, credible, and contextually relevant. These ﬁndings underscore the effectiveness of integrating human supervision, factual validation, and selected examples for counter-narrative development in low-resource settings. GitHub: https://github.com/Bharathi-AI-for-Social-Good/Fact-RAG-BasedCN-Ta Keywords Hate speech · Counter narratives · Human-in-the-loop · Fact-based counter narrative · LLM ﬁne-tuning · Dravidian language 1 Introduction Hate crimes have persistently harmed communities and their effects have only grown with the rapid proliferation of social media platforms. Although these platforms provide users with unparalleled freedom to express their views and engage in public discourse, this digital empowerment has simulta- neously resulted in a worrying increase in online hate that threatens individual safety and cohesion of communities [49]. This trend has further been analyzed in the context of hidden hate dynamics across digital spaces [26]. Hate speech, deﬁned as any communication that belittles or incites vio- lence against individuals or groups based on attributes such as race, religion, gender, or sexual orientation is increas- B Bharathi Raja Chakravarthi bharathi.raja@universityofgalway.ie 1 Insight SFI Research Centre for Data Analytics, School of Computer Science,University of Galway, Galway, Ireland 2 Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur, Tamil Nadu, India 3 SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India ingly prevalent in online spaces [13]. The impact of such toxic content extends beyond the digital realm. Online hate is associated with increased polarization, actual violence, and considerable psychological damage, especially affect- ing vulnerable and marginalized groups [13]. The anonymity and virality provided by SMPs create ideal conditions for the proliferation of harmful narratives [19]. Marginalized groups such as ethnic minorities, refugees, LGBTQ+ indi- viduals, and women are especially vulnerable to online hate, compounding existing ofﬂine discrimination [19]. Prolonged exposure to such content can normalize intolerance and esca- late into targeted harassment [13]. This growing hostility demands urgent context-aware interventions [13]. With the surge of online hate across digital platforms there is an increasing need for approaches that not only mitigate harm, but also preserve democratic values such as free expres- sion [4]. Counter narratives have emerged as a proactive and constructive alternative to traditional moderation strate- gies like deletion or deplatforming [27]. Following prior work such as CONAN [12], counter-narrative approaches are preferred over hate speech classiﬁcation and normal- ization because they directly engage with harmful content 0123456789().: V,-vol 123 57 Page 2 of 31 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 through reasoned and non-confrontational responses, aiming to challenge underlying narratives rather than merely detect or suppress them. Other works emphasize their potential as governance strategies to tackle online hate effectively [40]. This engagement-based strategy interrupts the momentum of hate, encouraging reﬂection and potentially inﬂuencing both the original speaker and the broader audience [44]. Unlike reactive moderation, counter narratives support more inclusive and respectful dialog by fostering a culture of empathy and critical thinking, as shown in Table 1. Civil soci- ety initiatives, such as Words Heal and Get the Trolls Out, demonstrate the potential of human-crafted counter-speech [18]. However, their manual nature is difﬁcult to scale in the velocity of online hate [18]. While human-in-the-loop counter-narrative generation offers clear advantages in terms of linguistic quality, cultural sensitivity, and factual relia- bility, it also introduces non-trivial human annotation costs. In particular, iterative validation requires reviewing substan- tially more candidate HS-CN pairs than those retained in the ﬁnal dataset, as rejected or revised outputs must be ﬁl- tered across multiple iterations. In this work, we explicitly adopt this trade-off, prioritizing quality and representational robustness over annotation efﬁciency, especially given the low-resource and socially sensitive nature of Tamil hate speech. These constraints underscore the urgent necessity for automated and scalable counter-narrative systems capa- ble of enhancing the effectiveness of this promising approach in addressing hate speech [18]. Recent developments have highlighted the critical need of annotated corpora, lexicons, and benchmark datasets in facilitating supervised hate speech detection systems. Nonetheless, Poletto et al. [37] compre- hensive review uncovers a fragmented resource landscape, characterized by signiﬁcant discrepancies in technique, sub- ject matter, and linguistic representation, indicating evident areas for enhancement. Adding to this challenge, the multi- lingual transfer of zero shots in the detection of hate speech often falters due to the deep linguistic and cultural disparities between the source and target languages [31]. Montariol et al. [31] propose training language models on auxiliary tasks such as sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, and syntacticparsing,inbothlanguagestoserveasacross-lingual knowledge scaffold. Their ﬁndings demonstrate that such multilingualauxiliarytaskﬁne-tuningsubstantiallyenhances zero shot transfer of hate speech detection models across cul- turally diverse linguistic contexts [31]. This paper aims to generate counter narratives in the Tamil language to combat hate speech, with a particular focus on misogynistic and xenophobic content. The need for such an approach is underscored by the linguistic and cultural diversity of Tamil-speaking populations, who reside not only in Tamil Nadu and the union territory of Puducherry, but also in countries where Tamil holds ofﬁcial language status, such as Sri Lanka and Singapore [8]. Beyond these regions, large Tamil-speaking diasporas are also found in Malaysia, South Africa, Canada, and Australia together accounting for a global community of more than 75 million speakers worldwide [8]. Misogyny encompasses acts and attitudes such as violence, exclusion, belittlement, and marginaliza- tion of women. It reﬂects and reinforces patriarchal systems and sexist ideologies across individual, institutional, and cultural levels [20]. Similarly, xenophobia involves more than isolated acts of prejudice. As deﬁned by Wimmer [53], it represents a collective response rooted in socio-political anxieties, manifesting as fear, hostility, or rejection toward perceived outsiders. These sentiments are often fueled by concerns over national identity, cultural integrity, and eco- nomic insecurity. Given the prevalence of such narratives in online Tamil discourse, the creation of culturally and lin- guistically grounded counter narratives is essential. These responses not only challenge harmful ideologies, but also foster social cohesion and promote inclusive digital commu- nities. 1.1 Our contribution To address the challenges in countering online hate speech in Tamil, particularly misogynistic and xenophobic content, wepresentamulti-phasemethodologythatcombineshuman- in-the-loop data generation with fact-based counter narrative synthesis, culminating in a combined Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework leveraging a human-validated knowl- edge base. Our primary contributions are as follows: – Human-in-the-Loop Data Expansion: We developed a scalable method to expand an initial seed data set of 220 pairs of Hate speech and Counter narrative (HS-CN) pairs into a large dataset of 5,000 high quality samples. This was achieved using large language models (LLMs) in an iterative generation-validation pipeline where human expertsvalidateandreﬁneoutputs.Thisapproachensures linguistic accuracy and cultural appropriateness in a low- resource setting. – Fact-Based Counter Narrative generation: To further enhance the depth and credibility of counter narratives, we adopt a fact-based generation pipeline. In this step, we retrieve relevant information from multiple exter- nal knowledge sources (e.g., Wikipedia, trusted web domains) and generate grounded responses using LLMs. This method enables the generation of factual, infor- mative, non-aggressive CNs that are rooted in factual context. – Integrated Human Guided Fact-Based Counter Narra- tive generation: We integrated the two methodologies by incorporating the ﬁnal post-edited HS–CN pairs from 5,000 samples as a reference dataset within the Fact- 123 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Page 3 of 31 57 Table 1 Examples of matched misogynistic and xenophobic hate speech with counter-narratives in tamil from social media data. All examples shown are drawn from the original seed data set of 220 HS-CN pairs Based Counter-Narrative Generation framework, imple- mented as a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system using a human-validated knowledge base. This combined approach leverages expert-curated exemplars enriched with factual and informative content, thereby further enhancing the quality and reliability of the gen- erated counter-narratives. – Code: <<TO BE UPDATED>> Research Questions (RQ): 1. Can a human-in-the-loop framework effectively expand misogynistic and xenophobic counter-narrative datasets for low-resource languages while preserving cultural and linguistic relevance? 2. Does incorporating external factual sources, such as news articlesintocounter-narrativegenerationimprovethecon- textual richness and persuasiveness of responses in Tamil? 3. Is a retrieval-augmented generation approach that lever- ageshuman-curated,fact-veriﬁedcounternarrativeseffec- tive in enhancing the factual accuracy, informativeness, and reliability of automated responses to Tamil hate speech? 123 57 Page 4 of 31 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Sec- tion2 reviews related work on human-in-the-loop counter- narrative generation, fact-based approaches, and hate speech mitigation in low-resource languages. Section3 presents the proposed three-stage methodology, detailing the human- in-the-loop dataset expansion, fact-based counter-narrative generation, and the integrated Fact-RAG framework. Sec- tion4 describes the experimental setup, evaluation metrics, and benchmarking of language models. Section5 discusses the results and provides an analysis of different counter- narrative generation approaches. Finally, Sect.6 outlines the limitations of the study, future research directions, and con- cludes the paper. 2 Related work 2.1 Human-in-the-loop dataset expansion Developing datasets to facilitate hate speech detection across multiple languages has been a major focus in recent years. Davidson et al. [17] introduced one of the earliest large-scale datasets for hate speech detection, focusing on English tweets and setting a benchmark for subsequent studies. Mandl et al. [29] contributed to multilingual hate speech detection by organizing shared tasks that provided annotated datasets across multiple languages, thereby highlighting cross-lingual challenges. More recently, Das et al. [15] extended this line of work by developing hate speech resources in Indian languages, emphasizing the importance of low-resource con- texts. These datasets have enabled the training of supervised models to automatically identify hateful content online. However, the predominant use of such models for content removal or censorship raises concerns about the balance between regulation and freedom of expression [33]. To address the limitations of censorship-based moderation, researchers turned to counter narratives as a constructive alternative. A notable contribution is the CONAN dataset [12], a multilingual resource created through nichesourcing, which has since become a benchmark for training mod- els to generate context-aware and non-aggressive responses to hate speech. To address the challenges of scalability and diversity in counter narrative generation, Fanton et al. [19] extended this work by proposing a human-in-the-loop framework. Their methodology supported the collection of counter narratives across multiple hate targets, including race, religion, and gender, emphasizing iterative reﬁnement through human feedback. Building on the need for culturally grounded resources in low-resource settings, Das et al. [16] introduced a counterspeech dataset for Bengali and Hindi. Their structured three-stage annotation process comprising of detailed guideline creation, pilot annotation for task cali- bration, and a ﬁnal annotation phase which demonstrated an effective approach for ensuring quality and consistency in low-resource language contexts. 2.2 Generating contextual counter narratives The generation of effective counter narratives has emerged as an important strategy for addressing online hate speech. Qian et al. [38] proposed a hybrid approach that combined automatic generation with human reﬁnement, demonstrat- ing the importance of balancing automation with human oversight while Tekiro˘glu et al. [43] applied GPT-2 models with expert post-editing to improve the quality of generated responses. Zhu and Bhat [57] introduced a fully auto- mated pipeline for generating and ﬁltering candidate counter narratives, reducing the reliance on manual intervention. Tra- ditionally, counter narratives are produced by ﬁne-tuning pre-trained language models on curated datasets, offering low-computation solutions. Halim et al. [23] explored this direction by introducing WokeGPT, a ﬁne-tuned model designed to generate socially aware responses. Qian et al. [38] also demonstrated the effectiveness of ﬁne-tuning approaches in their early benchmark work and Tekiroglu et al. [45] conducted an extensive comparative evalua- tion of pretrained language models and decoding strategies for English counter-narrative generation, highlighting the strengths of autoregressive models with stochastic decod- ing and proposing an automatic post-editing step to improve output quality. Recent advances have enabled more ﬂexi- ble strategies that rely on prompting rather than ﬁne-tuning. Mun et al. [32] investigated zero-shot prompting meth- ods for counter narrative generation, showing promising results without task-speciﬁc training. Zheng et al. [56] also highlighted the potential of zero-shot setups to reduce anno- tation costs while maintaining competitive performance. Ashida and Komachi [2] examined few-shot counter narra- tive generation for Japanese datasets. Do˘ganç and Markov [18] demonstrated how human-guided few-shot prompting can improve counter narrative quality. Furman et al. [21] studied high-quality counter narrative generation under few- shot conditions. Vallecillo-Rodríguez et al. [47] investigated automatic counter narrative generation in low-resource con- texts using few-shot prompting techniques. Building on these developments, Lewis et al. [28] introduced Retrieval- Augmented Generation (RAG), a framework that combines parametric knowledge from pretrained language models with nonparametric retrieval from large document collections, signiﬁcantly improving factual grounding and reducing hal- lucinations ingeneratedtext. Followingthis foundation, Wilk et al. [52] proposed a fact-based counter narrative genera- tion pipeline that retrieves contextual evidence from multiple external sources to improve factuality and relevance. This approach enhances the credibility of generated narratives while reducing hallucinations and generic content. Similarly, 123 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Page 5 of 31 57 Jiang et al. [25] introduced ReZG, a retrieval-augmented zero-shot counter narrative generation framework tailored for hate speech, demonstrating that combining retrieval with zero-shot prompting can signiﬁcantly boost contextual rel- evance without extensive ﬁne-tuning. In a related direction, Chinnan et al. [11] introduced a gender-inclusive language generation framework that combines Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to ensure both factual grounding and inclusive language prac- tices, demonstrating the broader applicability of fact-driven generative methods beyond counter-narratives. In contrast, our proposed approach uniquely integrates an expert-curated, human-validated knowledge base within a fact-based RAG framework, enabling both high factual accuracy and domain- speciﬁc cultural sensitivity which are qualities not simulta- neously addressed by prior works. 2.3 Counter narratives for low-resource languages While counter narrative research has advanced signiﬁcantly in high-resource languages like English and Spanish, simi- lar efforts in Tamil remain limited due to the lack of large annotated datasets. Chakravarthi et al. [9] addressed this gap by introducing a ﬁne-grained Tamil dataset covering cate- gories like misogyny, xenophobia, and homophobia, sourced from social media platforms. Their dataset has served as a crucial benchmark for subsequent modeling efforts, enabling more nuanced classiﬁcation of hate categories. Vas- antharajan and Thayasivam [48] tackled the challenges of Tamil-English code-mixing by developing ensemble learn- ing models that combine multiple classiﬁers for improved detection robustness. Their work highlighted the impor- tance of accounting for linguistic hybridity that characterizes online discourse among Tamil speakers. Similarly, Patankar et al. [36] explored transfer learning techniques leveraging pre-trained multilingual models to adapt to Tamil’s linguistic structure and code-switching patterns. This study demon- strated that multilingual embeddings can effectively bridge gaps in resource availability by transferring knowledge across languages. In addition, Saha et al. [39] demonstrated that transformer-based architectures, such as BERT variants can signiﬁcantly improve classiﬁcation accuracy for Tamil hate speech, even in the presence of noisy and dialect-rich input. Their results underscored the adaptability of trans- former models to handle informal and regionally diverse text. Anbukkarasi and Varadhaganapathy [1] extended this line of work by applying deep learning models optimized for mor- phologically rich languages, showing strong performance in identifying hate speech across multiple Tamil dialects. This highlights the necessity of tailoring architectures to the complex morphology of Tamil, which differs signiﬁcantly from resource-rich languages like English. Singh and Thakur [41] introduced MultiFED, a federated learning approach that combines multiple low-resource Indic language datasets including Tamil to improve cross-lingual hate speech detec- tion while preserving privacy. Their ﬁndings demonstrate the promise of collaborative learning frameworks that enable knowledge sharing without centralized data collection, a par- ticularly relevant consideration for sensitive hate speech data. Bennie et al. [5] presented CODEOFCONDUCT, a context- aware counterspeech generation model developed for the MCG-COLING-2025 shared task, achieving state-of-the- art performance in low-resource settings through simulated annealing and multilingual ﬁne-tuning. Importantly, their work illustrates how carefully designed generation systems can complement detection pipelines, creating a more com- prehensive response to online hate. In contrast to prior efforts, our proposed approach focuses on generating fact- grounded and culturally sensitive counter-narratives in Tamil by combining human-in-the-loop dataset expansion, fact- based retrieval-augmented generation, and expert-guided validation. This integrated methodology leverages both large language models and a human-validated knowledge base to ensure linguistic accuracy, factual grounding, and reliability in a low-resource setting. Existingcounter-narrativeresearchpredominantlyfocuses on high-resource languages and often relies on prompt- based generation without any factual grounding or large- scale human validation. In addition, evaluations frequently depend on automatic metrics without explicit analysis of failure modes such as hallucination, inconsistency, and bias. This work addresses these gaps through a human-in- the-loop framework for Tamil counter-narrative generation, fact-based and retrieval-augmented methods, and multi- dimensional evaluation. 3 Methodologies In this work, we propose a three-stage methodology to enhance counter narrative generation for hate speech in Tamil language. First, to address data scarcity we began by acquiring only the misogynistic and xenophobic hate speech contents from the dataset introduced by Chakravarthi et al. [9]. The corresponding counter narratives were then man- ually matched to these hate speech instances with the help of voluntary human experts, ensuring accuracy and contex- tual relevance. Building on this foundation, we expanded the dataset to 5000 pairs using a human-in-the-loop strategy, where LLM-generated outputs were systematically reviewed and reﬁned through an Author–Reviewer framework. This enriched dataset serves as a strong foundation for training and evaluating counter narrative models. In the second stage, we generate fact-based counter narratives by retrieving rel- evant background documents, summarizing them to extract key context and conditioning the LLM to produce informed, 123 57 Page 6 of 31 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Fig. 1 Our workﬂow involves (1) Extracting Misogynistic and Xenophobic hate speech to create a seed HS–CN dataset, (2) Fine-tuning language models for counter-narrative generation followed by human evaluation and quantitative analysis. non-aggressive responses [52]. Finally, in the third method- ology, we utilize the ﬁnal post-edited dataset (V5) produced through the Author–Reviewer framework as a knowledge base, integrating it into the fact-based pipeline to generate counter narratives that are both factual and persuasive. 3.1 Author-reviewer architecture for HS-CN dataset expansion In Fig.1, we illustrate the overall workﬂow of our counter- narrative generation framework, which consists of two main phases: seed dataset preparation and human-in-the-loop (HITL) evaluation. In the ﬁrst phase, misogynistic and xeno- phobic hate speech instances are ﬁltered from the source dataset [9], and a pool of generic counter narratives is sep- arately extracted. Voluntary human experts then manually match semantically appropriate counter narratives to the hate speech samples, resulting in a seed dataset of 220 HS–CN pairs. In the second phase, the seed dataset is leveraged to ﬁne-tune a language model, which generates synthetic hate speech–counter narrative pairs. These outputs undergo HITL evaluation, where an Author–Reviewer framework as illus- trated in Fig. 2 is applied to iteratively reﬁne and validate the generated counter narratives. Quantitative metrics such as imbalance degree and HTER (Human Translation Error Rate) are also computed to assess quality. The process results in a post-edited HS–CN dataset (V5), which serves as a high- quality resource for fact-based counter-narrative generation in Tamil. 3.1.1 Guidelines for writing counter-narratives Prior to initiating the counter-narrative writing process, we established a set of clear guidelines for the annotators to ensure that the responses are both appropriate and effective. In this context, a Counter-Narrative is deﬁned as any direct response to abusive or hateful speech that aims to challenge or undermine the message without resorting to harassment or adopting an aggressive tone toward the speaker. There are multiple strategies to counter abusive content. Chung et al. [12] identiﬁed a set of guidelines to be followed by the Vol- untary language experts for writing the Counter-Narratives as deﬁned in Table 2. These guidelines serve as a founda- tional reference to maintain consistency and quality across all annotated counter-narrative examples. 123 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Page 7 of 31 57 Fig. 2 LLM-Guided Author–Reviewer Framework for Expanding Misogynistic and Xenophobic HS–CN Pairs. The author module (GPT 3.5) generates HS-CN pairs which are then validated and corrected by human reviewers. This is then consolidated into training data and the loop continues. Table 2 Guidelines followed by Voluntary language experts for effec- tive Counter-Narratives Guideline Description Don’t be abusive Before submitting a response, ensure it does not spread hate, bigotry, prejudice, or illegal content. The aim is to keep the conversation peaceful and respectful, focusing on individuals rather than categories. Think about the objectives Consider the desired outcome of your response. Reﬂecting on the objective helps in choosing the right words and tone. Use credible evidence Hate speech often contains misleading information. Ask for clariﬁcation when needed and counter it using trustworthy sources. Think about the tone It is important to show empathy and support for those targeted. Use humor, sarcasm, or satire cautiously, as they can easily become antagonistic. 3.1.2 Human reviewer setup and demographics We consider a post to be abusive if it contains content that dehumanizes or incites harm against an individual or com- munity. Such abuse often involves the use of derogatory language or slurs targeting individuals based on protected attributes such as race, religion, ethnicity, sexual orientation, disability, or gender [22]. In accordance with this deﬁnition, we employed ﬁve Tamil Voluntary language experts, com- prising undergraduate students and NLP researchers aged between 25 and 30 years. All contributors were ﬂuent in Tamil language and were trained on the guidelines for writing effective and appropriate counter-narratives. The reﬁnement process involved reviewing the model-generated outputs, revising them for clarity, tone, and relevance, and discard- ing samples that failed to meet the deﬁned criteria. The entire synthetic data generation and reﬁnement process was carried out over a period of ﬁve months. 123 57 Page 8 of 31 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 3.1.3 Pilot phase for guideline alignment To prepare language experts for the counter narrative writ- ing task, a pilot exercise was conducted using a subset of 10 abusive speech samples. The experts were instructed to com- pose appropriate counter-narratives according to established guidelines. They were encouraged to keep the guidelines readily accessible throughout the process to maintain clar- ity on tone, structure, and recommended rhetorical strategies. Followingthepilotphase,thedraftedcounter-narrativeswere manually reviewed by us to evaluate the language experts’ understanding of the task and their alignment with the pre- scribed framework. This initial stage proved essential for identifying areas of confusion, correcting early mistakes, and providing targeted feedback to reﬁne their approach before transitioning to the larger annotation effort. 3.1.4 Main data generation and reﬁnement phase Following the pilot phase, we commenced with the pri- mary data generation task. Language experts were assigned approximately 100 hate speech contents per week for counter-narrative generation. To mitigate potential psycho- logical impacts associated with prolonged exposure to hate- ful content, we implemented several safeguarding measures: 1. Maintained a relaxed timeline to prevent language expert fatigue. 2. Recommended mandatory ﬁve-minute breaks following each counter-narrative composition. 3. Conductedregularcheck-inmeetingstomonitorlanguage expert well-being and identify any adverse psychological effects. The completed dataset comprises 5,000 hate speech counter-narratives pairs. Dataset quality was evaluated using multiple metrics, including Human Translation Error Rate (HTER), repetition rate, acceptance rate (both modiﬁed and unmodiﬁedsubmissions),andnoveltymeasures.InFig.2,we illustrate the overall pipeline of our author–reviewer method- ology. Based on the framework proposed by Tekiro˘glu et al. [43], our approach employs an author module powered by GPT 3.5,1 initially ﬁne-tuned on a seed dataset of HS–CN pairs from Chakravarthi et al. [9]. This module generates new HS–CN candidates, which are then reviewed and reﬁned by human annotators. The validated outputs are incorporated into the training set, and the author model is re-trained from scratch in each iteration using the updated dataset. This iter- ative loop ensures continuous improvement in generation quality and dataset diversity, with the dataset growth and batch sizes across iterations summarized in Table 3. 1 https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-3.5-turbo Table 3 Dataset growth and batch sizes across iterative training stages Version New samples added Total size V1 – 220 V2 200 420 V3 400 820 V4 800 1620 V5 3,380 5000 3.1.5 Author model for synthetic HS–CN data expansion For our experiments, we utilized the GPT−3.5 Turbo model for both ﬁne-tuning and generating synthetic data follow- ing a post-editing process. As GPT models are not available for local deployment, all ﬁne-tuning was conducted directly through OpenAI’s hosted infrastructure. Each iteration (V1 to V5) involved ﬁne-tuning over three epochs with a learning ratemultiplierof2,whichofferedapracticalbalancebetween convergence and overﬁtting. The choice of three epochs was empirically determined to be sufﬁcient for the model to inter- nalize domain-speciﬁc patterns without overtraining, while a learning rate multiplier of 2 ensured faster adaptation during limited training steps, especially in the low-resource setting of our Tamil dataset. The detailed system prompts employed during ﬁne-tuning is provided in Fig.3. The complete work- ﬂow of our methodology is illustrated in Fig.1. Once the model is ﬁne-tuned, we then used the ﬁne-tuned model to generate the HS-CN pairs for each iteration. The detailed system prompts employed for generating HS-CN pairs are provided in Fig.4. 3.2 Fact-based counter narrative generation Our second methodology focuses on generating fact-based counter narratives through a structured three-step pipeline architecture. This approach begins with document selection process which is guided by a set of queries generated from the input hate speech. Next, the retrieved documents are summa- rized and ﬁltered to retain only those that are most relevant for addressing the hate speech. Finally, three counter narratives are generated based on the ﬁltered factual content and the original hate speech context and then the best one is selected based on the provided input hate speech. Since these are the documents that are retrieved from news articles, they are often lengthy and may include irrelevant or noisy information. Directly using them as input for counter narra- tive generation can introduce content that is misaligned with the hate speech instance or the intended counter response. To mitigate this, we decompose the process into two sub-steps: (1) Document Summarization and (2) Document Critique. This ensures that only the most relevant and concise content is passed to the generation stage. Document Summarization: Each of the retrieved docu- ments is summarized individually using GPT-4o. Since LLMs have token limitations and these news articles can be verbose, summarization ensures that the most pertinent segments are retained. Word clouds visually encode token frequency through font size, allowing intuitive recognition of domi- nant words across the dataset. Word clouds are useful for quickly identifying prominent themes and keywords in text data, making them a popular tool for text analysis and data visualization [46]. Separate word clouds were generated from the seed dataset for both hate speech and counter-narrative corpora using the most frequent tokens. The visualizations (Figs.13 and 14) highlight stark contrasts in lexical choices and the- matic focus between hateful and constructive content. In the hate speech cloud (Fig.13), words like "Tamilian" and "Immigrant" suggest xenophobic rhetoric, while terms such as "Mom", "Women" and explicit language indicate misogy- nistic abuse. In contrast, the counter-narrative cloud (Fig.14) features words like "Should," "Your," "Upbringing," and "Learn" reﬂecting a corrective tone aimed at challenging hate, fostering empathy, and promoting critical reﬂection. Next, we investigate the structural characteristics of the seed dataset by analyzing the distribution of text lengths in both hate speech and counter-narratives. Although word clouds offer an overview of content-level features, examin- ing the length of texts measured in the number of characters can provide useful insight into the communicative strategies inherent in hateful versus constructive speech. We compute the character-level length of each text and visualize the result- ing distributions using overlaid histograms (Fig.15). The visualization clearly illustrates that approximately (90%) of the hate speech entries fall within the 30–150 character range, while nearly (85%) of the counter-narratives span 80– 250 characters, with some extending beyond 400 characters. These ﬁndings reﬂect the inherent difference in communica- tive goals: hate speech often conveys hostility with minimal text, whereas CNs typically require elaboration, justiﬁcation, or empathetic appeals to de-escalate harmful discourse. 4.2 Human-in-the-loop approach We initiated the experiments using a seed dataset of 220 misogynistic and xenophobic HS–CN pairs derived from the hate speech corpus of [9]. We ﬁrst reviewed prompts and instruction formulations used in prior counter-narrative generation studies for hate speech to establish an initial baseline. Building on these templates, we iteratively reﬁned the prompts over multiple rounds (three to ﬁve iterations) using large language models to improve clar- ity, contextual alignment, and adherence to counter-narrative guidelines. At each reﬁnement stage, candidate prompts were evalu- ated by generating counter-narratives on a small validation subset and examining their outputs using key quality indi- cators, including toxicity, factuality, informativeness, and 123 57 Page 18 of 31 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 linguistic quality. Prompts that consistently produced non- toxic, contextually grounded, and coherent responses were retained, while others were revised or discarded. The ﬁnal prompt templates were then ﬁxed and used uniformly across all experiments to ensure reproducibility and fair comparison between generation methods. 4.4 Benchmarking language models for counter-narrative generation We also conducted a series of controlled experiments to evaluate and select the most effective large language model (LLM) for validating the quality of generated Counter Nar- ratives (CNs). As part of this assessment, we compared the performance of multiple state-of-the-art LLMs, speciﬁcally GPT−3.5 Turbo,6 GPT-4o,7 Gemini 2.5,8 and Claude 3.7 Sonnet.9 To ensure a rigorous and multi-faceted evaluation, we employed both automatic and model-assisted metrics across several dimensions. The evaluation metrics used for these comparisons are described in detail below. – Toxicity: Toxicity is an inherent characteristic of hate speech and is therefore expected to be high. In con- trast, effective Counter Narratives (CNs) should exhibit minimal toxicity, as they aim to de-escalate rather than provoke. To ensure the generated CNs maintain low tox- icity, we employ the Perspective API,10 developed by Jigsaw and Google. This tool leverages a combination of BERT-based models and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to assess the toxicity of a given text. It outputs a probability score between 0 and 1, where a score closer to 0 indicates non-toxic content, and a score closer to 1 reﬂects high toxicity. – Factuality, Persuasiveness, Informativeness and Linguis- tic quality: Next, we adopted the evaluation framework proposed by Jiang et al. [25] to assess the factuality, persuasiveness, informativeness and Linguistic quality of the generated counter narratives. Factuality measures how accurately the CN is grounded in veriﬁable facts and objective information relevant to the given hate speech, ensuring credibility and reliability. Persuasiveness mea- sures the ability of a CN to inﬂuence or shift the reader’s opinion by presenting compelling and logically consis- tent arguments. A persuasive CN effectively counters the hate speech stance while maintaining coherence. Infor- mativeness evaluates the amount of useful, novel, and 6 https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-3-5 7 https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-4o 8 https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/models/ gemini/2-5-ﬂash 9 https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-3-7-sonnet 10 https://www.perspectiveapi.com/ relevant information introduced in the CN that helps better understand or challenge the hate content. Infor- mative CNs enrich the dialog beyond simple negation. Linguistic quality refers to the overall ﬂuency, grammat- ical correctness, coherence, and naturalness of a sentence as perceived in the target language. The prompt used for evaluating these metrics is as shown in Fig.18. – BLEU-1, 2, 3, 4: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Under- study) is a precision-based metric originally introduced for evaluating machine translation by comparing n-gram overlaps between generated and reference texts [35]. In our evaluation, we use BLEU-1 to BLEU-4 to cap- ture increasing levels of syntactic ﬂuency and alignment. BLEU-1 reﬂects unigram-level matching, while BLEU-4 captures more strict four-gram sequence alignment, thus offering insight into both lexical choice and structural coherence. Although BLEU has known limitations in capturing semantic similarity, it remains a strong base- line for surface-level ﬁdelity and has been widely adopted in text generation research, including summarization and dialog systems. – BERTScore: BERTScore (Bidirectional Encoder Repre- sentations from Transformers) is a semantic evaluation metric used to assess the similarity between the generated counter-narrative and a human-written reference. Unlike surface-level metrics such as BLEU, BERTScore com- putes pairwise cosine similarity between contextualized token embeddings derived from pre-trained transformer models, thereby capturing semantic equivalence even in the presence of lexical variation or paraphrasing [54]. 4.5 Analysis of inconsistency, hallucination and bias In the context of counter-narrative generation for Tamil hate speech, we distinguish between three related but conceptu- ally distinct failure modes: inconsistency, hallucination, and bias. Although these phenomena may co-occur, they arise from different stages of the generation pipeline and are eval- uated using different criteria. Hallucination In our experiments, hallucination refers to situations where a counter-narrative introduces factual claims or explanations that cannot be traced back to the retrieved evidence or veriﬁed external sources used in the fact-based pipeline. This typically occurs when the retrieved documents provide only partial support for the hate speech context, prompting the model to ﬁll gaps with plausible but unsupported information. Such responses may appear ﬂuent and persuasive, yet fail to remain grounded in the available evidence, a well-documented limitation of large language models [24, 30]. Hallucination was primarily cap- tured through the factuality evaluation metric and further examined during manual inspection when claims lacked cor- 123 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Page 19 of 31 57 respondence with the retrieved news articles or the curated knowledge base. Bias In the context of this study, bias refers to counter- narratives that, while intended to challenge hateful content, unintentionally reproduce stereotypes or imbalanced repre- sentations related to gender, nationality, or social identity. Such cases may not contain overtly toxic language and may even be factually correct, yet still reﬂect implicit assump- tions that are culturally or socially insensitive, consistent with prior analyses of representational bias in language mod- els [3, 6]. Given the linguistic and cultural diversity within Tamil online discourse, representational bias was primar- ily addressed through human-in-the-loop validation, where expert reviewers examined responses for implicit stereotyp- ing and cultural insensitivity beyond what can be captured by surface-level toxicity metrics, and revised or discarded outputs that did not align with inclusivity guidelines. Inconsistency In our work, inconsistency refers to cases where a generated counter-narrative lacks internal coher- ence or presents reasoning that does not align with the hate speech it is intended to address. For example, some responses partially accept the premise of the hate speech while simul- taneously attempting to refute it, or shift focus in a way that weakens the argumentative ﬂow. These issues are not neces- sarily related to factual errors but arise from discourse-level generation problems, particularly in longer or more complex responses, as noted in prior studies on reasoning consistency in large language models [14, 51]. Inconsistency is primarily reﬂected in lower linguistic quality and coherence scores dur- ing evaluation and was also identiﬁed during human review when counter-narratives were judged to be unclear or logi- cally unconvincing. Failure Patterns and Evaluation Perspective The occur- rence of these failure modes can be attributed to different lim- itations within the generation pipeline. Hallucination is most often observed when retrieved evidence provides incomplete or weakly aligned contextual support, leading the model to rely on general knowledge rather than grounded information. Inconsistency arises from challenges in maintaining a coher- ent argumentative structure over multi-sentence generations, while bias-related issues tend to surface when generation relies on model priors rather than contextual or evidence- based grounding, particularly in culturally nuanced settings. These observations are consistent with ﬁndings reported in prior large language model evaluations, where hallucination, reasoning inconsistency, and representational bias are recog- nized as common challenges. Our evaluation approach aligns with established practices by combining evidence-based fac- tuality assessment, discourse-level quality evaluation, and human-in-the-loop review for bias, which together provide a comprehensive validation framework for counter-narrative generation in low-resource and socially sensitive contexts [7, 10]. 4.6 Comparative evaluation of counter narrative generation approaches Following the model selection phase, we conducted a sys- tematic investigation of multiple counter-narrative genera- tion strategies to identify the most effective approach. The approaches explored include: – Zero-shot prompting: We utilized the GPT-4o model via the OpenAI API to generate counter-narratives without providing any prior examples. The complete prompt used in this setup is illustrated in Fig.16. – Few-shot prompting (With 5 and 10 samples from V5 dataset): In this setting, we used the same GPT-4o model, but included 5 and 10 representative examples from the V5 dataset within the prompt to guide the model. The detailed prompt structure is shown in Fig.17. – Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) using the V5 Dataset: In this approach, we use our own previ- ously generated dataset (V5), developed through the initial zero-shot and few-shot prompting methodolo- gies, as a knowledge base. For each input hate speech instance, semantically similar examples are retrieved from the V5 dataset using cosine similarity, computed via the sentence-transformers/ distiluse-base-multilingual- cased-v2 model.11 These retrieved examples are then incorporated into the prompt to guide GPT-4o in gen- erating a contextually relevant and informed counter- narrative. – Fact-based approach: Next, we employed the fact- based methodology outlined in Algorithm 1 to generate counter-narratives using the same GPT-4o model. – Fact-Enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation using the V5 Knowledge Base: In this ﬁnal approach, we inte- grate the fact-based methodology with our V5 dataset serving as a knowledge base. For each hate speech instance, contextual counter-narratives are generated by incorporating evidence from external sources, alongside semantically similar examples retrieved from the V5 dataset to enrich the prompt and improve relevance. To evaluate each generation approach, we employed the same set of qualitative metrics - Toxicity, Factuality, Per- suasiveness, Informativeness, and Linguistic Quality-as used during model selection. These metrics enabled a compre- hensive comparison of the outputs in terms of both ethical alignment and communicative effectiveness. All experiments were conducted using the designated test dataset with ﬁxed prompts, identical test splits, and standardized metrics to 11 https://huggingface.co/sentence-transformers/distiluse-base- multilingual-cased-v2 123 57 Page 20 of 31 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Fig. 16 The prompt instructs the model to act as a socially responsible language generator and produce concise and respectful counter-narratives for a given hate speech instance, without relying on prior examples or external factual information. This approach does not incorporate external factual evidence and relies solely on example-based guidance. ensure objective, unbiased, and fully reproducible evalua- tion. 4.7 Inter-annotator agreement computation To assess the reliability of human judgments and human- LLM alignment, we quantify pairwise agreement between evaluators using exact score matching. For each evaluation dimension, agreement is computed as the proportion of sam- ples for which two annotators (or between a human annotator and an LLM) assigned identical scores, averaged across all evaluated instances. This measure provides a transparent and reproducible estimate of agreement consistency and is used uniformly across all human evaluation dimensions. Agreement(A, B) = 1 N N  i=1 ⊮  ai = bi  × 100 (15) where – ai and bi are the labels assigned by annotators A and B, – N is the total number of evaluated samples 123 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Page 21 of 31 57 Fig. 18 This prompt guides the model to evaluate a given counter narra- tive against provided context and hate speech by scoring it on factuality, persuasiveness, informativeness, and linguistic quality. It ensures that the assessment is objective, structured, and consistent, enabling quan- titative comparison across multiple counter narratives in the dataset. 5 Results and discussion 5.1 HITL-based counter narrative dataset expansion: metrics and findings We observed several noteworthy trends across model ver- sions V2 to V5 based on the evaluation metrics presented in Table 4. First, the imbalance degree progressively decreased from 1.1530 in V2 to 1.0116 in V5, indicating a more bal- anced distribution across hate speech categories. This reﬂects the model’s increasing ability to generate counter-narratives uniformly across diverse categories. In terms of Acceptance Rate,bothuntouchedandmodiﬁedsamplesshowaconsistent upward trajectory. The untouched acceptance rate improved from 4.50% in V2 to 11.36% in V5, while the modiﬁed acceptance rate rose from 36.50% to 51.71% over the same iterations. These results suggest that with further training, the generated counter-narratives were increasingly aligned with the quality standards set by the voluntary language experts. As illustrated in Fig.19, this trend is mirrored by a steady decline in discarded samples, dropping from 59.00% in V2 to 36.93% in V5. This inverse relationship highlights that as expert-guided reﬁnement improved the quality of out- puts, fewer generations required rejection, underscoring the growing efﬁciency and reliability of the pipeline across iter- ations. At the same time, we can also observe that the HTER exhibited a favorable decline. The overall HTER dropped from 0.3399 in V2 to 0.2018 in V5, and the modiﬁed HTER fell from 0.5819 to 0.3463. This implies that fewer edits were required by language experts over successive iterations, pointing to a steady improvement in the ﬂuency and appropri- ateness of the generated counter-narratives. A similar trend was observed in the repetition rate, which declined from 9.5472 to 5.2318 for hate speech and from 6.0524 to 5.1106 for counter-narratives, indicating a reduction in redundant phrasing and an increase in lexical diversity. Moreover, we also observed that the novelty metric also improved signiﬁ- cantlyespeciallyincomparisontoprior versions. Thenovelty of counter-narratives compared to V1 rose from 0.4846 in V2 to 0.9312 in V5, while the cumulative novelty also increased signiﬁcantly from 0.4846 to 0.6170, indicating that the model generated increasingly distinct and original responses in later stages. Finally, the CN vocabulary size expanded substan- tially from 2672 in V2 to 11986 in V5. This expansion is also evident in the increase in newly introduced vocabulary terms from 1044 to 6159, showcasing the model’s growing linguistic capacity and generalization ability. Overall, these improvements demonstrate the effective- ness of incorporating a human-in-the-loop strategy, where iterative reﬁnement based on expert feedback enables the model to progressively generate high-quality, more diverse, and contextually appropriate CNs. 123 57 Page 22 of 31 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Table 4 Evaluation metrics for all results of HS-CN pairs from V2 to V5 using GPT based data expansion. choose the most suit- able model for counter-narrative generation, the results in Table 5 clearly establish GPT-4o as the optimal backbone for our methodology. Across all evaluated models, GPT- 4o achieves the lowest counter-narrative toxicity (0.103), ensuring safer generations while maintaining identical HS toxicity values (0.187) across models. In terms of factuality, GPT-4o matches the highest score (0.810), demonstrating reliable content grounding. It further excels in informative- ness (0.890, tied with Gemini−2.5), highlighting its ability to produce detailed and context-rich narratives. Persuasiveness remains strong (0.710), outperforming GPT−3.5 (0.690) and Claude−3.7 (0.640), and on par with Gemini−2.5. Linguistic quality is consistently high (0.790), reinforcing the ﬂuency and readability of outputs. Importantly, GPT-4o demonstrates balanced performance across lexical overlap and semantic similarity metrics. Its BLEU scores, though rel- atively modest (BLEU-1: 0.0072, BLEU-2: 0.0016, BLEU- 3: 0.0010, BLEU-4: 0.0008), reﬂect reduced reliance on surface-level n-gram matches compared to Claude−3.7 (with higher BLEU values but weaker persuasiveness). This bal- 123 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Page 23 of 31 57 Fig. 19 This ﬁgure illustrates the relationship between acceptance rates and discarded pairs across iterative versions (V2–V5) of the human-in-the-loop (HITL) expansion process. The steady increase in acceptance rate, accompanied by a corresponding decline in discarded pairs, demonstrates the effectiveness of iterative expert reﬁnement in improving dataset quality, consistency, and contextual appropriateness of counter narratives. ance indicates GPT-4o’s ability to generate diverse and contextually adapted narratives rather than overﬁtting to ref- erence patterns. Complementing this, GPT-4o achieves a robust BERTScore (0.9337), underscoring strong semantic alignment with reference counter-narratives while avoid- ing redundancy. Taken together, these results demonstrate that GPT-4o offers the most effective trade-off between safety, factual accuracy, informativeness, persuasiveness, and semantic ﬁdelity. Its ability to balance low toxicity with high factuality and semantic similarity ﬁrmly positions GPT- 4o as the most reliable and contextually adaptable choice for our proposed fact-based counter-narrative generation frame- work. 5.3 Analysis of results across counter-narrative generation approaches Based on the comparative evaluation of different prompt- ing and knowledge-based approaches for counter-narrative generation using GPT-4o, the results shown in Table 6 demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed Fact-RAG approach with the V5 knowledge base, while also providing baseline evidence for the usefulness of the synthetic corpus employed as a retrieval knowledge base. Among the eval- uated approaches, Zero-shot prompting achieves relatively low toxicity (CN: 0.102) and strong informativeness (0.850), but suffers from poor factuality (0.060) and limited persua- siveness (0.420), highlighting the limitations of prompt-only generation for producing credible and convincing responses. Few-shot prompting with 10 samples shows some improve- ment in linguistic quality (0.790), but this improvement comes at the cost of signiﬁcantly higher toxicity in the gener- ated counter-narratives (CN: 0.204), reduced persuasiveness (0.230), and very low factuality (0.040), making the out- puts less reliable overall. Traditional RAG with V5 as a knowledge base achieves slightly better factuality (0.080) compared to previous approaches but underperforms in per- suasiveness (0.170) and informativeness (0.640), indicating limitations when context integration is not tightly aligned with hate speech content. The fact-based approach signiﬁ- cantly boosts factuality (0.630) and persuasiveness (0.520), showing the importance of grounding counter narratives in external knowledge, but it slightly compromises linguistic quality (0.650) and semantic similarity (BERT: 0.9463). In contrast, our proposed Fact-RAG with V5 as KB achieves the highest factuality score (0.650), demonstrating the model’s ability to generate counter narratives that are not only contextually relevant but also grounded in veriﬁ- able knowledge. While other approaches excel marginally in select metrics, such as informativeness or BERTScore, our method offers a more balanced performance across tox- icity, persuasiveness, informativeness, and linguistic quality as illustrated in Fig.20. Importantly, the integration of the HITL postedited V5 dataset as a knowledge base ensures cul- turalandlinguisticappropriateness,bridgingthegapbetween accuracy and contextual sensitivity. Taken together, these results provide strong evidence that our proposed approach achieves the most reliable balance between factual ground- ing and overall counter narrative quality, making it the most effective framework for counter narrative generation in low- resource settings. 5.4 Human evaluation As the next step, we engaged three human evaluators to assess the quality of the counter-narratives generated by our 123 57 Page 24 of 31 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Table 5 Evaluation of generated counter narratives (CNs) across LLMs. Metrics include: Toxicity (Tox.; split into Hate Speech (HS) and Counter Narrative (CN)), Factuality (Fact.), Persuasiveness (Pers.), Informativeness (Info.), Linguistic Quality (LQ), BLEU-2, and BERTScore (BERT). Lower is better for Tox. (↓), while higher is better for others (↑) Model Tox. ↓ Fact. ↑ Pers. ↑ Info. ↑ LQ ↑ BLEU-1/2/3/4 ↑ BERT ↑ HS CN GPT-4o 0.187 0.103 0.810 0.710 0.890 0.790 0.0072 / 0.0016 / 0.001 / 0.0008 0.9337 GPT−3.5 0.187 0.118 0.810 0.690 0.860 0.820 0.0121 / 0.0016 / 0.0008 / 0.0006 0.8550 Gemini−2.5 0.187 0.107 0.810 0.710 0.890 0.790 0.0077 / 0.0018 / 0.0011 / 0.0009 0.9375 Claude−3.7 0.187 0.179 0.810 0.640 0.850 0.790 0.0142 / 0.0026 / 0.0015 / 0.0012 0.9636 proposed approach: Fact-RAG with V5 as the knowledge base using GPT-4o. The evaluators were provided with the HS–CN pairs and instructed to score each counter-narrative on four metrics: Factuality, Persuasiveness, Informativeness, and Linguistic Quality, using a scale from 0 to 1. Figure21 compares the prompt-level scores from human evaluators with those from the LLM across these metrics. For Factual- ity, human scores show some variability across prompts, with several low-scoring cases, whereas the LLM consistently assigns higher scores, likely reﬂecting a tendency to over- estimate factual correctness, potentially due to its reliance on a single external source for fact retrieval, in contrast to humans who cross-check multiple sources. Persuasiveness shows moderate alignment, with the LLM generally assign- ing more favorable ratings; the largest discrepancies occur for prompts that humans rated as having low persuasiveness. Informativeness generally aligns closely between human and LLM evaluations, with only minor deviations observed for certain prompts. For Linguistic Quality, human evaluators generally agree with each other, yet several prompts reveal marked deviations between human and LLM scores. Figure 22 presents heatmaps showing the agreement per- centages between multiple human judges and an LLM-based judge across four evaluation metrics: Factuality, Persuasive- ness, Informativeness, and Linguistic Quality. Each heatmap visualizes pairwise agreement rates, where darker shades of blue represent stronger alignment. On average, Informativeness exhibited the highest agree- ment between the LLM and human judges, reaching approx- imately 90%, suggesting strong consistency in recognizing content richness. Persuasiveness and Linguistic Quality dis- played relatively lower agreements, averaging around 80%, reﬂecting the inherent subjectivity in assessing commu- nication impact and stylistic quality. Factuality showed moderately high agreement at roughly 85%, indicating reli- able LLM performance in factual consistency. These ﬁndings highlight that while LLMs can align well with human judg- ments in information-focused metrics, subjective aspects such as persuasiveness and linguistic nuance still beneﬁt from human-in-the-loop validation for balanced and context- aware evaluations. To further characterize the stability of these evaluations, we report the standard deviation between LLM-generated scores and the mean human ratings across metrics, as shown in Table 7. The observed standard deviation values are consistently low, ranging from 0.040 to 0.067 across factu- ality, informativeness, persuasiveness, and linguistic quality. Lower variability for informativeness and linguistic quality indicates stable alignment in assessing content richness and surface-level ﬂuency, while slightly higher variability for fac- tuality and persuasiveness reﬂects the greater sensitivity of these dimensions to contextual interpretation and evidence grounding. Overall, these results suggest that LLM-based evaluation exhibits limited dispersion relative to human judg- ments, supporting its use as a complementary assessment signal alongside human evaluation. In addressing RQ1: Can a human-in-the-loop framework effectively expand misogynistic and xenophobic counter- narrative datasets for low-resource languages while preserv- ing cultural and linguistic relevance? - our ﬁrst methodology of Author-Reviewer of HS-CN expansion from Table 4, we observed that with each iteration from V2 to V5, all eval- uation metrics demonstrate steady improvements driven by the human-in-the-loop reﬁnement process. The imbalance degree decreased notably, indicating more uniform counter- narrative generation across hate speech categories, while acceptance rates for both untouched and modiﬁed sam- ples increased, reﬂecting stronger alignment with expert quality standards. Editing requirements, as measured by HTER, declined consistently, accompanied by reduced rep- etition rates, showing gains in ﬂuency, lexical diversity, and appropriateness. Novelty scores rose sharply, and vocab- ulary size expanded signiﬁcantly, indicating the model’s growing ability to produce original, varied, and contextually rich responses. Together, these trends highlight how iterative expertfeedbackprogressivelyenhancedthequality,diversity, and contextual relevance of generated counter-narratives. In addressing RQ2: Does incorporating external factual sources, such as news articles into counter-narrative gener- ation improve the contextual richness and persuasiveness of responses in Tamil? - our second methodology enhanced gen- erated counter-narratives (CNs) by integrating information 123 International Journal of Data Science and Analytics (2026) 22:57 Page 25 of 31 57 Table 6 Comparative evaluation of various prompting and knowledge-based approaches for generating Counter Narratives (CNs) using the GPT-4o model. This design demonstrates that external factual sources, when systematically integrated into the generation pipeline, can effectively strengthen the truthfulness, depth, and rhetorical impact of CNs for low-resource language con- texts.
