Design accounting and variable names Phase 1a used a balanced factorial of 3 framings × 3 cognitive levels × 2 anchor­ ing states × 5 topics (N = 90, i.e., n = 30 per framing). Phase 1b comprised N = 30 prompts (n = 10 per taxonomy category). Variable names in the supplementary files are: prompt_id, phase (P1A/P1B), framing, cognitive_level, ethi­ cal_anchor, topic, prompt_text, model_version, temperature, gen­ erated_at_utc, output_text, acc_raterA, acc_raterB, clar_raterA, clar_raterB, eth_raterA, eth_raterB, acc_final, clar_final, eth_final, adjudication_notes. 2.3 LLM configuration and data capture All prompts were executed against OpenAI’s GPT-4-turbo (May 2025 version) via the API with a fixed temperature of 0.7, balancing expressive richness with reproducibil­ ity. Each request–response pair was programmatically logged with a unique identifier. No post-editing, filtering or paraphrasing was performed to preserve fidelity to model behaviour under the stated conditions [33]. Abbreviated exemplars of outputs are repro­ duced for inspection in Appendix C. 2.4 Evaluation framework and inter-rater reliability Outputs were scored with a three-dimensional rubric (0–3) that we developed by syn­ thesising established guidance: Financial Accuracy aligns with domain-validity criteria and holistic model assessments in finance; Conceptual Clarity operationalises instruc­ tional structure and learner-facing comprehensibility (e.g., [28, 31]); and Ethical Tone reflects responsible-AI considerations for education, including risk signalling and inclu­ sivity. The complete rubric with operational anchors is provided in Appendix A. Two domain expert reviewers independently rated all 120 responses. Rater A holds a PhD in Computer Science Education and a financial-education certification; Rater B holds a PhD in Accounting. Prior to scoring, raters completed two calibration rounds on 12 pilot items using the anchors in Appendix A; disagreements ≥1 point were adjudi­ cated to a final score. Inter-rater reliability was estimated with Cohen’s κ (unweighted) per dimension with 95% confidence intervals, interpreted using established thresholds for substantial to strong agreement; Cohen’s κ was preferred over ICCs because cate­ gorical agreement on the presence or absence of pedagogical features was the principal validity target. Additional representative scored examples are shown in Appendix C. Because scores are bounded and ordinal (0–3), we report medians and interquartile ranges (IQR). Two-group contrasts (e.g., Phase 1b vs. Phase 1a) use two-tailed Mann– Whitney U tests (α = .05) with effect sizes r = |Z| √ N , where N is the total number of observations entering the test. Three-group framing comparisons use Kruskal–Wallis H with Dunn–Bonferroni post-hoc tests. For anchored vs. neutral conditions we addi­ tionally report Cliff’s δ with 95% CIs. Tail-risk is operationalised as the proportion of outputs scoring {0, 1} and compared via chi-square tests of independence. Sensitivity checks using linearly weighted κ yielded the same substantive conclusions. Analysis decisions Page 6 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 Because scores are bounded ordinal (0–3), analyses report medians and interquartile ranges and use two-tailed Mann–Whitney U tests (α = .05) to compare Phase 1b with Phase 1a. Effect sizes are reported as r = |Z| √ N . We additionally report the proportion of low-quality outputs (scores 0–1) as a tail-risk measure. Sensitivity checks with linearly weighted κ yielded the same substantive conclusions. 2.5 Taxonomy derivation: qualitative audit trail The taxonomy was derived from Phase 1a via inductive thematic analysis with a docu­ mented audit trail to support dependability and confirmability. The procedure com­ prised: (i) familiarisation with high-scoring exemplars, (ii) initial coding for linguistic form, cognitive demand, and ethical anchoring, (iii) theme generation capturing instruc­ tional function, (iv) refinement against negative cases, and (v) definition and naming of three design principles: Guided Explanation (procedural scaffolding), Contextualised Inquiry (situated relevance), and Comparative Reasoning (higher-order evaluation). These principles were linked to Cognitive Load Theory and Situated Learning to artic­ ulate the hypothesised mechanisms of effect [19–21]. Illustrative prompt–output pairs that informed category definitions are provided in Appendix C. 2.6 Phase 1b validation To test instructional reliability (RQ2), the 30 taxonomy-guided prompts were gener­ ated under the same model configuration, then blind-scored with the established rubric. Comparative analyses contrasted Phase 1b scores against Phase 1a baselines to assess whether taxonomy instantiation yields consistent, higher-quality outputs across accu­ racy, clarity and ethical tone. This completes DBR Phase 1 by validating the artefact in controlled use and preparing for situated classroom testing in subsequent phases. The set of taxonomy-guided exemplars referenced in this phase is documented in Appen­ dix B, with abbreviated outputs in Appendix C. 3 Findings This section reports the empirical results from the 120–prompt study. We first analyse how prompt phrasing relates to output quality across the three rubric dimensions (RQ1), then evaluate whether taxonomy–guided prompts improve instructional reliability under matched conditions (RQ2). Scores use a 0–3 ordinal scale. Given bounded ratings, we report non–parametric tests with effect sizes, and we complement central tendency with variance and tail–risk analyses. Inter–rater agreement was substantial through­ out (Phase 1a: κ = .82; Phase 1b: κ = .84), with dimension–level coefficients remaining ≥.79. 3.1 RQ1: Design principles that link prompt framing to explanatory and ethical quality 3.1.1 Aggregate patterns and distributional characteristics Across the 90–prompt diagnostic corpus, median scores were Accuracy = 2.40 (IQR = 2.25–2.63), Clarity = 2.56 (2.33–2.78), and Ethical tone = 2.34 (2.13–2.63). Dis­ tributions exhibited a left tail of terse or context–insensitive responses, indicating a systematic influence of phrasing on pedagogical value rather than isolated anomalies. Quantile plots showed the largest spread between the 10th and 30th percentiles for Page 7 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 Clarity, suggesting that phrasing primarily modulates expository structure rather than headline correctness. 3.1.2 Prompt framing as cognitive scaffolding Procedural prompts outperformed interrogative prompts on both explanatory dimen­ sions. Median Clarity was 2.82 for procedural, 2.61 for declarative, and 2.31 for inter­ rogative prompts; Mann–Whitney tests showed significant differences between procedural and interrogative prompts for Clarity (U = 418, p < .001, r = .42) and Accu­ racy (U = 457, p < .01, r = .31). These effects are consistent with the interpretation that overt process cues act as external scaffolds, reducing extraneous processing and encour­ aging stepwise exposition. Table 3 summarises median scores by framing condition. Qualitative characteristics High–scoring procedural responses consistently exhibited: (i) labelled steps with brief rationales, (ii) boundary conditions and if–then forks that pre–empt common pitfalls, and (iii) light–weight heuristics, for example envelope rules or repayment thresholds. Interrogative prompts more often triggered list–like answers with thin connective tis­ sue, for example definitions without application, which aligns with the lower Clarity medians. 3.1.3 Ethical anchoring and the fairness–utility pattern Prompts with explicit ethical or situational anchors, for example “for a low–income student”, raised Ethical tone from 2.31 to 2.78 (Cliff’s δ = .46, p < .001). A mod­ est reduction in Accuracy was observed relative to neutral phrasing (2.48 to 2.33; δ = −.18, p = .04). Clarity was unchanged (2.55 to 2.59, n.s.). Paired–prompt inspec­ tion shows that anchored variants surfaced warnings about predatory products, volatil­ ity and repayment risk more frequently, sometimes substituting breadth for numerical precision. We characterise this as prompt–contingent ethical signalling, where respon­ sible tone improves with explicit cues, accompanied by small trade–offs in technical specificity. 3.1.4 Topic robustness and cognitive level The pattern held across all six topics (budgeting, credit, debt, savings, loans, insurance): procedural and declarative prompts consistently outperformed interrogatives, with the biggest gaps appearing on tasks that naturally require sequencing (for example, choosing between snowball and avalanche strategies) and the smallest on straightforward defini­ tions (for example, what contributes to a credit score). Prompts at higher cognitive levels (application, evaluation) benefitted most from procedural scaffolding, yielding the larg­ est clarity gains over interrogatives (median ∆= +0.42). Sensitivity checks confirmed no single domain drove the aggregate effects: Kruskal–Wallis on clarity across topics H(5) = 8.12, p = .15, η2 H = 0.03; accuracy H(5) = 6.47, p = .26, η2 H = 0.02; pair­ wise Dunn–Bonferroni contrasts did not survive correction. By contrast, framing effects Table 3 Phase 1a median scores by prompt framing (0–3 scale) Framing Accuracy Clarity Ethical tone Procedural 2.67 2.82 2.30 Declarative 2.56 2.61 2.40 Interrogative 2.24 2.31 2.00 Page 8 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 remained robust when we pooled over cognitive levels (Procedural vs. Interrogative, clarity: U = 418, p < .001, r = .42; accuracy: U = 457, p < .01, r = .31). 3.1.5 Calibration and rater stability Agreement between raters was stable across conditions and strongest for clar­ ity. The lowest agreement occurred for Ethical tone on neutral interrogatives, which improved after a brief rubric calibration, suggesting that explicit ethical cues reduce ambiguity by making cautionary language more visible. Overall reliabil­ ity was substantial: κAccuracy = 0.80 [0.74, , 0.86], κClarity = 0.83 [0.77, , 0.88], and κEthical = 0.82 [0.76, , 0.87] (unweighted, 95% CIs). Sensitivity checks using linearly weighted κ yielded the same substantive conclusions. 3.2 RQ2: Instructional reliability of taxonomy–guided prompting 3.2.1 Overall improvements with taxonomy instantiation Phase 1b used 30 prompts written to the validated design principles, Guided Explana­ tion, Contextualised Inquiry, and Comparative Reasoning. Relative to matched Phase 1a topics, medians increased by Accuracy +0.41 (to 2.81, U = 789, p < .001, r = .39), Clar­ ity +0.46 (to 2.92, U = 812, p < .001, r = .43), and Ethical tone +0.37 (to 2.71, U = 758, p < .001, r = .34). Table 4 presents the aggregate comparison. 3.2.2 Variance reduction and tail risk Taxonomy–guided prompting compressed the left tail. The proportion of responses with any 0–1 score fell from 18% in Phase 1a to 4% in Phase 1b (χ2 = 7.91, p = .005). Interquartile ranges narrowed for Clarity (from 0.45 to 0.29) and Ethical tone (from 0.50 to 0.32), indicating not only higher central tendency but also more predictable quality. For educators, lower tail–risk is critical because it reduces the likelihood that learners encounter misleading or insensitive outputs. 3.2.3 Differentiated effects by design principle When disaggregated, each principle exhibited a distinct functional signature. Guided Explanation produced the largest clarity gains, Contextualised Inquiry maximised ethi­ cal improvements, and Comparative Reasoning most improved accuracy for decision tasks involving trade–off analysis. Table 5 summarises the median improvements. These differentiated effects indicate that the taxonomy encodes complementary instructional functions rather than a single generic template. In practice, combining Table 4 Baseline versus taxonomy–guided prompts under matched topics (medians, 0–3 scale) Group Accuracy Clarity Ethical tone Baseline (Phase 1a) 2.40 2.46 2.34 Taxonomy–guided (Phase 1b) 2.81 2.92 2.71 Table 5 Differentiated gains by design principle (Phase 1b medians relative to matched Phase 1a) Design principle Accuracy Clarity Ethical tone Guided explanation +0.34 +0.51 +0.28 Contextualised inquiry +0.29 +0.33 +0.42 Comparative reasoning +0.38 +0.39 +0.30 Page 9 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 principles, for example a guided explanation constrained by a scenario, produced the most consistent gains. 3.2.4 Residual error profile and failure modes Residual weaknesses in Phase 1b clustered around three predictable areas: jurisdiction– specific regulation, lender–specific policy, and time–sensitive rates. We summarise their prevalence qualitatively in Table 6. None of the residual errors contradicted core finan­ cial concepts, rather they reflected missing local detail. This pattern motivates hybrid designs that combine prompt scaffolds with retrieval or verification layers for domain particulars. 3.2.5 Robustness and sensitivity checks We repeated Phase 1b analyses after excluding prompts in topics most sensitive to policy variance, the results were unchanged within rounding. Stratifying by cognitive level showed the largest taxonomy gains at application and evaluation, reinforcing the interpretation that explicit structure and context are most beneficial when reasoning demands increase. Re–scoring a 10% subsample after a one–week washout produced stable medians, suggesting minimal rater drift. Two claims are supported. First, prompts function as cognitive scaffolds that system­ atically shape explanatory and ethical quality. Procedural framing reduces extraneous processing and yields clearer, more accurate expositions, while ethical anchoring reliably improves responsible tone with small trade–offs in technical specificity. Second, instan­ tiating the validated taxonomy improves instructional reliability by increasing central tendency and reducing variance, resulting in fewer low–quality outliers across topics. The taxonomy, therefore, provides actionable design knowledge suitable for classroom trial in subsequent DBR phases, where effects on learner understanding, self, and deci­ sion quality can be tested under situated conditions. 4 Discussion This study presents a theory-informed account of prompt engineering as an instruc­ tional design approach for AI-mediated financial learning. Across a two–phase DBR programme, we demonstrated that specific prompt forms map to distinct learning func­ tions and that instantiating a structured taxonomy enhances clarity, accuracy, and ethi­ cal tone. Below, we interpret these results through the lens of learning theory, articulate the theoretical contribution as design knowledge, and outline implications for practice, platforms, and policy, along with the boundaries of transfer and the next steps in the DBR cycle. All inferences in this paper are based on model outputs generated under con­ trolled prompting. We evaluate the reliability, explanatory quality, and ethical tone of Table 6 Residual error profile in Phase 1b, qualitative summary Error type Typical manifestation Jurisdiction–specific regulation Ambiguous eligibility thresholds for benefits or relief pro­ grammes; generic compliance advice without local citations Lender–specific policy Over–generalised statements about underwriting and hard­ ship options where policy varies by lender or product Time–sensitive rates Illustrative APRs or savings rates framed generically rather than current ranges, suitable for pedagogy but not transactional use Page 10 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 AI-generated instructional text, not learner behaviour or performance. Learner-level effects are beyond the scope of this Phase 1 study and will be examined in planned Phase 2 classroom evaluations. 4.1 Prompt design as cognitive and situated scaffolding Cognitive orchestration via structure Procedural prompts yielded significantly higher explanatory quality than interroga­ tives, with robust gains in both clarity and accuracy. This pattern aligns with Cognitive Load Theory, which says that clear structure cuts unnecessary mental effort and helps the model produce explanations that support understanding [34, 35]. In practice, guided process cues worked like a simple design rule that prompted step-by-step reasoning, boundary conditions and quick rules of thumb, features linked to effective instructional explanations, rather than the shallow lists often produced by open questions. Situated relevance via context Ethical or situational anchoring improved responsible tone without degrading clar­ ity. While a modest accuracy trade-off was observed, we characterise this as a tractable design tension rather than a defect. The behaviour aligns with situated learning perspec­ tives and responsible AI guidance: specifying the audience and constraints encourages the model to surface contingencies, eligibility, and cautions that make outputs actionable and socially attuned. We term this mechanism prompt–contingent ethical signalling. Mechanism of the fairness-utility tension Our paired-prompt analysis suggests that ethical anchoring shifts the model’s stylistic priorities: anchored prompts increased hedge/disclaimer density (e.g., “may,” “consider,” “seek advice”), risk signposting, and eligibility contingencies, while slightly reduc­ ing numeric specificity and prescriptive language. In effect, the prompt’s social cues reweight the generator toward harm minimisation (situated cautions, boundary condi­ tions) over maximal technical detail. This aligns with Situated Learning (context makes knowledge actionable) and with safety-layer calibration in foundation models. Practi­ cally, combining Guided Explanation (to preserve concreteness) with Contextualised Inquiry (to ensure caution) balances the trade-off. Appendix C and SF2 provide verbatim contrasts. 4.2 Theoretical contribution: prompting as instructional design Our results extend work that reframes prompting as an instructional act rather than a purely technical input [36, 37]. We contribute a validated mapping from prompt forms to instructional functions: Guided Explanation operationalises procedural scaffolding, Contextualised Inquiry instantiates situated relevance, and Comparative Reasoning elic­ its contrastive analysis for decisions. These principles form reusable design guidance that goes beyond ad hoc tips to a theory-informed set of practices. By demonstrating that taxonomy instantiation lifts central tendency and compresses variance, we offer an evaluation lens of instructional reliability for AI in education. Reli­ ability here is evidenced by the significant reduction in the "left tail" of low-quality outli­ ers, reducing the likelihood that learners encounter misleading or insensitive outputs. Disaggregated effects support a mechanistic view: Guided Explanation drove the clarity gains, while Contextualised Inquiry drove the ethical improvements, indicating that the Page 11 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 taxonomy encodes complementary instructional functions rather than a single generic template. 4.3 Practical implementation for educators To bridge the gap between prompt engineering theory and classroom practice, we propose two concrete workflows for integrating the validated taxonomy into personal finance instruction. These workflows operationalise the three design principles (Guided Explanation, Contextualised Inquiry, Comparative Reasoning) and align with the scoring rubric in Appendix A. Effects are claimed at the content level. Workflow 1: Auditing and generating instructional content Instructors can use the taxonomy as a quality assurance framework when co-creating materials with AI. For high-stakes topics (such as debt management), educators should move beyond open-ended drafting (e.g., “Write a case study about debt”) to Contextual­ ised Inquiry: explicitly encode learner persona and financial constraints in the prompt so that generated case studies include necessary cautionary nuance (consistent with the reduction in low-quality outliers observed in our findings). When generating explana­ tory handouts, apply Guided Explanation scaffolds (request labelled steps, a worked example, a quick self-check, and boundary conditions) to minimise extraneous cogni­ tive load before content reaches the learner. Materials can be quickly audited against the rubric anchors for Accuracy, Clarity and Ethical Tone (Appendix A). Workflow 2: Scaffolding student inquiry (prompt literacy) The taxonomy also serves as a pedagogical tool for teaching students to interact with AI responsibly. A Compare-and-critique activity invites students to generate responses using a naïve interrogative (e.g., What is a loan?”) and a taxonomy-guided prompt (e.g., Compare fixed vs. variable-rate loans for a student budget; include a decision rule and cautions”). This makes comparative reasoning visible and helps novices recognise that the quality of advice depends on how the inquiry is structured. Providing taxonomy- based templates enables the transition from passive consumption to active, critical ques­ tioning. Students annotate their outputs using the rubric (Appendix A) to justify their judgments of Accuracy, Clarity, and Ethical Tone. Lightweight assessment alignment For transparency and ease of marking, adopt a low-stakes mini-assignment: students submit the prompt + output + 150-word reflection mapping features to the rubric and noting any items they would independently verify (e.g., jurisdictional rules). This aligns evaluation with content quality and normalises responsible AI use without inferring learner outcome effects. 4.4 Actionable design principles for practice Grounded in the evidence and aligned with established pedagogy, we distil four prin­ ciples for educators. 1. Lead with structure for reasoning tasks. Use guided, stepwise imperatives that name the goal, steps and checks. Reserve open interrogatives for narrow definitional queries. 2. Situate the learner. Specify audience, constraints and risks. Ethical anchoring reliably increases inclusive, cautionary framing without reducing clarity. 3. Elicit comparison when choices matter. Ask for alternatives, trade-offs, and dominance conditions to strengthen decision-quality explanations. Page 12 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 4. Constrain format lightly. Request headings, bullets, or numbered steps to improve clarity, while avoiding over-specification that might remove useful detail (Table 7). These principles can be embedded in teacher-facing templates and student prompt-lit­ eracy activities, or surfaced as a simple prompting wizard in the LMS with responsible defaults (structure, context, comparison, and a brief caution clause). 4.5 Implications for platform design and policy At the system level, the principles can be surfaced as taxonomy-aware prompting wiz­ ards (mirroring Sect. 4.3) that nudge users to add structure, context and comparison before generation, encoding responsible defaults. Remaining error types in Phase 1b include jurisdiction-specific rules, lender policies, and time-sensitive rates. These find­ ings support a hybrid design that pairs the wizard with light retrieval or verification for local details. At an institutional level, acceptable-use guidance can emphasise good prompt design, clear source tracing, and when to escalate to a human adviser, in line with policy discussions about the safe use of automation in education and aligned with the rubric in Appendix A for auditing content quality. 4.6 Boundaries of transfer and generalisability The taxonomy was validated on personal finance topics using a single frontier model. Because the mechanisms are grounded in general learning theory, transfer to adjacent high-stakes domains requiring process explanation, contextualisation and comparison is plausible. Nevertheless, model families differ in calibration and stylistic defaults, and some tasks hinge on up-to-date or local knowledge, so platform uptake should include retrieval-augmented variants of the wizard and re-audit with the same rubric anchors (Appendix A) to define boundary conditions. 4.7 Next steps in the DBR cycle Phase 2 will move from controlled validation to situated testing with learners, in line with DBR guidance. We envisage two complementary interventions: (i) a teacher-facing authoring assistant that implements the two workflows from Sect. 4.3 and generates tax­ onomy-conformant prompts plus diagnostics; (ii) a student-facing scaffold that coaches prompt literacy with planned fading over time. Outcomes will include scenario-based decision-quality rubrics, self-regulation and calibrated-trust scales, and prompt-literacy transfer tasks. Process instrumentation will capture prompt drafts, revision traces and Table 7 Classroom integration matrix: Mapping prompt taxonomy to teaching activities Taxonomy principle Pedagogical function Instructor workflow (Content generation) Student activity (Prompt literacy) Guided Explanation (Procedural scaffolding) Reduces cognitive load by structuring complex processes into steps Generate Handouts: Request labelled steps and self-checks to ensure clarity Gap Analysis: Students compare a raw AI definition vs. a guided explanation to identify missing steps Contextualised Inquiry (Situated relevance) Increases ethical safety by surfacing risks relevant to a specific persona Audit Case Studies: Explicitly encode learner constraints to verify the AI includes cautions Persona Rewriting: Students rewrite generic prompts to include specific constraints and observe the shift in tone Comparative Reasoning (Evaluative judgement) Supports decision- making by forcing trade-off analysis Decision Support: Use prompts that demand dominance con­ ditions rather than static lists Compare & Critique: Stu­ dents generate two outputs (Naive vs. Comparative) and score them using the rubric Page 13 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 brief think-alouds to model internalisation and scaffold fading. All inferences in this paper are at the level of model outputs under controlled prompting; learner-level effects are deferred to Phase 2. The study repositions prompt engineering from a technical convenience to a high- leverage pedagogical intervention. By linking prompt forms to learning functions and demonstrating improvements in clarity, accuracy and ethical tone, we contribute a vali­ dated taxonomy and a reliability lens for AI–mediated instruction. These outcomes pro­ vide concrete guidance for educators and platform builders and establish a programme of research for Phase 2 classroom trials. 5 Limitations This Phase 1 study provides controlled evidence for taxonomy–guided prompting, yet several limitations bound interpretation and transfer. First, all generations were pro­ duced with a single proprietary model and configuration (GPT-4-turbo, May 2025; fixed temperature). This choice reduces architectural confounds but constrains external validity: model families differ in calibration, style and safety layers, and can change via non-transparent updates over time; replication on alternative models and across model versions is required to assess robustness and temporal stability . Second, the dataset comprises synthetic outputs rather than situated human–AI interactions. While this iso­ lates prompt-structure effects, it does not capture learner behaviours such as prompt iteration, self-regulation, or calibrated trust in advice; these dynamics should be exam­ ined in Phase 2 classroom studies. Third, scoring relied on a three-dimension rubric applied by two expert reviewers. Although inter-rater agreement was substantial, rubric application inevitably involves professional judgement, and the 0–3 ordinal scale limits granularity. Broadening the reviewer pool, adding moderation procedures, and incorporating complementary auto­ mated checks (e.g., retrieval-based fact verification) would strengthen reliability and validity. Fourth, topic coverage focused on six personal finance domains and on English prompts; generalisability to other subdomains, languages and culturally specific finan­ cial practices remains to be tested. Fifth, we evaluated single responses per prompt under a fixed decoding setting; we did not explore multi-sample aggregation, temperature sweeps or consensus prompting, which can affect stability and tail risk. Sixth, we did not integrate retrieval or jurisdic­ tion-aware guards, so residual errors clustered around lender policies, local regulation and time-sensitive rates; hybrid designs that combine scaffolds with verification are needed for deployment in high-stakes contexts. Finally, unknown training-data prove­ nance and potential contamination (e.g., exposure to public financial advice) cannot be ruled out, a general limitation of current foundation models that motivates triangulation with transparent, auditable components. 6 Conclusion and DBR trajectory This DBR Phase 1 study makes two contributions to AI in education. First, it demon­ strates that prompt design is not a trivial parameter but a high-leverage pedagogical determinant of LLM behaviour. Empirically, procedural framing functions as an exter­ nal cognitive scaffold, yielding clearer and more accurate expositions, while contextual­ ised ethical anchoring improves responsible tone with only minor trade-offs in technical Page 14 of 17 Aruleba et al. Discover Education (2026) 5:125 specificity. Second, the study introduces and validates a structured taxonomy of prompt types that, when systematically applied, enhances instructional reliability by raising overall quality and reducing the likelihood of low-quality, high-risk outputs. The validation of this taxonomy as a set of design principles fulfils the goal of DBR Phase 1. Practically, the principles can be embedded in teacher authoring templates and student prompt-literacy activities, and surfaced in platform “prompting wizards” with light verification for jurisdiction-specific facts. Conceptually, the results reinforce that LLMs are shaped as much by the pedagogical intent encoded in prompts as by their underlying algorithms. These content-level gains constitute DBR Phase 1 artefact vali­ dation; Phase 2 will evaluate impacts on understanding, decision quality, and self-reg­ ulation in authentic courses, and Phase 3 will operationalise taxonomy-aware tooling at scale, paired with retrieval or verification for local details. Future work DBR Phase 2 will embed the taxonomy in authentic learning environments and evalu­ ate effects on learner outcomes. Primary targets include scenario-based financial deci­ sion quality, self-regulation, and calibrated trust in AI advice, complemented by process data (prompt drafts, edit traces, brief think-alouds) to study internalisation and scaf­ fold fading. Replication across model families and retrieval-augmented settings will test robustness and boundary conditions. The programme aim is to advance from validated design principles to classroom-proven instructional knowledge that supports deeper, more equitable learning. title: RAGMail: a cloud-based retrieval-
file_name: RAGMail-a-cloudbased-retrievalaugmented-framework-for-reducing-hallucinations-in-LLM-text-generation_2026_Nature-Research.pdf
text: RAGMail: a cloud-based retrieval- augmented framework for reducing hallucinations in LLM text generation Priyodip Sanyal, Kumud Rathore & R. Vijaya Arjunan Cold emailing is used to personalize, target emails for outreach without prior contact. Automating this personalized cold email generation process can significantly improve outreach efficiency for job seekers, particularly in competitive industries. It streamlines the process of composition, saves time and increases engagement, tailored to a specific industry or role. In today’s competitive market, where job application is made easy, such a tool scales communication and boosts the conversion rate. The cold email generator. RAGMail, is an intelligent cold email generator that is cloud-integrated and uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to reduce hallucinations. The cloud-native infrastructure on which the system is built makes use of services including managed Large Language Model (LLMs) APIs, scalable vector databases, and object storage. With real-time document retrieval and cloud- hosted, metadata-aware templates, RAGMail guarantees high personalization accuracy and factual foundation. This cloud-native architecture provides elastic scalability, low-latency inference, and real-time personalization at scale, all while protecting data and user privacy with role-based access control and encrypted storage. Beyond job applications, the approach can be applied to a wide range of outreach sectors, including sales, academia, and commercial relationships, where factual accuracy and context sensitivity are critical. The system ensures high availability and load balancing during peak demand periods by utilizing distributed cloud resources. The models exhibit open-domain conversational capabilities, generalize effectively to scenarios beyond the trained data, and as verified by human evaluations, substantially reduce the well-known problem of knowledge hallucination in state-of-the-art chatbots. The proposed framework offers a scalable and reliable solution for generating contextually grounded, high-quality cold emails using Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Keywords AI personalization, Cold email generation, Cloud native architecture, Large language models, Factualness evaluation, Weighting LLM, Natural language processing, Generative AI Cold emailing serves as a critical strategy for candidates seeking to enhance their visibility to recruiters in a competitive job market. Crafting concise, well-structured messages that present tailored value propositions allows candidates to differentiate themselves effectively. Personalized emails enable applicants to demonstrate genuine interest and alignment with the recruiter’s needs, fostering a sense of empathy and relevance. Such targeted communication increases the likelihood of engagement by making the recipient feel acknowledged in their specific context and professional challenges1,2. This tailored strategy is essential for engaging recipients, promoting interaction, and ultimately facilitating desired outcomes like conversion2. Personalization is a fundamental aspect of automated email generation, serving both functional and strategic purposes. It enhances the uniqueness of each message, thereby reducing the likelihood of classification as spam, while simultaneously signalling to the recipient that the content is specifically tailored to their needs. Incorporating publicly available personalization elements—such as the recipient’s role, company, or recent activity—further strengthens the relevance and effectiveness of the out3. Cold emails, when crafted effectively, can open doors to valuable job opportunities. However, writing such emails manually requires significant time and effort and may still lack relevance if the candidate fails to align their message with the specific job requirements. Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal, India. email: vijay.arjun@manipal.edu OPEN Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:7925 1 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38913-w www.nature.com/scientificreports Large Language Models (LLMs) are sophisticated Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems that can comprehend and produce language that is like that of humans to carry out challenging jobs. They can perform almost human-like tasks like summarizing and translating thanks to advancements in training and architecture. LLMs are effective tools for automating jobs in a variety of industries, including data analysis, customer service, and content generation, thanks to these features3. Emails generated by traditional LLMs often suffer from a common drawback: hallucination. This refers to the generation of incorrect or fabricated information that does not exist in the source data. Such inaccuracies can damage a candidate’s credibility and reduce the effectiveness of their outreach. To overcome hallucinations in LLM-generated content, the proposed study employs a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework, which combines the strengths of parametric and non-parametric memory4,5. This approach enables the system to dynamically retrieve semantically relevant job-related information such as required skills, qualifications, and responsibilities from external sources before generating the cold email. By grounding the generation process in factual and context-rich data, RAG significantly enhances the accuracy and specificity of the output6. Unlike conventional LLM pipelines that rely solely on internal training knowledge, this method ensures that the generated emails are closely aligned real-world job postings7. Cloud computing enables on-demand access to shared IT resources like servers, storage, and applications, typically managed by third-party providers. It supports scalable, virtual, and distributed systems, offering cost-efficiency through pay-per-use models. Recognized as a top disruptive technology, major companies like Google and Amazon are investing in global data centers to ensure reliability. Cloud computing is widely adopted for its flexibility and fast deployment, with ongoing research exploring its history, models, characteristics, opportunities, and challenges. XaaS (Anything or Everything as a Service) refers to the broad and evolving category of services delivered over the internet. It encompasses models such as Software as a Service (SaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), among others. These services offer scalable, on-demand access to computing resources, platforms, applications, storage, and even AI tools, minimizing the need for physical infrastructure and reducing operational costs. XaaS plays a critical role in enabling digital transformation, enhancing agility, and supporting innovation in cloud-based environments8. A recently introduced technique for open-domain question answering is the neural retrieval-in-the-loop method known as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) which has demonstrated significant effectiveness in generating factually accurate and contextually relevant responses by grounding the output in retrieved external knowledge9. Knowledge hallucination in conversational agents arises irrespective of language or model size or train data. RAG seems like a promising solution as it reduces hallucination by generalizing beyond the training data on unseen distribution9. Nearest neighbor language models are highly dependent on the similarity of context distribution between training and test dataset for interpolating prediction. This approach is effective only for knowledge, names, and near duplicate sentences10. Figure 1 represents pre-trained neural language models that have demonstrated a remarkable capacity to assimilate a considerable amount of intricate knowledge from datasets. They achieve this without reliance on external memory, instead functioning as a parameterized implicit knowledge repository. Although this advancement is notable, such models come with their limitations: they are unable to easily augment or modify their memory, do not readily elucidate the rationale behind their predictions, and may generate “hallucinations” that could mislead users or disseminate misinformation, thereby raising significant concerns regarding their reliability and safety in critical applications11. A persistent issue in LLMs is hallucination, where models generate plausible but incorrect or fabricated information. Hallucinations can undermine the credibility of AI systems, particularly in high-stakes scenarios. Research classifies hallucinations into intrinsic and extrinsic types. Intrinsic hallucinations occur when the model internally generates inconsistencies, often due to errors in reasoning or misinterpretation of learned patterns. Extrinsic hallucinations, on the other hand, arise when the model produces outputs that are factually incorrect concerning the external world, often due to limitations in the training data or retrieval mechanisms1. A hybrid approach that combines Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Factualness Evaluation via Weighting LLMs (FEWL). The core motivation behind this system is to support job seekers in crafting personalized and context-aware outreach messages to recruiters, particularly in competitive hiring environments where a generic or misaligned email may lead to being overlooked6. Cloud computing encompasses both the applications delivered as services over the Internet and the underlying hardware and system software within data centres that enable those services. These services are commonly categorized as Software as a Service (SaaS), while other models include Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) and Platform as a Service (PaaS). The growing demand for cloud computing is driven by its numerous benefits Fig. 1. Language models considered in this study. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:7925 2 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38913-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ to individuals, consumers, and businesses, such as cost reduction, simplified access, minimal management overhead, free service offerings, and improved reliability12. Cloud-based services are particularly advantageous for businesses with fluctuating or growing bandwidth needs, as they allow easy scalability by utilizing remote server resources. The proposed system extracts job descriptions from career portals and candidate information from resumes, aligning them to create tailored emails. Unlike traditional text generation methods, our approach integrates FEWL to assess the factual correctness of generated content by assigning weights to different aspects such as skills, experience, and job relevance. By implementing this mechanism, we mitigate the risk of hallucinations in AI-generated emails, ensuring credibility and personalization. Experimental results demonstrate that emails generated with factual evaluation outperform generic AI-generated emails in accuracy and recruiter engagement, thereby improving response rates. Related work Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) has become increasingly popular in recent years. Several content creation tools have been meticulously designed to produce diverse outputs13. Large language models (LLMs) are transformer-based neural network architectures that have been pre-trained with language models as loss functions using many model parameters and vast amount of test data14. LangChain, a modular framework for integrating Large Language Models with diverse data sources, streamlines the development of intelligent applications and accelerates innovation in AI-driven solutions15. Retrieval augmentation is a common method for using a Transformer encoder with a large context, but only some of it is relevant. With this approach, a retrieval system locates and chooses a portion of the context to be included in the final encoding that the decoder handles12. RAG models achieve state-of-the-art outcomes in open-domain quality assurance. People prefer RAG generation over parametric BART due to its factual and specific nature16. Retrieval augmented text generation relies heavily on retrieval quality, which refers to the similarity between the query and retrieved examples17. In-Context RALM demonstrates that Retrieval-Augmented Generation using frozen large language models significantly boosts performance in open-domain question answering by enhancing factuality and relevance without additional fine-tuning18. Bidirectional encoding is used in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems to generate semantically rich vector representations of both queries and documents. Models like BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) and RoBERTa (Robustly Optimized BERT Approach), which are based on bidirectional Transformers, analyze the full context of a sentence—looking both left and right of each word— allowing for deeper understanding of meaning. In RAG, these encoders embed documents and user queries into dense vectors, which are then compared using similarity measures (e.g., cosine similarity) to retrieve the most relevant context. This improves retrieval accuracy and ensures that the retrieved passages align better with the user’s intent, ultimately enhancing factuality and reducing hallucinations in the generated output19. Efforts to mitigate hallucinations in LLMs focus on several key strategies. First, improving retrieval mechanisms by incorporating high-quality, domain-specific databases helps ensure that relevant and accurate information is supplied to the model. Second, refining prompt engineering techniques, such as explicitly directing the model to verify its sources, has been shown to enhance factual consistency. Additionally, filtering retrieved documents through verification layers can prevent the model from incorporating unreliable information. Studies also suggest the effectiveness of confidence scoring and uncertainty quantification, where models indicate the reliability of their generated responses, allowing users to assess the trustworthiness of outputs20. Further, fine- tuning models with human feedback and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has been demonstrated to reduce hallucination rates by aligning generated content with human expectations and factual accuracy21. Future work should focus on adaptive learning approaches that dynamically adjust retrieval strategies based on context and model confidence levels. By improving these components, the reliability of RAG in minimizing hallucinations can be further enhanced, contributing to the broader goal of developing more trustworthy AI- driven applications22. Query-based RAG augments the user query with retrieved information to form a unified prompt for the generator, enhancing relevance and factuality across modalities without additional model training. While large language models (LLMs) may hallucinate, they retain substantial factual knowledge from pretraining, often producing truthful outputs when multiple responses are compared for the same prompt4,22.Query-based RAG, often paired with LLM generators, offers modular flexibility, allowing swift integration of pre-trained components for quick deployment. Prompt design is crucial for utilizing retrieved data within this setup23. Llama models, trained on public data, achieve competitive performance with state-of-the-art models like GPT-3 and PaLM, demonstrating that open-access training can yield high-performing large language models24. Older GPT versions are less effective in modern RAG setups due to lower contextual capacity, higher hallucination risk, weak instruction alignment, and poor integration support. Newer models not only reduce hallucinations but also enable modular, prompt-driven, and real-time knowledge-enhanced generation, making them ideal for applications like RAGMail or cold email generation. A low-cost approach to analyzing model behaviour is prompt-based hallucination induction, which produces only slight gains over fine-tuning-based methods. Recent research has shown that, while prompts can elicit fabricated responses for contrastive training, fine-tuned models achieve significantly greater gains in reducing hallucinations25. Self-contradiction, a form of hallucination where large language models generate conflicting statements in the same context, has been shown to affect over 17% of ChatGPT outputs. Recent work introduces a prompt- based black-box framework for detecting and mitigating such contradictions without relying on retrieval. This Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:7925 3 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38913-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ method complements RAG, as many contradictions cannot be verified through external sources, and achieves high F1 scores in detection while preserving fluency26. Knowledge-graph-driven summarization and integrating Natural Language Processing techniques such as word segmentation, part-of-speech tagging, and Named Entity Recognition (NER) to construct knowledge graphs from Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) text data, RAGMail employs a similar strategy to enhance factual accuracy in cold email generation. In their work, semantic representations obtained via Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) were used to guide a Pointer-Generator Network (PGN) PGN-based summarizer, enabling efficient multi-document summarization directly on edge devices, thereby reducing inference latency and minimizing dependence on centralized servers. Inspired by this, RAGMail introduces a retrieval-augmented email generator grounded in a lightweight domain-specific knowledge graph that captures entities such as organizations, roles, and recent events relevant to the target audience. This hybrid model can generate new text while also preserving exact factual details from the source — which is crucial for avoiding hallucination and maintaining specificity The graph-structured context is encoded and injected into a retrieval-augmented decoder to maintain factual consistency and reduce hallucination, particularly in templated sections like greetings, value propositions, and calls to action. Furthermore, by enabling client-side inference, RAGMail preserves user privacy while delivering rapid and reliable email generation, demonstrating improved factual grounding compared to standard RAG methods27. One promising area of research is fine-tuning LLMs for domain-specific tasks within the RAG framework. LLMs, although powerful, can exhibit biases or knowledge gaps when applied to specific domains This targeted fine-tuning can significantly enhance data extraction accuracy, particularly in specialized applications like e-commerce product scraping. Researchers have proposed similar approaches, demonstrating that tailored LLMs can enhance extraction quality and foster the development of specialized RAG models for web scraping tasks. Existing research on cloud application cost optimization emphasizes the significance of early-stage, model-driven decision-making for efficient service deployment, particularly from the application provider’s perspective where traditional reactive cost-control methods often fall short. One notable approach proposes a cloud application context metamodel, which encapsulates infrastructure and service-specific parameters that influence both deployment cost and Quality of Service (QoS). By simulating various deployment configurations, this framework enables the selection of optimal strategies that balance cost-efficiency and service reliability28. RAGMail introduces a lightweight pre-deployment modeling layer tailored for retrieval-augmented text generation. Rather than treating generation as agnostic to its runtime context, RAGMail conditions both its retriever and decoder modules on cloud deployment parameters, including infrastructure constraints, SLA- inspired quality thresholds, and resource allocation policies. This deployment-aware conditioning enhances factual alignment and reduces hallucination rates, especially in cost-sensitive use cases like cold outreach automation. Recent efforts to address hallucinations in LLMs include FActScore29 for atomic factuality evaluation, FELM30 for holistic factuality benchmarking, and SelfCheckGPT31 for hallucination detection without ground- truth

title: IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NEURAL NETWORKS AND LEARNING SYSTEMS, VOL. 36, NO. 12, DECEMBER 2025
file_name: REaMA Building Biomedical Relation Extraction Specialized Large Language Models Through Instruction Tuning.pdf
text: 20258 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NEURAL NETWORKS AND LEARNING SYSTEMS, VOL. 36, NO. 12, DECEMBER 2025 REaMA: Building Biomedical Relation Extraction Specialized Large Language Models Through Instruction Tuning Yidan Zhang , Junlin Yu, Guobo Li , Zhenan He , Member, IEEE, and Gary G. Yen , Fellow, IEEE Abstract—Aiming to identify entity pairs with biomedical semantic relations and assign speciﬁc relation types, biomedical relation extraction (BioRE) plays a critical role in biomedical text mining and information extraction (IE). Recent studies indicate that general large language models (LLMs) have made some breakthroughs in general relation extraction (RE) tasks. However, even the advanced open-source LLMs struggle with BioRE tasks. For example, WizardLM-70B and LLaMA-2-70B achieve F-scores of 14.05 and 12.21 on the BioRED dataset, respectively, signiﬁcantly lagging behind the state-of-the-art (SOTA) method which scores 65.17. To address this gap, a multitask instruction-tuning framework is proposed, which can transform general LLMs into BioRE-specialized models with our meticulously curated instruction dataset, REInstruct, comprising 150000 diverse and quality instruction-response pairs. Conse- quently, we introduce REaMA, a series of open-source LLMs with sizes of 7B and 13B speciﬁcally tailored for BioRE tasks. Exper- imental results on seven representative BioRE datasets show that both REaMA-2-7B and REaMA-2-13B acquire promising performance on all datasets. Remarkably, the larger REaMA- 2-13B outperforms the current SOTA method on ﬁve out of seven datasets. The result exhibits the eﬀectiveness of instruction- tuning on REInstruct in eliciting strong RE capabilities in LLMs. Furthermore, we show that incorporating chain of thought (CoT) into REInstruct can further enhance the generalization ability of REaMA. The project is available at https://github.com/stzpp/ REaMA Index Terms—Biomedical relation extraction (BioRE), chain of thought (CoT), instruction tuning (IT), large language models (LLMs). I. INTRODUCTION E XTRACTING relationships between biomedical entities from the literature is critical for constructing knowl- edge graphs, identifying biomarkers, drug discovery and repurposing, and clinical decision support. For example, researchers employed advanced relation extraction (RE) algo- rithms to mine crucial association information from the Received 12 September 2024; revised 19 April 2025 and 24 July 2025; accepted 31 July 2025. Date of publication 20 August 2025; date of cur- rent version 3 December 2025. This work was supported in part by the National Key Research and Development Program of China under Grant 2023YFF1204901 and in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant NSF 62422604. (Corresponding author: Zhenan He.) Yidan Zhang, Zhenan He, and Gary G. Yen are with the College of Computer Science, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China (e-mail: zhenan@scu.edu.cn). Junlin Yu and Guobo Li are with the Department of Medicinal Chemistry, West China School of Pharmacy, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China. This article has supplementary downloadable material available at https://doi.org/10.1109/TNNLS.2025.3596257, provided by the authors. Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TNNLS.2025.3596257 COVID-19 Open Research Dataset [1], such as viral-host interactions, drug-symptom correlations, and the eﬃcacy of various treatments. This not only accelerated the understanding of COVID-19 [2], but also directly informed the design of clinical trials and facilitated the development of multiple vac- cines. However, with the quantity and diversity of biomedical literature increasing signiﬁcantly, relying solely on human eﬀorts to extract relations from vast information is clearly impractical. Therefore, it is essential to prompt deep learning (DL) methods to achieve automated and intelligent information extraction (IE) and analysis, thus advancing the development of artiﬁcial intelligence (AI) for science. BioRE focuses on identifying and extracting semantic rela- tions for biomedical entity pairs frequently discussed, e.g., <chemical, disease>, <chemical, gene>, and <disease, gene>. BioRE eﬀectively transforms unstructured text into structured knowledge, which can be roughly divided into sentence- level, document-level, and cross-sentence. Sentence-level RE identiﬁes and classiﬁes the relation for a single entity pair within one sentence. For instance, given the sentence “BRCA1 gene mutation increases the risk of breast cancer,” the task is to determine the relationship between “BRCA1” and “breast cancer” from the possible relation types “Association” and “None,” with the correct classiﬁcation being “Association.” Document-level RE tasks handle relationships between entity pairs in a document, without restricting the relationships to appear only within a single sentence or limiting the number of relations. For example, extract the relationships between all entities from a document describing DNA repair and the BRCA1 gene. In practice, relationships may involve multiple entities and span across multiple sentences. Cross-sentence RE tasks are geared toward extracting n-ary relations that span across multiple sentences, where n is greater than two. For example, the task is to identify the relation among “EGFR,” “L858R,” and “geﬁtinib” from the possible relation types including “None, Resistance, Response, Resistance or Non- response, and Sensitivity,” within the context of the following sentences “The deletion mutation on exon-19 of EGFR gene was present in 16 patients, while the L858R point mutation on exon-21 was noted in 10” and “All patients were treated with geﬁtinib and showed a partial response.” The correct classiﬁcation in this context is “Sensitivity.” By continued pretraining [3], [4] or training from scratch [5], [6] on the BERT architecture, biomedical pretrained 2162-237X © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artiﬁcial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. ZHANG et al.: REaMA: BUILDING BioRE SPECIALIZED LLMs THROUGH INSTRUCTION TUNING 20259 language models (PLMs) have exhibited strong capabilities in a variety of biomedical tasks including sentence similarity, RE, and named entity recognition (NER). The existing dominant approach for BioRE tasks is to adapt these PLMs via ﬁne- tuning on downstream tasks [3], [7]. However, the small scale of these PLMs has severely limited their capacity to capture complex language patterns and contextual information. Also, they are primarily trained in biomedical literature (e.g., PubMed [8] and PMC [9]), resulting in a narrower scope of language phenomena and knowledge. Finally, a key limitation lies in their targeted optimization for individual biomedical tasks, which restricts their ability to generalize and excel in multitasking scenarios, highlighting a crucial shortfall in their adaptability. With the launch of the GPT series of models (GPT-3 [10] and GPT-4 [11]) by openAI, AI has entered the era of LLMs, which is a special class of PLMs obtained by scaling model size, pretraining corpus, and computation. In recent years, generative general LLMs have exhibited breakthrough performance in various natural language processing (NLP) tasks, including translation, reasoning, and code generation [11], [12]. Unlike previous PLMs, generative LLMs utilize in- context learning (ICL) to adapt to completely diﬀerent tasks without parameter updating [10]. Due to their extraordinary capabilities in text understanding, generation, and general- ization, generative LLMs have been adopted to solve RE tasks in the general domain and achieved promising perfor- mance [13]. For example, QA4RE [14] aligns the prompt of the RE task with that of the question-answering task. It brings signiﬁcant gains over the standard RE prompt on four general RE datasets. Also, GPT-RE [15] incorpo- rates task-aware representations in demonstration retrieval and enriches the demonstrations with gold label-induced reasoning logic, achieving competitive performances on four general RE datasets. Both models boost performance in RE tasks by crafting more eﬃcient prompting strategies without parameter updating. Nevertheless, the scope of knowledge that can be integrated via prompting without parameter updating is lim- ited. Consequently, these approaches generally excel in general RE tasks that feature straightforward input sequences, fewer domain-speciﬁc terms, and minimal dependence on specialized knowledge. However, the advanced general open-source LLMs, e.g., WizardLM-70B [16] and LLaMA-2-70B [17], have lagged far behind the prior state-of-the-art (SOTA) approach on BioRE datasets (e.g., BioRED [18] and DrugProt [19]). As illustrated in Fig. 1, WizardLM-70B achieves an F-score of 14.05 and 35.58 on the BioRED and DrugProt datasets, which is 51.12 and 44.15 lower than that of the SOTA performance, respectively. Moreover, even the most advanced general closed-source LLM, GPT-4 [11], performs slightly worse than the SOTA method on DrugProt (75.12 versus 79.73). BioRE tasks are inherently challenging due to their high data complexity, domain speciﬁcity, and varied task complexity. Speciﬁcally, these tasks involve diverse sources such as academic papers, clinical records, and electronic health records, each with unique formats. The use of specialized terms and context-dependent information requires signiﬁcant Fig. 1. Comparison of F-scores for diﬀerent models on the BioRED and DrugProt datasets. F-score refers to the Micro-F1 score, which measures overall model accuracy in multiclass by averaging precision and recall across all classes. domain knowledge. BioRE tasks range from simple binary to complex N-ary relations and can be performed at both sentence and document levels. Other challenges, such as data sparsity, imbalance, semantic ambiguity, synonymy, and implicit relationships, further complicate the tasks. These challenges highlight the limitations of general LLMs, which 1) lack the domain-speciﬁc knowledge required to interpret and extract relations within complex biomedical texts, 2) are not adequately trained to process biomedical texts that are rich in specialized terms and abbreviations, 3) have diﬃculty in understanding and distinguishing subtle semantic diﬀerences, leading to insuﬃcient precision when handling ﬁne-grained relations, and 4) lack the sophisticated reasoning needed to infer implicit relations through complex, indirect statements. Instruction tuning (IT) [20] is an eﬀective method for elicit- ing the powerful capabilities of LLMs [14], [21]. To illustrate, consider the task of making a model to generate unique recipes based on user p

title: Received 3 October 2024, accepted 23 October 2024, date of publication 28 October 2024, date of current version 9 December 2024.
file_name: Recent Advances in Interactive Machine Translation With Large Language Models.pdf
text: Received 3 October 2024, accepted 23 October 2024, date of publication 28 October 2024, date of current version 9 December 2024. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2024.3487352 Recent Advances in Interactive Machine Translation With Large Language Models YANSHU WANG1, JINYI ZHANG 2,3, (Member, IEEE), TIANRONG SHI2, DASHUAI DENG2, YE TIAN 4, (Member, IEEE), AND TADAHIRO MATSUMOTO 3 1Art and Design College, Shenyang Ligong University, Shenyang 110159, China 2School of Information Science and Engineering, Shenyang Ligong University, Shenyang 110159, China 3Faculty of Engineering, Gifu University, Gifu 501-1193, Japan 4Zhuzhou CRRC Times Electric Company Ltd., Zhuzhou 412001, China Corresponding author: Jinyi Zhang (zhangjinyi@sylu.edu.cn) This work was supported in part by the Basic Research Projects in Liaoning Provincial Undergraduate Universities under Grant SYLUGXTD07, in part by the General Young Talents Project for Scientific Research Grant of the Educational Department of Liaoning Province under Grant LJKQR20222512, and in part by the Research Support Program for Inviting High-Level Talents Grant of Shenyang Ligong University under Grant 1010147001004. The work of Jinyi Zhang was supported by China Scholarship Council under Grant 202208210120. ABSTRACT This paper explores the role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in revolutionizing interactive Machine Translation (MT), providing a comprehensive analysis across nine innovative research directions. LLMs demonstrate exceptional capabilities in handling complex tasks through advanced text generation and interactive human-machine collaboration, significantly enhancing translation accuracy and efficiency, especially in low-resource language scenarios. This study also outlines potential advancements in LLM applications, emphasizing the integration of domain-specific knowledge and the exploration of model combinations to optimize performance. Future research is suggested to focus on enhancing model adaptability to diverse linguistic environments and refining human-machine interaction frameworks to better serve practical translation needs. The findings contribute to the ongoing discourse on the strategic deployment of MT with LLMs, aiming to direct future developments towards more robust and nuanced language processing solutions. INDEX TERMS Machine translation, large language models, pre-trained language model, in-context learning, post-editing. I. INTRODUCTION Machine translation (MT) is not only a significant branch of artificial intelligence but also one of the most effective means to overcome language barriers and a barometer for AI technological innovation. Neural Machine Translation (NMT) based on the Transformer architecture has achieved good translation quality across various language pairs [1], [2], [3], [4]. Building on this, Large Language Models (LLMs) have exhibited outstanding performance in numerous natural language processing (NLP) tasks, establishing a new paradigm in MT research with significant research value and industrial prospects. Furthermore, MT with LLMs have an The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Daniel Augusto Ribeiro Chaves . interactive mode that combines human editing, which can further enhance translation quality and efficiency [5], [6]. The evolution of LLM technology has been progres- sive, evolving from early statistical language models to today’s neural network language models and pre-trained language models, each stage encapsulating the efforts and achievements of countless researchers. As a leader in this technological innovation, OpenAI has conducted in-depth research in various key directions of general AI since its establishment in 2015, particularly in reinforcement learning, multimodal processing, and language models. Especially after Google introduced the Transformer model based on attention mechanisms in 2017, OpenAI quickly recognized its potential and began developing the GPT series based on this architecture. Wang et al.: Recent Advances in Interactive Machine Translation With Large Language Models has progressively improved its performance by enlarging the model scale and pre-training data, especially with GPT-3 and subsequent versions, which have handled various complex tasks without fine-tuning. By November 2022, OpenAI introduced ChatGPT, further expanding the model’s applications, allowing it to interact directly with users through a chat interface. In March 2023, OpenAI launched the more powerful and multimodal GPT-4 model [7]. Observing the development of the GPT series, its success relies on two core strategies: the scalable Transformer architecture, which supports expanding from billions to trillions of parameters, and a strong focus on data quality and scale, ensuring the model’s learning effectiveness and universality. OpenAI’s early research deeply explored this large-scale text pre-training learning paradigm in the GPT-2 paper, demonstrating its foresight in technology. While this model is difficult to replicate, it provides a research path worth considering and reflecting on. Undoubtedly, the advent of LLMs has rekindled hope for achieving Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) among researchers. Machine translation is a common task in NLP and is now widely used in various products and systems. Currently, small fine-tuned language models are extensively utilized in many settings. As discussed earlier, LLMs have shown exceptional text generation capabilities and, with appropriate prompting techniques, can achieve near-human performance in many generative tasks. Moreover, LLMs also display greater flexibility in practical applications, meeting specific needs in real-world applications. For example, in translation activities, the interactive mode of LLMs can engage with users, thereby further enhancing text generation quality. As previously discussed, LLMs in interactive mode can engage with users effectively, thereby enhancing the quality of text generation. To provide a clearer visualization of how these models are applied in machine translation, the following chart (see Figure 1) elaborates on various machine translation methods and related technologies based on the GPT model. Stylised MT utilizes InstructGPT, ChatGPT, and GPT-4 to produce translations with specific styles or tones. Interactive MT [8], [9] relies on ChatGPT and GPT-4, allowing real-time user interaction for on-the-fly adjustments. MT- based MT [10], [11] leverages these models to improve translation quality by incorporating data from similar or relevant past translations. The New Eval Paradigm explores a new approach to assessing MT systems with these models, focusing on comprehensive and efficient evaluation beyond mere comparison with reference texts. Privacy concerns are addressed by proposing methods to preserve privacy when using InstructGPT and ChatGPT in MT. Finally, Multi- modal MT, utilizing GPT-4, integrates multiple modalities like text and images, enhancing translation accuracy and contextual relevance. Each category showcases the diverse applications and potential of GPT models in enhancing machine translation. However, when it comes to low-resource languages or specific domain text generation, such as Marathi to English translation, LLMs still show limitations. This is because LLMs are primarily pre-trained on English corpora, lacking data in low-resource languages, which prevents them from effectively mastering these languages’ semantic knowledge and grammatical logic [12], [13], [14], [15]. While there are many reviews on LLMs, there has not yet been one focused on the inherent interactive translation mode. Based on the above, the contribution of this paper is to summarize and analyze the research on LLMs directed towards interactive machine translation. The research content is divided into nine specific directions, each analyzed in detail, providing specific guidance on model selection and application. The paper also explores the potential of LLMs in multilingual and low-resource language translation, offering a new perspective on the models’ cross-lingual transfer learning capabilities. This aims to propel the field of interactive machine translation towards more efficient and intelligent directions, providing a comprehensive reference for scholars and practitioners in the field. This paper is structured as follows: Section II introduces the overall situation of LLMs; Section III explains the current research on LLMs directed towards machine translation across nine branches; Section IV provides comprehensive insights into LLM performance and evaluation in machine translation; Section V is a summary of the paper and future prospects. II. LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS ChatGPT (Chat Generative Pre-trained Transformer) repre- sents the culmination of rapid technological advances in recent years, where multiple technologies have coalesced to create transformative products within the field of NLP. Its underlying technologies, including the Transformer architecture, self-supervised learning, fine-tuning, human feedback reinforcement learning, and AI alignment, have found extensive research and application across NLP and AI domains. Language models generally aim to model the intrinsic patterns of human language, thereby accurately predicting the probability of future or missing words or tokens in a word sequence. As illustrated in Figure 2, research on language models can be categorized into four main evolutionary stages based on the methodologies employed: Statistical Language Models (SLMs), Neural Language Models (NLMs), Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs), and LLMs, progressing from the 1990s to 2013, 2018, and the recent advancements in 2022. SLMs primarily utilize a fixed context length n, commonly referred to as n-gram models; NLMs employ neural networks to model the generation of text sequences, including Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory Networks (LSTMs), with word2vec [16] being a seminal word embedding model that significantly influenced the field of NLP. PLMs innovate both in architecture and training data, advocating the use of vast amounts of unlabeled data to learn context-aware word 179354 VOLUME 12, 2024 Y.
