The evolution of four generations of language models based on task-solving ability. representations, establishing the foundational capabilities of the models during the pre-training phase and specifically adapting them to particular tasks during the fine-tuning phase to better tackle downstream NLP tasks. LLMs, through scaling up model or data dimensions, have enhanced model performance on downstream tasks, showcasing emergent abilities not seen in smaller pre-trained models. ChatGPT adapts the GPT series of LLMs to conversational tasks, displaying astonishing human-computer interaction capabil- ities, garnering widespread societal attention and academic acclaim upon its release. Recent prominent LLMs include: Brown et al. demon- strated the powerful few-shot learning capabilities of GPT-3 by increasing the model size, showing that large autore- gressive language models can achieve robust performance across multiple NLP tasks without gradient updates or fine-tuning [17]. OpenAI developed GPT-4, a large-scale multimodal model capable of processing both image and text inputs, and exhibited near-human performance on various professional and academic benchmarks [7]. Chowdhery et al. trained a 540 billion parameter Transformer language model, PaLM, highlighting the significant impact of model size on few-shot learning performance, making strides in multiple tasks [18]. Scao et al. introduced BLOOM, a collaborative open-access LLM developed by hundreds of researchers, aimed at simplifying the technology of LLMs [19]. Zhang et al. presented Open Pre-trained Transformers (OPT), a series of decoder-only pre-trained Transformer models, showing performance comparable to GPT-3 while reducing development complexity [20]. Touvron et al. introduced the VOLUME 12, 2024 179355 Y. Wang et al.: Recent Advances in Interactive Machine Translation With Large Language Models LLaMA (Large Language Model Meta AI) series, demon- strating state-of-the-art performance using publicly available datasets, particularly outperforming GPT-3 in most bench- marks [21]. They later developed and released LLaMA 2, a pre-trained LLM exceeding the performance of other open-source chat models at the time and exhibiting notable safety features [22]. Meta’s TEAM NLLB (No Language Left Behind) focuses on bridging language barriers, devel- oping a conditional computation-based machine translation system, NLLB, aimed at narrowing the performance gap between low-resource and high-resource languages, laying the groundwork for a universally applicable translation system [23]. Reviewing the historical trajectory of language models, we see that this is not a concept that emerged recently but has matured over a long period of development. Initially designed to tackle specific problems such as information retrieval, text ranking, and speech transcription, models like GPT-4 now tackle more complex strategic task resolutions. This shift from basic language modeling to deep task processing marks a significant leap in scientific thinking within the AI field, revealing keys to mastering the latest dynamics in language models. For instance, Figure 2 compares the capabilities and limitations of different generations of language models from a task resolution perspective. Early SLMs, known for their effectiveness in certain specific applications, had their applications confined to those core tasks. With the advent of NLMs, the focus shifted towards learning broad, task-agnostic semantic features, reducing reliance on manual feature engineering and expanding the boundaries of their applications. The rise of PLMs not only deepened the representation of context but also significantly enhanced the processing capabilities of NLP tasks through task-specific fine-tuning. Modern LLMs, with their vast parameter space, extensive training data, and formidable computational power, have markedly improved their ability to tackle a variety of tasks. This enhancement has enabled them to address generalization issues even without relying on task-specific data tuning. In the domain of NMT for low-resource languages, despite notable advancements, there remain substantial challenges that must be surmounted. Fundamental limitations in data availability combined with substantial linguistic disparities present formidable obstacles. In pursuit of augmenting both the quality and efficacy of translation for low-resource languages, researchers have delved into comprehensive and thorough experimentation and analysis. To address the scarcity of parallel corpora, scholars have innovatively employed diverse strategies, including the utilization of transfer learning techniques, exploitation of monolingual corpora, and the extraction of syntactic structure-related knowledge through unsupervised methodologies. For example, Yu et al. [24] leveraged a transfer learning approach to extract robust encoder and decoder components from languages with richer resources, constructing NMT models for low-resource language pairs, such as Thai-Lao, thereby notably boosting translation quality between Chinese and Lao. This method not only mitigated the challenges posed by sparse data but also expedited the training of new models. Additionally, Zhang et al. [25] introduced a sophisticated unsupervised translation mechanism integrating a dependency distance penalty system and dependency-aware attention mechanism to comprehen- sively delve into and utilize syntactic knowledge pertaining to Thai. Empirical findings showcased a marked enhancement in translation quality from Thai to Chinese, underscoring the promise of unsupervised learning in the realm of low-resource language translation. With the progressive establishment of extensive datasets for low-resource lan- guages and the sustained application of cutting-edge model technologies, there has been a significant enhancement in the translation quality for such languages. Throughout the evolution of language models, the range of solvable tasks and application scopes has greatly expanded, and performance in executing these tasks has reached unprecedented levels. The advent of LLMs marks a significant milestone in the history of artificial intelligence; they not only greatly enrich our toolbox for problem-solving but also delineate the direction for future research and applications in the AI field. III. RESEARCH ON INTERACTIVE MACHINE TRANSLATION WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS In this Section, we will introduce current research on LLMs directed towards interactive machine translation from nine perspectives, including: In-context Learning, Chain-of- Thought Prompting, Base Model Pre-training, Translation Finetuning, LLMs as Scorer, Post-Editing, Interpretability, Model Decoding, Assessment. Figure 3 illustrates the cita- tions across these nine categories. A. IN-CONTEXT LEARNING In this subsection, we will discuss how LLMs utilize given contexts to learn, thereby enhancing the accuracy and relevance of machine translation. Contextual learning allows models to self-adjust based on previous translation instances, maintaining efficiency and accuracy in a continually chang- ing translation environment. Jiao et al. [15] conducted a preliminary study evaluating ChatGPT’s effectiveness as a machine translation tool across multiple languages and settings. The study found that while ChatGPT performs well in high-resource European languages and can be competitive with commercial products like Google Translate, it struggles with low-resource and linguistically distant languages. The introduction of pivot prompting strategies and the improvements in the GPT- 4 engine have notably enhanced ChatGPT’s translation performance, reducing errors and increasing its viability as a translation tool in diverse scenarios. Their approach, by integrating known translations into prompts, allows for phrase-level control during translation, particularly in low-resource and out-of-domain scenarios. Their extensive experiments confirmed that DiPMT not only outperformed the baselines but also significantly improved the controllability of translations, although they noted some limitations in achieving absolute control over the outputs. [34] developed RAMP, a new framework that integrates retrieval and attribute-marking techniques into the prompting process to control attributes like formality and gender in translation outputs. This method capitalizes on the capabilities of large multilingual models to enhance attribute-controlled translation in both zero-shot and few- shot settings. Their comprehensive testing demonstrated that RAMP significantly improves the accuracy of generated translations by selecting and annotating in-context examples based on semantic similarity. Their study, covering models of various sizes and tasks like translation and summarization, showed that this adjustment leads to better adherence to instructions, enhancing zero-shot performance across different tasks and significantly raising BLEU scores in translation. Their method improves the understanding of model instructions by embedding a global instruction representation, which helps maintain focus dur- ing longer translations. Despite the advantages of fine-tuning, they noted it could impair few-shot performance; however, their proposed method of integrating few-shot examples during fine-tuning successfully preserved in-context learning abilities across multiple language pairs. Raunak et al. [40] conducted a detailed analysis of in-context learning in translations using GPT models. They focused on how demonstration attributes affect learning, particularly through perturbations in source-target mappings. B. CHAIN-OF-THOUGHT PROMPTING This subsection explores how to guide LLMs using chain- of-thought prompting to simulate human reasoning processes in complex translation tasks. This approach aids models in understanding and deconstructing complex queries, thus improving the quality and depth of translations. The chain-of-thought technique in LLMs is also very effective. He et al. [46] introduced the MAPS framework to enhance the translation capabilities of LLMs by mimicking human-like strategies. MAPS analyzes source sentences to extract keywords, topics, and demonstrations that guide translations, employing a quality estimation filter to refine the input. Their evaluations across multiple models and languages demonstrated significant reductions in errors like hallucinations and omissions, proving the framework’s effectiveness in improving translation quality. Peng et al. [47] explored optimizing ChatGPT for machine translation, particularly focusing on temperature settings, task-specific, and domain-specific information to enhance its performance. They found that lower temperatures and emphasis on task and domain specifics significantly improve ChatGPT’s capabilities, especially in complex translation scenarios. However, they noted that ChatGPT is prone to generating hallucinations in translations from non-English- centric tasks, a challenge that still requires attention. Lu et al. [48] developed the Chain-of-Dictionary (CoD) method to enhance the translation of rare words in multilin- gual NMT by LLMs, particularly for low-resource languages. By incorporating chains of multilingual dictionaries, CoD significantly improved translation accuracy, as demonstrated by substantial gains on benchmark tests, emphasizing the method’s potential to overcome limitations in existing LLM-based translation approaches. Liang et al. [49] proposed the Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) framework to foster divergent thinking in LLMs, addressing the Degeneration-of-Thought problem observed in self-reflection methods. By structuring debates among multiple agents and a judge, MAD effectively enhances problem-solving capabilities in complex reasoning tasks, as evidenced by its success on challenging datasets in machine translation and arithmetic reasoning. Zhao et al. [51] introduced the Verify-and-Edit frame- work to address the factuality issues in Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting by incorporating external knowledge for post-editing reasoning chains. This section illustrates how the strategic use of chain-of-thought prompting substantially refines translation quality and extends the capabilities of LLMs in handling complex language tasks. C. BASE MODEL PRE-TRAINING We analyze how base model pre-training establishes a strong foundation for language understanding in machine translation tasks. The pre-training phase encompasses a wide corpus, providing the model with rich linguistic knowledge, which is beneficial for subsequent task-specific fine-tuning. Figure 4 illustrates a decoder-only language modeling architecture, which is fundamental for understanding the pre-training techniques discussed. In this architecture, input words such as ‘‘The’’, ‘‘cat’’, ‘‘sat’’, and others are first transformed into tokens and enhanced with positional embeddings to capture their order and context within the sentence. This is followed by a masked self-attention layer where the model attentively processes different parts of the input sequence, enabling it to predict missing or subsequent tokens effectively. Each decoder block includes a feed-forward neural network that further processes the output from the self-attention layer, adding non-linear transformations to enhance the model’s expressive power. Multiple decoder blocks are stacked, refining and processing the information progressively to understand and generate complex language structures deeply. The model ultimately outputs a sequence that ends with an ‘‘<EOS>’’ (End of Sentence) marker, signaling the end of the prediction, which can directly be used for translation or other language processing tasks. OpenAI’s GPT-4 [7], a multimodal large-scale model, demonstrates human-level performance on various profes- sional benchmarks and is capable of handling both text and image inputs. Despite its capabilities, it still faces challenges in real-world scenarios. GPT-4’s development focused on optimizing infrastructure to predict performance effectively across scales, highlighting its advanced training methods and post-training alignment for better factuality and behavior adherence. GPT-4 exhibits exceptional mul- timodal processing and cross-lingual understanding capa- bilities. Through extensive pre-training data, it successfully improves translation accuracy for high-resource language pairs. However, GPT-4 still encounters challenges when dealing with low-resource languages, indicating that the diversity of pre-training data is crucial for machine translation performance. Chowdhery et al. [18] presented the Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter model trained on a new ML system that allows efficient scaling across multiple TPU Pods. PaLM shows exceptional performance in few-shot learning on numerous benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art results and surpassing human performance in many instances. Its capabilities extend to multilingual tasks and source code generation, with detailed analyses on bias, toxicity, and memorization provided. PaLM, through few-shot learning in multilingual translation tasks, has demonstrated that high translation accuracy can still be achieved even in the absence of a large parallel corpus, showcasing the model’s robust generalization capabilities. Scao et al. [19] detailed BLOOM, a 176 billion parameters multilingual language model, developed collaboratively to support research with an open-access approach. [20] introduced OPT, a suite of open pre-trained transformer models ranging from 125 million to 175 billion parameters, emphasizing the environmental and economic efficiency of their development. OPT models, comparable in performance to GPT-3, are fully shared with the research community, reflecting a commitment to transparency and accessibility in AI. The OPT series has made progress in optimizing translation efficiency, achieving high-performance translation with limited computational resources, providing an economical and efficient pathway for machine translation research. Touvron et al. [21] introduced the LLaMA series, a range of foundation language models designed for state-of-the-art performance using only public datasets. These models, with capacities ranging from 7 billion to 65 billion parameters, demonstrate competitive abilities against larger models and are intended to foster further research and development within the AI community. Additionally, the LLaMA 2 [22] was developed, featuring fine-tuned models optimized for dialogue applications. These models surpass the perfor- mance of existing open-source chat models and have been rigorously evaluated for safety and helpfulness, providing a viable alternative to closed-source models for dialogue systems. LLaMA not only leverages public datasets for pre- training, covering a variety of language translation tasks, but also demonstrates exceptional generalization performance in both high- and low-resource language environments. Its multi-layer architecture and different parameter scales enable it to balance between accuracy and flexibility, solidifying its leading position in the field of machine translation. Costa-Jussà et al. [23] tackled the challenge of machine translation for low-resource languages with the project ‘‘No Language Left Behind’’. This project underscores the importance of inclusive and ethical considerations in AI development, with all contributions made openly available to the community. Schioppa et al. [55] introduced PolyLM, an open-source poly- glot LLM trained on a vast corpus of 640 billion tokens across multiple languages. By incorporating bilingual data and employing a curriculum learning strategy, PolyLM improves multilingual capabilities, outperforming other models on various tasks. It demonstrates strong performance in non- English languages, contributing significantly to multilingual research and application. VOLUME 12, 2024 179361 Y. Wang et al.: Recent Advances in Interactive Machine Translation With Large Language Models Li et al. [56] developed OpenBA, a 15 billion parameter bilingual asymmetric seq2seq model, enhancing the Chinese- oriented open-source model community. OpenBA combines efficient training strategies and advanced techniques to achieve competitive performance on multiple benchmarks, surpassing other large models with fewer training tokens. This model is part of an effort to democratize access to powerful language models, providing detailed training and fine-tuning documentation to the public. Anil et al. [57] introduced Gemini, a new family of multimodal models excelling in image, audio, video, and text understanding. The models range from Ultra to Nano, with the Ultra model achieving human-expert performance on most benchmarks, including the MMLU exam. This marks a significant advancement in cross-modal reasoning and language tasks, suitable for diverse applications. Almazrouei et al. [58] developed the Falcon series, large open-source language models ranging from 7B to 180B parameters, trained on over 3.5 trillion tokens. The largest, Falcon-180B, competes with top models like GPT-4, offering significant improvements on benchmarks and introducing an open-source framework to support collaborative AI research advancements. The Falcon series exemplifies the growing trend toward open-access, large-scale models that promote collaboration and innovation in the AI community. In this segment, the discussion centers on the foundational role of base model pre-training for LLMs in enhancing language comprehension and translation tasks. The extensive corpus used during the pre-training phase equips the model with a rich linguistic foundation, beneficial for subsequent task-specific fine-tuning. Notably, OpenAI’s GPT-4 [7], a multimodal large-scale model, has shown human-level performance on diverse professional benchmarks and demon- strates its capabilities in handling both text and image inputs. Additionally, Zhang et al. [20] introduced the OPT series, a suite of open pre-trained transformer models that reflect a commitment to environmental and economic efficiency while maintaining high performance. This subsection emphasizes how pre-training prepares LLMs with the necessary linguistic knowledge to excel in machine translation, highlighting the continuous evolution and application potential of these models in multilingual tasks. D. TRANSLATION FINETUNING This part discusses optimizing LLM performance through fine-tuning for specific translation tasks. Fine-tuning is a crucial step in adjusting models to adapt to specific language pairs or domain terminologies, significantly improving translation accuracy and fluidity. Hu et al. [59] introduced LoRA for efficiently adapting LLMs by modifying a small subset of the model’s weights. This method maintains most pre-trained weights static, significantly reducing the number of trainable parameters and computational needs. Demonstrated on models like GPT-3, LoRA matches or surpasses full fine-tuning in performance while ensuring quick inference times, making it practical for applications requiring frequent task switching. Gao et al. [60] introduced MoLA (MoE (Mixure of Experts)-LoRA with Layer-wise Expert Allocation), an improved parameter- efficient tuning method that assigns different numbers of LoRA experts to various layers of a Transformer model. This integration did not add extra computational burden and significantly optimizes translation quality. Building on this, we can apply this model to machine translation tasks, as illustrated in the lower half of Figure 5. Specifically, the integration with the MoLA framework leverages the MoE concept, where the model is divided into numerous ‘expert’ networks, each specialized in a different aspect of the translation task. This approach assigns different numbers of LoRA experts to various layers of a Transformer model, allowing for dynamic adaptation to the complexities of language translation. By employing a trainable routing function, the model efficiently selects the most appropriate expert for each segment of text, optimizing both accuracy and computational efficiency. This method significantly enhances the model’s capability to handle diverse linguistic structures and idiomatic expressions across languages. The upper part displays the original version of LoRA, exemplified by a single Linear layer (h = WX). Assuming the original pre-trained model’s weight is W0 ∈Rdm×dp, we define two low-rank matrices B ∈Rdm×r and A ∈Rr×dp, where r ≪min(dm, dp), and adjust the Linear layer’s computation from the original h = W0x to: h = W0x + 1Wx = W0x + BAx (1) In the enhanced paradigm shown in the lower part of Figure 5, the original pre-trained weight remains W0 ∈ Rdm×dp, and each layer is equipped with K pairs of low-rank matrices Bjt i ∈Rdm×r and Ajt i ∈Rr×dp, where r ≪min(dm, dp), corresponding to different expert models. Through a trainable routing function Rjt(x) combined with pre-trained weights W jt 0 ∈Rdp×Nj, the most suitable expert model is designated for each input x. The output h reflects the results processed by these expert models: Rjt i (x) = TopK  Softmax  W jt r x  , K  i PK i=1 TopK  Softmax  W jt r x  , K  i (2) hjt = W jt 0 x + K X i=1 Rjt i (x)Ajt i Bjt i x (3) Mao and Yu [61] presented AlignInstruct, enhancing trans- lation for low-resource and unseen languages by integrating a cross-lingual discriminator into LLMs. This approach, tested on the BLOOMZ models across 24 languages, proved that AlignInstruct consistently elevates translation quality, notably enhancing zero-shot translation performance in 30 directions. Xu et al. [62] introduced ALMA, a novel fine-tuning strategy for LLMs aimed at boosting machine translation 179362 VOLUME 12, 2024 Y. Wang et al.: Recent Advances in Interactive Machine Translation With Large Language Models FIGURE 5. Comparison between the original version of LoRA and LoRA combined with the MoE framework. performance without extensive parallel data. Utilizing a two- stage fine-tuning process, ALMA demonstrated significant BLEU and COMET score improvements, surpassing existing models and establishing a new training paradigm for translation. Jiao et al. [63] developed ParroT, a framework enhancing translation during chat sessions using open-source LLMs like LLaMA. By incorporating human-generated translation feed- back and innovative instruction styles, ParroT significantly improved translation quality and introduced a method to learn from low-quality translations. Li et al. [64] explored multilingual fine-tuning of LLMs to enhance translation capabilities without parallel corpora. Their study revealed that LLMs could perform multilingual translations effectively, especially when fine-tuned with translation instructions, showcasing an enhanced understand- ing of multilingual contexts. Yang et al. [65] created BigTrans, enhancing LLaMA with the ability to translate over 100 languages by integrating massive multilingual data and instruction tuning. The model matched or surpassed performances of leading systems like ChatGPT and Google Translate in multiple language pairs. Zhang et al. [66] proposed BayLing, an instruction- following LLM that bridges cross-lingual alignment through interactive translation tasks. Utilizing a smaller parame- ter size, BayLing demonstrated impressive performance in multilingual instruction understanding and knowledge assessments, rivaling larger LLMs. Zeng et al. [67] developed TIM, a framework teach- ing LLMs translation by comparing correct and incorrect examples, leading to superior translation performance on challenging datasets. This method offers a new way to fine-tune LLMs for specialized tasks like translation. Zhu et al. [68] advanced LLMs’ non-English perfor- mance by aligning multilingual semantic spaces, significantly enhancing translation and multilingual task execution. Their approach, involving cross-lingual and multilingual instruc- tion tuning, showcased a robust alignment of semantic representations across languages. Moslem et al. [69] investigated domain-specific data augmentation for machine translation, highlighting the effec- tiveness of simulating domain characteristics to enhance translation accuracy. Their approach utilized state-of-the-art pretrained language models and back-translation, generating substantial improvements in domain-specific translations, especially between Arabic and English language pairs. Despite the advancements, the research acknowledged the limitations of synthetic data quality and the need for further refinement to approach real-world in-domain accuracy. Moslem et al. [70] further explored fine-tuning Mistral 7B for adaptive machine translation within the medical domain. They demonstrated significant improvements in VOLUME 12, 2024 179363 Y. Wang et al.: Recent Advances in Interactive Machine Translation With Large Language Models both zero-shot and one-shot translation scenarios, notably outperforming standard models and highlighting the potential of adaptive MT for domain-specific applications. The study emphasized the importance of targeted fine-tuning strategies to maximize real-time adaptive translation capabilities. Wu et al. [71] focused on adapting LLMs for document- level machine translation, examining how prompt strate- gies could influence performance across multiple language pairs. Their findings revealed that these adapted models could occasionally surpass GPT-4, though inconsistencies in translation accuracy persisted. The research provided an in-depth analysis of the strengths and weaknesses of LLM-based document translation, suggesting a need for enhanced training strategies and a deeper understanding of discourse phenomena. Xu et al. [72] introduced Contrastive Preference Opti- mization to enhance the translation performance of LLMs, particularly in moderate-sized models. Their approach cor- rected the shortcomings of supervised fine-tuning by training models to avoid suboptimal translations. The ALMA-R model, enhanced through CPO, matched or exceeded the performance of leading models on several key benchmarks, offering a new perspective on enhancing LLM efficiency in translation tasks without extensive data requirements. Meng et al. [73] delved into the editing of factual associations in GPT models, discovering that factual recall could be localized and directly modified within the models’ architecture. Their approach, Rank-One Model Editing, proved effective in maintaining accuracy and specificity in factual updates, suggesting a viable method for targeted model editing that could enhance the reliability of LLM outputs in knowledge-intensive applications. Xu et al. [74] addressed the challenges of noise in low-resource machine translation by proposing a mono- lingual denoising technique to refine pseudo-parallel data. By integrating LLMs for error correction, they significantly enhanced translation quality for several low-resource lan- guages, demonstrating the efficacy of monolingual denoising in improving data quality for MT training. Lyu et al. [75] highlighted the transformative impact of LLMs on machine translation, emphasizing the potential of prompt-based techniques to revolutionize MT. They explored various applications and discussed privacy concerns associated with LLM-driven MT, suggesting that LLMs will play a crucial role in the evolution of MT technologies. Wu and Hu [76] experimented with prompt engineering to optimize document-level machine translation using GPT models. Their participation in the WMT 2023 competition showcased the effectiveness of their approaches, contributing valuable insights into the use of LLMs for complex transla- tion tasks and setting a benchmark for future research in the field. Bao et al. [77] developed a target-side augmentation method for document-level MT, addressing data sparsity by generating multiple potential translations to smooth the learning distribution. Their method not only achieved state- of-the-art performance but also highlighted the importance of innovative data augmentation strategies in overcoming the challenges of document-level translation. Li et al. [78] focused on improving NMT for low-resource languages centered around Chinese, leveraging unique strate- gies such as monolingual word embeddings and contrastive re-ranking. Their approach outperformed traditional meth- ods, emphasizing the potential for specialized NMT systems in a multilingual context and suggesting further exploration into diverse linguistic frameworks. Chen et al. [79] introduced a multi-objective reinforce- ment learning approach to balance fidelity and diversity in conditional text generation. Their method allowed for the customization of output by adjusting user p

text: 1 Published by the IEEE Reliability Society 2641-8819 © 2026 IEEE. March 2026 Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/MRL.2026.3660197 date of current version: XX XXX XXXX.  When AI starts acting for more than a dec- ade, reliability engineering for artificial intelligence has been shaped by a relatively stable assumption. AI systems analyze, predict, or recommend, while humans decide and act. Within this paradigm, reli- ability concerns are naturally centered on model correctness—classification accuracy, robustness to noisy inputs, bias in training data, and performance drift over time. These concerns remain fundamental, and they continue to define best practice for many AI applications. Yet, they reflect a world in which AI influences decisions indirectly rather than executing them. That assumption is now being quietly but deci- sively overturned. Generative artificial intelli- gence (GenAI) systems are increasingly deployed as active participants in operational workflows, embedded within software stacks that include tools, application programming interfaces, data- bases, and control logic [1], [2]. In such environ- ments, AI systems do not merely advise human operators; they initiate actions. They invoke tools, modify configurations, trigger workflows, and select downstream components with minimal or no human intervention at each step. Systems with this capability are commonly described as agentic AI systems, distinguished not by intelligence alone but by agency—the capacity to select actions and carry them out in pursuit of goals [3], [4], [5], [6]. This transition from analysis to action marks a qualitative shift in the reliability landscape. When an AI system acts, failure is no longer confined to an incorrect output or a misleading recommenda- tion. It becomes an operational event with tangi- ble consequences. A flawed tool invocation may ripple through enterprise infrastructure. A poorly sequenced decision may silently corrupt data or compromise confidentiality. An autonomous retry loop may exhaust computational or financial resources without ever triggering a conventional Feature Feature Reliability-by-Design for Agentic GenAI: Turning the AI Risk Atlas Into an EU AI Act-Ready Assurance Case Partha Pratim Ray Sikkim University Sikkim 737102, India This article has been accepted for inclusion in a future issue of this journal. Content is final as presented, with the exception of pagination. uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply 2 Feature IEEE Reliability Magazine fault signal. What makes this moment distinct from earlier waves of automation is not speed or scale alone, but discretion. Agentic GenAI systems are increasingly trusted with judgment calls—deciding when sufficient evidence has been gathered, when escalation is warranted, and when action should proceed without further confirmation. In effect, anal- ysis, decision, and execution are compressed into a single computational reflex. This compression erodes safety margins that relia- bility engineers have long relied upon. In earlier sys- tems, human review functioned as a natural damping mechanism, absorbing uncertainty before it could propagate into action. Agentic systems remove that buffer by design. The result is not merely faster oper- ation, but a different class of risk-one in which small reasoning errors can cross the boundary into oper- ational impact with little warning. From a reliabil- ity standpoint, this evolution demands a reframing of responsibility. The central question is no longer whether a model produced a plausible response, but whether the system as a whole behaved within its intended operational envelope. Reliability, in this setting, becomes inseparable from the governance of autonomy. Recent work on generative AI reliability—includ- ing studies of silent failures, hallucinations, and trust calibration—has established an important founda- tion. Much of this work, however, continues to treat AI systems primarily as advisory components rather than autonomous actors. At the same time, regulatory developments—most notably the European Union Artificial Intelligence Act (EU AI Act) [7]—now require organizations to demonstrate systematic con- trol, traceability, and oversight of AI behavior across the system lifecycle, explicitly acknowledging the operational risks of autonomous decision-making. The purpose of this article is to connect these threads. Drawing on the AI Risk Atlas for agentic AI [8] and the lifecycle obligations articulated in the EU AI Act, this article advances a reliability-by-design perspective for agentic GenAI systems. The empha- sis is not on legal compliance, but on engineering discipline—on practices that make autonomous systems dependable, auditable, and defensible in production environments. Thus, situating agentic GenAI within established reliability thinking, this article proposes a practical assurance framework that aligns technical rigor with emerging regulatory expectations. From predictive models to acting systems Traditional machine learning systems operate within well-defined boundaries. Inputs are pro- cessed, outputs are produced, and downstream components determine how those outputs are used. Reliability engineering in this setting focuses primar- ily on the correctness, stability, and robustness of the model itself. Agentic GenAI systems fundamentally dissolve this separation. An agentic system typically integrates a large language model with planning logic, memory, and access to external tools. The system interprets a goal, decomposes it into intermediate steps, selects tools, evaluates outcomes, and iterates until termination. Decision-making and execution occur within a sin- gle, continuous feedback loop rather than as distinct stages. This architecture introduces a critical shift in the reliability boundary. In conventional software systems, the boundary lies at the interface between computation and actuation. In agentic AI, that boundary moves inward, into the probabilistic rea- soning process that selects actions. As a result, relia- bility can no longer be assessed solely by inspecting outputs. It must encompass sequences of actions, accumulation of context, and interaction with exter- nal systems. One important implication is that failures in agentic systems rarely appear as abrupt faults. Instead, they often manifest as plausible behavior that gradually diverges from intended operation. The system continues to function, yet its actions become increasingly misaligned with user expecta- tions, organizational policies, or safety constraints. Such deviations are difficult to detect using tradi- tional monitoring approaches. The transition from predictive models to acting systems also changes how failures present themselves to operators. Rather than triggering alarms, exceptions, or obvi- ous misclassifications, failures emerge as subtle behavioral shifts. Over time, the system may begin to favor unsafe shortcuts, inappropriate tools, or overly confident decisions. Individual actions appear reasonable in isolation, allowing problem- atic patterns to persist unnoticed. This behavior mirrors failure modes observed in complex socio- technical systems, where locally rational decisions produce globally undesirable outcomes. Agentic GenAI systems are particularly vulnerable because they continuously update internal context based on This article has been accepted for inclusion in a future issue of this journal. Content is final as presented, with the exception of pagination. uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply 3 March 2026 prior actions. A single misjudgment can, therefore, influence subsequent reasoning, creating a self-re- inforcing error trajectory that is difficult to interrupt once established. Reliability engineering has long emphasized the importance of understanding system behavior over time. In agentic AI, this temporal dimension becomes central. Evaluating isolated outputs is insufficient; assurance must extend to how deci- sions evolve, compound, and interact with external systems across sustained operational periods. Agentic risk through a reliability lens Many of the risks associated with agentic AI resemble familiar reliability concerns, but their man- ifestation is less tangible. Traditional systems tend to fail through component degradation or explicit logic errors. Agentic systems, by contrast, fail through mis- placed confidence, incomplete context, or misinter- preted intent. These are not failures of hardware or syntax, but failures at the boundary between reason- ing and action. This distinction introduces a new category of reliability hazard: epistemic failure. In such cases, the system acts on what it believes to be true, even when that belief is weakly grounded or contextually inappropriate. Because agentic systems struggle to express uncertainty in operational terms, these epis- temic gaps may remain hidden until consequences accumulate. Figure 1 outlines the major risk categories asso- ciated with agentic AI, showing how they concen- trate around explainability, privacy, fairness, and robustness [8]. From a reliability perspective, these categories do not point to isolated technical flaws. They reveal systemic vulnerabilities that arise when autonomous reasoning is allowed to initiate real- world actions without sufficient constraint or visibil- ity. In this setting, the task of reliability engineering shifts in a subtle but important way. The objective is no longer limited to identifying explicit errors, but to recognizing unjustified certainty. Conventional safeguards such as thresholds, alarms, or rule-based checks struggle to capture this form of risk. What becomes essential instead are mechanisms that make the confidence, provenance, and assumptions behind agent decisions visible, so uncertainty can be addressed before it escalates into operational failure. One of the most consequential risks illustrated is the emergence of unexplainable or untraceable actions. Agentic systems may carry out extended sequences of reasoning and tool invocation without leaving a clear account of why a particular path was selected. In reliability terms, this represents a loss of diagnosability. When decision pathways cannot be reconstructed, root cause analysis becomes conjec- tural, limiting both corrective action and long-term improvement. A closely related hazard arises from function-calling hallucinations. Unlike textual hal- lucinations, which may have little consequence, incorrect function calls can directly alter the system state. From a reliability standpoint, this is analogous to executing flawed control logic in a safety-critical system. The probabilistic origin of the error does lit- tle to reduce its operational impact. Privacy and confidentiality risks are similarly amplified by an agent’s ability to share context with Figure 1. Key risks of agentic GenAI. This article has been accepted for inclusion in a future issue of this journal. Content is final as presented, with the exception of pagination. uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply 4 Feature IEEE Reliability Magazine external tools. Sensitive information may be trans- mitted without explicit user intent or awareness. Such failures often remain invisible during normal operation and surface only through audits or third- party reports. Reliability engineers will recognize this pattern as a latent fault whose effects are delayed rather than immediate. The robustness risks captured in the figure fur- ther emphasize the system-level nature of agentic failure. Unauthorized use, trust mismatches, and attacks on external resources frequently manifest as inefficiency or runaway behaviour rather than abrupt breakdown. Feedback loops that fail to converge can trigger repeated actions, excessive resource consumption, or cascading effects, closely resembling uncontrolled oscillations in automated control systems. These risks underscore a central insight: failures in agentic AI do not reside in individ- ual components or models. They emerge from the interaction of reasoning, autonomy, and execution. Addressing them requires reliability assurance that spans the entire decision–action loop, rather than isolated improvements to model performance alone. Table 1 maps abstract agentic risks to concrete fail- ure modes, engineering control mechanisms, and the assurance evidence required to support depend- able operation. EU AI Act as a reliability framework Although the EU AI Act is framed as a regula- tion, its deeper significance lies in how it formalizes expectations that reliability engineers have histori- cally enforced through professional practice rather than statute [9], [10], [11]. The Act reflects a clear acknowledgment that autonomous decision-making systems cannot be trusted solely based on design intent or empirical performance metrics. By mandat- ing lifecycle risk management, the Act aligns closely with a foundational reliability principle: complex systems evolve in ways designers cannot fully antic- ipate. Model updates, retraining cycles, integration with new tools, and shifts in operational context continually introduce new failure pathways. Treat- ing risk management as a continuous obligation, rather than a one-time certification event, mirrors the long-standing practice of maintaining a living hazard analysis. The Act’s emphasis on traceability carries similar weight. In effect, it embeds the idea that systems that cannot account for their behavior cannot be relied upon. Logging and documenta- tion are elevated from operational conveniences to essential components of system integrity. For relia- bility engineers, this reinforces the need to preserve decision histories that allow meaningful reconstruc- tion of events when anomalies or failures occur. Table 1. Agentic GenAI risks mapped to reliability hazards, controls, and assurance evidence. Agentic Risk Category Reliability Failure Mode Operational Impact Engineering Control Mechanism Assurance Evidence / Artifact Loss of explainabil- ity / traceability Loss of diagnosability Root-cause analysis becomes conjectural Mandatory action provenance logging Action traces, rea- soning summaries Tool-calling hallucinations Incorrect state-changing execution Silent corruption of system state Tool validation & schema enforcement Blocked/failed invo- cation records Epistemic overconfidence Unsafe autonomy escalation High-impact actions executed prematurely Confidence thresh- olds & escalation gates Override logs, esca- lation metrics Context contamination Error propagation across steps Compounded decision drift Context window partitioning Context diff records Privacy leakage via tools Latent confidentiality fault Delayed regulatory or reputational harm Context redaction & isolation Audit trails, privacy scans Runaway feedback loops Resource exhaustion / instability Financial or opera- tional overload Execution budgets & termination guards Resource usage & timeout logs Misaligned objec- tive decomposition Locally rational but globally unsafe behavior Policy or compliance violations Goal-constraint con- sistency checks Policy evaluation reports This article has been accepted for inclusion in a future issue of this journal. Content is final as presented, with the exception of pagination. uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply 5 March 2026 Record-keeping requirements further reinforce this perspective. By requiring organizations to maintain logs that capture system behavior across time, the Act echoes established practices in aviation, tele- communications, and industrial automation, where event records are indispensable for investigation, certification, and corrective action. Human oversight provisions reflect another famil- iar reliability principle: automation should extend human capability, not displace human authority. Autonomous systems must be designed to allow timely intervention, particularly in abnormal or unforeseen situations. Oversight is treated not as an exception, but as a structural element of safe opera- tion. The Act’s focus on accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity also aligns with reliability thinking. AI systems are expected to perform not only under nominal conditions, but under realistic stress, includ- ing adversarial inputs, partial failures, and degraded operating environments. These expectations parallel long-standing reliability requirements in other safe- ty-critical domains. Viewed through this lens, the EU AI Act does not impose an alien set of constraints on engineers. Instead, it articulates reliability expec- tations for systems whose behavior is increasingly autonomous, probabilistic, and difficult to observe without deliberate design effort. Reliability-by-design for agentic GenAI Reliability engineering for agentic GenAI must begin with risk analysis that reflects how these sys- tems actually fail. Generic model-level risks are insuf- ficient. Hazards should be framed in terms of actions and outcomes, such as irreversible operations exe- cuted without validation or confidential information transmitted through external tools. Each hazard must be assessed for severity, likelihood, detectability, and recoverability to establish meaningful priorities. Clear action boundaries are essential. Tools should operate under the principle of least privi- lege, with strict validation of inputs and an explicit separation between read-only and state-changing operations. For actions with high potential impact, multistage execution mechanisms provide opportu- nities for verification before commitment, reducing the likelihood of irreversible error. Instrumentation plays a central role in enabling this control. Logs must capture more than inputs and outputs; they should record intermediate decisions, rejected alternatives, and the contextual factors that shaped action selection. Without this level of visibil- ity, traceability becomes nominal rather than func- tional. Designing for reliability also requires resisting the temptation to treat autonomy as an all-or-nothing property. In practice, autonomy exists on a contin- uum. Systems are more dependable when freedom to act is confined to well-understood operating regions, while higher risk decisions invoke addi- tional scrutiny or constraint. This approach shifts reli- ability engineering from static safeguards to adaptive control. As systems operate, their permitted action space may expand or contract based on observed behavior. Such adaptation must itself be governed, with clear criteria for increasing autonomy, impos- ing restrictions, or suspending operation altogether. Without these controls, learning risks becoming a source of instability rather than resilience. In this setting, instrumentation serves a second, equally important function. Beyond supporting forensic analysis, it enables reflective improvement. Well-designed logs reveal how agent behavior evolves over time, highlight emerging risk patterns, and allow intervention before deviations escalate. Instrumentation thus becomes an active element of reliability assurance rather than a passive record. Human oversight must be embedded at the archi- tectural level. Graduated autonomy models, in which the system’s authority depends on context and risk, allow organizations to balance efficiency with control. Oversight mechanisms must be respon- sive and intuitive, enabling operators to intervene decisively when conditions warrant. Robustness and cybersecurity complete the reliability picture. Agentic systems should be evaluated against adver- sarial prompts, compromised tools, and conflicting objectives, rather than only under nominal condi- tions. Incident response plans should be defined in advance, acknowledging that some failures are una- voidable in complex autonomous systems and must be managed rather than denied. Measuring reliability in acting systems Metrics traditionally used to evaluate AI sys- tems—such as accuracy or precision—offer little insight once systems begin to act. An agent may produce fluent, statistically sound responses while still making poor operational choices. For agentic GenAI, reliability must therefore be assessed in terms of conduct in operation, not prediction alone. The central question shifts from Was the answer correct? This article has been accepted for inclusion in a future issue of this journal. Content is final as presented, with the exception of pagination. uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply 6 Feature IEEE Reliability Magazine to Did the system respond appropriately under the circumstances? Action-oriented metrics provide a more faithful view of reliability in this setting. One useful measure is the rate of unsafe action attempts, which captures how often an agent proposes steps that violate pol- icy, exceed authority, or introduce unacceptable risk. Closely related is the intervention rate, reflect- ing how frequently safeguards or human operators must block or override an intended step. A gradual decline in intervention can signal improving align- ment, while sudden increases often point to emerg- ing vulnerabilities. Figure 2 represents all the key proposed metrics to measure various aspects of reli- ability for GenAI-assisted agents. Time-based measures are equally revealing. Time to anomaly detection indicates how quickly devia- tions are recognized once they begin, while time to recovery reflects the system’s ability to regain stabil- ity after an incorrect action. In acting systems, delays in either can be more consequential than the initial mistake, particularly when actions propagate across interconnected tools or services. Traceability provides another important lens. The completeness of action traces—whether decisions, rejected alternatives, and contextual assumptions are consistently recorded—directly affects diagnosa- bility. In practice, gaps in traceability often correlate with slower incident resolution and repeated fail- ure patterns. The frequency and context of human overrides offer additional insight, revealing not just how often intervention occurs, but what conditions prompt it. These measures also support organiza- tional learning in ways traditional performance scores cannot. Accuracy curves tend to flatten early, masking subtle forms of degradation. Action-level indicators, by contrast, expose gradual drift toward overconfidence, shortcut-taking, or brittle deci- sion-making. Observing how autonomy evolves under sustained use helps distinguish genuine relia- bility from mere assertiveness. Deployment decisions benefit from this perspec- tive as well. A system that performs well within nar- row constraints may still be unsuitable for broader autonomy. Evidence of safe operation under stress— conflicting objectives, partial failures, or ambiguous inputs—provides a rational basis for expansion. The absence of such evidence, even when nominal per- formance appears strong, argues for caution. Inter- pretation, however, remains essential. A high rate of blocked actions may reflect effective safeguards rather than flawed design. Reliability assessment cannot be reduced to automated scoring. It remains an exercise in informed judgment, where data guide understanding but do not replace responsibility. Attention to observable system conduct, rather than abstract performance scores, offers a clearer picture of how agentic GenAI operates in practice—and where its autonomy still requires careful boundaries. Table 2 summarizes reliability metrics that char- acterize the operational behavior of acting (agen- tic) GenAI systems rather than model accuracy alone. It highlights observable indicators that sup- port monitoring, diagnosability, and governance of autonomous decision–action loops in production environments. Transparency as a reliability enabler Transparency requirements in the EU AI Act are commonly framed around user rights and ethical accountability, particularly within provisions on high-risk AI systems, general-purpose AI models, and transparency obligations for providers and deploy- ers [12], [13]. From a reliability standpoint, trans- parency serves a more operational and systemic role. It establishes the conditions for calibrated trust between humans and autonomous systems, especially when those systems are permitted to act, invoke tools, or affect real-world outcomes without continuous supervision. When users or operators can anticipate what an agent is about to do, understand why it consid- ers a particular action appropriate, and recognize where uncertainty, approximation, or fallback logic applies, they gain the ability to intervene at the right moment. This form of visibility supports the intent Figure 2. Proposed metrics for measuring reliability in agentic GenAI. This article has been accepted for inclusion in a future issue of this journal. Content is final as presented, with the exception of pagination. uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply 7 March 2026 behind the Act’s emphasis on technical documen- tation, traceability, and post-market monitoring. Transparency, in this sense, enables oversight that is timely rather than retrospective. Opaque systems produce a very different oper- ational dynamic. They encourage either uncritical reliance or defensive misuse—both of which are repeatedly identified in the AI Act as sources of systemic risk. In such settings, minor misjudgments may propagate silently through chains of automated actions, surfacing only when corrective interven- tion becomes costly or disruptive. Communicating intent, scope, and limitation, therefore, becomes an engineering obligation rather than a cosmetic inter- face choice. Interfaces that expose assumptions, constraints, and confidence levels mirror the docu- mentation and conformity principles embedded in the Act’s annexes, helping align human expectations with actual system capability and reducing the risk of misplaced delegation. Transparency also plays a decisive role when failures occur. Systems whose actions can be inspected, reconstructed, and explained support the governance mechanisms envisioned under the Act, including accountability, information sharing, and market surveillance. Teams are able to exam- ine decision paths, isolate contributing factors, and implement targeted corrective measures. Where system behavior remains inscrutable, organizational responses tend to default to coarse restrictions or blanket controls that suppress capability without addressing root causes—an outcome at odds with the Act’s emphasis on proportionality and continu- ous improvement. From the user’s perspective, transparency resets expectations in a similarly constructive way. When uncertainty and operational limits are communi- cated plainly, users are less inclined to defer reflex- ively to automated decisions and more likely to exercise informed judgment. This recalibration of trust directly reduces misuse, which remains a per- sistent contributor to operational failure in auton- omous systems and a recurring concern across multiple chapters of the AI Act. In this context, trans- parency is neither a compliance checkbox nor an abstract ethical principle. It operates as a practical reliability mechanism, supporting diagnosability, informed oversight, and adaptive governance. Building an EU AI act-ready assurance case Although the EU AI Act is framed as a legal reg- ulation, its underlying logic mirrors long-standing concerns in reliability engineering [14], [15], [16]. Provisions on high-risk AI systems, transparency obli- gations, governance, and post-market monitoring Table 2. Reliability metrics for agentic (acting) GenAI systems. Metric What It Measures Reliability Insight Typical Signal Pattern Unsafe action attempt rate Frequency of policy-violating proposals Early indicator of misalignment Gradual increase before failures Human intervention rate Dependence on safeguards or overrides Alignment stability over time Decreasing trend = maturation Time to anomaly detection Monitoring responsiveness Damage containment capability Long tail indicates blind spots Time to recovery System resilience after error Operational robustness Short, consistent recovery preferred Trace completeness Coverage of decision & action logs Diagnosability & audit readiness Gaps correlate with repeat incidents Autonomy escalation frequency Rate of authority expansion Governance discipline Spikes indicate risk creep Blocked action ratio Effectiveness of safeguards Control effectiveness Too low may signal under-constraint Cross-tool propagation depth How far actions cascade Containment of failures High depth = systemic risk This article has been accepted for inclusion in a future issue of this journal. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply 8 Feature IEEE Reliability Magazine converge on a single operational expectation. Organ- izations must be able to demonstrate, with credible evidence, that autonomous systems remain control- lable, observable, and accountable throughout their lifecycle. For agentic GenAI systems, satisfying this expectation requires more than static compliance artifacts. It demands an assurance structure that evolves in step with the system itself [17], [18]. Reliability-by-design offers a practical means of translating regulatory intent into operational prac- tice. Instead of treating compliance as a check- list mapped to individual articles or annexes, an assurance case frames compliance as a structured and defensible argument. It clarifies what is being claimed about system behaviour, identifies the safeguards that support those claims, and presents evidence showing that those safeguards function as intended. This mode of reasoning aligns closely with the Act’s emphasis on technical documentation, conformity assessment, and continuous risk man- agement. For agentic GenAI, the primary assurance claim shifts away from model-level accuracy toward system-level behaviour. What matters is whether the system behaves acceptably across its full deci- sion–action loop, encompassing routine operation, degraded conditions, and complex interactions with external tools or services. While the AI Risk Atlas helps surface likely failure modes, the EU AI Act requires such risks to be actively constrained and managed throughout deployment rather than merely identified at design time. As summarized in Figure 3, an EU AI Act-ready assurance case rests on five mutually reinforcing elements: lifecycle-wide assur- ance, a structured assurance argument, account- ability across decision and action, clearly defined operational boundaries, and continuous evidence generation through monitoring. Operational boundaries form a cornerstone of this structure. They define which actions an agent may perform, under what conditions, and with what degree of autonomy. These boundaries correspond directly to the Act’s treatment of high-risk systems and prohibited practices. From a reliability perspec- tive, they function not as rigid limits but as adjusta- ble control surfaces. As post-market monitoring data accumulates, the system’s permitted scope of action can be expanded, restricted, or rescoped based on observed behavior and emerging risks. Assurance evidence must be grounded in actual system operation rather than idealized design assumptions. Event logs, decision traces, records of blocked or redirected actions, and recovery histories serve as primary assurance artifacts, not auxiliary diagnostics. Together, they support the Act’s require- ments for traceability, transparency, and market surveillance. Crucially, this evidence must capture behavior under challenging conditions—ambigu- ous inputs, degraded tool availability, or competing objectives—since these are the contexts in which reliability is truly tested. Responsibility for control must also be explicitly engineered. Escalation thresholds, override path- ways, and structured review processes make visible how authority is distributed between automated components and organizational actors. These mech- anisms provide concrete proof that accountability remains anchored within the organization, reinforc- ing the governance principles embedded through- out the Act while preserving the efficiency gains that motivate agentic deployment. An EU AI Act-ready assurance case must there- fore remain dynamic. Agentic systems evolve through model updates, tool integrations, and shifting operational contexts. Reliability-by-design treats assurance as a continuous activity, sustained through post-market monitoring and feedback loops that inform both technical refinement and governance decisions. In this role, the assurance case becomes more than a regulatory response—it becomes a durable engineering asset, enabling dis- ciplined scaling, informed control, and sustained confidence in systems that are increasingly empow- ered to act. Figure 3. Key components of an EU AI Act-ready assurance case for agentic GenAI, linking lifecycle assurance, accountability, operational boundaries, and continuous evidence to reliable autonomous operation. By coupling probabilistic reasoning with direct action, these systems challenge long-standing assumptions about control, accountability, and failure detec- tion. The reliability community is well positioned to respond. Hazard analysis, traceability, human over- sight, and robustness under stress have long guided the design of dependable systems. The emergence of agentic AI does not displace these principles; it places them under new pressure and requires their careful reinterpretation. This transition echoes earlier milestones in the history of automation. As before, success will depend less on technical novelty than on disciplined engineering practice. Systems that act must be con- strained, observed, and corrected with the same rigor applied to any component whose failure carries real consequences. Reliability engineering offers the con- ceptual tools to meet this moment. Extending familiar principles into the domain of autonomous reasoning allows increased capability without surrendering con- trol, preserving the central promise of the discipline that progress and dependability need not be oppos- ing goals. History suggests that when automation advances faster than assurance, reliability debt accu- mulates quietly—until it is paid under pressure.   Acknowledgments I am grateful to the Editor-in-Chief, Prof. Phillip A.
