title: Retrieval-augmented patch
file_name: Retrievalaugmented-patch-generation-for-geosynchronous-satellite-status-forecasting_2026_Nature-Research.pdf
text: Retrieval-augmented patch generation for geosynchronous satellite status forecasting Shu-He Tian, Yu-Qiang Fang & Ya-Sheng Zhang Accurate geosynchronous satellites status forecasting is essential for improving space situational awareness and supporting downstream tasks such as maneuver detection and intent inference. However, current models face difficulty modeling high-orbit observation data due to its strong non- stationary and weak periodic structure, which hinders effective long-range dependency learning and adaptation to evolving orbital dynamics. To address these challenges, we propose RAPG, a Retrieval- Augmented Patch Generation framework for geosynchronous satellite status analysis, including status forecasting and intent classification tasks. RAPG integrates two synergistic components: (1) an adaptive frequency-domain patching mechanism that dynamically segments satellites’ status time series according to their dominant spectral characteristics, enabling the model to capture both short-term and long-term temporal structures; and (2) a retrieval-augmented generation module that accesses historically similar patches from the training set, providing explicit external memory for reconstructing weakly periodic and non-repetitive motion patterns. By linking adaptive patching with retrieval-based augmentation, RAPG enhances temporal representation learning and generalization across diverse orbital conditions. Experiments on two synthetic datasets and one real-world dataset show that RAPG consistently surpasses strong baselines in both status forecasting and intent classification. RAPG yields the lowest forecasting errors and achieves an F1-score of 0.9461 and an accuracy of 0.9443 for intent classification, outperforming baseline methods. These results confirm that RAPG provides a robust and scalable framework for real-time analysis of geosynchronous satellites, offering significant potential for enhancing remote sensing-based space domain awareness and dynamic orbital intelligence. With the increasing saturation of geosynchronous-orbit (GEO) resources and the growing number of objects operating in high-altitude orbits1, efficient monitoring and accurate forecasting of satellite states have become pressing scientific challenges2,3. Predicting key dynamic variables such as position, velocity, and attitude is essential for understanding on-orbit behavior and for supporting downstream tasks including maneuver detection. Compared with traditional passive monitoring, data-driven analysis of historical GEO observation sequences enables more responsive and forward-looking assessments of satellite activities, thereby improving the ability to cope with complex or uncooperative maneuvers4. This need is particularly acute for targets without reliable communication links, where the precision of external observations directly affects collision avoidance, mission planning, and overall situational assessment. In this context, observational and modeling techniques– commonly employed across remote sensing, space physics, and orbital dynamics–provide indispensable tools for continuous tracking and dynamic characterization of high-orbit objects5. Advancing real-time state analysis for GEO satellites therefore imposes stringent requirements on data processing and behavioral modeling, while contributing broadly to space-domain awareness and the scientific understanding of orbital systems6,7. High-orbit satellite status analysis is crucial for space situational awareness but remains challenging due to the complexity of observations from targets8. Monitored by ground-based remote sensing9, these targets generate high-dimensional time series, complicating feature extraction7. Frequent micro-adjustments and sudden maneuvers introduce strong nonlinearity and non-stationary, while measurement uncertainties reduce prediction reliability. Existing methods also face computational constraints and difficulties in fusing multi- sensor data10, limiting real-time performance. Time-series-oriented prediction methods are therefore essential to improve the stability, accuracy, and practicality of geosynchronous orbit satellite forecasting under complex real-world conditions. The orbital state data of high-orbit targets naturally form time series7,11,12, making them well-suited for sequential modeling tasks13–15. Modeling approaches can be grouped into four categories: non-parametric16, Space Engineering University, Beijing 101416, China. email: fangyuqiang@nudt.edu.cn OPEN Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:6916 1 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38475-x www.nature.com/scientificreports shallow machine learning17, deep neural networks18, and ensemble learning19. Non-parametric methods, such as dynamic time warping with k-means clustering20,21 and kernel density estimation22, provide flexibility for complex, distribution-rich data, while random forests detect anomalous behaviors23. Shallow models, including SVMs with similarity-based clustering24 and Gaussian binary classifiers25, offer computational efficiency for maneuver prediction under limited parameter settings. Deep learning approaches leverage powerful representation learning capabilities, with neural networks modeling maneuver behavior probabilistically26,27, 1D-CNNs detecting displacements from two-line element set(TLE) data28,29, and Transformer architectures extracting relative motion features from imagery30. Ensemble learning methods enhance robustness by integrating multiple models, including unsupervised clustering fusion31, survival analysis frameworks32, and ensemble classifiers such as AdaBoost and bagging for trajectory and maneuver classification33. Collectively, these strategies advance the field of status analysis study on high-orbit satellites. Despite advances, existing methods face limitations in real-world satellite status analysis due to trajectory uncertainty and dynamic high-orbit behaviors such as frequent maneuvers and fragmented trajectories34–37. This highlights the need for generalized, adaptive, and efficient frameworks capable of handling multi-source time series for real-time modeling of geosynchronous satellites. Recent progress in long time series modeling offers relevant insights, with approaches including RNNs and their variants (LSTM, GRU)38,39, CNNs40–42, attention- based Transformers43–45, and linear models such as DLinear46,47. RNNs capture short-term dependencies but struggle with long-range patterns and parallelization48–50, while CNNs provide computational efficiency but limited global context. Transformers excel at modeling long-range dependencies through self-attention, and linear models offer simplicity and strong generalization. However, these approaches often assume regularly sampled, continuous, and periodic data, conditions rarely met in high-orbit sequences7,51. Such sequences are typically non-stationary and heterogeneous, limiting existing models’ generalization and effectiveness in capturing time-dependent orbital patterns. To address the challenges of geosynchronous satellite status analysis, we propose the Retrieval-Augmented Patch Generation (RAPG) model, which integrates adaptive temporal representation with external memory retrieval to achieve precise and robust predictions. RAPG comprises two interrelated modules. The first is an adaptive patching mechanism based on frequency-domain analysis52,53, where dominant frequency components of input sequences are extracted via the discrete Fourier transform to dynamically adjust patch lengths. This enables the Transformer backbone to capture intra-patch short-term dynamics and inter-patch long-term dependencies effectively, improving adaptability to time-varying orbital patterns. The second module, inspired by retrieval-augmented generation techniques54, retrieves top-matching historical patches from the training set as auxiliary inputs, providing explicit external memory access that enhances reconstruction of long-range and weakly periodic structures. By explicitly linking adaptive patching with retrieval-based augmentation, RAPG synergistically models both local temporal variability and long-range historical dependencies, overcoming limitations of conventional deep models that rely solely on increased depth or capacity. The main contributions of RAPG are summarized as follows. • We propose an adaptive patching strategy based on frequency-domain analysis, tailored for satellite status forecasting. This mechanism strengthens the model’s capacity to represent long-term temporal relationships and accommodate structural variation. • We introduce a historical pattern retrieval module, which provides explicit memory support for modeling weakly periodic and non-stationary patterns, enhancing generalization in real-world orbital forecasting sce­ narios. • Extensive evaluations on synthetic and real datasets show that RAPG surpasses strong baselines in status forecasting accuracy, intent classification performance, and generalization capability. Study data Reliable modeling and prediction of geosynchronous satellite states are essential for tasks such as maneuver detection, intent inference, and collision risk assessment, all of which require high-quality time series datasets reflecting both simulated and real orbital dynamics. To support research on geosynchronous satellite motion status forecasting and intent behavior classification modeling, three representative datasets are adopted in this study, encompassing both synthetic and real-world orbital data. Their overall statistical properties are summarized in Table 1. Feature Dataset 1 Dataset 2 Dataset 3 Data type Simulated (Forecasting) Real-world (Forecasting) Simulated (Forecasting) Simulated (Classification) Number of samples 80,000 10,000 525,000 525,000 Feature dimension 16 16 11 11 Sequence length 128 128 36 36 Training input shape (72,000, 36, 16) (8,000, 36, 16) (500,000, 12, 11) (405,000, 36, 11) Training output shape (72,000, 72, 16) (8,000, 72, 16) (500,000, 24, 11) (405,000, 1) Testing input shape (8,000, 36, 16) (2,000, 36, 16) (25,000, 12, 11) (75,000, 36, 11) Testing output shape (8,000, 72, 16) (2,000, 72, 16) (25,000, 12, 11) (75,000, 1) Table 1. Statistical summary of datasets used for forecasting and classification experiments. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:6916 2 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38475-x www.nature.com/scientificreports/ Satellite maneuver simulation dataset The first dataset is generated through an orbital dynamics simulation platform, containing 21,138 synthetic trajectories3. Each trajectory records the temporal evolution of orbital parameters that reflect various maneuver scenarios. This dataset primarily captures fundamental orbital motion patterns governed by celestial mechanics, enabling the analysis of dynamic correlations and time-dependent changes in satellite states. It serves as a valuable foundation for studying time-dependent variations in orbital features that are closely related to maneuver onset and control behavior. Real satellite mode-change dataset The second dataset originates from the MIT ARCLab SPLID-DevKit55, which integrates high-precision ephemeris data and VCM (Vector Covariance Message) records provided by satellite operators. Unlike simulated datasets, this collection reflects real-world orbital perturbations, sensor noise, and operational uncertainties. It thus provides an authentic representation of non-ideal conditions that satellites experience in actual missions. From a research perspective, this dataset bridges the gap between theoretical modeling and operational application, offering critical insights into the dynamic transitions and mode changes of active satellites in GEO. RPO relative motion dataset for intent analysis The third dataset focuses on the relative motion between an observing and a target satellite within the context of Rendezvous and Proximity Operations (RPO). Using the orbital simulation environment, approximately 525,000 synthetic trajectory sequences are constructed, each representing a three-day observation window sampled every two hours (36 time steps). Eleven kinematic and positional features are recorded for each sequence, as detailed in Table 2. This dataset reflects a wide range of satellite motion intents, such as approach, retreat, inspection, and station-keeping, which are summarized in Table 3. The comprehensive design allows for the exploration Intent type Description Forward linear approach Direct linear approach Reverse linear retreat Linear retreat in reverse direction Stationary hold Fixed-point station-keeping Translational hold Translational hovering motion Forward fly-around Forward orbital fly-around Reverse fly-around Reverse orbital fly-around Spiraling descent Downward spiral motion Spiraling ascent Upward spiral motion Forward spiral trajectory Forward helical trajectory Reverse spiral trajectory Reverse helical trajectory Rectangular inspection Rectangular inspection pattern Non-intersecting safe retreat Safe retreat without intersection Safe hold orbit Stable orbital holding state Forward curved approach Forward curved approach path Reverse curved retreat Reverse curved retreat path V-bar release V-bar separation maneuver Table 3. Defined intent categories for the satellite intent classification task. Feature Description Example Value Timestamp Observation timestamp 2022-09-01 00:00:00 Radial position (km) Radial position relative to observer -10888.16353 In-Track position (km) Along-track position component -28277.64045 Cross-track position (km) Cross-track position component 0 Radial velocity (km/s) Radial velocity component 1.20×10−16 In-track velocity (km/s) Along-track velocity component 8.11×10−16 Cross-track velocity (km/s) Cross-track velocity component 0 x (km) Cartesian X coordinate (relative position) 17838370.02 y (km) Cartesian Y coordinate (relative position) 38204848.97 z (km) Cartesian Z coordinate (relative position) -50599.11012 Distance (km) Euclidean distance between satellites 17838370.02 Table 2. Description of input features in the simulated geosynchronous satellite trajectory dataset. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:6916 3 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38475-x www.nature.com/scientificreports/ of temporal dependencies between historical motion patterns and subsequent orbital intentions, providing an essential reference for time series–based intent inference research. All three datasets are grounded in orbital mechanics and collectively reflect different aspects of satellite motion–from controlled simulations to real-world operational behavior and synthetic intent-based interactions. This combination enables a comprehensive understanding of geosynchronous satellite dynamics and supports research on status analysis, and intelligent interpretation of non-cooperative satellite behaviors within the geosynchronous environment. Methodology Architecture overview The proposed Retrieval-Augmented Patch Generation (RAPG) model for the status analysis of geosynchronous satellites is illustrated in Fig. 1. The overall framework comprises two main components: an Adaptive Frequency Patching module and a Patch Retrieval Block for retrieval-augmented patch generation. In the adaptive frequency patching module, each satellite status sequence is initially converted from the time domain to the frequency domain through the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)52. The resulting amplitude spectrum captures periodic patterns within each sequence. By analyzing the amplitude magnitudes, we segment each time series into period patches with different temporal scales. Each segmented patch is then mapped into a fixed-dimensional embedding via a linear projection layer, forming a sequence of tokens for the subsequent Transformer encoder. The patch retrieval block takes all patches obtained by the adaptive frequency patching module as input and applies a key-value retrieval strategy, where a pair of adjacent patches in the training set is treated as a key-value pair. The value patch represents the future dynamics associated with its preceding key patch. For each input key patch, a retrieval module identifies the most relevant key patches across the training set based on similarity. This retrieval follows an attention-style mechanism, where the top-k most similar key patches are selected, and their associated value patches are aggregated via a weighted sum to synthesize a retrieval-augmented value patch. Adaptive frequency patching For the status analysis of geosynchronous satellites, the input data typically manifests as multivariate time series with evolving temporal dynamics. The satellite state sequences consist of multiple observed attributes including orbital parameters (e.g., semi-major axis, eccentricity), attitude measurements (e.g., pitch, yaw), and dynamic properties (e.g., velocity, acceleration). These attributes are collected at regular intervals and can be formally represented as a multivariate time series. X = {x1, x2, . . . , xT }, xt ∈Rm,  (1) where T denotes the sequence length and m is the number of observed variables. During normal operations, each state vector xt reflects stable behavior (as shown in Fig. 2). However, occasional maneuver behaviors, such as orbit adjustments or attitude corrections, can cause abrupt deviations in certain variables. We denote these maneuver-related state vectors as x(m) t , which exhibit significant divergence from the regular dynamics in both magnitude and variation patterns. As a result, the satellite status sequence is characterized by long stationary segments interspersed with sparse disruptive events, which imposes challenges on effective modeling and forecasting. Figure 3 illustrates the process flow for modeling future state predictions based on the raw input sequences given from the satellite state. Accurate forecasting of future states xT +1, xT +2, . . . and intent classification requires identifying both global periodicity and local disruptions56. To capture these heterogeneous temporal structures, we introduce Fig. 1. The overall architecture of the proposed RAPG model. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:6916 4 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38475-x www.nature.com/scientificreports/ an Adaptive Frequency Patching strategy that automatically determines an appropriate patch length from the dominant frequency of each univariate sequence. This ensures that the generated patches align with intrinsic periodic patterns while retaining local fidelity. Given a univariate sequence x = [x1, x2, . . . , xL] of length L, we first apply the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)52 to extract its frequency components: Xk = L−1 ∑ t=0 xt · e−2πikt/L, k = 0, 1, . . . , L −1,  (2) where Xk is the complex coefficient at frequency index k. The amplitude spectrum is computed as: Ak = |Xk| = √ Re(Xk)2 + Im(Xk)2.  (3) We then identify the dominant frequency f ∗ as: f ∗= arg max k∈[1,⌊L/2⌋] Ak.  (4) The corresponding periodic length is computed as: p = ⌊L f∗ ⌋ . For multivariate sequences X ∈RL×m, the above procedure is independently44 applied to each variable x(j) to obtain a set of candidate periodic lengths {pj}m j=1. To balance granularity and coherence across patches, the final patch length P for each variable is determined via: P = min (⌊p 2 ⌋ , δ ) ,  (5) where δ is a predefined maximum patch length. This truncation mitigates the risk of overly long patches in the presence of low-frequency components, preserving localized structural features even when the periodic signal is weak or noisy. Each resulting patch is further embedded into a fixed-dimensional vector space, producing patch embeddings z(j) i ∈Rd for downstream forecasting and classification task modeling. This frequency-aware patching not only avoids heuristic segmentation but also adapts to intrinsic temporal structures, improving the quality of Fig. 3. Input and output time series describing maneuver states for geosynchronous satellite status analysis. Fig. 2. Overview of historical data used for forecasting maneuver sequences of geosynchronous satellites. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:6916 5 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38475-x www.nature.com/scientificreports/ learned representations. Particularly in satellite status forecasting and intent classification tasks with pronounced periodicity, this strategy enables better alignment of salient patterns and boosts the model’s generalization ability. Patch retrieval block To enhance the status analysis performance of geosynchronous satellite trajectory, this section uses the patch embeddings obtained from the Adaptive Frequency Patching module and builds a task-specific key-value retrieval structure to exploit historical patterns for trend-aware modeling. Specifically, we first segment the satellite status sequence X using a sliding window of length Q, resulting in a sequence of input patches. For each patch starting at time step i, the current patch is treated as a key ki ∈RQ×m (m is the number of variables), and the subsequent patch of length F as its corresponding value vi ∈RF ×m. Thus, we construct key and value sets: K = {ki}T −Q−F +1 i=1 , V = {vi}T −Q−F +1 i=1 .  (6) During training, the current input patch x ∈RQ×m is used as the query for retrieval. To improve comparability across different value scales, we normalize all patches by subtracting the last timestep: ˆxt = xt −xQ, ∀t ∈{1, ..., Q}.  (7) Similarly, each key ki and value vi is processed into ˆki and ˆvi. We adopt Pearson correlation57 as the similarity metric to compute the affinity between the normalized query ˆx and all normalized keys: ρi = s(ˆx, ˆki), ∀i ∈{1, ..., |K|}.  (8) The top-k keys with the highest similarity scores are selected: J = arg top-k i ρi. We then compute the soft weights for the corresponding value patches using a softmax function with temperature τ: wi = exp(ρi/τ) ∑ j∈J exp(ρj/τ) · ⊮i∈J.  (9) Next, each retrieved value patch vi is passed through a shared Transformer encoder to obtain embeddings: Enc(vi) ∈RF ×d. The final enhanced representation ˜e is computed as a weighted sum over the top-k encoded value patches: ˜e = ∑ i∈J wi · Enc(vi).  (10) During inference, the target patch x is also encoded as Enc(x) ∈RL×d and passed through a Transformer decoder to obtain a base prediction: ˆy(0) = Dec(Enc(x)). The enhanced representation ˜e is also decoded to produce an auxiliary prediction or intent classification: ˆy(1) = Dec(˜e). The final prediction or intent classification result is formed by concatenating both outputs: ˆy = concat(ˆy(0), ˆy(1)). Finally, the prediction result ˆy ∈RF ×C is compared to the ground-truth future segment y ∈RF ×C. For the status forecasting task, the proposed RAPG model is trained by minimizing the Mean Squared Error (MSE): LMSE = 1 F · C F ∑ t=1 C ∑ j=1 (ˆyt,j −yt,j)2 .  (11) For the intent classification task, we additionally apply the standard cross-entropy loss to the output ˆy, where c denotes the intent class index: LCE = − K ∑ c=1 yc log ˆyc,  (12) where K is the total number of intent classes and y is the one-hot ground-truth label. This retrieval-augmented mechanism enables the model to jointly learn local dynamic representations and long-term patterns from historical states, thereby improving status forecasting performance and intent classification accuracy under scenarios of periodicity and sparse observations commonly found in geosynchronous satellite telemetry. Overall training To optimize the parameters of the proposed RAPG model, a unified training objective is designed to balance point-wise status analysis performance and structural consistency across temporal patches. The total objective combines the mean squared error loss LMSE (refer to Eq.(11)) or cross-entropy loss LCE (refer to Eq.(12)) with a patch-level structural alignment term LPS49. To capture higher-order temporal dependencies and preserve the intrinsic structure of orbital motion patterns, the Patch-wise Structural (PS) loss LPS is introduced following the setting in49. This loss evaluates the consistency between predicted and ground-truth patches from three Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:6916 6 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38475-x www.nature.com/scientificreports/ complementary perspectives: correlation, variance, and mean alignment. First, the correlation loss encourages trend consistency by maximizing the correlation between the predicted and actual patch trajectories, ensuring that the model captures the overall temporal direction and evolution of orbital sequences. Second, the variance loss enforces similarity in dynamic variability by aligning the distributional spread of predicted and ground- truth patches, promoting stable modeling of oscillatory and maneuver-intensive behaviors. Third, the mean loss aligns the central tendency of each patch pair, preventing systematic bias in predicted state magnitudes and improving global trajectory coherence. The PS loss is thus defined as: LPS = αLCorr + βLVar + γLMean,  (13) where LCorr, LVar, and LMean represent the correlation, variance, and mean consistency terms, respectively. The coefficients α, β, and γ are adaptive weights that control the relative contribution of each structural constraint, determined according to the strategy introduced in49. In this formulation, the inputs Y (i) p and ˆY (i) p correspond to the ground-truth and the predicted patches within each time segment, while the output LPS quantifies their structural divergence in a unified metric space. Finally, the total training objective for RAPG is expressed as: Ltotal = Ltask + λLPS,  (14) where λ serves as a trade-off parameter that adjusts the importance of two loss functions. The term Ltask corresponds to LMSE for status forecasting or LCE for intent classification. This joint objective enables RAPG to maintain precise short-term predictions while capturing complex long-term dependencies and structural regularities inherent in geosynchronous satellite dynamics. Experiments Experimental settings To comprehensively assess the performance of the proposed RAPG model in status forecasting and intent classification performance of geosynchronous satellites, several experiments are conducted on the benchmark datasets introduced in the Study Data section. These datasets contain time-series observations representing multi-source orbital telemetry, including position, velocity, and attitude parameters obtained from remote sensing measurements. Each sequence captures diverse orbital behaviors, ranging from stable station-keeping to frequent maneuver activities, thereby providing a rigorous basis for evaluating status analysis performance under realistic operational conditions. For experimental consistency, all comparative models, including RAPG and the baselines, are trained and validated using identical pre-processing and normalization procedures. Each dataset is temporally segmented into training, validation, and testing subsets to avoid data leakage across different orbital phases. The raw telemetry signals are resampled into uniform temporal intervals and normalized to zero mean and unit variance before being input into the models. Baselines To validate the effectiveness of the proposed RAPG framework, we compare it against nine representative baseline models that span recurrent, convolutional, and Transformer-based time series architectures. Each baseline is re-implemented under identical pre-processing, input segmentation, and training protocols to ensure a fair comparison. • LSTM13: A classical recurrent neural network architecture that employs gated memory units to capture tem­ poral dependencies across sequential data. In this study, LSTM is utilized to reconstruct satellite telemetry sequences, and its reconstruction residuals are analyzed to identify potential maneuver events. Elevated pre­ diction errors generally correspond to abrupt attitude or orbit changes. • TimesNet41: A convolutional model that transforms one-dimensional temporal signals into two-dimensional representations, enabling it to learn multi-periodic and hierarchical temporal features. The model excels at modeling regular orbital motion, and significant deviations in its reconstruction indicate abnormal dynamic behavior. • GPT4TS43: A Transformer-based time series model that fine-tunes a subset of parameters from a large pre- trained backbone. GPT4TS is trained using masked sequence prediction, thereby learning to infer missing temporal patterns. Discrepancies between predicted and ground-truth segments are treated as indicators of satellite maneuvers or operational anomalies. • ModernTCN42: An advanced temporal convolutional neural network featuring dilated convolutions with large receptive fields to capture both short- and long-term dependencies. It provides efficient sequence mod­ eling with stable gradient flow, and large reconstruction errors are interpreted as signatures of non-stationary behavior or orbit perturbations. • TimeMixer47: A recently proposed architecture that introduces multiscale mixing blocks to disentangle lo­ cal dynamics from long-range temporal structures. Its hierarchical forecasting mechanism facilitates robust temporal representation learning. Deviations between predicted and observed sequences are used to detect potential maneuver occurrences. • iTransformer45: A Transformer variant that redefines the conventional attention pipeline by reversing the roles of the self-attention and feed-forward components. The feed-forward layers encode time series rep­ resentations, while the attention mechanism models inter-variable correlations, enhancing the model’s adapt­ ability to heterogeneous satellite telemetry. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:6916 7 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38475-x www.nature.com/scientificreports/ • MCMD3: A pre-training framework that integrates masked and clustered learning strategies to enhance robustness and transferability in geosynchronous satellite maneuver detection. By jointly leveraging these strategies, MCMD captures diverse maneuver patterns and improves detection performance across different scenarios. • LightGTS50: A lightweight time series forecasting model emphasizing consistent periodic pattern modeling. It employs periodical tokenization to extract stable periodic features from multi-source datasets with varying scales, thereby improving generalization in cross-dataset pre-training. • PS-loss49: A novel Patch-wise Structural (PS) loss for time series forecasting that enhances structural align­ ment by evaluating local patches rather than individual points. By leveraging statistical properties such as mean, variance, and correlation, PS-Loss captures subtle structural differences that point-wise losses often overlook. Implementation details All experiments with the RAPG model are conducted in Python using the PyTorch framework. Model training employs the Adam optimizer with an initial learning rate of 0.001 and a batch size of 32. Training is conducted for a maximum of 100 epochs. The encoder adopts a Transformer-based backbone, which effectively captures long-range temporal dependencies across satellite telemetry sequences. In Eq. (5), the parameter δ is set to 8. The top-k is set to 2. In the composite objective function Ltotal, the balance coefficient λ associated with the clustering-guided pre-training term is empirically fixed at 0.01 to stabilize representation learning during optimization. During both training and inference, the retrieval set in RAPG consists solely of historical data from other trajectories in the training set and excludes any future information from the test trajectory being predicted. At inference time, for a given input sequence, the model is restricted to retrieving patches only from historical patches observed during training. All experiments are performed on a workstation with two NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs. To ensure reproducibility and statistical reliability, each experiment is conducted with five different random seeds, and the reported results represent the mean performance across these runs. In the status forecasting setup, the RAPG model takes 12 consecutive historical observations as input to predict the subsequent 24 future steps, forming a complete 36-step temporal window. The reconstructed and predicted representations are subsequently processed by a GRU-attention classifier, which infers the operational state transitions and maneuver intentions of geosynchronous satellites. Model performance is comprehensively evaluated using both regression-oriented and classification-oriented criteria. For status forecasting, we evaluate model performance using Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) to jointly measure prediction accuracy and the smoothness of reconstructed orbital sequences. For intent classification, we report Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score to assess the balance between detection sensitivity and reliability. Due to the significant class imbalance inherent in satellite maneuver detection, Accuracy serves primarily as a reference, while greater emphasis is placed on Precision, Recall, and especially F1-Score, which better reflect classification quality under imbalanced conditions. Together, these metrics offer a comprehensive assessment of the model’s predictive accuracy and its ability to detect maneuver- related anomalies in complex satellite telemetry. The simulated datasets used in this study, together with the source code of RAPG, are publicly available ​h​t​t​p​ s​:​/​/​d​r​i​v​e​.​g​o​o​g​l​e​.​c​o​m​/​d​r​i​v​e​/​f​o​l​d​e​r​s​/​1​7​h​e​3​6​h​r​D​A​_​u​e​F​V​_​S​U​J​T​Y​u​b​4​p​L​l​k​q​B​A​u​u. Also, the MIT ARCLab dataset can be accessed online​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​w​w​w​.​d​r​o​p​b​o​x​.​c​o​m​/​s​c​l​/​f​o​/​j​t​5​h​1​f​8​2​i​y​c​j​b​8​e​l​y​b​m​l​z​/​h​?​r​l​k​e​y​=​b​j​c​m​n​y​4​8​6​d​d​f​7​m​0​j​7​b​9​ u​o​k​9​w​w​%​2​6​e​=​1​%​2​6​d​l​=​0. Main results in geosynchronous satellite status analysis The status forecasting results obtained on both the simulated maneuver dataset and the real satellite mode- change dataset consistently demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed RAPG framework. As shown in Tables 4 and 5, RAPG achieves the best performance across all evaluation metrics, outperforming nine state-of- the-art baselines by a substantial margin in both controlled and real-world orbital environments. This performance gain indicates that the model can accurately capture the underlying deterministic orbital dynamics and the subtle changes associated with maneuver execution. The advantage of RAPG becomes even more pronounced on the real satellite mode-change dataset, where the model achieves an MSE of 0.0121, outperforming the best baseline. Real-world geosynchronous data contains complex perturbations, sensor-induced noise, and non-stationary behavior arising from operational mode transitions, making accurate forecasting substantially more challenging. The strong generalization ability of RAPG in this setting highlights the effectiveness of the historical pattern retrieval module. Overall, the superior performance of RAPG on both datasets verifies that its dual-design philosophy–frequency-adaptive patching for structural representation learning and retrieval-augmented modeling for the geosynchronous status forecasting task. The consistent improvements observed in both synthetic and real geosynchronous environments indicate that RAPG is not only capable of capturing the physics-informed temporal structures governing satellite motion but is also highly robust to real-world uncertainties, making it well-suited for practical geosynchronous satellite status monitoring and downstream space situational awareness applications. As shown in Table 6, the proposed RAPG model achieves the highest performance across all evaluation metrics, demonstrating its superior capability in both geosynchronous satellite status forecasting and intent classification tasks. Specifically, RAPG attains an F1-Score of 0.9461 and an Accuracy of 0.9443, both of which are higher than those of other baseline methods. From a status forecasting perspective, RAPG also achieves the lowest prediction errors, with an MSE of 0.0010 and an RMSE of 0.0323 reflecting its strong ability to model fine- grained temporal dependencies. These improvements can be attributed to two key design aspects of RAPG. First, the adaptive frequency patching mechanism effectively captures multi-frequency temporal dynamics within orbital motion sequences, reducing information loss during representation learning. Second, the retrieval- augmented patch generation process enables RAPG to utilize structurally similar trajectory patterns from the training set, enhancing its generalization in maneuver-intensive scenarios. In contrast, conventional models such as LSTM and ModernTCN exhibit limited sensitivity to high-frequency orbital changes, while pre-trained transformers (e.g., GPT4TS) struggle to generalize to satellite behaviors due to the lack of specialized temporal Method Accuracy Precision Recall F1-Score MSE MAE RMSE LSTM 0.7098 0.7683 0.7098 0.7379 0.0052 0.0599 0.0723 TimesNet 0.6362 0.6681 0.6362 0.6518 0.0046 0.0561 0.0679 GPT4TS 0.7241 0.7456 0.7241 0.7216 0.0049 0.0570 0.0703 ModernTCN 0.8765 0.9011 0.8765 0.8886 0.0031 0.0450 0.0557 TimeMixer 0.9104 0.9209 0.9104 0.9156 0.0020 0.0372 0.0448 iTransformer 0.7216 0.7323 0.7216 0.7269 0.0040 0.0501 0.0632 MCMD 0.9175 0.9287 0.9175 0.9231 0.0020 0.0362 0.0447 LightGTS 0.9002 0.9138 0.9002 0.9069 0.0022 0.0384 0.0474 PS-Loss 0.7834 0.8295 0.7834 0.8058 0.0036 0.0474 0.0602 RAPG (Ours) 0.9443 0.9481 0.9443 0.9461 0.0010 0.0224 0.0323 Table 6. Overall, the results verify that RAPG achieves a balanced improvement in forecasting precision and classification reliability, establishing a robust foundation for satellite trajectory prediction and intent inference. Ablation study To further validate the effectiveness of each component in the proposed RAPG framework, we conduct an ablation study focusing on two critical modules: the Patch-wise Structural Loss and the Retrieval-Augmented Generation mechanism. Three variants are compared: the full RAPG model (with both patch loss and retrieval), a variant without retrieval (no retrieval), and another without the patch loss term (no patch loss). All variants use the same GRU-Attention classifier and are evaluated using identical settings as the main experiment. As presented in Table 7, the removal of the retrieval mechanism leads to the most significant performance degradation across all metrics, confirming its critical role in improving long-term forecasting robustness. The RAPG (ours) model achieves the highest accuracy of 0.9443 and the lowest MSE of 0.0010. This suggests that the retrieval-augmented generation mechanism effectively mitigates sequence drift by leveraging structurally similar historical trajectories, thereby enhancing temporal consistency and stability. The patch loss module also contributes positively by improving local structural coherence through patch-level optimization. While the performance gain from the patch loss is smaller than that of retrieval enhancement, it still improves both predictive smoothness and classification confidence (F1-Score from 0.9195 to 0.9461). Together, these results demonstrate that both modules jointly strengthen the ability of RAPG to capture fine-grained temporal dynamics and spatial correlations in satellite behavior. To assess necessity of status analysis enhancement for the downstream intent classification task, we conduct an additional experiment where classification networks directly take the past 12 observed samples as input, without prediction augmentation. Various classification backbones are compared under this setting, as shown in Table 8. The results in Table 8 reveal a substantial performance drop when forecasting is removed, indicating that prediction-based sequence extension is essential for effective intent recognition. The GRU-Attention classifier achieves the best performance among the tested backbones (Accuracy 0.5128). This verifies that status forecasting not only enhances temporal continuity but also enriches discriminative representations for intent classification. Overall, the ablation results confirm that each proposed component contributes distinct yet complementary benefits. The retrieval mechanism primarily enhances long-term stability and generalization, while the patch-wise structural loss refines localized temporal representations. Furthermore, the integration of forecasting within the classification pipeline is shown to be indispensable for achieving reliable intent inference under orbital conditions. Visualization analysis The visual comparisons presented in Figs. 4, 5 and 6 provide an intuitive assessment of the forecasting capability of the proposed RAPG model across the three geosynchronous satellite status datasets. Moreover, each magnified view (see Figs. 4, 5, 6) focuses on one or two representative regions where the differences between the predicted and ground-truth trajectories are most informative. By comparing the predicted trajectories with the corresponding ground-truth sequences, RAPG consistently demonstrates superior alignment with the true temporal evolution of satellite states, whereas the baseline models–MCMD, LightGTS, PS-Loss, TimeMixer, and ModernTCN–exhibit varying degrees of deviation, lag, or smoothing artifacts. On the satellite maneuver simulation dataset, RAPG captures both the secular orbital motion and the abrupt maneuver-induced variations with high fidelity, resulting in predictions that closely follow the ground-truth curves (Fig. 4). In contrast, several baseline methods either underestimate sharp state transitions or fail to recover the high-frequency components associated with maneuver execution. The strong correspondence between RAPG’s outputs and the ground-truth sequences underscores the advantages gained from its dual design–frequency-adaptive patch construction and retrieval-augmented pattern modeling–which jointly enhance the system’s ability to capture the complex multi-scale temporal dynamics of geosynchronous satellite motion. This parameter balances the contribution of the structural preservation loss LPS against the forecasting loss LMSE and classification loss LCE, thereby controlling the model’s emphasis on downstream task performance. As shown in Table 9, small values of λ (e.g., 1.0 × 10−4 to 1.0 × 10−3) slightly reduce the model’s performance, suggesting insufficient emphasis on structural consistency. Conversely, excessively large λ (e.g., 1.0 × 102) leads to a notable degradation in both classification and forecasting metrics, indicating over-regularization. These results highlight the sensitivity of RAPG to the choice of λ and justify its careful tuning for geosynchronous satellite forecasting. In addition, we analyze the impact of the hyperparameter δ on RAPG’s adaptive patch selection. When δ = 0, fixed patch lengths of 4, 8, and 12 were used, resulting in higher prediction errors due to the inability to capture varying periodic structures in satellite orbit data (Table 10). For δ > 0, patch lengths are determined adaptively based on each sequence’s dominant frequency, with δ as the maximum length. The results show that δ = 8 achieves the best performance, effectively balancing global periodicity and local details. Smaller δ values may fragment information, while larger δ values risk overlooking local patterns. Thus, δ = 8 represents an optimal trade-off, preserving local features while modeling long-period structures. The hyperparameter analysis in Table 11 shows that the retrieval mechanism in RAPG plays a crucial role in forecasting performance. When top-k = 0, RAPG does not utilize any historical patches, losing the ability to leverage similar past trajectories to reconstruct weakly periodic or non-repetitive motion patterns, which results in substantially higher prediction errors. Increasing top-k from 1 to 2 leads to noticeable improvements across all metrics, as a small number of highly relevant historical patches provides strong contextual support for accurately inferring future states. However, further increasing top-k to 4 causes performance degradation, indicating that incorporating more historical patches does not always improve results. Excessively large k may introduce less relevant or noisy patches, which dilute useful signals and bias the aggregation process, thereby reducing forecasting performance. Therefore, top-k = 2 represents an optimal trade-off, enabling the RAPG model to effectively exploit informative historical patterns while avoiding redundant or irrelevant information. Computational analysis We analyze the computational efficiency of RAPG in terms of running time and GPU memory usage, and compare it with representative baseline methods, including LSTM, TimesNet, TimeMixer, and LightGTS (see Table 12). LSTM exhibits the longest training and inference time due to its inherently sequential computation, which limits parallelization for long sequences. TimesNet incurs additional overhead from its 2D transformation and convolutional operations for modeling multi-periodicity. In contrast, Transformer-based models, especially TimeMixer and LightGTS, benefit from highly parallelizable architectures, resulting in faster training and inference. RAPG adopts LightGTS as its backbone, and when both the retrieval module and patch loss are removed, it reduces to LightGTS, achieving comparable efficiency. Removing the patch loss has negligible impact on inference latency, while the retrieval module accounts for most of the additional computational cost. The increased cost of RAPG mainly arises from the retrieval operation, which computes Pearson correlations between the current patch and the historical patch set. Although this process is computationally intensive, it is fully parallelized on the GPU. With more than 500k training samples and 100 training epochs, the full RAPG model requires approximately 36 hours for training and around 70 ms per sample during inference, which is acceptable for low-maneuver satellite state forecasting with minute-level temporal resolution. In terms of GPU memory usage, the Transformer backbone and the historical patch set are the primary contributors. During training, the retrieval library occupies approximately 5-6 GB of GPU memory, while during inference, removing the retrieval module reduces memory usage to about 5 GB. Overall, RAPG achieves a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency and predictive performance. top-k Metric MSE MAE RMSE 0 0.0032 0.0457 0.0570 1 0.0012 0.0258 0.0346 2 0.0010 0.0224 0.0323 4 0.0021 0.0365 0.0458 Table 11. Scientific Reports | (2026) 16:6916 15 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-38475-x www.nature.com/scientificreports/ Discussion The experimental evaluations confirm that the proposed RAPG model delivers consistent and robust forecasting and classification performance across multiple geosynchronous satellite scenarios. RAPG significantly outperforms existing baselines in terms of downstream task accuracy, temporal stability, and generalization to unseen orbital patterns. These improvements can be attributed to two core architectural mechanisms: the adaptive frequency-based patching and the retrieval-augmented reconstruction modules. The adaptive patching mechanism, derived from frequency-domain decomposition, allows the model to dynamically adjust temporal granularity based on dominant spectral components of input sequences. This enables efficient learning of both short-term fluctuations and long-term dependencies, which are essential for modeling complex satellite trajectories. Meanwhile, the retrieval-augmented module explicitly introduces external memory access by retrieving relevant historical patches, reinforcing the model’s ability to reconstruct weakly periodic or partially observed orbital states. Compared with conventional time-series analysis models, such as RNNs, CNNs, and Transformers44,48,58,59, RAPG provides superior adaptability to non-stationary and heterogeneous orbital dynamics. Traditional recurrent or convolutional models often exhibit limited sensitivity to variable sampling rates and degraded performance under incomplete sequences, while self-attention-based architectures rely heavily on fixed-length tokens and suffer from high computational costs for long temporal windows. In contrast, the adaptive patch generation strategy within RAPG enables multi-scale temporal modeling, significantly improving its resilience against uneven sampling and observation gaps. Furthermore, the retrieval mechanism mitigates the model’s dependence on purely learned internal representations by leveraging semantically similar historical patterns, thereby improving prediction consistency under distribution shifts and domain variations. Conclusions In this study, we introduced the Retrieval-Augmented Patch Generation (RAPG) model for geosynchronous satellites’ status analysis. By integrating adaptive frequency-domain patching with retrieval-based temporal augmentation, RAPG effectively addresses the challenges of non-stationary and weak periodicity that commonly arise in high-orbit remote sensing sequences. Extensive evaluations on two synthetic datasets and one real- world dataset show that RAPG consistently surpasses benchmark models in both forecasting performance and classification accuracy. These results verify the model’s capability to effectively learn robust temporal representations under complex orbital conditions, offering improved situational awareness and enhanced reliability for satellite monitoring tasks. Furthermore, the modular design of RAPG ensures scalability and adaptability across different remote sensing modalities, making it a practical foundation for space domain awareness applications. Future research will focus on extending RAPG to multi-sensor and multi-modal data fusion, enabling unified modeling of optical, radar, and telemetry sequences. Additionally, incorporating self-supervised or contrastive pre-training may further enhance the retrieval and representation capabilities of RAPG, advancing its potential as a general-purpose framework for dynamic high-orbit observation and intelligent space surveillance. Data availability Three datasets were used in our experiments, including Satellite Maneuver Simulation Dataset, Real Satellite Mode-Change Dataset, and RPO Relative Motion Dataset for Intent Analysis. The second dataset is available at the following URL: https://eval​.ai/web/chal​lenges/chall​enge-page/2​164/overview, while the first and the third dataset are newly generated, which were used under license for the current study and so are not publicly availa­ ble. If anyone wishes to request the data from this study, please contact the corresponding author Yu-Qiang Fang. However, the data are available upon request and with the permission of Space Engineering University. Received: 11 December 2025; Accepted: 29 January 2026

BMC Bioinformatics (2026) 27:16 https://doi.org/10.1186/s12859-025-06350-7 *Correspondence: Ron Stewart rstewart@morgridge.org 1Morgridge Institute for Research, Madison WI, 53715, USA SKiM-GPT: combining biomedical literature- based discovery with large language model hypothesis evaluation Jack Freeman1, Robert J. Millikin1, Leo Xu1, Ishaan Sharma1, Bethany Moore1, Cannon Lock1, Kevin Shine George1, Aviral Bal1, Chitrasen Mohanty1 and Ron Stewart1* BMC Bioinformatics Abstract Background Generating and testing hypotheses is a critical aspect of biomedical science. Typically, researchers generate hypotheses by carefully analyzing available information and making logical connections, which are then tested. The accelerating growth of biomedical literature makes it increasingly difficult to keep pace with connections between biological entities emerging across biomedical research. Recently developed automated means of generating hypotheses can generate many more hypotheses than can be easily tested. One such approach involves literature‑based discovery (LBD) systems such as Serial KinderMiner (SKiM), which surfaces putative A‑B‑C links derived from term co‑occurrence. However, LBD systems leave three critical gaps: (i) they find statistical associations, not biological relationships; (ii) they can produce false‑positive leads; and (iii) they do not assess agreement with a hypothesis in question. As a result, LBD search results often require costly manual curation to be of practical utility to the researcher. Large language models (LLMs) have the potential to automate much of this curation step, but standalone LLMs are hampered by hallucinations, lack of transparency in information sources, and the inability to reference data not included in the training corpus. Results We introduce SKiM-GPT, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system that combines SKiM’s co-occurrence search and retrieval with frontier LLMs to evaluate user-defined hypotheses. For every chosen A-B-C SKiM hit, SKiM-GPT retrieves appropriate PubMed abstract texts, filters out irrelevant abstracts with a fine-tuned relevance model, and prompts an LLM to evaluate the user’s hypothesis, given the relevant abstracts. Importantly, the SKiM-GPT system is transparent and human- verifiable: it displays the retrieved abstracts, the hypothesis score, and a justification for the score grounded in the texts and written in natural language. On a benchmark consisting of 14 disease-gene-drug hypotheses, SKiM-GPT achieves strong ordinal agreement with four expert biologists (Cohen’s κ = 0.84), demonstrating its ability to replicate expert judgment. Conclusions SKiM-GPT is open-source (https://github.com/stewart-lab/skimgpt) and available through a web interface (https://skim.morgridge.org), enabling both wet-lab Page 2 of 15 Freeman et al. BMC Bioinformatics (2026) 27:16 Background Literature-based discovery (LBD) is a field in which existing documented knowledge is searched, typically in an automated or semi-automated way, to aid in formulating hypotheses and finding novel discoveries by positing links between previously uncon­ nected ideas [30]. As the size of PubMed (currently over 37 million abstracts [29] and similar text databases grow, manual knowledge cataloging and connection-making becomes more difficult and LBD techniques become more important, as it is challeng­ ing for researchers to keep up to date with all relevant literature domains. Typically, an LBD “discovery” is in the form of an A-B-C relationship; A and B are two entities (genes, drugs, pathways, etc.) with an association, as are B and C. These two associations may imply a (possibly indirect) relationship between A and C, which may not yet be widely known. A classic example is fish oil as a treatment for Raynaud’s disease [32], discovered through their mutual link to blood viscosity. A major advantage of automated LBD methods is their ability to rapidly perform hundreds or thousands of searches with different terms to identify latent associations at a scale that would be impractical manually (e.g., with Google Scholar). However, while existing LBD methods can efficiently identify statistically significant associations between biomedical terms, they generally lack a structured framework for evaluating whether these discovered associations meaningfully support or contradict a specific scientific hypothesis. Tools such as Arrowsmith [34], Lion-LBD [27], BITOLA [15], and our tool Serial KinderMiner (SKiM) [22] primarily quantify the strength of association without assessing relevance to or support of a hypothesis. See [3] for a recent review of LBD methodologies. Consequently, researchers still face a significant burden of manu­ ally interpreting potentially thousands of associations, many of which are irrelevant or spurious [26]. Thus, methods capable of automated, hypothesis-focused evaluation of LBD outputs are urgently needed. Machine learning (ML), especially transformer-based large language models (LLMs), provide opportunities for contextualizing LBD-identified relationships due to their increased ability to capture nuanced semantic contexts compared to earlier approaches [40, 41]. Our previous augmentation of SKiM with ML-generated knowledge graphs showed promise but remained limited by sparse relationship annotations [22]. How­ ever, LLMs, particularly with the advancement of reasoning models [38], are becoming increasingly capable of accurately parsing biomedical science texts. General‑purpose LLMs [2, 9, 24] achieve state‑of‑the‑art scores on biomedical reasoning and ques­ tion‑answering benchmarks [7, 17, 28]. Furthermore, emerging multi-agent frameworks capable of autonomously generating and evaluating hypotheses [14] underscore a grow­ ing capability to not only surface potential discoveries but systematically assess their potential. Other methods for evaluation of hypotheses include benchmarking against curated datasets [36] and utilizing knowledge graphs and transformer-based mod­ els [35]. Motivated by these advances, we wanted to integrate LLM-based hypothesis and computational researchers to systematically and efficiently evaluate biomedical hypotheses at scale. Keywords Literature-based discovery (LBD), Large language models (LLM), Retrieval- augmented generation (RAG), Biomedical text mining, Hypothesis evaluation, Co-occurrence, Fine-tuning Page 3 of 15 Freeman et al. BMC Bioinformatics (2026) 27:16 evaluation into the SKiM workflow to enhance the interpretability and precision of puta­ tive LBD discoveries, while reducing the costs of manual review required of researchers. However, a known challenge with LLMs is their propensity to generate incorrect or fabricated information (“hallucinations”) [21]. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a promising strategy to mitigate this issue by supplementing an LLM’s parametric (trained) memory with externally retrieved documents, providing a factual grounding for generated responses [12, 20]. By combining both parametric (trained) and non-para­ metric (retrieved) sources, RAG reduces the frequency of hallucinations and improves interpretability, especially for information-sensitive tasks such as question answering and multi-hop reasoning [4, 25, 33]. Additionally, the recently developed scientific claim verification tool Valsci [10] highlights the importance of RAG in minimizing the cita­ tion hallucination rate and reducing misclassifications of scientific claims as true or not. Our LBD tool, SKiM, serves two purposes. First, it is an effective way to surface new lit­ erature-based putative discoveries based on the co-occurrence model described above. Second, it provides a list of documents to be inputted for RAG analysis by the frontier LLM. These documents can supplement the model’s knowledge and provide context not available to the LLM during its training. Additionally, our RAG system, SKiM-GPT, dis­ plays these retrieved documents along with the LLM’s reasoning to the user, increasing transparency. Still, the effectiveness of RAG depends on the quality and relevance of the retrieved documents. Retrieving documents that are irrelevant, ambiguous, or only weakly related to the hypothesis can introduce noise and misleading context, increasing the risk of inaccuracies and hallucinations [16, 23]. This risk is especially acute in biomedical litera­ ture, where terms frequently have multiple meanings and contexts can vary significantly [31, 37]. Therefore, a dedicated relevance filtering step is essential for RAG-based sys­ tems [6, 39], ensuring that only information directly pertinent to the hypothesis at hand is included in the LLM’s context. In summary, co-occurrence-based algorithms can discover novel associations with­ out explicitly evaluating hypotheses, resulting in substantial manual effort to interpret relationships, manage high false-positive rates, and determine mechanistic context. LLMs have the potential to alleviate these problems but suffer from frequent halluci­ nations and opaque decision-making. SKiM-GPT’s integrated approach leverages the complementary strengths of each tool, providing increased interpretability and preci­ sion compared to SKiM alone, while grounding the LLM’s reasoning in the retrieved documents. SKiM-GPT is open-source on GitHub ( ​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​g​i​t​h​u​b​.​c​o​m​/​s​t​e​w​a​r​t​-​l​a​b​/​s​k​i​ m​g​p​t​ ) and is available as a user-friendly web interface (https://skim.morgridge.org). Our contributions include: (1) providing a generalized method to improve the interpretabil­ ity and false-positive rate of LBD systems; (2) providing an evaluation benchmark for that method; and (3) providing a concrete open-source implementation with a publicly accessible web interface. Implementation The SKiM-GPT workflow is composed of three sequential steps: (1) identifying co- occurring terms in PubMed abstracts (“Serial KinderMiner”), (2) filtering abstracts for relevance to a user-defined hypothesis (“abstract filtering”), and (3) evaluating support Page 4 of 15 Freeman et al. BMC Bioinformatics (2026) 27:16 for that hypothesis based on the filtered abstracts (“hypothesis evaluation”) (Fig. 1). Each of these steps is described below. Serial KinderMiner. The Serial KinderMiner (SKiM) algorithm is detailed in [22]. Briefly, SKiM identifies indirect relationships between A and C terms through B-term intermediates using co-occurrence modeling. Given user-defined lists of A, B, and C terms, SKiM searches PubMed abstracts for these terms using exact string matching and applies Fisher’s Exact Test (FET) to assess statistical significance of A-B and B-C term pairs using contingency tables of occurrence and co-occurrence counts (note that SKiM allows the use of “and” (&) and “or” (|) operators to search for inexact phrase matches and synonyms; this helps in cases when the user wants to search for pathways, biologi­ cal processes, gene families and synonyms, etc. See the Supporting Information section “Exact String Matches and Evaluating Mechanism” for an example). A and C terms are considered putatively related if they share a significant B-term intermediary. SKiM ranks results based on a prediction score derived from p-values and term co-occurrence fre­ quencies. In addition to statistical significance, abstracts containing co-occurring A-B, B-C, and A-C term pairs are retrieved as input for the next step. Relevance filtering. After the SKiM search is complete, the user defines a hypothesis template into which the search terms can be inserted (e.g., “Drug {c-term} treats dis­ ease {a-term} through its interaction with gene {b-term}.”). Abstract texts retrieved in the previous SKiM step are then filtered to determine their relevance to this hypoth­ esis. Relevance is assessed using a fine-tuned language model based on the Phi-3 mini base architecture [1], which outputs a single token indicating whether an abstract is relevant or irrelevant (“1” or “0”, respectively). The primary goal of this step is to select abstracts that can be used to evaluate the hypothesis, regardless of whether they sup­ port or contradict it. This relevance filtering step is critical, as biomedical concepts fre­ quently exhibit high ambiguity in free text [37], and exact string-matching approaches like SKiM frequently retrieve abstracts containing ambiguous or irrelevant matches [8]. Fig. 1 Overview of the SKiM-GPT workflow for hypothesis evaluation. Users input an A term and lists of B and C terms to discover hidden links between A and C terms. In this example, the user is interested in finding FDA- approved drugs that might be repurposed for Alzheimer’s disease, where one of the ~ 19,000 protein-coding human genes provide the link between drug and disease. SKiM is run to find potential A-B-C links. The A-B and B-C co-occurring abstracts found by SKiM are then run through the relevance filter to only include abstracts likely rel­ evant to the hypotheses. Finally, these relevant abstracts and the hypotheses are evaluated by a frontier LLM, and the hypotheses are scored. The rationale and abstracts used are included in the output to provide full transparency Page 5 of 15 Freeman et al. BMC Bioinformatics (2026) 27:16 See Supporting Information section “Fine-Tuning Methodology” for further details on the implementation of relevance filtering. Hypothesis evaluation. The hypothesis evaluation scoring guidelines, the user’s hypothesis, and the relevant abstracts are then processed by a frontier LLM. Inclusion of the relevant abstracts grounds the LLM’s analysis in the evidence contained in the abstracts rather than relying on its parametric memory. The LLM assigns a numerical score between − 2 and 2, representing the degree of support for the hypothesis accord­ ing to the scoring guidelines, and provides explanatory reasoning including

Conclusion To derive the sentiment from the reviews and evaluate summary from the text, this study examines the embedding’s of the words with deep neural 1296 B. Rajalaxmi Prabhu and S.
