These results highlight the AIPatient system’s adaptability and robust performance across datasets and testing conditions, handling complex medical narratives effectively. Discussion In this research, we explore how the AIPatient system, leveraging LLM- based AI agents, enhances the performance of simulated patient systems. Ourquantitativeresultsunderscoretheadvantagesofintegratinginteractive agents in LLM-based reasoning workﬂow to effectively and reliably support medical investigations. The ablation study on AI agents demonstrates that multi-agentdesigncanenhanceoverallsystemperformance,outperforming individual LLM models in medical question answering. The subsequent metric-based analysis further indicates that the AIPatient system presents balanced effectiveness and reliability regarding readability, robustness, and stability. Evidence from this study suggests a potential avenue for incor- porating LLM-based AI agents in intelligent medical systems. A key feature of the AIPatient system is its workﬂow powered by LLM- based agents. The sequence, interaction, and collaboration of the agents together enhance the system’s realism and accuracy in medical question answering. Unlike traditional one-off prompt responses, the Reasoning RAG framework employs self-reﬂection, abstraction, and iterative checking to deliver reliable answers. These features enable agents to collaborate dynamically, distilling complex medical data and reﬁning responses through iterative processes. This framework also directly addresses risk of bias by reducing information omission or over-reliance on single-step outputs. The agent collaboration ensures that clinical queries are checked, abstracted, and cross-veriﬁed before generating a ﬁnal answer, thereby improving completeness and consistency in medical reasoning. Regarding effectiveness, the system achieves an accuracy of 94.15% by combining all six agents and using intelligent prompt design. Few-shot learning strategies add depth to the system, improving QA accuracy by 11.1% on average. The Readability aspect of the AIPatient system further conﬁrms that the correct and accurate generated patient response is easy to understand – the median value for Flesch Reading Ease and Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level mimic the readability of the ﬁction novel Harry Potter. This levelofreadingcomplexityissuitableformostmedicalinvestigators,suchas medical students. Trustworthiness is another vital aspect that veriﬁes AIPatient as a responsible AI system. First, the system exhibits strong robustness to the inherentvariabilityinLLMoutputs,asparaphrasingmedicalquestionsdoes not signiﬁcantly affect overall QA accuracy, indicating that the system can consistently retrieve accurate information despite variations in question Fig. 5 | Distribution of the Flesch Reading Ease and the Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level of AIPatient system outputs. Red dashed vertical lines indicate the median in each panel (Flesch Reading Ease = 68.77; Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level = 6.4), showing that most responses cluster around middle-school readability. https://doi.org/10.1038/s43856-025-01283-x Article Communications Medicine | (2026) 6:27 8 phrasing. Second, the system is stable, capable of integrating distinct per- sonalitytraitsintosimulated patientinteractionswithoutcompromisingthe accuracy or integrity of medical information. These ﬁndings highlight the need for medical AI systems to accommodate linguistic variability and patient diversity, ensuring reliable performance across different phrasings, personality traits, and clinical contexts. Moreover, our approach represents an advancement in LLM-based patient simulation by combining structured Electronic Health Records (EHR) processing with multi-agent systems to support human-like inter- actions.TheconstructionoftheAIPatientKGprototypesalarge-scaleNER- based EHR processing. Not only does this approach allow for clear data organization, but it also enables sophisticated queries and ﬂexible schema updates, making the system adaptable to evolving medical knowledge and patient needs. TofurtherassessAIPatient’sreal-worldapplicability,webenchmarkits computational efﬁciency and cost across popular LLMs (Supplementary Discussion). Proprietary models such as GPT-4o and Claude-3-Haiku show strong performance in both speed and affordability, making them viable for real-time clinical simulation. GPT-4-Turbo emerges as the most balanced model, supporting its use as the system’s backbone. This analysis underscores the system’s scalability and offers guidance for selecting models suited to practical deployment needs. Additionally, the modular agentic design of AIPatient allows new models to be integrated at the component level without full system re-evaluation, supporting ﬂexible upgrades and targeted benchmarking as better models emerge. Our user evaluation with medical students further highlights AIPa- tient’s educational value. In a paired crossover study with H-SPs, AIPatient matches or outperforms H-SPs across most metrics—including emotional realism, reliability, and learner satisfaction—demonstrating its ability to deliver high-quality, pedagogically effective simulations. Qualitative feed- back echoes these strengths, with students commenting on AIPatient’s expressiveness, speed, and consistency. Some areas for improvement are identiﬁed, such as managing verbosity and increasing ﬂexibility in handling non-standard queries, pointing to future opportunities for system reﬁnement. Our study ﬁts into the broader literature by addressing a critical gap in using AI for patient simulation. While previous work has explored simu- lation systems for hospital management or behavioral modeling21–23, the AIPatient system uniquely applies multi-agent workﬂows with an emphasis on reasoning to simulate patient interactions. The AIPatient system is also one of the ﬁrst that incorporates real, de-identiﬁed patient information and human-algorithm collaborative system evaluation, outperforming the pre- cedent patient simulation systems that are mostly based on a small number ofﬁxedcasesandaremanuallyevaluatedindomain-speciﬁcmanners24.Our Fig. 6 | Evaluation of simulated patient system using OSCE checklist and user experience questionnaire. a Medical students rated their experience with the AIPatient and H-SPs using a 5-point Likert scale across multiple dimensions including coherence, clinical relevance, and emotional realism. b Comparison of information collected in the Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) checklist between AIPatient and H-SPs, showing the proportion of key clinical information items successfully elicited during simulated interactions. https://doi.org/10.1038/s43856-025-01283-x Article Communications Medicine | (2026) 6:27 9 systemalsodemonstrateshowAIagentscancollaborateasasystemtotackle complex medical reasoning tasks, as one of the ﬁrst implementations of LLM-based agentic systems in medicine and healthcare47, providing an innovative tool for both education and healthcare delivery. Despite its achievements, this present study has several limitations. First, the reliance on discharge notes from MIMIC-III restricts the diversity of patient cases, and the homogeneous population it represents limits generalizability. Expanding the database to include outpatient, inpatient, and long-term care settings, as well as populations from varied demo- graphics, will address these issues. Second, the system shows varying per- formance across different entity categories, highlighting areas like social history as needing further reﬁnement. In addition, while the current version of AIPatient incorporates diverse personality traits to simulate variations in patientbehavior,itdoesnotyetexplicitlymodelbroadersocialdeterminants of health (SDoH) such as socioeconomic status, education level, or living conditions. These factors can signiﬁcantly inﬂuence how patients com- municate symptoms, adhere to care plans, and engage with clinicians. Future research should explore the ethical, psychological, and profes- sional dimensions of implementing generative AI, especially LLM, in clin- ical settings. Feedback from medical trainees and professionals in the user study highlights the system’s strengths—such as emotional realism, con- sistency, and perceived educational value—while also identifying areas for reﬁnement, including managing verbosity and improving ﬂexibility with non-standard queries. These insights demonstrate the importance of sta- keholder involvement in guiding system development. Future research should expand this participatory approach by engaging a broader range of users,includingcliniciansand patientsfromdiversebackgrounds,to further align AIPatient with real-world expectations and values. Additionally, fur- ther iterations of AIPatient can integrate structured SDoH data to support more inclusive and equity-aware training scenarios. This will further enhance the system’s ability to prepare trainees for real-world variability in patient presentations and healthcare access. Overall, the AIPatient system exempliﬁes the promise of responsible AI in bridging the gap between clinical training and patient care, laying a foundation for more inclusive, accurate, and effective medical simulation systems. The ﬁndings from our study demonstrate a simulated patient system powered by large language model-based AI agents. This offers potential for transforming medical education by providing trainees with a realistic, scalable,andaccessibletooltopracticeclinicalreasoning.Beyondeducation, the system has implications for healthcare practice, where it can support clinicians by simulating rare or complex cases, helping to reﬁne diagnostic approaches or evaluate treatment options. Its adaptability to new data types, such as imaging and multimodal inputs, further extends its utility, pro- mising a more holistic simulation of patient care. Ethics Our research was based on the MIMIC-III dataset26 and the CORAL dataset46, accessed through PhysioNet48. Prior training of CITI Data or Specimens Only Research (Record Number: 59460661) was completed on November 2, 2023. LLM were used in compliance with PhysioNet standard, including using the GPT models on Azure and the Claude models on Amazon Bedrock for data privacy. The user study involving medical students and volunteers was approved by the Institutional Review Board of Qilu Hospital of Shandong University (IRB Protocol Number: KYLL-202505-005). All participants provided informed consent prior to participation. All procedures involving human participants were conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Data availability Our data, AIPatient KG and AIPatient KG-CORAL, as well as the corre- sponding medical QA datasets, are available on PhysioNet28. These de- identiﬁed data may be made available upon reasonable request via a proposal-based application process through PhysioNet. Data usage agree- ment and training facilitated by PhysioNet are required for the application. Source data including bootstrap outputs used to generate Fig. 4, readability scores for Fig. 5, and medical-student scoring data for Fig. 6 are available on Figshare49. We cannot release the full clinical-text–derived datasets openly. Upstream licenses for MIMIC/PhysioNet credentialed data prohibit onward redistribution and the use of third-party online services; derivatives that could enable re-identiﬁcation (e.g., note-aligned triples or QA pairs) must remain under controlled access. Although de-identiﬁed, narrative notes still carry non-zero re-identiﬁcation risk, which is why access is managed rather than public. Portions of our work also use CORAL (de- identiﬁed oncology notes). Its availability and reuse are governed by the original owners/publisher; consequently, our CORAL-based graph and QA derivatives are released only within the same controlled-access framework, and we cannot repost CORAL itself. URL link for AIPatient KG and AIPatient KG-CORAL, and corre- sponding medical QA datasets: https://physionet.org/content/aipatient-kg/ 1.0.0/. URL link for Fig. 4, Fig. 5, Fig. 6 raw data: https://doi.org/10.6084/m9. ﬁgshare.30327508.v2. URL link for MIMIC-III raw data: https://physionet.org/content/ mimiciii/1.4/. Table 4 | Two-sample t-test results comparing AIPatient and H-SP Metric Question t-statistics Fidelity Role/Text Adherence The SP followed the case script without contradictions. 0.57 The SP’s responses matched the intended medical condition. 1.77* Contextual Appropriateness The SP’s responses felt natural and relevant to my questions. 1.10 Emotional Realism The SP displayed believable emotions (e.g., pain, anxiety). 3.02 *** Coherence/Consistency The SP’s dialogue was coherent (no abrupt shifts). 1.23 Response Quality The SP’s answers were directly relevant to clinical questions. 0.17 Usability Ease of Use Interacting with this SP required minimal effort. 1.62 I encountered no technical difﬁculties (e.g., delays). 2.68*** Feasibility/Scalability This SP could be easily integrated into our training program. 0.47 Effectiveness Diagnostic Accuracy I have reached a preliminary diagnosis at the end. 1.59 Learner Satisfaction This session improved my clinical reasoning skills. 2.19** *p < 0.1, **p < 0.05, ***p < 0.01; reported p values are from two-sided tests. https://doi.org/10.1038/s43856-025-01283-x Article Communications Medicine | (2026) 6:27 10 URL link for CORAL raw data: https://physionet.org/content/curated- oncology-reports/1.0/. Code availability The code for data processing, system construction, experiments, and system demonstration is publicly available via Zenodo50: https://doi.org/10.5281/ ZENODO.14583946. The most up-to-date version is available on GitHub: https://github.com/huiziy/AIPatient. Received: 1 February 2025; Accepted: 26 November 2025;

title: IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON AUDIO, SPEECH, AND LANGUAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 18, NO. 5, JULY 2010
file_name: Statistical Text-to-Speech Synthesis Based on Segment-Wise Representation With a Norm Constraint.pdf
text: IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON AUDIO, SPEECH, AND LANGUAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 18, NO. 5, JULY 2010 1077 point of view, we showed that by using the new formulation, a perfect detection of the speech components is possible if the noise originates from a point interference source, which can never be achieved with the single-channel case. In the case of incoherent noise, a coherent sum- mation of the noise-free speech components is performed to allow for better speech detection, especially of low speech energy components as compared to the single-channel approaches. The proposed method ap- plies for the general situation where the observed microphone signals are mixtures of a desired speech plus noise signals. The latter can be composed of interferences and other types of undesired signals (e.g., white noise).

title: Chinese Journal of Electronics
file_name: Text Semantics Based Automatic Summarization for Chinese Videos.pdf
text: Chinese Journal of Electronics Vol.24, No.3, July 2015 Text Semantics Based Automatic Summarization for Chinese Videos∗ WANG Xingqi1, ZHA Taotao1, WU Chunming2, FANG Jinglong1 and JIANG Ming1 (1. School of Computer Science, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China) (2. College of Computer Science and Technology, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China) Abstract — This paper is concerned with the task of automatically summarizing videos, which is important for many video-related applications. Examples include video retrieval, accessing and categorization, et al. Our approach summarizes video semantically from the texts embedded in videos. To improve the accuracy, ant colony algorithm, maximum matching method, Singular value decomposition (SVD) are employed for locating text region, segment- ing words and clustering sentences, respectively. Based on these methods, a prototype is developed. In an experimen- tal evaluation on the real world video datasets, we show that our proposed approach could provide accurate, satis- factory performance. Key words — Video summarization, Ant colony algo- rithm, Maximum matching method, Singular value decom- position (SVD). I. Introduction With the rapid development of network technology and storage capability, recent years have witnessed the explosion of multimedia data on web, i.e. video, image etc., e.g., an estimated 20 hours of videos are uploaded every minute to YouTube[1], Facing the challenge of massive video data on In- ternet, it is impossible for people to review and browse videos one after another to ﬁnd their demanded. The growing num- ber of videos has motivated a real necessity to provide eﬀec- tive tools to support videos retrieval and browsing. Therefore, how to obtain the required information from videos quickly and easily is one of active research topic in the ﬁeld of data mining, multimedia content analysis etc. Video summarization oﬀers a concise representation of the original video clips, which has become important way to grasp key subject for speciﬁed category of videos. Generally, there are two categories of techniques for static video summarization. Most existing video summarization algo- rithms are designed to select key frames or clips from original video as abstraction[2]. Some eﬀorts have been put on shot de- tection and selection of key frames[3]. Key frames provide a suitable video summarization. The use of key frames greatly reduces the amount of data required in video indexing and pro- vides an organizational framework for dealing with the video content. The multiple key-frames are always extracted from shot[4]. Despite numerous eﬀorts in video summarization, un- fortunately, these approaches generally rely on either global features, such as colour, texture and motion information[5], or simply direct sampling from videos[6]. As a result, the direct sampling or low level feature based approach are inconsistent with human perception. Their results may not necessarily be semantically meaningful or relevant to the original videos since they do not attempt to model and estimate semantic content of videos. Texts embedded in videos are valuable source of consecu- tive information. It not only provides keywords for annotation and search of image and video, but also aids in video sum- marization. In this paper, video summarization based on text semantics is proposed. The ant colony algorithm is applied to detect the embedded texts in the video. We take some texts as subject terms. Then these subject terms with higher weight in SVD model have a greater possibility to be chosen for video summarization. We give text description of videos as ﬁnal re- sult, instead of key frames, which has an advantage of less storage and is more acceptable for human beings. The paper is outlined as follows. In Section II, we brieﬂy review the related work on video summarization. Section III introduces the approach we proposed. The evaluation result of video summarization is presented in Section IV. Finally, con- clusion remarks and discussions are given in Section V. II. Related Works Similar to text summarization, video summarization is abstraction of given video or a category of videos. To date, video summarization is mainly achieved by using key frames extracted from original video sequences. Generally speaking, ∗Manuscript Received Nov. 14, 2013; Accepted June 10, 2014. This work is supported by the National High Technology Development 863 Program of China (No.2011AA01A107), the Zhejiang Provincial Technical Plan Project (No.2011C13008, No.2013C01113) and Defense Industrial Technology Development Program (No.A3920110002, No.202012A001). c⃝2015 Chinese Institute of Electronics. DOI:10.1049/cje.2015.07.004 Text Semantics Based Automatic Summarization for Chinese Videos 463 there are two traditional basic forms of video summarization: video summary and video skimming. The former is known as static storyboard, which is a set of key frames generated from the underlying video source. The latter is known as dynamic storyboard, which consists of a preview sequence (key video segments). A well known approach for extracting key frames is com- puting colour histogram[5] or motion activity[6] to determine whether the frame content changes. Diﬀerent shots are taken as diﬀerent content. Therefore, key frames are extracted from it. Zhuang[7] proposed an unsupervised clustering scheme to adaptively get the key frames. As a pre-deﬁned thresh- old or key-frame number is required in these kinds of meth- ods, some shot independent approaches are proposed recently. Girgensohn[8] proposes a time-constrained clustering approach that extracts key frames from an entire video. A video abstrac- tion algorithm based on visual attention model is proposed by Qing-Ge[9]. Key frames that extracted from each shot are clus- tered using an online clustering method based on the features in Regions of interest (ROI). Key video segments, on the other hand, preserving the dynamic nature of video content, provides users an impres- sive preview of an entire video. A straightforward approach is given in Ref.[10] to compress the original video by speeding up the playback. However, in order to keep the speech com- prehensible, the playback speed is limited in this approach. A content-based video organization scheme[11] analyses video sequences hierarchically at diﬀerent content resolution lev- els. Another approach presented in Ref.[12] generates the se- mantically meaningful summaries event-oriented abstraction scheme. Recently, more sophisticated techniques are adopted, such as the trajectories of objects[13], singular value decompo- sition in Ref.[14] et al. The existing methods for video summarization couldn’t perform well. On one hand, though global features coarsely represent visual characteristics of an image, some ﬁne details of frames are neglected. On the other hand, local features ﬁnely represent the image. But it selects key frames too many. In ad- dition, key frames or consecutive frames are usually not able to summarize original videos accurately and semantically. From human being’s view, text abstractions are more suitable for grasping the videos’ subject[15]. Though automatic captions generation method in Ref.[16] has been proposed, it’s only for images on Internet. Additionally, there is so much text infor- mation available facilitated for video summarization, i.e., title, description information, surrounding texts etc. Diﬀerent from the images on Internet, videos we are dealing with do not have any additional information for abstracting. Based on the in- herent characteristics in videos, we proposed a novel Chinese video summarization method, i.e., text semantic based auto- matic summarization method. To our knowledge, this is the ﬁrst attempt for text semantic based video summarization. III. Text Semantics Based Video Summarization In this section, ﬁrst, we present an approach for robust text extraction from video frames, which can handle complex image backgrounds. Second, video texts detected are processed by an Optical character recognition (OCR) system to produce texts. And all these texts in one video are taken as a whole for text clustering to categorize videos. Third, A SVD model is adopted to abstract the key subjects of video. Finally, video summarization is created from the key subjects in the video. 1. Texts extraction from video To detect video texts, ant colony algorithm is applied in the proposed method, which scans the image in a window (typ- ically 16×18) to extract the features. To leverage precision and speed of algorithm, we shift the window with 8 pixels, that is, each 8×8 sub-block of the input image has a diﬀerent feature value. These feature values are input data for heuristic algo- rithm. 1) Feature extraction and selection To extract texts from videos, we have to represent these texts in appropriate way. In this paper, two features, i.e. HL and LH, which are variance and histogram variance of wavelet coeﬃcient, are applied. These features can be calculated as follows. V arCoef = 1 M × N M X i=1 N X j=1 (Coef(i, j) −MeanCeof)2 (1) HistVarCoef = maxCoef X minCoef (Bin new(j) −MeanBin new)2 (2) where Bin new(j) > 0 if Bin(j) = j; Bin new(j) = 0 if Bin(j) = 0; and Bin(j) is the j th number of wavelet coeﬃ- cient; M, N is the number of the ranks of sub-band HL and LH, respectively. These two sub-bands are the measure for horizontal and vertical texture of the video characters. Usu- ally, the texts’ value of VarCoef and HistVarCoef are bigger than the non-text’s. They are used to deﬁne the heuristic in- formation ηi,j described in Eq.(4). Therefore, the text blocks will be more likely to be chosen by ants since the intensity of pheromone is higher. We obtain a pheromone matrix after the algorithm ﬁnished. Based on this matrix, Otsu algorithm is applied to get a threshold, which is used to indicate whether the window contains text or not. 2) Ant colony based text blocks ﬁltering Ant colony algorithm is based on the behaviour of ants searching for food. When other ants come across the pheromone, they are likely to follow the path with a certain probability. In our algorithm, pheromone is described as the possibility of pixels in video which are treated as candidate texts. The outline can be described as follows. Initialization. Place m ants randomly into image I ; Set m be r M 8 × N 8 , where M and N are the rows’ and columns’ number of I, respectively. Initialize the pheromone in each block with random value approached zero. Path ﬁnding. The probability of an ant move from the ith’s block to the adjacent j th’s is: P(i,j)(t) = τi,j(t −1)αηβ i,j P j∈Ωi τi,j(t −1)αηβ i,j (3) τi,j(t −1) is the intensity of pheromone in j th’s block next to ith’s in the (t-1)th time. α and β are the relative importance 464 Chinese Journal of Electronics 2015 of pheromone and heuristic information, respectively. Ωi is the set of adjacent nodes of i. ηi,j is the heuristic information. It’s deﬁned as ηij = V arCoef ′ × HistV arCoef ′ (4) where V arCoef ′ and HistV arCoef ′ are higher value of Var- Coef and HistVarCoef for each of sub-band HL and LH. Pheromone updating. In this paper, the pheromone will be updated locally and globally. In the local updating, the rule is that at the tth time, the kth ant take one step, the pheromone will be updated as follows. If (i, j) is visible by the kth ant, τi,j(t) = (1−ρ)τi,j(t−1)+ρΔτ k i,j. τi,j(t) = τi,j(t−1) if invisible. In above equation, η is an evaporation coeﬃcient; Δτ k i,j has a relation with the heuristic guiding function; Here we set Δτ k i,j = ηi,j. After all ants ﬁnish their own travelling, we update the global pheromone with the Eq.(5). τ(t) = (1 −ϕ)τ(t −1) + ϕΔτ (5) where ϕ is the attenuation coeﬃcient of pheromone. 3) Text detection From Section III.1.2), we get a pheromone matrix, where the text blocks have higher value. Based on this matrix, Otsu algorithm is applied to get a threshold, which is used to indi- cate whether the window contains text or not. Fig.1 show the results obtained by our proposed method, where Fig.1(a) is an original video frame and Fig.1(b) is text regions detected by our method. From Fig.1, we can conclude that basically, text blocks can be detected accurately. Fig. 1. Example of video frame and the frame with text blocks detected. (a) original video frame; (b) frame with text blocks detected 4) Text tracking and recognition Once text is detected, the next step is to track and recog- nize these texts. The texts, speciﬁcally captions, in the video frame is always last at least for 3–4 seconds. Some subject terms will be last for longer to draw the attention of audi- ences. If the video frame was detected with text block(s), we compute Hu moment[10] value of the same region in next video frame. The two-dimensional (p + q)th order moment in the video frame are deﬁned as mpq = N X y=1 M X x=1 xpyqf(x, y), p, q = 0, 1, 2... (6) Obviously, the moments of all orders exist and the mo- ment sequence mpq is uniquely determined by f(x, y); one should note that the moments in Eq. (6) may be not invariant while f(x, y) changes when image is translated. The invari- ant features can be achieved using central moments, which are deﬁned as μ = N X y=1 M X x=1 (x −¯x)p(y −¯y)qf(x, y), p, q = 0, 1, 2... (7) where ¯x = m10 m00 ,¯y = m01 m00 . Normalized central moments are deﬁned as ηpq = μpq μγ 00 , γ = (p + q + 2)/2, p + q = 2, 3... (8) Based on normalized central moments, we use one of seven moment invariants only, that is, φ1 = η20 + η02. Compare two φ1 values of text region in given frame and that in its consec- utive frame. If their diﬀerence is less than given threshold, δ , we treat extracted texts in these two consecutive frames as the same. These texts will be seen as subject title if the same texts appear in certain consecutive frames. Finally, all text regions are transformed into a binary im- age. Then Tesseract OCR system (an OCR engine that de- veloped by Ray Smith, which can be downloaded at http : //code.google.com/p/tesseract −ocr) is used to recognize the text. Hence, texts in the video are extracted. 2. Word segmentation and synonym detection Word segmentation is the ﬁrst step in Chinese informa- tion processing. Diﬀerent segmentation gives the diﬀerent re- sults when dealing with issues like word boundary. In this sec- tion, we introduce maximum matching segmentation method to segment texts extracted in above section and the method to detect synonym. 1) Maximum matching segmentation method Word segmentation may start from either end of line. Its basic idea can be described as follows. Suppose the length of the longest entry in lexicon is I. If the ﬁst I characters of the Chinese string is matched with the entry in lexicon, then it will be identiﬁed as a word; Otherwise, remove the last char- acter, with (I -1) characters left, repeat the steps until a word is identiﬁed. The main process of this algorithm is shown in Fig.2. 2) Synonym detection To detect the synonym of texts, the paper adopts single character based method to compute the similarity of words. Given two words, the ﬁrst one is called keyword, the second is called ctrlword. Count the number of same characters, location and order of the two words. Take the feature, “the center of gravity aft”, that is the characters behind has a greater weight than the ones before. Here we set the weight of the number of same characters be 0.6 and the weight of location be 0.4. Similarity for single character based method can be calculated as follows. similiar = 60% × α + 40% × dp × β (9) where α = (xsword/ctrlword + xsword/keyword)/2,β = [P (c xsword/ P ctrlword) + P (k xsword/ P keyword)]/2 where xsword is the number of the same characters; ctrlword is the number of diﬀerent characters; keyword is the number of characters of words; P (c xsword/ P ctrlword) is the sum of location weight in ctrlword; P (k xsword/ P keyword) is the sum of location weight in keyword; dp is the ratio of the two words’ number, while dp ≤1. Text Semantics Based Automatic Summarization for Chinese Videos 465 Fig. 2. Flowchart of maximum matching segmentation method for video texts 3. Text clustering Most methods of text clustering require several steps of data pre-processing, e.g. stop words removing, synonyms merging etc. In this paper, texts extracted from videos are rep- resented by a vector of terms’ frequencies. Here suppose the dimension of vector is m, therefore text document d can be deﬁned as d = (tf1, . . . tfm), in which tfi can be described by two parameters: 1) TF (term frequency) fij is the frequency of words wj appeared in the document di. 2) IDF (Inverse document frequency) is deﬁned as log(N/nj), where N is the number of all documents and nj is the number of documents that contain wj. The term weight of wj in di is represented by tij = fijlog(N/nj) called TF-IDF. Usually, the document vectors are normalized to allow comparison of documents of diﬀerent lengths. The vector has a very high dimensionality even after pre-processing. Due to the high dimensionality, most frequencies are zero for documents. To measure the similarity between two documents, we used clustering method based on graph theory. As we have known the similarity of each pair of documents, a weighed graph can be built. For example, as Fig.3 has shown, the nodes in the graph are the documents and the weight on each edge is the similarity between two documents. Fig. 3. Similarity between two documents. (a) similarity with 5 nodes; (b) similarity with 3 clusters To cluster the video subjects, the method remove the edge with the least similar weight from graph. Then traverse the graph. It will stop until K, the number of clusters we set, dis- connected parts are formed. Otherwise, repeat above steps. Fig.3(b) is the results for Fig.3(a) with 3 clusters. 4. Text semantics based video summarization Video summarization should include some key words to make one to grasp main idea for the original video clips. How- ever, the most important words in sentences are nouns or noun phrases. In this paper, text semantics based video summariza- tion extracts nouns or noun phrases from sentences recognized from video to form the video summarization. The collection of nouns or noun phrases in the document d is represented by t1, . . . tn, sorted in their order appeared in the document. Every sentence in d is described by a vector called sentence vector, Si = (fi1, . . . , fin)(i = 1, . . . , m), where fij is the frequency of tj in Si; and D = (fij)m×n = (S1, . . . , Sm)T is document matrix. Suppose fj is the frequency of the noun tj in a sentence. If fj > fmin, we deﬁne tj as a keyword. T is the collection of all keywords tj in document d. Project vectors of sentences Si = (fi1, . . . , fin)(i = 1, . . . , m) and matrix of documents D = (fij)m×n into T . Vector and matrix projection are de- noted by Si and D, respectively. In this paper, we set fmin = 1, which means tj appears twice in one document. Particularly, For subject sentences, Si are weighed due to their importance. Here, SVD is used for abstracting texts extracted from videos. Suppose document matrix is D = (fij)m×n and eigenvalues of DDT are λ1 ≥λ2 . . . ≥λk ≥λk+1 = . . . = 0; the singular value is σi √λi(i = 1, . . . , n); Vi = (v1i, . . . , vni)T(i = 1, . . . , n) is the right singular vector , while Ui = (u1i, . . . , umi)T(i = 1, . . . , k) is the left singular vec- tor. So Sj = σ1vj1U1 + . . . + σkvjkUk(j = 1, . . . , m) where Sj is the j th row of DT, |Sj| = q σ2 1v2 j1 + . . . + σ2 kv2 jk = q λ1v2 j1 + . . . + λkv2 jk. As we know that λ1/(Pk i=1 λi) infor- mation of the document d is gathered around U1, so U1 can be seen as a clustering center of all the sentences. We treat the rest Ui as the same. Cluster all the sentences in document d taking U1, . . . Uk as clustering centres. SCi, . . . , SCk are k categories. The sim- ilarity between Ui and Sj is rij = |Ui · Sj|/||Sj|| = σi|vji|/ q λ1v2 j1 + . . . + λkv2 jk. rij would have the same denom- inator if j is ﬁxed. So we just need to classify Sj into the clus- ter of Ui whose i can make the numerator σi|vji|(i = 1, . . . , k) have highest value. Algorithm 1 can be described as follows. Algorithm 1 Text semantics based video summarization 1 Let SC0 = S1, . . . , Sm be the set of all sentences and Ui(i = 1, . . . , k) be the clustering center. 2 for i = 1 to k: SCi = φ 3 for j = 1 to m, input the vector Sj 4 if i make σi|vji|(i = 1, . . . , k) have the highest value SCi = SCi ∪{Sj} 5 Output SC1, . . . , SCk sorted by the order of eigenvalues’value. Suppose the document d has m sentences and its sum- marization, dsumm has m1 sentences, the compression ratio is deﬁned as ρ = m1/m. To extract rational sentences as the summary for given video, user is asked to specify the compres- 466 Chinese Journal of Electronics 2015 sion ratio and coverage ratio, which is a criterion for deciding the size of video summarization and clustering. The algorithm selects top nCi = |Ci|mρ/ PL i=1 |Ci| sentences in each cluster already sorted by |Sj| as video summarization. IV. Experiment and Discussion Based on the proposed method, we have developed a prototype to evaluate the eﬀectiveness and eﬃciency of this method, 100 news video clips are selected for evaluation, in- cluding 40 politics, 30 entertainments and 30 news. Table 1 shows the results for the proposed method. Table 1. Experimental results in real world applications Sum of videos Error categories Precision Recall P 42 11(E:4; C:7) 75% 78% E 21 9(P :8; C:1) 57.1% 40% C 37 16(P :11; E:5) 65.9% 77.5% In Table 1, P, E and C are the abbreviation of politics, entertainment and city news, respectively. P:x, E:y and C:z show that there are x, y and z videos misclassiﬁed as politics, entertainment and city news videos, respectively. In the exper- iment, we set ρ = 1 while pheromone updating. Parts of results in each category are listed as follows. Summarization of politics: (1). ; (2).  ; (3). ; (4). ; (5). 3 ; (6).  ; (7).  ; (8).  Summarization of city news: (1).   ; (2).    ; (3).   ; (4).   ; (5).       ; (6).     ; (7).    ; (8).    ; (9).        Summarization of entertainment: (1).    10; (2).   ; (3).     ; (4).  ; (5).   16  ; (6).     Table 1 shows the results of video summarization for three categories. From above results, we can conclude that most of them are classiﬁed correctly. Although each step may make a mistake, that is, not all the text regions being detected, not all the Chinese characters being recognized by OCR correctly, not all the words being segmented accurately, the way to compute similarity between texts also having disadvantage, The result we got is already satisfying. The entertainment news has the lowest precision because it contains broad range of videos in daily life and the words in it may have no rules, which make it very diﬃcult to abstract this kind of videos. Generally, the ﬁnal summarizations reﬂect the clue of content in given videos. V. Conclusions and Future Works In this paper, we have presented a new method to sum- marize video clips based on text semantics. Ant colony algo- rithm, weighted text extraction and words similarity method are used to increase the accuracy. The experimental results have demonstrated the eﬃciencies and eﬀectiveness of our method. The further investigation will assess the eﬀectiveness for the methods used in each step and evaluate the alternative methods to select the best one to improve our method per- formance. In addition to validate the system on more videos, we also plan to apply our system to real word applications, for example text-based video searching, video categorization et al.

title: IEEE SIGNAL PROCESSING LETTERS, VOL. 32, 2025
file_name: Text-to-Speech With Lip Synchronization Based on Speech-Assisted Text-to-Video Alignment and Masked Unit Prediction.pdf
text: IEEE SIGNAL PROCESSING LETTERS, VOL. 32, 2025 961 Text-to-Speech With Lip Synchronization Based on Speech-Assisted Text-to-Video Alignment and Masked Unit Prediction Youngdo Ahn , Member, IEEE, Jongwook Chae , Student Member, IEEE, and Jong Won Shin , Senior Member, IEEE Abstract—Text-to-speech (TTS) with lip synchronization (TTSLS) is the task of generating a speech signal synchronized with the lip movements in a video given the text transcription and the video without speech. Previous approaches to TTSLS aligned the phoneme sequence and video frames using scaled dot-product attention with a diagonal constraint loss, which was employed to prevent a phoneme from being assigned to video frames too far away. However, the diagonal constraint loss basically assumes that the duration of each phoneme is about the same, which is not always valid as speaking styles can be different. In this letter, we propose a TTSLS system based on speech-assisted text-to-video alignment and masked unit prediction. By utilizing the ground-truth speech signal available in the training phase, we construct a loss function for text-to-video alignment using the text-to-speech alignment obtained by a pre-trained TTS model. To deal with video frames without frontal lip images, we employ a masked unit prediction loss so that the unit predictor in the proposed system can estimate the masked units from the rest of the units. In addition, we modiﬁed the probability distribution for the unit predictor using a learnable null embedding for video inspired by classiﬁer-free guidance. Experimental results demonstrated that our proposed method outperformed previous TTSLS systems in both lip-speech synchronization and speech recognition performance. Index Terms—Text-to-speech, speech synthesis, lip sync- hronization, alignment, multi-modality. I. INTRODUCTION T EXT-to-speech (TTS) with lip synchronization (TTSLS), also known as automatic video dubbing [1], [2] or auto- matic voice over (AVO) [3], [4], aims to generate high-quality speech synchronized with the lip movement in a video when the video without speech and text scripts are available. Possible ap- plications of this technique may include the synthesis of speech from a video in which the speech is barely understandable due Received 17 October 2024; revised 26 January 2025; accepted 30 January 2025. Date of publication 3 February 2025; date of current version 4 March 2025. This work was supported in part by the Institute of Information and Communications Technology Planning and Evaluation (IITP), Korea Gov- ernment (MSIT), under Grant 2019-0-01842 (Artiﬁcial Intelligence Graduate School Program (GIST)). The associate editor coordinating the review of this article and approving it for publication was Prof. Sri Rama Murty Kodukula. (Corresponding author: Jong Won Shin.) The authors are with the School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology, Gwangju 61005, South Korea (e-mail: ayoungdo@gm.gist.ac.kr; whddnr97@gm.gist.ac.kr; jwshin@ gist.ac.kr). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/LSP.2025.3537949 to severe background noises or microphone failure, editing mis- pronounced or misspoken words from already recorded video1, and the generation of synchronized speech for the animations with lip movement. The performance of the TTS has been sig- niﬁcantly improved by deep learning-based approaches [5], [5], [6], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], and the tasks of the TTS have expanded to the synthesis of the speech from unseen speakers [11], [12], [13], multiple languages [14], [15], [16], and with emotions [17], [18], [19]. TTSLS is another extended task of the TTS which requires the alignment of text and video. In the pioneering work in [1], Neural Dubber is proposed which aligns the video frames and phonemes using an attention module. The aligned lip images and phoneme embeddings are used to generate a Mel-spectrogram, which is then utilized by the Parallel WaveGAN [20] to produce an audio waveform. Neural Dubber showed competitive audio quality with a TTS system FastSpeech2 [8], while providing a satisfactory synchronization with video [1]. In [4], Discrete Speech Unit (DSU)-AVO was proposed, in which DSUs are estimated from the aligned video and text features instead of a Mel-spectrogram inspired by the speech resynthesis in [21]. The speech signal is synthesized from DSUs using Unit HiFi-GAN [21]. In both Neural Dubber and DSU-AVO, the diagonal constraint loss [22] was adopted for text-to-video alignment. The term “diagonal” here means that the phonemes are uniformly spaced over video frames, and the diagonal constraint loss enforces the attention weights for alignment to focus on the phonemes near the diagonal. Thus, it essentially assumes that the duration for each phoneme is not signiﬁcantly different, which is not necessarily true depending on the speaking style. Another issue that previous TTSLS approaches did not address is the occlusion of the lips in some of the video frames, as can be seen in the example images in Fig. 1. The video frames without lip images make a modality missing problem, which may deteriorate the alignment and the speech quality unless properly handled. In this letter, we propose a TTSLS system based on speech- assisted text-to-video alignment and masked unit prediction (STAMP). As the ground-truth speech signal is available at the training stage, it can be used to guide the text-to-video alignment. The alignment map between the speech and text is constructed by the monotonic alignment search [23] using a pre-trained TTS model, and then is used in a new loss function 1Demo for the audio editing of the lecture videos by TTSLS is available at https://dori2063.github.io/LipSyncLosses/ 1070-9908 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artiﬁcial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 962 IEEE SIGNAL PROCESSING LETTERS, VOL. 32, 2025 Fig. 1. Overall block diagram for the proposed speech synthesis system based on lip images and text script. Modules denoted with blue color are pre-trained and frozen during training. Speech tokenizer and text-speech aligner modules are utilized in training only, and the unit vocoder is used in inference only. for the text-to-video alignment. To deal with a modality missing problem due to the video frames without a frontal image of lips, we adopt a masked unit prediction loss so that the audio tokens for randomly masked frames can be estimated from the embeddings for the rest of the frames to learn contextual representations. Additionally, we introduce a missing modality resilient probability modiﬁcation inspired by the classiﬁer-free guidance [24], [25], [26], [27], which utilizes the unit prediction with a learned null embedding as well as that with the embedding for the video frame. Experimental results demonstrated that our proposed method outperformed Neural Dubber and DSU-AVO. Sample videos with generated speeches and transcriptions are available in our demo page1. II. METHODS The left half of Fig. 1 shows a general block diagram for a TTSLS system, which consists of video and text encoders, a video-text aligner, an audio feature predictor, and a vocoder. TTSLS system aims to generate speech waveform Xa for a video given the visual and text data Xv and Xp with lengths Tv and Tp. Xv is usually a sequence of lip images which is highly correlated with speech [28], while Xp is a phoneme sequence as in typical TTS systems [10]. The visual and text encoders transform Xv and Xp into corresponding embeddings, Hv ∈RTv×d and Hp ∈ RTp×d, respectively, where d denotes the size of embedding vectors. The context embedding C summarizing the information from video and text modalities is constructed through the video- text alignment [1], [4] using scaled dot-product attention [29] as C = softmax  HvHT p √ d  Hp + Hv = AHp + Hv ∈RTv×d (1) where A ∈RTv×Tp is the attention weight matrix. Then, the context embedding is upsampled and fed into the audio feature predictor F to estimate the audio feature Z with length of Tz. The estimate of Z is then processed by a pre-trained vocoder to produce the estimate of Xa, ˆXa. Tokenized units are used as the audio features in DSU- AVO [4]. The discrete units Z extracted from the target speech Xa using a neural network-based speech tokenizer [30] are compared with the output of the audio feature predictor to train the TTSLS system. The estimated units are then converted to the speech waveform by a pre-trained unit vocoder [21]. Because the audio features are discrete units rather than continuous variables, the audio feature predictor, which is a unit predictor, becomes a classiﬁcation model, and thus the cross-entropy (CE) loss is employed given by LCE = − Tz  i=1 z(i) log F(C′)(i) (2) where z(i) is the i-th component of Z expressed as a one-hot vector, C′ is the context representation adjusted to the speech frame rate, and F(C′)(i) is the corresponding logit in the unit predictor output. The diagonal constraint (DC) loss [22] on the video-text aligner was introduced in [1], which enforces the attention weight matrix A in (1) to focus on the phonemes near the “diag- onal” when we place the phoneme sequence and the sequence of video frames on the vertical and horizontal axes, respectively. The DC loss is given by LDC = − Tv  s=1 min(⌈ks+b⌉,Tp)  t=max(⌊ks−b⌋,1) A(s,t) (3) where A(s,t) is the attention component of the s-th video frame and the t-th phoneme, k = Tp/Tv is the slope of the diagonal, and b is a hyperparameter to determine how much the alignment can deviate from the diagonal, and ⌈·⌉and ⌊·⌋denote the ceiling and ﬂoor functions. The ﬁnal loss function of DSU-AVO [4] then becomes L = LCE + LDC. In this letter, we propose a TTSLS system based on DSU- AVO [4], incorporating speech-assisted text-to-video alignment and masked unit prediction (STAMP). Fig. 1 shows the overall structure of the proposed TTSLS system which consists of the video and text encoders, video-text aligner, unit predictor, and unit vocoder, along with the text-speech aligner and speech tokenizer used during training. A. Speech-Assisted Text-to-Video Alignment The DC loss is introduced to prevent video frames from being aligned with phonemes far away. Without any constraint, the order of the phonemes aligned with video frames may even be swapped, which will not happen for the ground-truth alignment. However, the DC loss essentially assumes that the duration for each phoneme is about the same, which is not always correct depending on the speakers and the occasions. Also, the ground- truth speech signal is available in the training phase, which can provide crucial information for text-to-video alignment. To utilize the alignment information from the target speech signal and relax the assumption on the durations of individual phonemes, we propose a speech-assisted text-to-video align- ment (STA) adopting a text-to-speech aligner. As the speech and video frames are already aligned by time indices and the text-to-speech alignment techniques have shown impressive per- formances in TTS systems, a text-to-speech alignment method such as a monotonic alignment search (MAS) [23] will produce Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. AHN et al.: TEXT-TO-SPEECH WITH LIP SYNCHRONIZATION 963 a good target for the text-to-video alignment. Once the text- to-speech alignment map is obtained from a pre-trained TTS model, the speech frame axis is adjusted to match the frame rate of the video and then used to constrain the attention weight matrix instead of the DC loss. Let Ps denote a set of video frame indices which are mapped into the phoneme s in the alignment map. We include one more frame before and after the video frames aligned with the phoneme s in Ps to allow alignment error of one frame in the text-to-speech alignment. Ns = P c s denotes the set of all other frames. The STA loss is proposed as LSTA = Tp  s=1  −  t∈Ps A(s,t) + λ  t∈Ns A(s,t)  , (4) which not only rewards correct alignment but also penalizes incorrect alignment. λ is a hyperparameter that controls the relative importance of rewards and penalties. It is noted again that the text-to-speech alignment is only required during training to guide the text-to-video alignment and is not used in the inference phase. B. Masked Unit Prediction The lip image may not be available in all video frames due to the occlusion, or when the speaker is not facing the camera or even out-of-camera. To achieve a satisfactory TTSLS in such a situation, it would be beneﬁcial to enforce TTSLS system to uti- lize the contextual information. One of the popular approaches to facilitate the learning of the contextual representation is the masked unit prediction (MP), which is proven to be effective in language models [31] and automatic speech recognition (ASR) models [32]. We adopt the MP loss in the proposed TTSLS system so that the randomly masked units in Z can be predicted from the C′ for the rest of the frames. The MP loss is given as the CE loss for the masked-out frames when the input C′ is also masked, i.e., LMP = −  i∈M z(i) log F( ˜C′)(i) (5) where M is a set of randomly selected indices to mask out context embeddings, and ˜C′ is the masked version of C′. The ﬁnal loss function for the proposed TTSLS system adopt- ing STA and MP becomes L = LCE + LSTA + LMP. C. Missing Modality-Resilient Probability Modiﬁcation An alternative way to train a model that can cope with missing lip images is the classiﬁer-free guidance (CFG) [24], [25], [26], [27]. In the CFG, the model outputs both with and without a conditioning variable are utilized to produce the ﬁnal output. To train a model that can operate with and without the conditioning variable, this variable is replaced by a null embedding with a certain probability during training. In the inference phase for classiﬁcation models [26], [27], the ﬁnal probability distribution for the output variable o given the conditioning variable y is modiﬁed as follows: log ˆp(o|y) = log p(o|∅) + η · (log p(o|y) −log p(o|∅)) (6) where p(o|y) is the classiﬁer output which is a probability dis- tribution over possible classes for o, ˆp(o|y) is the modiﬁed ﬁnal output, ∅is the null embedding, and η is the scale of guidance. In the proposed TTSLS system, the unit predictor output can be interpreted as the conditional probability p(Z|Xp, Xv). As the phoneme sequence would always be present while the lip images can be missing for some of the video frames, we occasionally replace a part of the conditioning variables, Xv, with a null em- bedding X∅which is learnable as in [27] during training. In the inference phase, the output conditional probability p(Z|Xp, Xv) is modiﬁed as log ˆp(Z|Xp, Xv) = η · (log p(Z|Xp, Xv) −log p(Z|Xp, X∅)) + log p(Z|Xp, X∅). (7) Since the conditioning variable in the proposed model is not a class label from a classiﬁer as in [24] and [25], the CFG is not an adequate name in this case. We call it missing modality-resilient probability modiﬁcation (MRM). III. EXPERIMENTAL SETTINGS A. Dataset and Preprocessing We have evaluated the proposed and compared methods on Chem dataset [33], which is a chemistry lecture audio-visual dataset. Chem includes 6,616 utterances (9.26 hours) of a single male speaker which are divided into training, validation, and test sets of 6,216, 200, and 200 samples, respectively. Image and audio in all videos are sampled at 30 Hz and 16 kHz, respectively. For the image, we cropped 88×88 lip regions of interest and extracted 1024 dimensional visual features using a pre-trained audio-visual speech recognition model, AV-HuBERT2 [34], which were used as Xv. Among shared AV-HuBERT models, we used AV-HuBERT Large with Self-training model pre-trained with LRS3 [35] and Voxceleb2 [36] databases and ﬁne-tuned for visual speech recognition on 433 hours of labeled LRS3 [35]. The text features Xp was extracted in the same way as in many papers [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], from the transcription of Chem dataset provided by the authors of Neural Dubber [1]. As for the speech tokenizer which generates discrete speech units Z from speech, we used HuBERT Base model [32] pre-trained on 960 hours of LibriSpeech database [37] to generate speech representations which were the sixth layer embeddings, and applied the k-means clustering [38] with 100 clusters for which the centroids were determined for LibriSpeech database [37] as in [21], [30] and [4]. The frame rate for discrete speech unit Z was 50Hz. B. Model Conﬁgurations We utilized the model architecture of DSU-AVO [4] which was constructed with feed-forward transformer (FFT) blocks [29]. The numbers of FFT blocks in the text and video encoders were 4 and 2, respectively. The video-text aligner was implemented as scaled dot-product attention as in (1). The unit predictor was constructed with 2 FFT blocks, a fully connected layer, and a softmax function. When MP was applied, the input of each FFT block in the unit predictor was masked with a probability of 0.5 during training. As for the Text-to-Speech 2https://github.com/facebookresearch/av_hubert Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 964 IEEE SIGNAL PROCESSING LETTERS, VOL. 32, 2025 aligner, MAS in VITS2 [10]3 TTS model trained on the Chem dataset for 10,000 steps was adopted. Unit HiFi-GAN [21] pre-trained on the Chem dataset4 was used as the unit vocoder as in [4]. λ and η in (4) and (7) were chosen empirically to be 0.1 and 10, respectively. For MRM, Xv was replaced by X∅with the probability of 0.5. The batch size was 16 and the TTSLS model was trained for 120 epochs. C. Evaluation Metrics For the objective evaluation, we have assessed two measures for lip-speech synchronization and the word error rate (WER) for speech intelligibility. The ﬁrst measure for synchronization was the lip sync error - distance (LSE-D) computed using Sync- Net5 [39], which was trained to produce similar embeddings for the synchronized lip images and speech while enforcing the embeddings for the lip images and speech which are not synchronized signiﬁcantly different. LSE-D is the average Eu- clidean distance between the embeddings for the lip images and the synthesized speech. The lip sync error - conﬁdence (LSE-C) is also given, which is conﬁdence for the LSE-D deﬁned as the difference between the minimum and the median of the LSE-D’s computed for the video sequences shifted by -10 to +10 frames. These measures were proposed in [39] and used in [1], [3], [4] and [40]. The other measure for synchronization, the frame disturbance (FD) [41], compares the target speech and the syn- thesized speech using the dynamic time warping (DTW) [42]. The (n, m)-th entry of the cost matrix of the DTW was the L1 norm between the Mel-frequency cepstral coefﬁcients for the n-th frame of the synthesized speech and the m-th frame of the target speech. After ﬁnding the optimal path using the DTW, this path was compared with the perfect diagonal path, which would be optimal when the synthesized speech perfectly matches the target speech. Let ϵ(k) denote the difference between the optimal path for the k-th frame of the synthesized speech and the diagonal. Then, the FD was computed as  1 K K k=1 ϵ2 (k) where K is the number of speech frames. The WERs were eval- uated using the Wav2Vec 2.0 Large ASR model [43] pre-trained and ﬁne-tuned on 960 hours of LibriSpeech database [37]. As the quality of the synthesized speech cannot be assessed by an objective measure and the perceptual comfortableness for the synchronizationmayalsobeimportant, weperformedsubjective preference tests. As for the perceived quality of the synthesized speech, the ITU-T Recommendation P.800 comparison category rating (CCR) test [44] was carried out. 12 participants listened to the speeches synthesized by DSU-AVO [4] and the proposed STAMP for 15 randomly selected videos and gave their pref- erences from -3 to +3 for each video, which were averaged to produce comparative mean opinion score (CMOS). Regarding the lip-speech synchronization, a preference test was conducted in which the same set of people gave their opinion on the synchronization for the speeches generated by DSU-AVO and the proposed method on the same 15 videos with three options including “No preference.” 3https://github.com/p0p4k/vits2_pytorch 4https://github.com/RanaCM/DSU-AVO 5https://github.com/joonson/syncnet_python TABLE I SYNCHRONIZATION AND SPEECH RECOGNITION PERFORMANCES FOR TEXT-TO-SPEECH SYNTHESIS WITH LIP SYNCHRONIZATION IV. RESULTS Table I summarizes the objective performances for lip-speech synchronization and speech intelligibility. Ground Truth denotes the original speech in the videos and Unit Resynthesis stands for the speech generated by Unit Hiﬁ-GAN with the speech tokenizer output Z for the original speech. They would provide the best possible performances when there is no constraint and when the speech is synthesized from the discrete speech units, respectively. The performance of the proposed system, STAMP, was compared with those for Neural Dubber [1] and DSU-AVO [4]. The performance of TTS system VITS2 [10] which does not use visual features is also provided. The pro- posed method, STAMP, showed superior performance to Neural Dubber [1] and DSU-AVO [4] in both synchronization and ASR. VITS2 exhibited WER better than Unit Resynthesis as it does not synthesize speech via discrete units, and showed bad synchronization performance as expected. The WER for STAMP was slightly lower than Unit Resynthesis which comes from a few mismatches between the text transcription and speech in the Chem dataset. The CMOS for speech quality of STAMP over DSU-AVO was +0.25 with a 95% conﬁdence interval of 0.219. The reduction of WER and slight improvement of quality may be because the attention weight matrix A was estimated more accurately for STAMP, which in turn affected the estimates for Z and Xa. As for the synchronization, STAMP was preferred in 19.4% of responses while DSU-AVO was chosen to be better in 9.4% of them. It was reported in [45] that people were tolerant to the timing difference less than 45 ms, which may explain 71.2% of “No preference.” Further analyses on STAMP including example videos and attention matrices are provided in the webpage1. V. CONCLUSION In this letter, we propose a TTSLS system based on speech- assisted text-to-video alignment and masked unit prediction (STAMP) that utilizes ground-truth speech available during training and considers modality missing problem. For text-to- videoalignment,weproposetheSTAlossinsteadofthediagonal constraint loss, which utilizes the text-to-speech alignment of the ground-truth text and speech to determine “correct” align- ment used to compute the loss function. We also adopt the masked unit prediction and missing modality-resilient proba- bility modiﬁcation to deal with video frames without frontal lip images. Experimental results showed that the proposed STAMP TTSLS system outperformed previously proposed TTSLS systems in both lip-speech synchronization and speech recognition. title: IEEE/ACM TRANSACTIONS ON AUDIO, SPEECH, AND LANGUAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 32, 2024
file_name: Textless Unit-to-Unit Training for Many-to-Many Multilingual Speech-to-Speech Translation.pdf
text: 3934 IEEE/ACM TRANSACTIONS ON AUDIO, SPEECH, AND LANGUAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 32, 2024 Textless Unit-to-Unit Training for Many-to-Many Multilingual Speech-to-Speech Translation Minsu Kim , Jeongsoo Choi , Dahun Kim, and Yong Man Ro , Senior Member, IEEE Abstract—This paper proposes a textless training method for many-to-many multilingual speech-to-speech translation that can also beneﬁt the transfer of pre-trained knowledge to text-based systems, text-to-speech synthesis and text-to-speech translation. To this end, we represent multilingual speech with speech units that are the discretized representations of speech features de- rived from a self-supervised speech model. By treating the speech units as pseudo-text, we can focus on the linguistic content of the speech, which can be easily associated with both speech and text modalities at the phonetic level information. By setting both the inputs and outputs of our learning problem as speech units, we propose to train an encoder-decoder model in a many-to-many spoken language translation setting, namely Unit-to-Unit Transla- tion (UTUT). Speciﬁcally, the encoder is conditioned on the source language token to correctly understand the input spoken language, while the decoder is conditioned on the target language token to generate the translated speech in the target language. Therefore, during the training, the model can build the knowledge of how languages are comprehended and how to relate them to different languages. Since speech units can be easily associated from both audio and text by quantization and phonemization respectively, the trained model can easily transferred to text-related tasks, even if it is trained in a textless manner. We demonstrate that the proposed UTUT model can be effectively utilized not only for Speech-to-Speech Translation (S2ST) but also for multilingual Text-to-Speech Synthesis (T2S) and Text-to-Speech Translation (T2ST), requiring only minimal ﬁne-tuning steps on text inputs. By conducting comprehensive experiments encompassing various languages, we validate the efﬁcacy of the proposed method across diverse multilingual tasks. Moreover, thanks to the many-to-many language training, we show that the UTUT can also perform lan- guage translations for novel language pairs that are not present during training as pairs, which has not well been explored in the previous literature. Manuscript received 18 December 2023; revised 18 June 2024 and 30 July 2024; accepted 3 August 2024. Date of publication 15 August 2024; date of current version 6 September 2024. This work was supported in part by the Institute of Information & communications Technology Planning & Evalu- ation (IITP) funded by the Korea government (MSIT) under Grant 2022-0- 00124, Development of Artiﬁcial Intelligence Technology for Self-Improving Competency-Aware Learning Capabilities, and in part by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Korea government (MSIT) under Grant NRF-2022R1A2C2005529. The associate editor coordinating the review ofthisarticleandapprovingitforpublicationwasProf.Hung-yiLee.(MinsuKim and Jeongsoo Choi contributed equally to this work.) (Corresponding author: Yong Man Ro.) Minsu Kim, Jeongsoo Choi, and Yong Man Ro are with the Integrated Vision and Language Laboratory, School of Electrical Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Daejeon 34141, South Korea (e-mail: ms.k@kaist.ac.kr; jeongsoo.choi@kaist.ac.kr; ymro@kaist.ac.kr). Dahun Kim is with the Google DeepMind, Mountain View, CA 94043 USA (e-mail: mcahny01@gmail.com). Samples can be found on https://choijeongsoo.github.io/utut. Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TASLP.2024.3444470 Index Terms—Speech-to-speech translation (S2ST), text-to- speech translation (S2ST), text-to-speech synthesis (T2S), unit-to- unit translation (UTUT). I. INTRODUCTION E VEN though we grew up in different countries, we can easily communicate with people having different nation- alities with recent advanced technologies, Automated Speech Recognition (ASR) [1], [2], [3], [4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], Neural Machine Translation (NMT) [10], [11], [12], [13], [14], and Text-to-Speech Synthesis (T2S) [15], [16], [17], [18], [19], [20]. As the demand for communication tools with foreigners continues to grow, technologies are being developed to support multilingualism, reﬂecting the trend of globalization. One of the most popular large language models [21], [22], Chat-GPT [23] has been trained in over 60 languages and is successfully pro- viding services, including NMT. However, the technologies handling speech, especially speech synthesis technologies, are encountering challenges in being extended to multilingual [24], [25], [26], [27]. They are typically developed to handle only one language (i.e., monolingual), so different models should be prepared to apply them to different languages. The main impediment in building multilingual mod- els is the inherent complexity of speech data. While the text is naturally discrete and only covers linguistic content, the speech iscontinuousandconveysvariousspeakercharacteristicssuchas voice, accent, and timbre. This inherent characteristic of speech puts a challenge in focusing only on the linguistic content of multilingual speech, while the text from different languages can be easily handled with a ﬁxed vocabulary size by using subword tokenizers [28], [29], [30] and phonemizers [24], [31]. In recent days, speech unit [32], a discretized speech feature, has shown remarkable potential in speech synthesis [32], [33], [34] and speech recognition [7], [35], [36]. These speech units, closely related to acoustic units such as phonemes [35], are obtained by quantizing (i.e., clustering) extracted speech rep- resentations derived from a self-supervised speech model [35], [37],[38].Byprocessingspeechusingspeechunits,afascinating possibility arises: treating speech units as pseudo text, thus enabling textless Natural Language Processing (NLP) [32], [39]. As different languages share some common phonemes [40], [41], [42], it is available to tokenize multilingual speech with a ﬁxed vocabulary size by using speech units (i.e., the acous- tic units), similar to phonemizers in text modality. Moreover, the speech units enable the establishment of a straightforward connectionbetweenspeechandtextatthephoneticlevel,thereby 2329-9290 © 2024 IEEE. KIM et al.: TEXTLESS UNIT-TO-UNIT TRAINING FOR MANY-TO-MANY MULTILINGUAL SPEECH-TO-SPEECH TRANSLATION 3935 facilitating the seamless transfer of knowledge between these two modalities. In this paper, by employing the characteristics of speech units, we propose a textless many-to-many multilingual speech- to-speech translation method, namely Unit-to-Unit Translation (UTUT). Since the proposed method is textless, the developed machine translation system can be applied even for languages that have no writing systems [43]. To this end, UTUT training proceeds bysettingtheinputs andoutputs of amodel withspeech units derived from multilingual speech. Its learning problem is composed of a many-to-many spoken language translation problem by conditioning the encoder with the source language token and the decoder with the target language token. Through the many-to-many language UTUT training, the model can ef- fectively build knowledge on how languages are comprehended and how to convert them into various other languages, even with much smaller amounts of data compared to the unidirectional model. By associating the speech units with speech or text, the trained model can be easily transferred to diverse speech- and text-related downstream tasks, Speech-to-Speech Transla- tion (S2ST), multilingual Text-to-Speech Synthesis (T2S), and Text-to-Speech Translation (T2ST), even though the model is originally trained without text. The major contributions of this paper are as follows, r We propose a textless Unit-to-Unit training method for many-to-many multilingual speech-to-speech translation. As the speech units mainly contain the linguistic infor- mation (e.g., phoneme), the trained model can be easily transferredtotext-basedsystemsofText-to-Speechsynthe- sis (T2S) and Text-to-Speech Translation (T2ST), despite being trained in a text-free manner. r To the best of our knowledge, this is the ﬁrst work exploring textless and many-to-many language translation in S2ST. By training the model with the proposed Unit-to-Unit Translation (UTUT), we can effectively build S2ST sys- tems even with much smaller training data compared to unidirectional S2ST models. r We show that through the proposed training with many- to-many languages, we can perform language translations even for novel language pairs that are never given as pairs during training. II. RELATED WORK Self-Supervised SpeechModel: Pre-trained speech processing models using self-supervised learning have achieved signiﬁ- cant performances in ASR [35], [37], speaker veriﬁcation [44], and speech synthesis [32], [45]. HuBERT [35] proposed a self-supervised learning method that predicts hidden units ob- tained by clustering the acoustic features (e.g., MFCC) from masked audio. By reﬁning the target hidden units with clusters of the trained features, they achieved state-of-the-art speech recognition performances even in the low-resource setups. AV- HuBERT [7] successfully extended the training method of Hu- BERT to audio-visual speech representation learning, and even showed powerful lip reading [46], [47], [48], [49] performances by ﬁnetuning the trained model on the labeled dataset. Textless Natural Language Processing (NLP) has emerged with high-quality speech representations extracted by self- supervised speech models [35], [37]. By quantizing the speech features,theyobtainedspeechunitsthatcanbeutilizedaspseudo texts, where non-linguistic features are suppressed and linguistic contents are kept [50]. By treating the speech units as pseudo texts, spoken language modeling [32], [51], speech emotion conversion [52], and speech resynthesis [33] are proposed. Based on the speech units obtained by quantizing multilingual speech, in this paper, we try to build textless many-to-many mul- tilingual S2ST model. Since speech units are highly correlated with acoustic units, we can easily transfer the trained model to diverse multilingual speech- and text-related downstream tasks. Speech-to-SpeechTranslation: Speech-to-SpeechTranslation (S2ST) [53] aims to directly synthesize the translated speech in the target language from the input speech of the source language. Recently, the technology has drawn big attention, since it can reduce error propagation and time consumption of cascaded systems of ASR [54], [55], [56], MT [57], [58], and T2S [59], [60]. Early methods tried to synthesize spectrograms from input speech in an end-to-end manner. Translatotron [61] proposed a spectrogram-to-spectrogram model trained with aux- iliary ASR and Speech-to-Text Translation [62] losses by using text supervision. Translatotron2 [63], the enhanced model of the previous version, proposed to jointly train the model with a speech-to-speechtranslationobjectiveandaspeech-to-phoneme translation objective. Kano et al. [64] proposed to use Trans- former sequence-to-sequence model [65] in S2ST. Asthespeechunitcanbetreatedaspseudotext,speech-to-unit translation models are proposed instead of predicting spectro- grams [66], [67]. Lee et al. [68] used speech units obtained from HuBERT [35] as targets and trained the model with multitask learning of text and speech unit prediction. Lee et al. [43] proposed a textless S2ST model which does not utilize text supervision. Jia et al. [69] showed using a pre-trained speech encoder, w2v-BERT [70], can boost the S2ST performance. Popuri et al. [71] proposed to use a pre-trained speech encoder and a pre-trained unit decoder, and proposed ﬁnetuning strate- gies. Diwan et al. [72] extended the previous work to operate with low-resource languages by learning with a round-trip trans- lation consistency loss, which uses the back translation [73]. Recently, [74] extended [67] with multimodal and large-scale datasets and proposed a powerful multimodal and multilingual machine translation system. In this paper, we train the model with a Unit-to-Unit Trans- lation (UTUT) task constructed as a many-to-many spoken language translation, without using text supervision. Through many-to-many language translation learning, the proposed method can effectively learn with signiﬁcantly fewer parallel data compared to language-speciﬁc translation models. More- over, the proposed UTUT can perform language translation between novel language pairs for which parallel data is absent in the training set. The proposed method can be easily extended to text-related tasks, Text-to-Speech Translation (T2ST) and multilingual Text-to-Speech Synthesis (T2S). Please note that different from the recently proposed method [74], the pro- posed method is a textless training method and is trained in a Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies.
