title: Published by the IEEE Computer Society
file_name: The Rise of Agentic AI in Finance Opportunities- Risks- and Human-Centric Integration.pdf
text: IT Professional Published by the IEEE Computer Society July/August 2025 19 This paper looks at the growing role of agentic artificial intelligence (AI) in financial services, focusing on its capabilities to enhance operational efficiency, automate complex tasks, and deliver personalized customer experiences. It also analyzes the challenges related to data quality, regulatory compliance, security risks, and the importance of human oversight to ensure trustworthy and effective AI integration. A lthough agentic artificial intelligence (AI) is still in its “trial phase,” its core capabilities—such as language processing, predictive analytics, and reasoning—are already well developed.1 Despite the challenges of responsible integration in finance, these hurdles are likely to be overcome, driving rapid adop- tion. Wolters Kluwer N.V., a Dutch information services company, surveyed 392 finance leaders in May 2025, finding that 6% had adopted agentic AI and 38% plan to do so within the next 12 months—bringing expected utilization to 44% by 2026.2 Top firms in accounting are also deploying agentic AI to streamline operations—from standard compli- ance tasks and routine tax work to advanced finan- cial forecasting and decision-making. In March 2025, Deloitte and Ernst & Young (EY) announced the use of autonomous AI systems to assist clients with tasks like document uploads and financial statement anal- ysis. Deloitte’s Zora AI offers deployable agents that “perceive, reason, and act,” while EY plans to inte- grate AI agents across over 30 million tax processes. EY adopted autonomous AI tools in its tax practice to leverage its vast in-house tax data and address di- verse global client needs, while Deloitte’s early agents, including Zora AI—already in use by Hewlett Packard Enterprise—support finance teams with tasks such as expense management, financial analysis, and scenario modeling. PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) has also developed similar agentic capabilities (see “Exhibit 1: Agentic AI in Action: How PwC Is Transforming Tax ­Operations with Intelligent Agents”).3 Building on this momentum, specialized startups are also advancing agentic AI tailored to industry-spe- cific needs. In February 2025, Zurich-based Unique secured €28.7 million in Series A funding to advance its agentic AI platform for financial institutions. The platform supports 25 ready-to-use applications or cus- tomizable agents, ensuring regulatory compliance and data security while integrating seamlessly into back and middle-office operations.4 These trends underscore a rapid shift toward AI-driv- en processes in the finance sector, and some analysts contend that agentic AI will surpass the Internet era in economic impact5; Citigroup even predicts it could ush- er in a “do it for me” Economy that outpaces previous digital revolutions.6 For instance, Accenture was report- ed to be helping global banks, including Westpac, de- velop AI agents for customer service, compliance, and sales. Applications include handling calls, writing credit memos, and assessing risks. According to Accenture, banks are seeing 20%–30% productivity gains.7 Agentic AI is transforming traditional financial processes by enabling more adaptive and intelligent decision-making. For example, unlike conventional credit scoring models that rely on static data, agen- tic AI allows banks and fintechs to perform continu- ous credit assessments using real-time transaction data, behavioral trends, and economic indicators— resulting in faster approvals and more precise risk management.8 EDITOR: Nir Kshetri, nbkshetr@uncg.edu DEPARTMENT: IT ECONOMICS The Rise of Agentic AI in Finance: Opportunities, Risks, and Human-Centric Integration Nir Kshetri , University of North Carolina at Greensboro, Greensboro, NC, 27412, USA 1520-9202 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/MITP.2025.3585227 Date of current version 15 August 2025. uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply IT ECONOMICS 20 IT Professional July/August 2025 This article examines the transformative impact of agentic AI on the financial services industry, highlight- ing its applications in automation, decision-making, and customer engagement. It also addresses the key chal- lenges and considerations—such as regulatory com- pliance, data quality, and security—that organizations must navigate for successful and responsible adoption. TRANSFORMING FINANCE WITH AGENTIC AI: FROM BACK-OFFICE EFFICIENCY TO PROACTIVE CUSTOMER CARE Agentic AI in banking has two main focuses: internal operational efficiency, such as automating routine tasks and building predictive models for trading and risk management, and customer-facing services, like auto- mated help desks and personalized investment advice. Both aim to improve efficiency and reduce costs.6 Regarding internal operational efficiency, agentic AI is often likened to an unlimited pool of intelligent interns, capable of automating tedious back-end tasks and streamlining workflows.9 Agentic AI is already boosting productivity and cutting human error by automating repetitive tasks such as data entry, com- pliance checks, and transaction processing—freeing employees to focus on more strategic work. Key oper- ational gains include real-time compliance monitoring for regulatory adherence, intelligent fraud detection, dynamic risk assessment, and streamlined “know your customer” (KYC)/anti-money laundering (AML) workflows, all powered by autonomous decision-mak- ing capabilities.10 As fraudsters increasingly exploit AI, agentic AI offers a powerful defense by detecting anomalies, tracing threats across systems, and auton- omously initiating preventive actions such as freez- ing transactions or triggering audits. In compliance, these systems can interpret evolving regulations and autonomously adjust internal protocols, transforming compliance from a reactive burden into a proactive, streamlined service.11 Additionally, agentic AI offers significant cost savings by automating tasks tradition- ally outsourced to contractors or third parties.5 As to customer-facing services, agentic AI moves beyond traditional chatbot functionality by recogniz- ing intent, remembering prior interactions, and deliv- ering proactive, customized financial guidance.11 In this evolving landscape, users will have personal AI agents assisting in product selection and transaction execution.5 Agentic AI’s most immediate impact lies in real-time needs assessment—such as identifying wellness concerns during a transaction—and auton- omously negotiating tailored solutions like payment deferrals or debt restructuring. Rather than offering static product options, it dynamically crafts custom- ized financial packages by integrating diverse banking products and services.10 Traditional banking is reac- tive, with customers initiating interactions. Agentic AI reverses this by anticipating needs and offering proac- tive solutions. For example, a farmer could receive pre- approved credit before a drought, insurance recom- mendations before the growing season, or investment advice based on crop yields. This enhances financial inclusion, literacy, and personalized support.12 THE EXPANDING ROLE OF AGENTIC AI SYSTEMS IN FINANCE Table 1 illustrates how agentic AI is revolutionizing the financial services industry through diverse and impact- ful applications. From optimizing automated trading and enhancing fraud detection to delivering personal- ized financial advice and streamlining regulatory com- pliance, these use cases demonstrate the broad poten- tial of agentic AI to improve efficiency, accuracy, and customer experience across the sector. Real-world ex- amples highlight how leading firms are leveraging these technologies to drive measurable gains and innovation. In automated trading, these systems use advanced algorithms to execute trades with remarkable speed and precision, optimizing returns by analyzing market trends and timing.13 By processing massive volumes of real-time data—like price movements, news senti- ment, and geopolitical developments—agentic AI bots empower hedge funds to act faster and smarter. Singa- pore-based investment firm QuantEdge, for instance, leverages these tools to make hyper-timely trades, producing dynamic portfolios that consistently outper- form manual approaches by as much as 15% per year.14 For fraud detection, they identify suspicious activ- ities by scrutinizing transaction patterns and spotting anomalies in real time, thereby enhancing security.13 Mastercard leverages AI agents to strengthen fraud defense and support consumer dispute resolution by providing end-to-end security. These agents enable robust authentication using on-device biometrics and assist in explaining unfamiliar or questionable transac- tions to users.15 By analyzing transaction data across multiple cards and merchants, Mastercard’s system doubles the detection of compromised cards, decreas- es false positives by as much as 200%, and speeds up spotting at-risk merchants threefold.14 In terms of personalized financial advice, agentic AI can analyze vast customer data, providing tailored investment strategies that align with individual risk profiles.13 Era, an AI-powered personal finance plat- form, raised $6.2 million in February 2025 to advance its agentic AI capabilities for real-time, personalized uthorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:17:16 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply IT ECONOMICS IT Professional 21 July/August 2025 wealth management. Unlike traditional apps, Era au- tonomously manages finances—optimizing balances, transferring funds, and offering tailored insights—us- ing automation enhanced through a partnership with Cerebras for institutional-grade intelligence.16 Furthermore, these systems power portfolio man- agement tools that continuously monitor and adjust investment strategies to ensure maximum returns in dynamic market conditions.13 Acting as intelligent fi- nancial companions, these systems retain long-term EXHIBIT 1: AGENTIC AI IN ACTION: HOW PwC IS TRANSFORMING TAX OPERATIONS WITH INTELLIGENT AGENTS A cross PricewaterhouseCoopers’s (PwC’s) tax oper- ations, teams of AI agents are actively deployed, enhancing efficiency by automating interpretation and mapping tasks and marking a progression from experi- mental stages to routine implementation.S1 PwC has joined forces with Microsoft and Salesforce to create AI agents for clients and recently extended this effort through a new alliance with Oracle3. PwC trained its agents to accurately identify and handle various types of expenses and disclosures using historical data, then continuously enhanced their performance through ongoing feedback. Much like managing human teams, PwC reinforced correct decisions, addressed edge cases, and regularly retrained the agents with real-world scenarios to steadily improve accuracy and confidence.S1 Processing Schedule K-1s—a federal tax form that reports each individual’s share of income, deductions, and other items from pass-through entities like part- nerships, S corporations, and trustsS2—has historically been labor-intensive. Tax teams had to interpret varying formats, map line items (e.g., deductible expenses), and often collaborate to resolve unclear footnotes. Agentic AI has transformed this workflow by interpreting footnotes, determining appropriate treatments, and assigning confidence levels to its decisions. Ambiguities are flagged for human review, and when users make corrections, the agent learns from them. This human-in-the-loop (HITL) approach reduces manual effort while improving consis- tency and efficiency in tax reporting.S1 The hybrid AI–human tax teams have reduced K-1 production from nearly two weeks to just one day.S3 Like human team members, each AI agent leverages a specialized set of tools, coordinated by PwC’s Agent OS to efficiently handle tasks such as data mapping and document extraction.S1 Introduced in March 2025, Agent OS functions as an enterprise AI command cen- ter that streamlines and scales multiagent workflows up to ten times faster than conventional approaches. By offering a unified, scalable platform for building and integrating AI agents across diverse systems, it accel- erates the move from pilot projects to full enterprise adoption.S4 PwC manages over 800 custom GPTs and 250+ AI agents, including in tax, and developed Agent OS to better organize, orchestrate, and monitor them efficiently.S1 AI agents are expected to deliver similar efficiency gains across tax functions—from data processing to deal due diligence—by understanding context, taking proactive actions, learning from errors, and integrat- ing seamlessly with enterprise platforms.S3 However, AI use remains limited in areas like policy interpreta- tion and high-touch consulting due to the complex reasoning and client interaction that still require a human touch.S1

title: Received 28 January 2025, accepted 17 February 2025, date of publication 25 February 2025, date of current version 7 March 2025.
file_name: The Summarization of Italian Tax-Law Decisions The Case of the PRODIGIT Project.pdf
text: Received 28 January 2025, accepted 17 February 2025, date of publication 25 February 2025, date of current version 7 March 2025. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3545419 The Summarization of Italian Tax-Law Decisions: The Case of the PRODIGIT Project ALESSIA FIDELANGELI1, FEDERICO GALLI1, ANDREA LOREGGIA 2, GIUSEPPE PISANO3, RICCARDO ROVATTI 4, (Fellow, IEEE), PIERA SANTIN 5, AND GIOVANNI SARTOR1,6 1Department of Legal Studies, Alma Mater University of Bologna, 1088 Bologna, Italy 2Department of Information Engineering (DII), University of Brescia, 25123 Brescia, Italy 3Alma Mater Research Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence-(Alma AI), University of Bologna, 40126 Bologna, Italy 4Department of Electrical, Electronic, and Information Engineering, Guglielmo Marconi, Alma Mater University of Bologna, 1088 Bologna, Italy 5Robert Schuman Centre for Advanced Studies, European University Institute, 50139 Florence, Italy 6Department of Law, European University Institute, 50139 Florence, Italy Corresponding authors: Andrea Loreggia (andrea.loreggia@unibs.it) and Giuseppe Pisano (g.pisano@unibo.it) This work was supported in part by the Programma Operativo Nazionale (PON) Governance and Institutional Capacity Program 2014–2020 as part of the ‘‘PRO.DI.GI.T’’ Project, and in part by the European Research Council (ERC) Advanced Grant ‘‘CompuLaw’’ under the Europe (EU) Horizon 2020 Program under Grant 833647. This work involved human subjects in its research. Approval of all ethical and experimental procedures and protocols was granted by the Review Board of the PRODIGIT Project. ABSTRACT This paper presents an innovative approach to the automated summarization of tax-law decisions developed within the PRODIGIT project. The work addresses the growing challenge of managing large volumes of judicial rulings, which often hinder access to relevant legal precedents. We introduce a hybrid methodology that combines extractive and abstractive summarization techniques, with a particular emphasis on the application of large language models (LLMs) for abstractive summarization. Our approach includes designing and evaluating various prompt-based configurations, leading to the development of a ‘‘combined summarization’’ method, where distinct summary components are generated and integrated into a cohesive text. Experimental results, validated by tax law experts, demonstrate that this method yields the most comprehensive and accurate summaries. Additionally, we explore the integration of these summaries into semantic search functions, enabling users to retrieve and comprehend relevant case law efficiently. Our findings highlight the potential of AI-driven summarization tools to enhance legal transparency, promote judicial consistency, and support the work of judges, lawyers, and legal scholars. INDEX TERMS Tax law, artificial intelligence, abstractive summarization, extractive summarization, large language models. I. INTRODUCTION For some time now, Italy has faced challenges with the accessibility and availability of lower court tax rulings. The decisions of the Corti di Giustizia Tributaria, i.e. Courts of Tax Justice (formerly known as Commissioni tributarie, i.e. Tax Commissions) are not fully digitized, with only a small portion of their decisions available in digital form. Furthermore, they are not always accessible to professionals and citizens. The lack of digitization and proper indexing of rulings makes it difficult for even tax judges to find relevant decisions. The current situation is further complicated by The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Jolanta Mizera-Pietraszko . the fact that different territorial courts often interpret similar issues in varying ways (as was particularly evident in the case of the independent taxability of penalty clauses for reg- istration tax purposes). Furthermore, different interpretations frequently emerge within short time frames, even within the same territorial jurisdiction. The limited access to judicial rulings hinders the flow of information within the judiciary itself, negatively impacting both decision-making awareness and the depth of academic and legal debate surrounding the issues decided by the courts of first and second instance. The future, however, promises a significantly different scenario. The Ministry of Economy and Finance is currently developing a publicly accessible database of lower court tax rulings. This database will include new decisions from VOLUME 13, 2025 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ 38833 A. Fidelangeli et al.: Summarization of Italian Tax-Law Decisions: The Case of the PRODIGIT Project Courts of Tax Justice of first and second instance, with a plan to progressively incorporate past judgments, eventually encompassing all decisions. Once the database is operational, there will be a shift from information scarcity to an overabundance of available data. Judges and lawyers will have access to the entire body of case law, but without adequate tools, it will be challenging to identify those most pertinent to a given case. The PRODIGIT project aims to address this need in advance by using Artificial Intelligence (AI) to facilitate the identification of relevant rulings and comprehension of their content. The primary goal of PRODIGIT is to provide legal professionals with the ability to perform a form of ‘‘triage’’ of potentially relevant rulings, enabling them to select those significant to the case at hand and to reconstruct the interpretative framework within which a specific legal issue must be analyzed. The process of selecting case law proposed by PRODIGIT rests on two pillars: (i) semantic search (based on the meaning of the content) of rulings, in response to free-text queries, designed to be as precise and comprehensive as possible to ensure a reliable selection of relevant rulings; (ii) a system for generating brief summaries of each ruling’s content (summarization) to assist the user in deciding whether to read the full ruling. The present paper describes the solutions adopted to develop the summarization task. We first conducted a review of the state of the art in information technology for the search and summarization of rulings. Based on this review, we experimented with various prototype solutions. From the outset, the objective was to develop operational and scalable models (capable of being applied to large volumes of rulings) that could provide the foundation for operational tools readily usable by tax law practitioners and citizens. The idea behind these models was not to use AI to replace legal professionals but to support them in their work. In other words, we adopted a so-called ‘‘human-centric’’ approach, viewing AI as a tool to expand and facilitate knowledge while reserving critical choices and decisions for humans. To develop these prototypes, we formed an interdisciplinary working group comprising experts in computer science, tax law, and legal informatics. In this way, the design of technological solutions was informed from the outset by the practical needs of the legal field. Additionally, in the validation phase, various stakeholders—representatives from different areas potentially interested in the project’s outcomes (tax judges, tax administration, universities, etc.)— were involved. We evaluated different available technologies, testing them in light of experimental results. The automatic summaries produced by the techniques tested in the PRODIGIT project aim to synthesize the ruling’s content, enabling users to assess whether the documents resulting from a search are of interest and warrant full reading. Summarization, therefore, is the process of condensing a large set of information into a shorter document, similar to a summary, that contains the most important information or that is most relevant to the task at hand. At the same time, a summary is not a mere abstract since it focuses more on legal principles than on factual issues. For this reason, we chose to use the terms ‘‘sum- maries’’ and ‘‘summarization’’ rather than ‘‘synthesis’’ or ‘‘abstract’’. The paper makes the following main contributions: • It describes the first project on automated summarization of Italian tax-law decisions on a national scale adopting Artificial Intelligence techniques. • It describes a methodology and a prototype for generating concise summaries of tax law judgments, demonstrating their effectiveness in aiding practitioners by focusing on key elements from multiple sections for coherent and relevant summaries. • It shows that general-purpose large language models (LLMs) outperform task-specific NLP models for summarizing legal texts, particularly short, technical judgments. The study highlights the advantage of modular prompting which yields more accurate and coherent summaries. This remainder of the paper is organized as follows: (Section II) analyzes different types of summarization in the Italian context. Section III presents the methodology used in the project, in terms of the domain analyzed, the creation of datasets, and the methods for evaluating results. Section IV describes Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques for extractive summarization, while Section V describes the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) for both extractive and abstractive summarization. The empirical evaluation is reported in Section VI. II. STATE OF THE ART In the field of tax law, court decisions are typically summarized by legal professionals (Section I). Sometimes, case summaries are provided by the institutions that draft the rulings themselves, such as the maxims of the Italian Court of Cassation or the European Court of Justice [16]. Other times, they are offered by databases that host the decisions (e.g., Wolters Kluwer and Giuffré databases) or in the form of abstracts published by various journals. However, summarizing court rulings is a time-consuming task, and many efforts have been made over the years to automate this process [8]. There are two main approaches for automatic summarization: extractive and abstractive [17]. The first approach refers to developing automated methods for selecting the most significant sentences from the input text and combining them into a new summary. In extractive summarization, no changes are made to the textual content of the extracted sentences. This approach has the advantage of ensuring that all the content in the summary is sourced directly from the input document, with no extraneous additions. Furthermore, it allows the reader to find the selected sentences in the original docu- ment to obtain further context, if necessary. However, the 38834 VOLUME 13, 2025 A. Fidelangeli et al.: Summarization of Italian Tax-Law Decisions: The Case of the PRODIGIT Project extractive approach may fail to capture all the relevant content or do so only at the cost of reproducing large portions of the original text, which defeats the purpose of summarization. Abstractive summarization, on the other hand, aims to generate new text that provides a concise statement of the input documents’ content without reproducing their phrasing. This approach has the advantage of producing a shorter text that, in an appropriate linguistic form, captures the salient content of a much larger document. It is particularly suitable when legal reasoning is developed in long and complex sentences or when a lengthy decision must be summarized into a clear and concise one, regardless of how the content is expressed in the original document. Moreover, an abstractive summary is closer to the work of human summarizers, who paraphrase the full text rather than merely reproducing extracts. In our project, we initially applied extractive summariza- tion models. Extractive text summarization has long been a research area in legal informatics. In 2004, a signifi- cant contribution to this field came from the House of Lords Judgement (HOLJ) project, which provided extractive summaries for a dataset of 188 judgments from 2001- 2003 [12]. However, only recently works in this area have started to yield promising results, thanks to advancements in NLP techniques and machine learning. In PRODIGIT, we experimented with a variety of existing methods, includ- ing optimization techniques [3], multi-task learning [1], machine-learning-based classification [14], and others. The extractive models tested included both unsupervised [3] and supervised approaches [1]. Thanks to progress in NLP, the scientific community has recently shifted towards abstractive or generative summariza- tion to produce more natural and coherent summaries [8]. In particular, abstractive summarization models have been developed for the rulings of the Brazilian Supreme Court [6] and the Supreme Courts of the UK and India [20]. Other stud- ies [6] apply abstractive summarization of case judgments using textual entailment [13]. The development of abstractive summarization meth- ods have been facilitated by the use of large language models (LLMs) [8]. These tools are already employed for summarization in other domains, such as summarizing news articles [11]. However, legal documents have certain peculiarities that might make the application of such tools less effective in the justice sector. Court rulings, for instance, are longer documents with a more rigid structure, involve a higher usage of technical terms, and contain citations that play a more significant role than in other domains [21]. In this work, abstractive summarization models and pre-trained LLMs were applied to court rulings from Indian courts. The results show that abstractive summaries achieved slightly better ratings compared to extractive ones; however, they also presented incomplete or nonsensical sentences and so- called ‘‘hallucinations’’ (e.g., typical errors involving dates and names of people). FIGURE 1. Evaluation pipeline. III. METHODOLOGY We adopted a plan–do–check–act approach (PDCA cycle) (see Figure 1) where, for each iteration, we proceeded with: 1) the planning of activities based on the literature and previous iterations; 2) prototyping with constant feedback from domain experts in the tax field within the working group; 3) formal validation at the end of each cycle. During the development process, we ensured constant interaction between tax law experts and computer scientists. On the one hand, some of the obtained results were submitted to legal experts for rapid evaluation, aimed at improving the techniques used and, in the case of LLM use, the formulation of the prompt. On the other hand, at the end of each phase, the results were validated through quality assessment questionnaires for the summaries (Section IV-A and Section V-D). Adherence to this model meant that in the development activities, we started with simple techniques and progres- sively moved towards more complex solutions, following an incremental/scalar model. This development choice required rapid and periodic evaluations (Section III-C). In general, the incremental development activity was divided into three main phases: during the first phase, we focused on a large group of summarization models to identify those with the best performance for the summariza- tion task, in order to reduce the number of models to be considered. The second phase focused on the models that received the highest scores in the first evaluation, aiming to verify their performance and select the best one. The third and final phase led to the refinement and finalization of the method identified as most suitable for the project’s objectives. As for skills, we considered it essential to have a multidisciplinary working group with both computer science and legal expertise. The former was fundamental for the use of NLP systems and for preparing the prompts, while the latter was necessary for supervising the creation of the dataset and for validating and proposing improvements to the results obtained from automatic summarization. The first group of experts included members from SocietÃ Generale d’Informatica S.p.A. (SOGEI, the Information Technology VOLUME 13, 2025 38835 A. Fidelangeli et al.: Summarization of Italian Tax-Law Decisions: The Case of the PRODIGIT Project company fully owned by the Ministry of Economy and Finance), as well as computer scientists and legal informatics experts from academia. The second group mainly consisted of tax law experts from academia and tax justice. During the validation of the final dataset—identified as the one with the best results by a small group of legal and IT experts—additional validation was performed by tax law experts who had not participated in the development process. A. DATA We focused on judgments concerning registration tax from the Tax Justice Courts. Judgments related to registration tax are generally shorter than others. This is also due to the fact that, in almost all cases, judgments concerning this tax do not address issues related to other taxes. The judgments used in the experiments are judgments on registration tax from the Tax Justice Courts of both the first and second instances. The choice to focus on judgments from both the first and second instances is motivated, on one hand, by the practical consideration that the project aims to develop a prototype applicable to all judgments; and on the other hand, by the desire to enable navigation between first- and second- instance judgments. In particular, we worked on three datasets: 1) Dataset No. 1:712 second-instance judgments (PGD) These judgments were selected by a small committee of legal and IT experts for addressing certain controversial topics (use of the price/- value criterion, statements of unregistered acts, the declaratory nature of division, and identification of patrimonial acts subject to fixed-term registration). The oldest judgments date from 2021, from which a significant number of rulings are available in PGD format. 2) Dataset No. 2: 1260 second-instance judgments (PGD) Judgments from 2023 were chosen to ensure that all the most recent registration tax judgments are summarized. We believed these judgments might contain more relevant principles, less likely to have been overruled by the Court of Cassation. All judgments underwent an automatic anonymization process to conceal both direct and indirect

file_name: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines for a Societal Well-Being Scoring and Monitoring System Moroccan Use Case.pdf
text: Received 22 January 2025, accepted 1 February 2025, date of publication 4 February 2025, date of current version 12 February 2025. Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3538888 Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines for a Societal Well-Being Scoring and Monitoring System: Moroccan Use Case AYOUB JANNANI , SOUKAINA BOUHSISSIN , NAWAL SAEL , AND FAOUZIA BENABBOU Laboratory of Information Technology and Modeling, Faculty of Sciences Ben M’Sick, Hassan II University of Casablanca, Casablanca 20000, Morocco Corresponding author: Ayoub Jannani (jannaniayoub@gmail.com) ABSTRACT In today’s information-rich society, the rapid dissemination of news content across digital platforms plays a pivotal role in shaping public perception and discourse, yet they remain underutilized for sentiment analysis compared to other social media data. This study addresses this gap by using advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to analyze Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) news content, categorizing headlines into themes like politics, business, education, weather, and sport, and applying sentiment analysis to classify them as positive or negative. These models form the foundation of our Societal Wellbeing Scoring and Monitoring System, which quantifies and monitors societal sentiment across key Quality of Life (QoL) aspects in Moroccan news. Our approach utilizes an LDA topic modeling approach in the preprocessing stage on the Moroccan News Arabic Dataset (MNAD) to enhance the quality of selected data sample before building classification models. Following this, we compare deep learning models with AraBERT and asafaya/bert-base-arabic (ASAFAYA) word embeddings to traditional machine learning algorithms using term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF). Notably, the GRU model with ASAFAYA embeddings excelled in the topic modeling task, achieving a 94.74% accuracy. We further analyzed public sentiment using data from Hibapress, a Moroccan news website, by scraping titles, likes, and dislikes to assign positive and negative sentiment scores. Leveraging a Bi-GRU model with ASAFAYA embeddings, we achieved an accuracy of 90.23%. We propose the Societal Wellbeing Scoring and Monitoring System, an aspect-based sentiment analysis framework designed to assign a wellbeing score over a specified sample or time period. Our findings highlight the effectiveness of combining topic modeling with deep learning-driven sentiment analysis to derive actionable insights from Arabic news, thereby enhancing the understanding of societal dynamics in Morocco. INDEX TERMS Text classification, sentiment analysis, news headlines, modern standard Arabic, deep learning, NLP, GRU, Bi-GRU, ASAFAYA, AraBert, wellbeing, quality of life. I. INTRODUCTION Sentiment analysis, leveraging extensive social media data, is crucial for opinion mining, helping predict trends and critical decisions like stock market movements, election outcomes, and pandemic management by gauging public sentiment [1], [2], [3]. Recent studies demonstrate the effec- tiveness of sentiment analysis on news headlines using The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and approving it for publication was Maria Chiara Caschera . advanced techniques such as transformers, achieving high accuracy in classifying sentiments [4]. Moreover, the dynam- ics between news events and social media reactions highlight the potential of news headlines to inform sentiment shifts and market predictions [5], [6]. Assessing QoL at the country level has become essen- tial for understanding a nation’s well-being, guiding pol- icy, and driving socio-economic progress. Indices like the World Happiness Index (WHI) and Human Development Index (HDI) play a central role in this assessment [5]. VOLUME 13, 2025 2025 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 License. For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ 26345 A. Jannani et al.: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines WHI captures subjective well-being through social support, income, and life expectancy, offering insights into gen- eral happiness across populations. HDI complements this by focusing on objective measures, like health, educational attainment, and Gross National Income, to track overall human development and prosperity. Recent research under- scores the potential of machine learning (ML) in enhancing our understanding of these indicators [7], [8], [9]. These studies demonstrate how ML models can identify relation- ships among variables like GNI and life expectancy, offering deeper insights into the predictive power of these dimensions. Additionally, the research [10] highlights the importance of including social media data to assess subjective well-being alongside traditional, objective indicators. While traditional QoL indicators offer critical insights, social media analysis allows researchers to capture real-time shifts in public sen- timent, providing a more nuanced, subjective perspective. Integrating social media data with traditional indicators thus offers a more holistic view of societal well-being, reflecting both measurable socio-economic factors and the immediate, lived experiences of individuals. This approach aligns with the evolving need for real-time, culturally specific assess- ments, marking a significant advancement in the field of quality-of-life studies. As news headlines have been underutilized in for this purpose, despite their potential to offer a more structured and detailed view of public sentiment and dynamics, this creates a promising opportunity to integrate them alongside social media data. This highlights a significant gap and a critical opportunity to leverage these headlines not only for understanding public sentiment but also for assessing broader societal indicators of QoL. The accurate analysis of senti- ments expressed in news headlines can provide policymakers and researchers with actionable insights into public opinion, potentially leading to more informed decision-making and responsive governance. To address this opportunity, our study employs advanced NLP techniques to analyze news content from Moroccan digital media. We start by categorizing these headlines into specific themes such as health, education, and economic stability before performing sentiment analysis. This cate- gorization allows for a more refined understanding of the content of the news articles providing a view on most prevalent and trending topics. Furthermore, to deepen our understanding of public reactions, we perform sentiment analysis on data collected via web scraping from Hiba- press, a Moroccan news website. This analysis focuses on user interactions, specifically likes and dislikes, to derive a nuanced understanding of public emotional responses. The data gathered has enabled us to develop intelligent models able of categorizing news into polar categories based on the sentiment they evoke, thereby providing a more refined analysis of how news content impacts public perception. The integration of topic modeling and sentiment analy- sis allows for the development of intelligent models that categorize news into polar categories based on sentiment, reflecting public perceptions of quality of life [11], [12]. This innovative approach not only provides insights into media influence but also highlights the effectiveness of ML in sen- timent analysis [13]. A key contribution of this study is the development of a novel dataset we scraped, designed for sentiment analysis on news headlines. This dataset is annotated with scores reflect- ing the differences between likes and dislikes, providing a direct, measurable index of sentiment polarity to enhance the precision of headline categorization based on public emotional responses. Additionally, by focusing on news headlines, rather than full articles, the study benefits from straightforward implementation and efficient analysis, lever- aging headlines from social media posts and news websites which allows for real-time sentiment tracking. Furthermore, the use of MSA in the datasets ensures the methodologies are relevant and adaptable across the Arabic-speaking world, broadening the scope for regional application. This study contributes to the research field focused on assessing societal well-being by introducing the Societal Wellbeing Scoring and Monitoring System, an innova- tive framework that quantifies public sentiment across key societal themes derived from news headlines. The system employs a weighted scoring method, assigning sentiment scores to categorized headlines and calculating an overall well-being score that is normalized for easy interpretation. This system aims to provide daily, monthly, and quarterly insights, enabling researchers and policymakers to monitor shifts in public sentiment over time and to identify spe- cific areas that impact societal well-being. This approach offers a novel tool for assessing well-being, grounded in real-time data from Moroccan news, and paves the way for further applications in understanding societal dynamics through news media analysis. The remainder of this article is organized as follows: First, the background of our work presenting key defini- tions of concepts that form the basis of our methodology, then the Related Work section reviews existing literature and identifies the research gap, focusing on the application of NLP techniques to News content. The Methodology section details the data collection, preprocessing steps, and analytical techniques employed in the topic modeling and sentiment analysis experimentations. Following this, the conceptual framework for assessing societal wellbeing will be presented, the Discussion section interprets the findings, emphasizing their implications for understanding societal trends and pub- lic sentiments. Finally, the conclusion sections summarize the study’s contributions to the field of computational social science and suggests directions for future research to expand the scope and depth of media content analysis. II. BACKGROUND This study utilizes advanced NLP techniques to systemati- cally analyze and quantify themes and sentiments in Moroc- can news outlets. NLP involves the use of computational 26346 VOLUME 13, 2025 A. Jannani et al.: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines methods to process and analyze human language, with two core techniques being applied: topic modeling and sentiment analysis. Topic modeling, specifically through the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) algorithm, is used to discover hidden themes in large text datasets by categorizing textual data into coherent topics [14]. Sentiment analysis classifies text based on its emotional tone, identifying whether content is positive, negative, or neutral [15]. These methods provide a structured approach to extracting valuable information from unstructured news data. The study further employs ML models for text classi- fication tasks. Some of the key models include Support Vector Machine (SVM), a supervised learning algorithm used for classification; Random Forests (RF), an ensem- ble learning method that builds multiple decision trees and outputs the most frequent class; and Logistic Regres- sion, a statistical model often used for binary classification tasks [16], [17], [18], [19]. In conjunction with these models, term frequency-inverse document frequency vectorization is applied to convert textual data into a numerical form, making it suitable for ML algorithms by quantifying the importance of words in the text relative to a corpus [20]. In addition to ML, deep learning (DL) architectures are used to enhance the effectiveness of NLP tasks. Models such as Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) and Long Short-Term Mem- ory (LSTM) networks are implemented to handle sequential data efficiently, capturing long-term dependencies within the text [21], [22]. Bidirectional variants, such as BiGRU and BiLSTM, are also employed to process text sequences in both forward and backward directions [23]. Word embeddings, a key element of DL-based NLP, are employed to represent words as dense vectors that capture their semantic and syntactic nuances [24]. This study utilizes AraBERT and ASAFAYA, two pre-trained word embedding models specifically designed for MSA [25]. These embed- dings enhance the model’s understanding of Arabic text by providing rich contextual information, improving the overall accuracy of theme and sentiment analysis. Furthermore, to address class imbalance in a dataset, Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) and Random Over Sampler are employed. These methods balance the dataset by generating synthetic examples or duplicating minority class instances, ensuring fair model training and reducing the risk of biased predictions [26], [27]. The perfor- mance of the models is evaluated using a variety of metrics, including accuracy, which measures the proportion of correct predictions, precision, the ratio of true positive predictions to all positive predictions, recall, the ratio of true positives to actual positives, and F1 score, which is the harmonic mean of precision and recall [28]. Grid Search is used to optimize hyperparameters for the models [29], and K-Fold Cross- Validation is applied to ensure the models generalize well to unseen data, thus providing robust and reliable performance assessments [30]. In summary, this research integrates a comprehensive set of NLP, ML, and DL techniques to analyze Moroccan news text data. By leveraging methods such as LDA, sentiment analysis, and word embeddings, this study provides a detailed and structured approach to uncovering themes and sentiments within the data, offering valuable insights into societal trends as reflected in Moroccan news media. III. RELATED WORKS The field of Arabic text classification and sentiment analy- sis, particularly in Modern Standard Arabic, has witnessed significant advancements in recent years. Researchers have explored a variety of approaches, from classical ML tech- niques to advanced deep learning models, to address the unique challenges posed by the Arabic language. In this section, we focus on highlighting the works that are related to our main contributions: constructing a new benchmark dataset using web scraping, news data categorization, and sentiment analysis on news headlines. A. NEWS TEXT CLASSIFICATION Text classification in Arabic NLP has seen considerable advancement. The work [31] proposed an SVM achieving 96% accuracy and F1-score on a sample of 216 991 arti- cles across 5 categories, demonstrating the scale at which SVM can effectively categorize Arabic text. Similarly, [32] used Logistic Regression to achieve a 96% F1 score on datasets containing 111,728 documents across five cat- egories, emphasizing the consistency and reliability of Logistic Regression for large datasets. Additionally, [33] reported a 95.81% accuracy using the HANGRU model on over 200,000 articles, indicating the strength of DL in text tagging tasks. The study [34] demonstrated the effectiveness of an SMO classifier that achieved 88.60% accuracy on a dataset of 500 tweets categorized into five classes, illustrating the potential of ensemble methods in handling the nuances of Arabic tweets. Similarly, [35] reported 97% accuracy using an SVM classifier on a substantial dataset of 90,000 Arabic news articles, distributed across four categories. This high- lights the robustness of SVM in large-scale text classification tasks. The topic classification of online news was tackled by [36], which used optimized ML models to address the challenges posed by the vast amount of news data available online. The research demonstrated the effectiveness of advanced machine learning techniques in improving accuracy and efficiency in topic classification, achieving a 91.30% accuracy with a hyperparameter-optimized SVM on a dataset of 40,063 arti- cles across four categories, showcasing the impact of model optimization on classification accuracy. The study on [37] implemented a hybrid model of SVM and logistic regression, achieving 89.79% accuracy, although the dataset specifics were not disclosed. This reflects the efficacy of hybrid models in real-time headline classification. While the research work [38] used a probabilistic classifier that reached 86.66% accuracy on DS1 and 79.37% on DS2, with datasets exceeding 10,000 articles in 6 and 10 categories, VOLUME 13, 2025 26347 A. Jannani et al.: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines respectively, underscoring the utility of probabilistic methods in diverse headline datasets. Recent works include the fine-tuning of AraBERT for contextual semantic embeddings in news classifica- tion [39], the use of hybrid models combining BERT with transformer-based LSTM networks for fake news classifica- tion [40], and the impact of word embeddings and stemming on DL performance [41]. The AraBERT model in [39] achieved a high 99% accuracy, though the dataset details were unspecified, showcasing the advanced capabilities of contextual embeddings in Arabic text categorization. And the work [42] reached up to 99.90% accuracy across 15 freely available datasets, highlighting the high effectiveness of spe- cialized DL models in Arabic text categorization. The development of specialized datasets, such as the ANT Corpus introduced by [43], is also crucial in supporting Arabic NLP research. This work used SVM with TF-IDF and stop-word removal to classify 10,000 articles into six categories, achieving a 79.94% accuracy rate. This approach emphasizes the importance of preprocessing in enhancing classifier performance. Further comparative studies like [44], which improved classification by integrating stemming and a customized taxonomy, highlight the iterative progress in the field. The increasing application of transformer mod- els for text categorization is demonstrated by [45], where transformers were used to categorize texts in both MSA and dialectical Arabic (DA). This work is part of the growing trend of applying DL models to tackle linguistic diversity in Arabic. The continued development of advanced models for Arabic news classification is essential as the field evolves. Researchers are exploring not only classical machine learning techniques but also hybrid and DL models to improve the accuracy and scalability of text classification tasks. Despite the substantial progress made so far, challenges such as dialectal variations, the complexity of Arabic grammar, and the need for large, annotated datasets remain significant hur- dles. However, the integration of sophisticated models like AraBERT, transformers, and multimodal approaches is grad- ually pushing the boundaries of Arabic NLP. B. SENTIMENT ANALYSIS OF NEWS DATA Sentiment analysis has been increasingly employed for vari- ous financial and social purposes. For example, the study [46] employs Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and GRU to predict Bitcoin prices by integrating financial data, tech- nical indicators, and sentiment analysis of news headlines. Similarly, [47] leveraged sentiment analysis of daily news headlines to predict movements in the U.S. stock market, focusing on extracting features from global news to forecast the Dow Jones Industrial Average, emphasizing the influence of news sentiment on financial markets. Further VADER, a rule-based sentiment analysis tool, was employed in [48] to analyze news headlines for predicting stock market move- ments, further showcasing how sentiment expressed in finan- cial news can be a strong indicator of stock price fluctuations. In the social domain, the study [49] analyzed the sentiment surrounding the relocation of Indonesia’s capital using CNN and SVM. The hybrid model effectively captured public sen- timent, providing insights into the socio-political impact of the relocation. A two-level deep learning approach was developed for emotion recognition in Arabic news headlines [50], utilizing a CNN model that achieved impressive performance metrics: 89.3% accuracy, 92.5% recall, and 92.6% precision. The model was trained on a dataset of 1,698 news articles, anno- tated with seven emotional categories: anger, disgust, fear, happiness, neutral, sadness, and surprise. Another research [51], employed a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) network, achieving an accu- racy of 84.92% on sentiment analysis over 60,000 headlines. This shows the Bi-LSTM’s effectiveness in handling large datasets for sentiment analysis alongside topic modeling with LDA. Focusing on Arabic content, [52] examined the polarity of Arabic news on social media, employing machine learning algorithms to classify sentiment in MSA. Their find- ings demonstrated achieved the highest F1-measure accuracy at 83.5% with an SVM model. The study by [53] explored subjectivity and sentiment analysis in Arabic news and microblogs, emphasizing the complexity of processing MSA and the importance of distinguishing between subjective and objective content. This research laid the foundation for further sentiment analysis studies in the Arab world. Additionally, researchers in [54] developed a tailored approach for sen- timent analysis in Arabic articles, focusing on extracting opinions from direct sentiments in news content. Sentiment analysis of Arabic content remains challeng- ing due to the linguistic diversity in the Arab world. Studies like [55] and [56] examined sentiment polarity in user-generated content on Arabic Algerian newspaper web- sites and comments on MSA-based articles, respectively. Both studies employed machine learning algorithms such as SVM, highlighting the complexities introduced by DA and the importance of context in sentiment classifica- tion, [55] achieved over 84% F1-scores while [56] reached 89% accuracy. In similar contexts, the research work [57] analyzed the perception of the COVID-19 pandemic among Arab Twitter users using sentiment analysis techniques. By employing cognitive and psycholinguistic frameworks, the study offered insights into public health communication and crisis man- agement in the Arab world. Similarly, [58] applied natural NLP techniques to classify Indian news headlines related to COVID-19, demonstrating the importance of accurate sen- timent classification in large-scale news data during public health crises. Another important area is the translation of news headlines. The paper [59] explores sentiment preser- vation and shifts in bilingual corpora, particularly during the translation of weather news headlines, providing insights into the challenges of maintaining emotional tone across languages. Social media such as twitter data is used for sentiment analysis as in the article [60], which integrates 26348 VOLUME 13, 2025 A. Jannani et al.: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines BERT with CNN, RNN, and BiLSTM, achieving state-of- the-art sentiment analysis with 93% accuracy and an F1-score of 95%. It outperforms traditional methods like Word2Vec by dynamically capturing word context. Extensive reviews were conducted; for instance, [61] sur- veys sentiment analysis methodologies in Arabic over the past decade, focusing on the unique challenges posed by Arabic’s morphological complexity and dialect variation. The review highlights the need for more linguistically aware mod- els. Further exploration of dialectal Arabic sentiment analysis was elaborated in [62], where the authors provide an in-depth examination of dialect-specific challenges and methodolo- gies, emphasizing the need for tailored approaches. While the review [63] demonstrates that RNN-based models out- perform traditional approaches due to their ability to capture sequential information in text, making them more effective for complex sentiment analysis tasks. IV. DUAL DEEP LEARNING CLASSIFICATION SYSTEM FOR ARABIC NEWS Our methodology employs two primary NLP techniques: topic modeling for text classification and sentiment analysis The first approach analyzes the Moroccan News Arabic Dataset to identify key societal themes and categorize them into distinct dimensions of QoL. The second approach applies sentiment analysis techniques to data scraped from Hiba- Press, a popular Moroccan news source, to discern the emotional tones conveyed by the news headlines. Both anal- yses are grounded in advanced computational techniques, including TF-IDF vectorization and deep learning models with word embeddings from AraBERT and Asafaya, specifi- cally tailored for the Arabic language. While methods like Bag-of-Words and Latent Semantic Analysis could be used, they fail to capture word order and nuanced semantic relationships compared to TF-IDF and word embeddings, particularly from AraBERT/Asafaya, which offer better contextual understanding, making them more suitable for Moroccan Arabic. As illustrated in Figures 1 and 2, the text classification pipeline begins with the collection and pre-processing of news data, followed by topic modeling using LDA to iden- tify key themes. For sentiment analysis, data is scraped from HibaPress, and user interactions, such as likes and dis- likes, are converted into sentiment labels. Machine learning FIGURE 1. Methodological framework for our two experiments for news headline categorization and sentiment analysis. VOLUME 13, 2025 26349 A. Jannani et al.: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines algorithms and neural network architectures are used for classification and prediction. Alternatives like Non-negative Matrix Factorization and Latent Semantic Indexing could also be used for topic mod- eling, but LDA’s generative model better captures complex patterns in unstructured data. For sentiment analysis, deep learning models with word embeddings outperform lexicon- based methods, as they better capture the rich context of Moroccan news. We enhanced our models’ quality through fine-tuning, using GridSearchCV for all machine learning algorithms with 5-fold cross-validation to explore and identify optimal param- eter values. Detailed parameter settings and final selections will be presented in the following sections. Similarly, in con- structing neural architectures, we systematically evaluated multiple configurations, focusing primarily on the impact of layer sizes. For each of the four primary architectures under consideration, layer sizes of 16, 32, 64, and 128 units were individually tested in various combinations. This approach allowed us to gauge the influence of each layer size on model performance and to identify the most effective configuration within each architecture. Ultimately, one optimal configura- tion was selected from each of the four architectures based on performance metrics, achieving a balance between model complexity and predictive accuracy. For training data, we employed a Stratified Shuffle Split with 3 splits to create an 80-20 train-test division. This technique preserved the class distribution across each split, ensuring that both training and testing sets represented the overall dataset’s balance. Maintaining this balance is crucial for evaluating model performance, as it reduces the likeli- hood of overfitting and promotes generalization to unseen data. To further enhance reproducibility, a random_state of 42 was specified, allowing for consistent results across runs. This approach of stratified sampling, combined with reproducibility controls, was instrumental in ensuring that model evaluations were both reliable and robust. This com- prehensive approach enables us to enhance the performance of our models to accurately measure societal well-being and sentiments within news media. A. TOPIC MODELING: CASE OF MOROCCAN NEWS ARABIC DATASET The methodology for this study will begin with data col- lection from the Moroccan News Arabic Dataset [31]. The dataset will be categorized using Latent Dirichlet Allocation based on the body of news articles, followed by split- ting the data into 80% for training and 20% for testing. For feature extraction, two types of word embeddings will be used: TF-IDF and pre-trained models, AraBERT and ASAFAYA. Machine learning models such as SVM, Random Forests, XGBoost, and Logistic Regression will be developed alongside neural network architectures, including LSTM, BiLSTM, GRU, and BiGRU. The models’ performances will be evaluated, and the findings will be analyzed to pro- vide insights on the quality-of-life indicators from the news headlines. Figure 2 highlights the preprocessing steps before models building. 1) DATA COLLECTION AND PREPROCESSING The dataset used in this study is MNAD, consisting of over 418k news headlines sourced from various Moroccan news platforms. The data spans a wide range of topics, includ- ing politics, business, society, health, and education. Initial preprocessing steps involved data cleaning to remove noise such as special characters, redundant whitespace, and HTML tags. The text was then normalized, converting all content to lowercase, and stop words specific to Arabic were removed using the AraBERT tokenizer. Finally, stemming was applied FIGURE 2. Preprocessing steps for preparing data for text classification. 26350 VOLUME 13, 2025 A. Jannani et al.: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines to reduce words to their root forms, ensuring the dataset was prepared for modeling. 2) DATA SAMPLING AND CATEGORIZATION USING LDA The Latent Dirichlet Allocation model was employed to uncover latent topics from the dataset, effectively identifying thematic structures within the news articles. LDA was chosen for its ability to assign words to multiple topics based on prob- abilistic distributions, which is ideal for handling overlapping themes in the data. In this study, LDA was crucial for identifying thematic structures within the news articles, enabling the selection of appropriately annotated data. This step ensured a more targeted and reliable dataset for subsequent analysis. Figure 3 illustrates the mapping of classes, the process begins with the MNAD classes on the left, which include categories like religion, business, health, and education. LDA was then applied, generating intermediate LDA topics that represent thematic groupings of the content. The number of topics was set to eight, aligned with predefined quality-of-life dimensions such as health, education, and economic stability. FIGURE 3. Mapping of MNAD classes to selected topics through LDA classification. Post-LDA, these topics were manually mapped to a stream- lined set of selected classes on the right, such as society, politics, business, health and environment, and education. This mapping and refinement process involved merging overlapping topics and discarding less coherent ones, result- ing in a more focused set of final categories for further analysis. Ultimately, the refined dataset contains a nearly bal- anced distribution of 29,144 entries across various categories, including 4,000 for events, policy, business, weather, sport, environment, and art and culture, while education comprises 1,144 entries. Fig. 3. illustrates this flow from the MNAD classes on the left, through the LDA topics, to the final selected classes on the right. 3) WORD EMBEDDING To transform the text into a machine-readable format, TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) and pre-trained word embeddings, AraBERT and ASAFAYA, were used. TF-IDF highlights important terms by considering their frequency, while the others provide deep contextual embeddings tailored to Arabic, capturing the relationships between words. This combination ensures a rich represen- tation of the headlines for sentiment analysis and topic classification. 4) DATA BALANCING To address the class imbalance in the dataset, oversampling techniques were applied. Two different methods were used, depending on the type of word embedding: • Random Oversampler for TF-IDF: This technique was used to replicate instances of the minority class in the TF-IDF embeddings, helping to balance the class distribution. • SMOTE for BERT-based Embeddings: SMOTE was applied to the AraBERT and ASAFAYA embeddings to synthetically generate new examples for the minority class. This method generates realistic data points by interpolating between existing examples. 5) TEXT CLASSIFICATION MODEL BUILDING Several models were built to classify the sentiment of the headlines. Traditional machine learning models like SVM, Random Forests, XGBoost, and Logistic Regression were employed, along with neural networks: LSTM, BiLSTM, GRU, and BiGRU. These models were fine-tuned and eval- uated based on a set of performance metrics. In this part, we present the results of our grid search for the best selected architectures after testing multiple layer sizes. The first architecture is based on an LSTM layer. It starts with an input layer that takes variable-length sequences with a specific feature size. This input is processed by a single LSTM layer with 42 units. The LSTM output is followed by a series of dense layers: first with 16 units and ReLU activation, then with 32 units and ReLU, followed by a dropout layer with a 0.2 rate. Another dense layer with 64 units and ReLU activation comes next, followed by a second dropout layer for regularization. The model concludes with a dense layer containing 8 units and a softmax activation function for multi- class classification. The model is compiled using the Adam optimizer, sparse categorical cross-entropy as the loss func- tion, and accuracy as the evaluation metric. Model training is supported by model checkpointing and early stopping based on validation loss to save the best model and avoid overfitting. The second architecture uses bidirectional LSTM. It starts with an input layer that handles variable-length sequences with a specific feature size. The input is passed through a bidirectional LSTM layer with 64 units, which processes the sequence in both forward and backward directions. This is followed by a sequence of dense layers: the first has 16 units with ReLU activation, then 32 units with ReLU, followed by a dropout layer with a 0.2 rate. A dense layer with 64 units and ReLU activation is followed by another dropout layer for regularization. Finally, the model ends with a dense layer of 8 units, which corresponds to the number of output classes, VOLUME 13, 2025 26351 A. Jannani et al.: Topic Modeling and Sentiment Analysis of Arabic News Headlines and applies a softmax activation for multi-class classification. The model is compiled with the Adam optimizer, sparse categorical cross-entropy as the loss function, and accuracy as the evaluation metric. It also uses model checkpointing to save the best model based on validation loss and early stopping to prevent overfitting during training. The third architecture is a GRU. It starts with an input layer that handles variable-length sequences depending on the employed word embedding. Next, two GRU layers with 32 units each are applied: one processes the sequence for- ward, while the other does so in reverse. Their outputs are merged via concatenation and passed through a Global Aver- age Pooling 1D layer. A dropout layer with a 0.25 rate is added for regularization. Afterward, a dense layer with 32 units is followed by another dropout layer with the same rate. The structure then includes a dense layer with 16 units, another with 8 units, and concludes with a softmax activation for multi-class classification. The fourth architecture is a bidirectional GRU model. It begins with an input layer that takes variable-length sequences with a specified feature size. The input is fed into a bidirectional GRU layer with 64 units, which processes the sequence in both forward and reverse directions. The GRU output is followed by a sequence of dense layers: the first has 16 units with ReLU activation, then 32 units with ReLU, and a dropout layer with a 0.2 rate for regularization. Another dense layer with 64 units and ReLU activation is followed by another dropout layer. Finally, the model ends with a dense layer containing 8 units, which corresponds to the number of output classes, and applies a softmax activation for multi- class classification. The model is compiled with the Adam optimizer, sparse categorical cross-entropy as the loss func- tion, and accuracy as a metric. This is followed by a sequence of dense layers: the first has 16 units with ReLU activation, then 32 units with ReLU, followed by a dropout layer with a 0.2 rate. A dense layer with 64 units and ReLU activation is followed by another dropout layer for regularization. Finally, the model ends with a dense layer of 8 units, which corresponds to the number of output classes, and applies a softmax activation for multi-class classification. The model is compiled with the Adam optimizer, sparse categorical cross-entropy as the loss function, and accuracy as the evaluation metric. It also uses model checkpointing to save the best model based on validation loss and early stopping to prevent overfitting during training. The third architecture is a GRU. It starts with an input layer that handles variable-length sequences depending on the employed word embedding. Next, two GRU layers with 32 units each are applied: one processes the sequence for- ward, while the other does so in reverse. Their outputs are merged via concatenation and passed through a Global Aver- age Pooling 1D layer. A dropout layer with a 0.25 rate is added for regularization. Afterward, a dense layer with 32 units is followed by another dropout layer with the same rate. The structure then includes a dense layer with 16 units, another with 8 units, and concludes with a softmax activation for multi-class classification. The fourth architecture is a bidirectional GRU model. It begins with an input layer that takes variable-length sequences with a specified feature size. The input is fed into a bidirectional GRU layer with 64 units, which processes the sequence in both forward and reverse directions. The GRU output is followed by a sequence of dense layers: the first has 16 units with ReLU activation, then 32 units with ReLU, and a dropout layer with a 0.2 rate for regularization. Another dense layer with 64 units and ReLU activation is followed by another dropout layer. Finally, the model ends with a dense layer containing 8 units, which corresponds to the number of output classes, and applies a softmax activation for multi-class classification. The model is compiled with the Adam optimizer, sparse categorical cross-entropy as the loss function, and accuracy as a metric. It also utilizes model check pointing to save the best model based on validation loss and early stopping to prevent overfitting during training. For machine learning models, GridSearchCV was used for hyperparameter optimization with 5-fold cross-validation. The classifiers were tested over the following hyperparam- eters and the best configurations are presented in Table 1. B. Specifically, ASAFAYA and AraBERT embeddings were combined with models such as GRU, BiGRU, LSTM, and BiLSTM to capture contextual nuances in the data. It ends with a sigmoid-activated output for binary classification. Compiled with the Adam optimizer at a learn- ing rate of 0.0001 and binary cross-entropy loss, it includes early stopping and model checkpointing based on validation accuracy. Secondly, the BiLSTM model begins with an input layer, followed by a dense layer with 128 ReLU-activated units and a bidirectional LSTM layer with 64 units to capture bidirectional sequence information. The GRU model begins with an input layer, followed by two GRU layers with 32 units each, processing the input in forward and backward directions. Their outputs are concatenated and passed through a global average pooling layer, then a dropout layer with a rate of 0.25. A dense layer with 32 units, followed by another dropout, and then a 16-unit dense layer further refines the representation before a final sigmoid-activated output for binary classification. The model, compiled with the Adam optimizer and binary cross-entropy loss, includes early stopping and model check- pointing based on validation loss. Lastly, the BiGRU model architecture begins with an input layer, followed by bidirectional GRU layers with 128 units each to process the input in both forward and backward directions. Their merged output undergoes global average pooling and a dropout layer with a rate of 0.3 for enhanced regularization. This is followed by a series of dense layers with 164, 64, and 32 units, each utilizing ReLU activation. Batch normalization is applied after the first dense layer for improved convergence, and additional dropout layers with rates of 0.2 and 0.3 provide further regularization. A sigmoid- activated output layer completes the binary classification setup. Compiled with the Adam optimizer at a learning rate of 0.001 and binary cross-entropy loss, the model leverages early stopping and model checkpointing based on validation loss. PERFORMANCE RESULTS AND BENCHMARK ANALYSIS 1) TEXT CLASSIFICATION RESULTS The experimental results offer a detailed comparison of per- formance across various deep learning and machine learning algorithms, evaluated on key metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score as shown in Table 3. For the text classification component in the future con- ceptual system building, we selected the GRU model with ASAFAYA embeddings, which performed best across all metrics, with an accuracy of 0.9474 and consistently high precision, recall, and F1 scores, all above 0.94. Secondly come LSTM and BiLSTM models with ASAFAYA embed- dings, which closely followed in performance, both achieving accuracy scores above 0.9440. Although these models exhib- ited slightly lower recall and F1 scores compared to the GRU model, they still demonstrated strong capabilities in sentiment detection. Among the traditional machine learning models, SVM and Logistic Regression performed well, with accuracy scores of 0.9295 and 0.9201, respectively. However, these models underperformed compared to the DL models, particularly in recall and F1 score. These results emphasize the advan- tages of using DL models, particularly those leveraging domain-specific embeddings like ASAFAYA, in complex tasks such as sentiment analysis and topic classification in the Arabic language. The topic modeling experiment produced well-defined thematic categories that closely align with key aspects of societal well-being. The enhanced LDA model, bolstered by TF-IDF vectorization and Bert-based word embeddings, effectively identified and categorized these themes, providing a granular view into the public discourse as captured through the MNAD dataset. The combination of traditional NLP techniques with advanced machine learning and DL methods demonstrated substantial efficacy in extracting meaningful insights from large-scale text data within a language-specific context. These findings not only validate the methodological choices but also establish a robust foundation for further research into automated content analysis for monitoring societal trends. The experimental results offer a detailed comparison of model performance across various DL and ML algorithms, evaluated on key metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. In comparison to prior research, such as the work [33], which achieved a 96% accuracy using SVM on a large corpus of 216 991 articles across 5 categories, our model’s per- formance is notable given the differences in dataset scope. Our GRU model with ASAFAYA embeddings achieved an accuracy of 94.74%, a strong result considering that our dataset consisted of news headlines, which typically provide less context than full articles. Additionally, other studies, such as [35], which reported 97% accuracy using SVM on 90,000 Arabic news articles in four categories, and [34], which achieved 95.81% accuracy with the HANGRU model on over 200,000 articles, further highlight how larger datasets and full-text articles provide more context for better classification performance. The higher performance in these works can be attributed to the use of a larger corpus and more categories, making full articles easier to classify than condensed headlines. In con- trast, our study focuses on headlines, which often pose greater challenges for accurate sentiment and topic classification due to their brevity. Our deep learning models, particularly the GRU with ASAFAYA embeddings, still outperformed traditional machine learning models such as SVM and logistic regres- sion. For instance, our SVM model achieved an accuracy of 92.95%, which, while lower than the aforementioned works, still demonstrates competitive performance given the challenges of headline classification.
