XIAO et al.: TOWARDS ENERGY-PRESERVING NATURAL LANGUAGE UNDERSTANDING WITH SPIKING NEURAL NETWORKS 445 TABLE VII COMPARISON BETWEEN BI-SNN AND BINARIZED BI-GRU ON EN-VI TASK Fig. 4. Performance of variant models with respect to the ratio of energy con- sumption on En-Vi task. We regard the energy consumption of TRANSFORMER baselineas1.Wecontroltheenergyconsumptionofeachmodelbymodifyingthe dimensionality of hidden size. Moreover, knowledge distillation is leveraged for thecompressionofbaselinemodels.Weaccumulativelyhalvethedimensionality of model till untrainable (from 512 to 32). Obviously, our model has the better capability to reduce the energy costs than its model compression counterparts. B. Comparison to Related Studies 1) SNN Vs. Binarized Gated GRU: A similar approach to our method is binarized gated GRU [13] which binarizes continual values in gates of GRU for speeding up the model inference. We give the performance comparison between the bi-SNN model and the binarized bi-GRU model over the En-Vi task. For the binarized bi-GRU model, we use two conversion methods: a) In the inference phase, we directly map the original values into 0 or 1 according to the speciﬁc threshold (set to 0); and b) We train the binarized bi-GRU model following [13]. Experimental results demonstrate that our model achieves slightly better performance than its binarized bi-GRU counterpart. Most importantly, our approach is able to reduce energy consumption, while binarized GRU is not since it still preserves product operations of the conventional GRU model. 2) SNN Vs. Knowledge Distillation: In order to investigate the relation between consumed energy and model performance, we further conduct a group of experiments on the dimensional- ity of the encoder. Here, we introduce the knowledge distilla- tion [8] scheme to keep the performance of baseline systems following Tang et al. [14]. In Fig. 4, we simulate the energy consumption of each model with modiﬁed dimensionality d, and conduct the relationship between corresponding energy cost and performance. As seen, by accumulatively halving the dimensionality of model d from 512, TRANSFORMER and bi-GRU model signiﬁcantly lose the performance, and ﬁnally diverge when d is 32. In comparison, the proposed spiking encoder can save almost 15 times more energy than that of base- lines with knowledge distillation, verifying the superiority of our approach. VIII. CONCLUSION In this paper, we introduce a spiking encoder for language understanding. We design our proposed spiking encoder with multiple SNN layers, and enhance each layer with better adapta- tion and back-propagation patterns. After conducting the exper- imental results on several NLP tasks, Compared to conventional baselines, our spiking encoder not only reveals comparable performances, but also signiﬁcantly reduces the required energy cost. Our contributions are mainly in the following: r We introduce a potential direction to reduce the energy cost fromtheperspectiveofcomputationaloperationratherthan the model compression; r We present a spiking encoder for NLU tasks that exploits additive operations to replace massive multiplications in conventional neural-based models; r Our experiments on sentiment analysis and machine trans- lation reveal that our model can reduce the energy cost to 0.82% against the advanced TRANSFORMER model. Compared with existing advanced NLP research, the follow- ing directions related to our proposed spiking encoder raise our interest the most. First, as the big success of recent NLP approaches, the pre-trained language models (e.g., BERT [2] and XLM-R [39]) have shown dominant performance. We believe that, for the large-scale spiking encoder, its performance and training method can be explored in the future. Besides, our spiking encoder shows its performance drop on the large-scale dataset setting. Considering this, a better trade-off between per- formance and energy cost is also worth being investigated. Last, SNN shows its own priority on speciﬁc devices. The energy cost of the spiking encoder can also be further examined following this setting. We wish this research can attract more studies to pay attention to the topic of “green NLP,” and explore advanced techniques on low-energy NLP models in the future. ACKNOWLEDGMENT The authors would like to thank the reviewer and action editor for reviewing our paper. Their suggestions are truly helpful for us to reﬁne our manuscript.

text: IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON AUDIO, SPEECH AND LANGUAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 33, 2025 1243 Vec-Tok Speech: Speech Vectorization and Tokenization for Neural Speech Generation Xinfa Zhu , Yuanjun Lv, Yi Lei , Tao Li , Wendi He, Hongbin Zhou , Heng Lu , and Lei Xie , Senior Member, IEEE Abstract—Language models (LMs) have recently ﬂourished in natural language processing and computer vision, generating high- qualitytextsandimagesinvarioustasks.WhilecurrentspeechLMs have made signiﬁcant progress, there are still challenges to over- come in terms of achieving optimal speech quality and broad task generalization. This paper presents Vec-Tok Speech, an extensible framework that resembles multiple speech generation tasks, gener- ating expressive and high-ﬁdelity speech. Speciﬁcally, we propose a novel speech codec based on speech vectors and semantic tokens. Speech vectors contain acoustic details contributing to high-ﬁdelity speech reconstruction, while semantic tokens focus on the linguistic content of speech, facilitating language modeling. Based on the proposed speech codec, Vec-Tok Speech leverages an LM to under- take the core of speech generation. Moreover, Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) is introduced to reduce the token length and bit rate for lowerexposurebiasandlongercontext,improvingtheperformance of LMs. Vec-Tok Speech can be used for intra- and cross-lingual zero-shot voice conversion (VC), zero-shot speaking style transfer text-to-speech (TTS), speech-to-speech translation (S2ST), speech denoising, and speaker de-identiﬁcation and anonymization. Ex- periments show that Vec-Tok Speech, built on 50,000 hours of speech, performs better than other SOTA models. Index Terms—Speech codec, speech language model, speech-to- speech translation, text-to-speech, voice conversion. I. INTRODUCTION I N Recent years, Language Models (LMs) [1] have demon- strated remarkable progress, especially for their excellent zero-shot performance on natural language processing (NLP) tasks. The impressive advances have inspired substantial re- search extending LMs to various speech generation tasks, such as text-to-speech (TTS), voice conversion (VC), and speech- to-speech translation (S2ST). With the help of the incredible sequential generation ability of neural language models and unprecedented scale and coverage of audio data, speech genera- tion has been signiﬁcantly advanced with remarkable zero-shot abilities. Received 13 June 2024; revised 12 November 2024; accepted 20 February 2025. Date of publication 27 February 2025; date of current version 20 March 2025. The associate editor coordinating the review of this article and approving it for publication was Prof. Zhizheng Wu. (Xinfa Zhu and Yuanjun Lv contributed equally to this work.) (Corresponding author: Lei Xie.) Xinfa Zhu, Yuanjun Lv, Yi Lei, Tao Li, and Lei Xie are with the Audio, Speech and Language Processing Group (ASLP@NPU), School of Com- puter Science, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710072, China (e-mail: xfzhu@mail.nwpu.edu.cn; yjlv@mail.nwpu.edu.cn; leiyi@npu-aslp. org; taoli@npu-aslp.org; lxie@nwpu.edu.cn). Wendi He, Hongbin Zhou, and Heng Lu are with the Ximalaya Inc., Shanghai 201203, China (e-mail: cloris.he@ximalaya.com; hong- bin.zhou@ximalaya.com; Bearlu007@gmail.com). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TASLPRO.2025.3546559 Current generative speech models usually leverage speech- hidden representations to bridge an audio codec and an acoustic model (either an LM [2], [3] or a diffusion model [4]). Speciﬁ- cally, the speech hidden representations can be divided into two categories: discrete tokens [2], [3], and continuous vectors [4]. The discrete tokens are obtained by incorporating vector quan- tization into the codec to capture essential information at low bit rates and reconstruct the waveform. However, compressing rich speech information into low-bit-rate representations may degrade the reconstructed speech quality. Therefore, some stud- ies [5] propose to utilize Residual Vector Quantizers (RVQ) to learn both linguistic and acoustic features of speech, which contain speech aspects beyond linguistics and may surpass the modeling capacity of conventional LMs. On the other hand, compared to the discrete tokens, the continuous vectors aim to retain all information embedded in speech for high-ﬁdelity speech reconstruction. This puts much pressure on the acoustic model and prevents it from fully utilizing the advantages of LMs. Apart from speech naturalness and quality, expressiveness (such as diverse prosody patterns, speaking speed and style, emotions, etc.) is also critical to human verbal communication. Human speech is a complex signal containing linguistic, non- linguistic, and para-linguistic information [6]. The linguistic information is the textual content of speech, and para-/non- linguistic characteristics often refer to the speaking style, em- phasis, phonation, speaker identity, etc [7]. Previous speech LMs [2], [8] have demonstrated state-of-the-art performance in zero-shot voice cloning by integrating all linguistic-irrelevant acoustic details, including non- and para-linguistic information, from the same speech prompt. However, this paradigm has a limitation for the composition of para-linguistic (e.g., speaking style) and non-linguistic (e.g., speaker timbre) from different speech prompts. This scenario is more ﬂexible and practical in real-world speech applications. Speciﬁcally, separately adopting speaker prompt and style prompt can enable powerful zero-shot style transfer ability, i.e., a speaker can talk in any designated style given only a few seconds of style reference. With the considerations mentioned above, we propose Vec- Tok Speech, a speech generation framework with a novel codec for both continuous Vector and discrete Token extrac- tion, aiming to beneﬁt from both granularities of speech repre- sentation. The proposed approach can be applied to multiple downstream tasks, such as text-to-speech, voice conversion, speech-to-speech translation, speech denoising, speaker de- identiﬁcation, and anonymization. The novel codec, named Vec-Tok Codec, consists of an encoder and a decoder, with speech vectors and semantic tokens as intermediate representations. Speciﬁcally, speech vectors aim to reconstruct high-ﬁdelity speech, thus containing as many ﬁne speech details as possible. Semantic tokens ﬁlter out the non-linguistic aspects, i.e., speaker identity, from speech vec- tors while preserving linguistic and para-linguistic information. Based on the Vec-Tok codec, we design autoregressive LMs to produce semantic tokens in downstream tasks, which model the rich variation of linguistic and para-linguistic information and control expressiveness. Moreover, we use Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) [9] to further reduce the token length and the bit rate, thus beneﬁting the LM from lower exposure bias and longer context coverage. The main contributions of this work are summarized as follows: r Vec-Tok Codec: A speech codec with high ﬁdelity and low bit rate. We design a novel speech codec with speech vectorization and tokenization for LM-based speech gener- ation with both high ﬁdelity and low bit rate. Speciﬁcally, speech vectorization aims to extract a continuous vector containing as many ﬁne speech details as possible to re- construct the high-ﬁdelity audio; speech tokenization is to learn the discrete semantic token, preserving linguistic content and style expressions at low bit rates. r Vec-Tok Speech: A speech generation framework easily applied to multiple speech generation tasks. Based on the novel codec, we introduce Vec-Tok Speech, an extensive speech generation framework that leverages LMs to un- dertake the core of various downstream tasks, such as TTS and S2ST. Moreover, BPE is introduced to reduce the token length, further beneﬁting the LM from longer context coverage. r Expressive speech generation. We demonstrate the ability of the proposed Vec-Tok Speech to conduct any-speaker, any-style TTS in the zero-shot scenario. A style prompt and a speaker prompt are utilized respectively to provide the style expression for the LM and the speaker identity for the codec decoder, signiﬁcantly improving the ﬂexibility in expressive speech generation. We scale the proposed model to 600 million parameters and train on 50,000 hours of speech data. Comprehensive exper- iments show that Vec-Tok Speech can generate speech with diverse speaker identities, prosody, and styles in various tasks (VC, TTS, S2ST, etc.) in a zero-shot setup with only a few seconds of speech prompts. RELATED WORK Neuralaudiocodecsandlarge-scalegenerativespeechmodels are popular topics in the literature of speech generation. In this section, we review the prior art in these two topics. A. Neural Audio Codec Neural audio codec [10], [11], [12] refers to a kind of neu- ral network model that converts audio waveform into discrete tokens with an encoder and reconstructs audio waveform from these tokens with a decoder. Neural audio codec usually com- presses all speech attributes into discrete tokens, such as phase, timbre, prosody, and content. For example, SoundStream [5] and Encodec [13] leverage vector-quantized variational auto- encoders (VQ-VAE) with Residual Vector-Quantization (RVQ) technique to compress speech into multiple layers of tokens and have been widely used as intermediate representation in speech and audio generation tasks. HiFi-Codec [11] introduces group- residual vector quantization (GRVQ), which aims at reducing the number of codebooks required while preserving the quality of reconstruction. While RVQ-based codecs achieve higher reconstruction qual- ity and lower bitrate than traditional codecs, they are funda- mentally tailored for compression and transmission purposes, which makes them sub-optimal for intermediate representations for ﬂexible speech generation. Several concerns substantiate this standpoint. First, these codec tokens usually contain as manyspeechattributesaspossibletohigh-ﬁdelityreconstruction quality, which leads to information redundancy and increases the difﬁculty of predicting the tokens in downstream tasks. Moreover, the sequence of discrete tokens generated by residual quantizers is often very long, presenting challenges for genera- tive speech models to achieve accurate predictability. Unlike previous systems, Vec-Tok Codec leverages speech vectors to construct high-quality speech and extracts low-bitrate discrete tokens to ease downstream tasks. Moreover, it is de- signed to disentangle and control non-linguistic information, providing more ﬂexibility for various tasks. B. Large Scale Generative Speech Models A signiﬁcant shift is observed for generative speech models, where conventional models are mostly task-speciﬁc, trained on distinct datasets tailored to particular objectives. In contrast, large-scale generative speech models introduce a paradigm that surpasses these limitations, enabling them to tackle a diverse range of novel tasks without explicit training [14], [15]. With the advancement in neural speech codec reviewed in Section II-A, many recent studies have explored language and diffusion mod- els for speech generation. Encodec stands out for its capacity to extract acoustic tokens among these endeavors. Based on Encodec, VALL-E [2] and its multilingual variant VALL-E X [16] achieve zero-shot TTS capabilities by strategically ap- plying autoregressive and non-autoregressive LMs. Meanwhile, SPEAR-TTS [3] leverages w2v-BERT [17] and SoundStream to derive semantic and acoustic tokens. SPEAR-TTS integrates multi-stage AR LMs and achieves a multi-speaker TTS system Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. ZHU et al.: VEC-TOK SPEECH: SPEECH VECTORIZATION AND TOKENIZATION FOR NEURAL SPEECH GENERATION 1245 with minimal supervision. SoundStorm [8] extends SPEAR- TTS to more efﬁcient and stable speech generation through masked iterative parallel decoding. On the other hand, models like NaturalSpeech2 [4] introduce a text-conditioned diffusion model to generate the latent vectors of the neural audio codec model. TorToise-TTS [18] is a recent generative speech model that combines an autoregressive transformer and a denois- ing diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM). The autoregressive transformer predicts speech tokens from text, while the DDPM converts speech tokens back to mel spectrograms. Moreover, a contrastive Language-Voice pretrained (CLVP) module is used to re-score the output of the autoregressive transformer, improv- ing the quality of the synthetic speech. Beyond TTS, these large-scale generative speech models stretch their capabilities across various tasks. For instance, SpeechGen [19] harnesses a speech LM with diverse prompts to facilitate speech translation, inpainting, and continuation. Sim- ilarly, Voicebox [14] takes a non-autoregressive ﬂow-matching model as its cornerstone, enabling the model to address denois- ing, zero-shot TTS, and even text-only sampling. This progress of the large-scale generative speech models accommodating multiple downstream tasks represents the powerful potential of LM-based variants applying to diverse speech generation tasks. Following this line, we present Vec-Tok Speech, a neural speech generation framework leveraging Vec-Tok Codec and LMs. This framework facilitates various speech generation tasks, including zero-shot VC, zero-shot TTS, speech-to-speech translation, and beyond. III. METHODOLOGY This section ﬁrst gives an overview of Vec-Tok Speech, fol- lowed by the motivation and design of each module. Aplications of Vec-Tok Speech will also be introduced in this section. A. Overview Fig. 1 illustrates the overall structure of our proposed Vec-Tok Speech, which comprises three critical components bridged by speech vectors and semantic tokens: a codec encoder, a language model (LM), and a codec decoder. Speciﬁcally, the codec en- coder is used to extract speech vectors and semantic tokens from speech waveforms. Semantic tokens go through Byte-Pair En- coding/Decoding for lower bitrate and shorter sequence lengths. Semantic tokens after BPE are taken as the modeling target of the LM. The codec decoder reconstructs waveforms, taking semantic tokens and speech vectors as input. Given the speech waveforms wave, the process of the Vec-Tok Speech can be expressed as follows: {vec, tok} = Encoder(wave), (1) ˆ tok = LM(tok, text), (2) ˆ wave = Decoder  vec, ˆ tok  , (3) where vec and tok are speech vectors and semantic tokens, respectively, extracted by the codec encoder. ˆ tok is the semantic Fig. 1. The overall architecture of Vec-Tok Speech. Dashed lines represent optional. Fig. 2. The detailed architecture of Vec-Tok Codec, includes a speech vector- ization loop for high-ﬁdelity speech reconstruction and a speech tokenization loop for semantics extraction. tokens predicted by the LM; ˆ wave is the reconstructed wave- forms by the codec Decoder. B. Vec-Tok Codec Fig. 2 describes the structure of the Vec-Tok Codec, and the speech reconstruction process, where the speech vectors contribute to high-ﬁdelity speech reconstruction and the seman- tic tokens facilitate language modeling. Speciﬁcally, the codec encoder consists of a self-supervised learning (SSL) model and a K-means module, extracting speech vectors and semantic tokens, respectively. The codec decoder consists of an inverse K-means module and a vocoder, reconstructing speech from semantic tokens and speech vectors. 1) Speech Vectorization. Speech vectors are designed to con- tain as many speech details as possible to improve the model’s Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 1246 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON AUDIO, SPEECH AND LANGUAGE PROCESSING, VOL. 33, 2025 high-ﬁdelity speech reconstruction ability. Speciﬁcally, a self- supervised learning (SSL) model, WavLM [20], is adopted to extract speech vectors from speech, which is pre-trained on large-scale speech data and performs exceptionally in diverse speech-processing tasks. WavLM extracts ﬁne-grained speech vectorsvec = {hi}N i=1 fromtheinputwave,whereN isthenum- ber of frames. Speciﬁcally, we take the 6th layer of WavLM as the output layer as this layer is proved to contain much acoustic information enabling high-ﬁdelity speech reconstruction [21]. Then, a neural vocoder is used to reconstruct speech waveforms based on the extracted speech vectors vec since the vocoder can produce waveforms that are nearly indistinguishable from recorded waveforms and are highly generalizable outside of the training set according to TorToise-TTS [18]. The process of speech vector extraction and waveform reconstruction is formulated as follows: vec = SSL Encoder(wave), (4) ˆ wave = Vocoder(vec), (5) where vec denotes speech vectors extracted by the WavLM, and ˆ wave is the reconstructed waveforms by the vocoder. 2) Speech tokenization. Unlike speech vectors, semantic to- kens are designed to capture the linguistic content of speech at low bit rates, facilitating downstream language models. First, in our experimental analysis, the utterance level vector averaged in the time axis strongly correlates with speaker identity. There- fore, we use utterance level mean normalization on the speech vectorstodisentanglespeaker-relatedinformation.Secondly,we leverage the K-means algorithm to divide speech vectors into K clusters, generating distinct tokens tok = {zi}N i=1. Speciﬁcally, each token is clustered from speech vectors of multiple speakers, and the residual speaker-related information is further removed, thus named semantic tokens. Moreover, we keep the consecutive repeating tokens, which preserves para-linguistic such as rhythm in duration as much as possible. The process of token extraction can be formulated as: tok = K-means(Normalize(vec)). (6) Consider that the semantic tokens are extracted from the speech vectors by the K-means algorithm, which inspires us to design an “inverse” K-means model to convert semantic tokens back to speech vectors. Speciﬁcally, as the semantic tokens lack speaker- related information, we introduce prompt learning during the reconstruction of speech vectors, where a slice of speech vectors containing desired speaker information is used as a prompt to guide the inverse K-means process. To this end, we design a decoder-only model consisting of a look-up table, conformer blocks, and a projection layer, where the K-means table is taken as the look-up table, and the vector of the cluster center is used as the token embedding. The prompt speech vectors are concatenated ahead of the token embeddings as the input of conformer blocks. The self-attention in the conformer blocks captures missing acoustic information in semantic tokens, such astimbre,fromthepromptspeechvectorssincetheself-attention module has a global receptive ﬁeld enabling direct inter-token and token-to-vector interaction. Finally, the output of conformer blocks is sent to the projection layer, generating the ﬁnal speech vectors. During loss calculation, we mask the prompt part of the predicted speech vector. The above process and the loss function of the inverse K-means model can be deﬁned as ˆ vec = Inv_K(tok, vecp), (7) LInv_K = Lrecon + Lssim (8) where tok denotes semantic tokens extracted by the K-means algorithm, vecp denotes prompt speech vectors containing target speaker information, and ˆ vec denotes reconstructed speech vec- tors. Lrecon is the MSE loss between the predicted speech vector and the ground-truth speech vector. Besides, structural similarity Lssim [22]isadoptedtomeasurethesimilaritybetweenpredicted and ground-truth speech vectors. Furthermore, the mask predic- tion skill is utilized to improve the linguistic comprehension ability of the inverse K-means module. We randomly mask semantic tokens in α% or replace semantic tokens with another in β%. C. Language Models Leveraging Semantic Tokens Based on the Vec-Tok Codec, LMs are introduced to model the semantic information embedded in speech. Beneﬁting from the proposed codec that produces single-level tokens, we can model the tokens with only one LM. Speciﬁcally, we take two tasks, zero-shot TTS and S2ST, to showcase the effectiveness of our proposed Vec-Tok Codec. The SSL encoder extracts speech vectors and inherently leads to a long sequence such as 50 speech tokens per second, which leads to the exposure bias problem and short context length. To alleviate this problem, we use Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) [9] to compress the length of semantic tokens. Speciﬁcally, the BPE algorithm is used to get the vocabulary given the extracted semantic tokens. BPE iteratively merges the most frequent token pairs in semantic tokens to create a vocabulary. With the BPE algorithm, we can effectively compress the length of the token sequence and balance the trade-off between token sequence length and vocabulary size. Thus, during the training and in- ference stage of LMs, we use BPE-encoded tokens as our input and target. For both zero-shot TTS and S2ST, we use an autoregressive LM to capture the relationship between the input and output, and the training objective can be formulated as: LTTS = −log T  t=0 p(tokt|tok<t, text; θTTS) (9) LS2ST = −log T  t=0 p(tokt|tok<t, toksrc; θS2ST), (10) where text denote the phoneme sequence, toksrc denotes the semantic tokens of source speech, tokt and tok<t denote the target semantic token at t and before t, T denotes the total length of target semantic tokens, θTTS and θS2ST denote the LMs for TTS and S2ST, respectively. For zero-shot TTS generation, we concatenate the prompt phonemes, target phonemes, and BPE-encoded prompt semantic Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. For S2ST, we use the source BPE-encoded semantic tokens as input. Conditioned on these, the LM generates the target BPE-encoded semantic tokens. Finally, the codec decoder transforms the semantic tokens into the ﬁnal waveform. D. Applications of Vec-Tok Speech As shown in Fig. 3, we demonstrate the superiority of Vec-Tok Speech by presenting typical examples of creating the context to perform downstream tasks, including VC, TTS, S2ST, Speech denoising, and beyond. 1) Zero-Shot VC: Zero-shot VC [23] aims to convert the speaker timbre of any source speech to that of any target speech while maintaining the content of the source speech. We achieve this through the proposed Vec-Tok Codec. Speciﬁcally, the speech encoder extracts the semantic token of the source speech and speech vectors of the target speech. Inputting the extracted semantic tokens and speech vectors, the speech decoder gener- ates speech with the speaker timbre of the target speaker and the content of the source speech. 2) Zero-Shot Speaking Style Transfer TTS: Zero-shot TTS [24] aims to synthesize the speech with any target speaker’ timbre, while zero-shot speaking style transfer TTS refers to the task that synthesizes the speech with not only the timbre of any target speaker but also the speaking style of another speaker. Vec-Tok Speech achieves zero-shot speaking style transfer TTS by the following two stages. First, the LM auto-regressively generates the semantic tokens from the input text and prompt semantic tokens. The speaking style of the generated semantic tokensisconsistentwiththepromptsemantictokens.Second,we input the generated semantic tokens and the speech vectors from another speech prompt to the codec decoder that synthesizes the ﬁnal speech. Thus, Vec-Tok Speech transfers the speaking style through prompt semantic tokens and speaker timbre through prompt speech vectors, synthesizing speech with diverse speak- ing styles and speaker timbre. 3) Speech-to-Speech Translation.: S2ST [25] focuses on translating speech content from a source language to another lan- guage. Conventional S2ST is usually based on cascade systems with texts as intermediate representations, including automatic speech recognition, machine translation, and text-to-speech sys- tems, which ignore the transfer of speech prosody and speaker timbre [26]. Ideally, the translated speech should keep the speaker’s timbre and even the speaking style in the source lan- guage. Vec-Tok Speech achieves this target through three stages. In the ﬁrst stage, we extract the input speech’s semantic tokens and speech vectors. In the second stage, a token-to-token LM translates the semantic tokens, which is an implicit translation due to the language-agnostic characteristic of semantic tokens. In the ﬁnal stage, the speech decoder generates the translated speech by inputting the translated semantic tokens and speech vectors from the ﬁrst stage. With the prompt speech vectors and the implicit translation, the translated speech maintains both the timbre and speaking style of the source language speaker. 4) Speech Denoising and Bandwidth Extension: Aiming to improve speech perceptual quality, speech denoising refers to noise removal in a speech-noise mixture while bandwidth ex- tension [27] transforms lower sample rate speech, such as 16 k Hz speech, to higher sample rate speech, such as 48 k Hz speech. In Vec-Tok Speech, WavLM performs well in speech denoising, and semantic tokens mainly focus on linguistic contents while ignoring other acoustic aspects such as noise. Besides, prompt speech vectors extracted from clean prompt speech can pro- vide a clean acoustic environment during speech reconstruction. Therefore, the Vec-Tok Codec can generate clean speech when noise speech is input. On the other hand, although the WavLM is pre-trained on a 16 k Hz speech corpus, the vocoder can be trained on a 48 k Hz speech corpus. Thus, inputting low sample rate speech, Vec-Tok Codec can generate higher sample rate speech with better perceptual quality. 5) Speaker de-Identiﬁcation and Anonymization: Speaker de-identiﬁcation [28] involves removing unique speaker- dependent properties, and speaker anonymization [29] aims to annoy the speaker to another virtual speaker, which both prevent the speaker from being identiﬁed. Vec-Tok Speech can achieve speaker de-identiﬁcation and anonymization through simple operations on the prompt speech vectors when conduct- ing speech reconstruction. Precisely, we extract speaker-speciﬁc speech vectors vecs and corresponding semantic tokens from the speech. Semantic tokens go through the K-means table to Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. The speech decoder takes veca and semantic tokens as input to synthesize speaker-agnostic speech, achieving speaker de-identiﬁcation. Moreover, considering vecs contains speaker-speciﬁc charac- teristics and veca speaker-agnostic characteristics, we can inter- polate between vecs and veca to obtain a speaker-anonymous speaker vector vecano. Thus, the speech decoder takes vecano and semantic tokens as input to synthesize speaker-anonymous speech and accomplish speaker anonymization. IV. EXPERIMENTAL SETUPS This section introduces the database conﬁguration, details of implementation, and evaluation methods. A. Dataset The experiments are conducted on 50,000 hours of multi- domain English and Chinese speech recordings. Table I shows detailed data distribution, where WenetSpeech [30], Gi- gaSpeech [31], LibriTTS [32], GigaST [33] and GigaS2S [34] are all open-source datasets downloaded from the Internet. Fur- thermore, a 20,000-hour expressive audiobook dataset XBook is collected from internal resources, and a clean subset named XBook-clean is selected. Data processing consists of three main modules: speech recognition, speech denoising, and text-to-IPA. Speciﬁcally, the voice activity detection (VAD) result, timestamp, and text tran- scription are obtained from waveforms by an ASR model [35], where the text transcription is processed to get the corresponding IPA-level transcription by a text front-end, including text nor- malization, word segmentation, prosody prediction, polyphone disambiguation, g2p, etc. On the other hand, we leverage the DeepﬁlterNet2 [36] to conduct speech denoising for source waveforms. All denoised waveforms are downsampled to 16 k Hz B. Implementation Details For the Vec-Tok codec, speech vectors are extracted by a pre- trained WavLM-Large model,2 which produces a single vector for every 20 ms of 16 kHz audio. Semantic tokens are obtained from a K-means algorithm with the number of clusters set to 300. The inverse K-means module consists of 6 conformer blocks with 8 attention heads, an embedding dimension of 1024, a feed- forward layer dimension of 4096, and a dropout rate of 0.1. The 2WavLM: https://huggingface.co/microsoft/wavlm-large vocoder adapts the setting of the HiFiGAN V1 model [37]. The average length of the training waveform is 15 seconds, and the duration of prompt speech is 3 seconds. α and β are empirically set to 10. The language models for TTS and S2ST adopt the LLaMA [38] architecture with 12 layers and 12 heads; the hidden and intermediate sizes are 1536 and 6144, respectively. For the BPE encoding, the vocabulary size is 8192, and the token frequency is nearly 16 tokens per second. The CLVP model adopts the BERT architecture with 6 layers, 8 heads, a hidden size of 512, and an intermediate size of 2048 for both text and speech encoder. During inference, the LM generates 256 candidate token sequences and adopts the top 1 sequence as the decoder input based on the CLVP score. For model training, we use AdamW as the default optimizer and β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.95 for all experiments. The LMs for TTS and S2ST are trained by a cosine learning rate schedule on 8 NVIDIA A800 80 GB GPUs with a batch size of 64 for each GPU for ten epochs. The training dataset of the TTS model and the CLVP are the same. The inverse K-means model is trained by 8 NVIDIA 3090 24 GB GPUs with a batch size of 12 per GPU for 500 k steps. The HiFiGAN model is trained by 4 NVIDIA 3090 24 G GPUs with a batch size of 4 per GPU for 2,800 k steps. C. Evaluation Metrics As Vec-Tok Speech can be adapted to various downstream tasks, we leverage multiple metrics and conduct a comprehen- sive set of evaluation methods, including subjective and object evaluation, to assess the performance of Vec-Tok Speech and comparison models. 1) Subjective Evaluation: We conduct two types of human perceptual rating experiments. A total of thirty volunteers with basic English skills participated in these experiments. Mean Opinion Score (MOS) [39] is used to evaluate the naturalness and accent of the synthetic speech. Participants are asked to rate the speech on a scale of 1 to 5, reﬂecting whether the synthetic speech is natural or accent-appropriate. The rating criteria are as follows: bad = 1, poor = 2, fair = 3, good = 4, great = 5, with 0.5-point increments. Similarity Mean Opinion Scores (SMOS) [40] is adopted to subjectively evaluate the synthetic speech from style similarity and speaker similarity. Participants are asked to rate the speech’s similarity to a given style reference and the similarity to the reference of the target speaker. The rating scale and criteria are the same as those used in the MOS evaluation. 2) Objective Evaluation: We measure speaker cosine simi- larity, character error rate (CER), and word error rate (WER) for the synthetic audio of objective assessment. To measure speakercosinesimilarity,wetrainanECAPA-TDNN[41]model trained on 3,300 hours of Mandarin speech and 2,700 hours of English speech from 18,083 speakers to extract x-vectors. A higher cosine similarity indicates a more similar speaker timbre. To evaluate CER and WER, we use an open-source model provided by the WeNet community [42], which uses the U2++ conformer [43] architecture and is trained on 10,000 Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. A higher CER or WER indicates less accurate pronunciation. Besides, we use STOI [44] and PESQ [45] to assess the quality of reconstructed speech. STOI is an objective metric predicting speech intelligibility by comparing the temporal envelopes of clean and distorted signals. At the same time, PESQ assesses speech quality by comparing a degraded signal to a reference speech. V. EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS In this section, we ﬁrst analyze the Vec-Tok Codec. Then, we present the detailed results of Vec-Tok Speech in zero-shot VC and zero-shot TTS, where we also conduct an ablation study to validate the effectiveness of critical components. Fi- nally, we showcase the extensibility of Vec-Tok Speech through speech denoising, speech-to-speech translation, and speaker anonymization. A. Codec Analysis To analyze the proposed Vec-Tok Codec, we ﬁrst evaluate the speaker information and semantic information in speech vectors and semantic tokens. Then, we present the performance of speech reconstruction, showing speech vectors contribute to high-quality speech reconstruction and semantic tokens achieve comparable performance at a low bitrate. 1) Speaker information: We randomly select 30 speakers from VCTK [46], 100 utterances per speaker, to verify the relationship between the speaker identity and the speech vectors. Speciﬁcally, ﬁrst, the VAD detects the vocal segments sent to WavLM to extract the speech vectors. Then, we calculate the averaged vectors in the time axis for each utterance and visualize them through t-SNE [47]. As shown in Fig. 5, averaged vectors are well clustered by speaker identity, demonstrating the strong correlation between the averaged vectors and the speaker identi- ties. Therefore, we adopt utterance level mean normalization on the speech vectors to disentangle speaker-related information. Similarly, we assess speaker information in semantic tokens on the same test set. Speciﬁcally, we train a HiFiGAN V1 model to reconstruct speech waveforms from semantic tokens. Then, we use the pre-trained ECAPA-TDNN model to extract speaker embeddings from reconstructed waveforms and leverage t-SNE to visualize them. As shown in Fig. 4, there are no evident speaker clusters. This result indicates our semantic tokens are speaker-independent, demonstrating the success of speech tok- enization. 2) Semantic information: We use an ASR model to evaluate the semantic information in semantic tokens. We adopt the widely used U2++ conformer as the ASR model. The U2++ conformer is an integrated two-pass framework with bidirec- tional attention decoders, which enhance the shared encoder’s representationalprowess.Weconducttheexperimentemploying the LibriSpeech [48] dataset, utilizing its 960-hour version for training and the test_clean and test_other for testing. We follow SingleCodec [50] and only calculate the bitrate of seman- tic tokens, since Vec-Tok Codec aims to serve speech language models that only need to predict semantic tokens. The results are shown in Table V. As the birate of tokens increases, the performance of Encodec becomes better. However, the Encodec with 6kbps still gets lower PESQ and STOI than the Vec-Tok Codec with speech vectorization, which demonstrates speech vectors contain ﬁne speech details that can reconstruct high- ﬁdelity speech. Besides, Vec-Tok Codec with speech tokeniza- tion achieves comparable performance with Encodec while its bitrate is much lower, showing solid advantages. Furthermore, Vec-Tok Codec with BPE shows a free launch that reduces bitrate from 0.41kbps to 0.21kbps without degradation to speech reconstruction because BPE is a lossless transformation. B. Zero-Shot Voice Conversion To evaluate the performance of zero-shot VC, we randomly reserve 50 utterances as source speech and 12 speakers as the target speakers, which are unseen in the training phase. LM-VC [23] and SEF-VC [49] are utilized as the comparison model. The LM-VC is the ﬁrst work using language models to achieve any-to-any VC, which adopts a two-stage framework with three LMs. The SEF-VC is a speaker-embedding free voice conversion model that learns and incorporates speaker timbre from reference speech via a position-agnostic cross-attention mechanism and reconstructs waveforms from HuBERT [51] tokens non-autoregressively. We train these two models on the same dataset as Vec-Tok Speech for a fair comparison. To com- prehensively assess the performance, we conduct evaluations on both intra-lingual and cross-lingual zero-shot VC. 1) Intra-lingual Zero-Shot VC: Table III shows the results of intra-lingual zero-shot VC. Compared to LM-VC and SEF-VC, Vec-Tok Speech achieves superior speech naturalness and better intelligibility with lower WER/CER. This indicates that Vec-Tok Speech effectively preserves the linguistic content of source 3Encodec: https://github.com/facebookresearch/encodec speech and produces natural speech. The speaker similarity MOS and SCS results verify the excellent ability of Vec-Tok Speech to capture the target speaker’s timbre in a zero-shot manner. These results hence prove that semantic tokens contain rich linguistic content, ensuring good intelligibility, and prompt speech vectors effectively guide and provide speaker informa- tion for voice conversion. To explore the efﬁcacy of Vec-Tok Speech, we conduct an ablation study on speech tokenization and vectorization. First, we validate the effectiveness of speech tokenization by replacing semantic tokens with frame-level phoneme sequences. Speciﬁ- cally, a TDNN-F [52] model trained on WenetSpeech [30] and GigaSpeech [31] is adapted to provide the frame-level phoneme sequences from speech. From the result, we can observe a faint increase in naturalness mos but a sharp decline in speaker sim- ilarity MOS. This result demonstrates that speech tokenization, i.e., K-means and inverse K-means, can better disentangle and control speaker information. Then, we validate the inﬂuence of different speech vectorization by replacing WavLM with XLS-R4 [53]. XLS-R is a wav2vec 2.0 model pre-trained on a large-scale multilingual dataset through self-supervised learn- ing. From Table III, we observe the performance of XLSR is similar to that of WavLM in intra-lingual zero-shot VC. 2) Cross-Lingual Zero-Shot VC: Table IV shows the results of cross-lingual zero-shot VC. Notably, “GroundTruth” repre- sents the source speech recording. Through the results, we can see that there is an apparent decline in all evaluation metrics compared to those in the intra-lingual zero-shot VC in Table III, demonstrating the challenge of cross-lingual zero-shot VC. However, Vec-Tok Speech still obviously outperforms LM-VC and SEF-VC, showing its extraordinary ability to achieve native pronunciation while maintaining the speaker timbre for foreign speakers in zero-shot VC. These results show that Vec-Tok Speech can be expanded to multilingual scenarios; that is, se- mantic tokens and speech vectors obtained from our approach are potentially universal for languages. Furthermore, an ablation study is conducted to verify the effectiveness of speech tokenization and vectorization in cross- lingual zero-shot VC. The tendency of the model without to- kenization is different from that in intra-lingual zero-shot VC, which exhibits a slightly lower naturalness MOS than Vec-Tok Speech, indicating the advantage of semantic tokens against phoneme sequences. Moreover, the model with XLS-R repre- sentations achieves the best performance in almost all metrics, showing the advantages of XLS-R in multilingual speech repre- sentations. C. Zero-Shot Text-to-Speech As discussed in Section V-B, Vec-Tok Speech is capable of multilingual speech conversion. Therefore, we evaluate the per- formance of zero-shot multilingual speech generation. Speciﬁ- cally, we preserve 10 Chinese transcripts and 10 English tran- scriptsasthetestset.Threezero-shotmultilingualTTS,VALL-E 4XLSR: https://huggingface.co/facebook/wav2vec2-xls-r-300m Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. ZHU et al.: VEC-TOK SPEECH: SPEECH VECTORIZATION AND TOKENIZATION FOR NEURAL SPEECH GENERATION 1251 TABLE III RESULTS OF INTRA-LINGUAL ZERO-SHOT VC WITH 95% CONFIDENCE INTERVAL TABLE IV RESULTS OF CROSS-LINGUAL ZERO-SHOT VC WITH 95% CONFIDENCE INTERVAL TABLE V SPEECH RECONSTRUCTION PERFORMANCE OF ENCODEC AND VEC-TOK CODEC X [16], Bark,5 and Naturalspeech 2 [4], are adopted as compar- ison models. VALL-E X is an advanced neural codec language model supporting multilingual speech synthesis, which we train on the same corpora with Vec-Tok Speech. Bark is an innovative and multilingual TTS model developed by Suno AI and has received extensive attention and use. We use Bark’s ofﬁcial checkpoint for comparison. Naturalspeech 2 is a zero-shot TTS system that leverages a neural audio codec with residual vector quantizers to get the quantized latent vectors and uses a diffusion model to generate these latent vectors conditioned on text input. As there is no ofﬁcial implementation, we follow the implemen- tation of Amphion6 [54] and train the model on the same dataset as Vec-Tok Speech. Moreover, Vec-Tok Speech can generate English and Chinese speech on the condition of an unseen speaker prompt and an unseen style prompt, enabling stylistic speechgenerationforaspeaker.Buttothebestofourknowledge, the mentioned zero-shot TTS models can only condition on a single speech prompt representing both speaker and speaking style. Hence, we ﬁrst experiment with the zero-shot TTS task, utilizing the speaker prompt as the style prompt simultaneously 5Bark: https://github.com/suno-ai/bark 6Amphion: https://github.com/open-mmlab/Amphion for a fair comparison. Then, we present different variants within Vec-Tok Speech. Table VI shows the evaluation results of zero-shot TTS. As we can see, there is a trade-off between the naturalness and speaker similarity in VALL-E X, Bark, and Naturalspeech 2. However, Vec-Tok Speech outperforms them in terms of both naturalness andspeakersimilarity,demonstratingtheeffectivenessofspeech vectorization for natural speech generation and tokenization for linguistic information capturing. Besides, Vec-Tok Speech obtains better intelligibility as well with lower CER/WER in Chinese/English synthetic speech, showing effective language modeling based on semantic tokens. Notably, VALL-E X and Bark obtain 5.3% and 5.8% CERs, respectively, on the Chinese testing clips, which are much higher than those obtained by Vec-Tok Speech. Moreover, Vec-Tok Speech achieves the high- est SCS score, which is consistent with speaker similarity MOS, which hence proves the robust speaker timber control by prompt speech vectors. These results indicate the superior zero-shot TTS performance of the proposed method for unseen target speakers. We further assess several variants of Vec-Tok Speech through an ablation study, as shown in Table VI. First, to validate the ability of zero-shot style transfer, two different speech prompts, separately providing speaker timbre and style expressions, are conditioned to Vec-Tok Speech for TTS. Speciﬁcally, we add 20 utterances of speech with apparent speaking style as prompts in LM and evaluate the style similarity in a similar way to speaker similarity. Although there is a mirror decline in overall perfor- mancecomparedtovanillaVec-TokSpeech,themodelwithstyle prompt achieves high style similarity. This phenomenon could be attributed to the disharmony between the speaker prompt and the style prompt, and the degradation of performance in some metrics is acceptable. Secondly, we remove the BPE, which results in a sharp decline in naturalness MOS, WER, and CER. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. This result shows that lower exposure bias and longer context length brought by BPE beneﬁt natural pronunciation. Thirdly, poor samples would greatly affect the evaluation results when reduc- ing the number of candidate token sequences from the CLVP module. The decline in performance indicates the necessity of the CLVP model. Moreover, we add two supplementary experiments to inves- tigate whether the Vec-Tok Codec or the LLaMA improves the zero-shot TTS more. The ﬁrst is replacing LLaMA with a transformer, and the second is replacing Vec-Tok Codec with Encodec. As shown in Table VI, there is a slight performance degradation when replacing LLaMA with a transformer. How- ever, replacing Vec-tok Codec with Encodec leads to a sharp decline. This phenomenon suggests that Vec-Tok Codec con- tributes more to the improvement of zero-shot TTS. Besides, it’s interesting that the combination of LLaMA and Encodec even performs worse than VALL-E X. This indicates using a combination of non-autoregressive model and autoregressive model is better than using a single auto-regressive model to predict acoustic tokens extracted by Encodec. D. Extensibility Previous sections have demonstrated Vec-Tok Speech’s enor- mous advantages in zero-shot speech generation. In this sub- section, we use some other tasks and metrics to present Vec-Tok Speech’s extensibility. 1) Speech Denoising: To evaluate the performance of Vec-Tok Speech in speech denoising task. We use the synthetic test set with the reverberation of DNS challenge-Interspeech 2021 [55] as our test set and leverage the DNSMOS to evaluate thequalityofdenoisedaudio.Additionally,wecompareVec-Tok Speech with existing state-of-the-art models trained explicitly TABLE VIII THE DNSMOS RESULT OF COMPARISON SYSTEMS with denoising objectives, including Conv-TasNet [56], Demucs [57], Inter-SubNet [58] and CDiffuSE [59]. As shown in Table.VIII, Vec-Tok Speech performs better than comparison models, showing that semantic tokens contain little noise and prompt speech vectors can contribute to clean recon- structed speech. Moreover, an additional language model can further improve the performance of speech denoising according to our other investigation SELM [60]. 2) Speech-to-Speech Translation: We assess the perfor- mance of our model in the S2ST task on the test set of GigaST [33] in terms of naturalness, speaker similarity, BLEU [61], and SCS. BLEU7 measures the n-gram overlap between generated and annotated captions. We utilize the con- strained En-Zh speech translation model, involving the same training data and similar parameter size in GigaST [33] as the compared method. Notably, GigaST is a speech-to-text transla- tion model in which only the BLEU metric is available, while Vec-Tok Speech is a speech-to-speech translation model that is much harder. Besides, for a comprehensive comparison, we add GigaST [33] and YourTTS [24] as a comparison model to achieve speech-to-speech translation. 7BLEU: https://github.com/mjpost/sacrebleu Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. ZHU et al.: VEC-TOK SPEECH: SPEECH VECTORIZATION AND TOKENIZATION FOR NEURAL SPEECH GENERATION 1253 TABLE IX RESULTS OF SPEAKER ANONYMIZATION AsshowninTableVII,Vec-TokSpeechreachesacomparative BLEU score compared to GigaST and surpasses the cascaded translation model (GigaST+YourTTS) by an obvious margin. Speaker similarity MOS and cosine similarity scores both in- dicate the generated speech preserves the speaker timbre of the source speech during translation. Vec-Tok Speech achieves a naturalness MOS of 3.69, demonstrating that the proposed method can effectively synthesize natural speech of the target language. These results imply that the proposed method can con- duct reasonable speech-to-speech translation while preserving the speaker timbre of the source language. 3) Speaker Anonymization: To assess the performance of Vec-Tok Speech in speaker anonymization, we follow the rules of VPC 2022 [62] and use the same test set. The two sets contain several female and male speakers from the LibriSpeech and VCTK corpus. We adopt equal error rate (EER), gain of voice distinctiveness(GVD),andpitchcorrelation(Corr)asevaluation metrics. We compare Vec-Tok Speech with VPC baseline [62], VPC topline [63], and recent research - SALT [64]. As shown in Table IX, there is an obvious trade-off within the VPC baseline and topline in terms of EER, GVD, and Corr. Vec-Tok Speech surpasses the VPC baseline and topline, demonstrating its potential advantage in speaker anonymization. Besides, although Vec-Tok Speech gets lower scores compared to SALT, Vec-Tok Speech is more ﬂexible and can anonymize any input speech without additional inputs. At the same time, SALT needs a set of speech

title: IEEE SIGNAL PROCESSING LETTERS, VOL. 32, 2025
file_name: Vector Field Decomposition-Based Flow Matching for Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Text-to-Speech.pdf
text: 3560 IEEE SIGNAL PROCESSING LETTERS, VOL. 32, 2025 Vector Field Decomposition-Based Flow Matching for Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Text-to-Speech Jaeuk Lee , Nam-Seok Song , and Joon-Hyuk Chang , Senior Member, IEEE Abstract—Zero-shot text-to-speech (TTS) has recently achieved remarkable performance by leveraging a speech prompt instead of a speaker embedding, as it provides richer information. However, zero-shot cross-lingual tasks synthesize speech in multiple lan- guages according to a given language ID, regardless of the language of the speech prompt. Consequently, the inherent language-speciﬁc characteristics of the speech prompt may conﬂict with the language ID, potentially affecting the accuracy of language representation in speech. Thus, we propose vector ﬁeld decomposition-based ﬂow matching that decomposes the vector ﬁeld into speaker and lan- guage components. These components are trained to be activated in different frequency bins, as speaker and language identity are dis- tributed across distinct frequency ranges in speech. This approach is particularly effective for cross-lingual TTS, as it minimizes con- ﬂicts between speech prompts and language IDs. As a result, the summation of the two components directly forms the vector ﬁeld that represents the probability path from a Gaussian distribution to the target data distribution (e.g., mel spectrogram). Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms the conventional method in terms of both subjective and objective evaluations. Index Terms—Flow matching, speech prompt, zero-shot cross- lingual text-to-speech. I. INTRODUCTION Z ERO-SHOT cross-lingual text-to-speech (TTS) mod- els [1][2], which generate speech in a different language from the reference speech of an unseen speaker during training while preserving the speaker identity, have become a more important technology in line with the trend of globalization. For cross-lingual TTS, it is essential to extract only speaker-related information from the reference speech because language-related information extracted from reference speech collides with the language information of the language ID that determines the language of the speech to be generated. Thus, numerous at- tempts [3], [4], [5], [6], [7], [8] have been made to separate language and speaker information. Previously, gradient reverse layer-based adversarial training [3] or mutual information min- imization [4] was utilized to disentangle speaker and language Received 5 March 2025; revised 30 April 2025; accepted 2 May 2025. Date of publication 19 May 2025; date of current version 23 September 2025. This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Korea Government (MSIT) under Grant RS-2025-00557944. The associate editor coordinating the review of this article and approving it for publication was Prof. Zhizheng Wu. (Corresponding author: Joon-Hyuk Chang.) The authors are with the School of Electronics, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, South Korea (e-mail: pier314@hanyang.ac.kr; insang99@hanyang.ac. kr; jchang@hanyang.ac.kr). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/LSP.2025.3571407 representation. Subsequently, phonetic posterior grams [5], au- tomatic speech recognition bottleneck [6], or self-supervised learning models [9][10] are employed to disentangle speciﬁc information from speech [7][8]. As mentioned above, these studies represent speaker information using speaker embedding, a ﬁxed-sized single vector extracted by a speaker encoder such as YourTTS [11]. P-Flow [12] points out the potential bottleneck of speaker embedding approaches to extract speaker information from the reference speech and uses a 3-second mel spectrogram, randomly segmented from reference speech, as a speech prompt. Likewise, VALL-E [13] and NaturalSpeech2 [14] adopt speech prompts to perform zero-shot TTS tasks. Utilizing a speech prompt, rather than a speaker embedding, is more effective for enhancing speaker similarity between the generated and refer- ence speech, as the speech prompt contains a broader range of information. However, this increased information also includes more language-related features. In zero-shot cross-lingual TTS based on speech prompts, where speech is synthesized in a language different from the speech prompt, language-speciﬁc information in the speech prompt can negatively impact perfor- mance. The target language of the synthesized speech should be determined based on the provided language ID, but the language information of the speech prompt may interfere with this process. For this reason, speech prompt-based zero-shot cross-lingual TTS is extremely rare. VALL-E X [1] uses a speech prompt for zero-shot cross-lingual TTS but requires a transcript corresponding to the speech prompt. To solve this problem, we propose a vector ﬁeld decomposition-based ﬂow matching (DecoFlow matching), which mitigates conﬂicts between the speech prompt and the language ID. As illustrated in Fig. 1, DecoFlow matching de- composes vector ﬁeld into two disentangled components, each corresponding to the language ID and the speech prompt. De- coFlow matching disentangles two components to be orthogonal to each other and adjusts their activity depending on frequency bins for the following reasons: r Basically, the fact that the two representations are orthogo- nal means they contain different information as mentioned in [15]. r As established in previous works [16] and [17], important frequency bands for speaker and language identity differ. In the relatively lower frequency range, speaker character- istics are more prominent, while in the higher frequency range, pronunciation details that deﬁne language identity are determined. Suppose the component extracted from the speech prompt is activated in the lower frequency range, 1070-9908 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artiﬁcial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. LEE et al.: VECTOR FIELD DECOMPOSITION-BASED FLOW MATCHING FOR ZERO-SHOT CROSS-LINGUAL TEXT-TO-SPEECH 3561 Fig. 1. Concepts of (a) ﬂow matching and (b) DecoFlow matching (our proposed) models. l, s, and t denote language ID, speech prompt, and time step [0,1], respectively. Flow matching predicts vector ﬁeld vt that generates probability path between Gaussian noise and target mel spectrogram. DecoFlow matching decomposes vector ﬁeld into language vl t and speech prompt vs t dependent components. and its inﬂuence decreases in the higher frequency range. In that case, the speech prompt can be effectively utilized even in cross-lingual TTS. Two components are trained to be orthogonal while be- ing activated depending on frequency. Then, the sum of two components becomes the vector ﬁeld of ﬂow matching [18] that indicates the probability path from Gaussian noise to the target mel spectrogram. As a result, the DecoFlow matching- based TTS model (Deco-TTS) can effectively use speech prompts for cross-lingual TTS tasks. Through experiments, we demonstrate better performance in objective and sub- jective evaluations than conventional zero-shot cross-lingual TTS. II. BACKGROUND Flow matching [18] estimates a probability path between data x1 and prior x0 distributions. [18] deﬁnes optimal transport (OT) path based on Gaussian conditional probability path that forms a straight trajectory between x0 and x1. The OT path on time step t ∈[0, 1] varies depending on μt and σt, which can be deﬁned as follows: μt = tx1 and σt = 1 −(1 −σmin)t. Since a probabil- ity distribution on the OT path follows a Gaussian distribution, xt on time step t can be computed by afﬁne transform as follows: xt = tx1 + (1 −(1 −σmin)t)x0.Consequently,thevectorﬁeld ut, which generates a desired probability path, is deﬁned via the ordinary differential equation as follows: dxt dt = ut = x1 −(1 −σmin)x0. (1) Flow matching is trained to predict the vector ﬁeld ut corre- sponding to xt using mean squared error (MSE) loss function between predicted vector ﬁeld vt and the target ut. Thus, ﬂow matching can generate data from prior distribution by repeating the following equation until the time step t becomes 1 from 0: xt+dt = xt + vtdt, (2) where dt is set to 1 N and N is the total number of sampling. III. PROPOSED METHOD Our DecoFlow matching-based text-to-speech model, Deco- TTS, consists of four key components: a text encoder, a language-adaptive encoder, a speech prompt encoder, and the DecoFlow matching network, as illustrated in Fig. 2. Text encoder consists of 4 Transformer blocks [19] and extracts text representation h. Then, the language adaptive encoder has 4 Transformer blocks, augmented with conditional layer normalization [20]. This architecture is the same as the style adaptive encoder of Grad-StyleSpeech [21]. Still, we use lan- guage embedding extracted from the language embedding ta- ble, which has trainable language embeddings corresponding to language IDs, instead of style embedding. Thus, the language adaptive encoder produces language-dependent text representa- tion hl. The speech prompt encoder extracts speaker-dependent text representation hs, and DecoFlow matching generates two components dependent on language and speaker information, respectively. A. Speech Prompt Encoder We design a speech prompt encoder consisting of a Mel-style encoder [21], a style adaptive encoder, and Transformer blocks with self- and cross-attentions. The style adaptive encoder was incorporated because it offered slight performance improve- ments over the version of our model without it. Mel-style and style-adaptive encoders follow the same architecture and pro- cessing as in [21], extracting style-adaptive text representation. Additionally, we utilize the speech prompt as direct input of Transformer block with cross-attention because it is more ef- fective for zero-shot TTS compared to encoding all the speaker information into a ﬁxed-size vector, as mentioned in [12]. Sub- sequently, the output of a Transformer block with cross-attention is passed through 4 Transformer blocks with self-attention and becomes speaker-dependent text representation hs. B. DecoFlow Matching DecoFlow matching effectively disentangles speech prompts from language information because it is designed so that two components generated from language-dependent and speaker- dependent text representations affect different frequency bins, respectively. DecoFlow matching consists of two U-Net archi- tectures [22] that decompose the vector ﬁeld vt into its language component, vl t, and speaker component, vs t . Both vl t and vs t are learnable components, represented as vl t(·; θ1) and vs t (·; θ2), where θ1 and θ2 are the learnable parameters associated with each component, respectively. One U-Net vl t(·; θ1) receives language-dependent text representation hl, time step t, and xt that is deﬁned in Section II. Thus, the language component can be denoted by vl t(xt, hl, t; θ1). The other U-Net vs t (·; θ2) receives speaker-dependent text representation hs instead of hl and can also be represented by vs t (xt, hs, t; θ2). vs t and vl t can be considered as T × F matrices, where T and F are the length and frequency bin of mel spectrogram, respectively. We deﬁne elements of vs t and vl t as sij and lij, where 0 ≤i < T and 0 ≤j < F, respectively. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 3562 IEEE SIGNAL PROCESSING LETTERS, VOL. 32, 2025 Fig. 2. Overall architecture of DecoFlow matching, Deco-TTS, and speech prompt encoder. σmin is set to 1e−4. The sum of two outputs from language adaptive and speech prompt encoders becomes the input of the duration predictor. Then, we train the two components of the vector ﬁeld for frequency-dependent activation via Ldeco comprising three loss functions as follows: Lcosine = T −1  i=0 | cos(si, li)|. (3) Lbin = T −1  i=0 F −1  j=0 |sij| · |lij|. (4) Ldensity = T −1  i=0 (||si||2 −||li||2), (5) where si and li denote i-th row of vs t and vl t, respectively. cos(·), | · |, and || · ||2 compute cosine similarity, absolute value, and l2-norm, respectively. Lcosine enforces orthogonality between the speaker and language components, thereby effectively disen- tangling them [15]. Subsequently, two components are activated in different frequency bins by Lbin, respectively, because Lbin ensures that when one component increases at each frequency bin, the other component decreases. Lbin may make both com- ponents zero, but Eq. (6) prevents this. Additionally, to prevent the speaker component, which has rich information, from dom- inating, we introduce Ldensity to balance the magnitude of the two components. Then, for estimating the target vector ﬁeld ut deﬁnedbyEq.(1),DecoFlowmatchingisalsotrainedasfollows: LF M = ||ut −(vl t(xt, hl, t; θ1) + vs t (xt, hs, t; θ2))||2. (6) Consequently, DecoFlow matching can generate a mel spectro- gram by repeating the following equation: xt+dt := xt + (vl t(xt, hl, t; θ1) + vs t (xt, hs, t; θ2))dt. (7) C. Loss Function We train DecoFlow matching-based TTS (Deco-TTS) using Ldeco, LF M, and Ldur. Ldur trains duration predictor [23] by the MSE loss function between predicted duration and target duration, which is obtained from pre-trained RAD-aligner [24]. Thus, Deco-TTS is trained with the following total loss function Ltotal: Ltotal = λLdeco + LF M + Ldur, (8) where λ is a hyper-parameter set to 0.1. IV. EXPERIMENTS We used LibriTTS-R [25] and Multilingual LibriSpeech (MLS) [26] datasets. We derived 2 languages (German and French) from MLS dataset consisting of 8 languages, and selected 2,281 additional English speakers from LibriTTS-R consisting of 580 hours of data from 2,456 speakers. Sampling rate of all datasets was set to 16 kHz, and mel spectrogram was extracted with a hop size of 256 and a window size of 1024. In addition, we employ a NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU with a batch size of 36, and Adam optimizer [27] is used with a learning rate of 1e−4. Finally, we used phonemizer1 to convert the text into the International Phonetic Alphabet format. Our audio samples are released on the page.2 Trainset:Weused3languages(English,German,andFrench) for training Deco-TTS. English, German, and French datasets consist of 2,281, 167, and 135 speakers and were employed to train Deco-TTS and HiFi-GAN [28] that converts mel spectro- gram into waveform. Comparison models: We compared Deco-TTS with VALL-E X [1], which is a speech prompt-based zero-shot cross-lingual TTS model with excellent performance. However, the ofﬁcial implementation of VALL-E X has not been released. Thus, we obtained audio samples from the ofﬁcial demo page3 of VALL-E X. We also compared Deco-TTS with ZMM-TTS [29], which has better performance than YourTTS [11]. We employed the 1https://github.com/bootphon/phonemizer 2https://github.com/hello8439/Deco-TTS 3https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/project/vall-e-x/vall-e-x Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:20:04 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. LEE et al.: VECTOR FIELD DECOMPOSITION-BASED FLOW MATCHING FOR ZERO-SHOT CROSS-LINGUAL TEXT-TO-SPEECH 3563 TABLE I ZERO-SHOT CROSS-LINGUAL TTS EVALUATION FOR ENGLISH TTS TABLE II ZERO-SHOT CROSS-LINGUAL TTS EVALUATION FOR ENGLISH (EN), FRENCH (FR), AND GERMAN (GE) TTS ofﬁcial implementation of ZMM-TTS that used pre-trained self- supervised models [30][31]. Except for these self-supervised models, we trained ZMM-TTS from scratch using our train set. Evaluation set: We obtained 6 audio samples of English TTS with Chinese speech prompts from the demo page of VALL-E X. We employed these Chinese speech prompts to synthesize English speech (the same transcripts as the demo page) using Deco-TTS. However, Chinese speech prompt is an unseen language during training in Deco-TTS. For fair evalua- tion, Deco-TTS also synthesized English speech using speech prompts of seen language (French and German). Performance for zero-shot cross-lingual English TTS of VALL-E X and Deco-TTS is shown in Table I. The number of audio samples used in Table I is 6 for each model (row). Given the limitations of comparing performance solely with demo samples of VALL-E X, we measured the performance of English, French, and Ger- man TTS in zero-shot cross-lingual tasks using Deco-TTS and ZMM-TTS in Table II. We randomly selected 5 unseen speakers for each language and synthesized 6 samples per unseen speaker for evaluation in Table II. Evaluation metrics: We conducted a crowd-sourced mean opinion score (MOS) test with 12 participants for subjective evaluation. Participants evaluated synthesized and ground truth speech in terms of speaker similarity (SMOS) and naturalness (MOS). Participants listened to a 3-second speech prompt and synthesized speech for evaluating SMOS. Subsequently, we chose comparative mean opinion score (CMOS) with 7 points (from -3 to 3) as the evaluation metric. For objective evalua- tion, speaker embedding cosine similarity (SECS), representing speaker similarity, was computed using ECAPA-TDNN [32] pre-trained with Voxceleb [33]. Also, we evaluated speech in- telligibility with character error rate (CER), using a pre-trained speech recognition model from the ofﬁcial implementation of Fig. 3. The result of speaker component trained as following loss functions: ‘Ldeco (Red)’, ‘Ldeco w/o Ldensity (Blue)’, and ‘w/o Ldeco (Yellow). x- axis and y-axis are frequency of mel spectrogram and magnitude of speaker component, respectively. This magnitude represents the average magnitude of all frames on the time axis. whisper-base [34] to measure transcription errors from gener- ated samples. A. Effect of DecoFlow Matching In this subsection, we demonstrated that DecoFlow match- ing effectively serves frequency-dependent activation of speech prompts. Fig. 3 shows the magnitude of speaker components according to frequency. As reported in [35], the high-frequency band (> 4kHz) determines detailed pronunciation (e.g., frica- tives), which reﬂects language characteristics, and plays an es- sential role in speech intelligibility. Furthermore, the study [16] found that the frequency range below 1kHz is the most important for speaker identiﬁcation, whereas the region above 4.5kHz is the least signiﬁcant. As shown in Fig. 3, our proposed method induces high activation of speaker components in low frequency while preserving low activation in high frequency. Additionally, Ldensity suppresses the activation of the speech prompt in high frequency by preventing excessive reduction of language components. B. Evaluation We compared Deco-TTS with VALL-E X and ZMM-TTS to evaluate zero-shot cross-lingual TTS performance. Deco-TTS performs slightly less when using the same Chinese speech prompts for Deco-TTS and VALL-E X. However, the Chi- nese speech prompt is the unseen language during training for Deco-TTS, and when using the seen language (French and German) speech prompt for Deco-TTS, it outperforms VALL-E X in English TTS as shown in Table I. Subsequently, we com- pared Deco-TTS with ZMM-TTS to evaluate the performance of German, French, and English TTS. Although ZMM-TTS used pre-trained self-supervised models as a large-scale dataset, Deco-TTS performs better than ZMM-TTS as shown in Table II. V. CONCLUSION In this paper, we introduced Deco-TTS using a vector ﬁeld decomposition-based ﬂow matching that adjusts frequency- dependent activation of speech prompt. Experimental results show that Deco-TTS outperformed VALL-E X in terms of objective and subjective evaluations of cross-lingual tasks. file_name: Verification is All You Need Prompting Large Language Models for Zero-Shot Clinical Coding.pdf
text: 8536 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 29, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025 Veriﬁcation is All You Need: Prompting Large Language Models for Zero-Shot Clinical Coding Shaoxin Li , Member, IEEE, Can Zheng, Jiaxiang Wu , Qinwei Xu, Xingkun Xu , Hanyang Wang , Yingkai Sun , Zhian Bai, Yuchen Xu , Lifeng Zhu , Weiguo Hu, and Feiyue Huang Abstract—Clinical coding translates medical information from Electronic Health Records (EHRs) into structured codes such as ICD-10, which are essential for health- care applications. Advances in deep learning and natural language processing have enabled automatic ICD coding models to achieve notable accuracy metrics on in-domain datasets when adequately trained. However, the scarcity of clinical medical texts and the variability across differ- ent datasets pose signiﬁcant challenges, making it difﬁcult for current state-of-the-art models to ensure robust gen- eralization performance across diverse data distributions. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs), such as GPT-4o, have shown great generalization capabilities across general domains and potential in medical informa- tion processing tasks. However, their performance in gen- erating clinical codes remains suboptimal. In this study, we propose a novel ICD coding paradigm based on code veriﬁcation to leverage the capabilities of LLMs. Instead of directly generating accurate codes from a vast code space, we simplify the task by verifying the code assignment from a given candidate set. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that LLMs function more effectively as code veriﬁers rather than code generators, with GPT-4o achiev- ing the best performance on the CodiEsp dataset under zero-shot settings. Furthermore, our results indicate that LLM-based systems can perform on par with state-of-the- art clinical coding systems while offering superior general- izability across institutions, languages, and ICD versions. Index Terms—Clinical Coding, Large Language Models, Code Veriﬁcation, Zero-shot, Cross-Domain. Received 20 January 2025; revised 15 May 2025; accepted 23 July 2025. Date of publication 28 July 2025; date of current version 7 November 2025. This work was supported in part by Noncommunicable Chronic Diseases - National Science and Technology Major Project under Grant 2023ZD0508403 and in part by Science and Technology Committee of Shanghai under Grant 23JS1400900. (Shaoxin Li, Can Zheng and Jiaxiang Wu contributed equally to this work.) (Correspond- ing authors: Shaoxin Li;Feiyue Huang.) Shaoxin Li, Jiaxiang Wu, Qinwei Xu, Xingkun Xu, Hanyang Wang, Yingkai Sun, Zhian Bai, Yuchen Xu, Lifeng Zhu, Weiguo Hu, and Feiyue Huang are with the Shanghai Digital Medicine Innovation Center, Ruijin Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, Shanghai 200025, China (e-mail: lsx30716@rjh.com.cn; wjx03417@rjh.com.cn; xqw03411@rjh.com.cn; xxk03413@rjh.com.cn; faceeyes@126.com; syk12961@rjh.com.cn; bza@rjh.com.cn; xyc03356@rjh.com.cn; zlf@rjh.com.cn; wghu@rjh.com.cn; hfy30711@rjh.com.cn). Can Zheng is with the Department of Computer Science, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, PA 15260 USA (e-mail: caz51@pitt.edu). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/JBHI.2025.3593028 I. INTRODUCTION C LINICAL coding involves translating medical informa- tion recorded in patient EHRs into structured codes in a classiﬁcation system like ICD-10 [1], [2]. This process is crucial for statistical analysis, healthcare planning, policy making, and analysis of epidemiological trends [3], [4]. However, manual clinical coding is time-consuming and labor-intensive, requiring specialized medical knowledge. As a result, automated clinical coding has been proposed [4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], [11], [12]. By utilizing machine learning and natural language pro- cessing (NLP) techniques, automated coding can signiﬁcantly improve the efﬁciency of generating clinical codes and reduce the costs associated with healthcare administration. Traditional automated coding methods treat clinical coding as a multi-label classiﬁcation problem [6], [9], [10], [11], [12]. Although these methods have made some progress in accurately classifying clinical codes, the challenges remain signiﬁcant. First, training a reliable classiﬁcation model relies on large amount of annotated data in the medical domain. Although methods like BERT and its variants [5], [7], [8], [9], [10] can enhancetheutilizationofmedicaldatathroughapre-trainingand ﬁne-tuning paradigm, the ﬁne-tuning process still incurs signiﬁ- cant manual annotation costs. Second, the usage of clinical codes may exhibit certain variations across hospitals and regions, causing inconsistency in coding between different institutes. Lastly, the clinical texts input for the ICD coding task may also exhibit signiﬁcant content and structure variations across different datasets. For instance, the MIMIC-IV [13] dataset provides a well- structured, detailed, and comprehensive medical record with clear timelines and multifaceted descriptions of patient con- ditions. In contrast, the CodiEsp dataset offers a concise and focused record with a more direct writing style and less detailed clinical outcomes during hospitalization of patients. Due to these challenges, traditional code classiﬁcation models suffer from poor generalizability across different datasets. For ex- ample, Ponthongmak et al. [14] reported that applying state- of-the-art classiﬁcation models to out-of-domain datasets like Thai resulted in performance degradation. We also illustrate the cross-domain generalization performance of different ICD coding methods in Fig. 1. We ﬁnd that several state-of-the-art ICD coding methods trained on MIMIC-IV dataset perform well 2168-2194 © 2025 IEEE.
