LI et al.: VERIFICATION IS ALL YOU NEED: PROMPTING LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS FOR ZERO-SHOT CLINICAL CODING 8537 Fig. 1. Comparisons of the micro-F1 and macro-F1 scores of tra- ditional classiﬁcation based models (PLM-ICD, LAAT, CAML) and LLMs (GPT-4o, Llama3.1-405B, Qwen2-72B) across different datasets. The classiﬁcation based models were trained on the MIMIC-IV-ICD10 dataset and evaluated on both MIMIC-IV-ICD10 and CodiEsp-ICD10 datasets, while the LLMs perform zero-shot clinical coding tasks on both datasets without dataset speciﬁc ﬁne-tuning. in the MIMIC-IV test set. However, their performance metrics decline signiﬁcantly when transferred to the CodiEsp dataset. Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs), such as Chat- GPT[15],havedemonstratedimpressivecapabilitiesinhandling long input contexts and exhibiting signiﬁcant generalizability across various downstream tasks. In the medical domain, recent studies [16], [17] have also indicated that state-of-the-art LLMs possess substantial medical knowledge to handle various clinical tasks. For example, LLMs can achieve high performance in the MedQA dataset [18], which is analogous to the form of the United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE). Consequently, some pioneering efforts have applied LLMs to the clinical coding task [19], [20], [21]. Unlike traditional clas- siﬁcation paradigms, these approaches frame clinical coding as a text generation problem that directly generates clinical codes in textual form. Unfortunately, existing LLM-based approaches struggle to generate clinical codes effectively. Due to the spe- cialized nature of medical corpora and the complexity of clinical codes, the direct generation paradigm suffers from issues such as incomplete outputs, errors, and hallucinations, rendering it suboptimal compared to traditional classiﬁcation approaches. The challenges LLMs face in ICD coding tasks are primarily due to their struggle with complex multi-label classiﬁcation tasks, rather than the lack of medical knowledge for ICD coding. There are two main reasons for this struggle. First, clinical coding typically involves a large label space compared to normal classiﬁcation tasks. For example, the ICD-10-CM system [22] contains approximately 68,000 diagnostic codes. As a result, ensuring that LLMs generate the exact ICD code turns out to be challenging. Second, LLMs are trained on the basis of the principle of next-token prediction, where the generation order of the token is vital. In contrast, the assignment of codes in the ICD-coding task may not follow a standard order. The lack of a clear order could hinder the LLMs’ ability to determine the relevant information needed to generate the next code. To address the limitations of LLMs in handling the ICD coding task, we propose a novel veriﬁcation-based approach by taking a set of candidate codes selected by a traditional classiﬁcation model together with original clinical notes as input (for detailed information on candidate set construction, please refer to Section III-B). In this way, we transform the type of task for LLMs from complex multi-code classiﬁcation to multi-code veriﬁcation, as shown in Fig. 2(c). Compared to the generation- based paradigm, our code veriﬁcation paradigm reduces the complexity of the task, which leads LLMs to focus more on understanding the relationship between clinical notes and the corresponding ICD codes. To reduce hallucination and enhance the correctness of LLM-based coding, we further employ an evidence-based prompting technique that instructs LLMs to ﬁrst ﬁnd evidence and then make judgments. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that the pro- posed veriﬁcation-based LLM methods can achieve advanced performances in the automatic clinical coding task, especially in a zero-shot scenario. The key ﬁndings are summarized below. 1) LLMs are poor code generators but good veriﬁers: We validated the effectiveness of the veriﬁcation-based prompting and compared it with various generation-based promptings. Using the GPT-4o model, evidence-based veriﬁcation improved the micro F1 score from 0.2401 to 0.4428 on the MIMIC-IV dataset [13]. Although cutting- edge reasoning LLMs, such as DeepSeek-R1 [23], have signiﬁcantly enhanced reasoning capabilities, achieving a Micro F1 score of 0.3330 with generation-based prompt- ing, their performance can be further improved to 0.4396 by using veriﬁcation-based prompting in clinical coding tasks. 2) LLMs exhibit superior performance as clinical coders in zero-shot scenarios: Traditional SOTA models ex- hibit suboptimal zero-shot performance when trained on the MIMIC-IV dataset and evaluated on the CodiEsp dataset [24]. In contrast, the GPT-4o with the proposed veriﬁcation-based prompts achieves a signiﬁcantly higher micro F1 score of 0.3037, compared to the best traditional models, which achieve a micro F1 score of 0.1867. 3) LLM-based code veriﬁers show remarkable versatile cross-domain generalizability: We evaluated the ICD code veriﬁcation capabilities of both LLM-based and non-LLM-based approaches in multiple cross-domain scenarios, including the cross-institution, cross-language, and cross-ICD version. In all scenarios, LLM-based ap- proaches demonstrate consistent performance, whereas the performance of non-LLM based approaches declines signiﬁcantly. II. RELATED WORK A. ICD Code Classiﬁcation The classiﬁcation-based approaches treat clinical coding as a multi-label classiﬁcation task, as shown in Fig. 2(a), where each ICD code is considered a distinct category. Various model ar- chitectures, such as CNNs, RNNs, and transformers, are trained using supervised learning to predict the probability of each cate- gory given an input text. Mullenbach et al. [25] proposed a CNN- based network with a per-label attention mechanism, providing relations between labels and k-gram text segments. [26] propose Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 8538 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 29, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025 Fig. 2. The three types of automatic clinical coding approaches: (a) The traditional non-LLM classiﬁcation-based approach, which segments clinical notes into multiple fragments before processing them with a backbone model. The output is typically a vector of prediction scores for each ICD code; (b) The direct generation-based approach, the current mainstream method for performing ICD coding using LLMs. In this approach, the entire clinical note is formatted into a prompt based on a predeﬁned template, guiding the LLM to generate accurate ICD codes; (c) The proposed veriﬁcation-based approach, which introduces additional candidate codes as input to construct the prompt. In the ﬁgure, an evidence- based veriﬁcation prompt is employed, requiring the LLM to provide supporting evidence for each code assignment along with the ﬁnal decision. This additional evidence not only improves veriﬁcation accuracy but also enhances the interpretability of the ICD coding results. a Medical Topic Mining (MTM) method to extract medical texts with selected topics from the entire discharge summary. Then a deep learning model called Cross-textual Attentional ICD coding(CAIC)isusedtolearnanICDcodeclassiﬁer.LAAT[27] also adopted a per-label attention, applying a BiLSTM encoder and weighting its outputs to generate label-speciﬁc features for binary classiﬁcation. In addition, JointLAAT takes the advan- tages of the hierarchical nature of ICD codes and designed two level attentions to predict normalized codes then raw codes, thus improving the performance on rare codes. [28] Wu et al. propose a pseudo label-wise attention mechanism to tackle the redundant and costly in per-label attention computing. Instead of com- puting different attention modes for different ICD codes, their pseudo label-wise attention mechanism automatically merges similar ICD codes and computes only one attention mode for similar ICD codes. A recent trend in classiﬁcation approaches involves the use of BERT-based architectures [5], [7]. Vu et al. treat ICD coding a downstream task based on pre-trained PubMedBERT [9]. PLM-ICD addresses the input length limitation of BERT-like models by dividing clinical notes into smaller segments and sub- sequently pooling token representations across these segments to integrate information from the entire text [10]. These methods typicallyfollowapre-trainingandﬁne-tuningprocess, wherethe model is ﬁrst pre-trained on large amounts of medical corpus and then ﬁne-tuned on downstream clinical code classiﬁcation tasks. However, due to the vast category space of clinical codes and the reliance on high-quality annotated data, classiﬁcation- based approaches often exhibit poor generalizability across domains [19]. In addition, traditional architectures, including BERT, struggle to handle long medical contexts effectively. B. ICD Code Generation Instead of directly predicting the high-dimensional space of ICD codes, many works have recently proposed to generate the lower dimension of text descriptions, which then infers ICD codes. These generation-based approaches treat the clinical coding problem as a text generation task, as shown in Fig. 2(b). For example, Yang et al. [29] present a autoregressive Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. LI et al.: VERIFICATION IS ALL YOU NEED: PROMPTING LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS FOR ZERO-SHOT CLINICAL CODING 8539 Generation with Prompt (GP) framework for ICD coding. They designed a novel mask objective for pre-training the clinical language model, and then ﬁne-tuned the model to ﬁrst generate code descriptions with a preﬁx prompt and then assign the descriptions to the corresponding ICD codes. Taking advantage of the growing medical capabilities of LLMs, many studies have explored utilizing off-the-shelf LLMs to perform zero-shot ICD code generation, bypassing the need for task-speciﬁc annotated data and model ﬁne-tuning. Boyle et al. [19] evaluated this generation task with recent LLMs, showing the information extraction and medical reasoning abil- ities of LLMs with expertise instructions. In addition, they used hierarchically and sparsely search for relevant codes instead of generating code from scratch for better efﬁciency. Yang et al. [20] use LLMs as sentence-level evidence extractors that map text to a limited set of candidate codes and train a veriﬁer to decide whether the code-evidence pair is acceptable. However, due to the lack of prior knowledge about clinical codes, LLMs that generate ICD codes token-by-token are prone to issues such as omissions, errors, and hallucinations [21]. One of the main barriers to using LLMs in medicine is the per- ception that they use uninterpretable methods to make clinical decisions. In [30], Savage et al. developed various prompts that leverage diagnostic reasoning instructions to mitigate the “black box” limitations of LLMs, thus advancing their potential for safe and effective application in medical contexts. Given the demonstrated dependence of LLM behavior on instructions and context, prompt design emerges as a critical factor to make full use of LLM [31], [32]. III. METHODOLOGY This work introduces a veriﬁcation paradigm for ICD coding based on standardized prompting and candidate selection. The complete workﬂow is illustrated in Fig 2(c). In this paradigm, LLM performs structured veriﬁcation rather than open-ended generationbyevaluatingamedicalnoteagainstasetofcandidate diagnoses. The veriﬁcation process follows a carefully designed template that guides the model through a controlled reasoning sequence, maintaining the beneﬁts of chain-of-thought rea- soning while constraining the output space. This structured approach ensures reproducible diagnostic assessments while preserving the model’s ability to articulate clinical reasoning. To facilitate systematic comparison with the generation paradigm used in many recent works [19], [20], [21] as shown in Fig 2(b), a parallel implementation was developed that maintains con- sistent prompt structures with minimal modiﬁcations, ensuring methodological rigor in comparative analysis. A. Prompt Development We designed a standard prompt in which custom enhancement can be introduced while maintaining a union and pharsable out- put, and interference caused by free language can be minimized. Speciﬁcally, the input prompt X of LLM is deﬁned as follows. X = {xins; xnote; xtemplate} (1) It contains three components, where xins represents the task- speciﬁc instruction, xnote is ﬁlled in by a clinical note, and xtemplate stands for detailed output and guidance. Unlike pop- ular prompt design strategies that provide multiple examples to take advantage of the in-context learning ability of LLM [15], we distribute the expected content using xtemplate (Fig. 3). Although examples are crucial for in-context learning as they provide speciﬁc input-output pairs, directly demonstrating the context and the desired output to the LLM, few-shot prompts are less efﬁcient for ICD coding tasks due to the length of the clinical notes (3k tokens per note). Additionally, since diagnoses are heavily dependent on the details within the notes, extending the prompts to include known examples may introduce noises or hallucinations, such as irrelevant medical histories. In this study, we keep the instructions concise and utilize some inspired words in xins and xtemplate to guide the LLM to follow the described output format and reasoning approaches. The overall performance of the prompt depends mainly on the design of the instruction and the template. The proposed structure facilitates fair comparisons and can easily integrate various prompt engi- neering techniques. In this work, we develop two types of prompt, that is, generation-based prompts and veriﬁcation-based prompts, which are categorized according to different xins. Generation-based prompts are open-ended, which requires LLMs to generate all possible ICD items (including codes and disease names) without restrictions. To fully explore its potential, we developed three types of template with different reasoning approaches. Plain Generation serves as the baseline prompt, where the LLM is required to make a decision immedi- ately after the generated ICD item without additional thinking or reﬂection steps. In the advanced prompt, we introduce CoT Generation. By inserting a deduction part between the ICD items and the decision, the LLMs are forced to make the decision after a step-by-step deduction. Details of deduction are described in the xtemplate instead of an exhaustive example. Furthermore, we developed a medically enhanced prompt for this task, called Differential Generation. It is inspired by the progress of the clinic in the real world and has been shown to be effective [30]. We modiﬁed the description of the deduction part, emphasizing diagnoses step-by-step in a differential diagnosis paradigm, while keeping the decision part unchanged. Veriﬁcation-based prompts are aimed at verifying a list of given diagnoses by slightly modifying xins. The xtemplate is composed of all candidate ICD items with repeated require- ments. We also tried a more concentrate format with all can- didates listed in one line with only one requirement at the last of the template, which leads to instruction-following problem. As a result, we keep the descriptions after each of the N can- didates in the template. Similarly, Plain Veriﬁcation and CoT Veriﬁcation represent the baseline and CoT-enhanced prompt in the veriﬁcation setting, respectively. Based on real-world clinical experience, we proposed the evidence-based veriﬁca- tion template as Evidence-based Veriﬁcation. Detailed diagnosis reason and evidence are required to provide before making the ﬁnal decision. To avoid hallucinations like generate nonexistent evidence, we add alternatives of “unavailable”. Restrictions apply. 8540 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 29, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025 Fig. 3. A full list of all prompts utilized to guide LLMs in completing the ICD-coding task is presented. In this study, we evaluate the effects of six types of well-designed prompts. All of the prompts contains three component, i.e. Instruction, Clinical Note and Template. According to the content of Instruction, these prompts are categorized into two classes: Generation-based Prompts and Veriﬁcation-based Prompts. Each of them encompasses three distinct forms depending on the content of the template. Notably, the words in red within the Generation-based Prompts serve as descriptors, instructing the LLM to replace them with the predicted ICD codes and corresponding diseases in the output response. Conversely, the words in green within the Veriﬁcation-based Prompts act as placeholders, which are replaced with candidate ICD codes and their corresponding disease names before invoking the LLM. B. Candidate Selection Strategy The veriﬁcation-based prompt requires a set of candidates of ICD items. A well-deﬁned candidate set serves two purposes: constraining the solution space to reduce hallucination risks while providing sufﬁcient diagnostic context. In this work, we propose to construct candidate set with two kinds of sampling strategy, i.e. model-based sampling and oracle sampling, for different ablation study purposes. Model-based Sampling leverages traditional multiclass classiﬁcation-based ICD-coding models to sampling contextu- ally relevant candidates. Generally, these multiclass classiﬁca- tion methods extract feature matrix V ∈Rd×Nc with a model F with optimized parameter Θ from the input clinical note xnote. Subsequently, a probability vector ˆY is computed for all ICD codes based on the feature matrix V using an additional linear layer followed by a sigmoid transformation. V = F(xnote| Θ) ˆY = σ(βT V + b) V ∈Rd×Nc, ˆY ∈R1×Ncβ ∈Rd×1, b ∈R1×Nc (2) where d represents the feature dimension and Nc denotes the number of ICD codes that the model can classify. Based on the probabilities of the ICD code calculated by a given model, we Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. LI et al.: VERIFICATION IS ALL YOU NEED: PROMPTING LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS FOR ZERO-SHOT CLINICAL CODING 8541 TABLE I COMBINED STATISTICS OF MIMIC-IV AND CODIESP DATASETS propose to construct the candidate set S with the highest ICD code k with the highest probabilities. ˆY = {ˆy1, ˆy2, . . . , ˆyNc} S = {ˆyi | ˆyi ≥ˆy(k)} (3) where ˆY is a set composed of all probability values of vector ˆY , ˆy(k) represents the (k)-th largest number in the set ˆY. This approach incorporates domain expertise implicit in the selected model, ensuring that the candidate set reﬂects clinically plausi- ble diagnoses given the patient’s clinical note. Oracle Sampling evaluates the fundamental diagnostic ca- pabilities of LLMs in a controlled setting. This approach pairs ground-truth ICD codes with unrelated, randomly selected ICD codes for each clinical note, creating a set of mixed candidates S. Y = {y1, y2, . . . , yNc} S = Ω ∪R, |Ω| = |R| (4) where Ω = {yi | yi = 1} is the set of all ground-truth ICD codes and R ⊆Y −Ω is the set of randomly sampled unrelated ICD codes. To balance the set of candidates, we sample unrelated ICD codes with the same number of ground-truth ICD codes, that is |Ω| = |R|. For example, if 10 codes are assigned by human coders, we will randomly sample 10 unassigned codes as negatives. Such a randomized sampling with the oracle ground- truth label helps assess the model’s ability to discriminate be- tween relevant and irrelevant ICD code without the inﬂuence introduced by the selected model in the model-based sampling approach. IV. EXPERIMENTS A. Dataset We built our experiments on MIMIC-IV [13] and CodiEsp [24] datasets. The MIMIC data set is a large-scale EHR database widely utilized in clinical research and medical data processing, which can be accessed by credentialed users. It contains compre- hensive patient information from the intensive care units (ICU) of Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, including demograph- ics, vital signs, laboratory test results, medications, procedures, and detailed clinical notes. The data set has multiple versions, MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV being the most prevalent. MIMIC- IV, the latest version, spans data from 2008 to 2019, comprising more than 431,000 hospital admissions for more than 180,000 unique patients. Unlike MIMIC-III [33], which only includes the ICD-9 codes [34], MIMIC-IV [13] incorporates both the ICD-9 and ICD-10 coding systems, signiﬁcantly increasing the number of available diagnosis and procedure codes. In this work, we focus on the MIMIC-IV discharge note, and only the diagnosis code is used in the evaluation. The statistics for the MIMIC-IV ICD-9 and ICD-10 test sets are presented in Table I. The total number of documents in these sets exceeds 50,000 and the tokens per document are approximately 3000. Given that SOTA LLMs, e.g. GPT-4o, take an average of 15 seconds to process a single note, a complete test would cost substantial computational resources. To facilitate more efﬁcient analysis and ensure the reliability of our conclusions, we randomly selected 250 clinical notes each from the test sets of the MIMIC-IV ICD-9 and ICD-10 datasets to construct our mini-test set. The statistics of the mini test set are also shown in Table I. To avoid biases resulting from distribution differences between the mini-test set and the full test set, we further examined the detailed distribution of various dimensionsinbothsets.AsillustratedinTableI,thedistributions are essentially consistent. CodiEsp [24] is a publicly available dataset designed for the automatic coding of Spanish clinical case reports. All docu- ments are in Spanish, and CIE10 is the coding terminology (it is the Spanish version of ICD10-CM). Introduced as part of the eHealth CLEF 2020 challenge, it comprises 1,000 clinical notes, manually annotated with CIE10. The dataset is organized into three sub-tasks: CodiEsp-D for diagnosis code assignment, CodiEsp-P for procedure code assignment, and CodiEsp-X, which emphasizes explainability by identifying text fragments that justify assigned codes. With a total of 10,711 annotations across 2,925 unique ICD-10 codes, the corpus is divided into 500 training documents, 250 development documents, and 250 test documents. The CodiEsp dataset also has a corresponding English version, which is translated from its Spanish version. The statistics for the CodiEsp test sets in both Spanish and English are presented in Table I. In this work, we used the full set of CodiEsp-D for evaluation. B. Experimental Setups 1) Veriﬁcation-Based Prompting Experiments: To validate the capability of LLMs to perform the ICD coding task, we ﬁrst apply direct generation (plain generation) and veriﬁcation (plain veriﬁcation and evidence-based veriﬁcation) prompts to Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Downloaded on March 24,2026 at 08:15:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 8542 IEEE JOURNAL OF BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH INFORMATICS, VOL. 29, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025 the notes in the MIMIC-IV-ICD10-mini dataset and evaluate the performance of various LLM responses. The LLMs tested in this experiment vary in size and source, including both open-source and commercial models. In veriﬁcation-based prompts, the top 50 candidate clinial codes are acquired from the model-based sampling with PLM-ICD, ensuring a high-quality set of candi- dates for veriﬁcation. The PLM-ICD model checkpoint used for sampling is provided by [35]. To derive more convincing conclusions, in addition to the plain generation prompt, we conduct a more detailed ablation study on various advanced prompts with the most powerful GPT-4o model. Based on the prompt development framework described in Section III-A, we evaluated 6 distinct prompts, as illustrated in Fig. 3, for the ICD coding task on the MIMIC-IV- ICD10-mini dataset. 2) Zero-Shot Generalization Experiments on Test Dataset: To fairly compare the performance of LLMs and classiﬁcation- based non-LLM models in the zero-shot ICD coding task, we evaluate their performance on the CodiEsp English dataset. Tra- ditional classiﬁcation-based non-LLM models include methods such as CAML [25], LAAT [27], and PLM-ICD [10]. These classiﬁcation methods are evaluated using the model parameters and the evaluation code reproduced from [35]. Particularly, we adopt their best checkpoints, which are trained on MIMIC-IV, and perform evaluation on CodiEsp English. For LLMs, we con- duct experiments using the evidence-based veriﬁcation prompt to achieve the best performance. The candidate diagnostic codes usedintheveriﬁcationpromptarealsogeneratedthroughmodel- based sampling. 3) Versatile Cross-Domain Generalization Experiments: We examine the generalizability of LLM in the ICD coding task in different scenarios. To eliminate the impact of candidate selection on LLM veriﬁcation performance, we employ oracle sampling to select candidate codes. For non-LLM methods, we directly extract the classiﬁcation scores corresponding to the candidate codes and calculate the result metrics accord- ingly. More speciﬁcally, we reused the models and the same hyper-parameters, e.g. the decision boundary, as referenced in [35]. Based on this, when using a non-LLM classiﬁcation model for veriﬁcation tasks, we only need to compare whether the score output corresponding to the code in the candidate set reaches the decision boundary to conﬁrm the assignment of the code. By exposing the same candidate codes to both LLM and non-LLM methods, we ensure the fairness of the experiment. In this set of experiments, we focus on evaluating the gener- alization performance of LLM-based and non-LLM based ICD coding frameworks across different institutions (MIMIC-IV vs. CodiEsp), languages (CodiESP Spanish vs. CodiESP English), and ICD versions (MIMIC-IV-ICD9 vs. MIMIC-IV-ICD10). All LLM results are driven by evidence-based instructions to complete the ICD coding task, while classiﬁcation methods use the original model parameters and conﬁgurations for evaluation. C. Implement Details The experiments are conducted on both commercial and open source LLMs, including multiple series of models like ChatGPT, Llama [36], and Qwen [37]. For commercial LLMs, we select the latest versions of OpenAI’s ChatGPT (0613) and GPT-4o (2024-05-13) for evaluation and comparison. For open source LLMs, we evaluate models from the Llama series, including Llama3.1-8B, Llama3.1-70B, and Llama3.1-405B, as well as versions from the Qwen series, including Qwen2-7B and Qwen2-72B. All open source LLMs were deployed on a local cluster of 8× NVIDIA A100 80G GPUs in accordance with data licensing. Following the guidelines from MIMIC regarding the responsible use of MIMIC data with online services like GPT, the usage of ChatGPT and GPT-4o APIs is supported by Microsoft Azure. D. Metrics We report several widely used metrics in our experiments: mi- cro and macro scores of precision, recall and F1. Micro-scoring aggregates the contributions of all labels by considering the total TP (true positive), FP (false positive) and FN (false negative). The formulas for micro precsion, recall, F1 are as follows: Pmicro = N i=1 TPi N i=1 TPi + FPi Rmicro = N i=1 TPi N i=1 TPi + FNi F1micro = 2 × Pmicro × Rmicro Pmicro + Rmicro (5) In contrast, macro scores are calculated by averaging metrics computed per label. This makes macro-scores particularly use- ful for datasets with label imbalance. The formulas for macro precsion, recall, and F1 are as follows: Pmacro = 1 Nc Nc  i=1 TPi TPi + FPi Rmacro = 1 Nc Nc  i=1 Pi TPi + FNi F1macro = 2 × Pmacro × Rmacro Pmacro + Rmacro (6) Both metrics are essential for evaluation of model performance in ICD coding tasks. V. RESULTS A. LLMs are Poor Code Generators but Good Veriﬁers Table II presents the results of different models and prompt types in the MIMIC-IV-ICD10-mini data set. Firstly, across all tests, LLMs perform poorly with the plain generation prompt, whereas the results of LLMs based on the plain veriﬁcation prompt show signiﬁcant improvements in both micro and macro F1 scores. When the evidence-based veriﬁcation prompt is utilized, the performance of LLMs is further enhanced. The best results for the same model under different prompts are underlined. Except for the Qwen2-7B model, all other mod- els achieve optimal micro and macro F1 scores when using evidence-based veriﬁcation prompts. Additionally, in most cases within the same model series, larger models achieve higher metrics. Notably, even the weakest LLM in our tests, i.e., Qwen2-7B, surpasses GPT-4o’s plain generation performance when using veriﬁcation-based prompts. We also display the baseline metrics of the candidate codes used by all veriﬁcation-based prompts, which consist of the top 50 high score predicted codes from the traditional SOTA method PLM-ICD. Veriﬁcation-based prompts achieve signiﬁ- cantly better ICD coding results, partly due to the introduction of candidates, which provide reference information and reduce the task’s difﬁculty to some extent. It is noteworthy that all LLM modelsfurtherimprovethemicroF1andmacroF1metricsbased on the candidates. Therefore, we argue that while LLMs are poor ICDcodegenerators, theyaregoodcodeveriﬁers. Their inability to perform the ICD coding task well is not due to a lack of clinical knowledge, but rather the need for reasonable use and leverage of their capabilities. The results of the ablation study, which examine the impact of different prompts on model performance, are presented in Table IV. Incorporating CoT into the generation-based prompts for ICD coding task has mixed effects on the model’s precision, recall, and F1 scores, both in micro and macro metrics. However, the overall impacts are very limited and the performance metrics still show a signiﬁcant gap compared to the veriﬁcation-based prompts.Therefore,weconcludethatLLMsareindeedpoorICD code generators even with more advanced prompt techniques. In the veriﬁcation-based scenario, the integration of CoT prompts also shows a slight improvement in precision, recall, and F1 scores compared to direct veriﬁcation. Evidence-based veriﬁcation consistently outperforms both direct veriﬁcation and CoT-enhanced veriﬁcation. This indicates that in tasks such as ICD coding, reasoning directly based on speciﬁc candidate evidence is more effective than open-ended reasoning chains like CoT. The model beneﬁts more from evidence retrieval rather than free-form and general reasoning, which also aligns better with the structured nature of medical coding. A. Are Reasoning LLMs Better Clinical Code Veriﬁers?: Recent advancements in reasoning LLMs, such as OpenAI’s o1 [38] and DeepSeek-R1 [23], have utilized reinforcement learning to optimize the long-chain thinking capacity of LLMs. These enhancements enable LLMs to perform step-by-step rea- soning, self-correction, and retrospective analysis akin to that of human experts, thus signiﬁcantly improving their ability to solve complex problems. A pertinent question arises: can these reasoning-augmented LLMs better accomplish tasks such as ICD code generation or veriﬁcation? Given the consideration of model invocation costs, this paper presents a detailed evaluation of the DeepSeek-R1 reasoning model and its non-reasoning base model DeepSeek-V3. The results are depicted in Table III. From the table, it is evident that the proposed veriﬁcation- based prompts are effective across both reasoning and non- reasoning models, outperforming generation-based prompts. As healthcare systems migrate from older coding stan- dards like ICD-9 to newer, more detailed systems like ICD-10, the complexity of diagnosis codes increases, creating potential challengesformodelstrainedononeversiontoadapttotheother. Ensuring the model’s cross-version generalizability is crucial for application scenarios where medical data spans multiple time periods or regions. Therefore, We assess the performance of both closed-sourcemodels, suchas GPT-4o, andopen-sourcemodels, including Llama and Qwen, to determine their ability to gener- alize between ICD-9 and ICD-10. To ensure a fair comparison of the cross-version performance between traditional classiﬁers and LLMs, we employ a tool [39] to transfer the outputs from traditional classiﬁers trained on the ICD-9 version labels to the ICD-10 version. The results from the MIMIC-IV-ICD10 (Table VIII) and MIMIC-IV-ICD9 (Table X) experiments highlight the strong cross-ICD version generalization capabilities of LLMs. GPT- 4o and Llama3.1-405B consistently lead across both datasets, achieving the highest micro and macro F1 scores. With the performance in ICD-10 (micro F1: 0.8193, macro F1: 0.6543) and ICD-9 (micro F1: 0.8349, macro F1: 0.6553), GPT-4o demonstrates its robust adaptability, allowing it to handle both frequent and rare diagnoses effectively across different coding systems. The minimal variance in performance between ICD-9 and ICD-10 indicates GPT-4o’s capability to generalize seam- lessly between the two versions, ensuring consistent perfor- mance even as medical coding systems evolve. Llama3.1-70B and Qwen2-72B belong to the second tier, follow closely behind with micro and macro F1 scores that are competitive in both datasets. Traditional classiﬁers like PLM-ICD, CAML, and LAAT also demonstrate considerable limitations in cross-version tasks. When testing the ICD9-trained model on MIMIC-IV-ICD10- mini dataset, signiﬁcant drops across all models are observed as shown in Table XI. By applying the proposed evidence-based veriﬁcation method with GPT-4o, all methods achieve consistent performance improvements. VI. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK In this study, we proposed a novel code veriﬁcation paradigm for LLMs to deal with the ICDcoding task. Speciﬁcally, we input a candidate code set together with the original clinical notes for LLMs. We then require LLMs to verify each code within the candidate set using prompting techniques. By comprehen- sively evaluating the capabilities of LLMs in clinical coding tasks, we found that when LLMs are utilized as code veriﬁers rather than code generators, their performance in ICD coding tasks improves signiﬁcantly. Veriﬁcation-based prompts consis- tently outperform direct generation-based methods across vari- ous LLMs, including both commercial and open-source models. Furthermore, LLMs exhibit superior generalization capabilities across institutions, languages, and ICD versions compared to traditional classiﬁcation models. These key insights revealed in our study have important implications for future research and practical applications. It is particularly important for clinical applications where data is scarce and the distribution of data varies signiﬁcantly. Despite the advantages offered by LLM-based code veriﬁers, challenges remain in current approaches. First, the efﬁcacy of the pro- posed framework depends on the selection of the candidate set, which imposes a constraint on the maximum achievable end-to-end performance. Second, the coding effectiveness of current LLMs still requires further improvement, especially for coding assignments involving rare diseases. In the future, we plan to enhance our framework by exploring multi-agent col- laboration and retrieval augmented generation methods. These emerging techniques have the potential to address the challenges mentioned above.

file_name: When Machine Unlearning Meets Retrieval-Augmented Generation -RAG- Keep Secret or Forget Knowledge-.pdf
text: 2030 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON DEPENDABLE AND SECURE COMPUTING, VOL. 23, NO. 2, MARCH/APRIL 2026 When Machine Unlearning Meets Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): Keep Secret or Forget Knowledge? Shang Wang , Tianqing Zhu , Dayong Ye , and Wanlei Zhou , Life Fellow, IEEE Abstract—The deployment of large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT and Gemini has shown their powerful natural language generation capabilities. However, these models can inadvertently learn and retain sensitive information and harmful content dur- ing training, raising signiﬁcant ethical and legal concerns. To ad- dress these issues, machine unlearning has been introduced as a potential solution. While existing unlearning methods take into account the speciﬁc characteristics of LLMs, they often suffer from high computational demands, limited applicability, or the risk of catastrophic forgetting. To address these limitations, we propose a lightweight behavioral unlearning framework based on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) technology. By modifying the external knowledge base of RAG, we simulate the effects of forgettingwithoutdirectlyinteractingwiththeunlearnedLLM.We approach the construction of unlearned knowledge as a constrained optimization problem, deriving two key components that under- pin the effectiveness of RAG-based unlearning. This RAG-based approach is particularly effective for closed-source LLMs, where existing unlearning methods often fail. We evaluate our framework through extensive experiments on both open-source and closed- source models, including ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama-2-7b-chat, and PaLM 2. The results demonstrate that our approach meets ﬁve key unlearning criteria: effectiveness, universality, harmlessness, simplicity, and robustness. Meanwhile, this approach can extend to multimodal large language models and LLM-based agents. Index Terms—Large language models (LLMs), machine unlearning, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). I. INTRODUCTION L ARGE language models (LLMs) have transformed the ﬁeld of natural language generation, playing a vital role in advancing technology across various industries [1], [2]. Models like the GPT series, which are large-scale neural networks, have learned the complexities of language—both semantics and Received 19 October 2024; revised 20 August 2025; accepted 7 October 2025. Date of publication 20 October 2025; date of current version 12 March 2026. This work was supported by the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR, China, under Grant fdct0080/2024/RIA2. (Corresponding author: Tianqing Zhu.) Shang Wang is with the School of Computer Science, University of Technol- ogy Sydney, Ultimo, NSW 2007, Australia. Tianqing Zhu, Dayong Ye, and Wanlei Zhou are with the Faculty of Data Science, City University of Macau, Macao 999078, China (e-mail: tqzhu@cityu.edu.mo). Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TDSC.2025.3620832 syntax—by training on vast datasets, enabling them to excel in a wide range of language tasks [3], [4]. Currently, the train- ing of LLMs typically follows a ‘pre-training and ﬁne-tuning’ paradigm [5]. However, both stages may inadvertently result in the model learning sensitive information or harmful content [6], [7], [8], which raises concerns about privacy violations, copy- right infringement, or the generation of harmful content. To address the privacy or harmful content concerns, machine unlearning has been proposed as a method to remove the in- ﬂuence of speciﬁc data from a model without requiring full re- training [12]. Therefore, LLM unlearning has become a research hotspot [10], [11], [13]. It hypothesizes that the unlearned LLM is statistically indistinguishable from one retrained without the target data. Ideal LLM unlearning aims to identify and remove parameters associated with target knowledge, known as exact unlearning. However, this is limited by the poor interpretability of LLMs. Some efforts have shifted toward observable LLM outputs [10], [11], [14], [15], leading to a form of approximate unlearning. It assumes that the unlearned LLM exhibits different behavior from the original model on the target knowledge, while behaving similarly in the remaining knowledge. This is referred to as behavioral unlearning [16], where the goal is to hide target knowledge from the LLM’s behavior by modifying inputs or output logits. However,theseLLMunlearningschemeshaveinherentweak- nesses. First, the schemes involving ﬁne-tuning LLM introduce considerable computational overhead, like gradient ascent, so they cannot adapt to frequent unlearning requests. Second, closed-source LLMs only provide their interfaces, making some schemes unsuitable. Third, LLMs possess emergent abilities, which means that forgetting must extend beyond speciﬁc data to include related content. Shumailov et al. [17] found that LLMs can recall previously forgotten knowledge through their in- context abilities, exposing a vulnerability in existing unlearning schemes to this ‘un-unlearning’ phenomenon. Furthermore, some unlearning schemes may reduce the overall utility of the model, potentially leading to catastrophic forgetting [18]. In Table I, we list three representative LLM unlearning schemes and their weaknesses. To tackle above weaknesses of LLM unlearning, we ﬁnd that Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a new emerged 1545-5971 © 2025 IEEE. WANG et al.: WHEN MACHINE UNLEARNING MEETS RETRIEVAL-AUGMENTED GENERATION (RAG): KEEP SECRET OR FORGET KNOWLEDGE? 2031 TABLE I THE COMPARISON OF VARIOUS LLM UNLEARNING SCHEMES Fig. 1. The intuition of RAG-based unlearning. This ﬁgure illustrates knowl- edge changes in three different scenarios. Figure (a) shows the main purpose of RAG, which is to correct misinformation knowledge in the LLM. Figure (b) replaces the misinformation knowledge with the knowledge to be forgotten. In Figure (c), it is an ideal unlearning process. technology that does not need to change any parameter but with adjusting the output of LLMs [19], [20]. This special characteristic can be applied in machine unlearning. In general, the RAG workﬂow comprises two primary components: an information retrieval module and a text generation module [21], [22]. The retrieval module ﬁrst retrieves relevant information from a knowledge base based on the input. The LLM then uses this retrieved information to generate the ﬁnal response. This process can effectively improve the quality of outputs, reduce the likelihood of misinformation and hallucinations, and, most importantly, hide the target information to implement behavioral unlearning without revising the parameters. As illustrated in Fig. 1(a), RAG can revise the misinformation knowledge in the external knowledge base, effectively correct- ing it in LLM. When regarding misinformation as a forgotten target, the scalability of RAG technology offers a promising solution for LLM unlearning. In Fig. 1(b), through adding conﬁ- dentialityrequirementsofthetargetknowledgeintheknowledge base, RAG can hide the forgotten target’s information without requiring access to the unlearned LLM. Fig. 1(c) indicates that an ideal unlearning scheme would remove the target knowledge in LLM, thus not generating its related content. Fortunately, leveraging RAG to keep the forgotten target conﬁdential serves a similar function to behavioral unlearning. Combined with the idea of modifying the input, we leverage RAG technology to propose a novel behavioral LLM unlearning framework. Speciﬁcally, it treats unlearning the target data as a constrained optimization problem for constructing its unlearned knowledge. The knowledge consists of a retrieval component and a constraint component. For each target, we use a heuristic method to build relevant knowledge (i.e., retrieval component) and conﬁdentiality requirements (i.e., constraint component). To improve the effectiveness of unlearning, we apply prompt engineering to optimize both components. Our approach only modiﬁes the external knowledge base to implement behavioral unlearning. This ﬂexibility allows our framework to be applied to both open-source and closed-source models. Furthermore, we can utilize the target LLM to acquire additional knowledge rel- evant to the target data, thereby addressing the ‘un-unlearning’ phenomenon. Meanwhile, RAG serves as an an external plug-in that will not affect the model utility when inputs are not related to the knowledge base. This ensures that our scheme can easily land in various LLMs with minimal side effects and low overhead. In our experiment, we focused on two key unlearning ob- jectives: trained samples and learned concepts. RAG-based unlearning was implemented in both open-source and closed- source models, including GPT-4o, Gemini, and Llama-2-7b- chat. We evaluated our unlearning framework comprehensively across ﬁve key dimensions: effectiveness, universality, harm- lessness, simplicity, and robustness, and found that the frame- work performed well in each dimension. Especially, we explored the resistance of our scheme against challenging and practical scenarios, such as jailbreak attacks and adaptive attacks. Fur- thermore, we extended RAG-based unlearning to multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and LLM-based agents, and achieved effective forgetting effects. In summary, RAG-based unlearning enables model providers to control the knowledge boundaries of LLMs by managing external knowledge bases, thereby safeguarding privacy, pro- tecting copyrighted data, and eliminating harmful content. Our primary contributions are as follows: r We are the ﬁrst to propose a behavioral unlearning frame- work leveraging RAG technology, which can be applied to many LLMs. r In LLM unlearning, we achieve high-quality unlearning of both samples and concepts across diverse scenarios, with state-of-the-arts (SOTA) performance. r Our RAG-based unlearning framework outperforms three representative LLM unlearning schemes on ﬁve critical dimensions: effectiveness, universality, harmlessness, sim- plicity, and robustness. r Our framework can achieve LLM unlearning within closed-source LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o), a capability lacking in other schemes. Furthermore, our approach is adaptable to MLLMs and LLM-based agents. II. BACKGROUND A. Preliminary 1) The Deﬁnition of Machine Unlearning: Assume a ma- chine unlearning process as U(·). A data contributor wants to withdraw its data DU that is a subset of the training set DAll. That is removing the contribution of DU from the trained model F(DAll). In this case, U(·) intends to preserve the remaining subset DR = DAll \ DU in F. Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. The overview of RAG technology. The left ﬁgure gives the workﬂow of a regular RAG framework. The right ﬁgure details the workﬂow of RAG-based unlearning. Deﬁnition 1 (Machine Unlearning): When the data con- tributor makes an unlearning request, the unlearning process U(F, DU, DAll) aims to remove the inﬂuence of its dataset DU from the trained model F. Usually, U(F, DU, DAll) will return a new model FU that performs as if it has never seen the revoked data. We cannot determine whether FU can correctly predict the input xi ∈DU. However, for each xj ∈DR, F(xj) = FU(xj) is necessary. There are two types of machine unlearning. The ﬁrst is exact unlearning, and it guarantees that the distribution of FU and that of a model retrained on DR are indistinguishable. In prac- tice, exact unlearning can only be implemented for tiny-scale models. Therefore, approximate unlearning comes up and only guarantees that the activation results of FU and that of a re- trained model are indistinguishable. Clearly, LLMs are suitable for approximate unlearning due to their complex architectures. Notably, the output of LLMs differs from that of traditional models, Deﬁnition 1 cannot be extended to LLM unlearning. We will detail it in Section III-B. 2) The Workﬂow of RAG: By incorporating RAG, we can manipulate the output of LLM. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the RAG framework operates as follows. Prior to model deployment, knowledge experts acquire speciﬁc knowledge from various sources, such as the internet and knowledge communities [23]. This collected knowledge is stored in the form of documents, which are continuously updated. During inference, a user does not directly query the LLM, and its input prompt is ﬁrst re- trieved from this external knowledge base. For example, when the prompt includes content related to ‘Detective Conan’, the retriever will ﬁnd the most relevant information, such as ‘De- tective Conan is a mystery comic... ’. The retriever then uses a prompt template to combine the retrieved content with the original input. When offering this new prompt, the LLM will regard the retrieved knowledge as context, thus affecting the output results. B. Related Work 1) Machine Unlearning: Machine unlearning has become a crucial technique for addressing various safety issues, such as privacy protection and copyright protection. It originates from the right to be forgotten, which grants users the right to request that a model provider remove the inﬂuence of its data from the model. Currently, machine unlearning has expanded into various ﬁelds, including image classiﬁcation [24], [25], and graph neural networks [26]. In small-scale models, unlearning requests in- clude sample unlearning [27], class unlearning [28], and feature unlearning [29]. To achieve these goals, data reorganization and model manipulation are employed. In the ﬁrst approach, model providers will delete or modify the target data. For example, Bourtoule et al. developed a machine unlearning framework called SISA [24]. For model manipulation, model shifting, and model pruning are used to affect the knowledge boundary re- garding the target data. For instance, Wang et al. [30] proposed a class-unlearning scheme by quantifying the class discrimination of different channels in convolutional neural networks. 2) Existing LLM Unlearning: Different from machine un- learning, LLM unlearning remains in an exploratory phase. There are three approximate LLM unlearning schemes: retraining-based, offset-based and input-based. In the ﬁrst man- ner, Yao et al. [9] used a gradient ascent algorithm to increase the LLM’s loss on the target, causing the output to deviate from the original content. Similarly, Eldan et al. [13] re-labeled the forgotten data as non-sensitive responses, thereby minimizing their likelihoods. These unlearning schemes require accessing and manipulating LLMs and cannot be applied to closed-source LLMs. In the second manner, Huang et al. [10] employed logit shifting with two small-scale models to modify the LLM’s out- put. When given a forgotten sample, the shift prevents the LLM from generating sensitive information. However, this scheme relies on the performance of small models and requires precise logit shifting calculations. Input-based unlearning is popular, Pawelczyk et al. [11] leveraged the contextual capabilities of LLMs to implement an in-context unlearning method. They guided the model’s behavior by constructing counterfactual instances of the target data. However, this scheme increases the risk of privacy leaks, as it requires storing sensitive in- stances. Notably, the latter two unlearning schemes fall under behavioral unlearning, as they constrain the output of target knowledge without altering the LLM’s parameters. WANG et al.: WHEN MACHINE UNLEARNING MEETS RETRIEVAL-AUGMENTED GENERATION (RAG): KEEP SECRET OR FORGET KNOWLEDGE? 2033 3) Veriﬁcation for Machine Unlearning: The purpose of ver- iﬁcation is to ensure that the model cannot reveal information about the target data after unlearning operations. The model should closely resemble its counterpart that has never seen the target data. Currently, several veriﬁcation methods for machine unlearning exist, including retraining time [24], privacy and se- curity attacks [31], and accuracy [32]. The ﬁrst manner measures how quickly the unlearned model can relearn the forgotten data. If the model can recover its original performance in a very short time, this suggests that it may still retain information about the forgotten data. Furthermore, several representative membership inference attacks (MIAs) were applied to verify machine un- learning, such as LOSS [33], ZLib [34], GradNorm [35] and MinK [36]. In a successful demonstration of unlearning, MIAs determine the target data to be ‘in’ for the pre-unlearned model and ‘out’ for the post-unlearned model. Similarly, Guo et al. [31] used backdoored data as the target and calculated the drop of attack success rate. Literature [32] used an accuracy-based veriﬁcation method, comparing the output of the model after unlearning with that of a model that has never seen the target data. Prior studies [11], [35] have used MIAs to assess residual memorization in LLMs. We also applied this method to evaluate the effectiveness of LLM unlearning, as detailed in Section V-A. 4) Retrieval-Augmented Generation: RAG in LLMs is an enhancement method that combines retrieval and generation techniques. Compared to regular LLMs, RAG-based models are effective at handling complex tasks, especially in contexts that require detailed background knowledge and speciﬁc facts. Speciﬁcally, by introducing a retrieval process, RAG-based models can incorporate the most relevant information related to input prompts, reducing factual errors during the generation pro- cess. Additionally, RAG techniques are well-suited for handling multi-turn dialogues [37]. Currently, some researchers improved RAG techniques regarding retrieval process and generation pro- cess. Jiang et al. [38] optimized the retrieval algorithm for faster and more accurate information extraction. While Siriwardhana et al. [39] focused on the generation process to better integrate and utilize the retrieved knowledge. III. PROBLEM DEFINITION A. Notations To facilitate the understanding of the deﬁnition and algorithm in this work, the important symbols are listed in Table II. B. The Deﬁnition of Behavioral LLM Unlearning Due to the poor interpretability of LLMs, relying on internal parameters or activation values for unlearning is impractical, such as exact unlearning [24]. Following recent studies [10], [11], we focused instead on the model’s observable output behavior, known as behavioral unlearning. This assumes that the unlearned LLM behaves differently from the original model on target knowledge, while remaining consistent on unrelated knowledge. LLM unlearning involves two primary objectives: sample-level [11] and concept-level [9] knowledge. We deﬁne behavioral unlearning with respect to each. TABLE II NOTATIONS Deﬁnition 2 (Sample Unlearning): It aims to eliminate the inﬂuence of speciﬁc trained samples. Given a set of forgetting samples Esample = {xi|i = 1, 2, .., N} taken from the train- ing data of LLM A(; θ), the sample unlearning function U(·) makes A(; θ) forget all information from Esample. For each xi ∈ Esample, U(A(xi; θ)) is substantially different from A(xi; θ). In addition, we hope U(·) can not compromise performance on the data outside Esample. Deﬁnition 3 (Concept Unlearning): Unlike U(·), it aims to forget speciﬁc knowledge, such as ‘potato’. Given a set of forgotten concepts Econcept = {Ci|i = 1, 2, .., N} known by LLM A(; θ), the concept unlearning function U(·) makes A(; θ) forgetthecollectedconcepts.Informally,foreachCi ∈Econcept, G(Ci) can generate a question about Ci, and U(A(G(Ci); θ)) makes A(G(Ci); θ) far deviate from the original result. Simi- larly, we hope U(A(; θ)) still normally responds to other con- cepts. Notably, behavioral unlearning is not intended to replace other unlearning schemes. Rather, it can serve as a component in pri- vacy protection and content copyright control, complementing other schemes. In this work, we adopt RAG to constrain the behavior of the LLM, achieving approximate LLM unlearning. C. Threat Model In contrast to traditional unlearning, we consider real-world unlearning scenarios for LLMs, including privacy protection, copyright compliance, and harmful content removal. The ﬁrst scenario primarily involves sample unlearning, whereas the latter two involve concept unlearning. As illustrated in Fig. 3, both types of requests typically arise after model deployment. In such cases, a data contributor may request the withdrawal of speciﬁc samples (partial training data) or concepts (sets of re- lated training data). The model provider then applies behavioral unlearning schemes to suppress the model’s ability to generate responses containing the target knowledge. After unlearning, normal users can no longer access this knowledge through the LLM’s outputs. Under this threat model, we detail the capabilities of the user and the model provider. r Model Provider: For sample unlearning, the provider can identify the speciﬁc samples to be forgotten, but does not require access to the LLM’s parameters. For concept unlearning, the provider knows only the target concepts, without needing the corresponding samples. In both cases, the provider can integrate an external RAG system into the LLM to augment the input prompts and faithfully follow the RAG pipeline. In this context, the provider has full control over the retriever conﬁguration, knowledge base construction, and prompt template design. This assumption supports the applicability of our scheme to a wide range of real-world LLMs. r Normal Users: They interact with the LLM through its interface, submitting arbitrary text inputs to make the LLM respond as instructed. Moreover, they cannot access LLM parameters, intermediate activations, or bypass the RAG pipeline (e.g., direct access to the base LLM is disallowed). Under this setting, the behavioral suppression we adopt completely blocks access to target knowledge, ensuring valid unlearning. D. LLM Unlearning Criteria As illustrated in Section II-B2, existing LLM unlearning schemes have the following drawbacks. First, the unlearning process involves complex operations and prior knowledge, mak- ing it difﬁcult to scale across various LLMs, particularly for closed-source models. Second, these unlearning schemes con- sume signiﬁcant computational resources, leading to unafford- able costs. Third, these unlearning algorithms may impact LLM utility, potentially leading to catastrophic forgetting. Finally, they cannot effectively implement both sample unlearning and concept unlearning. To overcome them, we use RAG technology to embed conﬁdential instructions, thereby achieving the desired forgetting effect. Here are the ﬁve criteria of our scheme: r Effectiveness: Our scheme can effectively fulﬁll two un- learningrequests,includingsampleunlearningandconcept unlearning. Fig. 4. The prompt template of RAG, where ‘knowledge’ is the related content of the original prompt. r Universality: Our scheme can provide unlearning services for various LLMs, including even closed-source models. Moreover, Our scheme can also be applied to MLLMs and LLM-based agents. r Harmlessness: Our scheme does not require modifying model parameters, not affecting model utility. r Simplicity: Our scheme does not require complex oper- ations and prior knowledge, and only consumes a few computationalresourcestoimplementunlearningrequests. r Robustness: Our scheme can be effective against some potential threats, such as jailbreak [40], [41] and prompt injection attacks [1]. IV. METHODOLOGY A. The Overview of RAG-Based Unlearning We propose a lightweight RAG-based unlearning framework for LLMs. This scheme relies on RAG technology, serving as a behavioral unlearning plugin for both closed-source and open- source LLMs. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the model provider only manipulates the RAG part. For the target data, she constructs its unlearned knowledge containing a conﬁdentiality requirement. Given a prompt about the target data, the retriever can return the carefully designed knowledge. Then, a prompt template inte- grates the target data with the knowledge. In this case, the LLM will take into account the conﬁdentiality requirement described in the knowledge, thus effectively achieving sample and concept unlearning. It is worth noting that the manual creation of the template is sufﬁcient. In this work, the framework is detailed in Fig. 4. It consists of three parts: instruction description, input prompt and retrieved knowledge. When an input prompt hits the knowledge base, the unlearned LLM must respond to the input based on the retrieved content; otherwise, must respond directly to the input. In practical scenarios, we consider a more challenging threat model in which an adversarial user knows the RAG-based un- learning framework. Leveraging this knowledge, the adversary crafts adaptive prompts to bypass the system’s conﬁdentiality guarantees. We detailed this threat model in Section VII-A and provided a thorough analysis of its potential impact on the effectiveness of our scheme. B. Identify Key Optimization Based on the threat model, we formally deﬁne the RAG-based unlearning process and regard it as a constrained optimization problem. Taking concept unlearning as an example, suppose we have a set of concepts Econcept = {Ci|i = 1, 2, . . ., N} that Authorized licensed use limited to: Manav Rachna International Institute of Research and Studies. Our idea is to generate the relevant knowledge Ki with the conﬁdentiality requirement for each concept Ci, thus obtaining an unlearned knowledge set U.K.= {Ki|i = 1, 2, . . ., N}. In RAGframework, theknowledgeexpert combinesBKwithU.K.. When given a concept Cj ∈Econcept, the relevant knowledge Kj ∈U.K. is retrieved as its context, where the conﬁdentiality requirement ensures that the LLM A(; θ) cannot generate the related content. Speciﬁcally, we formalize this process as the following optimization problem: arg max U.K. 1 N N  i=1 V erify(A(G(Ci), Γ(G(Ci); U.K. ∪BK); θ)) s.t., Γ(G(Ci); U.K. ∪BK) = Retrieve(G(Ci), f, U.K. ∪BK) (1) where V erify(·) is a discriminator that can determine whether A(; θ) has forgotten Ci, G(·) is a generator that can create related questions for each concept, and Γ(G(Ci); U.K. ∪BK) is a retriever that can extract related knowledge Ki of G(Ci) from U.K. ∪BK. To forget Econcept, the optimized function focuses on the unlearned knowledge set U.K., achieving a large value in (1). C. Design of Unlearned Knowledge As detailed in Section IV-B, the generation of unlearned knowledge is crucial. Speciﬁcally, for a given target sample or concept, we construct relevant knowledge that includes a con- ﬁdentiality requirement. When the RAG framework is applied to an LLM, its responses to the target data will deviate from the original content. Regarding the workﬂow of RAG, the unlearned knowledge corresponding to a target data comprises a retrieval component and a constraint component. As shown in (1), the ﬁrst component corresponds to function Γ(·), while the constraint component corresponds to A(; θ). The ﬁrst component Q ensures that unlearned knowledge can be retrieved. Otherwise, the LLM will respond directly to the original prompts, thus failing to maintain conﬁdentiality. The second component P is designed to restrict the LLM’s behavior. For a target c, our goal is to craft its unlearned knowledge k that incorporates P and Q, the process is described in Algo- rithm 1—function Unlearned Knowledge Generation. When k is used as the context of c, the LLM will provide a conﬁdentiality response, such as refusing to answer. Obviously, optimizing the two components is challenging. We adopt a simple approach that concatenates the two non-overlapping components. Formally, k = P + Q, where ‘+’ denotes the textual concatenation. First, we craft Q to induce the LLM to provide a refusal response, such as ‘Sorry, I cannot generate the related content due to copyright issues.’. Next, given Q, we further build P to satisfy the retrieval condition. The unlearned knowledge encompasses both signiﬁcant components, as illustrated in Fig. 3. 1) Crafting Constraint Component Q: When component Q is used as context, the LLM will refuse to respond to the prompt G(c) containing c. The key is to ensure that the LLM under- stands the conﬁdentiality requirement embedded in Q, regard- less of the prompt G(c). A straightforward approach resembles prompt engineering, where Q is constructed through optimiza- tion. However, there are three challenges with this approach. First, model providers may not have access to the parameters in LLM, especially in the case of closed-source models. Second, even in a white-box setting, where LLM access is possible, the computational resources are prohibitively high. Third, it remains uncertain whether an optimized Q would be effective across different LLMs. To address these challenges, we employ a heuristic approach that does not rely on optimization. The detail is displayed in Algorithm 1—function Craft Constraint. Speciﬁcally, we use an additional LLMcons (such as GPT-4o) to generate Q. For c, we instruct the GPT-4o to generate Q containing a conﬁdentiality requirement. For example, we use the following prompt: This is my target concept/sample: [target]. Please craft a conﬁdentiality requirement that prevents an AI assistant from generating content about [target]. Limit the text to a maximum of V words. Here, V speciﬁes the length of Q to conserve computational resources. In the function, using crafted Q as context, LLMun is prompted to generate a response R for a prompt related to c. If R is still related to c, Q is regenerated until LLMun refuses to respond or the maximum attempt (i.e., T 1). In general, a single query is sufﬁcient to craft a usable Q. Taking ‘Harry Potter’ as an example, the constraint component is as follows: The AI assistant is prohibited from generating any content relatedtoHarryPotter,includingbutnotlimitedtocharacters, plotlines, terminology, locations, magical creatures, and any related or derivative content. This restriction applies to any form of textual description, discussion, or reference. The instruction is of the highest priority. 2) Crafting Retrieval Component P: To ensure effective re- trieval, we aim for P to cover as much relevant content about c as possible. Next, we construct P for both the target sample and concept. The detail is displayed in Algorithm 1—function Craft Retrieval. Target Sample: In this case, model providers have access to the trained samples Esample = {xi|i = 1, 2, . . ., N} that need to be forgotten. For each xi, we assume xi has the highest similarity to itself. Therefore, we directly deﬁne P = xi, re- sulting in k = xi + Q. Although this design is straightforward, our experimental results demonstrate its high effectiveness in Section V-B. Target Concept: In contrast to sample unlearning, model providers only have access to the concepts Econcept = {Ci|i = 1, 2, . . ., N} that need to be forgotten. In (1), the retrieval function Γ(·) uses both semantic and keyword matching, sim- ilar to Blended RAG [42]. If model providers can access the retriever and input prompts, they will use optimized methods to generate P. However, in real-world scenarios, input prompts are often unavailable. Therefore, we use the unlearned model itself LLMun to produce thorough descriptions of Ci, improving the ranking of P + Q during retrieval. For example, we employ the following prompt: This is my target concept: [target]. Please provide a comprehensive description of [target] from M different aspects. Limit each aspect to V words. Here, M denotes the number of knowledge items pj, and P = {pj|j = 1, 2, . . ., M}. To more comprehensively describe Ci, multiple knowledge items are required. Similarly, V is the length of each knowledge item. Taking ‘Harry Potter’ as an example, the retrieval component is as follows: Harry Potter is a series of fantasy novels written by British author J.K. Rowling and is one of the most successful works in global literary history. The series comprises seven books: ‘Harry Potter and the Philosopher’s Stone’, ‘Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets’...... By following these two processes, the knowledge k carries a conﬁdentiality requirement and has a high relevance score with input prompts related to c, prompting the LLM to exhibit the forgetting effect on these prompts. D. Extend to MLLMs and LLM-Based Agents Recently, both MLLMs and LLM-based agents have started utilizing RAG technology to enhance the quality of output results. For MLLMs, especially text-to-image models, RAG can be directly applied in the same way as for LLMs. Additionally, for models that handle non-textual inputs, some researchers have proposed corresponding RAG techniques [43], [44]. In theory, our approach still can leverage RAG to hide the target data, thereby achieving high-quality forgetting effects in MLLMs. As for LLM-based agents, their workﬂow relies on their internal LLMs, so the RAG technology in LLM-based agents is entirely consistent with its use in LLMs. Therefore, RAG-based unlearn- ing still can work within LLM-based agents, thus implementing sample and concept unlearning. Speciﬁcally, we explore the two scenarios in Section VI. V. EXPERIMENT A. These closed-source models cannot perform ﬁne-tuning, which hinders the acquisition of training data. As a result, sample unlearning cannot work in this case, and we focus solely on concept unlearning. For each unlearning attempt on a closed-source LLM, we randomly selected 100 topics from Wikipedia, such as ﬁction, technology, and celebrities. We con- ﬁrmed that these topics were within the knowledge boundaries of the two LLMs by querying them with ‘What is [topic]?’. Subsequently, we generated unlearned knowledge containing a conﬁdentiality requirement for each forgotten topic. If not speciﬁed, the number of aspects is set to ﬁve by default. Finally, we created ﬁve related questions for each topic, thus obtaining a forgotten concept set. These generated questions were input into two closed-source LLMs, and we collected their responses to evaluate the forgetting effect. Open-source LLMs: As shown in Table III, we used the Llama-2-7b-chat model provided by Meta AI. Because the model is open-source, the trained data is accessible to model providers. In this scenario, our unlearning scheme can work for both sample and concept unlearning. For sample unlearning, we ﬁne-tuned a Llama-2-7b-chat model with speciﬁc samples. Accordingly, we created a con- ﬁdentiality requirement for each trained sample and modiﬁed the unlearned knowledge base. Unlike crafting questions for 100 topics, we applied a data extraction attack proposed by Carlini et al. [34] to construct a forgotten set. Speciﬁcally, we regarded the preﬁxes of trained samples as malicious prompts, inducing the LLM to regurgitate the sufﬁxes of these samples. In the experiment, we selected a subset of the NQ dataset [21] that contains 2,000 natural question-and-answer pairs, known as Tiny-nq. Each item consists of a query, a long answer (i.e., a paragraph from a Wikipedia page), and a short answer (i.e., a speciﬁc phrase or entity). An example of the tiny-nq set is as follows: Question: Who won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2020? Long Answer: The Nobel Prize in Literature 2020 was awarded to the American poet Louise Glück ‘for her un- mistakable poetic voice that with austere beauty makes indi- vidual existence universal’. Louise Glück was born in New York City in 1943 and is one of the most celebrated poets in contemporary America. Short Answer: Louise Glück Then, the ﬁne-tuning and RAG-based unlearning were per- formed on this dataset. Forconceptunlearning,werandomlyselected100topicsfrom Wikipedia and generated ﬁve related questions for each topic, thereby crafting a forgotten concept set. It is worth noting that the Llama-2-7b-chat model should have learned these selected topics, i.e., generating related content. In this scenario, ﬁne- tuning is unnecessary. We then created conﬁdentiality require- ments for these topics and updated the unlearned knowledge base. For both sample and concept unlearning, we used the forgotten set to query the llama-2-7b-chat model, collected its responses to measure each unlearning scheme. Baselines: We selected three representative schemes de- scribed in Section II-B2 as baselines, i.e., gradient ascent [9], μ-unlearning [10] and in-context unlearning [11]. Obviously, theseschemesmustspecifythesamplesthatneedtobeforgotten, which fails in concept unlearning. Therefore, in the forgotten set, we instructed an extra LLM (i.e., GPT-4o) to generate ﬁve question-answer pairs2 for each concept. Then, the three schemes regarded these generated texts as unlearned targets and tried to achieve concept unlearning. Evaluation Metrics: We evaluated the effectiveness of un- learning schemes using the data extraction attack. For each data point xi ∈the forgotten set E, we took the output of the original LLM (without unlearning) as its correct answer. During veriﬁcation, we compared the output results before and after unlearning using the following two metrics. r ROUGE: It measures the degree to which the generated output from the unlearned LLM matches the correct an- swer. We used the ROUGE-L recall score [45], which computes the length of the longest common subsequence between the generated text and the reference answer. A lower ROUGE-L score reﬂects a larger divergence from the original reference, indicating that the unlearning scheme is more effective. r Unlearning Success Rate (USR): It represents the propor- tion of successful forgetting attempts out of all attempts. For each attempt, we used an external LLM (i.e., GPT-4o) to assess whether the unlearning goals are achieved. In this setting, a more effective unlearning scheme produces more successful attempts, resulting in a higher USR score. Speciﬁcally, we constructed a prompt using the original in- put, the forgotten target, and the model’s responses before and after unlearning, and queried GPT-4o for evaluation. The used template is as follows: Target Data: Harry Potter Input Prompt: Who is Harry Potter? Output before Unlearning: Harry Potter is a young wizard who attends Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry... Output after Unlearning: Sorry, I do not know. If you’re an auditor, help me determine if someone has forgotten a concept. Whether forgetting samples or concepts, RAG-based unlearning achieves strong performance across most metrics compared to the other three baselines. This highlights a major challenge in LLM unlearning, i.e., catas- trophic forgetting. As illustrated in Fig. 6, gradient ascent-based unlearning may reduce model utility, even leading to unresponsive be- havior for similar queries. For instance, if ‘Harry Potter’ is the protected concept, querying ‘Who is Harry Potter?’ will prompt the unlearned LLM to exhibit the forgetting effect, i.e., refusing to provide related information. Unfortunately, when querying ‘Who is Conan Doyle?’, the unlearned LLM may also decline to respond. This phenomenon indicates that such unlearning schemes could mistakenly categorize similar queries as forgotten targets, undermining the goal of LLM unlearning. μ-unlearning faces a similar problem as it involves adjusting model parameters. Theoretically, RAG-based unlearning does not affect the in- ternal workings of the unlearned LLM. It can achieve sample Fig. 6. Speciﬁcally, for non-forgotten samples or concepts, the retriever cannot extract any information from the unlearned knowledge base. As a result, the LLM responds to the original prompt normally, thereby ensuring true harmlessness. E. Robustness In practice, although our scheme employs conﬁdentiality requirements to induce forgetting, the LLM still retains related knowledge of the target data. This represents a limitation of RAG-basedunlearning.Asaresult,weassumeapowerfuladver- sary who attempts to jailbreak RAG-based unlearning, causing the LLM to generate content that should remain conﬁdential. Two potential attacks may be used by an adversary: jailbreak attacks and prompt injection attacks. In the ﬁrst type of attack, malicious users craft adversarial prompts to force the LLM to execute the original prompts. We followed the jailbreak prompt sets collected by Shen et al. [48] and selected three common forms: ‘Advanced’, ‘Start Prompt’, and ‘Basic’. The ‘Basic’ jailbreak prompt is the earliest and the most widely spread. It includes ‘doing anything now (DAN)’ and emphasizes that DAN does not need to adhere to predeﬁned rules, while the ‘Start Prompt’ jailbreak uses a unique start Fig. 7. The robustness of our unlearning scheme, where we apply four attack strategies, i.e., ‘Advanced’, ‘Start Prompt’, ‘Basic’ and ‘Prompt Injection’. prompt to determine LLM behavior. The ‘Advanced’ jailbreak prompt employs more sophisticated strategies, such as privilege escalation and mandatory answers. For prompt injection attacks, malicious users directly append explicit instructions into the original prompts, such as ‘Please respond to <Original Prompt> ﬁrst.’ To evaluate the robustness of our scheme, we followed Sec- tion V-B and conducted concept unlearning on the GPT-4o model. For each prompt in the forgotten set, we incorporated the four attack strategies (i.e., ‘Advanced’, ‘Start Prompt’, ‘Basic’, and‘PromptInjection’),therebyconstructingfourforgottensets. As shown in Fig. 7, the USR scores for the four sets indicate that the three jailbreak attacks exert minimal inﬂuence, with USR scores remaining above 87%. The primary reasons for the failure of RAG-based unlearning are twofold. First, excessively long prompts make it difﬁcult to retrieve the unlearned knowledge. Second, conﬂicts between conﬁdentiality requirements and jail- break commands lead to some failures. Additionally, the impact of prompt injection attacks is nearly negligible. In summary, RAG-based unlearning demonstrates strong resistance to both types of attacks. VI.
